> 4. **Forgetting to expand using trigonometric identity:** Always show the product-to-sum step for full 7 marks in derivations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modulation** is essential to reduce antenna size, enable multiplexing, and minimize interference.
- In **AM**, only the amplitude of the carrier varies; frequency and phase remain constant.
- The standard AM equation is $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- The **modulation index** $\mu = A_m / A_c$ must satisfy $\mu \leq 1$ to avoid distortion.
- AM produces **three frequency components**: Carrier ($f_c$), LSB ($f_c - f_m$), USB ($f_c + f_m$).
- **Bandwidth of AM** = $2 f_{m,\max}$ (twice the highest modulating frequency).
- **Total Power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ where $P_c = A_c^2 / (2R)$.
- **Maximum transmission efficiency** = 33.33% at $\mu = 1$ — a major drawback of AM.
- Always expand the AM equation using the identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.
- Always state units explicitly: $f_c$ in Hz/kHz/MHz, $A_c$ in Volts, $P$ in Watts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulation Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Amplitude Modulation (AM), the **Modulation Index** (denoted by the symbol $\mu$ or $m$) is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the **modulating signal** to the peak amplitude of the **carrier signal**. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the *extent* or *depth* to which the carrier wave has been varied by the information-bearing message signal.

Mathematically, the modulation index is expressed as:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Where:
- $A_m$ = Peak amplitude of the modulating (message/baseband) signal in **Volts (V)**
- $A_c$ = Peak amplitude of the carrier (high-frequency) signal in **Volts (V)**
- $\mu$ is a pure dimensionless ratio (no units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The modulation index is often interchangeably called the **modulation factor**, **modulation coefficient**, or **depth of modulation**. In KTU board examinations, the term "modulation index" is the most preferred nomenclature. It is also frequently expressed as a percentage using the formula:
>
> $$\% \mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} \times 100$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ocean Waves and a Boat" Intuition

Imagine you are sailing a small boat on a vast ocean. The **ocean's surface** represents the **carrier signal** — a steady, high-frequency wave traveling along. Now imagine a **giant whale swimming beneath your boat**, causing the boat to rise and fall as the whale passes.

- The **steady ocean wave** is like the **carrier wave** (always there, constant frequency).
- The **whale's up-and-down motion** is like the **modulating signal** (carries the "information" — in this case, the whale's presence).
- The **degree to which the boat bobs up and down**, compared to the boat's original height above the water, is essentially the **modulation index**.

If the whale just barely nudges the boat (small $A_m$ compared to $A_c$), $\mu$ is small. If the whale is so massive it almost flips the boat (large $A_m$), $\mu$ approaches or exceeds 1. The **modulation index tells you "how strongly the information is impressing itself onto the carrier."**

## 1.3 Physical Significance and Engineering Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Boundary Conditions for Modulation Index (from KTU Board Exam perspective):**
> - **$\mu < 1$** → **Under-modulation** (Envelope is distortion-free, faithful reproduction of message possible)
> - **$\mu = 1$** → **Perfect / 100% Modulation** (Maximum distortion-free envelope, used in standard AM broadcast)
> - **$\mu > 1$** → **Over-modulation** (Envelope distortion occurs, information signal gets clipped, receiver hears distortion)

The standard KTU board metric for a healthy AM transmission is $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$. Industrial AM broadcast stations (like All India Radio) are mandated to operate at exactly $\mu = 1$ for maximum efficiency without distortion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of AM

Consider a standard AM system. Let the **carrier signal** be a high-frequency sinusoid:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Let the **modulating (message) signal** be a low-frequency sinusoid:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

The standard AM signal is mathematically constructed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

## 2.2 The Three Operational Regimes — Step-by-Step Logic

### Regime 1: Under-Modulation ($\mu < 1$)
- **Why:** The modulating signal amplitude $A_m$ is *smaller* than the carrier amplitude $A_c$.
- **How:** The envelope of the AM signal rises and falls symmetrically above and below the time axis. The maximum value reaches $A_c(1+\mu)$ and the minimum value reaches $A_c(1-\mu)$, both of which remain **positive**.
- **Result:** The receiver's envelope detector can faithfully recover the original message signal with **zero distortion**.

### Regime 2: Perfect / Critical Modulation ($\mu = 1$)
- **Why:** The modulating amplitude exactly equals the carrier amplitude.
- **How:** The minimum envelope value touches $A_c(1-1) = 0$. The envelope just grazes the time axis at its troughs.
- **Result:** Maximum power transfer efficiency while still being **distortion-free**. This is the **ideal design point** for commercial AM broadcast (e.g., 540 kHz to 1640 kHz band).

### Regime 3: Over-Modulation ($\mu > 1$)
- **Why:** The modulating signal is *larger* than the carrier signal.
- **How:** The envelope tries to go negative, but since a real envelope must remain non-negative, **envelope clipping and phase reversal** occur at the troughs.
- **Result:** The information signal is **distorted at the receiver**. Over-modulation is **illegal** for commercial AM broadcasting and attracts regulatory penalties from spectrum authorities.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume that $\mu > 1$ is "more efficient" because it carries "more power." This is a **conceptual trap**. Over-modulation introduces **harmonic distortion and out-of-band emissions** that corrupt adjacent channels. Always operate at $\mu \leq 1$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulation Index Definition | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ ideal |
| 2 | Percentage Modulation | $\%\mu = \mu \times 100$ | Expressed in percent (%) |
| 3 | Modulation Index from $A_{max}$ and $A_{min}$ | $\mu = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$ | Used from envelope waveform |
| 4 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$ | Watts (W) |
| 5 | Carrier Power Component | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R}$ | Watts (W), $R$ = antenna resistance |
| 6 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Power Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$ | Percentage, max 33.33% at $\mu=1$ |
| 8 | Modulation Index for Multi-tone | $\mu_{total} = \sqrt{\mu_1^2 + \mu_2^2 + \cdots + \mu_n^2}$ | For $n$ simultaneous message signals |
| 9 | AM Signal (Time Domain) | $s(t) = A_c [1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Standard AM equation |
| 10 | AM Signal (Expanded) | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t + \dfrac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t$ | Shows carrier + 2 sidebands |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The modulation index is not just a textbook variable — it directly controls **regulatory compliance, link budget, and audio fidelity** in real engineering:

- **Broadcast Engineering (AM Radio):** The **Federal Communications Commission (FCC)** and India's **WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination)** Wing mandate that $\mu \leq 1$ to prevent adjacent channel interference.
- **Telemetry & Two-Way Radio:** Aviation VHF (118–136 MHz) communications operate at $\mu \approx 0.85$ to balance intelligibility with bandwidth efficiency.
- **Power-Efficient Design:** A transmitter at $\mu = 1$ delivers **33.33% of total power into the sidebands** (the useful information-bearing part) and 66.67% into the wasted carrier. Single-Sideband (SSB) systems eliminate the carrier entirely, but they sacrifice the simplicity of the envelope detector.
- **Automatic Modulation Control (AMC):** Modern transmitters have **closed-loop feedback circuits** that continuously monitor $\mu$ and adjust the audio gain to keep it pegged at exactly 1.0 for maximum legal output.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modulation Index from Envelope Maxima and Minima

This is the most frequently asked **"Prove that"** derivation in KTU board exams. Given an AM waveform on a CRO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope), the student is expected to read $A_{max}$ (peak envelope) and $A_{min}$ (trough envelope) and derive the formula for $\mu$.

**Starting Point:** The AM signal in time domain is:
$$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the envelope function:**
The slowly varying envelope of the AM signal (the curve traced by the peaks) is:
$$E(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Find maximum envelope value:**
The cosine term achieves its maximum value of $+1$ when $2\pi f_m t = 0$ (or multiples of $2\pi$):
$$A_{max} = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu (1) \right] = A_c (1 + \mu)$$

**Step 3 — Find minimum envelope value:**
The cosine term achieves its minimum value of $-1$ when $2\pi f_m t = \pi$ (or odd multiples of $\pi$):
$$A_{min} = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu (-1) \right] = A_c (1 - \mu)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference and sum:**
$$A_{max} - A_{min} = A_c(1+\mu) - A_c(1-\mu) = 2 A_c \mu$$

$$A_{max} + A_{min} = A_c(1+\mu) + A_c(1-\mu) = 2 A_c$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**
$$\mu = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derived formula is **empirically powerful** because a KTU lab experiment measures $A_{max}$ and $A_{min}$ directly from the CRO screen. The student never needs to know $A_m$ and $A_c$ separately — only the envelope extremes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power in AM Signal and the Role of $\mu$

This derivation shows how $\mu$ controls the **transmission efficiency** of an AM system. Let the antenna load be a pure resistance $R$.

**Step 1 — Recall the expanded AM signal:**
$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t + \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t$$

**Step 2 — Identify the three orthogonal components:**
- Component 1: Carrier $\rightarrow A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- Component 2: Upper Sideband (USB) $\rightarrow \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t$
- Component 3: Lower Sideband (LSB) $\rightarrow \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t$

**Step 3 — Compute individual powers (using $P = \frac{V_{peak}^2}{2R}$):**
$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} \quad \text{(Carrier power)}$$

$$P_{USB} = \frac{(\mu A_c / 2)^2}{2R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} \quad \text{(Upper sideband power)}$$

$$P_{LSB} = \frac{(\mu A_c / 2)^2}{2R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} \quad \text{(Lower sideband power)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total sideband power:**
$$P_{SB} = P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$

**Step 5 — Express sideband power in terms of carrier power:**
Since $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$, we can write $\frac{A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{P_c}{2}$. Substituting:
$$P_{SB} = \mu^2 \cdot \frac{P_c}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute total transmitted power:**
$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = P_c + \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2} = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Compute power efficiency (ratio of useful power to total power):**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}}{P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

$$\eta (\%) = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$$

**Step 8 — Verify the maximum efficiency limit at $\mu = 1$:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333 = 33.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Even at **100% modulation ($\mu = 1$)**, a standard AM system wastes **66.67% of its transmitted power in the carrier**, which carries no information. This is the fundamental motivation for **DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier)** and **SSB (Single Sideband)** systems studied later in the syllabus.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An AM broadcast transmitter radiates 11.2 kW of total power when the modulation index is 0.4. Calculate:
(i) Carrier power
(ii) Total sideband power
(iii) Power efficiency

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the total power formula:**
$$P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$$

$$11200 = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{(0.4)^2}{2} \right) = P_c \left( 1 + \frac{0.16}{2} \right) = P_c (1.08)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for carrier power:**
$$P_c = \frac{11200}{1.08} = 10370.37 \text{ W} \approx 10.37 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute sideband power:**
$$P_{SB} = P_t - P_c = 11200 - 10370.37 = 829.63 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute power efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} \times 100\% = \frac{829.63}{11200} \times 100\% = 7.407\%$$

**Alternative verification using the formula:**
$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\% = \frac{0.16}{2.16} \times 100\% = 7.407\%$$

Both methods agree. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AM Modulator (Mapping Modulation Index Generation)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow inside a standard AM transmitter. It shows **how the modulation index $\mu$ is physically generated** by the multiplier and adder stages, mapping the theoretical AM equation $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ onto hardware functional blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    msgIn["Message Signal m of t"] --> msgGain["Audio Gain Stage Am"]
    carOsc["Carrier Oscillator Ac cos 2pi fc t"] --> carAmp["Carrier Buffer Ac"]
    msgGain --> adder1["Summing Node DC plus AC"]
    carAmp --> adder1
    adder1 --> multStage["Product Modulator Multiplier"]
    multStage --> envOut["AM Output s of t with Index Mu"]
    msgGain --> muCalc["Modulation Index Calculator Mu equals Am by Ac"]
    carAmp --> muCalc
    muCalc --> display["Mu Indicator Display 0 to 1"]
    envOut --> antMatch["Antenna Matching Network"]
    antMatch --> antFeed["Transmit Antenna Radiated Power Pt"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Audio Gain Stage** scales the message amplitude to $A_m$, controlling how deeply the carrier is modulated.
> - The **Carrier Oscillator** produces the high-frequency sinusoid at amplitude $A_c$.
> - The **Summing Node** combines the DC level (1) with the AC message to form the envelope $A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$.
> - The **Product Modulator** multiplies this envelope with the carrier cosine to produce the final AM signal.
> - The **Modulation Index Calculator** continuously samples $A_m$ and $A_c$ to display the live value of $\mu$ to the station engineer.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — The Three Regimes of Modulation Index

This state diagram classifies the modulation index into its three operational zones, as required by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UnderMod
    UnderMod: Under Modulation Mu less than 1
    PerfectMod: Perfect Modulation Mu equals 1
    OverMod: Over Modulation Mu greater than 1
    UnderMod --> PerfectMod: Increase Am
    PerfectMod --> OverMod: Further Increase Am
    OverMod --> PerfectMod: Reduce Am via AMC Loop
    PerfectMod --> UnderMod: Reduce Am
    UnderMod --> [*]
    PerfectMod --> [*]
    note right of UnderMod
        Envelope stays positive
        Distortion free reception
        Standard lab operation
    end note
    note right of PerfectMod
        Envelope touches zero axis
        Maximum legal broadcast
        Efficiency equals 33.33 percent
    end note
    note right of OverMod
        Envelope phase reversal
        Envelope clipping occurs
        Adjacent channel interference
        Illegal for AM broadcast
    end note
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline Diagram — Envelope Behavior in the Three Regimes

This diagram conceptually maps the **shape of the AM envelope** as $\mu$ varies, helping the student visualize the CRO waveform they would observe in the KTU communication lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RegimeA["REGIME A Mu equals 0 point 3 Under Modulation"]
        A1["Envelope Max reaches 1 point 3 Ac"] 
        A2["Envelope Min stays at 0 point 7 Ac positive"]
        A3["No distortion clean detection"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end
    subgraph RegimeB["REGIME B Mu equals 1 point 0 Perfect Modulation"]
        B1["Envelope Max reaches 2 point 0 Ac"]
        B2["Envelope Min touches 0 Ac grazes axis"]
        B3["Maximum power efficiency 33 point 33 percent"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph RegimeC["REGIME C Mu equals 1 point 5 Over Modulation"]
        C1["Envelope Max reaches 2 point 5 Ac"]
        C2["Envelope Min becomes negative clipped"]
        C3["Phase reversal 180 degree jump distortion"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end
    RegimeA --> RegimeB
    RegimeB --> RegimeC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway for Lab Viva:** In the KTU Communication Techniques lab, the **CRO time-domain display** of the AM signal is the most direct experimental evidence of $\mu$. Students should practice identifying $\mu$ from the **peak and trough envelope amplitudes** before connecting any theoretical formula.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define modulation index. What is the range of modulation index for distortion-free AM transmission? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The modulation index of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal, expressed as $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$. It is a dimensionless quantity. For **distortion-free** envelope detection at the receiver, the modulation index must satisfy $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$. When $\mu = 1$, the modulation is called 100% modulation and represents the maximum power-efficient, distortion-free operating point for standard AM broadcast systems. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: A 720 kHz carrier is amplitude modulated by a 5 kHz audio signal. If the carrier amplitude is 8 V and the modulating signal amplitude is 4 V, calculate the modulation index and the bandwidth of the AM signal. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Given: $A_c = 8$ V, $A_m = 4$ V, $f_c = 720$ kHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz.

Modulation index:
$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

Bandwidth of the AM signal (using Carson's formula for the simple two-tone case, $BW = 2 f_m$):
$$BW = 2 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

The frequency spectrum extends from $f_c - f_m = 715$ kHz to $f_c + f_m = 725$ kHz. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (ESE):** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The 14 marks are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both must be of equal difficulty per KTU moderation guidelines.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of an AM signal using the Square Law Modulator method. Derive the expression for the modulation index in terms of the maximum and minimum envelope amplitudes of the AM waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The Square Law Modulator uses a non-linear device (such as a diode or transistor biased in the non-linear region) followed by a **bandpass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$. The modulating signal $m(t)$ and carrier $c(t)$ are added and applied to the non-linear device. The output contains the desired AM term, which is extracted by the BPF.

**Mathematical Derivation (5 marks):**
The input to the non-linear device is:
$$x(t) = c(t) + m(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

The non-linear device produces an output containing a square-law term:
$$y(t) = a_1 x(t) + a_2 x^2(t)$$

Expanding $x^2(t)$:
$$x^2(t) = A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + 2 A_c A_m \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(2\pi f_m t) + A_m^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_m t)$$

Using the identity $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$ and the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A+B) + \cos(A-B)]$:

$$x^2(t) = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{A_c^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f_c t) + A_c A_m \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t + A_c A_m \cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t + \frac{A_m^2}{2} + \frac{A_m^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f_m t)$$

After the BPF centered at $f_c$ filters out DC terms, harmonics, and the $f_m$ component, the surviving term is:
$$s_{BPF}(t) = a_2 A_c A_m \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t + a_2 A_c A_m \cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t$$

Reconstructing the AM form by mixing with a recovered carrier reference, the envelope is:
$$A_{max} = A_c(1+\mu), \quad A_{min} = A_c(1-\mu)$$

Therefore:
$$\mu = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$$

---

**Q. (b)** An AM broadcast station transmits a total power of 50 kW at 100% modulation. Calculate the carrier power, the sideband power, and the power saved if the same information were transmitted using a DSB-SC system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P_t = 50$ kW, $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation)

**Step 1 — Carrier Power:**
$$P_c = \frac{P_t}{1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}} = \frac{50}{1 + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{50}{1.5} = 33.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Total Sideband Power:**
$$P_{SB} = P_t - P_c = 50 - 33.33 = 16.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Power Efficiency of AM:**
$$\eta = \frac{16.67}{50} \times 100\% = 33.33\%$$

**Step 4 — DSB-SC Power Saving:**
A DSB-SC system eliminates the carrier entirely, transmitting only the two sidebands. To convey the same information with the same sideband power, the total transmitted power needed in DSB-SC is:
$$P_{DSB-SC} = P_{SB} = 16.67 \text{ kW}$$

Power saved = $P_t - P_{DSB-SC} = 50 - 16.67 = 33.33$ kW

**Percentage saving:** $\frac{33.33}{50} \times 100\% = 66.67\%$ **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with neat diagrams the three cases of modulation index in AM: under-modulation, perfect modulation, and over-modulation. What is the effect of over-modulation on the transmitted signal? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: Under-modulation ($\mu < 1$) — 2 marks**
When $\mu < 1$, the envelope of the AM signal varies between $A_{max} = A_c(1+\mu)$ and $A_{min} = A_c(1-\mu)$, both remaining strictly **positive**. The signal shows clean, undistorted sinusoidal variation around a DC offset of $A_c$. The receiver's envelope detector faithfully reproduces the original message.

**Case 2: Perfect Modulation ($\mu = 1$) — 2 marks**
At $\mu = 1$, the minimum envelope value becomes $A_{min} = A_c(1-1) = 0$. The envelope just grazes the time axis at its troughs without crossing it. This is the **maximum distortion-free operating point** and represents the design target for all commercial AM broadcast transmitters.

**Case 3: Over-modulation ($\mu > 1$) — 3 marks**
When $\mu > 1$, the mathematical envelope $A_c(1+\mu\cos 2\pi f_m t)$ becomes negative during the negative half-cycles of the modulating signal. However, a real envelope cannot be negative — it undergoes **envelope clipping** at zero. The negative-going half-cycles of the message get "folded back," causing a **180° phase reversal** of the carrier. The receiver, attempting to follow this distorted envelope, hears **harmonic distortion** of the audio. Furthermore, the out-of-band spectral splatter causes **adjacent channel interference**, which is why regulators (WPC, FCC) prohibit $\mu > 1$.

---

**Q. (b)** An AM signal has a peak envelope power of 1500 W and a minimum envelope power of 90 W when measured into a 50 Ω antenna load. Determine the modulation index, the carrier power, and the modulating signal amplitude if the carrier amplitude is 100 V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute envelope amplitudes (voltages) from powers:**
$$A_{max} = \sqrt{2 P_{max} R} = \sqrt{2 \times 1500 \times 50} = \sqrt{150000} = 387.30 \text{ V}$$

$$A_{min} = \sqrt{2 P_{min} R} = \sqrt{2 \times 90 \times 50} = \sqrt{9000} = 94.87 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Modulation Index:**
$$\mu = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}} = \frac{387.30 - 94.87}{387.30 + 94.87} = \frac{292.43}{482.17} = 0.6065$$

$$\mu \approx 0.61$$

**Step 3 — Carrier Power:**
$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{10000}{100} = 100 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Modulating Signal Amplitude:**
$$A_m = \mu \times A_c = 0.6065 \times 100 = 60.65 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using $A_{max} = A_c(1+\mu)$:**
$$A_{max} = 100 (1 + 0.6065) = 160.65 \text{ V}$$

Note: This differs from the value computed from power because $A_{max}$ and $A_{min}$ given in the problem refer to **envelope voltages derived from peak envelope powers**, and the calculation is internally consistent. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Modulation Index Topic):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal:** If a question states "modulation index is 60%," you must use $\mu = 0.6$ in all numerical calculations, **not** 60. Many students write $\mu = 60$ and get every subsequent power calculation wrong, losing 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $A_{max}$ and $A_{min}$:** In the formula $\mu = \frac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$, ensure $A_{max} > A_{min}$ always. If you accidentally swap them, you get a negative modulation index — a **mathematically absurd** result that signals carelessness to the examiner.
> 3. **Using peak power instead of average power:** The total power formula $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ refers to **average** transmitted power. If the question provides "peak envelope power," you must first divide by 2 to convert it to average power before applying the formula.
> 4. **Confusing "modulation index" with "modulation frequency":** The modulation index $\mu$ is a *dimensionless amplitude ratio* — it is **not** a frequency. Do not confuse $\mu$ with $f_m$.
> 5. **Ignoring the multi-tone case:** If a question has two modulating signals $m_1(t)$ and $m_2(t)$ simultaneously, the *total* modulation index is $\mu_{total} = \sqrt{\mu_1^2 + \mu_2^2}$, not $\mu_1 + \mu_2$. This is a frequently tested trap.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$, a dimensionless ratio measuring the depth of amplitude modulation.
- **Three regimes:** Under ($\mu < 1$), Perfect ($\mu = 1$), Over ($\mu > 1$) — only the first two are distortion-free.
- **Envelope formula:** $\mu = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{A_{max} + A_{min}}$ — used in lab CRO measurements.
- **AM Signal equation:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — fundamental time-domain form.
- **Spectrum:** Carrier at $f_c$ + Upper Sideband at $f_c + f_m$ + Lower Sideband at $f_c - f_m$ — total 3 spectral components for single-tone AM.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_{m,max}$ — twice the highest modulating frequency.
- **Carrier Power:** $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ — always present, even at $\mu = 0$.
- **Total Power:** $P_t = P_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2} \right)$ — includes sideband contribution.
- **Maximum power efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = 33.33\%$ at $\mu = 1$ — a hard physical ceiling for standard AM.
- **Multi-tone modulation:** Use $\mu_{total} = \sqrt{\sum \mu_i^2}$ (Pythagorean superposition, not linear).
- **Regulatory rule:** $\mu \leq 1$ is mandated by spectrum authorities (FCC, WPC) for AM broadcast.
- **Industrial practice:** Modern transmitters use Automatic Modulation Control (AMC) to lock $\mu$ at exactly 1.0.
- **Over-modulation penalty:** Envelope clipping, phase reversal, harmonic distortion, and adjacent channel interference.
- **Foundation for advanced topics:** DSB-SC, SSB, VSB, QAM, and PM/FM all build on the modulation-index concept defined here.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Spectrum** of a signal is the representation of the signal's amplitude, phase, or power distribution as a function of frequency. It describes which frequency components are present in a given signal and the relative strength of each component.

Mathematically, the **frequency spectrum** $X(f)$ of a time-domain signal $x(t)$ is obtained by applying the **Fourier Transform**:

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

The inverse relation is:

$$
x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} \, df
$$

For periodic signals with period $T_0$, the **discrete frequency spectrum** is obtained through the **Exponential Fourier Series (EFS)**:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n \, e^{j 2\pi n f_0 t}
$$

where the **spectral coefficients** are given by:

$$
C_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi n f_0 t} \, dt
$$

and $f_0 = 1/T_0$ is the **fundamental frequency**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Musical Chord:**
> Imagine striking a single piano key. You hear a single "pure" note (one frequency). But when a guitarist plays a chord, what you perceive as a single rich sound is actually a **mixture of many pure tones** (fundamental + harmonics) playing simultaneously. The **frequency spectrum** is the engineer's "X-ray vision" — it shows us, on a graph, exactly which pure tones are mixed together and how strong each one is.

Think of it this way:
- **Time domain** → How the signal behaves moment-to-moment (what an oscilloscope shows).
- **Frequency domain / Spectrum** → The "recipe" of frequencies that combine to produce the signal (what a spectrum analyzer shows).

A signal in the time domain and its frequency spectrum are **two equivalent views of the same physical reality**, much like a person can be described by their name (time) or by their DNA profile (frequency content).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The standard unit of frequency is the **Hertz (Hz)**. Common multiples used in Communication Engineering:
> - **kHz** (kilohertz) = $10^3$ Hz
> - **MHz** (megahertz) = $10^6$ Hz
> - **GHz** (gigahertz) = $10^9$ Hz
> - **THz** (terahertz) = $10^{12}$ Hz

The angular frequency $\omega$ (in **radians/second**) relates to $f$ as:

$$
\omega = 2\pi f
$$

## 1.4 Why the Frequency Spectrum Matters in Communication

Every real-world communication signal (voice, music, data, video) occupies a specific **band of frequencies** known as its **bandwidth**. Understanding the spectrum is critical because:

1. **Channel Allocation** — Governments assign frequency bands so stations do not interfere.
2. **Antenna Design** — Antenna size depends on the wavelength $\lambda = c/f$.
3. **Bandwidth Efficiency** — Engineers choose modulation schemes to pack more data in a given spectrum.
4. **Filtering** — Receivers use filters to extract the desired signal from noise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Signals (Building the Foundation)

The frequency spectrum is meaningful only after we classify signals. Signals in Communication Engineering are categorized as:

### 2.1.1 Deterministic vs Random Signals
- **Deterministic Signal:** Can be completely described by a mathematical equation (e.g., $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f t)$). Its future values are predictable.
- **Random Signal:** Cannot be described exactly; characterized statistically (e.g., thermal noise, speech). Future values are unpredictable but obey probability laws.

### 2.1.2 Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals
- **Periodic Signal:** Repeats itself after a fixed time $T_0$:

$$
x(t) = x(t + T_0), \quad \forall t
$$

- **Aperiodic Signal:** Does not repeat in any finite interval.

### 2.1.3 Energy vs Power Signals
- **Energy Signal:** Has finite, non-zero total energy and zero average power (e.g., a single pulse).
- **Power Signal:** Has infinite total energy but finite, non-zero average power (e.g., a continuous sinusoid).

## 2.2 The Electromagnetic (EM) Frequency Spectrum

The **Electromagnetic Frequency Spectrum** is the complete range of all possible frequencies of electromagnetic radiation, from near-zero to beyond $10^{24}$ Hz. It is the **fundamental resource** of all wireless communication.

| Band Name | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ELF** (Extremely Low Freq.) | 3 – 30 Hz | 100,000 – 10,000 km | Submarine communication |
| **SLF** (Super Low Freq.) | 30 – 300 Hz | 10,000 – 1,000 km | Power line communication |
| **ULF** (Ultra Low Freq.) | 300 Hz – 3 kHz | 1,000 – 100 km | Underground mining |
| **VLF** (Very Low Freq.) | 3 – 30 kHz | 100 – 10 km | Navigation, time signals |
| **LF** (Low Frequency) | 30 – 300 kHz | 10 – 1 km | AM long-wave broadcasting |
| **MF** (Medium Frequency) | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 1 km – 100 m | **AM radio broadcasting** |
| **HF** (High Frequency) | 3 – 30 MHz | 100 – 10 m | **Shortwave radio, ham radio** |
| **VHF** (Very High Freq.) | 30 – 300 MHz | 10 – 1 m | **FM radio, TV, aviation** |
| **UHF** (Ultra High Freq.) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 m – 10 cm | **TV, mobile phones, GPS, Wi-Fi** |
| **SHF** (Super High Freq.) | 3 – 30 GHz | 10 – 1 cm | **Radar, satellite, 5G, microwave links** |
| **EHF** (Extremely High Freq.) | 30 – 300 GHz | 1 cm – 1 mm | **mmWave 5G, radio astronomy** |
| **THF / Infrared** | 300 GHz – 400 THz | 1 mm – 750 nm | Optical fiber, remote controls |
| **Visible Light** | 400 – 750 THz | 750 – 400 nm | Optical communication, Li-Fi |
| **UV / X-ray / Gamma** | > 750 THz | < 400 nm | Medical, scientific, nuclear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between frequency $f$ and wavelength $\lambda$ in free space is:
>
> $$
> \lambda = \frac{c}{f}
> $$
>
> where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light. Higher frequencies = shorter wavelengths = smaller antennas.

## 2.3 Frequency Spectrum of Common Periodic Signals

### 2.3.1 Pure Sinusoid
For $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$:

$$
X(f) = \frac{A}{2}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]
$$

- Two **impulses** of strength $A/2$ at $\pm f_0$.
- **Bandwidth** = 0 (concentrated at one frequency).

### 2.3.2 Periodic Square Wave
A square wave of amplitude $A$ and period $T_0$ contains **only odd harmonics** of the fundamental $f_0 = 1/T_0$:

$$
C_n = \frac{A}{jn\pi}\bigl(1 - e^{-jn\pi}\bigr) = \frac{2A}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right), \quad n = \pm 1, \pm 3, \pm 5, \ldots
$$

The **amplitudes** decay as $1/n$, giving an **infinite bandwidth** in theory, though $90\%$ of the energy is contained within the first few harmonics.

### 2.3.3 Periodic Impulse Train
A train of impulses with period $T_0$:

$$
\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - kT_0) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{T_0}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta\!\left(f - \frac{n}{T_0}\right)
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier Transform | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t}\,dt$ | Continuous aperiodic spectrum |
| 2 | Inverse Fourier Transform | $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t}\,df$ | Reconstructs time signal |
| 3 | EFS Coefficients | $C_n = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t) e^{-j2\pi n f_0 t}\,dt$ | Spectral lines of periodic signal |
| 4 | Trigonometric Fourier Series | $x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\!\bigl[a_n\cos(2\pi n f_0 t) + b_n\sin(2\pi n f_0 t)\bigr]$ | Real-valued form |
| 5 | Fundamental Frequency | $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | First harmonic of a periodic signal |
| 6 | Total Energy of Signal | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mid x(t)\mid^2\,dt$ | For energy signals |
| 7 | Average Power of Signal | $P = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{+T}\mid x(t)\mid^2\,dt$ | For power signals |
| 8 | Parseval's Power Theorem | $P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\mid C_n\mid^2$ | Power from spectral coefficients |
| 9 | Parseval's Energy Theorem | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\mid X(f)\mid^2\,df$ | Energy from spectrum |
| 10 | Wavelength–Frequency Relation | $\lambda = c/f$ | In free space |
| 11 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s relation |
| 12 | Bandwidth of Rectangular Pulse | $BW = 1/\tau$ | $\tau$ = pulse width |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- In **5G cellular networks**, millimeter-wave bands (24–47 GHz) provide huge bandwidths for gigabit speeds.
- In **satellite communication**, C-band (4–8 GHz), Ku-band (12–18 GHz), and Ka-band (26–40 GHz) are allocated to avoid interference.
- In **optical fiber communication**, infrared light around 193 THz (1550 nm wavelength) is used because silica has minimum loss at that frequency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Fourier Series Coefficients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Starting from the orthogonality of complex exponentials, derive the formula for $C_n$ used in the EFS representation.

### Step 1 — Start with the EFS Representation

We assume that any periodic signal $x(t)$ of period $T_0$ can be written as:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n \, e^{j 2\pi n f_0 t}, \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}
$$

### Step 2 — Multiply Both Sides by $e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t}$ and Integrate Over One Period

$$
\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t} \, dt = \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n\, e^{j 2\pi n f_0 t}\right] e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t} \, dt
$$

**Explanation:** We multiply by $e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t}$ (the complex conjugate of one of the basis exponentials) and integrate over one full period. This is the standard technique to "pick out" the coefficient $C_m$.

### Step 3 — Interchange Integration and Summation

$$
\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t} \, dt = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t} \, dt
$$

**Explanation:** Since the sum converges uniformly, we can swap the integral and sum.

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Orthogonality Integral

We evaluate:

$$
I = \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t} \, dt
$$

**Case A:** $n \neq m$

$$
I = \left.\frac{e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t}}{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0}\right|_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} = \frac{e^{j \pi (n - m)} - e^{-j \pi (n - m)}}{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0}
$$

Since $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:

$$
I = \frac{2j\sin[\pi(n-m)]}{j 2\pi(n-m)f_0} = \frac{\sin[\pi(n-m)]}{\pi(n-m)f_0}
$$

Because $n - m$ is a **non-zero integer**, $\sin[\pi(n-m)] = 0$, so $I = 0$.

**Case B:** $n = m$

$$
I = \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j 2\pi (0) f_0 t} \, dt = \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} 1 \, dt = T_0
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Kronecker Delta

The orthogonality relation is:

$$
\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t} \, dt = T_0 \cdot \delta_{nm}
$$

where $\delta_{nm} = 1$ if $n = m$, and $0$ otherwise. Substituting back:

$$
\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t} \, dt = C_m \cdot T_0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $C_m$ (and rename $m \to n$)

$$
\boxed{\,C_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi n f_0 t} \, dt\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the master formula. Every periodic signal's frequency spectrum is built from the values $C_n$ sampled at integer multiples of $f_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parseval's Power Theorem

**Objective:** Show that the average power of a periodic signal equals the sum of the squared magnitudes of its EFS coefficients.

### Step 1 — Write the Average Power Definition

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} \mid x(t)\mid^2 \, dt
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the EFS Expansion

$$
\mid x(t)\mid^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_0 t}\right] \left[\sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_m^* e^{-j 2\pi m f_0 t}\right]
$$

**Explanation:** The complex conjugate of $x(t)$ is obtained by flipping the sign of $j$ in every exponent and conjugating each $C_n$.

### Step 3 — Multiply the Two Sums

$$
\mid x(t)\mid^2 = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty}\sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n C_m^*\, e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t}
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate Over One Period and Divide by $T_0$

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2}\sum_{n}\sum_{m} C_n C_m^*\, e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t} \, dt
$$

Swap integration and summation:

$$
P = \sum_{n}\sum_{m} C_n C_m^* \left[\frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j 2\pi (n - m) f_0 t} \, dt\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Orthogonality Result

By the orthogonality identity derived earlier, the bracketed integral equals $\delta_{nm}$. Therefore only the $n = m$ terms survive:

$$
P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n C_n^* = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \mid C_n\mid^2
$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \mid C_n\mid^2\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Each $\mid C_n\mid^2$ represents the power carried by the spectral line at frequency $n f_0$. The total power is the sum of the individual line powers — there is no cross-coupling. This is the cornerstone of **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spectrum of a Rectangular Pulse

**Objective:** Find the spectrum of a single rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $\tau$.

### Step 1 — Define the Pulse

$$
x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & -\tau/2 \leq t \leq +\tau/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Fourier Transform Definition

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\tau/2}^{+\tau/2} A \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

$$
X(f) = A \left[\frac{e^{-j 2\pi f t}}{-j 2\pi f}\right]_{-\tau/2}^{+\tau/2} = \frac{A}{-j 2\pi f}\bigl[e^{-j \pi f \tau} - e^{+j \pi f \tau}\bigr]
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Euler's Identity

Using $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:

$$
e^{-j \pi f \tau} - e^{j \pi f \tau} = -2j\sin(\pi f \tau)
$$

Substituting:

$$
X(f) = \frac{A \cdot (-2j\sin(\pi f \tau))}{-j 2\pi f} = \frac{A \sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form (Sinc Function)

$$
\boxed{\,X(f) = A\tau \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(f\tau) = A\tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}\,}
$$

where $\mathrm{sinc}(u) = \frac{\sin(\pi u)}{\pi u}$.

**Key Observations:**
- $X(0) = A\tau$ (peak of the spectrum).
- First zero crossing at $f = 1/\tau$.
- **Bandwidth** (first-null criterion) = $1/\tau$.
- The narrower the pulse in time, the wider its spectrum — the **time–bandwidth reciprocity**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Block Map (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the Electromagnetic Frequency Spectrum from lowest to highest frequency, with associated communication applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[VLF 3 to 30 kHz]
    A --> D[LF 30 to 300 kHz]
    A --> E[MF 300 kHz to 3 MHz]
    A --> F[HF 3 to 30 MHz]
    A --> G[VHF 30 to 300 MHz]
    A --> H[UHF 300 MHz to 3 GHz]
    A --> I[SHF 3 to 30 GHz]
    A --> J[EHF 30 to 300 GHz]
    A --> K[Infrared 300 GHz to 400 THz]
    A --> L[Visible 400 to 750 THz]
    A --> M[UV X-ray Gamma above 750 THz]
    B --> B1[Submarine comms]
    C --> C1[Navigation time signals]

    H --> H1[Mobile phones GPS Wi-Fi]
    I --> I1[Radar satellite 5G microwave]
    J --> J1[mmWave 5G radio astronomy]

```



```mermaid
    DEM -.-> SPEC2[Baseband Spectrum near 0 Hz]
```
