# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define frequency spectrum. How is it obtained from a time-domain signal?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **frequency spectrum** of a signal is the representation of the signal's amplitude and phase as a function of frequency. It shows how the signal's energy or power is distributed across different frequency components.

For a **non-periodic** signal, the spectrum is obtained using the **Fourier Transform**:

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

For a **periodic** signal of period $T_0$, the discrete spectrum is obtained from the **Exponential Fourier Series coefficients**:

$$
C_n = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi n f_0 t}\, dt, \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}
$$

The plot of $\vert C_n\vert$ versus $nf_0$ gives the **amplitude spectrum**, and $\angle C_n$ versus $nf_0$ gives the **phase spectrum**.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> List any six frequency bands of the electromagnetic spectrum with one application each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Band | Frequency Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **MF** | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | AM radio broadcasting |
| **HF** | 3 – 30 MHz | Shortwave radio, amateur radio |
| **VHF** | 30 – 300 MHz | FM radio, television |
| **UHF** | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | Mobile phones, GPS, Wi-Fi |
| **SHF** | 3 – 30 GHz | Satellite communication, radar |
| **EHF** | 30 – 300 GHz | mmWave 5G, radio astronomy |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Exponential Fourier Series coefficients** of a periodic signal. State clearly the orthogonality condition used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A periodic rectangular pulse train has amplitude $A = 5$ V, pulse width $\tau = 1$ ms, and period $T_0 = 4$ ms. Determine the **fundamental frequency** and compute the **DC component and the first three non-zero harmonic amplitudes** of its spectrum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks
*(See the complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

The derivation proceeds in six steps:
1. Start with $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} C_n e^{j2\pi n f_0 t}$.
2. Multiply both sides by $e^{-j2\pi m f_0 t}$ and integrate over $-T_0/2$ to $+T_0/2$.
3. Interchange integration and summation.
4. Evaluate the orthogonality integral:
   - $I = T_0$ when $n = m$
   - $I = 0$ when $n \neq m$.
5. Apply the Kronecker delta $\delta_{nm}$.
6. Solve for $C_n = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t) e^{-j2\pi n f_0 t} dt$.

**Orthogonality Condition:**

$$
\int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} e^{j2\pi(n-m)f_0 t}\,dt = T_0\,\delta_{nm}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Fundamental Frequency**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 250 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Duty Cycle**

$$
D = \frac{\tau}{T_0} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — General EFS Coefficient Formula**

For a rectangular pulse train of amplitude $A$ and duty cycle $D$:

$$
C_n = A \cdot D \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(nD) = A \cdot D \cdot \frac{\sin(n\pi D)}{n\pi D}
$$

**Step 4 — DC Component (n = 0)**

$$
C_0 = A \cdot D = 5 \times 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5 — First Harmonic (n = 1)**

$$
C_1 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{\sin(0.25\pi)}{0.25\pi} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{\sin(45°)}{0.7854} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{0.7071}{0.7854} \approx 1.125 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Second Harmonic (n = 2)**

$$
C_2 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{\sin(0.5\pi)}{0.5\pi} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{1}{1.5708} \approx 0.7958 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 7 — Third Harmonic (n = 3)**

$$
C_3 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{\sin(0.75\pi)}{0.75\pi} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{0.7071}{2.3562} \approx 0.3752 \text{ V}
$$

**Summary of Spectrum:**

| Harmonic | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (V) |
|---|---|---|
| DC | 0 | 1.25 |
| 1st | 250 | 1.125 |
| 2nd | 500 | 0.7958 |
| 3rd | 750 | 0.3752 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** Explain the **classification of signals** based on determinism, periodicity, and energy/power criteria. Give one engineering example of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** State and prove **Parseval's Power Theorem** for a periodic signal. A periodic signal has EFS coefficients $C_0 = 2, C_{\pm 1} = 3, C_{\pm 2} = 1$, all others zero. Find the average power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**1. Determinism:**
- **Deterministic:** Can be described by an explicit equation; future values are known exactly. Example: $x(t) = 10\cos(100\pi t)$.
- **Random:** Cannot be described deterministically; characterized by statistical averages. Example: Thermal noise at a receiver input.

**2. Periodicity:**
- **Periodic:** $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for some $T_0 > 0$. Example: Square wave clock in a digital circuit.
- **Aperiodic (Non-periodic):** No repetition. Example: A single rectangular pulse.

**3. Energy vs Power:**
- **Energy Signal:** Total energy $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ is finite and non-zero; average power $P = 0$. Example: Single radar pulse.
- **Power Signal:** Average power $P$ is finite and non-zero; total energy is infinite. Example: Continuous sinusoidal carrier.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Statement:** The average power of a periodic signal equals the sum of the squared magnitudes of all its Exponential Fourier Series coefficients:

$$
P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert C_n\vert^2
$$

**Proof:** *(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$
P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert C_n\vert^2 = \vert C_0\vert^2 + 2\vert C_1\vert^2 + 2\vert C_2\vert^2
$$

$$
P = (2)^2 + 2(3)^2 + 2(1)^2 = 4 + 18 + 2 = 24 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** When summing $\vert C_n\vert^2$ over $n \neq 0$, students often write $\vert C_1\vert^2$ instead of $2\vert C_1\vert^2$ because both $C_{+1}$ and $C_{-1}$ carry equal power. **Lose 1 mark** if you forget this.
> 2. **Mixing up EFS and TFS formulas:** Trigonometric Fourier Series uses $a_n, b_n$; Exponential uses $C_n$. Do not interchange them in the same answer.
> 3. **Skipping the orthogonality statement:** In derivation questions, you must explicitly write the orthogonality integral — partial credit is lost if you just state the result.
> 4. **Wrong limits on the integration:** Always verify whether the question gives a signal defined on $[0, T_0]$ or $[-T_0/2, +T_0/2]$. The integration limits change, but the final $C_n$ is identical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Frequency Spectrum**

- The **frequency spectrum** is the Fourier-domain representation showing the strength of each frequency component in a signal.
- **Periodic signals** → **discrete (line) spectrum** via Fourier Series.
- **Aperiodic signals** → **continuous spectrum** via Fourier Transform.
- **Pure sinusoid** $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ → Two spectral lines at $\pm f_0$, each of strength $A/2$.
- **Rectangular pulse** of width $\tau$ → Sinc spectrum, first null at $f = 1/\tau$ (bandwidth).
- **Square wave** → Only odd harmonics, amplitudes decay as $1/n$.
- **Impulse train** in time → Impulse train in frequency with spacing $1/T_0$.
- **EM Spectrum** is divided into bands: ELF, SLF, ULF, VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF, Infrared, Visible, UV, X-ray, Gamma.
- **Lower frequencies** (MF, HF) → longer wavelengths → larger antennas, longer range, ground-wave/sky-wave propagation.
- **Higher frequencies** (SHF, EHF) → shorter wavelengths → smaller antennas, line-of-sight propagation, used in satellite and 5G.
- **Wavelength–Frequency** relation: $\lambda = c/f$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Parseval's Power Theorem:** $P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert C_n\vert^2$ — total power is the sum of line powers.
- **Parseval's Energy Theorem:** $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f)\vert^2 df$ — total energy is the area under the energy spectral density.
- **Time–Bandwidth Reciprocity:** Narrow pulses in time ⇒ wide spectrum in frequency. Wide pulses in time ⇒ narrow spectrum in frequency.
- **Energy signals** have finite $E$, zero $P$. **Power signals** have finite $P$, infinite $E$.
- The **fundamental frequency** is $f_0 = 1/T_0$. All other frequencies in a periodic signal are integer multiples (harmonics) of $f_0$.
- Always check **symmetry** of $x(t)$: even signals have only cosine terms, odd signals have only sine terms in the trigonometric Fourier Series.
- For exam problems, remember to include the **DC term** $C_0$ separately in Parseval's sum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Relations in AM Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Relations in AM Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Amplitude Modulation (AM), the transmitted signal $s(t)$ is a product of a high-frequency **carrier** and a low-frequency **message/modulating** signal, scaled by a factor called the **modulation index** $m$. Because the carrier amplitude is varied sinusoidally, the *instantaneous* envelope expands and contracts — but the *average* transmitted power is distributed among **three** distinct spectral components: the **carrier component** ($f_c$), the **upper sideband** ($f_c + f_m$), and the **lower sideband** ($f_c - f_m$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (High-Yield):** *Power relations in an AM wave describe how the total average transmitted power $P_t$ is partitioned between the carrier ($P_c$) and the two sidebands ($P_{USB}, P_{LSB}$), and quantify the proportion of useful (information-carrying) power relative to the total radiated power — encapsulated by the figure of merit called **power efficiency** $\eta$.*

The standard AM time-domain expression is:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where the parameters are strictly defined as:

- $A_c$ — Peak amplitude of the carrier (volts)
- $m$ — Modulation index (dimensionless), with $0 \le m \le 1$ for distortion-free AM
- $f_c$ — Carrier frequency (Hz)
- $f_m$ — Message (modulating) frequency (Hz)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery"

Imagine a pizza shop that delivers one large pizza (the **carrier**) to every customer. The chef always adds toppings (the **sidebands**) that carry the actual flavor (the **information**). The crust (carrier) costs the most in terms of dough and oven energy, but customers only taste the toppings. If the chef increases the topping quantity (higher $m$), the flavor improves — but the crust still dominates the bill. The **power efficiency** of the shop is essentially *what fraction of the bill is spent on actual flavor versus base dough*. For a fully topped pizza ($m = 1$), only about **33%** of the cost goes to the meaningful toppings — the rest is plain crust.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard reference impedance:** $R = 1\,\Omega$ (unless otherwise stated in problems).
> - **Maximum theoretical power efficiency** of conventional AM: $\eta_{max} = 33.33\%$ at $m = 1$.
> - **Transmitted power grows with modulation index**, while the *useful* (information-carrying) sideband power grows faster than the carrier power as $m$ increases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Spectral Decomposition — The Core Idea

Expanding the AM expression using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{m A_c}{2} \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{m A_c}{2} \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

This decomposition reveals **three distinct sinusoidal components**, each carrying its own average power:

- **Carrier term** $\rightarrow$ Amplitude $A_c$, frequency $f_c$
- **LSB term** $\rightarrow$ Amplitude $\dfrac{m A_c}{2}$, frequency $(f_c - f_m)$
- **USB term** $\rightarrow$ Amplitude $\dfrac{m A_c}{2}$, frequency $(f_c + f_m)$

For any sinusoid $A \cos(\omega t)$ across a load $R$, the average power is $A^2 / (2R)$. Therefore each component contributes independently — *no cross-terms exist between orthogonal frequencies*.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of the Power Relations

1. **Carrier is always transmitted**, regardless of whether modulation exists or not.
2. **Sidebands appear only when modulation occurs**, and their amplitude scales linearly with $m$.
3. **Sideband *power* scales with $m^2$** (since power $\propto$ amplitude$^2$).
4. **Total transmitted power** is the sum of all three independent components.
5. **Useful power = sideband power only** — the carrier carries no information.
6. **Efficiency** is the ratio of useful power to total radiated power.
7. **Efficiency is maximized** at $m = 1$, giving exactly **1/3 (≈33.33%)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory** high-yield reference for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions. Note that absolute-value notation uses `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Carrier Power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}$ | Watts | Always present |
| 2 | USB Power | $P_{USB} = \dfrac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R}$ | Watts | $\propto m^2$ |
| 3 | LSB Power | $P_{LSB} = \dfrac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R}$ | Watts | $\propto m^2$ |
| 4 | Total Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = \dfrac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{4R} = \dfrac{m^2}{2} P_c$ | Watts | $0 \le P_{SB} \le P_c/2$ |
| 5 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{m^2}{2}\right)$ | Watts | $P_c \le P_t \le 1.5 P_c$ |
| 6 | Power Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$ | Dimensionless (or %) | $\eta_{max} = 1/3$ at $m = 1$ |
| 7 | Power Saved (DSB-SC) | $P_{saved} = P_c$ | Watts | $100\%$ of carrier eliminated |
| 8 | Sideband Ratio | $\dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_c} = \dfrac{m^2}{2}$ | Dimensionless | $\le 0.5$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Broadcast AM radio (535 kHz – 1605 kHz):** Engineers use these relations to size transmitter power tubes and antenna feeders. A 50 kW AM transmitter at $m = 0.707$ (70.7%) actually delivers only 12.5 kW of *useful* sideband power — explaining why AM is power-inefficient.
- **Motivation for SSB/DSB-SC:** The $P_c$ component carries *no* information, so suppressing it (via Balanced Modulator / SSB filter) **doubles** the efficiency ceiling and saves up to **100%** of $P_c$.
- **Heatsink and PA design:** Total power $P_t$ determines thermal load, while $P_{SB}$ determines the actual SNR at the receiver — these are different design budgets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Carrier Power $P_c$

The unmodulated carrier component of the AM signal is the lone term at frequency $f_c$:

$$s_c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Average power across load $R$ is the mean-square value divided by $R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{s_c^{\,2}(t)}{R}\, dt \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{R \cdot T} \int_{0}^{T} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{R \cdot T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2}\, dt \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \underbrace{\frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R \cdot T}\!\left[\frac{\sin(4\pi f_c t)}{4\pi f_c}\right]_{0}^{T}}_{=\,0\ \text{over full periods}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_c &= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The $\cos^2$ identity splits the signal into a DC mean ($1/2$) and a zero-mean ripple ($\cos(4\pi f_c t)/2$). Over an integer number of carrier periods, the ripple integrates to zero, leaving only the DC mean.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Sideband Power $P_{SB}$

Each sideband has amplitude $m A_c / 2$. By identical logic as above, each sideband's average power is:

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{(m A_c / 2)^2}{2R} = \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R}$$

Because the upper and lower sidebands are at *different* frequencies, they are orthogonal over the integration window. Hence their powers **add directly**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{SB} &= P_{USB} + P_{LSB} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_{SB} &= \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{4R}}
\end{aligned}$$

Expressing $P_{SB}$ in terms of $P_c$ (since $P_c = A_c^{\,2}/2R$, so $A_c^{\,2} = 2 R P_c$):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{SB} &= \frac{m^2 \cdot (2 R P_c)}{4R} = \frac{m^2 P_c}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This compact form is **very high-yield** for KTU numerical problems.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Transmitted Power $P_t$

Since carrier, LSB, and USB are mutually orthogonal, total power is the simple sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\!\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{4} + \frac{m^2}{4}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\!\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_t &= P_c\!\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sanity check (boundary cases):**
- $m = 0$ (no modulation): $P_t = P_c$ ✓ (only carrier exists)
- $m = 1$ (100% modulation): $P_t = 1.5\,P_c$ ✓ (max power case)

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of Power Efficiency $\eta$

Power efficiency is the **ratio of useful (information-bearing) power to total radiated power**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{m^2 P_c}{2}}{P_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{m^2}{2}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{m^2/2}{(2 + m^2)/2} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\eta &= \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Critical maximum-efficiency derivation:** Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $m$ and set the derivative to zero.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{dm} &= \frac{2m(2 + m^2) - m^2(2m)}{(2 + m^2)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4m + 2m^3 - 2m^3}{(2 + m^2)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4m}{(2 + m^2)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $d\eta/dm = 0$ gives $m = 0$ (minimum). Since $\eta$ is **monotonically increasing** in $m$ for $m > 0$, the maximum occurs at the largest permissible $m$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \eta \big\vert_{m = 1} = \frac{1}{2 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An AM transmitter supplies 10 kW to a 50 Ω antenna when unmodulated. With modulation index $m = 0.8$, calculate (a) carrier power, (b) total sideband power, (c) total transmitted power, (d) power efficiency.

**Solution:**
(a) $P_c = 10$ kW (unmodulated carrier power)
(b) $P_{SB} = m^2 P_c / 2 = (0.8)^2 \cdot 10 / 2 = 0.64 \cdot 5 = 3.2$ kW
(c) $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2) = 10 \cdot (1 + 0.64/2) = 10 \cdot 1.32 = 13.2$ kW
(d) $\eta = m^2/(2 + m^2) = 0.64/2.64 = 0.2424 = 24.24\%$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Power-Distribution Flow (AM Transmitter Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m cos 2 pi f sub m t] --> B[Balanced Modulator]
    C[Carrier A sub c cos 2 pi f sub c t] --> B
    B --> D[Adder Summer with Carrier Insertion]
    D --> E[Power Amplifier PA]
    E --> F[Anenna Radiated Output]

    F --> P1[P sub c Carrier Power A sub c squared over 2R]
    F --> P2[P sub USB Upper Sideband]
    F --> P3[P sub LSB Lower Sideband]

    P1 --> SUM[Sum to Total P sub t]
    P2 --> SUM
    P3 --> SUM
    SUM --> EFF[Efficiency eta equals P sub SB over P sub t]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Signal Sources]
        MSG[Message m cos wt]
        CAR[Carrier A sub c cos wc t]
    end

    subgraph GEN[AM Generation Stage]
        MUL[Multiplier Mixer]
        ADD[Adder for Carrier Reinsertion]
    end

    subgraph POW[Power Distribution Block]
        PA[Power Amplifier]
        PWR[Power Splitter to Spectrum Components]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Spectrum and Power Taps]
        PCAR[P sub c Carrier]
        PUSB[P sub USB]
        PLSB[P sub LSB]
        PTOT[P sub t Total]
        ETA[Efficiency eta]
    end

    MSG --> MUL
    CAR --> MUL
    MUL --> ADD
    CAR --> ADD
    ADD --> PA
    PA --> PWR
    PWR --> PCAR
    PWR --> PUSB
    PWR --> PLSB
    PCAR --> PTOT
    PUSB --> PTOT
    PLSB --> PTOT
    PUSB --> ETA
    PLSB --> ETA
    PTOT --> ETA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Power Distribution Pie-Chart Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT[Total Power P sub t] --> PC[P sub c always present]
    PT --> PSB[Sideband Power P sub SB grows with m squared]
    PC --> NOTE1[Carries NO information]
    PSB --> NOTE2[Carries ALL information]
    NOTE2 --> ETA1[Useful Power for SNR]
    NOTE1 --> ETA2[Wasted Power Lowers eta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation tip:** For KTU board answers, always draw the spectrum diagram *alongside* these block diagrams — three vertical impulses at $f_c - f_m$, $f_c$, $f_c + f_m$ with heights $m A_c / 2$, $A_c$, $m A_c / 2$ respectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define power efficiency of an AM wave. What is its maximum possible value?*

**Model Answer:**
Power efficiency $\eta$ of an AM wave is the ratio of the total sideband power (which carries information) to the total transmitted power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$$

It is dimensionless, expressed as a percentage. Since $\eta$ increases monotonically with $m$, the maximum occurs at $m = 1$ (100% modulation):

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is conventional AM considered power-inefficient? Suggest two methods to overcome this.*

**Model Answer:**
Conventional AM is power-inefficient because the **carrier component — which carries NO information — consumes the majority of the transmitted power**. At best, only **33.33%** of total power is useful; the remaining **66.67%** is wasted in the carrier.

Two methods to overcome this:
1. **DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier):** The carrier is removed using a balanced modulator, saving all of $P_c$. This eliminates wasted power.
2. **SSB (Single Sideband):** In addition to suppressing the carrier, only one sideband is transmitted, halving the sideband power requirement and saving both $P_c$ and half of $P_{SB}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the total power transmitted in an AM wave, and hence obtain the power efficiency. Show that the maximum efficiency is 33.33%.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An AM broadcast transmitter radiates 20 kW of carrier power. The modulation index is 0.6. Determine:*
- *(i) Total sideband power* 
- *(ii) Total transmitted power* 
- *(iii) Power efficiency*
- *(iv) Peak amplitude of carrier if $R = 50\,\Omega$.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

Starting from the AM expression:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos[(\omega_c - \omega_m)t] + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos[(\omega_c + \omega_m)t]$$

The three components are mutually orthogonal. Computing the average power of each across load $R$:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}, \quad P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R}$$

Total power:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} \\[3pt]
&= \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} + \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} \\[3pt]
&= P_c\!\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^2/2}{1 + m^2/2} = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2}$$

Differentiating $\eta$ w.r.t. $m$ and analyzing monotonic behavior gives the maximum at $m = 1$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $P_c = 20$ kW, $m = 0.6$, $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i)** Total sideband power:

$$P_{SB} = \frac{m^2 P_c}{2} = \frac{(0.6)^2 \cdot 20}{2} = \frac{0.36 \cdot 20}{2} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii)** Total transmitted power:

$$P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \frac{m^2}{2}\right) = 20 \cdot (1 + 0.18) = 23.6 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii)** Power efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{m^2}{2 + m^2} = \frac{0.36}{2.36} = 0.1525 = 15.25\%$$

**(iv)** Peak carrier amplitude:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} \Rightarrow A_c = \sqrt{2 P_c R} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 20000 \cdot 50} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000} \approx 1414.2 \text{ V}$$

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]

**(a)** *Explain the significance of modulation index $m$ in AM. With a neat spectrum diagram, derive the carrier and sideband powers of an AM signal. Show that the carrier component contributes nothing to the message.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For an AM system with $A_c = 100$ V, $R = 50\,\Omega$, and $m = 1$, calculate:*
- *(i) Carrier power*
- *(ii) Each sideband power*
- *(iii) Total sideband power*
- *(iv) Total radiated power*
- *(v) Percentage of total power saved if DSB-SC is used.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The modulation index $m$ is defined as the ratio of peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier:

$$m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}, \quad 0 \le m \le 1$$

**Significance of $m$:**
- Controls depth of amplitude variation on the carrier envelope.
- At $m = 0$: no modulation (pure carrier).
- At $m = 1$: 100% modulation (max undistorted AM).
- At $m > 1$: **overmodulation** — envelope clipping causes distortion.

The AM signal $s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ expands to:

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos[(\omega_c - \omega_m)t] + \frac{m A_c}{2}\cos[(\omega_c + \omega_m)t]$$

**Spectrum diagram description:** Three spectral lines — carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$; LSB at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $m A_c/2$; USB at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $m A_c/2$.

Computing each component's average power across $R$:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}, \quad P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R}$$

The carrier $A_c\cos(\omega_c t)$ contains no trace of the message $m\cos(\omega_m t)$ — only its amplitude is *varied*. The actual information resides entirely in the sidebands, whose amplitudes are scaled by $m$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $A_c = 100$ V, $R = 50\,\Omega$, $m = 1$.

**(i)** Carrier power:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} = \frac{100^2}{2 \cdot 50} = \frac{10000}{100} = 100 \text{ W}$$

**(ii)** Each sideband power:

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{m^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8R} = \frac{1 \cdot 10000}{8 \cdot 50} = \frac{10000}{400} = 25 \text{ W}$$

**(iii)** Total sideband power:

$$P_{SB} = P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ W}$$

**Verification using $P_{SB} = m^2 P_c/2$:** $P_{SB} = 1 \cdot 100 / 2 = 50$ W ✓

**(iv)** Total radiated power:

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = 100 + 50 = 150 \text{ W}$$

**Verification using $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2)$:** $P_t = 100 \cdot (1 + 0.5) = 150$ W ✓

**(v)** Power saved with DSB-SC:
In DSB-SC, the carrier is suppressed completely. Saved power = $P_c = 100$ W.
Percentage saved:

$$\% \text{ saved} = \frac{P_c}{P_t} \times 100 = \frac{100}{150} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

This precisely demonstrates the famous AM inefficiency: at $m = 1$, **two-thirds** of total power is wasted in the carrier.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in sideband power:** Many students write $P_{SB} = m^2 A_c^{\,2}/8R$ — this is **per sideband**, not total. Always sum both.
> 2. **Mixing up which signal carries information:** The carrier carries *no* information. All information lives in the sidebands. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if students claim the carrier carries the message.
> 3. **Using wrong load impedance:** Default is $R = 1\,\Omega$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Always re-read carefully.
> 4. **Computing $\eta$ at $m=1$ as $50\%$:** It is **33.33%**, not 50%. Many students confuse $m^2/2$ (sideband/carrier ratio) with $\eta$.
> 5. **Skipping the orthogonal-frequency argument:** When summing powers, you must state that carrier, USB, and LSB are mutually orthogonal — otherwise cross-terms may be (incorrectly) added.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **AM signal:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$
- ✅ **Three spectral components:** Carrier ($f_c$), LSB ($f_c - f_m$), USB ($f_c + f_m$)
- ✅ **Carrier power:** $P_c = A_c^{\,2}/(2R)$ — independent of $m$
- ✅ **Sideband power (each):** $m^2 A_c^{\,2}/(8R)$
- ✅ **Total sideband power:** $P_{SB} = m^2 P_c / 2$
- ✅ **Total transmitted power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + m^2/2)$
- ✅ **Power efficiency:** $\eta = m^2/(2 + m^2)$
- ✅ **Max efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = 33.33\%$ at $m = 1$
- ✅ **At $m = 1$:** $P_t = 1.5\,P_c$, $P_{SB} = P_c/2$
- ✅ **DSB-SC saves:** Up to $P_c$ (entire carrier power)
- ✅ **Orthogonality** of carrier, USB, and LSB justifies direct power addition.
- ✅ **Carrier carries no information** — only sidebands do.
- ✅ **Power efficiency is monotonically increasing** in $m$ for $m > 0$.
- ✅ **Standard reference load:** $R = 1\,\Omega$ unless specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AM Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of AM Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amplitude Modulation (AM) Generation** is the process of systematically varying the instantaneous amplitude of a high-frequency **carrier signal** in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of a low-frequency **message (modulating) signal**, while keeping the carrier frequency and phase strictly constant.

Mathematically, the AM signal is expressed as:

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a \, m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Amplitude of the carrier signal
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (typically in RF range, 540 kHz – 1600 kHz for broadcast AM)
- $m(t)$ = Message/modulating signal
- $k_a$ = Amplitude sensitivity of the modulator (V⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The university expects students to master the mathematical modeling of an AM wave, understand modulation index, and derive the output of standard AM generation circuits such as the **Square Law Modulator**, **Switching Modulator**, **Balanced Modulator**, and **Collector (High-Level) Modulator**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boat and the Ocean"

Imagine you are sitting inside a small **wooden boat (carrier)** floating on a vast **ocean (message signal)**. When the ocean waves are calm, the boat just bobs gently at a fixed rhythm. When a **storm (loud audio signal)** arrives, the boat rides higher crests and dips into lower troughs — but the *speed* at which it bobs (frequency) remains exactly the same.

The **height of the boat's rise and fall** = Amplitude of the AM signal.
The **rhythm of bobbing** = Carrier frequency (unchanged).
The **ocean's wave pattern** = Modulating audio/music signal.

This is exactly how AM works — the **information rides on the amplitude**, while the carrier frequency stays untouched.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of Light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Standard AM Broadcast Band:** **535 kHz to 1605 kHz** (carrier frequencies)
- **Standard IF for AM Receivers:** **455 kHz**
- **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency (without distortion):** **33.33%** (when $\mu = 1$)
- **Standard Channel Spacing:** **10 kHz** in MW broadcast

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is efficiency capped at 33.33%?** Because in conventional AM, the carrier component — which carries **no information** — consumes **two-thirds** of the total transmitted power at $\mu=1$. This is a classic KTU 2-mark favourite.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of the AM Wave

The AM signal can be expanded into **three distinct frequency components**:

1. **Carrier Component** — Contains no information, exists at $f_c$.
2. **Upper Sideband (USB)** — Contains information, exists at $f_c + f_m$.
3. **Lower Sideband (LSB)** — Mirror image of USB, exists at $f_c - f_m$.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1:** The modulating signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a constant $k_a$ to produce the **modulation envelope**.
- **Step 2:** A DC offset of **1** is added so that the envelope is always **non-negative** (no over-modulation allowed in standard AM).
- **Step 3:** The result multiplies the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — a process called **product modulation**.
- **Step 4:** The carrier frequency is chosen **at least 10× the highest message frequency** to prevent sideband overlap.

### 2.2 Modulation Index ($\mu$) — The Heart of AM

$$
\mu = k_a \cdot A_m = \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $\mu < 1$ → **Under-modulation** (Clean envelope, no distortion)
> - $\mu = 1$ → **Critical modulation** (100% modulation, envelope just touches zero)
> - $\mu > 1$ → **Over-modulation** (Envelope distortion, generates harmonics — strictly avoided in broadcast)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| AM Signal | $s(t) = A_c[1+\mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Time-domain equation |
| Carrier Power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Watts | Power in carrier alone |
| Total Power | $P_t = P_c\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | Watts | Carrier + both sidebands |
| Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$ | Watts | Total power in USB + LSB |
| Modulation Index | $\mu = \dfrac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}$ | Dimensionless | From envelope waveform |
| Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Useful power ratio |
| Bandwidth | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Hz | For single-tone AM |
| USB Frequency | $f_{USB} = f_c + f_m$ | Hz | Upper Sideband |
| LSB Frequency | $f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m$ | Hz | Lower Sideband |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** In all power calculations, the resistance is assumed to be **1 Ω** unless specified. Always state this assumption in the exam to earn the assumption mark.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of AM Generation

- **Commercial MW/SW Broadcasting:** Conventional AM is the workhorse of long-distance voice broadcasting (All India Radio uses AM).
- **Aircraft Communication in VHF Band:** AM is preferred for voice clarity in aviation (118–136 MHz).
- **Subcarrier Telegraphy:** AM is used to multiplex multiple telegraph channels onto one RF carrier.
- **Cost-effective** for one-to-many broadcasting because receivers are extremely cheap (just a diode detector).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectrum of Single-Tone AM Signal

**Given:** 
- Modulating signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$
- Carrier signal: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

**Step 1:** Substitute $m(t)$ into the standard AM equation.

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $k_a A_m = \mu$ (modulation index).

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the bracket by distributive multiplication.

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$
\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]
$$

Applying this with $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2} \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Identify the three spectral components.

| **Component** | **Frequency** | **Amplitude** |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | $f_c$ | $A_c$ |
| LSB (Lower Sideband) | $f_c - f_m$ | $\mu A_c / 2$ |
| USB (Upper Sideband) | $f_c + f_m$ | $\mu A_c / 2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth of AM:** $BW = f_{USB} - f_{LSB} = 2f_m$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Relations in an AM Wave

**Assumption:** Signal is developed across a load resistance $R$ (in exam, $R = 1\,\Omega$ by default).

**Step 1:** Instantaneous voltage $s(t)$ is squared and divided by $2R$ to get average power.

**Step 2:** Using orthogonality property, cross-product terms average to zero over one period.

**Step 3:** Average power of each cosine squared term is $\frac{1}{2}$ of its amplitude squared.

**Carrier Power:**

$$
P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}
$$

**Sideband Power (each sideband):**

$$
P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{1}{2R}\left(\frac{\mu A_c}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R}
$$

**Total Sideband Power:**

$$
P_{SB} = 2 \times \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}
$$

**Total Transmitted Power:**

$$
P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Efficiency of Transmission:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2/4}{1 + \mu^2/2} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}
$$

**Maximum efficiency at $\mu = 1$:**

$$
\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2+1} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Square Law Modulator (Low-Level AM Generation)

The square law modulator exploits the **non-linear V-I characteristic** of a diode or transistor.

**Non-linear device equation** (Taylor expansion up to second order):

$$
i(t) = a_0 + a_1 v_i(t) + a_2 v_i^2(t)
$$

**Input voltage** (message + carrier applied simultaneously):

$$
v_i(t) = m(t) + c(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) + A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 1:** Square the input voltage.

$$
v_i^2(t) = A_m^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_m t) + A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + 2A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ and product-to-sum rule.

$$
v_i^2(t) = \frac{A_m^2}{2} + \frac{A_m^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f_m t) + \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{A_c^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f_c t) + A_m A_c \cos\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right] + A_m A_c \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the device equation. After bandpass filtering at $f_c$, only the components near $f_c$ survive:

$$
i_{out}(t) = a_1 A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + a_2 A_m A_c \cos\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right] + a_2 A_m A_c \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]
$$

**Step 4:** The term $2a_2 A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the **desired AM product** that emerges from the squaring action.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The square law modulator generates many unwanted frequencies. A **bandpass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2f_m$ is essential to extract clean AM.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Switching Modulator

The switching modulator uses a **diode switch** driven by a large carrier signal. When carrier is positive, diode is ON; when negative, diode is OFF. Effectively, the modulating signal is multiplied by a square wave:

$$
s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cdot \text{sq}(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The square wave has Fourier series:

$$
\text{sq}(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(6\pi f_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(10\pi f_c t) - \dots\right]
$$

After low-pass filtering and BPF selection, we obtain the AM signal cleanly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic AM Generation System

```mermaid
graph LR
    msgA["Message Source"] -->|"m(t)"| adderA["Combining Network"]
    carA["Oscillator"] -->|"c(t) = Ac cos 2πfct"| adderA
    adderA -->|"v_in(t)"| nonlinA["Nonlinear Device Diode or Transistor"]
    nonlinA -->|"i(t) with many freq"| bpfa["Bandpass Filter Centered at fc"]
    bpfa -->|"s(t) AM Signal"| outa["AM Output to Antenna"]
    style msgA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style carA fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style nonlinA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style bpfa fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32

```

    fclA["fc - fm Lower Sideband"] --- fclN["Component 2 LSB at fc - fm"]
