The integrator converts the message $m(t)$ into a phase-proportional signal $\phi(t)$, and the oscillator (modeled as a VCO) emits a sinusoid whose frequency is offset by $k_f$ times the input to its frequency-control port.

---

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Spectral Representation (DSB-SC vs FM)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DSB_SC [DSB SC Spectrum from Balanced Modulator]
        DSBLSB[Lower Sideband at fc minus fm Amplitude A Sub m A Sub c over 2]
        DSBCAR[CARRIER SUPPRESSED Zero Amplitude]
        DSBUSB[Upper Sideband at fc plus fm Amplitude A Sub m A Sub c over 2]
    end
    subgraph FMSPEC [FM Spectrum Bessel Expansion]
        FMCAR[Carrier at fc Amplitude J0 of beta times Ac]
        FM1L[1st Lower Sideband fc minus fm J1 of beta]
        FM1U[1st Upper Sideband fc plus fm J1 of beta]
        FM2L[2nd Lower Sideband fc minus 2fm J2 of beta]
        FM2U[2nd Upper Sideband fc plus 2fm J2 of beta]
        FMDOTS[Infinite Sidebands Jn of beta decays with n]
    end
    classDef dsbNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef fmNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    class DSBLSB,DSBCAR,DSBUSB dsbNode
    class FMCAR,FM1L,FM1U,FM2L,FM2U,FMDOTS fmNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contrast:** DSB-SC has only **two** spectral lines (no carrier), giving bandwidth $2 f_m$. FM has an **infinite** theoretical spectrum, but practically only $(\beta+1)$ sideband pairs on each side are significant, yielding $BW = 2(\beta+1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ by Carson's rule.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define a balanced modulator. Write its mathematical expression for a single-tone message signal.**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A balanced modulator is a non-linear device used to generate a **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** signal by multiplying the message and carrier, with the carrier component fully cancelled in the output.

**Mathematical expression (single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$):**

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

$$\boxed{\,s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]\,}$$

The output consists only of the upper sideband (USB) at $f_c + f_m$ and the lower sideband (LSB) at $f_c - f_m$, with **zero carrier component** at $f_c$. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the spectrum explanation.)**

---

### **Q2. Derive the mathematical expression of an FM signal for a single-tone modulating signal. Define modulation index.**

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Important]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

**Step 1:** Instantaneous frequency in FM:
$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t) = f_c + k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2:** Phase deviation by integration:
$$\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_0^{t} m(\tau) d\tau = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3:** FM signal:
$$\boxed{\,s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)\,}$$

**Modulation Index:**
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**(1 mark for instantaneous frequency, 1 mark for integration step, 1 mark for the final FM equation and modulation index.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Part B question has an **internal choice** (either-or). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the operation of a balanced modulator with a neat block diagram. Derive the mathematical expression for the DSB-SC output. **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 of these notes):**

The balanced modulator consists of:
- A phase splitter (0° and 180° outputs from carrier)
- Two identical multipliers (Channels A and B)
- A summing/subtracting junction
- The message $m(t)$ feeds both multipliers with the same polarity

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Output of Channel A (non-inverting carrier):**
$$s_A(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Output of Channel B (inverting carrier):**
$$s_B(t) = m(t) \cdot [-c(t)] = -A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Net Output (subtraction of Channel B from Channel A):**
$$s(t) = s_A(t) - s_B(t) = 2 A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Applying product-to-sum identity:
$$\boxed{\,s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_m A_c \big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]\,}$$

**Key Observation (1 mark):** The output contains only USB at $f_c + f_m$ and LSB at $f_c - f_m$, with **no carrier component** at $f_c$. The bandwidth is $2 f_m$.

**Practical Examples (1 mark):** MC1496 monolithic balanced modulator IC, diode ring (double-balanced) modulator used in SSB transmitters.

#### **(b)** A 1 MHz carrier of amplitude 10 V is amplitude modulated in a balanced modulator by a 5 kHz message of amplitude 8 V. Calculate: (i) the sideband frequencies, (ii) the sideband amplitudes, and (iii) the transmission bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A_c = 10$ V, $f_c = 10^6$ Hz = 1000 kHz
- $A_m = 8$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz

**Step (i) — Sideband frequencies:**

$$\text{USB frequency} = f_c + f_m = 1000 + 5 = 1005\ \text{kHz}$$

$$\text{LSB frequency} = f_c - f_m = 1000 - 5 = 995\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step (ii) — Sideband amplitudes:**

$$\text{Each sideband amplitude} = \frac{A_m A_c}{2} = \frac{8 \times 10}{2} = 40\ \text{V}$$

**Step (iii) — Transmission bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10\ \text{kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of marks:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 1 mark, with 2 marks for the neat presentation and units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **(a)** With necessary mathematical analysis, derive the expression for an FM signal. Define frequency deviation, modulation index, and derive Carson's rule for FM bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B]`

**Model Solution:**

**FM Signal Expression (4 marks):**

For message $m(t)$ and carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))$$

The instantaneous frequency:
$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

Phase deviation by integration:
$$\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau) d\tau$$

For single-tone $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:
$$\phi(t) = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

$$\boxed{\,s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)\,}$$

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$):** The maximum departure of instantaneous frequency from the carrier frequency.
$$\Delta f = k_f A_m \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

- **Modulation Index ($\beta$):** The ratio of peak frequency deviation to the message frequency.
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Carson's Rule Derivation (1 mark):**

Theoretically, FM bandwidth is infinite. Empirically, 98% of the total power is contained within the sidebands for which the Bessel coefficient $|J_n(\beta)| \geq 0.01$. Empirically, the order of the highest significant sideband is $\beta + 1$. Hence:

$$BW = 2 \times (\text{highest sideband order}) \times f_m = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$$

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

#### **(b)** An FM broadcast transmitter has carrier frequency 100 MHz, modulating signal $m(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \times 5 \times 10^3 t)$ V, and frequency sensitivity $k_f = 10$ kHz/V. Find: (i) the frequency deviation, (ii) the modulation index, (iii) the Carson's rule bandwidth, and (iv) the number of significant sideband pairs. **(7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f_c = 100$ MHz $= 10^8$ Hz
- $A_m = 5$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz $= 5 \times 10^3$ Hz
- $k_f = 10$ kHz/V $= 10^4$ Hz/V

**Step (i) — Frequency deviation:**

$$\Delta f = k_f A_m = (10 \times 10^3)(5) = 50{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 50\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step (ii) — Modulation index:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{50}{5} = 10$$

**Step (iii) — Carson's rule bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(50 + 5) = 110\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step (iv) — Number of significant sideband pairs:**

$$N \approx \beta + 1 = 10 + 1 = 11\ \text{pairs (22 significant sidebands total)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of marks:** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> **1. Forgetting the factor of 2 in bandwidth:** Students often write $BW = \Delta f + f_m$ instead of $2(\Delta f + f_m)$. **Always include the factor of 2** in Carson's rule — this is the most common 1-mark deduction.
>
> **2. Confusing $\Delta f$ with $f_c$:** Frequency deviation $\Delta f$ is the *maximum swing* from $f_c$, not the carrier frequency itself. Writing "$f_c = 50$ kHz" when $f_c = 100$ MHz is a **silly mistake** that loses 2–3 marks.
>
> **3. Missing the carrier suppression note in BM:** A balanced modulator output **has NO carrier component**. Students who write $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in the output equation lose 1 mark.
>
> **4. Not stating units:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per question for missing units (Hz, V, kHz, etc.).
>
> **5. Integration step skipped in FM derivation:** The transition from $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ to $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)d\tau$ **must be shown explicitly** — skipping the integration is a 1-mark loss.
>
> **6. Forgetting the Bessel function symmetry $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$:** In spectrum questions, students often write wrong signs for negative-order sidebands.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this section — read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Balanced Modulator Key Points
- ⭐ Output is **DSB-SC**: contains only **USB and LSB**, **no carrier**.
- ⭐ Mathematical form: $s(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- ⭐ For single-tone $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:
  $$s(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$
- ⭐ **Bandwidth** = $2 f_m$.
- ⭐ Practical ICs: **MC1496**, diode ring (double-balanced) modulator.
- ⭐ Carrier suppression ratio ≥ **40 dB** in good designs.
- ⭐ Used in **SSB transmitters**, **I/Q modulators**, **color TV**, **SDR**.

### ✅ Frequency Modulation Key Points
- ⭐ **Equation:** $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t))$.
- ⭐ **Instantaneous frequency:** $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.
- ⭐ **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ (Hz).
- ⭐ **Modulation index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ (dimensionless).
- ⭐ **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$.
- ⭐ **Bessel expansion:** $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_n J_n(\beta) \cos(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t)$.
- ⭐ **Symmetry:** $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$.
- ⭐ **Power identity:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$.
- ⭐ **Number of significant sideband pairs:** $\approx \beta + 1$.
- ⭐ FM is **non-linear modulation**; bandwidth grows with $\beta$.

### ✅ Critical Comparison (must memorize)
| Aspect | DSB-SC (BM) | FM |
|--------|------------|-----|
| Type | Linear (Amplitude) | Non-linear (Angle) |
| Carrier suppressed? | **Yes** | No |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ |
| Noise immunity | Poor (AM family) | **Excellent** |
| Power efficiency | High (no carrier) | Lower |
| KTU reference IC | MC1496 | Varactor-diode VCO |

### ✅ Common KTU Numerical Juggler Values
- $\beta = 0.5$ → narrowband FM (NBFM)
- $\beta \geq 1$ → wideband FM (WBFM)
- $\beta = 2.4$ → $J_0 = 0$ (carrier vanishes momentarily)
- Carson's rule captures 98% power (NOT 100%)

### ✅ Units Quick-Reference
- $f_c, f_m, \Delta f, BW$ → **Hz** (use kHz/MHz where convenient)
- $A_c, A_m$ → **Volts**
- $k_f$ → **Hz/Volt**
- $\beta$ → **dimensionless** (no unit)
- Bandwidth of DSB-SC is $2 f_m$, of AM (with carrier) is $2 f_m$.

> **Best of luck for your KTU ESE examination!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Waveforms ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **waveform** is the graphical representation of a signal expressed as a function of time, depicting the variation of amplitude (voltage, current, electric field, or any physical quantity) with respect to time. In Communication Techniques (PEEBT751), waveforms form the foundational language through which information is encoded, transmitted, and decoded across communication channels. Mathematically, a waveform is described as: $$ x(t) = A \cdot f(\omega t + \phi) $$ where: - $A$ = Peak amplitude of the signal (measured in **volts** or **amperes**) - $f(\cdot)$ = Shape function (sine, square, triangular, sawtooth, etc.) - $\omega$ = Angular frequency in **radians per second (rad/s)** - $\phi$ = Phase shift in **radians (rad)** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1: Waveforms** > The syllabus explicitly covers classification of signals, standard periodic waveforms (sinusoidal, square, triangular, sawtooth), Fourier series representation of periodic signals, and evaluation of power and RMS values. Mastery of these primitives is mandatory for all subsequent modules on modulation, sampling, and information theory. ### 1.2 Classification of Waveforms (Signals) A complete taxonomy as required by KTU 2024 scheme: | Classification Basis | Type 1 | Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **Repetition** | Periodic | Aperiodic (Non-periodic) | | **Continuity** | Continuous-time (CT) | Discrete-time (DT) | | **Determinism** | Deterministic | Random (Stochastic) | | **Energy/Power** | Energy signal | Power signal | | **Symmetry** | Even Signal | Odd Signal | | **Dimensionality** | One-dimensional (1-D) | Multi-dimensional (M-D) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Ocean Wave vs. The Heartbeat** > Imagine standing on a beach and watching ocean waves. Each wave rises and falls in a **predictable, repeating pattern** (periodic) — this is analogous to a **sinusoidal carrier wave** in communications. Now imagine a single pebble dropped in a calm pond: the ripple spreads once and never repeats — this is an **aperiodic (transient) signal**. In real communication systems (like a microphone capturing speech), signals are often a complex blend: periodic carriers modulated by aperiodic information-bearing signals. Understanding this distinction allows engineers to apply the right mathematical tool (Fourier Series for periodic, Fourier Transform for aperiodic). ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The fundamental physical constant governing all waveforms is the speed of light: $$ c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$ Other essential metrics highlighted in **bold** for KTU recall: - **Fundamental Period $T_0$**: smallest positive $T$ such that $x(t) = x(t + T)$ for all $t$ - **Fundamental Frequency $f_0 = 1/T_0$** measured in **Hertz (Hz)** - **Angular Frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$** measured in **rad/s** - **RMS Value $x_{rms}$**: equivalent DC value producing the same power dissipation - **Average Power $P_{avg}$** measured in **Watts (W)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals — Operational Logic A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if it satisfies: $$ x(t) = x(t + T_0) \quad \forall \, t \in \mathbb{R} $$ **Why this matters in Communication:** - Periodic signals carry **no new information** by themselves — they are carriers. - Information is impressed upon a periodic carrier via **modulation**. - Aperiodic signals (like human speech, image pixels) contain the **actual information** content. ### 2.2 Energy and Power Signal Classification **Total Energy** of a signal $x(t)$ over interval $[-T/2, T/2]$: $$ E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt $$ **Average Power**: $$ P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt $$ **Classification Rule:** - If $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$ → **Energy Signal** (e.g., single rectangular pulse) - If $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$ → **Power Signal** (e.g., sinusoid, square wave) ### 2.3 Even and Odd Symmetry — Critical for Fourier Series Simplification $$ x(-t) = x(t) \implies \text{Even Signal (e.g., }\cos(\omega t)\text{)} $$ $$ x(-t) = -x(t) \implies \text{Odd Signal (e.g., }\sin(\omega t)\text{)} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Trick:** Always check for even/odd/half-wave symmetry BEFORE computing Fourier coefficients — it can reduce your calculation time by **50% or more** in board exams. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Periodic condition | $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ | $T_0 > 0$ | | 2 | Fundamental frequency | $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | Hertz (Hz) | | 3 | Angular frequency | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | rad/s | | 4 | Trigonometric Fourier Series | $x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$ | General periodic | | 5 | DC component | $a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t)\, dt$ | Average value | | 6 | Cosine coefficient | $a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | $n \geq 1$ | | 7 | Sine coefficient | $b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | $n \geq 1$ | | 8 | Exponential Fourier Series | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{jn\omega_0 t}$ | Complex form | | 9 | Complex coefficient | $C_n = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | 10 | RMS value | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x^2(t)\, dt}$ | Volts / Amperes | | 11 | Average power | $P = x_{rms}^2 = a_0^2 + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n^2 + b_n^2\right)$ | Watts (Parseval) | | 12 | Even signal rule | All $b_n = 0$ | Symmetry shortcut | | 13 | Odd signal rule | $a_0 = 0$ and all $a_n = 0$ | Symmetry shortcut | | 14 | Half-wave symmetry | $x(t \pm T_0/2) = -x(t) \implies a_n = b_n = 0$ for even $n$ | Useful for square wave | | 15 | Quarter-wave symmetry | Combines even + half-wave → only odd $a_n$ survive (cosine terms only) | For sawtooth/triangular | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No Vertical Pipes in Tables:** All absolute value expressions use $\vert \cdot \vert$ syntax **outside** of markdown table cells to preserve table integrity. The `\vert` character must not collide with the column delimiter. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Waveform analysis is indispensable in: - **Telecommunications:** Designing AM/FM transmitters (sinusoidal carriers) - **Power Systems:** Computing RMS voltage for AC power transmission (50/60 Hz sinusoidal) - **Audio Engineering:** Synthesizing musical notes from Fourier harmonics - **Radar Systems:** Pulse waveform generation for time-of-flight ranging - **Digital Electronics:** Square wave clock signals (rise/fall time analysis) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Trigonometric Fourier Series of a Square Wave **Problem Setup:** Derive the Fourier series of an **even square wave** with amplitude $A$, period $T_0$, and 50% duty cycle (alternating $+A$ and $-A$). **Step 1 — State the Periodic Function** $$ x(t) = \begin{cases} +A, & 0 < t < T_0/2 \\ -A, & T_0/2 < t < T_0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with period } T_0 $$ **Step 2 — Apply Even Symmetry Test** Substitute $t \to -t$: $$ x(-t) = \begin{cases} +A, & -T_0/2 < t < 0 \\ -A, & -T_0 < t < -T_0/2 \end{cases} $$ This is identical to $x(t)$ reflected about the vertical axis. Therefore: $$ x(-t) = x(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Signal is EVEN} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Even-Symmetry Simplification** For an even signal, all sine coefficients vanish: $$ b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the DC Component $a_0$** $$ a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t)\, dt $$ Split into two intervals (using even symmetry, multiply by 2): $$ a_0 = \frac{2}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0/2} (A)\, dt = \frac{2A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{2} = A $$ **Step 5 — Compute Cosine Coefficient $a_n$** $$ a_n = \frac{2}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t) \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ Apply even symmetry ($x(t)$ even, $\cos$ even, product even): $$ a_n = \frac{4}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0/2} A \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ Evaluate the integral: $$ a_n = \frac{4A}{T_0} \left[ \frac{\sin(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T_0/2} $$ Substitute $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$, so $n\omega_0 \cdot T_0/2 = n\pi$: $$ a_n = \frac{4A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\pi)}{n\omega_0} = \frac{4A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\pi)}{n \cdot (2\pi/T_0)} $$ Simplify: $$ a_n = \frac{2A}{n\pi} \sin(n\pi) $$ **Step 6 — Apply the Key Identity $\sin(n\pi) = 0$ for integer $n$** For every integer $n \geq 1$: $$ \sin(n\pi) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a_n = 0 $$ **Wait — Correcting the Sign Error:** The above gives zero. The classic square wave has NO cosine terms. Re-check by shifting the function to a $\pm A$ symmetric version around $t=0$: Refined definition (centered at origin, period $T_0$): $$ x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & -T_0/4 < t < T_0/4 \\ -A, & T_0/4 < t < 3T_0/4 \end{cases} $$ Now $\int_{-T_0/4}^{T_0/4} A\, dt - \int_{T_0/4}^{3T_0/4} A\, dt = 0$, so $a_0 = 0$. Recompute $a_n$: $$ a_n = \frac{4A}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0/4} \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt - \frac{4A}{T_0} \int_{T_0/4}^{T_0/2} \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ For the second integral, use the fact that over a full half-period the cosine average is zero when $n$ is odd. Detailed result: $$ a_n = \frac{4A}{n\pi} \sin\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) $$ **Step 7 — Evaluate $\sin(n\pi/2)$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \ldots$** $$ \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1, \quad \sin(\pi) = 0, \quad \sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = -1, \quad \sin(2\pi) = 0 $$ Therefore: $$ a_n = \begin{cases} \frac{4A}{n\pi}, & n = 1, 5, 9, \ldots \text{ (}n = 4k+1\text{)} \\ -\frac{4A}{n\pi}, & n = 3, 7, 11, \ldots \text{ (}n = 4k+3\text{)} \\ 0, & n = \text{even} \end{cases} $$ **Step 8 — Assemble the Final Fourier Series** $$ x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \left[ \cos(\omega_0 t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_0 t) - \frac{1}{7}\cos(7\omega_0 t) + \cdots \right] $$ $$ \boxed{\,x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1} \cos\left((2k+1)\omega_0 t\right)\,} $$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Exponential Fourier Series of a Rectangular Pulse Train **Step 1 — Define the Signal** (pulse of width $\tau$, period $T_0$, amplitude $A$) $$ x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & -\tau/2 < t < \tau/2 \\ 0, & \tau/2 < t < T_0 - \tau/2 \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_n$** $$ C_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t) e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral** $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_0} \left[ \frac{e^{-jn\omega_0 t}}{-jn\omega_0} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} = \frac{A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{e^{-jn\omega_0 \tau/2} - e^{jn\omega_0 \tau/2}}{-jn\omega_0} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Sine Form** Use $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta)$: $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{-2j\sin(n\omega_0 \tau/2)}{-jn\omega_0} = \frac{2A}{T_0 \omega_0} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\omega_0 \tau/2)}{n} $$ Substitute $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$: $$ C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_0} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\pi\tau/T_0)}{n\pi\tau/T_0} $$ **Step 5 — Recognize the Sinc Function** $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ $$ \boxed{\,C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_0}\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right)\,} $$ **Step 6 — Final Series Form** $$ x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{jn\omega_0 t} = \frac{A\tau}{T_0} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right) e^{jn\omega_0 t} $$ ### 3.3 RMS Value of a Sinusoid — Quick Derivation For $x(t) = A_m \sin(\omega_0 t)$: $$ x_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} A_m^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ Apply identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ x_{rms}^2 = \frac{A_m^2}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2}\, dt = \frac{A_m^2}{2T_0} \left[ t - \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t)}{2\omega_0} \right]_0^{T_0} $$ Since $\sin(2\omega_0 T_0) = \sin(4\pi) = 0$: $$ x_{rms}^2 = \frac{A_m^2}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,x_{rms} = \frac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 A_m\,} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Waveform Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Signal x t] --> B{Is x t = x t + T0?} B -- Yes --> C[Periodic Signal] B -- No --> D[Aperiodic Signal] C --> E{Is Energy finite?} E -- Yes --> F[Power Signal] E -- No --> G[Check Symmetry] D --> H{Is Energy finite?} H -- Yes --> I[Energy Signal] H -- No --> J[Neither] G --> K{x -t = x t?} K -- Yes --> L[Even Signal b n = 0] K -- No --> M{x -t = -x t?} M -- Yes --> N[Odd Signal a n = 0] M -- No --> O[No Symmetry] L --> P[Apply Fourier Series] N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Compute a0, an, bn] Q --> R[Reconstruct x t Harmonic Sum] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Waveform Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Signal Source
Microphone / Function Generator] --> COND[Conditioning
Amplify and Filter] COND --> ADC[Analog to Digital
Conversion] ADC --> ANA[Waveform Analysis
Module] ANA --> SYM[Symmetry Detection
Even / Odd / Half Wave] SYM --> FS[Fourier Series
Computation] FS --> COEF[Coefficient Bank
a0 an bn Cn] COEF --> SPEC[Frequency Spectrum
Display] COEF --> POW[Power and RMS
Calculator] SPEC --> OUT1[Modulation Index] POW --> OUT2[Power Spectral Density] ``` ### 4.3 Harmonic Spectrum of a Square Wave ```mermaid flowchart TD H1[Fundamental f0
Amplitude 4A by pi] --> H3[Third Harmonic 3f0
Amplitude 4A by 3pi] H3 --> H5[Fifth Harmonic 5f0
Amplitude 4A by 5pi] H5 --> H7[Seventh Harmonic 7f0
Amplitude 4A by 7pi] H7 --> HN[Higher Odd Harmonics
Roll off as 1 over n] HN --> FILT[Low Pass Filter
Truncate Series] FILT --> OUT[Reconstructed
Quasi Square Waveform] ``` ### 4.4 Symmetry Reduction Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Given Periodic x t] --> Q1{Half wave symmetry
x t + T0 by 2 = -x t?} Q1 -- Yes --> H1[All even n coefficients vanish
a0 = 0] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Even symmetry
x -t = x t?} Q2 -- Yes --> H2[All bn vanish
Only an terms] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Odd symmetry
x -t = -x t?} Q3 -- Yes --> H3[a0 = 0 and all an vanish
Only bn terms] Q3 -- No --> H4[No symmetry shortcut
Full computation needed] H1 --> COMPUTE[Compute Reduced Series] H2 --> COMPUTE H3 --> COMPUTE H4 --> COMPUTE ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define a periodic signal. A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t) + 3\sin(50\pi t)$ is given. Determine whether it is periodic, and if so, find the fundamental period.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if there exists a positive $T$ such that $x(t) = x(t + T)$ for all $t$. For the given signal: - First term: $\omega_1 = 100\pi \implies T_1 = 2\pi/\omega_1 = 1/50$ s - Second term: $\omega_2 = 50\pi \implies T_2 = 2\pi/\omega_2 = 1/25$ s Ratio: $T_1/T_2 = 1/2$, which is **rational**. Fundamental period: $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = 1/25$ s = **40 ms**. --- **Q2. State and prove Parseval's theorem for power signals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** The total average power of a periodic signal equals the sum of the squared magnitudes of its Fourier series coefficients. $$ P = a_0^2 + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n^2 + b_n^2\right) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_n \vert^2 $$ **Proof sketch:** Substituting the Fourier series expansion of $x(t)$ into the average power integral $P = (1/T_0)\int_{T_0} x^2(t)\, dt$ and using the orthogonality of sinusoids $\int_{T_0}\cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t)\,dt = 0$ for $n \neq m$ (and similarly for sine products), all cross-terms vanish, leaving only the squared magnitudes. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 1]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1** **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the classification of signals based on (i) periodicity, (ii) energy and power, and (iii) symmetry. Give one example for each category. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Diagram:** | Basis | Type A | Type B | |---|---|---| | (i) Periodicity | Periodic: $x(t) = x(t+T_0)$ e.g., $\sin(t)$ | Aperiodic: e.g., $e^{-t}u(t)$ | | (ii) Energy/Power | Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty, P = 0$ e.g., rectangular pulse | Power signal: $0 < P < \infty, E = \infty$ e.g., sinusoid | | (iii) Symmetry | Even: $x(-t) = x(t)$ e.g., $\cos(t)$ | Odd: $x(-t) = -x(t)$ e.g., $\sin(t)$ | **Energy of a Rectangular Pulse:** For pulse of amplitude $A$, width $\tau$: $$ E = \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A^2\, dt = A^2 \tau $$ Since $E$ is finite and $P = 0$, it is an **energy signal**. **Power of a Sinusoid:** For $x(t) = A_m\sin(\omega_0 t)$: $$ P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0} A_m^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 t)\, dt = \frac{A_m^2}{2} $$ Since $P$ is finite, it is a **power signal**. --- **(b)** Determine the trigonometric Fourier series of a half-wave rectified sine wave given by $x(t) = A\sin(\omega_0 t)$ for $0 < t < T_0/2$ and $x(t) = 0$ for $T_0/2 < t < T_0$. Hence find its RMS value. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — DC Component $a_0$** $$ a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} A\sin(\omega_0 t)\, dt = \frac{A}{T_0}\left[-\frac{\cos(\omega_0 t)}{\omega_0}\right]_0^{T_0/2} = \frac{A}{T_0\omega_0}[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)] = \frac{2A}{T_0\omega_0} = \frac{A}{\pi} $$ **Step 2 — Cosine Coefficient $a_n$** $$ a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} A\sin(\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ Using product-to-sum: $2\sin A\cos B = \sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)$: $$ a_n = \frac{A}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} \left[\sin((n+1)\omega_0 t) - \sin((n-1)\omega_0 t)\right] dt $$ Integrating (for $n \neq 1$): $$ a_n = \frac{A}{T_0\omega_0}\left[-\frac{\cos((n+1)\omega_0 t)}{n+1} + \frac{\cos((n-1)\omega_0 t)}{n-1}\right]_0^{T_0/2} $$ Substituting $\omega_0 T_0/2 = \pi$ and simplifying: $$ a_n = \frac{A}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{n+1} - \frac{1}{n-1}\right]\cdot[\cos((n+1)\pi) - 1] \cdot \frac{1}{2} $$ Final simplified result: $$ a_n = -\frac{2A}{\pi(4n^2 - 1)} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1 $$ For $n = 1$ (separate calculation): $$ a_1 = \frac{A}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} 2\sin(\omega_0 t)\cos(\omega_0 t)\, dt = \frac{A}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} \sin(2\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Sine Coefficient $b_n$** $$ b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} A\sin(\omega_0 t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt $$ For $n = 1$: $$ b_1 = \frac{2A}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0/2} \sin^2(\omega_0 t)\, dt = \frac{2A}{T_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{4} = \frac{A}{2} $$ For $n \neq 1$: $$ b_n = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Assemble the Series** $$ x(t) = \frac{A}{\pi} + \frac{A}{2}\sin(\omega_0 t) - \frac{2A}{\pi}\sum_{n=2,4,6,\ldots}\frac{\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n^2 - 1} $$ **Step 5 — RMS Value via Parseval's Theorem** $$ x_{rms}^2 = a_0^2 + \frac{1}{2}(a_1^2 + b_1^2) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}(a_n^2 + b_n^2) $$ $$ x_{rms}^2 = \frac{A^2}{\pi^2} + \frac{1}{2}\left(0 + \frac{A^2}{4}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{4A^2}{\pi^2(n^2-1)^2} $$ After evaluation (using standard series identity): $$ x_{rms}^2 = \frac{A^2}{\pi^2}\left(\frac{\pi^2}{4} + 1\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,x_{rms} = \frac{A}{2}\sqrt{1 + \frac{4}{\pi^2}} \approx 0.5366\,A\,} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2** **(a)** Derive the complex exponential Fourier series representation of a periodic rectangular pulse train with amplitude $A$, pulse width $\tau$, and period $T_0$. Plot its magnitude and phase spectrum. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the Signal** $$ x(t) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} A\,\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - mT_0}{\tau}\right) $$ where rect is the unit rectangle function. **Step 2 — Compute $C_n$** $$ C_n = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t) e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt $$ **Step 3 — Integration** $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_0}\left[\frac{e^{-jn\omega_0 t}}{-jn\omega_0}\right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} = \frac{A}{T_0}\cdot\frac{2\sin(n\omega_0\tau/2)}{n\omega_0} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify** $$ C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_0}\cdot\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Magnitude Spectrum** $$ \vert C_n \vert = \frac{A\tau}{T_0}\left\vert\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right)\right\vert $$ This has a maximum at $n = 0$ equal to $A\tau/T_0$ (the duty cycle), and zeros at $n = \pm T_0/\tau, \pm 2T_0/\tau, \ldots$ **Step 6 — Phase Spectrum** $$ \angle C_n = 0 \quad \text{when } \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right) \geq 0 $$ $$ \angle C_n = \pi \quad \text{when } \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right) < 0 $$ **Plot Description:** The magnitude spectrum is an **envelope** of the sinc function with samples at discrete frequencies $nf_0 = n/T_0$, decaying as $1/n$ in magnitude. --- **(b)** Find the trigonometric Fourier series of a sawtooth waveform $x(t) = t$ for $-\pi < t < \pi$ with period $2\pi$. Hence calculate the average power. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Symmetry Check** $x(-t) = -t = -x(t)$, so the function is **odd**. Therefore: $$ a_0 = 0, \quad a_n = 0 \quad \forall n $$ **Step 2 — Compute $b_n$** $$ b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} t \sin(nt)\, dt $$ Since $t\sin(nt)$ is **even** (odd × odd = even), use $2\int_0^{\pi}$: $$ b_n = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} t\sin(nt)\, dt $$ **Step 3 — Integration by Parts** Let $u = t$, $dv = \sin(nt)\,dt$. Then $du = dt$, $v = -\cos(nt)/n$. $$ \int_0^{\pi} t\sin(nt)\, dt = \left[-\frac{t\cos(nt)}{n}\right]_0^{\pi} + \frac{1}{n}\int_0^{\pi}\cos(nt)\, dt $$ $$ = -\frac{\pi\cos(n\pi)}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\left[\frac{\sin(nt)}{n}\right]_0^{\pi} = -\frac{\pi(-1)^n}{n} + 0 = \frac{(-1)^{n+1}\pi}{n} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute Back** $$ b_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{(-1)^{n+1}\pi}{n} = \frac{2(-1)^{n+1}}{n} $$ **Step 5 — Final Series** $$ \boxed{\,x(t) = 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\sin(nt) = 2\sin(t) - \sin(2t) + \frac{2}{3}\sin(3t) - \frac{1}{2}\sin(4t) + \cdots\,} $$ **Step 6 — Average Power via Parseval's Theorem** $$ P = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{4}{n^2} = 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2} = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{6} = \frac{\pi^2}{3} $$ **Verification:** Direct computation: $$ P = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} t^2\, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{2\pi^3}{3} = \frac{\pi^2}{3} \checkmark $$ ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Waveform Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply symmetry BEFORE computing integrals** — wastes 3-4 minutes and risks sign errors. Always do the symmetry test first. > 2. **Sign error in $b_n$ formula** — KTU uses the convention $b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int x(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$. Writing it as $\frac{1}{T_0}$ instead of $\frac{2}{T_0}$ is a frequent -2 mark error. > 3. **Confusing $a_0$ with $a_n$** — $a_0$ is the DC average; the $1/T_0$ factor is NOT squared. Some students write $a_0^2$ inside the integrand, leading to a halving of the DC term. > 4. **Forgetting to consider $C_0$** in the exponential form — many students skip the $n=0$ case, which carries the DC content. > 5. **Sinc normalization** — $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ is the **unnormalized** form used in signal processing. Engineering texts may use $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(x)}{x}$ (un-normalized). Read the convention from your reference; KTU generally uses un-normalized $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(x)/x$. > 6. **Phase spectrum jumps of $\pi$** — when sinc changes sign, the phase jumps by $\pi$ (NOT 0). Plotting it as continuous 0 is a -1 mark deduction. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Waveforms** - [x] **Definition of periodicity:** $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$; fundamental period is the **smallest positive $T$**. - [x] **Rational ratio test:** signal is periodic iff all frequency ratios are **rational numbers**. - [x] **Three symmetries:** Even ($b_n = 0$), Odd ($a_0 = a_n = 0$), Half-wave (only odd harmonics). - [x] **Quarter-wave symmetry:** even + half-wave → only **odd $a_n$** (cosine terms). - [x] **Energy signal:** finite $E$, $P = 0$. **Power signal:** finite $P$, $E = \infty$. - [x] **Trigonometric FS:** use $a_0, a_n, b_n$ with $2/T_0$ factor (except $a_0$ which is $1/T_0$). - [x] **Exponential FS:** use $C_n$ with $1/T_0$ factor. Relation: $C_n = (a_n - jb_n)/2$ for $n > 0$, $C_0 = a_0$, $C_{-n} = C_n^*$. - [x] **RMS of sinusoid:** $A/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707A$. - [x] **RMS of square wave (50% duty):** equals $A$ (peak value). - [x] **RMS of triangular wave:** $A/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577A$. - [x] **Parseval's Theorem:** $P = a_0^2 + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(a_n^2 + b_n^2) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\vert C_n \vert^2$. - [x] **Sinc envelope:** amplitude spectrum of pulse train has sinc shape. - [x] **Square wave spectrum:** contains **only odd harmonics** of cosine (for even-centered version) with amplitude $4A/[(2k+1)\pi]$. - [x] **Sawtooth spectrum:** contains **all harmonics** of sine with amplitude $2A/n$, alternating sign. - [x] **Triangular wave spectrum:** contains **only odd harmonics** of cosine with amplitude $8A/[\pi^2(2k+1)^2]$ (faster roll-off than square wave). - [x] **DC blocking:** if $a_0 = 0$, the signal has no DC component (e.g., symmetric square wave, sinusoid). - [x] **Units check:** always verify final answer carries correct units (V, A, W, Hz). - [x] **KTU pattern:** Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks; Part B = 1 × 14 = 14 marks; total 20 marks per module exam.

topics.title: Frequency Deviation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency Deviation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$)** is formally defined as the **maximum instantaneous departure of the instantaneous frequency of the frequency-modulated (FM) wave from the average (carrier) frequency**. It is a direct measure of how far the carrier frequency "swing" moves away from its resting (unmodulated) value, caused by the peak amplitude of the modulating baseband message signal.

Mathematically, for a single-tone modulating signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the frequency deviation is given by:

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m \quad \text{[Hertz (Hz)]}$$

where:
- $k_f$ = **frequency sensitivity (frequency deviation constant)** of the modulator, expressed in **Hz/volt**.
- $A_m$ = **peak amplitude** of the modulating signal in volts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** "Frequency deviation in an FM system is the maximum difference between the instantaneous frequency of the modulated wave and the carrier frequency, measured in Hertz, and is directly proportional to the peak amplitude of the modulating signal."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a pendulum that normally swings back and forth at a steady rhythm (this steady rhythm is your **carrier frequency**). Now, picture a child pushing the pendulum harder and harder — every push makes the pendulum swing a little faster (frequency goes up), and when the child stops pushing, it returns to its natural rhythm. The **maximum extra speed** the pendulum ever reaches, compared to its resting rhythm, is the **Frequency Deviation**.

In an FM radio station broadcasting music:
- The **carrier** is a steady RF sine wave.
- The **music** (loud or soft) pushes the carrier faster or slower.
- The **loudest peak** of the music causes the **largest push**, defining $\Delta f$.
- So $\Delta f$ is essentially **"how loud can the frequency swing get?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard FCC/International Metrics:**
> - For commercial FM broadcasting: $\Delta f_{max} = \mathbf{75 \text{ kHz}}$
> - For TV audio (analog): $\Delta f_{max} = \mathbf{25 \text{ kHz}}$
> - For narrowband two-way radio: $\Delta f_{max} = \mathbf{5 \text{ kHz}}$

### 1.3 Why is Frequency Deviation Important?

It governs three critical performance parameters of any FM system:
1. **Modulation Index ($\beta$)** — controls spectral content.
2. **Transmission Bandwidth (BW)** — directly determines channel allocation.
3. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — higher $\Delta f$ yields better noise immunity (the famous FM advantage over AM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics of Frequency Deviation

In Frequency Modulation, the **instantaneous frequency** $f_i(t)$ of the carrier is made to vary linearly with the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating signal $m(t)$:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \cdot m(t)$$

- When $m(t)$ is **positive** → $f_i(t) > f_c$ (frequency increases above carrier).
- When $m(t)$ is **negative** → $f_i(t) < f_c$ (frequency drops below carrier).
- When $m(t) = 0$ → $f_i(t) = f_c$ (no modulation).

The **maximum value** of $k_f \cdot m(t)$ is precisely the **frequency deviation**:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = k_f \cdot \left|m(t)\right|_{max}}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Modulator input**: Baseband signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ enters the FM modulator.
2. **Voltage-to-Frequency Conversion**: The Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) inside the modulator converts voltage variations to frequency variations with sensitivity $k_f$ (Hz/V).
3. **Frequency Swing**: As $m(t)$ swings from $+A_m$ to $-A_m$, the instantaneous frequency swings from $f_c + k_f A_m$ to $f_c - k_f A_m$.
4. **Peak Deviation**: The total swing is $2\Delta f$, but **deviation is defined as the one-sided maximum** = $k_f A_m$.

### 2.3 Related Parameters Derived from $\Delta f$

| Parameter | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Deviation** | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Peak swing from carrier | Hz |
| **Modulation Index (FM)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | Ratio of deviation to modulating frequency | Dimensionless |
| **Deviation Ratio (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{\Delta f_{max}}{f_{m,max}}$ | Worst-case modulation index across full message band | Dimensionless |
| **Carson's Rule Bandwidth** | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Bandwidth containing ~98% of total FM power | Hz |
| **% Modulation** | $\mu = \dfrac{\Delta f_{actual}}{\Delta f_{max}} \times 100\%$ | How "full" the deviation swing is | % |
| **Total Frequency Swing** | $2\Delta f$ | Peak-to-peak frequency excursion | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (often confused in KTU exams):**
> - **Modulation Index** $\beta$ uses the **actual** $f_m$ of the tone being transmitted.
> - **Deviation Ratio** $DR$ uses the **maximum** $f_m$ in the message band.
> - $DR$ is always $\geq \beta$ for any single tone.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast Engineering**: Determines channel spacing — FM broadcast uses 200 kHz channel BW because $\Delta f = 75$ kHz (Carson's rule: $2(75+15) = 180$ kHz $\approx 200$ kHz allocated).
- **Receiver Design**: The IF bandwidth of an FM receiver (e.g., 200 kHz) must accommodate $\Delta f$.
- **Spectrum Management**: Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU) use $\Delta f$ to assign frequency bands.
- **Noise Performance**: Higher $\Delta f$ → wider BW → better SNR (Carson & Friis formula: $(S/N)_o = (S/N)_i \cdot \beta^2 (\beta+1)$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deriving $\Delta f$ from the FM Signal Equation

The general FM signal is expressed as:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

Let the **phase argument** be denoted as $\theta_i(t)$:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

The **instantaneous frequency** in Hz is defined as:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$

Differentiating $\theta_i(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_c + 2\pi k_f \cdot m(t)$$

Substituting back into the definition of $f_i(t)$:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[2\pi f_c + 2\pi k_f \cdot m(t)\right]$$

$$\boxed{f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \cdot m(t)}$$

Since $\Delta f$ is the **maximum departure** of $f_i(t)$ from $f_c$:

$$\Delta f = \max \left\vert f_i(t) - f_c \right\vert = \max \left\vert k_f \cdot m(t) \right\vert = k_f \cdot \left\vert m(t) \right\vert_{max}$$

For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the maximum amplitude is $A_m$:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Modulation Index $\beta$ for Single-Tone FM

The FM signal for single-tone modulation is:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_0^t A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Substituting:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi k_f A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

where the **modulation index** is:

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth

The FM spectrum (using Bessel functions) contains sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$ with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$. The amplitude of the $n^{th}$ sideband is significant only when $J_n(\beta)$ is non-negligible. Empirically, the order of significant sidebands is approximately $n = \beta + 1$ (ignoring sidebands with amplitude less than ~2% of carrier).

Using this, the **highest significant sideband** is at $f_c + (\beta+1)f_m$. The total bandwidth (both upper and lower significant sidebands) is:

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

Substituting $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$:

$$BW = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) f_m$$

$$\boxed{BW_{Carson} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An FM broadcast transmitter has $f_c = 100$ MHz, modulating signal $m(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \cdot 5\text{kHz}\, t)$ volts, and modulator sensitivity $k_f = 25$ kHz/V. Determine: (a) Frequency deviation, (b) Modulation index, (c) Carson's rule bandwidth.

**Given Data:**
- $f_c = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $A_m = 10$ V
- $f_m = 5 \times 10^3$ Hz
- $k_f = 25 \times 10^3$ Hz/V

**Step (a) — Frequency Deviation:**

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = (25 \times 10^3) \times 10$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f = 250 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Step (b) — Modulation Index:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{5 \times 10^3}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = 50 \text{ (radians)}}$$

**Step (c) — Carson's Bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(250\text{ kHz} + 5\text{ kHz})$$

$$\boxed{BW = 510 \text{ kHz}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Varying Parameters

| Change in Variable | $\Delta f$ | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ | Carson's BW | SNR Performance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $A_m$ (louder audio) | ↑ Increases linearly | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Improves |
| Increase $f_m$ (higher pitch) | Unchanged | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Slightly increases | Unchanged |
| Increase $k_f$ (modulator gain) | ↑ Increases linearly | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Improves |
| Double both $A_m$ and $f_m$ | ↑ Doubles | Unchanged | ↑ Slightly increases | ↑ Improves |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Generation of Frequency Deviation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Modulating Signal m of t] -->|Amplitude A_m| B[Pre Emphasis Filter]
    B --> C[FM Modulator VCO]
    D[DC Bias Voltage] --> C
    C -->|Sensitivity k_f Hz per V| E[Frequency Deviation Delta f]
    C --> F[FM Output s of t]
    E --> G[Bessel Spectrum Sidebands]
    G --> H[Channel Bandwidth Allocation]
```

### 4.2 Frequency vs. Time Behavior (Deviation Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Unmodulated Carrier: f_i equals f_c] --> P1[Modulating signal goes positive]
    P1 --> P2[Instantaneous frequency RISES above f_c]
    P2 --> P3[Maximum peak: f_i equals f_c plus Delta f]
    P3 --> P4[Modulating signal crosses zero]
    P4 --> P5[Instantaneous frequency equals f_c]
    P5 --> P6[Modulating signal goes negative]
    P6 --> P7[Instantaneous frequency DROPS below f_c]
    P7 --> P8[Minimum peak: f_i equals f_c minus Delta f]
    P8 --> P9[Total Swing: 2 Delta f around f_c]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: From Modulation Index to Bandwidth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Delta f] --> N1[Define Modulation Index beta]
    M2[f_m] --> N1
    N1 --> O1{Beta Value}
    O1 -->|beta less than 1| P1[Narrowband FM NBFM]
    O1 -->|beta greater than 1| P2[Wideband FM WBFM]
    P1 --> Q1[Bandwidth approx 2 f_m]
    P2 --> Q2[Bandwidth approx 2 times Delta f plus f_m]
    Q1 --> R1[Carsons Rule]
    Q2 --> R1
    M1 --> R1
    M2 --> R1
```

### 4.4 Relationship Web: $\Delta f$ in the FM Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Modulating Signal m of t] --> B[Amplitude A_m]
    A --> C[Frequency f_m]
    B --> D[Frequency Deviation Delta f]
    E[Modulator Sensitivity k_f] --> D
    D --> F[Modulation Index beta]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Spectral Sideband Order]
    D --> H[Bandwidth BW]
    C --> H
    G --> H
    D --> I[SNR Improvement]
    F --> I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Q1. Define Frequency Deviation in an FM system. Mention the FCC standard value for commercial FM broadcasting.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

Frequency deviation ($\Delta f$) is the **maximum instantaneous difference between the instantaneous frequency of the FM wave and the unmodulated carrier frequency**, expressed in Hertz.

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot |m(t)|_{max}$$

The FCC standard for commercial FM broadcasting is $\Delta f_{max} = \mathbf{75 \text{ kHz}}$, with a maximum modulating frequency of 15 kHz.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for FCC value)*

---

#### **Q2. Distinguish between Modulation Index and Deviation Ratio in FM.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Modulation Index ($\beta$) | Deviation Ratio (DR) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of $\Delta f$ to actual $f_m$ of tone | Ratio of $\Delta f_{max}$ to maximum $f_m$ in band |
| Formula | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ | $DR = \Delta f_{max} / f_{m,max}$ |
| Specificity | Tone-specific | Worst-case across band |
| Value Range | Varies per tone | Fixed system parameter |

*(3 marks: 1 each for definition, formula, and distinction.)*

---

### **PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**A. (a)** Define Frequency Deviation in FM. Derive the expression for frequency deviation in terms of the modulating signal parameters and modulator sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** An FM transmitter has carrier frequency 96 MHz, modulating frequency 5 kHz, and frequency deviation 30 kHz. Determine: (i) Modulation index, (ii) Carson's rule bandwidth, (iii) Highest order significant sideband. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The FM signal is:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

The instantaneous phase is:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

By definition, instantaneous frequency:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

Since frequency deviation is the maximum departure from $f_c$:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = k_f \cdot |m(t)|_{max}}$$

For single-tone $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $|m(t)|_{max} = A_m$, hence:

$$\Delta f = k_f A_m$$

*(Breakdown: 2 marks for FM signal form, 2 marks for $f_i(t)$ derivation, 2 marks for $\Delta f$ definition, 1 mark for single-tone result.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_c = 96$ MHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $\Delta f = 30$ kHz.

**(i) Modulation Index:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{30 \text{ kHz}}{5 \text{ kHz}} = \mathbf{6}$$

**(ii) Carson's Rule Bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(30 + 5) \text{ kHz} = \mathbf{70 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Highest Order Significant Sideband:**

The number of significant sideband pairs is $n \approx \beta + 1 = 7$.

Hence, the highest order sideband is the **7th-order sideband**, located at:

$$f_{USB,\,max} = f_c + n f_m = 96{,}000 \text{ kHz} + 7 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = \mathbf{96{,}035 \text{ kHz}}$$

*(Breakdown: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including formula and sideband position.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**B. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of FM using a VCO. Define frequency deviation and modulation index. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** An audio signal of 5 V peak amplitude and 2 kHz frequency modulates an FM carrier of 50 MHz using a modulator of sensitivity 20 kHz/V. Find: (i) Frequency deviation, (ii) Modulation index, (iii) Carson's rule bandwidth, (iv) Effective percentage modulation if system max deviation is 75 kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram + Definitions (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description:**

Message signal → Pre-emphasis network → Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) → FM output + carrier input at VCO center.

**Frequency Deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$

**Modulation Index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A_m = 5$ V, $f_m = 2$ kHz, $f_c = 50$ MHz, $k_f = 20$ kHz/V, $\Delta f_{max,sys} = 75$ kHz.

**(i) Frequency Deviation:**

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 20 \times 5 = \mathbf{100 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Modulation Index:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{100}{2} = \mathbf{50}$$

**(iii) Carson's Rule Bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(100 + 2) = \mathbf{204 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iv) Percentage Modulation:**

$$\% \mu = \frac{\Delta f_{actual}}{\Delta f_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{100}{75} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \mu \approx 133.3\% \text{ (Over-modulated)}}$$

Since 133.3% > 100%, the transmitter is **over-modulated** and signal clipping/distortion will occur — a real-world regulatory violation.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks each for parts (i)-(iii), 1 mark for over-modulation interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_m$ and $f_{m,max}$**: Using the same value in both $\beta$ and $DR$ — examiners dock 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Carson's rule is for 98% power, not 100%**: Don't say "BW = $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ contains ALL power" — it does not.
> 3. **Mixing $\Delta f$ and $2\Delta f$**: Total swing is $2\Delta f$, but deviation is one-sided.
> 4. **Wrong Bessel function use**: For $\beta > 1$, do NOT use NBFM approximation $BW \approx 2f_m$. Always use Carson's rule.
> 5. **Units**: Always state Hz or kHz explicitly. Numerical answers without units get partial marks only.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Frequency Deviation** $\Delta f$ is a **one-sided** peak shift in Hz, defined as $\Delta f = k_f A_m$.
- ✅ It is **independent** of the modulating frequency $f_m$.
- ✅ **Modulation Index** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ governs whether FM is **narrowband** ($\beta < 1$) or **wideband** ($\beta > 1$).
- ✅ **Carson's Rule**: $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — used for all general FM bandwidth calculations in KTU.
- ✅ **FCC Commercial FM**: $\Delta f_{max} = 75$ kHz, $f_{m,max} = 15$ kHz, channel BW = 200 kHz.
- ✅ **% Modulation** $> 100\%$ ⇒ **over-modulation** ⇒ distortion and out-of-band emissions.
- ✅ For **multi-tone/multi-frequency** signals, use the **Deviation Ratio** $DR = \Delta f_{max} / f_{m,max}$.
- ✅ Frequency deviation directly improves **SNR** in FM (Carson–Friis law).
- ✅ The **highest significant sideband** order $n \approx \beta + 1$ (Bessel function rule of thumb).
- ✅ Always pair $\Delta f$ with units (Hz) in every line of the answer — **partial marks depend on units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandwidth Requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bandwidth Requirement ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of communication systems, **Bandwidth Requirement** refers to the minimum range of frequencies (measured in Hertz) that a communication channel must be able to pass without significant attenuation or distortion in order to faithfully transmit a given information-bearing signal. Mathematically, it is the difference between the upper and lower cutoff frequencies of the signal's spectral occupancy. $$B = f_{\text{upper}} - f_{\text{lower}} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$ In a broader KTU 2024 scheme context, bandwidth requirement is evaluated under three distinct but interrelated perspectives: 1. **Signal Bandwidth** — The frequency range occupied by the baseband information signal (e.g., voice, music, video). 2. **Transmission Bandwidth** — The frequency range consumed by the modulated (bandpass) signal at the channel input. 3. **Channel/Channel Capacity Bandwidth** — The minimum channel width required to support a given data rate without violating the **Nyquist Criterion** or the **Shannon–Hartley Theorem**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bandwidth Requirement is foundational to Module 1 because it directly bridges **signals & systems** with **modulation theory**. Every subsequent module (AM, FM, Pulse Modulation, Digital Transmission) reuses the bandwidth analysis framework established here. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-lane highway**. The **number of lanes** represents the bandwidth of a communication channel. Just as: - A **bicycle** (voice signal, ~3 kHz) needs only **one lane**. - A **car** (music signal, ~15 kHz) needs **wider lanes**. - A **truck** (video signal, ~6 MHz) needs **multiple lanes**. - A **freight convoy** (high-speed digital data) needs **express lanes with strict width and speed limits** governed by traffic laws (Nyquist & Shannon). The **bandwidth requirement** is essentially asking: *"How wide does the highway need to be so that my vehicle (signal) can travel without crashing into neighboring vehicles (adjacent channels)?"* ### 1.3 Standard Signal Bandwidths (Memorize These) | Signal Type | Approximate Bandwidth | |---|---| | Telegraphy | ~100 Hz | | Telephone (Voice) | ~3 kHz (300 Hz – 3.4 kHz) | | Hi-Fi Audio (Music) | ~15 kHz (20 Hz – 20 kHz) | | AM Radio Broadcast | 10 kHz | | FM Radio Broadcast | 200 kHz | | Standard TV (Analog) | 6 MHz | | HDTV (Digital) | ~6 MHz (compressed) | > [!NOTE] > The human ear can hear up to roughly **20 kHz**, but telephone systems allocate only **3 kHz** because intelligibility is preserved when frequencies above 3 kHz are filtered out — a classic **bandwidth-vs-fidelity trade-off** fundamental to communication engineering. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Bandwidth is the Most Precious Resource Bandwidth is the **fundamental currency** of communication engineering because: - **Regulatory bodies** (e.g., FCC, ITU, TRAI in India) allocate fixed spectral slots. - **Adjacent channels** must not overlap (otherwise, **adjacent-channel interference** occurs). - **Higher bandwidth** allows **higher data rates** (more information per second). - **Lower bandwidth** allows **more users per unit spectrum** (spectral efficiency). ### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Bandwidth Determination The determination of bandwidth requirement proceeds in **four structured steps**: - **Step 1 — Characterize the Source Signal:** Identify whether the source is analog (voice, music, video) or digital (binary data). The source's spectral content sets the *baseband bandwidth* $W$. - **Step 2 — Apply Modulation (if required):** Modulation shifts the baseband spectrum to a higher carrier frequency. Different modulation schemes expand or compress the spectrum differently: - DSB-SC: Bandwidth = $2W$ - Conventional AM: Bandwidth = $2W$ - SSB-SC: Bandwidth = $W$ - VSB: Bandwidth between $W$ and $2W$ - FM: Bandwidth depends on Carson's rule - **Step 3 — Check Channel Constraints:** Verify that the modulated signal fits within the **allocated channel bandwidth** without violating the **Nyquist sampling criterion** (for digital transmission). - **Step 4 — Validate Using Channel Capacity Theorems:** Apply the **Nyquist rate** (noiseless channel) and **Shannon's capacity** (noisy channel) to confirm whether the channel bandwidth can support the desired data rate. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $B = f_{\text{upper}} - f_{\text{lower}}$ | Basic bandwidth definition | Hz | | 2 | $B_{\text{AM}} = 2 W$ | Bandwidth of AM (DSB) signal, $W$ = message bandwidth | Hz | | 3 | $B_{\text{SSB}} = W$ | Bandwidth of Single Sideband | Hz | | 4 | $B_{\text{VSB}} = W + f_{v}$ | Bandwidth of Vestigial Sideband | Hz | | 5 | $B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + W)$ | Carson's Rule for FM bandwidth | Hz | | 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W}$ | Modulation index for FM | dimensionless | | 7 | $f_{s} \geq 2 W$ | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | Hz | | 8 | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | **Nyquist Channel Capacity** (noiseless), $M$ = signal levels | bps | | 9 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon–Hartley Capacity** (noisy) | bps | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{b}}{B}$ | **Spectral Efficiency** | bps/Hz | | 11 | $B_{\min} = \dfrac{R_{b}}{2 \log_{2}(M)}$ | Minimum bandwidth for $M$-ary signaling (Nyquist) | Hz | | 12 | $B_{\min} = \dfrac{R_{b}}{\log_{2}(1 + S/N)}$ | Minimum bandwidth from Shannon | Hz | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - The **Nyquist formula** applies to a **noiseless ideal channel**. > - The **Shannon formula** applies to a **realistic noisy channel** and gives an *absolute upper bound* — it cannot be exceeded regardless of modulation scheme. > - Many students incorrectly write $C = 2B \log_{2}(1+S/N)$ — this is **wrong** and costs marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cellular Networks (4G/5G):** Bandwidth determines the maximum number of users per cell and the achievable throughput. LTE uses bandwidths of 1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 20 MHz. - **Optical Fiber:** Single-mode fiber offers nearly 25 THz of low-loss bandwidth in the C-band, enabling Tbps data rates. - **Satellite Communication:** Transponders are allocated fixed bandwidths (e.g., 36 MHz or 54 MHz), and the bandwidth directly determines the link capacity. - **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Bandwidth flexibility is the core design parameter. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth of an AM (DSB) Signal Consider a baseband message signal $m(t)$ with Fourier transform $M(f)$ having bandwidth $W$ Hz, i.e., $M(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > W$. Let the carrier be $c(t) = A_{c} \cos(2\pi f_{c} t)$. The DSB-SC modulated signal is: $$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_{c} \cos(2\pi f_{c} t)$$ Taking the Fourier transform (using the modulation theorem): $$S(f) = \frac{A_{c}}{2}\Big[M(f - f_{c}) + M(f + f_{c})\Big]$$ **Step-by-step interpretation:** - $M(f - f_{c})$ shifts the message spectrum to the right by $f_{c}$ → produces the **USB (Upper Sideband)**. - $M(f + f_{c})$ shifts the message spectrum to the left by $f_{c}$ → produces the **LSB (Lower Sideband)**. - Since the original spectrum occupies $\vert f \vert \leq W$, after shifting it occupies $f_{c} - W \leq \vert f \vert \leq f_{c} + W$. - Therefore, the total width occupied is: $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{AM}} &= (f_{c} + W) - (f_{c} - W) \\ &= 2W \end{aligned}$$ Hence, **the bandwidth of an AM signal is twice the bandwidth of the modulating signal**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth of an SSB Signal In SSB-SC, only **one sideband** (either USB or LSB) is transmitted, while the carrier and the other sideband are suppressed. Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{SSB}} &= \text{width of one sideband} \\ &= (f_{c} + W) - f_{c} \\ &= W \end{aligned}$$ **Key Insight:** SSB uses **half the bandwidth of AM**, making it spectrally efficient. However, it requires more complex filtering and demodulation. ### 3.3 Derivation: Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth For an FM signal, the modulated spectrum is **theoretically infinite**, but its energy is concentrated around the carrier. Carson's rule provides the practical bandwidth containing **~98% of the total signal power**. The FM signal is: $$s(t) = A_{c} \cos\!\left(2\pi f_{c} t + 2\pi k_{f}\!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$ The instantaneous frequency deviation is $\Delta f = k_{f} \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert$. **Derivation Logic (Carson):** For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_{m} \cos(2\pi f_{m} t)$: - Frequency deviation: $\Delta f = k_{f} A_{m}$ - Modulation index: $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_{m}}$ - Bessel function analysis shows that significant sidebands exist up to order $n \approx \beta + 1$. The total bandwidth is: $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(\beta + 1) f_{m} \\ &= 2\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_{m}} + 1\right) f_{m} \\ &= 2(\Delta f + f_{m}) \\ &= 2(\Delta f + W) \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Case — NBFM (Narrowband FM):** When $\beta \ll 1$, $\Delta f \ll W$, then $B_{\text{FM}} \approx 2W$, identical to AM. > **Wideband FM (WBFM):** When $\beta \gg 1$, then $B_{\text{FM}} \approx 2\Delta f$. ### 3.4 Derivation: Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth for a Given Bit Rate For a noiseless channel, the maximum data rate using $M$ discrete signal levels is: $$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) \quad \text{bits/second}$$ To find the **minimum bandwidth** required to transmit a bit rate $R_{b}$, set $C = R_{b}$: $$\begin{aligned} R_{b} &= 2 B_{\min} \log_{2}(M) \\ B_{\min} &= \frac{R_{b}}{2 \log_{2}(M)} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering insight:** Using more signal levels ($M = 4, 8, 16, \ldots$) reduces bandwidth at the cost of increased susceptibility to noise — a direct application of the **bandwidth-power trade-off**. ### 3.5 Derivation: Shannon's Minimum Bandwidth For a noisy channel with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$: $$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ Setting $C = R_{b}$: $$\begin{aligned} R_{b} &= B_{\min} \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\ B_{\min} &= \frac{R_{b}}{\log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > As $S/N \to \infty$, $B_{\min} \to 0$, meaning with infinite power, any data rate is achievable in zero bandwidth — but infinite power is physically impossible, so Shannon's bound is **practically never reached**. ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A voice signal is bandlimited to 3.5 kHz. It is used to modulate a 1 MHz carrier using (a) AM, (b) SSB, (c) FM with $\Delta f = 15$ kHz. Calculate the bandwidth in each case. **Solution:** **(a) AM:** $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{AM}} &= 2W = 2 \times 3.5 = 7 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned}$$ **(b) SSB:** $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{SSB}} &= W = 3.5 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned}$$ **(c) FM (Carson's Rule):** $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{FM}} &= 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(15 + 3.5) = 37 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Bandwidth Determination Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD src[Source Signal
m of t] --> id1{Identify
Signal Type} id1 -->|Analog| ana[Determine
Baseband BW
equals W] id1 -->|Digital| dig[Determine
Bit Rate Rb
and Levels M] ana --> mod{Choose
Modulation
Scheme} dig --> chan{Channel
Type?} mod -->|AM| bam[Bandwidth
equals 2W] mod -->|SSB| bssb[Bandwidth
equals W] mod -->|FM| bfm[Bandwidth
equals 2 DeltaF plus W] chan -->|Noiseless| nyq[Apply Nyquist
C equals 2B log2 M] chan -->|Noisy| sha[Apply Shannon
C equals B log2 1 plus S over N] bam --> comp[Final Bandwidth
Requirement] bssb --> comp bfm --> comp nyq --> comp sha --> comp ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Bandwidth Comparison Across Modulation Schemes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph baseband[Baseband Signal] msg[Message W Hz] end subgraph mod[Modulated Signals] am[AM Bandwidth 2W] dsb[DSB-SC Bandwidth 2W] ssb[SSB Bandwidth W] vsb[VSB Bandwidth W plus fv] fm[FM Bandwidth 2 DeltaF plus W] pm[PM Bandwidth 2 DeltaF plus W] end baseband --> am baseband --> dsb baseband --> ssb baseband --> vsb baseband --> fm baseband --> pm ``` ### 4.3 Spectral Occupancy Comparison (Functional Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD f1[Carrier fc] --> usb[Upper Sideband
fc to fc plus W] f1 --> lsb[Lower Sideband
fc minus W to fc] usb --> amspec[AM Spectrum Width 2W] lsb --> amspec amspec -->|Filter SSB| ssbspec[SSB Spectrum Width W] amspec -->|Carsons Rule| fmspec[FM Spectrum Width 2 DeltaF plus W] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define bandwidth requirement of a communication system. List the bandwidths required for voice, music, and video signals.** **Model Answer:** Bandwidth requirement of a communication system is the minimum range of frequencies that must be transmitted by the channel to reproduce the original message signal with acceptable fidelity. - **Voice signal:** $\approx$ **3 kHz** (300 Hz to 3.4 kHz). - **Music (Hi-Fi audio) signal:** $\approx$ **15 kHz** (20 Hz to 20 kHz). - **Video (analog TV) signal:** $\approx$ **6 MHz**. The bandwidth is dictated by the spectral content of the source signal — a wider spectral content demands a wider channel. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain Carson's rule for determining the bandwidth of an FM signal.** **Model Answer:** Carson's rule gives the approximate transmission bandwidth of an FM signal that contains about **98% of the total signal power**: $$B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + W)$$ where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation and $W$ is the bandwidth of the modulating signal. For single-tone modulation with modulating frequency $f_{m}$ and modulation index $\beta = \Delta f / f_{m}$: $$B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\beta + 1) f_{m}$$ **Special cases:** - **NBFM** ($\beta \ll 1$): $B_{\text{FM}} \approx 2W$ (same as AM). - **WBFM** ($\beta \gg 1$): $B_{\text{FM}} \approx 2\Delta f$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`** **(a)** Derive the bandwidth of an AM signal. Explain why the AM bandwidth is twice the modulating signal bandwidth. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 5 kHz bandwidth signal modulates a 1 MHz carrier using AM and FM. For FM, the peak deviation is 25 kHz. Determine and compare the bandwidths in both cases. Comment on the bandwidth–SNR trade-off. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Let the message signal be $m(t)$ with Fourier transform $M(f)$, bandlimited to $W$ Hz. Carrier: $c(t) = A_{c} \cos(2\pi f_{c} t)$. AM signal: $s(t) = m(t) \cos(2\pi f_{c} t) \cdot A_{c}$. Taking Fourier transform using the modulation property: $$S(f) = \frac{A_{c}}{2}\Big[M(f - f_{c}) + M(f + f_{c})\Big]$$ The original $M(f)$ occupies $[-W, +W]$. After modulation, two replicas appear centered at $+f_{c}$ and $-f_{c}$. - Upper sideband: $[f_{c}, f_{c} + W]$ - Lower sideband: $[f_{c} - W, f_{c}]$ Total width: $$B_{\text{AM}} = (f_{c} + W) - (f_{c} - W) = 2W$$ The AM spectrum contains **both positive and negative frequency replicas**, hence the doubling. **(b) Numerical Comparison:** Given: $W = 5$ kHz, $f_{c} = 1$ MHz, $\Delta f = 25$ kHz. **AM:** $$B_{\text{AM}} = 2W = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$ **FM (Carson's Rule):** $$B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2(25 + 5) = 60 \text{ kHz}$$ **Comparison & Trade-off:** | Parameter | AM | FM | |---|---|---| | Bandwidth | 10 kHz | 60 kHz | | SNR performance | Lower | Higher (FM noise improvement ≈ $3\beta^{2}$) | | Spectral efficiency | Higher | Lower | **Trade-off:** FM uses **6× more bandwidth** than AM but provides significantly **better noise immunity** — a classic **bandwidth–power–SNR trade-off**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`** **(a)** State and explain Nyquist's channel capacity theorem. A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. Find the maximum data rate if the signal uses (i) 2-level encoding, (ii) 16-level encoding. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State Shannon–Hartley theorem. A channel has $B = 5$ kHz and $S/N = 100$ (20 dB). Find the maximum data rate. Also find the minimum bandwidth required to transmit 50 kbps over this channel. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Nyquist Theorem:** For a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz with $M$ discrete signal levels, the maximum data rate is: $$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) \text{ bits/sec}$$ Given: $B = 4$ kHz. **(i) For $M = 2$:** $$C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(2) = 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps}$$ **(ii) For $M = 16$:** $$C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(16) = 2 \times 4000 \times 4 = 32 \text{ kbps}$$ **Observation:** Quadrupling the number of levels quadruples the data rate without requiring additional bandwidth. **(b) Shannon–Hartley Theorem:** For a **noisy channel**, the maximum data rate (channel capacity) is: $$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ Given: $B = 5$ kHz, $S/N = 100$. $$C = 5000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 100) = 5000 \times \log_{2}(101)$$ $$\log_{2}(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658$$ $$C \approx 5000 \times 6.658 \approx 33.29 \text{ kbps}$$ **Minimum Bandwidth for $R_b = 50$ kbps:** $$B_{\min} = \frac{R_{b}}{\log_{2}(1 + S/N)} = \frac{50{,}000}{6.658} \approx 7510 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.51 \text{ kHz}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing Nyquist and Shannon formulas:** Students often write $C = 2B \log_{2}(1+S/N)$ — this is **wrong** and loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always convert kHz to Hz before substitution. > 3. **Misapplying Carson's rule:** Using $B = 2(\beta + f_{m})$ instead of $B = 2(\Delta f + W)$. > 4. **Ignoring special FM cases:** Failing to mention NBFM ($B \approx 2W$) and WBFM ($B \approx 2\Delta f$) loses 1 mark in "comment" type questions. > 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention "noiseless" for Nyquist and "noisy AWGN" for Shannon. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bandwidth Definition:** $B = f_{\text{upper}} - f_{\text{lower}}$ — the **fundamental currency** of communication. - **Standard Voice Bandwidth:** ~**3 kHz** (telephony). - **AM Bandwidth:** $2W$ — contains both USB and LSB. - **DSB-SC Bandwidth:** $2W$ (carrier suppressed). - **SSB Bandwidth:** $W$ — most spectrally efficient analog scheme. - **VSB Bandwidth:** $W + f_{v}$ — used in TV broadcasting. - **FM Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):** $2(\Delta f + W)$ — contains ~98% of signal power. - **PM Bandwidth:** Same formula as FM with $\Delta f$ replaced by peak phase deviation rate. - **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_{s} \geq 2W$ to avoid aliasing. - **Nyquist Channel Capacity (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$. - **Shannon Capacity (Noisy):** $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ — **absolute upper bound**. - **Spectral Efficiency:** $\eta = R_{b}/B$ — high $\eta$ is desirable. - **Bandwidth–Power Trade-off:** More levels reduce bandwidth but increase noise susceptibility. - **Information Capacity Hierarchy:** Increasing $M$ or $B$ increases $C$; increasing noise reduces $C$. - **Memorize the table of signal bandwidths** (voice, music, video, AM/FM radio) — KTU frequently tests this directly for 2–3 marks. - **Always check** whether the question is asking for **baseband bandwidth**, **transmission bandwidth**, or **minimum channel bandwidth** — these are three different quantities.

topics.title: FM Generation: Varactor Diode Modulator and Armstrong Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FM Generation: Varactor Diode Modulator and Armstrong Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Frequency Modulation (FM) — Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **FM Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Frequency Modulation is an angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude remains constant.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t}\! m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (constant in FM)
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency
- $k_f$ = Frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt)
- $m(t)$ = Message signal

### 1.2 Classification of FM Generation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** FM generation techniques are broadly divided into **Direct Methods** and **Indirect Methods**.

| Method Type | Technique | Key Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct FM** | Varactor Diode Modulator | Voltage-variable capacitor (VVC) |
| **Indirect FM** | Armstrong Method | Balanced Modulator + Integrator + Phase Shifter |

### 1.3 The Varactor Diode Modulator — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A Varactor Diode Modulator is a **direct FM generator** that exploits the voltage-dependent junction capacitance of a reverse-biased PN-junction diode (varactor) placed across the tank circuit of an LC oscillator.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Stretchy Guitar String":**
Imagine a guitar where instead of tightening/loosening the string with pegs, the **length of the string itself changes** with the pressure of your hand. 
- **More pressure (higher voltage) → string shortens → higher pitch (frequency)**
- **Less pressure (lower voltage) → string lengthens → lower pitch (frequency)**

Similarly, in the varactor modulator:
- The modulating signal **m(t) squeezes or releases** the depletion region of the diode.
- This **changes capacitance** → changes oscillator frequency → produces FM directly.

### 1.4 The Armstrong Method — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Armstrong Method is an **indirect FM generation technique** that first produces a **narrowband Phase Modulated (PM) signal** using a balanced modulator, and then converts PM to FM by integrating the message signal before modulation.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Indirect Coffee Maker":**
Think of FM as freshly brewed coffee. You don't have a coffee machine (direct FM is hard), so you:
1. **Integrate (mash) the coffee grounds** (integrate m(t) → ∫m(t)dt)
2. **Pass them through a phase modulator** (balanced modulator with 90° shift)
3. **Use frequency multipliers** to amplify the deviation to broadcast levels.

Result: high-quality FM without the oscillator instability of direct methods.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The Armstrong method was historically used by **Edwin H. Armstrong (1936)**. Its major advantage is **excellent frequency stability** because it uses a crystal oscillator as the carrier source.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
