Explain how the Balanced Slope Detector overcomes each limitation. **Model Solution:** | # | Limitation of Single Slope Detector | How BSD Overcomes It | |---|-------------------------------------|----------------------| | 1 | Only one slope used → output has a DC offset even when $\Delta f = 0$. | Two symmetric tanks give **zero DC at $f_c$** since equal voltages are subtracted. | | 2 | Demodulation sensitivity is limited (one slope only). | Slopes of **two tanks add algebraically**, doubling the sensitivity. | | 3 | Asymmetric S-curve → high even-order distortion. | Symmetric configuration **cancels even-order distortion terms**. | | 4 | Critical alignment of single tank frequency. | Slight mistuning of one tank is compensated by the other — more tolerant design. *(5 marks for table + explanation)* | | 5 | Poor AM rejection — amplitude noise on FM directly passes to output. | Subtraction cancels common-mode amplitude variations, improving AM rejection. *(2 marks)* | --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** The carrier frequency of an FM signal is $f_c = 88 \ \text{MHz}$ (broadcast FM). Each tuned circuit of a BSD has $Q = 45$ and $R = 3 \ \text{k}\Omega$. Design the circuit for: (i) Optimal frequency offset of each tank. (ii) Maximum sensitivity. (iii) State and justify whether this design is suitable for broadcast FM (max deviation $\pm 75 \ \text{kHz}$). **Model Solution:** **(i) Optimal frequency offset:** $$\Delta f_{a,opt} \;=\; \frac{f_0}{2\sqrt{2}\,Q} \;=\; \frac{88 \times 10^6}{2 \times 1.414 \times 45} \;\approx\; 691.4 \ \text{kHz}$$ Upper tank: $f_1 = 88 \ \text{MHz} + 691.4 \ \text{kHz} = 88.6914 \ \text{MHz}$ Lower tank: $f_2 = 88 \ \text{MHz} - 691.4 \ \text{kHz} = 87.3086 \ \text{MHz}$ **(ii) Maximum sensitivity:** $$S_{BSD,\,max} \;=\; \frac{1.54 \times 3000 \times 45}{88 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 2.36 \ \text{mV/kHz}$$ **(iii) Suitability check:** Linear range is approximately $\Delta f_{lin} \approx f_0 / (3Q)$: $$\Delta f_{lin} \;\approx\; \frac{88 \times 10^6}{3 \times 45} \;\approx\; 651.85 \ \text{kHz}$$ Since the broadcast FM deviation is only $\pm 75 \ \text{kHz} \ll 651.85 \ \text{kHz}$, the signal **lies well within the linear region** of the S-curve. ✓ **Conclusion:** The design is **suitable** for broadcast FM demodulation. The maximum output swing at $\Delta f = 75 \ \text{kHz}$ is $2.36 \ \text{mV/kHz} \times 75 \ \text{kHz} \approx 177 \ \text{mV}$ peak — easily amplified by a subsequent audio stage. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Students frequently lose marks on BSD problems due to the following errors: > 1. **Confusing sensitivity with gain.** Sensitivity $S_{BSD}$ has units of V/Hz (or V/kHz); it is *not* dimensionless. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** Many students write $S_{BSD} = K_{single}$ instead of $2 K_{single}$. The balanced configuration combines two slopes — always include the multiplication by 2. > 3. **Wrong sign in subtraction.** The output is $V_2 - V_1$ (or $V_1 - V_2$), producing a sign convention. Forgetting the subtraction leads to zero or doubled output — *immediately losing 2–3 marks*. > 4. **Missing the optimal offset.** A common 2-mark sub-question is "design the detector for maximum sensitivity." Always state $\Delta f_{a,opt} = f_0/(2\sqrt{2}\,Q)$ explicitly. > 5. **Not checking linear range.** When asked "is this suitable for broadcast FM?", verify $\Delta f_{max} < f_0/(3Q)$. Examiners reward this justification. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Balanced Slope Detector** - **Definition:** FM demodulator using two symmetrically tuned resonant tanks + envelope detectors + subtractor. - **Operating principle:** Frequency → Amplitude conversion via the linear slope of a parallel LC tank's impedance curve. - **Tank 1 (upper):** tuned at $f_c + \Delta f_a$ → operates on **descending** slope. - **Tank 2 (lower):** tuned at $f_c - \Delta f_a$ → operates on **ascending** slope. - **Sensitivity (single tank):** $K_d = \dfrac{2RQ\,u_0}{f_0(1+u_0^2)^{3/2}}$ - **Sensitivity (BSD):** $S_{BSD} = 2 K_d = \dfrac{4RQ\,u_0}{f_0(1+u_0^2)^{3/2}}$ - **Maximum sensitivity condition:** $u_0 = 1/\sqrt{2}$ → $\Delta f_{a,opt} = f_0 / (2\sqrt{2}\,Q)$ - **Maximum sensitivity value:** $S_{BSD,\,max} = \dfrac{8RQ}{3\sqrt{3}\,f_0} \approx \dfrac{1.54\,RQ}{f_0}$ - **Output voltage:** $v_{out}(t) = S_{BSD} \cdot k_f \cdot m(t)$ — linearly proportional to the message. - **Key advantage over single slope:** zero DC offset, doubled sensitivity, cancellation of even-order distortion. - **Key limitation:** requires precise tuning of two tanks; poor AM rejection compared to ratio detector. - **Typical parameters (broadcast FM IF):** $f_c = 10.7 \ \text{MHz}$, $Q \approx 50$, $R \approx 5 \ \text{k}\Omega$, $\Delta f_{a,opt} \approx 75 \ \text{kHz}$. - **Output S-curve:** passes through origin (zero at carrier) — symmetric about $f_c$. - **Examiner's mantra:** Always quote the *factor of 2*, the *optimal offset formula*, and check *linear range* before concluding.

topics.title: FM Transmitters and FM Receivers (Block Diagram)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FM Transmitters and FM Receivers (Block Diagram)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an FM Communication System?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a technique of angle modulation in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier signal is varied linearly in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude is held constant.

The formal mathematical definition of an FM signal is:

$$
s(t) \;=\; A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t \;+\; 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]
$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (constant)
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (Hz)
- $k_f$ = Frequency sensitivity of the modulator (Hz per volt)
- $m(t)$ = Message/modulating signal
- $2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau$ = Instantaneous phase deviation $\phi(t)$

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Siren on an Ambulance**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a footpath and an ambulance passes by. As the ambulance approaches you, the pitch (frequency) of its siren is **high**. As it moves away, the pitch becomes **low**. The **loudness (amplitude) of the siren never changes** — only the pitch (frequency) changes based on the ambulance's relative motion.
>
> In FM, the **amplitude of the carrier is like the loudness of the siren (always constant)**, while the **frequency of the carrier is like the pitch** — it swings higher and lower in lock-step with the loudness of your voice at the microphone. A louder voice → larger frequency swing. A softer voice → smaller frequency swing.

## 1.3 Why Two Different Blocks: Transmitter and Receiver?

| Block | Purpose | Real-World Example |
|:------|:--------|:-------------------|
| **FM Transmitter** | Generates and broadcasts the FM signal through space | The microphone + circuitry inside a radio station's broadcast tower |
| **FM Receiver** | Captures, selects, demodulates, and plays the FM signal back as audio | The circuit inside your car radio or mobile phone |

Both blocks form a complete **two-way communication link** in the **88–108 MHz** VHF Band-II commercial broadcast spectrum.

## 1.4 Key Parameters Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Commercial FM Broadcast Parameters (India & Global):**
> - Carrier frequency range: **88 MHz to 108 MHz** (VHF Band II)
> - **Maximum frequency deviation ($\Delta f$)**: $\pm 75$ kHz (FCC standard)
> - **Highest audio modulating frequency ($f_m$)**: 15 kHz
> - **Standard pre-emphasis time constant**: $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ (US/India); $50\,\mu s$ (Europe)
> - **Intermediate Frequency (IF) for FM receivers**: **10.7 MHz**
> - **Channel spacing**: 200 kHz

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: Block-by-Block Logic

### 2.1.1 FM Transmitter — Operational Logic

The **Armstrong Indirect FM Transmitter** (the most commonly tested transmitter in KTU) builds a wideband FM (WBFM) signal by **first generating a low-frequency, low-deviation Narrowband FM (NBFM)** signal and then **multiplying the deviation and frequency** up to the desired broadcast values.

**Step-by-step logic of each block:**

1. **Microphone/Audio Source** — Converts acoustic pressure (sound waves) into a low-amplitude electrical signal.
2. **Audio Amplifier** — Boosts the weak microphone signal to a usable voltage level.
3. **Pre-emphasis Network** — A simple high-pass RC filter with time constant $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ that **boosts high audio frequencies** before transmission. This is done because noise in FM systems is concentrated at higher frequencies — boosting high frequencies at the transmitter improves the overall Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) at the receiver.
4. **Audio Limiter / Amplitude Clipper** — Removes any unwanted amplitude variations from the audio signal to ensure that only frequency modulation (and not accidental AM) occurs.
5. **NBFM Modulator (Armstrong Method)** — A **balanced modulator** plus a **90° phase shift network** that produces a Narrowband FM signal with very small frequency deviation. The carrier frequency is also very low (e.g., a few hundred kHz from a crystal oscillator).
6. **Frequency Multipliers ($\times n_1, \times n_2, \ldots$)** — Non-linear stages that multiply both the carrier frequency **and** the frequency deviation by the same factor $n$. The product $n_1 \times n_2 \times \cdots$ is chosen to scale the deviation to the final $\pm 75$ kHz.
7. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** — Shifts the carrier up to the final broadcast frequency (in the 88–108 MHz band) **without altering the frequency deviation** (since adding a constant frequency to the carrier does not change its deviation).
8. **Power Amplifier (PA)** — Boosts the RF power to a level suitable for driving the antenna (typically tens of kilowatts for commercial broadcast).
9. **Antenna** — Radiates the modulated electromagnetic wave into free space.

### 2.1.2 FM Receiver — Operational Logic

The **FM Superheterodyne Receiver** is the universal architecture used in every commercial FM radio. It converts any incoming RF frequency to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF) of **10.7 MHz** for efficient amplification and demodulation.

**Step-by-step logic of each block:**

1. **Antenna** — Captures electromagnetic waves and converts them to a tiny RF voltage.
2. **RF Amplifier + Preselector** — A tunable bandpass filter (88–108 MHz) and low-noise amplifier that **selects the desired station** and rejects image frequencies and out-of-band interference.
3. **Mixer** — A non-linear device that multiplies the incoming RF with the Local Oscillator (LO) signal to produce sum and difference frequencies. The difference is selected as the IF.
4. **Local Oscillator (LO)** — A tunable oscillator that is always maintained at **$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + 10.7$ MHz** (high-side injection), so the difference frequency is always exactly **10.7 MHz**.
5. **IF Amplifier (10.7 MHz)** — Provides the bulk of the receiver's gain and selectivity. Multiple cascaded stages offer sharp bandpass filtering at 10.7 MHz.
6. **Limiter** — A hard-clipping amplifier that **removes all amplitude variations** from the IF signal (eliminating AM interference and impulse noise) before demodulation.
7. **FM Discriminator (Demodulator)** — Converts the frequency variations at IF back into the original audio voltage. Common types: **Foster–Seeley discriminator**, **Ratio detector**, or **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**.
8. **De-emphasis Network** — A low-pass RC filter with $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ that **attenuates the boosted high frequencies** to restore the original spectral balance and simultaneously suppress the high-frequency noise that pre-emphasis helped to overcome.
9. **Audio Amplifier** — Boosts the demodulated audio to a level sufficient to drive the loudspeaker.
10. **Loudspeaker** — Converts the audio voltage back into sound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Loops in FM Receivers:**
> - **AGC (Automatic Gain Control)** — Senses IF signal level and adjusts the RF amplifier gain to maintain constant output volume.
> - **AFC (Automatic Frequency Control)** — Uses the discriminator's DC output to correct any drift in the Local Oscillator, keeping it precisely at 10.7 MHz above the received RF.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau)d\tau\right]$ | FM signal in time domain | Volts |
| $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau)d\tau$ | Instantaneous phase deviation | Radians |
| $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ | Instantaneous frequency | Hz |
| $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t)\vert_{\max}$ | Peak frequency deviation | Hz |
| $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f \cdot m_p}{f_m}$ | Modulation index (dimensionless) | Unitless |
| $\text{BW}_{\text{Carson}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ | Carson's Rule Bandwidth | Hz |
| $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF} = 10.7$ MHz | Intermediate Frequency | Hz |
| $f_{\text{image}} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ | Image frequency | Hz |
| $H_{\text{pre}}(s) = 1 + s\tau$ | Pre-emphasis transfer function | — |
| $H_{\text{de}}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + s\tau}$ | De-emphasis transfer function | — |
| $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ (US/India), $50\,\mu s$ (Europe) | Standard time constant | seconds |
| $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_o = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta^2\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_c$ | FM output SNR (with $\beta \gg 1$) | Ratio (dB) |
| $\text{SNR improvement} \approx 13$ dB at 15 kHz | Due to pre/de-emphasis | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Carson's Rule is used (Engineering Utility):**
> In commercial FM broadcast, $\beta$ is typically very large (≈ 5 to 8). Theoretically, an FM signal has an **infinite** number of sidebands. Carson's rule gives a **practical engineering rule of thumb** that the receiver needs to pass only the sidebands containing at least ≈ 1% (more strictly, the sidebands where $J_n(\beta) \geq 0.04$ of the unmodulated carrier). This defines the **practical FM bandwidth** for designing RF filters and IF stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the FM Signal Equation

We start with the general form of an angle-modulated signal:

$$
s(t) \;=\; A_c \cos\bigl[\theta_i(t)\bigr]
$$

Here $\theta_i(t)$ is the **instantaneous phase** of the modulated signal.

**Step 1: Definition of Instantaneous Frequency**

The instantaneous frequency $f_i(t)$ is, by definition, the time derivative of the instantaneous phase divided by $2\pi$:

$$
f_i(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the FM Definition**

In frequency modulation, the instantaneous frequency is the carrier frequency plus a term proportional to the message signal:

$$
f_i(t) \;=\; f_c \;+\; k_f\, m(t)
$$

**Step 3: Solve for Instantaneous Phase**

Multiply both sides by $2\pi$ and integrate with respect to time:

$$
\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} \;=\; 2\pi f_c \;+\; 2\pi k_f m(t)
$$

$$
\theta_i(t) \;=\; 2\pi f_c t \;+\; 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back into $s(t)$**

$$
s(t) \;=\; A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t \;+\; 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]
$$

This is the **standard FM signal expression** that appears in every KTU exam. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of Carson's Rule (Heuristic Form)

For a **single-tone message** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the FM signal becomes:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]
$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ is the modulation index.

Using the **Bessel function expansion**:

$$
s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\!\bigl[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\bigr]
$$

**Step 1: Identify the Sideband Amplitudes**

The amplitude of the $n$-th order sideband is $A_c\, J_n(\beta)$. As $\beta$ increases, more sidebands carry significant energy.

**Step 2: Apply the Engineering Criterion**

A sideband is considered "significant" if its amplitude is at least 1% of the unmodulated carrier amplitude, i.e.:

$$
\vert J_n(\beta)\vert \;\geq\; 0.01
$$

**Step 3: Approximate the Significant Order**

For wideband FM ($\beta \gg 1$), it can be shown that the significant order extends approximately to:

$$
n_{\max} \;\approx\; \beta + 1
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Total Bandwidth**

The total bandwidth is from the lowest significant sideband to the highest, spanning $2 n_{\max} f_m$ Hz:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BW} \;=\; 2(\beta + 1) f_m \;=\; 2(\Delta f + f_m)\;}
$$

This is **Carson's Rule**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Example Problem (14-mark style):**
> An FM broadcast transmitter uses a modulating signal $m(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \cdot 5000\, t)$ volts. The frequency sensitivity of the modulator is $k_f = 15$ kHz/V. Calculate:
> (a) The frequency deviation $\Delta f$
> (b) The modulation index $\beta$
> (c) The bandwidth using Carson's rule
> (d) The bandwidth using the Bessel table (take the 1% criterion)

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Frequency Deviation**

$$
\Delta f \;=\; k_f \cdot \vert m(t)\vert_{\max} \;=\; (15 \times 10^3) \cdot 10
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f \;=\; 150 \text{ kHz}\;}
$$

**Step (b): Modulation Index**

Here $f_m = 5000$ Hz $= 5$ kHz.

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} \;=\; \frac{150 \text{ kHz}}{5 \text{ kHz}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\beta \;=\; 30\;}
$$

**Step (c): Carson's Rule Bandwidth**

$$
\text{BW} \;=\; 2(\Delta f + f_m) \;=\; 2(150 + 5)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BW} \;=\; 310 \text{ kHz}\;}
$$

**Step (d): Bessel Table Criterion (1% rule)**

For $\beta = 30$, the highest significant order sideband is approximately $n_{\max} \approx \beta + 1 = 31$. So:

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{Bessel}} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot 31 \cdot 5 \;=\; 310 \text{ kHz}
$$

Note that **Carson's rule and the 1% Bessel criterion agree exactly** — confirming Carson's rule is a valid engineering approximation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of FM Transmitter Methods

| Feature | Direct FM (Varactor/Oscillator) | Indirect FM (Armstrong Method) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Method | Varies tank capacitor of LC oscillator | Generates NBFM, then frequency multiplies |
| Frequency stability | Poor (depends on LC components) | Excellent (uses crystal oscillator) |
| Deviation obtained | Large directly | Built up by multiplication chain |
| Complexity at high $\Delta f$ | Simple hardware | Complex hardware (multipliers) |
| Used in | High-power broadcast (with AFC) | High-fidelity communication systems |
| KTU exam relevance | Mentioned conceptually | **Primary focus of questions** |

## 3.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio in FM Systems

For an FM receiver operating above threshold, the **output SNR** is:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_o \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\,\beta^2 \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_c
$$

where $(S/N)_c$ is the carrier-to-noise ratio at the receiver input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Output SNR in FM grows with the **square of the modulation index $\beta^2$**. This is why FM can trade bandwidth for noise immunity — wider bandwidth (larger $\beta$) yields better noise performance.

With **pre-emphasis and de-emphasis**, an additional improvement of ≈ **13 dB** is achieved at the top audio frequency (15 kHz), because the de-emphasis filter attenuates high-frequency noise that was not accompanied by a signal boost at the transmitter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FM Transmitter — Armstrong Indirect Method Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **Armstrong Indirect FM Transmitter** — the most commonly examined transmitter architecture in KTU papers. It uses a **Balanced Modulator + 90° phase shift** to generate NBFM, followed by **frequency multipliers and a mixer** to achieve wideband FM at the final carrier frequency.

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Microphone Audio Source] --> aa[Audio Amplifier]
    aa --> pre[Pre-emphasis Network tau equals 75 microseconds]
    pre --> lim[Audio Limiter]
    lim --> bm[Balanced Modulator DSB-SC]
    ps[90 Degree Phase Shifter Network] --> bm
    xtal[Crystal Oscillator Carrier Source] --> ps
    bm --> nbfm[NBFM Modulator Output Low Deviation]
    nbfm --> mult1[Frequency Multiplier Stage N1]
    mult1 --> mult2[Frequency Multiplier Stage N2]
    mult2 --> mixer[Mixer with Local Oscillator]
    lo[Local Oscillator High Frequency] --> mixer
    mixer --> pa[Power Amplifier RF Final Stage]
    pa --> ant[Broadcast Antenna]
    mixer -.-> ifmon[IF Monitor for Frequency Control]
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right signal flow):**
1. Audio input is amplified and pre-emphasized.
2. A **Balanced Modulator** multiplies the audio with a 90°-phase-shifted carrier, generating DSB-SC.
3. The DSB-SC, when added to the original carrier, becomes NBFM.
4. **Two frequency multiplier stages** scale both carrier frequency and deviation upward.
5. A **Mixer + Local Oscillator** shifts the carrier to the final 88–108 MHz band without changing deviation.
6. A **Power Amplifier** drives the antenna for transmission.

## 4.2 FM Receiver — Superheterodyne Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **universal FM receiver architecture** used in every commercial radio. The key design feature is the conversion of any incoming RF frequency to a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 10.7 MHz** for efficient amplification and demodulation. Two negative feedback loops — **AGC** and **AFC** — ensure stable operation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ant[Antenna RF Signal Input] --> rfamp[RF Amplifier with Preselector 88 to 108 MHz]
    rfamp --> mix[Mixer Non-linear Device]
    lo[Local Oscillator Tunable] --> mix
    mix --> ifamp[IF Amplifier Chain at 10.7 MHz]
    ifamp --> lim[Limiter Amplitude Clipper]
    lim --> disc[FM Discriminator Foster Seeley or Ratio Detector]
    disc --> de[De-emphasis Network tau equals 75 microseconds]
    de --> audioamp[Audio Power Amplifier]
    audioamp --> spk[Loudspeaker Output]
    ifamp -.-> agc[AGC Automatic Gain Control]
    agc -.-> rfamp
    disc -.-> afc[AFC Automatic Frequency Control]
    afc -.-> lo
```

**Reading the diagram (antenna → speaker):**
1. The **Antenna** picks up all RF signals.
2. The **RF Amplifier + Preselector** selects the desired station (88–108 MHz).
3. The **Mixer + Local Oscillator** down-converts RF to a fixed IF of 10.7 MHz.
4. The **IF Amplifier** provides most of the receiver's gain and selectivity.
5. The **Limiter** strips off any amplitude variations / AM interference.
6. The **Discriminator** converts frequency variations back to voltage (audio).
7. The **De-emphasis** network restores the original spectral balance.
8. The **Audio Amplifier** drives the loudspeaker.
9. The **AGC loop** stabilizes receiver gain; the **AFC loop** locks the local oscillator.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why This Architecture?

| Stage | Function Achieved | Why It Is Placed Here |
|:------|:-----------------|:----------------------|
| Preselector | Image rejection, station selection | Before mixing to prevent image-frequency interference |
| Mixer + LO | Frequency translation to fixed IF | Allows a single IF chain to handle all stations |
| IF Amplifier | Bulk gain + adjacent channel rejection | At fixed 10.7 MHz, filters can be precisely designed |
| Limiter | AM rejection | Just before discriminator to ensure clean FM input |
| Discriminator | FM → AM conversion (for demodulation) | Heart of the receiver — recovers the audio |
| De-emphasis | Inverse of pre-emphasis | Recovers flat audio response + reduces hiss |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define frequency modulation. Write the mathematical expression for an FM signal and identify the role of the phase deviation term. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Frequency Modulation (FM) is a method of angle modulation in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier wave is varied in direct proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude remains constant.

The mathematical expression for an FM signal is:

$$
s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right]
$$

The term $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)d\tau$ is called the **instantaneous phase deviation**. It represents the cumulative change in the phase of the carrier caused by the message signal, and it determines the bandwidth and sideband structure of the FM signal. The maximum value of $\phi(t)$ is the modulation index $\beta$.

### Q2. What is Carson's rule? State the formula for the bandwidth of an FM signal. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Carson's rule is an **empirical engineering approximation** used to determine the **practical transmission bandwidth** required for an FM signal. It is based on the observation that the Bessel function sideband coefficients $J_n(\beta)$ become negligibly small (less than 1% of the carrier) beyond the order $n \approx \beta + 1$.

The formula is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)\;}
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation, $f_m$ is the highest modulating frequency, and $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ is the modulation index. For commercial FM broadcast with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz, Carson's rule gives BW = **180 kHz**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **FM superheterodyne receiver**. Describe the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**Q.A (b)** Explain the functions of **pre-emphasis and de-emphasis** in FM systems. Why are they used and what is the standard time constant? **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The FM superheterodyne receiver converts any incoming RF signal in the 88–108 MHz band to a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 10.7 MHz** for efficient amplification and demodulation. The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.2** above. The function of each block:

1. **Antenna:** Captures the weak RF signal from free space and induces a corresponding voltage.
2. **RF Amplifier and Preselector:** A tunable bandpass filter (88–108 MHz) and low-noise amplifier that boosts the desired station and rejects image-frequency interference and out-of-band signals.
3. **Mixer:** A non-linear device that multiplies the RF signal with the Local Oscillator output. It produces sum $(f_{LO}+f_{RF})$ and difference $(f_{LO}-f_{RF})$ components. The difference is selected as the IF.
4. **Local Oscillator (LO):** A tunable oscillator maintained at $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + 10.7$ MHz (high-side injection) so the IF output is always exactly 10.7 MHz.
5. **IF Amplifier (10.7 MHz):** Provides the bulk of the receiver's gain and selectivity. Multiple cascaded ceramic or SAW filters at 10.7 MHz give sharp bandpass characteristics.
6. **Limiter:** A saturated amplifier that hard-clips the IF signal, removing all amplitude variations. This eliminates AM interference and impulse noise, ensuring that only frequency variations reach the discriminator.
7. **FM Discriminator (Foster–Seeley / Ratio Detector / PLL):** Converts the frequency variations at IF into a proportional audio voltage. The Foster–Seeley uses a phase-shift network; a ratio detector provides inherent AM rejection; a PLL provides the most linear demodulation.
8. **De-emphasis Network:** A low-pass RC filter with time constant $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ that attenuates high audio frequencies to undo the pre-emphasis boost, restoring flat frequency response and simultaneously suppressing high-frequency noise.
9. **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the demodulated audio to a level sufficient to drive the loudspeaker.
10. **Loudspeaker:** Reproduces the original sound.

**Auxiliary loops:**
- **AGC (Automatic Gain Control):** Samples the IF signal level, generates a DC control voltage, and adjusts the RF amplifier gain to maintain constant output volume despite signal fading.
- **AFC (Automatic Frequency Control):** Uses the DC error voltage from the discriminator to fine-tune the Local Oscillator and compensate for any drift, ensuring the IF stays exactly at 10.7 MHz.

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

In an FM system, **noise power spectral density is not uniform** — it increases as the square of the audio frequency (parabolic distribution). This means high-frequency audio components suffer from a much poorer SNR than low-frequency components. To compensate, FM systems use a complementary pair of filters called **pre-emphasis** (at the transmitter) and **de-emphasis** (at the receiver).

**Pre-emphasis:**
- A simple **high-pass RC filter** with time constant $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ (US/India standard) inserted between the audio amplifier and the FM modulator.
- Transfer function: $H_{\text{pre}}(s) = 1 + s\tau$
- It **boosts the high-frequency components** of the audio signal before transmission, ensuring they have a higher SNR at the receiver after being affected by the same noise.
- The 3-dB corner frequency is at $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau) \approx 2.12$ kHz.

**De-emphasis:**
- A complementary **low-pass RC filter** with the same time constant $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ placed after the FM discriminator.
- Transfer function: $H_{\text{de}}(s) = 1/(1 + s\tau)$
- It **attenuates the high-frequency components** that were boosted at the transmitter, restoring the original spectral balance of the audio signal.
- Crucially, it also **attenuates the high-frequency noise** that was added to the signal during transmission — the high frequencies now contain more signal but the same amount of noise, so de-emphasis improves the overall SNR.

**Standard Time Constants:**
- US, India, and most of the world: $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ (corner frequency $\approx 2.12$ kHz)
- Europe: $\tau = 50\,\mu s$ (corner frequency $\approx 3.18$ kHz)

**Performance Benefit:**
- The combination of pre-emphasis and de-emphasis provides an SNR improvement of approximately **13 dB at 15 kHz** and about **6 dB at 1 kHz** for typical audio signals.
- The overall audio frequency response remains flat (pre-emphasis and de-emphasis are inverses of each other), but the noise floor is significantly reduced at high frequencies.

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q.B (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **Armstrong indirect FM transmitter**. Why is the indirect method used? **[7 Marks]**

**Q.B (b)** An FM signal is produced by a modulator with frequency sensitivity $k_f = 25$ kHz/V using a message signal $m(t) = 8\cos(2\pi \cdot 4000\,t)$ volts. Calculate:
(i) Frequency deviation $\Delta f$
(ii) Modulation index $\beta$
(iii) Bandwidth using Carson's rule **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

The **Armstrong indirect FM transmitter** is used to generate wideband FM (WBFM) by first creating a low-frequency, low-deviation narrowband FM (NBFM) signal and then using **frequency multipliers** to scale up both the carrier frequency and frequency deviation to the desired broadcast values. The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** above.

**Working of Each Block:**

1. **Microphone:** Converts the audio (speech/music) into a low-amplitude electrical signal.
2. **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the weak microphone signal to a usable voltage level.
3. **Pre-emphasis Network:** A high-pass RC filter with $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ that boosts high audio frequencies to improve SNR.
4. **Audio Limiter:** Removes any unwanted amplitude variations to ensure pure FM (no accidental AM).
5. **NBFM Modulator (Armstrong Method):** This is the heart of the indirect method. It consists of:
   - A **Balanced Modulator** that multiplies the audio signal with the carrier to produce a DSB-SC signal.
   - A **90° Phase Shifter** that introduces a 90° phase shift in the carrier.
   - A **Summing Junction** that adds the DSB-SC output to the original (un-phased) carrier. The result is a NBFM signal with very small frequency deviation.
6. **Frequency Multipliers ($\times n_1$ and $\times n_2$):** Non-linear stages that multiply both the carrier frequency AND the frequency deviation by the same factor. For example, if $n_1 = 16$ and $n_2 = 5$, the total multiplication is $\times 80$. The product is chosen to bring the frequency deviation up to the final $\pm 75$ kHz.
7. **Mixer + Local Oscillator:** A second frequency translation stage. The mixer subtracts (or adds) a local oscillator frequency to shift the carrier to the final broadcast frequency (88–108 MHz) **without changing the frequency deviation**.
8. **Power Amplifier (PA):** Boosts the RF power to a level suitable for driving the antenna (kW range for broadcast).
9. **Antenna:** Radiates the FM signal as electromagnetic waves.

**Why the Indirect Method?**
- **Frequency Stability:** Direct FM (using a varactor-modulated LC oscillator) suffers from poor frequency stability because the carrier frequency drifts with temperature, supply voltage, and component aging. In the indirect method, the NBFM signal is generated from a **crystal oscillator**, which provides excellent frequency stability. The crystal-derived carrier is rock-solid, and any drift in the multipliers is negligible.
- **Center Frequency Accuracy:** For commercial broadcast, the FCC (and TRAI in India) require the carrier to be within $\pm 2$ kHz of the assigned frequency. This is easily achieved with crystal-stabilized indirect FM but very difficult with direct FM.
- **Trade-off:** The indirect method is more complex (requires multipliers and mixers) but offers superior spectral purity and stability — essential for commercial broadcasting.

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Frequency sensitivity: $k_f = 25$ kHz/V
- Message signal: $m(t) = 8\cos(2\pi \cdot 4000\,t)$ V
- So $A_m = 8$ V and $f_m = 4000$ Hz $= 4$ kHz

**Step (i): Frequency Deviation**

$$
\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = (25 \times 10^3) \cdot 8
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f = 200 \text{ kHz}\;}
$$

**Step (ii): Modulation Index**

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{200 \text{ kHz}}{4 \text{ kHz}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\beta = 50\;}
$$

**Step (iii): Bandwidth using Carson's Rule**

$$
\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(200 + 4)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BW} = 408 \text{ kHz}\;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> **1. Mixing up Direct and Indirect FM in Block Diagrams:** Many students draw a "Varactor diode" inside the Armstrong block diagram or draw "frequency multipliers" in a direct FM diagram. This is a **sure 2–3 mark deduction**. Remember: *Armstrong = crystal oscillator + multipliers; Direct = varactor in LC tank.*
>
> **2. Forgetting the 90° Phase Shifter:** The Armstrong NBFM modulator **must have** a 90° phase shifter between the crystal oscillator and the balanced modulator. Omitting it costs 2 marks.
>
> **3. Mixer vs. Frequency Multiplier Confusion:** A *multiplier* scales both frequency AND deviation by the same factor. A *mixer with LO* shifts the carrier frequency **without changing the deviation**. This is a very common 2-mark trap question.
>
> **4. IF Value for FM:** Always use **10.7 MHz** for FM (and **455 kHz** for AM). Confusing these will cost full marks in any problem involving IF/Image frequency.
>
> **5. Pre-emphasis time constant:** Indian/KTU exam default is **$\tau = 75\,\mu s$**. Mentioning 50 μs without specifying "Europe" will be marked wrong.
>
> **6. Carson's rule application:** Many students write BW = $2\Delta f$ (which is **wrong** for FM). The correct form is $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ or $2 f_m(\beta + 1)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Revision Bullet Points (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **FM = angle modulation where INSTANTANEOUS FREQUENCY varies with message amplitude; carrier amplitude is CONSTANT.**
- ✅ **FM Signal Equation:** $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)d\tau]$
- ✅ **Frequency Deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot m_p$ (Hz)
- ✅ **Modulation Index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ (dimensionless)
- ✅ **Carson's Rule:** BW $= 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$
- ✅ **Commercial FM:** $\Delta f = \pm 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz, BW = 180 kHz, Band = 88–108 MHz.
- ✅ **Armstrong Indirect FM** = Crystal Oscillator + Balanced Modulator + 90° Phase Shifter + Frequency Multipliers + Mixer + LO + PA + Antenna.
- ✅ **Frequency Multiplier** scales BOTH carrier AND deviation by the same factor $n$.
- ✅ **Mixer + LO** shifts carrier frequency WITHOUT changing deviation.
- ✅ **Superheterodyne FM Receiver** = RF Amp → Mixer → IF Amp (10.7 MHz) → Limiter → Discriminator → De-emphasis → Audio Amp → Speaker.
- ✅ **Limiter's role:** Removes AM interference and amplitude noise before demodulation.
- ✅ **FM Discriminator types:** Foster–Seeley, Ratio Detector, PLL demodulator.
- ✅ **IF for FM** = 10.7 MHz; **Image frequency** = $f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = f_{RF} + 21.4$ MHz.
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis** is a HIGH-PASS filter (boosts highs at Tx) with $\tau = 75\,\mu s$.
- ✅ **De-emphasis** is a LOW-PASS filter (cuts highs at Rx) with $\tau = 75\,\mu s$.
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis/De-emphasis purpose:** Improve SNR at high audio frequencies by ≈ 13 dB at 15 kHz. The flat overall response is preserved because they are inverse filters.
- ✅ **AGC** maintains constant output; **AFC** locks the local oscillator.
- ✅ **FM SNR advantage:** $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_o = \frac{3}{2}\beta^2 \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_c$ — SNR grows as $\beta^2$ (bandwidth-SNR trade-off).
- ✅ **Direct FM** = Varactor / LC oscillator modulation; **Indirect FM** = Armstrong method.
- ✅ **Why indirect FM?** Superior frequency stability because the carrier is derived from a crystal oscillator.
- ✅ **FM bandwidth is INFINITE in theory but PRACTICALLY limited by Carson's rule** (≈ 98% of signal power is contained within).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Pulse Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Pulse Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Communication Techniques, **Pulse Modulation** is defined as a modulation scheme in which a baseband message signal (analog, bandlimited) is used to vary one or more parameters of a **discrete-time periodic pulse train** (carrier), such as its **amplitude**, **width (duration)**, or **position (time shift)**. The carrier itself is not a continuous sinusoid (as in AM/FM), but a train of narrow rectangular pulses occurring at regular intervals determined by the **sampling frequency $f_s$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 – Core Definition**
> *"Pulse modulation is the process of transmitting an analog message signal using a train of pulses as the carrier, where a defining parameter (amplitude, width, or position) of each pulse is varied in proportion to the instantaneous samples of the message signal."*

Mathematically, the unmodulated pulse train can be represented as:
$$c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

where $A$ is the pulse amplitude, $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the **sampling period**, and $\tau$ is the **pulse width** (with $\tau \ll T_s$).

The three canonical analog pulse modulation families are:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Parameter Varied |
|---|---|---|
| **PAM** | Pulse Amplitude Modulation | Amplitude of each pulse |
| **PWM / PDM** | Pulse Width / Duration Modulation | Width ($\tau$) of each pulse |
| **PPM** | Pulse Position Modulation | Time position of each pulse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly treats pulse modulation as the **gateway to digital communication**. The natural progression in Module 2 is *PAM → PWM → PPM → PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)*, leading into digital transmission systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **watering a large garden** using a hose (this is your *message signal*). The continuous wave (CW) method is to keep the water flowing the entire time, varying the pressure smoothly. This wastes water, allows the wind to evaporate/drift some of it, and any small leak ruins the quality of the watering.

Now consider **pulse modulation** as a smarter approach: instead of a continuous stream, you open and close the tap at very regular short intervals (a *pulse train*). You can choose to control *how much* water comes out in each burst (**PAM**), *how long* the tap stays open in each burst (**PWM**), or *exactly when* the burst happens (**PPM**). Because each pulse is short and high-energy, it is far more resistant to slow leaks (analogous to noise). Moreover, you can now interleave pulses for several gardens using a single pipe — this is **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of the message).
- **Standard Voice Bandwidth:** $f_m = 3.4 \text{ kHz}$, hence $f_s \geq 8 \text{ kHz}$.
- **Duty Cycle:** $D = \tau / T_s$ (typically $\ll 1$, often $0.1$ to $0.01$).

> [!TIP]
> The **duty cycle $D$** is the single most important parameter that decides how much "off time" exists between pulses. A small duty cycle means the transmitter is *silent* for most of the time — and silence cannot be corrupted by noise. This is the philosophical heart of pulse modulation's noise immunity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Continuous-Wave (CW) Modulation Is Not Enough

Before justifying pulse modulation, it is critical to enumerate the **limitations of AM/FM (CW systems)** that pulse modulation overcomes. This is the core "Need" of the topic.

### 2.1.1 The Noise Problem in CW Systems
In AM, the information resides in the **envelope amplitude** of a sinusoid. Any additive noise shifts that envelope, and because the receiver cannot distinguish envelope fluctuations due to noise from those due to the signal, **the SNR at the output is permanently degraded**. There is no way to "clean" the signal once it is corrupted — the damage is irreversible.

In contrast, in **PAM, PWM, and PPM**, the decision at the receiver is binary-like (pulse present/absent, pulse early/late, pulse wide/narrow). By using a **decision threshold**, the receiver can **regenerate** the pulse, effectively removing accumulated noise. This principle is the foundation of the **regenerative repeater**.

### 2.1.2 The Multiplexing Problem
CW modulation natively supports **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** but requires sharp, stable bandpass filters (e.g., SSB-SC generation). Pulse modulation natively supports **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)**, which is mathematically simpler: just interleave samples from different sources in non-overlapping time slots.

### 2.1.3 The Digital Compatibility Problem
Modern communication is digital end-to-end. CW modulation produces an analog output, requiring an additional **A/D converter** before digital processing. Pulse modulation, especially its digital cousin **PCM**, is already in a discretized form ready for encryption, error correction, and storage.

### 2.1.4 The Power Efficiency Problem
In AM, the carrier is transmitted continuously even when the modulating signal is zero. In PWM/PPM, the **transmitter is OFF** between pulses (zero power), making the system power-efficient — critical for **satellite and battery-operated** links.

### 2.1.5 The Bandwidth–SNR Trade-off
The **Shannon–Hartley theorem** states:
$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bits/sec}$$
Pulse modulation schemes (especially PCM) allow us to **trade bandwidth for SNR** linearly — a luxury not available in pure CW schemes.

## 2.2 Structured Logic of "The Need for Pulse Modulation"

The "need" can be hierarchically broken down into **five driving requirements**:

1. **Noise Immunity** — Pulses can be regenerated at repeaters; envelope cannot.
2. **Multiplexing Flexibility** — TDM is the natural partner of pulse systems.
3. **Digital Transition** — Pulse systems are the bridge from analog to digital (PAM → PCM).
4. **Power Economy** — Low duty cycle means low average transmitted power.
5. **Processing Versatility** — Pulses can be stored, encrypted, error-corrected, and switched digitally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners frequently award marks for stating that pulse modulation **enables Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** and **allows regenerative repeaters** — these two phrases alone often secure full marks on "Need for Pulse Modulation" questions.

## 2.3 The Sampling Theorem (The Mathematical Foundation)

Pulse modulation is mathematically possible **only because of the Sampling Theorem** (also called the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**):

$$\boxed{\;f_s \geq 2 f_m\;}$$

- $f_s$ = sampling frequency
- $f_m$ = maximum frequency in the message spectrum

If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectra of the sampled signal **overlap** (a phenomenon called **aliasing**), and the original message cannot be recovered. The minimum $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing** is an *irreversible* distortion — once high-frequency components fold back into the message band, no filter can separate them. Hence an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff at $f_m$ is mandatory *before* sampling.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 2 | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Sampling period | seconds |
| 3 | $D = \tau / T_s$ | Duty cycle of pulse train | dimensionless |
| 4 | Average power $\propto D$ | Lower $D$ → lower average power | Watts |
| 5 | $B_{PAM} = 2 f_m$ | Bandwidth of double-sideband PAM | Hz |
| 6 | $B_{PPM} = (1/\tau) + 2 f_m$ | Approx. PPM transmission bandwidth | Hz |
| 7 | $SNR_{out} = 2^{2n} \cdot SNR_{in}$ | PCM SNR doubling per extra bit ($n$ bits) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/sec |
| 9 | $BW_{PWM} \approx 1/\tau$ | PWM bandwidth dominated by narrowest pulse | Hz |
| 10 | $t_p = nT_s \pm \Delta t$ | PPM pulse position (modulated) | seconds |

**Boundary Conditions (must be memorized for KTU):**
- $f_s = 2 f_m$ → **Nyquist Rate** (theoretical minimum).
- $f_s > 2 f_m$ → **Over-sampling** (used in practice to ease anti-alias filter design).
- $f_s < 2 f_m$ → **Under-sampling → Aliasing** (information loss).
- Duty cycle $D \ll 1$ → Approximates an impulse train in the limit.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN):** Uses **PCM** at 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits = **64 kbps** per voice channel.
- **Satellite Telemetry:** Uses **PWM/PPM** for low-power, high-noise environments.
- **Telemetry in Spacecraft (ISRO, NASA):** PPM is preferred due to constant pulse amplitude (no amplitude noise susceptibility).
- **IoT and Sensor Networks:** Low-duty-cycle PAM/PWM minimizes battery drain.
- **Digital Audio (CDs, MP3):** Direct descendants of the sampling theorem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Sampling Theorem and Recovery of the Original Signal

We will now derive, step by step, the relationship between the message signal $m(t)$, its sampled version $m_s(t)$, and the conditions under which $m(t)$ can be perfectly reconstructed.

### Step 1 — Define the message signal and its spectrum
Let $m(t)$ be a bandlimited message signal with Fourier transform $M(f)$:
$$m(t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; M(f), \quad \text{where } M(f) = 0 \text{ for } \vert f \vert > f_m$$

### Step 2 — Define the impulse train sampler
The sampling function is an ideal impulse train:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - nT_s), \quad T_s = 1/f_s$$

The Fourier series expansion of $p(t)$ is:
$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply the message by the impulse train
The sampled signal is the product:
$$m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} m(t)\, e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

### Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform (Convolution in Frequency)
By the multiplication property:
$$M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

This is a **periodic replication** of the original spectrum $M(f)$ at integer multiples of $f_s$.

### Step 5 — Apply the Non-Overlap Condition
For the spectra to **not overlap**, the spacing between adjacent replicas ($f_s$) must be at least twice the message bandwidth:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

This is the **Nyquist Criterion**.

### Step 6 — Reconstruction via Low-Pass Filter
A low-pass filter with gain $T_s$ and cutoff at $f_m$ recovers the original:
$$m(t) = T_s \cdot \text{LPF}\{m_s(t)\} = T_s \cdot \int_{-f_m}^{+f_m} M_s(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df$$

**Conclusion:** The derivation formally proves that the original message $m(t)$ can be recovered **without any distortion** if and only if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Power Saving in Pulse Modulation

The average power of a pulse train of amplitude $A$ and duty cycle $D = \tau/T_s$ is:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} c^2(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot A^2 \cdot \tau = A^2 D$$

For a continuous sinusoid of the same peak amplitude:
$$P_{CW} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

The **power ratio** is:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{avg}}{P_{CW}} = 2D$$

For $D = 0.1$ (a typical value), $\eta = 0.2$, i.e., the pulse system uses only **20% of the CW power** for the same peak amplitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** This is why PPM is the modulation of choice in **deep-space telemetry** (e.g., the NASA Voyager probes) — extreme power efficiency at the cost of bandwidth.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CW vs Pulse Modulation

| Performance Criterion | AM (CW) | PAM | PWM | PPM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Noise Immunity** | Poor (envelope-based) | Moderate | Good | Very Good |
| **Bandwidth** | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m$ | $\sim 1/\tau$ | $\sim 1/\tau$ |
| **TDM Support** | No (FDM only) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Transmitter Power** | Continuous | Pulsed | Pulsed | Pulsed (constant) |
| **Regenerative Repeater** | No | Limited | Yes | Yes |
| **Digital Compatibility** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Synchronization Need** | No | No | No | **Yes (strict)** |

## 3.4 Mathematical Relationship: Pulse Modulation to PCM

The natural progression is mathematically grounded in the **quantization** step. In PAM, samples $m(nT_s)$ are real-valued and continuous. In PCM, each sample is rounded to one of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, then encoded as an $n$-bit binary word. The quantization step-size is:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^n}$$

Quantization noise power is:
$$N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(V_{max} - V_{min})^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}$$

The **SNR of PCM** then becomes:
$$SNR_{PCM} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n} \cdot P_s}{V_{max}^2} = 2^{2n} \cdot \frac{3P_s}{V_{max}^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Every additional bit ($+1$ bit) in PCM gives a 6 dB improvement in SNR** — a unique and powerful property that justifies the bandwidth expansion in PCM. This is a favourite KTU examiner question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, drawing a **clean sketch** of the message $m(t)$, the sampling pulses, and the resulting PAM/PWM/PPM waveforms **side by side on the same time axis** almost guarantees full marks for "Need for Pulse Modulation" derivations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define pulse modulation. Why is it preferred over continuous wave modulation in modern communication systems?**

**Model Answer:**
Pulse modulation is a modulation technique in which a discrete-time **pulse train** acts as the carrier, and a parameter (amplitude, width, or position) of each pulse is varied in accordance with instantaneous samples of the message signal. The sampling theorem $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ governs the process.

It is preferred over continuous wave modulation because it offers:
1. **Better noise immunity** via regenerative repeaters.
2. **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** capability.
3. **Power efficiency** (transmitter OFF between pulses).
4. **Compatibility with digital systems** (smooth transition to PCM).
5. **Ease of signal processing** including encryption and error correction.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two advantages in detail.)*

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What happens if the sampling frequency is less than $2 f_m$?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states: *"A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$."*

If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of the message in the frequency domain **overlap**, causing **aliasing** — an irreversible distortion where high-frequency components fold back into the message band. This makes faithful recovery of $m(t)$ impossible.

*(3 marks: 1 mark statement, 1 mark formula, 1 mark aliasing explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **need for pulse modulation** in detail, comparing it with continuous wave modulation techniques. Highlight the role of regenerative repeaters and TDM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of neat waveforms and a block diagram, explain the generation and detection of **Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)**. State the Nyquist criterion for faithful recovery. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition & Context:**
Pulse modulation uses a discrete pulse train as the carrier. It is needed because modern communication demands:
- Resistance to noise (regenerative repeaters).
- Efficient multiplexing (TDM).
- Compatibility with digital hardware (PCM, encryption).
- Power efficiency for portable/satellite systems.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | AM (CW) | PAM (Pulse) |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | Continuous sinusoid | Pulse train |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m$ |
| TDM | Not natural | Native |
| Noise immunity | Low (envelope) | Moderate |
| Repeaters | Not regenerative | Regenerative possible |

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Role of Regenerative Repeaters:**
In CW systems, the noise added at every amplifier stage accumulates. In pulse systems, the receiver makes a **hard decision** (pulse/no-pulse) and **regenerates a clean pulse**, completely removing noise from previous sections. This is the *single most important advantage* of pulse modulation.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
Hence pulse modulation is essential for long-haul, multiplexed, and digital-ready communication systems.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2 above).**

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Generation:**
Message $m(t)$ is bandlimited by an anti-aliasing LPF (cutoff $f_m$), then multiplied by a pulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s = 1/f_s$. The output is PAM:
$$m_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n} m(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Waveform Sketch:**
Show $m(t)$ (smooth curve) and below it a pulse train whose pulse heights match $m(nT_s)$. This is **natural-sampled PAM** (or use flat-top PAM for sample-and-hold).

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Detection:**
At the receiver, the PAM signal is passed through a **low-pass filter** of cutoff $f_m$ to recover $m(t)$ (provided $f_s \geq 2 f_m$).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Nyquist Criterion:**
$$\boxed{\,f_s \geq 2 f_m\,}$$
Faithful recovery is guaranteed only if this is satisfied.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sampling Theorem** mathematically. Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal and discuss the condition to avoid aliasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **PAM, PWM, and PPM** in terms of bandwidth, noise immunity, transmitter power, and TDM suitability. State one application of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Statement:**
A bandlimited signal $m(t)$ with $M(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$ can be reconstructed from its samples $m(nT_s)$ if and only if $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Define Impulse Train:**
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s), \quad P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Sampled Signal Spectrum:**
$$m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) \;\Longrightarrow\; M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Non-Overlap Condition:**
For $M(f - nf_s)$ replicas to not overlap:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Aliasing Discussion:**
If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the replicas overlap; high-frequency components of $M(f)$ fold into the baseband — this is **aliasing**, which is irreversible. An anti-aliasing LPF with cutoff $f_m$ is essential before sampling.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | PAM | PWM | PPM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variable parameter | Amplitude | Width | Position |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | Higher $\sim 1/\tau$ | Higher $\sim 1/\tau$ |
| Noise immunity | Low | Moderate | High |
| Transmitter power | Variable | Variable | **Constant** |
| TDM suitability | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Synchronization need | No | No | **Strict** |
| Sample application | Telemetry | Motor control, class-D audio | **Space telemetry** |

**Step 1 (2 marks) — PAM:** Used in **simple telemetry** and as the **front-end of PCM systems**. The pulses are easiest to generate (just sample-and-hold).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — PWM:** Used in **motor speed control**, **class-D audio amplifiers**, and **switched-mode power supplies**. The constant amplitude gives moderate noise immunity.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — PPM:** Used in **spacecraft telemetry (ISRO, NASA)**, **optical communications**, and **remote controls**. Constant pulse amplitude means amplitude noise has *no effect*; only *timing* noise matters, which is small.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
The choice between PAM, PWM, and PPM is governed by the **noise environment**, **power budget**, and **bandwidth availability** of the specific application.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing PAM with AM** — PAM is a **baseband** sampled signal with **pulse** carrier, NOT a sinusoidal carrier. Drawing a sinusoid for the carrier will cost full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the Nyquist condition** in any sampling/PAM problem — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter** in block diagrams — examiners specifically check for this.
> 4. **Writing "PWM and PDM are different"** — they are **synonyms** (Pulse Width Modulation = Pulse Duration Modulation). Writing them as separate techniques is a conceptual error.
> 5. **Drawing PWM with varying amplitude** — the amplitude must be **constant**; only the *width* varies. This is the most common waveform error.
> 6. **PPM synchronization** — students often forget to mention that PPM requires **strict clock synchronization** between transmitter and receiver.
> 7. **Confusing $f_m$ with $f_s$** in the sampling theorem — the threshold is $2 f_m$ (the message bandwidth), NOT the carrier frequency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pulse modulation** uses a discrete pulse train as the carrier — its amplitude, width, or position carries the information.
- The fundamental enabler of pulse modulation is the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
- **Aliasing** (irreversible distortion) occurs when $f_s < 2 f_m$; prevented by an anti-aliasing LPF.
- The **need for pulse modulation** rests on **five pillars**: noise immunity, TDM capability, digital compatibility, power efficiency, and regenerative repeaters.
- **PAM** is the simplest pulse scheme and acts as the **front-end of PCM**.
- **PWM** has constant amplitude and variable width; widely used in **motor control and audio amplifiers**.
- **PPM** has constant amplitude and variable position; ideal for **high-noise, low-power** scenarios (space telemetry).
- The progression **PAM → PWM → PPM → PCM** mirrors the **evolution from analog to digital** communication.
- **PCM SNR** improves by **6 dB per additional bit** — a key KTU high-yield formula.
- **Regenerative repeaters** are the *single biggest advantage* of pulse modulation over CW modulation.
- **TDM** is the natural multiplexing partner of pulse systems, whereas **FDM** is the natural partner of CW systems.
- For KTU exams, always accompany derivations with a **neatly drawn waveform sketch** and a **labelled block diagram**.
- **Shannon's channel capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ sets the ultimate limit; pulse schemes (PCM) approach this limit by trading bandwidth for SNR.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "NAAAMP":**
> **N**yquist criterion, **A**nti-aliasing filter, **A**mplitude of pulse (PAM), **A**verage power saving, **M**ultiplexing (TDM) — covers 90% of any "Need for Pulse Modulation" question in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Modulation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEEBT751 – Communication Techniques (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Module 2** is dedicated to the study of **Different Types of Continuous-Wave (CW) Modulation Techniques**. Modulation is formally defined as the process by which one or more parameters of a high-frequency **carrier signal** are varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency **message signal** (also called the modulating or baseband signal).

The principal types of modulation studied in this module are categorized as follows:

**A. Amplitude Modulation (AM) Family**
1. Double Sideband Full Carrier (DSB-FC) — Conventional AM
2. Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)
3. Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier (SSB-SC)
4. Vestigial Sideband (VSB)

**B. Angle (Exponential) Modulation Family**
1. Frequency Modulation (FM) — Narrowband FM (NBFM) and Wideband FM (WBFM)
2. Phase Modulation (PM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must master the mathematical representation, time-domain equations, frequency spectra, bandwidth requirements, and power distribution for *each* of the modulation schemes listed above. Questions on comparison and bandwidth calculation are **high-weightage** in KTU ESE.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a large, noisy classroom trying to deliver a whispered message to a friend seated 50 meters away. Your whisper (the **message signal**) is too weak to travel that distance. What do you do?

You pick up a loud, steady musical note from a trumpet (the **carrier signal**). You then *sculpt* or *vary* the loudness (amplitude) of that trumpet note in sync with your whisper. Now, the trumpet sound carries your whisper across the room. This is exactly what **modulation** does in a communication system.

- If you vary the **loudness (amplitude)** of the trumpet according to your whisper → **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**.
- If you vary the **pitch (frequency)** of the trumpet according to your whisper → **Frequency Modulation (FM)**.
- If you vary the **timing of the beats (phase)** according to your whisper → **Phase Modulation (PM)**.

The "different types" refer to *which property* of the carrier is varied and *how completely* it is varied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Frequency** ($f_c$) is always much greater than the **Message Frequency** ($f_m$). For commercial AM broadcast radio, $f_c$ lies in the range **540 kHz to 1640 kHz**, while voice $f_m$ lies in **300 Hz to 3.4 kHz**. The standard FM broadcast band uses **88 MHz to 108 MHz**.

---

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Families — A Bird's-Eye View

$$
\text{Modulation} \begin{cases} \text{Continuous Modulation} \begin{cases} \text{Amplitude Modulation} \begin{cases} \text{DSB-FC (Conventional AM)} \\ \text{DSB-SC} \\ \text{SSB-SC} \\ \text{VSB} \end{cases} \\[2mm] \text{Angle Modulation} \begin{cases} \text{Frequency Modulation (FM)} \\ \text{Phase Modulation (PM)} \end{cases} \end{cases} \\[2mm] \text{Pulse Modulation} \begin{cases} \text{Analog: PAM, PWM, PPM} \\ \text{Digital: PCM, DM, ADM} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 of PEEBT751 typically restricts its scope to **Continuous-Wave (CW) Modulation** — that is, the **Amplitude** and **Angle** families. Pulse Modulation is covered in later modules.

---

### 1.4 The General Modulation Equation

Let the message signal be a single-tone sinusoid:

$$
m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)
$$

Let the carrier signal be:

$$
c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The **modulated signal** $s(t)$ always contains a parameter that depends on $m(t)$. We can write a unified form:

$$
s(t) = A(t) \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\right]
$$

- **Amplitude Modulation** → $A(t)$ varies with $m(t)$; $\phi(t) = $ constant
- **Angle Modulation** → $A(t) = A_c$ (constant); $\phi(t)$ varies with $m(t)$

This single equation is the cornerstone of Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Type 1 — Double Sideband Full Carrier (DSB-FC / Conventional AM)

**Concept:** The amplitude of the carrier is varied linearly with the message signal. The carrier itself is *not* suppressed — it remains present in the output.

**Time-Domain Equation:**

$$
s_{\text{AM}}(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a \, m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $k_a$ is the **amplitude sensitivity** of the modulator (units: V$^{-1}$).

**Modulation Index:**

$$
\mu = \frac{k_a \, A_m}{A_c} = \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For distortion-free DSB-FC transmission, we **must** have $0 \le \mu \le 1$. If $\mu > 1$, **over-modulation** occurs, producing envelope distortion and the message becomes unrecoverable via envelope detection.

**Frequency Spectrum:** Three impulses — Carrier at $f_c$, Upper Sideband (USB) at $f_c + f_m$, Lower Sideband (LSB) at $f_c - f_m$.

**Bandwidth:**

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{DSB-FC}} = 2 f_m
$$

**Total Transmitted Power:**

$$
P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)
$$

where $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2 R}$ is the carrier power.

**Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{SB}}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%
$$

Maximum theoretical efficiency is **33.33 %** (at $\mu = 1$). Real broadcast AM operates at $\mu \approx 0.3$, giving only **~4.3 %** efficiency.

---

### 2.2 Type 2 — Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)

**Concept:** Identical sidebands to DSB-FC, but the carrier is *suppressed* to save power. Only the sidebands carry information.

**Time-Domain Equation:**

$$
s_{\text{DSB-SC}}(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Spectrum:** Only USB and LSB remain; carrier amplitude becomes zero.

**Bandwidth:**

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{DSB-SC}} = 2 f_m
$$

**Key Feature:** DSB-SC is generated using a **balanced modulator**. Detection requires **coherent (synchronous) detection** — multiplication by a locally generated carrier that is *phase-locked* to the transmitter carrier.

---

### 2.3 Type 3 — Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier (SSB-SC)

**Concept:** Transmit only ONE sideband (USB *or* LSB) and suppress the other. The carrier is also suppressed. This is the most power-efficient and bandwidth-efficient AM scheme.

**Generation Methods:**
1. **Filter Method** — Start with DSB-SC, then pass through a sharp bandpass filter (BPF) to remove one sideband.
2. **Phase-Shift (Hartley) Method** — Use two balanced modulators and a 90° phase-shifting network.

**Time-Domain Equation (Upper Sideband):**

$$
s_{\text{USB}}(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2} \cos\!\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]
$$

**Bandwidth:**

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{SSB-SC}} = f_m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> SSB-SC cuts both bandwidth and power in half compared to DSB-SC, but requires complex receivers and is highly sensitive to phase/frequency errors. Used in **HF radio communication**, **amateur radio**, and **maritime communication**.

---

### 2.4 Type 4 — Vestigial Sideband (VSB)

**Concept:** A compromise between DSB-SC and SSB-SC. One full sideband + a *vestige* (small portion) of the other sideband is transmitted. Used where filtering a full sideband is impractical — most famously in **analog TV broadcast** (video signal).

**Bandwidth:**

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{VSB}} = f_m + f_v
$$

where $f_v$ is the width of the vestigial portion (typically **0.75 MHz** for NTSC, **1.25 MHz** for PAL).

---

### 2.5 Type 5 — Frequency Modulation (FM)

**Concept:** The *instantaneous frequency* of the carrier is varied linearly with the message signal amplitude.

**Time-Domain Equation:**

$$
s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]
$$

where $k_f$ is the **frequency sensitivity** (Hz/V).

**Modulation Index for FM:**

$$
\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}
$$

where $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ is the **frequency deviation** (maximum).

**Carson's Rule (Empirical Bandwidth):**

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta_f + 1)
$$

**Bessel Function Table (for NBFM and FM spectrum):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks for the **relative amplitudes of FM sidebands** using the **Bessel function of the first kind** $J_n(\beta_f)$. The carrier amplitude becomes $A_c J_0(\beta_f)$ and the $n$-th sideband amplitude is $A_c J_n(\beta_f)$. For NBFM ($\beta_f \ll 1$), $J_0 \approx 1$, $J_1 \approx \beta_f/2$, and $J_n \approx 0$ for $n \geq 2$.

---

### 2.6 Type 6 — Phase Modulation (PM)

**Concept:** The *instantaneous phase* of the carrier is varied linearly with the message signal.

**Time-Domain Equation:**

$$
s_{\text{PM}}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p \, m(t)\right]
$$

**Modulation Index for PM:**

$$
\beta_p = k_p A_m \quad (\text{radians})
$$

**PM–FM Relationship:**

$$
\text{PM of } m(t) \;\equiv\; \text{FM of } \frac{dm(t)}{dt}
$$

This is the single most important relationship between PM and FM. It tells us that **if you differentiate the message before feeding it to a PM modulator, the output is exactly FM**, and vice versa.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **DSB-FC** | **DSB-SC** | **SSB-SC** | **VSB** | **FM (WBFM)** | **PM** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Modulated Equation | $A_c[1+\mu m(t)]\cos\omega_c t$ | $A_c m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ | $A_c m(t)\cos\omega_c t \pm \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$ | Filtered DSB-SC | $A_c\cos\!\left[\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f\!\int m(\tau)d\tau\right]$ | $A_c\cos[\omega_c t + k_p m(t)]$ |
| Modulation Index | $\mu = k_a A_m / A_c$ | $\mu$ (not constrained) | — | — | $\beta_f = k_f A_m / f_m$ | $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m$ | $f_m$ | $f_m + f_v$ | $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | $2(\beta_p + 1) f_m$ |
| Carrier Power | $P_c$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $P_c$ | $P_c$ |
| Sideband Power | $\mu^2 P_c / 2$ | $A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}/2$ | $A_c^2 \overline{m^2(t)}/4$ | — | Distributed (Bessel) | Distributed (Bessel) |
| Detection Method | Envelope Detector | Coherent Detector | Coherent Detector | Coherent + Equalizer | Frequency Discriminator / PLL | Coherent or via FM chain |
| Application | AM Radio Broadcast | Color TV Chroma, Carrier Telephony | HF Radio, Amateur | Analog TV Video | FM Radio, Two-Way Radio | Some Digital Schemes |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the table uses `\vert` style phrasing in descriptions to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax with `|` characters. The pipe symbol is reserved strictly as a column separator.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSB-FC** powers **commercial AM broadcast** (e.g., All India Radio).
- **DSB-SC** is used in **color television chrominance subcarrier** (Quadrature DSB-SC of I and Q signals).
- **SSB-SC** dominates **long-distance HF communication** — aviation, maritime, military.
- **VSB** is the heart of **analog TV video transmission**.
- **WBFM** is the foundation of **FM radio**, **two-way VHF radio**, and **TV audio subcarrier**.
- **PM** appears in **QPSK / BPSK** digital modulation building blocks.
