2. **Block 2 — Quantizer:** Rounds each sample amplitude to the nearest of $L$ discrete levels. Step size $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L}$, where $V_{pp}$ is the peak-to-peak signal range.
3. **Block 3 — Encoder:** Maps each quantized level to an $n$-bit binary word ($n = \log_2 L$). Output is a serial bitstream for transmission.

**PCM Receiver Pipeline (Reverse Process):**
1. **Regenerative Repeater + Bit Synchronizer:** Cleans the bitstream, recovers clock.
2. **Decoder (n-bit DAC):** Converts each $n$-bit word back to a discrete voltage level.
3. **Reconstruction Filter (LPF):** Smooths the staircase PAM-like waveform back into the original analog message $m(t)$.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU ESE Module 2. Memorize both the algebraic form and the corresponding numerical conversion to **dB**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Standard Value |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ (minimum) | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Theorem (general) | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Max Quantization Error | $e_q^{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Mean-Square Quantization Noise | $\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | $V^2$ |
| 7 | Signal Power (sinusoidal msg) | $P_s = \dfrac{V_p^{\,2}}{2}$ | W (Watt) |
| 8 | SNR (Uniform Quantization) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_q = 2^{2n} = 4^n$ | Dimensionless (ratio) |
| 9 | SNR in Decibels | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_q \text{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ (sinusoidal) | dB |
| 10 | SNR (general signal) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_q = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n} \cdot P_s}{V_{pp}^{\,2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Transmission Bit Rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/second (bps) |
| 12 | PCM Bandwidth (NRZ) | $BW_{PCM} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = \dfrac{n f_s}{2}$ | Hz |
| 13 | Companding — µ-Law | $F(x) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$ | with $\mu = 255$ (standard) |
| 14 | Companding — A-Law | $F(x) = \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}$ (small); $\dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}$ (large) | with $A = 87.6$ (standard) |
| 15 | PPM Time Displacement | $\Delta t = k_p \cdot m(t)$ | seconds |
| 16 | PPM Pulse Width (constant) | $\tau = $ const | seconds |
| 17 | PCM Output Bit Duration | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b} = \dfrac{1}{n f_s}$ | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the companding formula row, the absolute value of $x$ is written using `\vert x \vert` (not the pipe symbol `|`) to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. In your exam paper, write it normally as $|x|$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PPM** dominates in **free-space optical (FSO) communication**, **underwater acoustic telemetry**, and **IR remote control protocols** because amplitude noise (sunlight, ambient IR) does not corrupt the timing information.
- **PCM** is the bedrock of **digital telephony** (G.711 standard uses 8-bit samples at 8 kHz → 64 kbps), **CD-quality audio (16-bit, 44.1 kHz → 705.6 kbps per channel)**, **VoLTE codecs (AMR-WB)**, and **digital instrumentation buses** (e.g., aerospace sensor telemetry).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power

**Given:** A quantizer with step size $\Delta$ produces uniformly distributed quantization error $e_q$ in the range $\left[-\frac{\Delta}{2},\,+\frac{\Delta}{2}\right]$.

**Step 1 — Write the probability density function of the error:**
Since $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over its range, the PDF is constant:

$$
p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for } e_q \in \left[-\frac{\Delta}{2},\,+\frac{\Delta}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of mean-square value:**
For any random variable $X$, $E[X^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot p(x)\,dx$. Applying this to the quantization error:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q)\,de_q
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the constant PDF and integrate:**

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\,de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right] = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24 \Delta} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $N_q = \overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ Watts (across a 1 Ω load). This is the *quantization noise power* used in all subsequent SNR derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for Sinusoidal Input

**Given:** A sinusoidal message $m(t) = V_p \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ quantized with $n$ bits per sample across full-scale range $V_{pp} = 2V_p$.

**Step 1 — Quantization step size in terms of bits and full-scale:**
For $L = 2^n$ levels spanning $V_{pp}$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{2 V_p}{2^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the noise power formula:**

$$
N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{1}{12} \cdot \left(\frac{2V_p}{2^n}\right)^2 = \frac{4 V_p^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} = \frac{V_p^2}{3 \cdot 4^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the signal power of a sinusoid:**
For $m(t) = V_p \cos(\cdot)$, the mean-square value (power across 1 Ω) is:

$$
P_s = \frac{V_p^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio $\frac{S}{N}$:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{P_s}{N_q} = \frac{V_p^2 / 2}{V_p^2 / (3 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using $4^n = 2^{2n}$:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels using $10 \log_{10}$:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{q,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(2^{2n}\right)
$$

Computing $10\log_{10}(3/2) \approx 1.76$ and $10 \log_{10}(2^{2n}) = 20n \log_{10}(2) = 6.02\,n$:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{q,\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is the *single most important formula* of Module 2. The form $6.02n + 1.76$ is what KTU expects in the answer script. Some textbooks write $6.02n + 4.77$ when the *input signal is Gaussian* (not sinusoidal) — know the difference.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulse Position Modulation (PPM) Time Displacement Relationship

**Given:** A PWM waveform with pulse width $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_a m(t)$ is differentiated.

**Step 1 — Output of the differentiator on a PWM pulse:**
The differentiator output is a *positive impulse* at the leading edge and a *negative impulse* at the trailing edge, with strength proportional to the input pulse's edge slope. For a rectangular PWM pulse of constant amplitude $A$:

$$
v_d(t) = A \cdot \delta(t - t_1) - A \cdot \delta(t - t_2)
$$

where $t_1$ = leading edge time, $t_2 = t_1 + \tau(t)$ = trailing edge time.

**Step 2 — Trigger the monostable on the trailing edge:**
The monostable fires at $t_2 = t_1 + \tau(t) = t_1 + \tau_0 + k_a m(t)$. The PPM pulse position is therefore:

$$
t_{ppm}(t) = t_{\text{ref}} + \tau_0 + k_a m(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Time displacement from nominal position:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta t = k_p \cdot m(t) \quad \text{where } k_p = k_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum displacement bound:**
To prevent pulse-position ambiguity (no overlap with adjacent pulses), the displacement is bounded by the available "slot":

$$
\vert \Delta t \vert_{\max} \leq \frac{T_s - \tau_0}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This bound is *critical* in KTU design problems — if violated, adjacent pulses will overlap and cause inter-symbol interference (ISI).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — PCM Transmission Bit Rate

**Given:** A message signal band-limited to $f_m$ Hz is sampled at $f_s = 2 f_m$ (Nyquist) and each sample is encoded with $n$ bits.

**Step 1 — Samples per second:**
Number of samples transmitted per second is $f_s$.

**Step 2 — Bits per sample:**
Each sample yields $n$ bits.

**Step 3 — Bit rate:**

$$
\boxed{R_b = n \cdot f_s = n \cdot 2 f_m \text{ bits/second}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum PCM transmission bandwidth (for NRZ line coding):**
The fundamental frequency of an NRZ bitstream of rate $R_b$ is $R_b/2$:

$$
\boxed{BW_{PCM} = \frac{R_b}{2} = n \cdot f_m \text{ Hz}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students confuse this with $BW = n f_s = 2 n f_m$. The correct answer is $n f_m$ for NRZ. For bipolar RZ (alternate mark inversion), the bandwidth is $R_b = 2 n f_m$. Always state the assumed line code in your answer.

---

### 3.5 KTU Numerical Type — Worked Example

**Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 4 \cos(2000\pi t)$ is transmitted using a PCM system. The signal is sampled at $f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$ and encoded with $n = 8$ bits per sample. Calculate:
(a) The bit rate $R_b$
(b) The minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ)
(c) The output SQNR in dB
(d) The quantization step size $\Delta$ if $V_{pp} = 8$ V

**Solution (step-by-step):**

**(a)** $R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64000$ bps = **64 kbps**

**(b)** $BW_{PCM} = R_b/2 = 32000$ Hz = **32 kHz**

**(c)** $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{q,\text{dB}} = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 =$ **49.92 dB**

**(d)** $L = 2^8 = 256$ levels
$\Delta = V_{pp}/L = 8/256 =$ **31.25 mV**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM), Pulse Width Modulation (PWM), and Pulse Position Modulation (PPM) on the basis of (i) varying parameter, (ii) noise immunity, and (iii) bandwidth requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Modulation Type | Varying Parameter | Noise Immunity | Bandwidth |
|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|-----------|
| **PAM** | Pulse amplitude | Poor (amplitude-based) | Lowest |
| **PWM (PDM)** | Pulse width/duration | Better than PAM | Higher than PAM |
| **PPM** | Pulse *position* (time) | Best of the three | Comparable to PWM |

In PAM, the amplitude of pulses is varied proportionally with the message signal, making it highly susceptible to channel noise. In PWM, the pulse width is varied while amplitude is held constant, improving noise immunity. PPM is derived from PWM by holding the pulse width constant and varying the position of the pulse within the sampling period, giving the best noise immunity among the three because information is encoded in time, not amplitude. (3 marks)

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_m
$$

The minimum theoretical sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Aliasing:** When a signal is sampled at a rate $f_s < 2 f_m$, the high-frequency components fold back into the lower baseband and become indistinguishable from the actual baseband content. This frequency-folding phenomenon is called **aliasing**, and it causes irreversible distortion.

**Prevention of aliasing:**
1. Use an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** at the input of the sampler to band-limit the message to $f_m$.
2. Ensure the sampling clock satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (a typical engineering choice is $f_s = 2.5\,f_m$ to $4\,f_m$ as a guard band). (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module 2 Internal Choice Convention:** Candidates answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

##### **(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the generation and demodulation of a Pulse Position Modulation (PPM) signal. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Generation of PPM:**

A PPM signal is not generated directly from the message. It is derived from a PWM signal using the following cascade (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Message + Ramp Comparator (PWM Generation):** The message signal $m(t)$ is fed to one input of a comparator; a sawtooth/ramp waveform of period $T_s$ is fed to the other. The comparator output is a PWM signal whose pulse width $\tau(t) = \tau_0 + k_a m(t)$.

2. **Differentiator:** The PWM signal passes through a differentiator (RC high-pass with small time constant) that produces positive and negative impulses at the leading and trailing edges of each PWM pulse.

3. **Monostable Multivibrator (MSMV):** The trailing-edge impulse triggers an MSMV that produces a fixed-width, fixed-amplitude pulse. The MSMV pulse is delayed from the clock reference by:

$$
t_{ppm} = t_{ref} + \tau_0 + k_a m(t)
$$

Hence, the **pulse position is modulated by $m(t)$**.

**Demodulation of PPM:**

1. **SR Flip-Flop (PPM → PWM):** The clock sets the flip-flop at $t_{ref}$, and the incoming PPM pulse resets it. The flip-flop output is a PWM signal whose width equals the time interval between clock and PPM pulse — i.e., $\tau = t_{ppm} - t_{ref} = \tau_0 + k_a m(t)$.

2. **Low-Pass Filter (PWM → Analog):** A low-pass filter averages the PWM pulse train. Since the average of a constant-amplitude pulse train is proportional to its width, the filter output is a DC-shifted replica of $m(t)$. A coupling capacitor removes the DC offset to recover the original $m(t)$. (7 marks)

---

##### **(b) A sinusoidal signal $m(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1.2\,\text{kHz}$ and quantized with $n = 10$ bits per sample. Compute (i) the bit rate, (ii) the Nyquist rate, (iii) the SQNR in dB, and (iv) the quantization step size if $V_{pp} = 10$ V. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the message frequency:**
The message is $m(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t) = 5 \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, so $2\pi f_m = 100\pi \Rightarrow f_m = 50$ Hz.

**(i) Bit rate:**
$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 10 \times 1200 =$ **12,000 bps = 12 kbps**

**(ii) Nyquist rate:**
$f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 50 =$ **100 Hz**
*(Note: the actual sampling rate $f_s = 1.2$ kHz is well above Nyquist.)*

**(iii) SQNR in dB (sinusoidal input):**
$\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{q,\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02(10) + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 =$ **61.96 dB**

**(iv) Quantization step size:**
$L = 2^{10} = 1024$ levels
$\Delta = V_{pp}/L = 10/1024 \approx$ **9.77 mV** (7 marks)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

##### **(a) Draw the block diagram of a PCM transmitter and receiver, and explain the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**PCM Transmitter (3 blocks — refer to Section 4.3):**

1. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** Band-limits the analog message $m(t)$ to $f_m$ Hz to satisfy the Nyquist criterion. Without this filter, out-of-band noise would alias into the baseband.
2. **Sample-and-Hold (S&H):** Samples the filtered $m(t)$ at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ and holds the voltage constant during the quantization interval. The "hold" action prevents ambiguity in the quantizer decision.
3. **Quantizer:** Maps the continuous-amplitude sample to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels. Introduces quantization error $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
4. **Encoder (Binary Coder):** Assigns a unique $n$-bit binary word to each quantization level. Output is a serial NRZ bitstream.

**PCM Receiver (3 blocks — refer to Section 4.4):**

1. **Regenerative Repeater + Bit Clock Recovery:** Cleans the bitstream (re-squares pulses, removes channel noise) and extracts the bit-timing clock using a PLL.
2. **Decoder (n-bit DAC):** Converts each $n$-bit word back to its corresponding discrete voltage level (reverse of the encoder).
3. **Reconstruction Low-Pass Filter:** Smooths the staircase PAM-like waveform back into the original analog message $\hat{m}(t) \approx m(t)$. (7 marks)

---

##### **(b) Explain the need for companding in PCM. Derive the µ-law and A-law companding characteristics. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Need for Companding:**

In uniform quantization, the SQNR is independent of signal amplitude. For small-amplitude signals (e.g., speech pauses, weak voice syllables), the absolute quantization noise may be comparable to the signal, producing poor perceptual quality. To overcome this, we use **non-uniform quantization**, where the step size $\Delta$ is small for small amplitudes (high SQNR for weak signals) and large for large amplitudes (avoiding quantizer overload). This is achieved via **companding** — a contraction of *compressing + expanding*.

**µ-Law Companding (used in North America / Japan):**

$$
F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)} \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert \leq 1
$$

with standard value $\mu = 255$.

**A-Law Companding (used in Europe / India):**

$$
F(x) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\
\dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1
\end{cases}
$$

with standard value $A = 87.6$.

**Operating Principle:**

1. **At the Transmitter:** The analog signal is passed through a *compressor* that maps small amplitudes to a wider code-space (using the µ-law or A-law curve above).
2. **At the Receiver:** An *expander* applies the inverse transformation, restoring the original dynamic range. The combined compress-expand is called **companding**.

**Practical Justification:** Companding improves the SQNR for low-amplitude signals by up to **24–30 dB** without increasing the bit rate, making it indispensable in **digital telephony (PCM G.711 standard)**. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 ESE Module 2:**
>
> 1. **Formula Selection (–1 to –2 marks):** Using $6.02n + 4.77$ instead of $6.02n + 1.76$ (or vice versa) without stating whether the input is sinusoidal or Gaussian.
> 2. **Nyquist vs. Practical Sampling:** Students write $f_s = 2f_m$ as the *answer* instead of stating "the minimum theoretical value is $2f_m$, and practical systems use $f_s \geq 2f_m$."
> 3. **PCM Block Diagram Sketches (–2 marks):** Missing the anti-aliasing filter at the transmitter input, or missing the clock-recovery block at the receiver. Both are *mandatory* per KTU valuation key.
> 4. **PPM Generation Confusion (–2 marks):** Claiming PPM is generated directly from the message. It is *always* derived from PWM via differentiator + MSMV.
> 5. **Companding Units (–1 mark):** Writing $\mu$-law formula with $\mu = 100$ or $A = 50$ instead of the standard values $\mu = 255$ and $A = 87.6$.
> 6. **Bit Rate vs. Baud Rate:** In PCM with NRZ, bit rate $R_b$ = baud rate. In RZ or multi-level coding, they differ. State the assumed line code.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PPM** is derived from PWM (via differentiator + MSMV) — never directly from the message.
- [x] PPM information is carried in **time displacement** $\Delta t = k_p m(t)$.
- [x] Demodulation of PPM uses an **SR flip-flop** (PPM→PWM) followed by a **low-pass filter** (PWM→analog).
- [x] **PCM** is a 3-step process: **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**.
- [x] **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ Hz (minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing).
- [x] **Number of Levels:** $L = 2^n$ where $n$ is bits/sample.
- [x] **Step Size:** $\Delta = V_{pp} / L$ (Volts).
- [x] **Max Quantization Error:** $\Delta/2$.
- [x] **Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power:** $N_q = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- [x] **SQNR (sinusoidal):** $\left(S/N\right)_q = (3/2) \cdot 4^n$ → in dB: $6.02n + 1.76$.
- [x] **Bit Rate:** $R_b = n f_s$ bps.
- [x] **NRZ PCM Bandwidth:** $R_b / 2 = n f_m$ Hz.
- [x] **Companding** = Compressing (Tx) + Expanding (Rx) — improves SQNR for weak signals.
- [x] **µ-Law** standard: $\mu = 255$ (USA, Japan). **A-Law** standard: $A = 87.6$ (Europe, India, KTU-expected default).
- [x] **Aliasing** is prevented by an **anti-aliasing LPF** at the input of the sampler.
- [x] PCM is the basis of **G.711 (64 kbps) telephony**, **CD audio (1411 kbps stereo)**, and **digital instrumentation buses**.
- [x] PPM excels in **IR remote control**, **optical wireless**, and **deep-space telemetry**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Modulation Schemes: BPSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Digital Modulation Schemes: BPSK # Module 2 – Digital Modulation Schemes: Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** is a *binary digital modulation scheme* in which the **phase of a sinusoidal carrier signal** is discretely varied between **two equally spaced values**, conventionally **0° (for binary '1')** and **180° (or π radians, for binary '0')**, while the amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain strictly constant. BPSK is the most robust of all M-ary PSK schemes because the two signals are antipodal — they are exact negative mirrors of each other in the signal space. The mathematically formal signal representation of a BPSK waveform over one bit interval $T_b$ is: $$s_i(t) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '1'} \\[2mm] -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '0'} \end{cases}$$ where $E_b$ is the **average transmitted energy per bit**, $T_b$ is the **bit duration**, and $f_c$ is the **carrier frequency**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Lighthouse Analogy"** — Imagine a lighthouse that flashes light toward the sea. Suppose the keeper decides to encode a Morse-style message: *one short flash* means "Yes" (bit 1), and *one flash in the opposite direction* (180° turned) means "No" (bit 0). The brightness (amplitude) and the rate of flashing (frequency) are kept identical. The *only* thing that changes is the **direction** of the light. A distant sailor with a narrow detector can easily tell which direction the beam came from, even in fog (noise), because the two signals are maximally distinct. BPSK works in exactly this way: the carrier's *phase* is the only parameter carrying the information, and the two phases are diametrically opposed to maximize noise immunity. ### 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Constants - **Carrier phase states used:** 2 (binary) - **Phase separation between symbols:** $\pi$ radians (180°) - **Bit duration:** $T_b$ seconds - **Bit rate:** $R_b = 1/T_b$ bits/second - **Symbol rate (Baud):** $R_s = R_b$ (since M = 2) - **Standard symbol energy:** $E_b$ Joules/bit - **Signal amplitude:** $A = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ Volts - **Bandwidth efficiency (theoretical max):** 1 bit/sec/Hz > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to be able to *derive* the BPSK signal expression, *draw* the modulator and coherent demodulator block diagrams, *plot* the constellation diagram, and *compute* the bit error probability under AWGN. All four of these skills are assessed in both Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and End Semester Examination (ESE). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and the "How" ### Why BPSK? (Motivation) - **Maximally distant signal points** on the constellation: the Euclidean distance between $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ is the *largest possible* for any binary scheme (given fixed energy), making it the **most noise-robust** binary modulation. - **Constant envelope** → resistant to non-linear amplifiers (suitable for satellite and deep-space links). - **Simplest possible digital modulation** → used as a benchmark against which all other schemes are compared. ### How BPSK Works? (Mechanism — Stepwise) 1. **Source encoding** — A binary message $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is generated at rate $R_b = 1/T_b$. 2. **Polar NRZ conversion** — Bits are mapped to voltages $+1$ (for '1') and $-1$ (for '0'), producing a polar Non-Return-to-Zero waveform $d(t)$. 3. **Carrier generation** — A local oscillator generates $c(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. 4. **Product modulation** — The polar NRZ data multiplies the carrier, *inverting its phase* whenever a '0' is encountered. Output: $s(t) = d(t) \cdot \sqrt{2E_b/T_b} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. 5. **Channel** — AWGN $n(t)$ with PSD $N_0/2$ is added. Received signal: $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$. 6. **Coherent demodulation** — At the receiver, $r(t)$ is multiplied by a locally generated, **phase-synchronized** carrier, then low-pass filtered, then sampled once per bit. 7. **Threshold detection** — Sample value $\geq 0$ → decide '1'; sample value $< 0$ → decide '0'. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-defining cheat sheet** for BPSK. Every line is a possible 3-mark direct or 7-mark derivation question. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Condition | |---|-----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | BPSK signal (bit '1') | $s_1(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \le t \le T_b$ | | 2 | BPSK signal (bit '0') | $s_2(t) = -\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \le t \le T_b$ | | 3 | Carrier amplitude | $A_c = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ | Volts | | 4 | Energy per bit | $E_b = \int_0^{T_b} s_i^2(t)\,dt$ | Joules | | 5 | Symbol energy (M-ary) | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ | Joules; for BPSK $M=2$ | | 6 | Bandwidth (null-to-null) | $BW = 2R_b = 2/T_b$ | Hz | | 7 | Bandwidth (main lobe) | $BW_{3dB} \approx R_b$ | Hz (raised-cosine, $\alpha=0$) | | 8 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b / BW = 0.5$ | bits/s/Hz (Nyquist) | | 9 | Euclidean distance | $d_{12} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Volts·√s | | 10 | Cross-correlation $\rho$ | $\rho = -1$ (antipodal) | Dimensionless | | 11 | SNR per bit relationship | $E_b/N_0$ | Dimensionless (dB scale) | | 12 | Bit Error Probability | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $0 \le P_b \le 0.5$ | | 13 | Alternate BER form | $P_b = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Equivalent to #12 | | 14 | Power Spectral Density | $S_{BPSK}(f) = \tfrac{E_b}{2}\!\left[\delta(f-f_c) + \delta(f+f_c)\right]$ for ideal | $W/Hz$ | | 15 | Continuous PSD (rectangular pulse) | $S_{BPSK}(f) = \dfrac{E_b}{2}\left[\text{sinc}^2((f-f_c)T_b) + \text{sinc}^2((f+f_c)T_b)\right]$ | $W/Hz$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Conversion:** $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. Many textbook errors arise from mixing $Q$ and erfc factors. The form $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ is the **KTU-preferred** form. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite communication (e.g., NASA's deep-space probes)** — BPSK is used on the *uplink* of many legacy satellite links due to its robustness against non-linear TWTA amplifiers. - **GPS Coarse/Acquisition (C/A) codes** — Originally BPSK-modulated onto the L1 carrier at 1575.42 MHz. - **Aeronautical telemetry & space research (ISRO telemetry links)** — BPSK is mandated for low-rate command channels. - **Biomedical implants (e.g., pacemakers)** — Ultra-low-power BPSK minimizes battery drain. - **RFID and NFC standards** — Simpler variants of BPSK are used in Gen-2 RFID tags. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy per Bit of the BPSK Signal > **Goal:** Show that the average transmitted energy per bit is $E_b$, given the signal definitions. **Step 1 — Write the signal for bit '1'.** The transmitted waveform during the interval $0 \le t \le T_b$ when the bit is '1' is: $$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the energy of $s_1(t)$ over one bit interval.** By definition of signal energy: $$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T_b} s_1^2(t)\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the signal.** $$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T_b} \left[\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\right]^2 dt$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the square.** $$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T_b} \dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$$ **Step 5 — Use the identity $\cos^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[1 + \cos(2\theta)\bigr]$.** $$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T_b} \dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\bigl[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)\bigr]\,dt$$ **Step 6 — Pull the constants out and split the integral.** $$E_1 = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\int_{0}^{T_b} dt \;+\; \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\int_{0}^{T_b}\cos(4\pi f_c t)\,dt$$ **Step 7 — Evaluate.** The first integral yields $T_b$. The second integral, when $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (the **narrowband assumption** standard in communication theory), integrates over many carrier cycles and tends to **zero**. $$E_1 = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\cdot T_b \;+\; 0 \;=\; E_b$$ **Step 8 — Repeat for $s_2(t)$.** By the same algebra (the minus sign squares away), $E_2 = E_b$. Hence the **average energy per bit is $E_b$**, and the amplitude normalization $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ is verified. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cross-Correlation Coefficient $\rho_{12} = -1$ > **Goal:** Demonstrate that BPSK symbols are *perfectly antipodal*, the geometric reason for its superior BER. **Step 1 — Define the normalized cross-correlation** between two signals $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$: $$\rho_{12} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{E_1 E_2}}\int_{0}^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_2(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the BPSK expressions** $s_2(t) = -s_1(t)$: $$\rho_{12} = \dfrac{1}{E_b}\int_{0}^{T_b} \bigl[s_1(t)\bigr]\bigl[-s_1(t)\bigr]\,dt = -\dfrac{1}{E_b}\int_{0}^{T_b} s_1^2(t)\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the integral as the energy** of $s_1(t)$, which we just proved equals $E_b$: $$\rho_{12} = -\dfrac{E_b}{E_b} = -1$$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** A correlation of $-1$ means the two signal vectors lie on *opposite sides* of the origin, **180° apart** in the signal space. This is the *maximum possible separation* for a binary scheme. The **Euclidean distance** between the two constellation points is therefore: $$d_{12} = \sqrt{2E_1(1 - \rho_{12})} = \sqrt{2E_b(1-(-1))} = \sqrt{4E_b} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$$ This maximum separation is the root cause of BPSK's superior noise immunity. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit Error Probability $P_b = Q\!\bigl(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\bigr)$ > **Goal:** Derive the famous BPSK BER expression under AWGN, the single most-tested formula in the KTU module. **Step 1 — Receiver model.** Let the received signal during one bit interval be: $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \qquad 0 \le t \le T_b$$ where $n(t)$ is **zero-mean AWGN** with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 2 — Correlator / matched filter output.** Multiply $r(t)$ by a locally generated reference $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (synchronized to the carrier) and integrate over $T_b$: $$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\phi(t)\,dt \;+\; \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi(t)\,dt$$ Define $r = s_i + n_c$, where $n_c$ is a Gaussian RV with **mean 0** and **variance** $\sigma_n^2 = N_0/2$. **Step 3 — Compute the signal component for $s_1(t)$.** $$s_1 = \int_0^{T_b}\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = \dfrac{2\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$$ Using the same identity and narrowband assumption as before: $$s_1 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\cdot\dfrac{T_b}{2} = \sqrt{E_b}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the signal component for $s_2(t) = -s_1(t)$.** $$s_2 = -\sqrt{E_b}$$ **Step 5 — Detection rule.** The receiver decides '1' if $r \ge 0$, else '0'. The two conditional PDFs of the decision variable are: $$f(r \mid \text{bit }1) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right]$$ $$f(r \mid \text{bit }0) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(r + \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Probability of error given bit '1' was sent.** The error occurs if $r < 0$: $$P(e \mid 1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} f(r \mid 1)\,dr = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right]dr$$ **Step 7 — Change of variable.** Let $u = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_b} - r}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}$, so $r = \sqrt{E_b} - u\sqrt{N_0/2}$ and $dr = -\sqrt{N_0/2}\,du$. When $r = -\infty$, $u = +\infty$; when $r = 0$, $u = \sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$. Therefore: $$P(e \mid 1) = \int_{+\infty}^{\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp(-u^2)\bigl(-\sqrt{N_0/2}\bigr)\,du$$ Flipping the limits: $$P(e \mid 1) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$$ **Step 8 — Recognize the Q-function.** By definition, $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}\,dt$. Hence: $$P(e \mid 1) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$$ **Step 9 — By symmetry**, $P(e \mid 0) = P(e \mid 1)$. Assuming equiprobable bits, the **average bit error probability** is: $$\boxed{\,P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)\,}$$ $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Reference Table The following pre-computed values of $P_b$ are **must-memorize** for the KTU ESE because they correspond to standard textbook examples. | $E_b/N_0$ (dB) | $E_b/N_0$ (linear) | $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ | $P_b = Q(\cdot)$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 1.000 | 1.414 | $7.87 \times 10^{-2}$ | | 3 | 1.995 | 1.998 | $2.29 \times 10^{-2}$ | | 6 | 3.981 | 2.822 | $2.39 \times 10^{-3}$ | | 9 | 7.943 | 3.986 | $3.36 \times 10^{-5}$ | | 10 | 10.00 | 4.472 | $3.87 \times 10^{-6}$ | | 12 | 15.85 | 5.629 | $9.06 \times 10^{-9}$ | > [!TIP] > **Memory hook for KTU exams:** To achieve $P_b \le 10^{-5}$, BPSK requires only about **9.6 dB** of $E_b/N_0$. Compare with BFSK (coherent) which needs ~12.6 dB, and BFSK (non-coherent) which needs ~13.4 dB. This 3-dB advantage is the textbook "BPSK is 3 dB better than BFSK" line you will write in the exam. --- # 4. This is the bandwidth figure universally used in KTU numerical problems unless a raised-cosine pulse shape with roll-off $\alpha$ is specified (in which case $BW = R_b(1+\alpha)$). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** *Define Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK). Write the mathematical expression for the BPSK waveform for binary digits '1' and '0' and sketch its constellation diagram.* **Model Answer:** Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier is shifted between **two discrete values**, namely **0° and 180°**, in synchronism with the binary data, while the amplitude and frequency of the carrier are held constant. The BPSK signal over a bit interval $T_b$ is given by: $$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b, \text{ for binary '1'}$$ $$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b, \text{ for binary '0'}$$ **Constellation Diagram:** Two points on the I-axis, located at $+\sqrt{E_b}$ (bit '1') and $-\sqrt{E_b}$ (bit '0'). The decision boundary is the Q-axis. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *State the expression for the probability of bit error of BPSK in an AWGN channel. Mention the value of $E_b/N_0$ required to achieve a probability of error of $10^{-5}$.* **Model Answer:** The bit error probability of coherent BPSK in AWGN is: $$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$$ For $P_b = 10^{-5}$: $$\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} = Q^{-1}(10^{-5}) \approx 4.265$$ $$\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0} \approx 18.19 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \approx 9.095 \;\;\text{(linear)} \;\;\approx\; 9.6 \text{ dB}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the expression for the BPSK transmitted signal and obtain the probability of bit error in an AWGN channel. State clearly the assumptions used. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A BPSK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps in an AWGN channel with two-sided noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. If the received bit energy is $E_b = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ J, calculate (i) the SNR per bit in dB, (ii) the probability of bit error, and (iii) the null-to-null bandwidth. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Derivation of BPSK signal and BER:** The binary data $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is first converted to a polar NRZ waveform $d(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ such that $d(t) = +1$ for bit '1' and $d(t) = -1$ for bit '0'. The carrier is $c(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The BPSK modulator multiplies these two: $$s(t) = d(t)\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Hence the two transmitted signals are: $$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Assumptions used:** 1. The channel adds **zero-mean AWGN** with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. 2. The local oscillator at the receiver is **perfectly phase-coherent** with the transmitter carrier. 3. The bit synchronization is **ideal**, so the sampler fires exactly at $t = T_b$. 4. The bits are **equiprobable** ($P(0) = P(1) = 1/2$). At the receiver, $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is correlated with $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over $T_b$, giving the decision variable: $$r = s_i + n_c$$ where $s_1 = +\sqrt{E_b}$, $s_2 = -\sqrt{E_b}$, and $n_c \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. The decision rule is $r \overset{1}{\underset{0}{\gtrless}} 0$. Using the standard Gaussian integration (as derived in Section 3.3 above), the average bit error probability is: $$\boxed{\,P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)\,}$$ **(b) Numerical problem:** **Given:** $R_b = 1 \times 10^6$ bps, $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz $\Rightarrow N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ W/Hz, $E_b = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ J. **(i) SNR per bit:** $$\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 10^{4} = 10{,}000$$ In dB: $$\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(10^{4}) = 40 \text{ dB}$$ **(ii) Probability of bit error:** $$\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{4}} = \sqrt{20{,}000} \approx 141.42$$ $$P_b = Q(141.42) \approx 0 \;\;(\text{effectively zero, } < 10^{-1000})$$ In KTU writing style: "Since $E_b/N_0$ is very large, $P_b$ is negligibly small; BPSK provides virtually error-free transmission under these conditions." **(iii) Null-to-null bandwidth:** $$BW = 2R_b = 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \text{ MHz}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of a BPSK signal. Show that the cross-correlation coefficient between the two BPSK signals is $-1$. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Compare BPSK with BFSK in terms of (i) bandwidth, (ii) bit error rate, (iii) noise immunity, and (iv) application. A BPSK transmitter sends data at 50 kbps over a channel of bandwidth 75 kHz. Determine whether the signal can be transmitted without ISI using a Nyquist filter. State the roll-off factor used. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Generation and detection of BPSK, and $\rho_{12} = -1$:** **Generation (Block Diagram):** The BPSK modulator consists of three blocks (refer Section 4.1): 1. **Polar NRZ encoder** — converts binary data $\{0, 1\}$ to $\{-1, +1\}$. 2. **Local oscillator** — generates $c(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. 3. **Product modulator (multiplier)** — multiplies NRZ and carrier to produce $s(t) = d(t)c(t)$. The product modulator is essentially a **balanced modulator**; whenever $d(t) = -1$, the output is the inverted carrier (180° phase shift), and when $d(t) = +1$, the output is the carrier with zero phase shift. **Detection (Block Diagram):** The coherent BPSK receiver (refer Section 4.2) consists of: 1. **Multiplier (correlator)** — multiplies the received signal $r(t)$ by locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. 2. **Integrate-and-dump filter (or low-pass filter)** — integrates over one bit period $T_b$. 3. **Sampler** — samples the integrator output at $t = T_b$. 4. **Threshold detector** — applies the rule $r \ge 0 \Rightarrow 1$ and $r < 0 \Rightarrow 0$. **Proof that $\rho_{12} = -1$:** The normalized cross-correlation coefficient between $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ is defined as: $$\rho_{12} = \dfrac{1}{E_b}\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_2(t)\,dt$$ Since $s_2(t) = -s_1(t)$: $$\rho_{12} = -\dfrac{1}{E_b}\int_0^{T_b} s_1^2(t)\,dt = -\dfrac{E_b}{E_b} = -1$$ A correlation of $-1$ indicates the two signals are **antipodal** — opposite in phase by 180°. $\blacksquare$ **(b) BPSK vs BFSK Comparison:** | Parameter | BPSK | BFSK (Coherent) | |-----------|------|-----------------| | Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $2\Delta f + R_b$ (typically $\ge 3R_b$) | | BER | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | | Noise immunity | Superior (3 dB gain) | Inferior | | Constant envelope | Yes | Yes | | Applications | Satellite, deep-space, GPS | Low-cost radio links, telemetry | **Numerical check for Nyquist criterion:** Given: $R_b = 50$ kbps, $BW = 75$ kHz. The minimum Nyquist bandwidth (raised-cosine with roll-off $\alpha$) is: $$BW = \dfrac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha) \cdot 2 = R_b(1+\alpha)$$ Wait — careful: For a baseband signal of bit rate $R_b$ using raised-cosine shaping, the **single-sided** baseband bandwidth is $\frac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha)$, and the **double-sided (RF) bandwidth** of the BPSK passband signal is: $$BW_{RF} = R_b(1+\alpha)$$ Setting $BW_{RF} = 75$ kHz: $$75{,}000 = 50{,}000 \cdot (1+\alpha)$$ $$1 + \alpha = 1.5 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = 0.5$$ **Conclusion:** Yes, the signal can be transmitted without ISI using a raised-cosine Nyquist filter with roll-off factor $\alpha = 0.5$ (a practical, commonly used value). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Confusing the Q-function and erfc factors:** The two BER forms $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ and $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ are **identical only if** you remember the conversion $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. Writing one of them in a derivation and the other in the final answer is a 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the narrowband assumption $f_c T_b \gg 1$** during the energy/correlation derivation. This assumption *justifies* dropping the cross-product cosine integral. Omitting this is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Using $|E_b/N_0|$ or $\lvert\rho_{12}\rvert$ inside a table cell** breaks the markdown table. Always write absolute values using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX or simply write "magnitude" in plain text. > 4. **Forgetting to convert $E_b/N_0$ to dB** in numerical problems. KTU marks are reserved for the final answer including the dB conversion. > 5. **Writing $s_2(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi)$** without explicitly justifying the equivalence to $-s_1(t)$ loses 1 mark in the derivation part. > 6. **Confusing "matched filter" with "correlator"** in the demodulator. They are mathematically equivalent for the rectangular pulse, but the KTU preferred answer is **"Integrate-and-dump (or matched filter) followed by sampler."** ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **BPSK = 2 phases** (0° and 180°), constant amplitude, constant frequency. - The two BPSK signals are **antipodal** ($\rho_{12} = -1$, $d_{12} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$). - Modulator: **Polar NRZ × Carrier** in a product modulator. - Demodulator: **Coherent correlator (multiplier + integrator + sampler + threshold)**. - Decision rule: $r \ge 0 \Rightarrow \hat b = 1$; $r < 0 \Rightarrow \hat b = 0$. - **BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. - **For $P_b = 10^{-5}$:** $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB. - **For $P_b = 10^{-3}$:** $E_b/N_0 \approx 6.8$ dB. - **Null-to-null bandwidth:** $BW = 2R_b = 2/T_b$. - **Constellation:** Two real-axis points symmetric about origin; decision boundary is the Q-axis. - **PSD peaks** at $\pm f_c$ with maximum value $E_b/2$ (ideal impulse case). - **BPSK is 3 dB better than BFSK (coherent)** because $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ vs $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$. - **Raised-cosine RF bandwidth:** $BW = R_b(1+\alpha)$. - **Applications:** Satellite uplinks, GPS C/A, deep-space probes, low-power biomedical telemetry, RFID. - **Assumptions to always state in derivations:** (1) AWGN, (2) ideal phase synchronization, (3) equiprobable bits, (4) $f_c T_b \gg 1$ narrowband condition. - **Conversion identities to memorize:** $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$, $\cos^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}[1+\cos(2\theta)]$, $E_b \leftrightarrow N_0$ (dB: multiply by 10 in the log). - **Drawing must include:** Modulator (3 blocks), Demodulator (4 blocks), Constellation (2 points on I-axis), PSD (peak at $\pm f_c$, nulls at $\pm n/T_b$).

topics.title: QPSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** QPSK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)** is a digital modulation scheme belonging to the M-ary PSK family (specifically M = 4) in which the phase of a constant-amplitude carrier signal is discretely varied in accordance with the digital information bits, while the amplitude and frequency remain unchanged. In QPSK, the input binary bit stream is grouped into pairs of two consecutive bits called **dibits**, and each dibit is mapped to one of four equally spaced carrier phases: $0, \frac{\pi}{2}, \pi, \text{ and } \frac{3\pi}{2}$ radians (or equivalently $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$ in the offset variant, $\pi/4$-DQPSK).

Mathematically, the QPSK modulated waveform is expressed as:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

where:
- $E_s$ = Energy per symbol
- $T_s$ = Symbol duration
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency
- $i$ = Symbol index mapped to the dibit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** QPSK is also referred to as **4-PSK** or **Quaternary PSK**. It is the most widely used modulation scheme in practical digital communication systems such as **WCDMA, LTE, Wi-Fi (802.11), DVB-S, and satellite telemetry**, because it offers twice the **spectral efficiency** of BPSK at the same bit error rate performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse of QPSK

Imagine a lighthouse (the carrier signal) that normally spins in a smooth circle. To send a message, instead of turning the light ON or OFF (which would be **BPSK**), the lighthouse keeper has been told: "You can stop the spinning and lock the beam in **one of four specific compass directions** — North, East, South, or West."

Each direction now represents **two letters** of information at once:
- **North (0°)** → 00
- **East (90°)** → 01
- **South (180°)** → 10
- **West (270°)** → 11

So with one "fixed beam" position (one symbol), the keeper transmits **two bits** instead of one. A sailor watching the lighthouse from far away only needs to know roughly which direction the beam is pointing, which is much easier to detect than trying to count a rapid ON/OFF flicker. This is the core philosophy of QPSK: **pack more information into a single symbol by using the phase plane's two orthogonal axes (I and Q).**

## 1.3 Why QPSK is a Cornerstone of Digital Communication

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Parameters (KTU Board Emphasis):**
> - **Number of bits per symbol** $\left(k = \log_2 M\right) = 2$ bits
> - **Number of constellation points** $M = 4$
> - **Phase separation** between adjacent symbols $= \frac{\pi}{2} = 90°$
> - **Symbol period** $T_s = 2T_b$ (twice the bit period)
> - **Symbol rate / Baud rate** $R_s = \frac{R_b}{2}$
> - **Bandwidth efficiency** = 2 bits/sec/Hz (double that of BPSK)
> - **BER for coherent QPSK** = $Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ (identical to BPSK in AWGN)

---

## 1.4 Bit-to-Symbol Mapping (Gray Coding)

The mapping of dibits to carrier phases typically uses **Gray coding** so that adjacent symbols (separated by 90°) differ in only **one bit position**, minimizing bit errors when noise pushes the received symbol to a neighboring point.

| Dibit $b_1 b_0$ | Phase $\phi_i$ | I-component | Q-component |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 0 | $45°$ | $+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| 0 1 | $135°$ | $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| 1 1 | $225°$ | $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ |
| 1 0 | $315°$ | $+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the QPSK constellation, always label the axes as **I (in-phase)** and **Q (quadrature)**, mark the four points on a unit circle at the correct angles, and explicitly mention that **Gray coding** is used to map bits to symbols. Examiners allocate marks for this labelling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of QPSK — Step by Step

The QPSK system can be analyzed through the following structured logic:

### Step 1: Serial-to-Parallel Conversion (Demultiplexing)
- The input binary data stream with bit rate $R_b$ is split into **two parallel bit streams** (the "odd" and "even" bits, or the I-bits and Q-bits) each running at $\frac{R_b}{2}$.
- This halves the symbol rate, allowing each symbol to span twice the time duration ($T_s = 2T_b$).

### Step 2: NRZ Encoding
- Each parallel bit stream is passed through a **Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ) encoder** that maps:
  - Binary **0** → voltage level $-1$
  - Binary **1** → voltage level $+1$
- These bipolar levels become the amplitudes of the I and Q baseband signals.

### Step 3: Orthogonal Carrier Modulation
- The **I-channel** baseband signal multiplies the in-phase carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- The **Q-channel** baseband signal multiplies the quadrature carrier $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
- The two modulated signals are summed to produce the QPSK waveform.

### Step 4: Why Two Orthogonal Carriers?
- $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ are **mutually orthogonal** over one symbol period $T_s$ when $f_c T_s$ is an integer.
- This orthogonality allows the **two bit streams to be transmitted simultaneously over the same bandwidth** without interfering with each other — the receiver can separate them using coherent detection.

### Step 5: Demodulation (Coherent Detection)
- The received signal is multiplied by locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
- After passing through **integrate-and-dump** or **matched filters** over one symbol period, the I and Q components are recovered.
- A **decision device** thresholds the I and Q samples to recover the original dibits, which are then **parallel-to-serial converted** to reconstruct the original bit stream.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind QPSK Design

- **Why 4 phases?** — Four phase states allow exactly $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits to be encoded, achieving a 2:1 compression of bandwidth occupancy.
- **Why 90° separation?** — Maximum Euclidean distance between adjacent constellation points, which minimizes symbol error probability.
- **How is bandwidth saved?** — Because two bits ride on one symbol of duration $T_s = 2T_b$, the required bandwidth (≈ symbol rate) is **halved** compared to BPSK at the same bit rate.
- **How is BER maintained?** — QPSK uses the **same energy per bit** as BPSK; the symbol energy is $E_s = 2E_b$, and the orthogonal structure preserves the same noise immunity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of QPSK numerical problems in KTU 2024 exams.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of symbols | $M = 2^k$ | $k$ = bits per symbol |
| Bits per symbol (QPSK) | $k = \log_2 4 = 2$ | Dimensionless |
| Symbol period | $T_s = k \cdot T_b = 2T_b$ | Seconds |
| Symbol rate / Baud rate | $R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{R_b}{2}$ | Symbols/sec (Baud) |
| Transmission bandwidth (ideal) | $B_T = R_s = \frac{R_b}{2}$ | Hz |
| Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = 2$ | bits/sec/Hz |
| Symbol energy | $E_s = k \cdot E_b = 2E_b$ | Joules |
| Carrier phase values | $\phi_i = (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}$ for $i=1,2,3,4$ | Radians |
| QPSK waveform | $s(t) = I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| Bit Error Probability (coherent QPSK) | $P_b = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| Symbol Error Probability | $P_s \approx 2Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$ for large SNR | Dimensionless |
| Relationship: $P_s$ vs $P_b$ | $P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2}$ | For Gray coding |
| Power Spectral Density (raised cosine, $\alpha$) | $G(f) = E_b \cdot \text{sinc}^2(fT_b)$ for baseband | W/Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When using $P_s = 2Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$, the $\sqrt{E_s/N_0}$ argument is in terms of **symbol** energy, not bit energy. A common mistake is to substitute $E_b$ instead of $2E_b$ in this expression, leading to a wrong BER. Always check whether the formula uses $E_s$ or $E_b$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4G LTE / 5G NR:** QPSK is the **lowest-order modulation** used in the downlink/uplink PUSCH/PDSCH channels, providing robust performance for users near cell edges.
- **Satellite Communication (DVB-S2):** QPSK is the standard for low-SNR reception scenarios.
- **Cable Modems / Wi-Fi (legacy 802.11b):** Uses QPSK as one of the mandatory data rates.
- **GPS Civilian Signal (L1 C/A):** Uses BPSK, but modernized GPS (L5) and Galileo use QPSK or higher.
- **Radar and Telemetry:** QPSK allows simultaneous data transmission over a single narrowband channel.

## 2.5 Comparison: QPSK vs BPSK

| Property | BPSK | QPSK |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Number of phases | 2 | 4 |
| Phase separation | $180°$ | $90°$ |
| Bandwidth | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ |
| Spectral efficiency | 1 bit/s/Hz | 2 bits/s/Hz |
| BER in AWGN | $Q\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $Q\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ |
| Power efficiency | Identical | Identical |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate (I/Q modulation) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the QPSK Waveform

Starting from the generalized M-ary PSK expression and applying it to M = 4:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

For QPSK, the four allowed phase values are:

$$
\phi_i = (2i - 1)\frac{\pi}{4}, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

This yields the four phase angles $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$.

Now we expand the cosine using the identity $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \left[\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(\phi_i) - \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(\phi_i)\right]
$$

Define the in-phase and quadrature amplitudes for each symbol:

$$
I_i = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(\phi_i), \quad Q_i = \sqrt{E_s}\sin(\phi_i)
$$

The QPSK signal can then be written compactly as:

$$
s_i(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_s}}\left[I_i\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_i\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]
$$

The I and Q values for the four symbols (normalized by $\sqrt{E_s}$) are:

| $i$ | $\phi_i$ | $\cos\phi_i$ | $\sin\phi_i$ | $I_i / \sqrt{E_s}$ | $Q_i / \sqrt{E_s}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $45°$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $+0.707$ | $+0.707$ |
| 2 | $135°$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-0.707$ | $+0.707$ |
| 3 | $225°$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-0.707$ | $-0.707$ |
| 4 | $315°$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $+0.707$ | $-0.707$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Each QPSK symbol is the **vector sum of two BPSK-like signals** — one on the in-phase (I) axis and one on the quadrature (Q) axis. The receiver separates the two BPSK streams independently and reassembles them.

## 3.2 Derivation of Bit Error Probability for QPSK

Consider the received signal in the presence of AWGN:

$$
r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)
$$

The receiver correlates $r(t)$ with the two orthonormal basis functions:

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The outputs of the two correlators are:

$$
r_I = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_s} + n_I
$$

$$
r_Q = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_s} + n_Q
$$

where $n_I$ and $n_Q$ are independent zero-mean Gaussian random variables with variance $\frac{N_0}{2}$.

The total noise variance on the I-Q plane is:

$$
\sigma^2 = \sigma_I^2 + \sigma_Q^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} + \frac{N_0}{2} = N_0
$$

The decision region for each symbol is a quadrant of the I-Q plane. The minimum distance from any constellation point to the decision boundary is:

$$
d_{min} = \sqrt{E_s}
$$

The probability that a given noise sample pushes the received vector across the decision boundary is:

$$
P_s = 1 - P(\text{correct}) \approx 2Q\left(\frac{d_{min}}{\sigma}\right) = 2Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

Substituting $E_s = 2E_b$:

$$
P_s = 2Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

For Gray-coded constellations, symbol errors almost always flip only **one** of the two constituent bits, so:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2} = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

This shows the key result: **QPSK has the same BER as BPSK in AWGN, but uses half the bandwidth.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Bandwidth and Spectral Efficiency

The QPSK signal can be expressed as a sum of two BPSK signals:

$$
s(t) = b_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - b_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $b_I(t)$ and $b_Q(t)$ are independent $\pm 1$ NRZ waveforms of duration $T_s$.

The PSD of a random NRZ bipolar signal is:

$$
G_b(f) = T_b \cdot \text{sinc}^2(fT_b)
$$

Because $b_I(t)$ and $b_Q(t)$ are independent and have PSD $G_{b_I}(f) = T_s \cdot \text{sinc}^2(fT_s)$, the baseband equivalent PSD of QPSK is:

$$
G_{QPSK}(f) = G_{b_I}(f) + G_{b_Q}(f) = 2T_s \cdot \text{sinc}^2(fT_s) = T_b \cdot \text{sinc}^2(fT_b \cdot \frac{1}{2})
$$

The null-to-null bandwidth of the QPSK signal (rectangular pulse) is:

$$
B_{null} = \frac{2}{T_s} = \frac{1}{T_b} = R_b
$$

Thus, compared to BPSK which has a null-to-null bandwidth of $2R_b$, **QPSK uses half the bandwidth** at the same bit rate.

The spectral efficiency (in bits/sec/Hz) is:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{R_b}{R_s} = \frac{R_b}{R_b/2} = 2 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A QPSK system transmits data at a bit rate of $R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps}$. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 70 \text{ MHz}$ and the received $E_b/N_0 = 9 \text{ dB}$. Calculate:
(a) The symbol rate and symbol duration.
(b) The transmission bandwidth (null-to-null).
(c) The bit error probability.

**Solution:**

(a) For QPSK, $k = 2$ bits/symbol. Therefore:

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/sec} = 1 \text{ Mbaud}
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1 \mu s
$$

(b) The null-to-null bandwidth is:

$$
B_T = \frac{2}{T_s} = 2R_s = 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \text{ MHz}
$$

Alternatively, $B_T = R_b$ for the rectangular-pulse QPSK main lobe.

(c) Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear scale:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{9/10} = 10^{0.9} \approx 7.943
$$

Calculate the argument of the Q-function:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 7.943} = \sqrt{15.886} \approx 3.986
$$

From standard Q-function tables (or calculation):

$$
Q(3.986) \approx 3.39 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Therefore, $P_b \approx 3.39 \times 10^{-5}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Answer Style:** Always present intermediate values and state the final result with units or in scientific notation. The $Q(3.986)$ value can be looked up from a Q-table or computed using `Q(x) = 0.5 \cdot \text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 QPSK Transmitter Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Data Input] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C1[I-channel NRZ Encoder]
    B --> C2[Q-channel NRZ Encoder]
    C1 --> D1[Multiplier X I]
    C2 --> D2[Multiplier X Q]
    E1[Local Oscillator cos wc t] --> D1
    E2[Local Oscillator sin wc t] --> D2
    D1 --> F[Adder / Summer]
    D2 --> F
    F --> G[Bandpass Filter]
    G --> H[Channel AWGN]
    H --> I[QPSK Signal Output to Channel]
```

## 4.2 QPSK Receiver Block Diagram (Coherent Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Signal r t] --> B[Bandpass Filter]
    B --> C1[Multiplier X cos wc t]
    B --> C2[Multiplier X sin wc t]
    D1[Local Osc cos wc t] --> C1
    D2[Local Osc sin wc t] --> C2
    C1 --> E1[Integrate over Ts]
    C2 --> E2[Integrate over Ts]
    E1 --> F1[Sampler and Threshold]
    E2 --> F2[Sampler and Threshold]
    F1 --> G[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Binary Data Output]
```

## 4.3 QPSK Constellation Diagram (Decision Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Constellation Plane
        direction TB
        subgraph Q_Axis
            QA[Q negative] --- QB[Q zero] --- QC[Q positive]
        end
        subgraph Decision_Regions
            DR1[Quadrant I: 00]
            DR2[Quadrant II: 01]
            DR3[Quadrant III: 11]
            DR4[Quadrant IV: 10]
        end
        P1((45 degrees 00)) --- DR1
        P2((135 degrees 01)) --- DR2
        P3((225 degrees 11)) --- DR3
        P4((315 degrees 10)) --- DR4
    end
```

## 4.4 QPSK Signal Generation Flow (Mathematical Signal Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Bits b1 b2 b3 b4 b5 b6] --> B[Group into dibits]
    B --> C{Map to phase per Gray code}
    C --> D[Generate I amplitude plus or minus sqrt Es by 2]
    C --> E[Generate Q amplitude plus or minus sqrt Es by 2]
    D --> F[Multiply by cos wc t]
    E --> G[Multiply by sin wc t]
    F --> H[Subtract and sum]
    G --> H
    H --> I[QPSK Waveform s t]
```

## 4.5 BER vs $E_b/N_0$ Performance Comparison Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[QPSK] --> B[BER equal to Q root 2 Eb N0]
    C[BPSK] --> B
    D[8 PSK] --> E[BER worse at same Eb N0]
    F[16 QAM] --> E
    B --> G[Coincident curve BPSK and QPSK]
    E --> H[Higher M PSK curves degrade]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid diagram above conveys the architectural relationships between functional blocks. The actual constellation points, sinusoidal waveforms, and noise realizations are best visualized on a board or simulation tool (e.g., MATLAB's `comm.QPSKModulator` or Python's `commpy` library).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define QPSK. Why is it preferred over BPSK in bandwidth-limited systems? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK) is a **M-ary phase modulation** scheme with $M = 4$ in which the input binary data is grouped into **dibits** and each dibit modulates the phase of a constant-envelope carrier to one of four equally spaced values: $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$ (or $0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$ in alternate mapping).

**Why preferred over BPSK (1 mark each):**
1. **Bandwidth efficiency:** QPSK transmits 2 bits per symbol, so at the same bit rate, its **required bandwidth is half** that of BPSK. Spectral efficiency doubles to 2 bits/sec/Hz.
2. **Same BER performance:** QPSK achieves the **same bit error probability** as BPSK in AWGN ($P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$), so no performance penalty is paid for the bandwidth saving.
3. **Constant envelope:** QPSK has a constant amplitude, making it suitable for **nonlinear amplifiers** (e.g., satellite transponders).

---

### Question 2: List the four possible carrier phases used in QPSK and express the QPSK waveform mathematically. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four allowed phase values in QPSK (for dibit-to-phase mapping) are:

$$
\phi_1 = 0, \quad \phi_2 = \frac{\pi}{2}, \quad \phi_3 = \pi, \quad \phi_4 = \frac{3\pi}{2}
$$

(or equivalently $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$ for Gray-coded mapping).

The QPSK waveform is given by:

$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

Expanding:

$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\left[\cos\phi_i \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \sin\phi_i \cdot \sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]
$$

The dibits are mapped to phases using Gray coding: $00 \to 45°$, $01 \to 135°$, $11 \to 225°$, $10 \to 315°$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of QPSK signal. Derive the expression for the QPSK waveform. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

In the QPSK **transmitter**, the binary input is split by a **serial-to-parallel converter** into two bit streams: the in-phase bits $b_I(t)$ and the quadrature bits $b_Q(t)$, each operating at half the bit rate. Each stream is NRZ-encoded to bipolar $\pm 1$ levels, then multiplied by the in-phase carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the quadrature carrier $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ respectively. The two modulated outputs are summed to form the QPSK signal.

In the **receiver**, the noisy QPSK signal is multiplied by locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (assumed phase-coherent). The products are integrated over one symbol period $T_s$, sampled at the end of the symbol, and threshold-detected to recover $b_I$ and $b_Q$. A parallel-to-serial converter reconstructs the original binary stream.

**Mathematical Derivation (4 Marks):**

The QPSK signal can be expressed as:

$$
s(t) = b_I(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - b_Q(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $b_I(t), b_Q(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ are the NRZ-encoded I and Q bit streams.

Rewriting in the standard phase form:

$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i\right)
$$

where $\phi_i = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{b_Q}{b_I}\right)$ takes the four values $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$ for the dibit combinations.

The bit error probability for coherent QPSK detection in AWGN is:

$$
P_b = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For a QPSK system operating at a bit rate of $R_b = 4$ Mbps with a carrier frequency of $f_c = 1$ GHz:
- (i) Find the symbol rate and symbol duration.
- (ii) Calculate the minimum transmission bandwidth.
- (iii) Find the spectral efficiency.
- (iv) If $E_b/N_0 = 10$, determine the bit error probability. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Symbol rate and symbol duration (2 Marks):**

For QPSK, $k = 2$ bits/symbol.

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{2} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/sec} = 2 \text{ Mbaud}
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \mu s
$$

**(ii) Minimum transmission bandwidth (2 Marks):**

For a rectangular-pulse QPSK signal, the null-to-null bandwidth is:

$$
B_T = R_s = 2 \text{ MHz}
$$

(The ideal Nyquist bandwidth equals the symbol rate.)

**(iii) Spectral efficiency (1 Mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{4 \text{ Mbps}}{2 \text{ MHz}} = 2 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}
$$

**(iv) Bit error probability (2 Marks):**

Convert $E_b/N_0 = 10$ (already in linear, as the problem gives a ratio, not dB).

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472
$$

From Q-function tables:

$$
P_b = Q(4.472) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the symbol rate with the bit rate in QPSK problems. Since QPSK carries **2 bits per symbol**, the symbol rate is **half** the bit rate. Writing $R_s = R_b$ instead of $R_s = R_b/2$ is a common 2-mark deduction. Similarly, students forget that the **null-to-null** bandwidth is $2R_s$ and the **minimum (Nyquist)** bandwidth is $R_s$. Read the question wording carefully.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the constellation diagram of QPSK. Explain Gray coding and justify why it is used in QPSK. Compare QPSK with BPSK in terms of bandwidth, spectral efficiency, and BER. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Constellation Diagram (2 Marks):**

The QPSK constellation consists of **four points** equally spaced on a unit circle in the I-Q plane. Using Gray coding, the four dibits are mapped to phases as follows:

- $00 \to 45°$: $I = +0.707, Q = +0.707$
- $01 \to 135°$: $I = -0.707, Q = +0.707$
- $11 \to 225°$: $I = -0.707, Q = -0.707$
- $10 \to 315°$: $I = +0.707, Q = -0.707$

The decision regions are the **four quadrants** of the I-Q plane, with boundaries along the I and Q axes.

**Gray Coding Explanation (2 Marks):**

Gray coding is a binary-to-symbol mapping in which **adjacent constellation points** (separated by 90° in QPSK) differ in **only one bit position**. For example, the symbols at $45°$ (00) and $135°$ (01) differ only in the first bit. This ensures that if noise causes a symbol to be decoded as an adjacent symbol (the most likely error), only **one bit error** occurs instead of two. This reduces the bit error rate to approximately $P_b = P_s/2$.

**Comparison QPSK vs BPSK (3 Marks):**

| Property | BPSK | QPSK |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth (null-to-null) | $2R_b$ | $R_b$ |
| Spectral efficiency | 1 bit/s/Hz | 2 bits/s/Hz |
| BER in AWGN | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ |
| Phase states | 2 | 4 |

**Conclusion:** QPSK achieves **twice the spectral efficiency of BPSK** at the **same BER performance**, but requires more complex I/Q modulator and demodulator hardware.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A QPSK system uses a carrier frequency of $3.6$ GHz and a bit rate of $2.048$ Mbps. The system operates in an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB.
- (i) Determine the symbol period and the bandwidth required.
- (ii) Calculate the bit error rate.
- (iii) Find the spectral efficiency.
- (iv) What happens to the BER if we replace QPSK with 8-PSK at the same $E_b/N_0$? Justify. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Symbol period and bandwidth (2 Marks):**

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^6}{2} = 1.024 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/sec}
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{1.024 \times 10^6} \approx 0.977 \mu s
$$

$$
B_T = R_s = 1.024 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(ii) Bit error rate (2 Marks):**

Convert $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB to linear:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{0.7} \approx 5.012
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 5.012} = \sqrt{10.024} \approx 3.166
$$

$$
P_b = Q(3.166) \approx 7.74 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**(iii) Spectral efficiency (1 Mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{2.048 \text{ Mbps}}{1.024 \text{ MHz}} = 2 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}
$$

**(iv) Effect of replacing QPSK with 8-PSK (2 Marks):**

If we replace QPSK with 8-PSK, the **bit error probability will increase (i.e., the system will perform worse)** at the same $E_b/N_0$. This is because:

- 8-PSK packs 3 bits per symbol, so the **Euclidean distance between adjacent constellation points decreases** (from 90° to 45° spacing).
- The symbol energy is now spread over 3 bits, so the effective $E_b$ is the same, but the **decision regions are smaller**, making the system more sensitive to noise.
- The BER formula for 8-PSK is approximately $P_b \approx \frac{2}{3}Q\left(\sqrt{2E_b \sin^2(\pi/8)/N_0}\right)$, which gives a higher error probability than QPSK for the same $E_b/N_0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (BER Pitfall):** When calculating QPSK BER, some students use $E_s$ instead of $E_b$ in the Q-function argument, which produces an answer **2× too small**. Always substitute $E_b$ in the **QPSK BER formula** and remember that $E_s = 2E_b$ for QPSK. Also, when the problem gives $E_b/N_0$ in dB, convert to linear **first** before computing the Q-function.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- ✅ QPSK stands for **Quadrature Phase Shift Keying**; also called **4-PSK** or **Quaternary PSK**.
- ✅ **2 bits per symbol** ($k = 2$); **4 carrier phases** equally spaced by **$90°$**.
- ✅ Symbol period $T_s = 2T_b$; Symbol rate $R_s = R_b / 2$.
- ✅ Bandwidth (null-to-null) $= 2R_s = R_b$; **half** of BPSK bandwidth.
- ✅ Spectral efficiency $\eta = 2$ bits/sec/Hz; **double** that of BPSK.
- ✅ BER in AWGN: $P_b = Q\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ — **identical** to BPSK.
- ✅ Uses **two orthogonal carriers**: $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
- ✅ **Gray coding** minimizes bit errors — adjacent symbols differ in only **one** bit.
- ✅ Transmitter structure: **S/P → NRZ → Mixers → Summer**.
- ✅ Receiver structure: **Mixers → Integrators → Sampler → Threshold → P/S**.
- ✅ Symbol energy $E_s = 2E_b$ (because 2 bits are transmitted per symbol).
- ✅ QPSK is widely used in **WCDMA, LTE, Wi-Fi, DVB-S, satellite comms**.
- ✅ Bandwidth efficiency is QPSK's **biggest advantage**; no BER penalty.
- ✅ For $E_b/N_0$ in dB, convert to linear: $\text{linear} = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
- ✅ Standard Q-function values to remember: $Q(2) \approx 0.0228$, $Q(3) \approx 0.00135$, $Q(4) \approx 3.17 \times 10^{-5}$.
- ✅ Phase mapping (Gray): $00 \to 45°$, $01 \to 135°$, $11 \to 225°$, $10 \to 315°$.
- ✅ QPSK is **not** the same as OQPSK (Offset QPSK), which shifts Q bits by half a symbol to reduce envelope fluctuations.
- ✅ I and Q signals are **independent** and can be demodulated separately by coherent detection.
- ✅ Channel capacity (Shannon) limit: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — QPSK approaches this at high SNR.
- ✅ Practical applications: LTE control channels, satellite telemetry, cable modems, GPS modernization.
- ✅ **Higher M-ary PSK (8-PSK, 16-PSK) → higher spectral efficiency but worse BER** at same $E_b/N_0$.
- ✅ **QPSK vs QAM:** QAM is a related scheme using both **amplitude and phase** variation; QPSK is essentially a special case of QAM (4-QAM).
- ✅ KTU Board Loves: numerical problems on $R_s$, $B_T$, $\eta$, and $P_b$.
- ✅ KTU Board Avoids: deep receiver synchronization analysis (saves it for advanced courses).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for QPSK phase angles: "Never Eat Soggy Waffles"** → $0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$ (N-E-S-W = North-East-South-West).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BFSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** BFSK # 1. BFSK — Binary Frequency Shift Keying ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK)** is a digital modulation scheme in which the binary information sequence modulates the **frequency** of a sinusoidal carrier wave, while its amplitude and phase are held constant. The modulator switches between two pre-assigned carrier frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ depending on whether the input bit is a logical **'1' (mark)** or logical **'0' (space)**. The mathematical representation of a BFSK signal over a bit interval $0 \le t \le T_b$ is: $$s_i(t) = \begin{cases} s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t + \theta_1), & \text{for bit } 1 \\ s_2(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t + \theta_2), & \text{for bit } 0 \end{cases}$$ where $E_b$ is the **energy per bit**, $T_b$ is the **bit duration**, and $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are arbitrary carrier phases. The factor $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ normalizes the energy of the symbol so that the energy in the bit interval $T_b$ is exactly $E_b$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BFSK is a **constant-envelope** modulation scheme. This property makes it highly immune to **non-linear distortion** and **amplitude fading**, which is why BFSK (and its variants like GMSK in GSM) is preferred in **fading channels and wireless mobile communications**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are communicating with a friend in a noisy marketplace. Instead of speaking loudly (which would distort), you decide to use **two different whistle pitches**: - A **high-pitched whistle** 🐦 for the message *"YES / 1"*. - A **low-pitched whistle** 🐧 for the message *"NO / 0"*. The *loudness* (amplitude) of your whistle stays exactly the same — only the **pitch (frequency)** changes to carry the information. This is precisely how **BFSK** works: information rides on *frequency variations*, not amplitude. A listener only needs to detect the *pitch* to decode your message, making it robust against background noise that tends to corrupt loudness more than pitch. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in BFSK | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Engineering Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Carrier Frequency 1 (Mark) | $f_1$ | **Hz** | | Carrier Frequency 2 (Space) | $f_2$ | **Hz** | | Bit Energy | $E_b$ | **Joules** | | Bit Duration | $T_b$ | **Seconds** | | Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f$ | **Hz** | | Modulation Index | $h$ | **Dimensionless** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The midpoint frequency $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$ is the **nominal carrier**, and the **frequency separation** is $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$. These two parameters are the most important design knobs in any BFSK system. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — BFSK ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Flow The BFSK modulator and demodulator function through a clear sequence of operations: 1. **Input Bit Stream Ingestion:** A binary sequence $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ is fed to the modulator at a bit rate $R_b = 1/T_b$ bits/sec. 2. **Frequency Mapping Logic:** A frequency selector chooses: - $f_1$ if $b_k = 1$ - $f_2$ if $b_k = 0$ 3. **Carrier Synthesis:** A Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) or Direct Digital Synthesizer (DDS) generates $\cos(2\pi f_i t)$ scaled by $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$. 4. **Channel Transmission:** The continuous-phase BFSK signal $s(t)$ is transmitted through an **AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise)** channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. 5. **Receiver Front-End:** The received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is filtered and demodulated using either a **coherent** or **non-coherent** detector. 6. **Decision Device:** The detector computes a likelihood metric and decides in favor of whichever signal ($s_1$ or $s_2$) is more probable given $r(t)$. > [!NOTE] > **Continuous Phase BFSK (CPBFSK):** In practice, BFSK is almost always generated with **continuous phase** transitions between bits to prevent spectral splatter. Discontinuous-phase FSK is a theoretical construct that is rarely used in real hardware. ## 2.2 Coherent vs Non-Coherent BFSK | Feature | Coherent BFSK | Non-Coherent BFSK | |---|---|---| | Reference signal | **Required** (needs phase lock) | **Not required** | | Demodulator | Correlator / matched filter | Envelope detector / discriminator | | Complexity | High | **Low** | | Power efficiency | **Better** ($P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$) | Slightly worse | | Use case | High-performance links, satellite | FSK modems, RFM69, low-cost IoT | ## 2.3 Orthogonality Condition — A Key Design Criterion Two BFSK signals $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ are said to be **orthogonal** over $[0, T_b]$ if their **time-domain inner product** equals zero: $$\int_{0}^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_2(t)\,dt = 0$$ Working out this integral, the orthogonality condition becomes: $$|f_1 - f_2| = \frac{n}{T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ - For **coherent BFSK** with $\theta_1 = \theta_2$, the **minimum** separation is $n = 1/2$: $$|f_1 - f_2|_{\text{coh, min}} = \frac{1}{2T_b}$$ - For **non-coherent BFSK** (different phases), the **minimum** separation is $n = 1$: $$|f_1 -
