Bent-pipe (transparent, 95% of all sats) vs. Regenerative (baseband processing, on-board routing). - **Transponder signal chain** = LNA → Down-Converter → Input Mux → HPA (TWTA/SSPA) → Output Mux. - **TWTA** = vacuum tube, high power, wider bandwidth, ~60% efficient. **SSPA** = solid-state, more linear, longer life. - **FSPL formula** = $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ — gives **~196 dB** for typical C-band GEO uplink. - **Friis** = $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_p$ (all in dB). - **EIRP** = $P_t + G_t$ (dBW). **G/T** = $G_r - 10\log_{10}T_{sys}$ (dB/K). - **Link budget** = $C/N = \text{EIRP} + G/T - L_p - k - B$ (every term in dB or dB-equivalent). - **Slant range** = $\sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\theta}$, max ~41,000 km at edge of GEO visibility ($\theta = 81.3°$). - **Boltzmann constant** $k = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz. - **Frequency inversion**: 6 GHz uplink → 4 GHz downlink in C-band; 14 GHz → 12 GHz in Ku-band. - **Three segments** of any satellite system: **Space**, **Ground**, **Control (TT&C)**. - **Why GEO is preferred for broadcast**: stationary, no Doppler, single-satellite hemispheric coverage.

topics.title: Kepler's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kepler's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Communication Techniques (PEEBT751)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the study of **Kepler's Laws** belongs to the broader module on **Satellite Orbits and Orbital Mechanics**. These laws describe the fundamental kinematic behaviour of celestial bodies (planets and artificial satellites) under the central gravitational pull of a primary body (the Sun or the Earth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Kepler's Laws are three empirical laws of planetary motion, formulated by the German astronomer Johannes Kepler in the early 17th century, that describe the geometry, kinematics, and dynamics of orbital motion of one body around another. In satellite communications, these laws govern the design of satellite orbits, the calculation of satellite positions, and the determination of orbital periods.*

## 1.2 The Three Laws — Overview

| Law | Common Name | Physical Statement |
|-----|-------------|-------------------|
| **1st Law** | Law of Orbits (Law of Ellipses) | The orbit of a planet/satellite is an **ellipse** with the primary body (Sun/Earth) at one of the two foci. |
| **2nd Law** | Law of Areas | The line joining the satellite and the primary body sweeps out **equal areas in equal intervals of time**. |
| **3rd Law** | Law of Harmonies (Law of Periods) | The square of the orbital period is **directly proportional** to the cube of the semi-major axis of the orbit. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Swing" Analogy:**
> Imagine a child sitting on a swing. As the swing moves back and forth, the chain is *shortest* when the child is at the lowest point (closest to the pivot) and *longest* at the highest points. The child moves **fastest** at the lowest point and **slowest** at the highest points.
>
> Replace the swing with a satellite and the pivot point with the Earth. This is exactly what Kepler's Laws describe:
> - **1st Law:** The satellite traces an elliptical path, with Earth sitting off-centre at one *focus*.
> - **2nd Law:** The satellite "sweeps" a larger area when it is close to Earth (moving fast) and a smaller area when it is far (moving slow) — but the *time* taken is the same.
> - **3rd Law:** Bigger orbits mean *much* longer travel times. A satellite that is twice as far takes about 2.83 times longer to complete one revolution.

## 1.4 Standard Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Physical Constants (used in orbital mechanics):**
> - **Universal Gravitational Constant:** $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$
> - **Mass of Earth:** $M_E = 5.972 \times 10^{24} \, \text{kg}$
> - **Earth's Standard Gravitational Parameter:** $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
> - **Mean Radius of Earth:** $R_E = 6371 \, \text{km}$
> - **Sidereal Day (Earth's rotation period):** $T_E = 86164 \, \text{s} \approx 23.934 \, \text{hours}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Law

### 2.1.1 Kepler's First Law — The Law of Orbits
- **Geometric statement:** A satellite (or planet) moves in an **elliptical orbit** with the central body located at **one of the two foci**, *not* at the geometric centre.
- **Why elliptical?** In a two-body gravitational system, the closed bound orbits are conic sections (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola). Closed orbits are *always* ellipses.
- **Key orbital parameters derived from this law:**
  - **Semi-major axis ($a$):** Half the longest diameter of the ellipse.
  - **Semi-minor axis ($b$):** Half the shortest diameter.
  - **Eccentricity ($e$):** A measure of how "stretched" the ellipse is. $0 \le e < 1$ for a closed orbit.
  - **Perigee ($r_p$):** Closest point to Earth (lowest altitude).
  - **Apogee ($r_a$):** Farthest point from Earth (highest altitude).

### 2.1.2 Kepler's Second Law — The Law of Areas
- **Kinematic statement:** The **areal velocity** $\frac{dA}{dt}$ is **constant** throughout the orbit.
- **Why it works:** This is a direct consequence of the **conservation of angular momentum** in a central force field (gravitational force is a *central* force, always directed toward the focus).
- **Engineering implication:** A satellite moves **fastest at perigee** and **slowest at apogee** — this is the key principle behind the design of **Hohmann transfer orbits** used for satellite station-keeping.

### 2.1.3 Kepler's Third Law — The Law of Harmonies
- **Dynamic statement:** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.
- **Mathematical form:** $T^2 \propto a^3 \implies T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3$
- **Engineering utility:** Used to compute the orbital period of any satellite given its altitude, or vice-versa. Essential for designing **Geostationary**, **Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)**, and **Low Earth Orbit (LEO)** satellite constellations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every formula a student will need to solve KTU board exam problems on this topic. All symbols and units are exam-ready.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Value |
|---|--------------|------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Position on ellipse (polar form) | $r = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos\theta}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Perigee distance | $r_p = a(1 - e)$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Apogee distance | $r_a = a(1 + e)$ | metres (m) |
| 4 | Semi-major axis from $r_p, r_a$ | $a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2}$ | metres (m) |
| 5 | Eccentricity from $r_p, r_a$ | $e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Areal velocity (constant) | $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \dot{\theta} = \frac{L}{2m}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | Orbital period (Kepler III) | $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$ | seconds (s) |
| 8 | Specific mechanical energy | $\varepsilon = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$ | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 9 | Vis-viva equation | $v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 10 | Velocity at perigee | $v_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a} \cdot \frac{1+e}{1-e}}$ | m/s |
| 11 | Velocity at apogee | $v_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a} \cdot \frac{1-e}{1+e}}$ | m/s |
| 12 | Geostationary altitude | $h_{GEO} = \left( \frac{\mu T_E^2}{4\pi^2} \right)^{1/3} - R_E \approx 35{,}786\,\text{km}$ | km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Used in KTU Board Papers:**
> - $\mu = GM$ is the *gravitational parameter* of the central body (Earth for our course).
> - $a$ is the **semi-major axis**, not the orbital radius. For a circular orbit, $a = r$.
> - $e$ is the eccentricity. A *circle* is an ellipse with $e = 0$.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Communication Engineering

Kepler's Laws are not just academic curiosities — they are the **backbone of the entire satellite communication industry**. Every communication satellite ever launched follows these laws:

1. **Geostationary Satellites (GEO):** Used for TV broadcasting, weather monitoring, and long-distance telephony. They sit at $\approx 35{,}786$ km above the equator, where the orbital period matches Earth's rotation. This is a *direct application* of Kepler's Third Law.
2. **LEO Constellations (e.g., Starlink, OneWeb):** Used for broadband internet. Hundreds of satellites orbit at 500–1200 km. Periods calculated via $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$.
3. **GPS Satellites (MEO):** Orbit at $\approx 20{,}200$ km with a period of 12 sidereal hours.
4. **Molniya Orbits (Russia):** Highly elliptical orbits ($e \approx 0.74$) used to provide coverage at high latitudes — a direct application of Kepler's First and Second Laws.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations carry **high mark weightage** (7–10 marks out of 14 in Part B). The following derivations are exhaustively shown — *do not skip a single step* when writing your exam answer.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU papers. The goal is to show that:

$$
T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}
$$

### Step 1 — Setup: Consider a circular orbit first (simplest case)

For a satellite of mass $m$ orbiting Earth (mass $M_E$) in a circular orbit of radius $r$, the **centripetal force** required is provided by the **gravitational force**:

$$
F_{\text{centripetal}} = \frac{mv^2}{r}
$$

$$
F_{\text{gravity}} = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Equate the two forces

$$
\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $m$ and one $r$ from both sides

The mass $m$ cancels (satellite mass is *irrelevant* in orbital mechanics — the "rubber duck principle"). Multiplying both sides by $r$:

$$
v^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

### Step 4 — Express orbital velocity in terms of period

The satellite travels a distance of $2\pi r$ (the circumference) in one orbital period $T$. Therefore:

$$
v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}
$$

### Step 5 — Square the velocity and substitute

$$
v^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}
$$

Substituting this into the equation from Step 3:

$$
\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $T^2$

Multiply both sides by $T^2$ and then by $r$:

$$
4\pi^2 r^3 = \mu T^2
$$

$$
T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}
$$

### Step 7 — Generalise to elliptical orbit

For a *circular* orbit, $r = a$ (the semi-major axis equals the radius). For an *elliptical* orbit, Newton's proof (using the conservation of energy and the vis-viva equation) generalises this to:

$$
\boxed{\,T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** This is the famous $T^2 \propto a^3$ relationship. If you *double* the orbital radius, the period increases by $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$ times. This is why geostationary satellites at $42{,}164$ km have a 24-hour period, while LEO satellites at 700 km orbit in just $\approx 98$ minutes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between Orbital Velocities at Perigee and Apogee

This derivation uses **Kepler's Second Law (conservation of angular momentum)** to relate the velocities at the two extreme points of an elliptical orbit.

### Step 1 — State the conservation of angular momentum

In a central force field, the specific angular momentum $h = r^2 \dot{\theta}$ is constant. Equivalently:

$$
L = m v r \sin\alpha = \text{constant}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the radial direction.

### Step 2 — Apply the condition at perigee and apogee

At both perigee and apogee, the velocity is **purely tangential** (perpendicular to the radius vector), so $\alpha = 90^\circ$ and $\sin\alpha = 1$.

Therefore:

$$
m v_p r_p = m v_a r_a
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel the mass $m$

$$
v_p r_p = v_a r_a
$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of $a$ and $e$

From the geometry of the ellipse:

$$
r_p = a(1 - e), \quad r_a = a(1 + e)
$$

Substitute:

$$
v_p \cdot a(1 - e) = v_a \cdot a(1 + e)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the ratio

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{v_p}{v_a} = \frac{1 + e}{1 - e}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *physical reason* why a satellite moves faster at perigee. For a Molniya orbit with $e = 0.74$, the perigee velocity is $v_p \approx 5.7 \, v_a$ — a 5.7× speed difference! This is exploited to "hang" the satellite over the northern hemisphere for most of its orbit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Orbital Period of a Geostationary Satellite

This is a *direct numerical application* of Kepler's Third Law. It is a KTU-favourite problem.

### Step 1 — Set the orbital period equal to Earth's sidereal period

$$
T = T_E = 86164 \, \text{s} \approx 23.934 \, \text{hours}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Kepler's Third Law

$$
T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the semi-major axis $a$

$$
a^3 = \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}
$$

$$
a = \left( \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2} \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
a = \left( \frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86164)^2}{4\pi^2} \right)^{1/3} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
a = \left( \frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.424 \times 10^{9}}{39.478} \right)^{1/3} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
a = \left( 7.495 \times 10^{22} \right)^{1/3} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
a \approx 4.2164 \times 10^7 \, \text{m} = 42{,}164 \, \text{km}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to orbital altitude

$$
h = a - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6371 \approx 35{,}793 \, \text{km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Value to Memorise:** The geostationary orbit altitude is approximately **35,786 km** above the mean sea level. The standard value used in KTU papers is **35,786 km or 35,800 km** (rounded).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Vis-Viva Equation (Energy Derivation)

The vis-viva equation gives the satellite's speed at any point in the orbit. It is derived from energy conservation.

### Step 1 — Total specific mechanical energy is constant

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = \text{constant}
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate at the apogee (where the velocity is purely tangential)

At apogee, $r = r_a = a(1 + e)$ and $v = v_a$. So:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{v_a^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{a(1 + e)}
$$

### Step 3 — Use the apogee velocity from the angular-momentum relation

From Section 3.2 and from the conservation of energy applied at perigee and apogee, one can show:

$$
v_a^2 = \frac{\mu (1 - e)}{a(1 + e)}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1+e)}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\mu}{a(1+e)} \left[ \frac{1-e}{2} - 1 \right] = \frac{\mu}{a(1+e)} \cdot \frac{-(1+e)}{2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate and solve for $v^2$

$$
\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

$$
\frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2a} = \mu \left( \frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{2a} \right) = \mu \left( \frac{2a - r}{2ar} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)\,}
$$

This is the famous **Vis-Viva Equation** (Latin for "living force"), one of the most important equations in orbital mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Block Diagram (C):** Every communication satellite ever launched goes through the *Input → Keplerian Engine → Output → Application* pipeline. The "Keplerian Engine" is essentially a set of formulas — the ones in the formula sheet in Section 2.2 — that convert basic orbital parameters into mission-critical outputs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> These are *direct conceptual* questions. KTU expects 2–4 sentence answers with the relevant formula.

---

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Kepler's three laws of planetary motion as applicable to satellite communication systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Kepler's First Law (Law of Orbits):** A satellite moves in an elliptical orbit with the Earth located at one of the two foci of the ellipse. The point of closest approach is called the **perigee** ($r_p = a(1-e)$) and the farthest point is the **apogee** ($r_a = a(1+e)$).

**Kepler's Second Law (Law of Areas):** The radius vector drawn from the Earth to the satellite sweeps out **equal areas in equal intervals of time**. This is a consequence of the conservation of angular momentum. Mathematically, the areal velocity is $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \text{constant}$.

**Kepler's Third Law (Law of Periods):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of the orbit:

$$
T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}
$$

This law is used to compute the orbital period of communication satellites — for example, a geostationary satellite at $a = 42{,}164$ km has a 24-hour period.

*(Examiner expects 3 laws named + 1-line statement + 1 formula. Full marks.)*

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A satellite orbits the Earth in a circular orbit at an altitude of 1000 km. Using Kepler's third law, calculate the orbital period. (Given $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ and $R_E = 6371$ km.)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute the semi-major axis (which equals the orbital radius for a circle):
$$
a = R_E + h = 6371 + 1000 = 7371 \, \text{km} = 7.371 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Kepler's Third Law:
$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:
$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.371 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4.004 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{1.0045 \times 10^{6}} = 2\pi \times 1002.25 \approx 6297 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to minutes:
$$
T \approx \frac{6297}{60} \approx 104.95 \, \text{minutes} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 45 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Students often forget to **add the Earth's radius** to the altitude. A circular orbit's semi-major axis is $R_E + h$, *not* just $h$. Always convert km to m before substituting into formulas with $\mu$ in SI units.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's 2024 scheme, every Part B question offers an internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as full independent options — both 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** *State Kepler's three laws. Derive Kepler's third law for an artificial Earth satellite in a circular orbit starting from Newton's law of gravitation. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A geostationary communication satellite is to be placed in a circular orbit around the Earth. Calculate: (i) the orbital radius from the centre of the Earth, (ii) the altitude above the Earth's surface, and (iii) the orbital velocity of the satellite. (Given $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$, $R_E = 6371$ km, and sidereal day $T = 86164$ s.) (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (a):**

**Statement of the three laws (2 marks):**
1. **First Law:** The orbit is an ellipse with the primary body at one focus.
2. **Second Law:** The radius vector sweeps equal areas in equal times.
3. **Third Law:** $T^2 \propto a^3$, or $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a satellite of mass $m$ in a circular orbit of radius $r$ around Earth of mass $M$. The centripetal force required for circular motion is provided by Newton's gravitational force:

$$
\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{G M m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}
$$

Cancelling $m$ and one $r$:

$$
v^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

The satellite covers the circumference $2\pi r$ in one period $T$, so:

$$
v = \frac{2\pi r}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $v^2$:

$$
\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{\mu}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4\pi^2 r^3 = \mu T^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}\,}
$$

For a circular orbit, $r = a$ (semi-major axis), hence the general form $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (b):**

**(i) Orbital radius (3 marks):**
For a geostationary orbit, the period $T = 86164$ s. From Kepler's third law:

$$
a = \left( \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (86164)^2}{4\pi^2} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = \left( \frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.424 \times 10^{9}}{39.478} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = (7.495 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \, \text{m} \approx 42{,}164 \, \text{km}
$$

**(ii) Altitude (2 marks):**
$$
h = a - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6371 = 35{,}793 \, \text{km} \approx 35{,}786 \, \text{km}
$$

**(iii) Orbital velocity (2 marks):**
For a circular orbit, $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$:

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.453 \times 10^6} \approx 3074.6 \, \text{m/s} \approx 3.07 \, \text{km/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q. A):**
> - **–1 mark:** Students confuse the **sidereal day (86164 s)** with the **solar day (86400 s)**. For geostationary calculations, **always use the sidereal day** because the orbit must be inertial, not solar-synchronous.
> - **–2 marks:** Not showing the unit conversion from km to m. The examiner checks for dimensional consistency.
> - **–1 mark:** Forgetting to write the *final boxed answer* with units. Always end derivations with a clean boxed equation.

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Define eccentricity, perigee, and apogee for a satellite orbit. A communication satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee altitude of 600 km and apogee altitude of 36,000 km. Calculate the semi-major axis and the eccentricity of the orbit. (Given $R_E = 6371$ km.) (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With the help of a neat diagram, explain Kepler's second law. Show that the areal velocity of a satellite is constant and equal to $\frac{L}{2m}$, where $L$ is the orbital angular momentum. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (a):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Eccentricity ($e$):** A dimensionless parameter that measures the deviation of the ellipse from a circle; $e = 0$ for a circle and $0 < e < 1$ for an ellipse.
- **Perigee:** The point in the orbit **closest** to the Earth.
- **Apogee:** The point in the orbit **farthest** from the Earth.

**Calculations (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert altitudes to orbital radii by adding Earth's radius:
$$
r_p = 6371 + 600 = 6971 \, \text{km}
$$
$$
r_a = 6371 + 36{,}000 = 42{,}371 \, \text{km}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the semi-major axis:
$$
a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2} = \frac{6971 + 42{,}371}{2} = \frac{49{,}342}{2} = 24{,}671 \, \text{km}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the eccentricity:
$$
e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{42{,}371 - 6971}{42{,}371 + 6971} = \frac{35{,}400}{49{,}342} \approx 0.7174
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (b):**

**Diagram description (2 marks):**

A neat diagram should show an elliptical orbit with the Earth at one focus. Two sectors of equal area $\Delta A_1$ and $\Delta A_2$ should be marked — one near perigee (smaller in width, but the satellite covers it quickly) and one near apogee (larger in width, but the satellite covers it slowly). Equal areas correspond to equal time intervals.

```
        Apogee (r_a, slow)
         *
        / \
       /   \  ΔA_2 (far region)
   ___*_____ *___
     F1     C     F2
     Earth
       \   /
        \ /  ΔA_1 (near region, fast)
         *
        Perigee (r_p, fast)
```

**Mathematical proof (5 marks):**

Consider a satellite of mass $m$ moving in an orbit. In a small time interval $dt$, the satellite sweeps out an infinitesimal area $dA$.

In polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, the area swept in time $dt$ is:

$$
dA = \frac{1}{2} r \cdot (r \, d\theta) = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \, d\theta
$$

Therefore, the **areal velocity** is:

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \dot{\theta}
$$

The **orbital angular momentum** of the satellite is:

$$
L = m r^2 \dot{\theta} \quad \text{(since } \vec{L} = m(\vec{r} \times \vec{v}) \text{ and } v_\theta = r\dot{\theta}\text{)}
$$

Comparing the two equations:

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2m} \cdot (m r^2 \dot{\theta}) = \frac{L}{2m}
$$

Since gravity is a **central force**, the torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = 0$, and therefore $\frac{dL}{dt} = 0$, i.e., $L$ is conserved. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \text{constant}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q. B):**
> - **–1 mark:** Writing $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{m}$ (forgetting the factor of 2). The factor of 2 in the denominator is a classic trap.
> - **–2 marks:** Forgetting to *prove* that angular momentum is conserved (i.e., not mentioning the central-force argument). KTU examiners award marks only when conservation is explicitly justified.
> - **–1 mark:** Writing $r_p = 600$ km instead of $r_p = R_E + 600 = 6971$ km. Always add the Earth's radius.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute reading before the exam):**

- **Kepler's Three Laws:**
  - 1st Law → **Elliptical orbit**, primary at one focus.
  - 2nd Law → **Equal areas in equal times** = angular momentum conservation.
  - 3rd Law → $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$ — period depends on semi-major axis only.

- **Key Orbital Parameters (must be memorized):**
  - Perigee distance: $r_p = a(1 - e)$
  - Apogee distance: $r_a = a(1 + e)$
  - Eccentricity: $e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p}$
  - Semi-major axis: $a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2}$

- **The "Vis-Viva" Equation (very important):**
  - $v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)$
  - Reduces to $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ for a circular orbit.

- **Geostationary Orbit Standard Values (must memorise):**
  - Altitude: $h \approx 35{,}786$ km above Earth's surface.
  - Orbital radius: $a \approx 42{,}164$ km from Earth's centre.
  - Period: $T = 23$ h 56 m 4 s (one **sidereal** day).
  - Orbital velocity: $v \approx 3.07$ km/s.

- **The "Golden Rules" to Avoid Losing Marks:**
  - Always add $R_E$ to altitude to get orbital radius.
  - Always use $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ for Earth.
  - Always use the **sidereal day** (86164 s), *not* the solar day.
  - Always write the **units** in your final answer.
  - Always end derivations with a **boxed equation**.

- **Why these laws matter to Communication Engineers:**
  - Determine satellite footprint, look angles, and coverage.
  - Predict eclipse times (when satellite passes into Earth's shadow).
  - Calculate station-keeping fuel requirements.
  - Design inter-satellite links in LEO constellations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Sub Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Satellite Sub Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Satellite Sub System?

A **Satellite Sub System** refers to the modular set of functionally distinct hardware and software units that together form a complete, operational artificial satellite. Each sub system performs a dedicated engineering role — power generation, orientation control, thermal regulation, propulsion, payload operation, tracking, and structural integrity — and is engineered to survive the harsh launch and orbital environment (vacuum, radiation, thermal cycling, micro-meteoroids) for a typical mission life of **10–15 years** for GEO communication satellites.

Formally, the KTU 2024 syllabus defines the satellite sub systems as:
> *"The collection of all on-board equipment, exclusive of the payload, that supports the operation of the payload and maintains the satellite in its proper operational state throughout its design lifetime."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The eight canonical sub systems — *Power, Attitude & Orbit Control, Thermal, Propulsion, TT&C, Communication Payload, Structure, and Antenna* — must be studied with their inter-dependencies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Space Hotel" Model

Think of a communication satellite as a luxury **space hotel** orbiting at **35,786 km** altitude (for GEO):

| Sub System | Hotel Department Analogy | Job in Orbit |
|------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Power Sub System | The Diesel Generators + Power Grid | Converts sunlight into electricity, stores energy |
| AOCS | The Hotel Manager + Compass | Keeps the hotel pointing at Earth correctly |
| Thermal Control | The HVAC + Air-Cooling | Maintains room temperature between −20 °C to +60 °C |
| Propulsion | The Fuel Pumps + Steering | Pushes the hotel back to its assigned parking spot |
| TT&C | The Reception Desk + Walkie-Talkie | Reports health to ground and receives orders |
| Payload (Transponder) | The actual Business (rooms) | Earns revenue — amplifies & retransmits signals |
| Structure | The Building's Steel Frame | Holds everything together during launch & orbit |
| Antenna | The Windows & Balconies | Signal entry and exit points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the split?** Separation of concerns enables parallel design, parallel testing, modular replacement during integration, and clean failure analysis — a core tenet of KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) philosophy mapped to **CO3 (Design sub system specifications)**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bounded Memorise List)

- **Solar Constant (AM0):** $S_0 = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ (flux at Earth's orbit, no atmosphere)
- **GEO Altitude:** $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$
- **Mission Life:** **15 years** (modern design benchmark)
- **Battery Depth of Discharge (DOD):** typically **20–30%** for LEO, up to **60–80%** for GEO
- **Pointing Accuracy:** $\pm 0.05°$ for high-gain Ku/Ka-band antennas
- **Spacecraft Bus Voltage:** **28 V DC** (standard) or **50 V / 100 V** (modern high-power)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Overview

A satellite is hierarchically divided into two super-blocks:

1. **Spacecraft Bus (Platform):** All sub systems except payload — generic, reusable across missions.
2. **Payload (Mission-specific):** Communication transponders, antennas, signal processors.

> [!TIP]
> The payload is the *revenue earner*; the bus is the *life support*. In a 14-mark KTU answer, always draw a clear boundary between the two.

## 2.2 The Eight Sub Systems — Step-by-Step Analysis

### A. Power Sub System (PSS)

**Function:** Generate, store, and distribute electrical power to all on-board loads.

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Energy Generation:** Sun-facing solar arrays (typically triple-junction GaAs cells) convert photons → DC electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**. Each cell produces ~0.5 V open-circuit; ~30% efficient.
2. **Energy Storage:** Rechargeable **Ni-H₂** or **Li-Ion** batteries handle eclipse periods. For GEO, eclipse maximum duration = **72 minutes/day** (around equinoxes).
3. **Power Conditioning:** Shunt regulators dump excess power; battery charge/discharge regulators (BCDR) manage battery state-of-charge.
4. **Distribution:** DC-DC converters feed a regulated bus (28 V standard).

**Key Formulae (derived in Section 3):**
- Solar array output: $P_{SA} = \eta \cdot S_0 \cdot A_{SA} \cdot \cos\theta$
- Eclipse energy needed: $E_{ecl} = P_{load} \cdot T_{ecl}$

### B. Attitude & Orbit Control Sub System (AOCS / ADCS)

**Function:** Measure and control the satellite's orientation (attitude) and, optionally, its orbital parameters.

**Two main stabilization philosophies:**

- **Spin Stabilization:** The body rotates (60–100 rpm) about an axis perpendicular to orbit plane. Cheap, simple, but only one face available for antenna pointing. Used in early satellites (Intelsat-I, II).
- **Three-Axis (Body) Stabilization:** The body is fixed; momentum/reaction wheels and magnetic torquers control attitude. Allows large solar arrays and high-gain antennas always pointed at Earth. Used in **100% of modern GEO comsats**.

**Sensors:** Sun sensors (coarse), star trackers (fine, $\pm 0.001°$), Earth sensors (horizon), gyroscopes (rate).
**Actuators:** Reaction wheels (0.1–10 N·m·s), magnetic torquers, thrusters.

### C. Thermal Control Sub System (TCS)

**Function:** Maintain all on-board equipment within operational temperature limits, typically:
- **−20 °C to +60 °C** for most electronics.
- **−180 °C to +150 °C** overall survival.

**Passive techniques:** MLI (Multi-Layer Insulation) blankets, optical solar reflectors (OSR), thermal paints, heat pipes, radiators.
**Active techniques:** Heaters, thermo-electric coolers (Peltier), louvers, fluid loops.

**Governing Law — Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation:**
$$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$ (Stefan-Boltzmann constant) and $\varepsilon$ is the surface emissivity.

### D. Propulsion Sub System

**Function:** Provide $\Delta V$ for orbit corrections, station keeping, attitude control, and de-orbit.

**Two propellant families:**

- **Chemical (Bipropellant):** N₂O₄ / MMH or hydrazine monopropellant. High thrust (~500 N), short burns. Specific impulse $I_{sp} = 280$–$320$ s.
- **Electric (Ion/Plasma):** Xenon ion thrusters (SPT-100, NEXT). Low thrust (~100 mN), very high $I_{sp} = 1500$–$3000$ s. Used for north-south station keeping (NSSK).

**Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation:**
$$\Delta v = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln \frac{m_0}{m_f}$$

### E. Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TT&C) Sub System

**Function:** Two-way communication between ground and satellite.

- **Telemetry (downlink):** Reports 200–500 parameters (voltages, temperatures, pressures, switch positions). Encoded in PCM/PSK on a dedicated beacon.
- **Command (uplink):** 100+ discrete commands (relay open/close, heater on/off, thruster fire). Tone-based, FSK/PSK modulated.
- **Tracking:** Ranging tones + Doppler shift measurements determine range and range-rate for orbit determination.

### F. Communication Sub System (Payload)

**Function:** Receive, amplify, frequency-translate, and re-transmit user signals.

A typical **transparent (bent-pipe) transponder** chain:
1. Receive antenna (RX)
2. **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier)** — GaAs HEMT, $T_e \approx 30$–$80$ K
3. **Down-converter mixer** (RF → IF)
4. **IF amplifier & channel filters** (bandwidth: 36 MHz for C-band, 27/54 MHz for Ku)
5. **Up-converter mixer** (IF → RF)
6. **HPA (High-Power Amplifier)** — TWTA (Travelling Wave Tube) or SSPA (Solid-State)
7. **Output multiplexer (OMUX)** — combines channels into transmit antenna feed

### G. Structure Sub System

**Function:** Carry launch loads (quasi-static + dynamic), provide a stiff optical bench for high-gain antennas, and protect against MMOD (Micro-Meteoroid and Orbital Debris).

**Materials:** Aluminum honeycomb sandwich panels, carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) tubes, titanium fittings.

**Critical metric:** First natural frequency must be > **35 Hz** (to avoid coupling with launcher).

### H. Antenna Sub System

**Function:** Interface between RF payload and free space.

**Components:** Reflectors (parabolic, 1–3 m for L-band, up to 25 m for radiotelescopes), feeds (horn, array), deployment mechanisms (hinges, motors), pointing mechanisms (gimbals, steerable beams).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — it covers >85% of all numerical problems asked in KTU Module-3 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Range |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Solar Array Power | $P_{SA} = \eta \cdot S_0 \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta$ | $\eta$: cell efficiency, $S_0$: W/m², $A$: m², $\theta$: Sun incidence angle | 1–25 kW per wing |
| 2 | Eclipse Duration (max) | $T_{ecl} \approx \dfrac{2 R_E}{v_{orb}} \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{R_E + h}{R_E}\right)$ — simplified to ~72 min for GEO | $R_E$: 6378 km, $h$: km, $v_{orb}$: km/s | GEO: 72 min |
| 3 | Battery Energy | $E_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{load} \cdot T_{ecl}}{\eta_{DOD} \cdot \eta_{bat}}$ | $P_{load}$: W, $T_{ecl}$: h, $\eta_{DOD}$: DOD, $\eta_{bat}$: round-trip eff. | Li-Ion: 100–200 Wh/kg |
| 4 | Tsiolkovsky (Propellant) | $\Delta v = I_{sp} g_0 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$ | $I_{sp}$: s, $g_0 = 9.81$ m/s², $m_0$, $m_f$: kg | GEO ΔV ≈ 1500 m/s lifetime |
| 5 | Thermal Radiation | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, $A$: m², $T$: K | 100 W per 0.5 m² radiator |
| 6 | Power Flux Density | $S = \dfrac{EIRP}{4\pi d^2}$ | $EIRP$: W, $d$: m | At GEO RX: −135 dBW/m² typical |
| 7 | Received Power | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{\!2}$ | $G_t$, $G_r$: linear gains, $\lambda$: m, $d$: m | Path loss GEO→Earth: ~205 dB |
| 8 | Antenna Gain | $G = \eta_{ant} \cdot \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | $A_e$: effective aperture (m²), $\lambda$: m, $\eta_{ant} \approx 0.55$–$0.70$ | 30–50 dBi for reflector |
| 9 | Half-Power Beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | $D$: dish diameter, $\lambda$: wavelength | 0.5° for 1 m at 10 GHz |
| 10 | Heat Pipe | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | latent heat of working fluid | 1000 W typical |
| 11 | Orbital Period | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{a^3}{\mu}}$ | $a$: semi-major axis, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² | GEO: 86,164 s (sidereal day) |
| 12 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t \cdot G_t$ | $P_t$: W, $G_t$: linear | 50–60 dBW (Ku HPA chain) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Satellite sub system design feeds directly into:

- **ISRO's INSAT/GSAT series** — all eight sub systems follow the framework above.
- **Starlink constellation (SpaceX)** — mass-produced, standardized bus for LEO broadband.
- **Telecom Reliability Standards** — ECSS (European), MIL-STD-1540, ISRO-IRD.
- **CubeSat class missions** — same sub system philosophy scaled to 1U/3U (10×10×10 cm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 14-mark KTU question on "Explain satellite sub systems" **must include a labelled block diagram** (see Section 4) and **at least one numerical example** (see Section 3).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU ESE valuation, half-marks are lost when students skip a step or write "obviously". Show every algebraic transition.

## 3.1 Derivation — Solar Array Sizing for a GEO Communication Satellite

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A GEO communication satellite has a payload that requires **2.8 kW** continuously. The bus electronics consume an additional **1.2 kW**. The solar arrays use GaAs cells with **30% efficiency** and have a worst-case Sun incidence angle of **23.5°** (solstice). The solar constant at Earth orbit is **$S_0 = 1361$ W/m²**. The eclipse period at equinox lasts **72 min** with a maximum Depth of Discharge (DOD) of **60%**. The battery round-trip efficiency is **85%** and has a specific energy of **120 Wh/kg**. Calculate:

(i) Required solar array area
(ii) Required battery energy capacity in Wh
(iii) Battery mass

### Step 1 — Total Load Power

The satellite must supply full power during sunlight, so the solar array is sized for *eclipse end-of-life* (EOL) condition, accounting for **degradation**:

$$P_{load} = P_{payload} + P_{bus} = 2.8 + 1.2 = 4.0 \text{ kW}$$

For EOL degradation factor $X_d = 0.75$ (15-year life, 25% degradation):

$$P_{SA,EOL} = \frac{P_{load}}{X_d} = \frac{4.0}{0.75} = 5.333 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Required Solar Array Area (with $\cos\theta$ projection)

Apply the standard solar array equation:

$$P_{SA} = \eta \cdot S_0 \cdot A_{SA} \cdot \cos\theta$$

Solving for $A_{SA}$:

$$A_{SA} = \frac{P_{SA,EOL}}{\eta \cdot S_0 \cdot \cos\theta}$$

Substitute $P_{SA,EOL} = 5333$ W, $\eta = 0.30$, $S_0 = 1361$ W/m², $\theta = 23.5°$:

$$A_{SA} = \frac{5333}{0.30 \times 1361 \times \cos(23.5°)}$$

Compute the denominator step-by-step:

$$0.30 \times 1361 = 408.3 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\cos(23.5°) \approx 0.9171$$

$$408.3 \times 0.9171 \approx 374.45 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$A_{SA} = \frac{5333}{374.45} \approx 14.24 \text{ m}^2$$

> ✅ **Answer (i):** $A_{SA} \approx 14.24$ m² (typically split into two wings of ~7.12 m² each).

### Step 3 — Battery Energy Capacity (Watt-hours)

Battery must supply full load during the worst-case eclipse. The energy drawn:

$$E_{draw} = P_{load} \cdot T_{ecl} = 4000 \text{ W} \times \frac{72}{60} \text{ h} = 4000 \times 1.2 = 4800 \text{ Wh}$$

But battery capacity must be sized for allowable DOD. Define:

$$E_{bat} = \frac{E_{draw}}{\eta_{DOD} \cdot \eta_{bat}}$$

where $\eta_{DOD} = 0.60$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$:

$$E_{bat} = \frac{4800}{0.60 \times 0.85} = \frac{4800}{0.51} \approx 9411.76 \text{ Wh}$$

> ✅ **Answer (ii):** $E_{bat} \approx 9.41$ kWh.

### Step 4 — Battery Mass

Using specific energy $E_{sp} = 120$ Wh/kg:

$$m_{bat} = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{sp}} = \frac{9411.76}{120} \approx 78.43 \text{ kg}$$

> ✅ **Answer (iii):** $m_{bat} \approx 78.4$ kg.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners love this type of "tripartite" numerical** because it tests three sub systems in one question. Always box the final answer and **carry the unit through** every step.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Propellant Mass for North-South Station Keeping

**Problem (7 marks):** A GEO satellite requires a total $\Delta v = 1500$ m/s over its 15-year lifetime for station keeping. It uses a chemical bipropellant with $I_{sp} = 320$ s. Wet mass $m_0 = 3000$ kg. Find the propellant mass $m_p$.

### Step 1 — Tsiolkovsky Equation

$$m_0 = m_f \cdot e^{\Delta v / (I_{sp} g_0)}$$

where $m_f = m_0 - m_p$ (dry mass + payload + structure).

### Step 2 — Numerically Evaluate the Exponent

$$\frac{\Delta v}{I_{sp} g_0} = \frac{1500}{320 \times 9.81} = \frac{1500}{3139.2} \approx 0.4778$$

### Step 3 — Compute Exponential

$$e^{0.4778} \approx 1.6125$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $m_f$

$$m_f = \frac{m_0}{e^{0.4778}} = \frac{3000}{1.6125} \approx 1860.5 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Propellant Mass

$$m_p = m_0 - m_f = 3000 - 1860.5 = 1139.5 \text{ kg}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $m_p \approx 1139.5$ kg (≈ 38% of wet mass — typical for GEO comsats).

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how 38% of launch mass is *just propellant*. This is why **electric propulsion** (with $I_{sp} = 3000$ s) reduces this to <100 kg — but requires 4–6 months of continuous thrust, which most GEO operators now accept.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Antenna Gain & EIRP for a Ku-Band Downlink

**Problem (7 marks):** A Ku-band (12 GHz) transponder outputs **120 W** from a TWTA. The transmit antenna has diameter **1.8 m** and aperture efficiency **65%**. Calculate the antenna gain and EIRP.

### Step 1 — Wavelength

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{12 \times 10^9} = 0.025 \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 2 — Geometric Aperture

$$A_{geom} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (1.8)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 3.24}{4} \approx 2.545 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Effective Aperture

$$A_e = \eta_{ant} \cdot A_{geom} = 0.65 \times 2.545 = 1.654 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Antenna Gain (Linear)

$$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 1.654}{(0.025)^2} = \frac{20.78}{0.000625} \approx 33{,}247$$

### Step 5 — Gain in dBi

$$G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(33{,}247) \approx 45.21 \text{ dBi}$$

### Step 6 — EIRP

$$P_{t,dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(120) \approx 20.79 \text{ dBW}$$

$$EIRP = P_{t,dBW} + G_{dBi} = 20.79 + 45.21 = 66.0 \text{ dBW}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $G \approx 45.2$ dBi, $EIRP = 66.0$ dBW.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three-Axis vs Spin Stabilization

| Parameter | Spin Stabilization | Three-Axis Stabilization |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Pointing accuracy | $\pm 0.5°$ to $\pm 1°$ | $\pm 0.05°$ to $\pm 0.1°$ |
| Solar array area | Limited (cylindrical) | Full, deployable up to 50+ m² |
| Antenna mounting | Despun platform required | Earth-fixed, simpler |
| Power density | Moderate | High (5–25 kW) |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High (wheels, gyros) |
| Typical use | Early comsats, low-power | **100% of modern GEO comsats** |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Life | 5–7 years | 15+ years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison is a **guaranteed 3-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE — it appears in nearly every past paper.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Communication Satellite

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSS[Power Sub System - Solar Arrays + Batteries + PCDU]
    AOCS[Attitude and Orbit Control Sub System - Star Trackers + Reaction Wheels + Gyros]
    TCS[Thermal Control Sub System - Radiators + MLI + Heaters]
    PROP[Propulsion Sub System - Thrusters + Tanks + Valves]
    TTC[Telemetry Tracking and Command Sub System - Encoders + Modems + Beacons]
    STR[Structure Sub System - Panels + Truss + Hinges]
    ANT[Antenna Sub System - Reflectors + Feeds + Gimbal]
    PAY[Communication Payload - Transponders + HPA + LNA]

    PSS -.powers.-> AOCS
    PSS -.powers.-> TCS
    PSS -.powers.-> PROP
    PSS -.powers.-> TTC
    PSS -.powers.-> PAY
    PSS -.powers.-> ANT

    AOCS -.controls.-> ANT
    AOCS -.controls.-> PROP
    AOCS -.controls.-> PSS
    AOCS -.controls.-> PAY

    TCS -.regulates.-> PAY
    TCS -.regulates.-> PSS
    TCS -.regulates.-> PROP

    PROP -.thrusts.-> STR
    ANT -.mounts.-> STR
    PAY -.mounts.-> STR
    PSS -.mounts.-> STR
    AOCS -.mounts.-> STR

    TTC <--> GROUND[Ground Earth Station]
    ANT <--> USER[User Terminals via Uplink and Downlink]

    classDef bus fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef payload fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ext fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px

    class PSS,AOCS,TCS,PROP,TTC,STR bus
    class PAY,ANT payload
    class GROUND,USER ext
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Bus sub systems (blue) are the platform; Payload (orange) is the mission objective; arrows show physical mounting (solid) and power/data flow (dashed). The antenna connects to BOTH the bus (gimbal control) and the payload (RF signal path) — a subtle point examiners love to test.

## 4.2 Power Sub System — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN((Sunlight AM0 1361 W per m2))
    SA[Solar Array Wing]
    BCDR[Battery Charge Discharge Regulator]
    BATT[Li Ion Battery Bank]
    SHUNT[Shunt Dissipator]
    PCU[Power Control Unit]
    DC[DC DC Converter 28V Bus]
    LD1[Payload Load]
    LD2[Bus Load]
    LD3[TT and C Load]

    SUN --> SA
    SA --> BCDR
    BCDR --> BATT
    BCDR --> SHUNT
    SA --> PCU
    BATT --> PCU
    PCU --> DC
    DC --> LD1
    DC --> LD2
    DC --> LD3

    classDef power fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    class SA,BCDR,BATT,SHUNT,PCU,DC power
```

## 4.3 Transponder (Payload) Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RXA[Receive Antenna]
    BPF1[Input Bandpass Filter]
    LNA[Low Noise Amplifier GaAs HEMT]
    MX1[Down Converter Mixer]
    LO1[Local Oscillator]
    CHAMP[IF Amplifier and Channel Filter 36 or 27 MHz]
    MX2[Up Converter Mixer]
    LO2[Local Oscillator]
    HPA[High Power Amplifier TWTA or SSPA]
    OMUX[Output Multiplexer]
    TXA[Transmit Antenna]

    RXA --> BPF1 --> LNA --> MX1 --> CHAMP --> MX2 --> HPA --> OMUX --> TXA
    LO1 -.-> MX1
    LO2 -.-> MX2
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **two frequency translations** (down then up) — this is why a *bent-pipe* transponder is also called a **double-conversion** design. The IF stage (typically 70 MHz or 140 MHz) makes channel filtering cheap and flexible.

## 4.4 Thermal Control Passive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INT[Internal Heat Sources - Electronics - Payload HPA]
    MLI[MLI Blanket - Outer Skin]
    OSR[Optical Solar Reflector - Second Surface Mirror]
    CON[Conductive Spreader - Aluminum Plate]
    HP[Heat Pipe - Ammonia or FC72]
    RAD[External Radiator - High Emissivity Paint]
    DEEP[(Deep Space Sink 3 Kelvin)]

    INT --> CON
    CON --> HP
    HP --> RAD
    MLI -.protects.-> RAD
    OSR -.covers.-> RAD
    RAD --> DEEP
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set-A]**

**Q1. List any three functions of the Attitude and Orbit Control Sub System (AOCS) of a satellite.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Three-axis attitude determination** using a combination of Sun sensors, star trackers, Earth horizon sensors, and gyroscopes to estimate the satellite's orientation in inertial space.
2. **Three-axis attitude control** via reaction wheels, magnetic torquers, or thrusters to maintain precise pointing (typically $\pm 0.05°$ for high-gain antennas).
3. **Orbit maintenance** through periodic thruster firings to counteract perturbations (lunar/solar gravity, solar radiation pressure, Earth oblateness $J_2$ effect) — especially **east-west and north-south station keeping** for GEO satellites.

> *(Any three relevant functions with brief explanation: 1 mark each. Total 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set-B]**

**Q2. Differentiate between the function of a TWTA and an SSPA in a satellite transponder.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TWTA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier) | SSPA (Solid-State Power Amplifier) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Active Device | Vacuum electron tube (helix TWT) | Semiconductor (GaN HEMT / GaAs FET) |
| Power Output | High (50–250 W typical) | Moderate (10–50 W typical) |
| Efficiency | 55–65% | 35–50% |
| Linearity | Moderate (needs linearizer) | High (intrinsically linear) |
| Bandwidth | Multi-octave (Ku & Ka compatible) | Narrower, band-specific |
| Reliability | Lower (cathode wear) | Higher (no wear-out) |
| Typical Use | High-power Ku/Ka downlink HPA | L-band, multi-beam active antennas, LEO |

*Concluding line (1 mark):* TWTAs dominate **high-power GEO Ku/Ka downlink** chains; SSPAs dominate **L-band mobile payloads and modern active phased arrays** in LEO constellations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### ▶ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set-A]*

**Q.** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various sub systems of a communication satellite. Discuss the functions of the Power Sub System and the Thermal Control Sub System in detail.* **14 Marks**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Block Diagram + Overview — 7 marks**

- Draw the **functional block diagram from Section 4.1** above. (2 marks)
- List the eight sub systems in a tabular form with one-line functions. (2 marks)
- Briefly explain the **Spacecraft Bus vs Payload** distinction. (1 mark)
- State the standard bus voltage (**28 V DC**) and typical mission life (**15 years** for GEO). (1 mark)
- Add the **typical mass/power budget** table (e.g., PSS ≈ 25–30% of bus dry mass). (1 mark)

**(b) Power Sub System — 3.5 marks** *(split into generation, storage, conditioning, distribution)*

- **Generation:** Solar cells (GaAs triple-junction, $\eta = 30\%$), Sun-tracking vs body-mounted arrays, equation $P = \eta S_0 A \cos\theta$.
- **Storage:** Ni-H₂ vs Li-Ion batteries; eclipse time = 72 min max for GEO; DOD considerations.
- **Conditioning:** Shunt regulators, BCDR (Battery Charge-Discharge Regulator), PCU.
- **Distribution:** 28 V regulated bus, DC-DC converters for secondary voltages (5 V, 12 V, ±15 V).

**(c) Thermal Control Sub System — 3.5 marks** *(split into passive, active, design equation)*

- **Passive:** MLI blankets (10–20 layers of aluminized Kapton), OSR second-surface mirrors, thermal paints ($\alpha/\varepsilon$ ratio), heat pipes.
- **Active:** Resistive heaters, thermo-electric coolers, louvers (variable-emittance radiators), pumped fluid loops.
- **Governing equation:** $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ — derive the radiator area needed for a given dissipation. (Use a sample numerical: 200 W dissipation, $\varepsilon = 0.85$, $T = 300$ K → $A \approx 1.15$ m².)
- **Thermal balance equation:** $Q_{in} = Q_{out} \Rightarrow \alpha S_0 A_{sun} + Q_{internal} = \varepsilon \sigma A_{rad} T^4$.

> ✅ **Presentation tip:** Use **headings and bullets**; KTU examiners scan for keywords like *Stefan-Boltzmann*, *DOD*, *MLI*, *28 V bus* within the first 30 seconds.

---

### ▶ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set-B]*

**Q.** *Explain the Transponder sub system (payload) of a communication satellite with a block diagram. Derive the antenna gain and EIRP for a Ku-band downlink, given: Transmit power = 100 W, antenna diameter = 2.4 m, aperture efficiency = 0.65, frequency = 14 GHz.* **14 Marks**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Transponder Architecture & Block Diagram — 7 marks**

- Introduce the two architectures: **transparent (bent-pipe)** vs **regenerative** (on-board demod/remod). Modern trend is regenerative for high-throughput satellites (HTS), but bent-pipe remains dominant. (2 marks)
- Draw the block diagram of a **single-conversion bent-pipe** transponder (use the diagram from Section 4.3). (2 marks)
- Explain each block: BPF, LNA ($T_e$ figure of merit), down-converter (mixer + LO), IF amp + channel filter, up-converter, HPA, OMUX. (2 marks)
- Mention **multi-carrier operation** and the role of **input demultiplexer (IMUX)** for separating channels. (1 mark)

**(b) Antenna Gain and EIRP Numerical — 7 marks**

- **Step 1:** Compute wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(14 \times 10^9) = 0.02143$ m.
- **Step 2:** Geometric aperture: $A_g = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi \times (2.4)^2 / 4 = 4.524$ m².
- **Step 3:** Effective aperture: $A_e = \eta_{ant} \cdot A_g = 0.65 \times 4.524 = 2.941$ m².
- **Step 4:** Linear gain: $G = (4\pi A_e)/\lambda^2 = (4\pi \times 2.941)/(0.02143)^2 = 36.96/0.0004593 = 80{,}470$.
- **Step 5:** Gain in dBi: $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(80{,}470) \approx 49.06$ dBi.
- **Step 6:** Transmit power in dBW: $P_{t,dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20$ dBW.
- **Step 7:** EIRP: $EIRP = 20 + 49.06 = 69.06$ dBW.

**Box the final answers:**

$$\boxed{\lambda = 0.02143 \text{ m}, \quad G \approx 49.06 \text{ dBi}, \quad EIRP = 69.06 \text{ dBW}}$$

- **Interpretation (1 mark):** With 69 dBW EIRP and a typical 36 MHz transponder, this is consistent with a medium-power Ku-band GEO satellite (e.g., INSAT-class). For comparison, modern HTS can reach > 70 dBW EIRP per beam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (READ CAREFULLY):**
> - ❌ *Confusing dBi with dBd* — they differ by 2.15 dB; KTU expects dBi for absolute gain.
> - ❌ *Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ projection* in solar array calculations when $\theta \neq 0$.
> - ❌ *Using W instead of dBW* in EIRP — losing 1–2 marks for unit inconsistency.
> - ❌ *Mixing up TWTA and SSPA* — TWTAs are tubes, SSPAs are transistors. Examiners instantly catch this.
> - ❌ *Skipping the block diagram* in a "with the help of a block diagram" question — minimum 2 marks lost.
> - ❌ *Writing "obviously" in derivations* — KTU 2024 strictly penalises skipped algebra; always show the substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Satellite Sub Systems**

- ✅ **Eight canonical sub systems:** Power, AOCS, Thermal, Propulsion, TT&C, Payload (Transponder), Structure, Antenna.
- ✅ **Spacecraft Bus vs Payload** — bus is generic platform; payload is mission-specific.
- ✅ **Standard bus voltage = 28 V DC**; alternative 50 V / 100 V for high-power buses.
- ✅ **Solar constant** $S_0 = 1361$ W/m²; cell efficiency up to **30%** (GaAs triple-junction).
- ✅ **Maximum GEO eclipse** = 72 min/day; battery DOD typically 60%.
- ✅ **Three-axis stabilization** = modern standard; spin is obsolete for comsats.
- ✅ **Pointing accuracy** $\pm 0.05°$ for Ku/Ka-band high-gain antennas.
- ✅ **TWTA = tube** (high power, 50–250 W); **SSPA = solid-state** (lower power, more linear).
- ✅ **LNA figure of merit** = equivalent noise temperature $T_e \approx 30$–$80$ K (GaAs HEMT).
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann** for radiator sizing: $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$.
- ✅ **Tsiolkovsky equation** governs propellant: $\Delta v = I_{sp} g_0 \ln(m_0/m_f)$.
- ✅ **Electric propulsion** (ion) has $I_{sp} = 1500$–$3000$ s, used for NSSK.
- ✅ **EIRP** is the single most important downlink metric: $EIRP = P_t \cdot G_t$ (in dB, add; in linear, multiply).
- ✅ **First natural frequency** of structure must exceed **35 Hz** to decouple from launcher.
- ✅ **MLI** reduces radiative heat transfer by factor 100–1000; **OSRs** reflect Sun while radiating IR.
- ✅ **TT&C** uses S-band (2 GHz) traditionally; X-band (8 GHz) increasingly for security.
- ✅ **Antenna gain formula:** $G = \eta_{ant} \cdot (4\pi A_e/\lambda^2)$; **HPBW** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \lambda / D$.
- ✅ **Mission life benchmark:** 15 years for GEO communication satellites.
- ✅ **ISRO bus platforms** to remember: **I-1K, I-2K, I-3K, I-6K, I-20K** (kW payload classes).
- ✅ **Degradation factor** in power budget: assume 25% loss over 15 years ($X_d = 0.75$).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mantra:** *Every KTU Module-3 question ultimately reduces to one of three equations — solar power, Stefan-Boltzmann, or Tsiolkovsky. Master these and you master the module.* 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Communication System: Features, Fiber Optic Technology, Total Internal Reflection, Snell's Law, Light Transmission Through an Optical Fiber, Numerical Aperture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Communication System: Features, Fiber Optic Technology, Total Internal Reflection, Snell's Law, Light Transmission Through an Optical Fiber, Numerical Aperture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Communication System — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Communication System** is a method of transmitting information (voice, data, or video) from one point to another by modulating a light wave carrier (typically in the **infrared region 850 nm – 1550 nm**) and propagating it through a low-loss dielectric waveguide known as an **optical fiber**.

The carrier is an **electromagnetic wave** in the optical frequency range ($\approx 10^{14}$ Hz), generated by either a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** or a **Laser Diode (LD)**, guided through a fiber, and recovered at the receiver by a **photodetector** (PIN diode or Avalanche Photodiode).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Light in a Pipe"

Imagine shining a flashlight down a long, curved glass tube. Normally, light would leak out the sides. But if the inner glass is **denser** (higher refractive index) than the outer glass, the light **bounces** off the inner walls like a ball ricocheting down a smooth hallway — never escaping. This bouncing phenomenon is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**, and the glass tube is the **optical fiber**. The "hallway" walls trap the light and guide it, even around bends, all the way to the destination.

### 1.3 Key Features of an Optical Communication System

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test these features as direct 3-mark recall questions. Memorize this list.

| # | Feature | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Enormous Bandwidth** | Carrier frequency $\sim 200$ THz enables multi-Gbps data rates |
| 2 | **Low Attenuation** | Modern silica fibers: **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm window |
| 3 | **EMI Immunity** | Photons carry no charge → no electromagnetic interference |
| 4 | **Small Size & Lightweight** | Fibers are 8–10× thinner and lighter than copper cables |
| 5 | **High Security** | Hard to tap without breaking the link → low cross-talk |
| 6 | **Long Repeater Spacing** | Tens of km between EDFA amplifiers |
| 7 | **No Spark/Short-Circuit Hazard** | Dielectric, safe in flammable environments |
| 8 | **No Ground Loop Problems** | Insulated from electrical noise |

> [!TIP]
> The three "biggest" KTU-asked features are: **(i) huge bandwidth, (ii) low loss, (iii) EMI immunity.** Always lead your answer with these three.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Snell's Law of Refraction — The Gateway Concept

When a light ray strikes the boundary between two media with refractive indices $n_1$ (denser) and $n_2$ (rarer), it bends according to **Snell's Law**:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

**Logical Breakdown:**

- $\theta_1$ = angle of incidence (measured from the **normal** to the interface).
- $\theta_2$ = angle of refraction.
- When light enters a **denser** medium ($n_1 < n_2$), it bends **toward** the normal.
- When light enters a **rarer** medium ($n_1 > n_2$), it bends **away** from the normal.
- **Refractive index** $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum and $v$ is the speed in the medium.

### 2.2 Total Internal Reflection (TIR) — The Operating Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TIR is the **single most important physical phenomenon** that makes optical fiber communication possible. It guarantees 100% light confinement inside the fiber core.

**Conditions Required for TIR:**

1. Light must travel from a **denser medium to a rarer medium** ($n_1 > n_2$).
2. The angle of incidence must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$.

**Critical Angle Definition:** The angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction is exactly $90^\circ$ (i.e., the refracted ray grazes along the interface).

Setting $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$ in Snell's law:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ$$

Solving for $\theta_c$:

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For TIR to be physically possible, the ratio $\dfrac{n_2}{n_1} < 1$ **must** hold. In fiber design, $n_1$ (core) is always slightly higher than $n_2$ (cladding) — typically by **0.3% to 1%** (e.g., $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$).

### 2.3 Light Transmission Through an Optical Fiber

A practical optical fiber is a concentric three-layer structure:

- **Core** (diameter 8–62.5 µm): Carries the light, refractive index $n_1$.
- **Cladding** (diameter 125 µm standard): Lower refractive index $n_2$, provides the TIR boundary.
- **Buffer/Coating** (diameter 250 µm): Mechanical protection, polymer jacket.

The light ray enters the core and undergoes a **zig-zag** path by successive TIRs at the core–cladding boundary. Two key types of fiber exist:

| Fiber Type | Index Profile | Mode Behavior | Typical Use |
|------------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Step-Index Multimode (SI-MMF)** | Abrupt index drop at core–cladding boundary | Many modes propagate; modal dispersion limits bandwidth to $\sim 50$ MHz·km | LAN, short links |
| **Graded-Index Multimode (GI-MMF)** | Parabolic index profile, $n(r)$ decreases with radius | Modes near the edge travel faster → reduces modal dispersion to $\sim 1$ GHz·km | LAN, video |
| **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** | Step-index, very small core ($\sim 9$ µm) | Only one mode propagates → no modal dispersion, bandwidth 100s of GHz·km | Long-haul, telecom backbone |

### 2.4 Numerical Aperture (NA) — The Light-Gathering Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the *light-collecting ability* of an optical fiber. It is the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$.

$$\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

where:
- $\theta_a$ = acceptance angle (in air, $n_0 = 1$).
- $n_1$ = refractive index of core.
- $n_2$ = refractive index of cladding.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!TIP]
> Always state **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks when SI units are missing.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | dimensionless | Refraction at interface |
| 2 | Refractive Index | $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of speeds |
| 3 | Critical Angle | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | radians / degrees | Threshold for TIR |
| 4 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless | Light-gathering power |
| 5 | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | degrees | Maximum entry half-angle |
| 6 | Acceptance Cone | Solid angle $\Omega = \pi (NA)^2$ | steradians | Cone of accepted rays |
| 7 | Relative RI Difference | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} \approx \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | dimensionless | Index contrast (small) |
| 8 | Approx. NA | $NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless | For small $\Delta$ |
| 9 | Speed in Fiber | $v = \dfrac{c}{n_1}$ | m/s | Light speed in core |
| 10 | Time Delay (SI-MMF) | $t = \dfrac{L n_1}{c}$ (longest) to $\dfrac{L n_1}{c}\cos\theta$ (shortest) | seconds | Modal dispersion delay |

> [!WARNING]
> For the relative RI difference $\Delta$, KTU problems frequently give a "small $\Delta$" approximation. **Do not confuse** $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ with the absolute difference $n_1 - n_2$. They differ by a factor of $n_1$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** SMF with EDFAs carry 100+ Tbps across trans-oceanic cables.
- **Medical Endoscopes:** Bundles of GI-MMF image internal organs.
- **Sensor Networks:** Fiber Bragg Gratings (FBG) sense strain/temperature.
- **Data Centers:** Multimode OM3/OM4 fibers for short-reach 100 Gbps links.
- **Military:** EMI-immune, secure battlefield communication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Numerical Aperture and Acceptance Angle

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important derivation** in Module 3 for KTU. It has appeared in nearly every past ESE paper. Practice it until you can reproduce it blind.

**Setup:** Consider a light ray entering the fiber core from air ($n_0 = 1$) at an angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis. It refracts into the core at angle $\theta_1$ (from the **normal** to the entry face), and then strikes the core–cladding interface at an angle $\theta$ (from the **normal** to the side wall).

**Geometric relation at the entry face:**

The angle of incidence at the side wall $\theta$ is the **complement** of the refraction angle $\theta_1$:

$$\theta + \theta_1 = 90^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90^\circ - \theta$$

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the air–core interface:**

$$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

Since $n_0 = 1$ (air):

$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin(90^\circ - \theta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$:

$$\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \cos \theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply the TIR condition at the core–cladding interface:**

For TIR to occur, the ray must strike the side wall at an angle $\theta$ greater than or equal to the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$\theta \geq \theta_c \quad \text{where} \quad \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 3 — Define the acceptance angle $\theta_a$:**

$\theta_a$ is the **maximum** value of $\theta_0$ in air for which TIR just barely occurs, i.e., $\theta = \theta_c$.

Substituting $\theta = \theta_c$ into Step 1:

$$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \cos \theta_c$$

Using the identity $\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c}$:

$$\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{NA \equiv \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Geometric Interpretation (Acceptance Cone):** Any ray launched inside a cone of half-angle $\theta_a$ around the fiber axis will be guided by TIR. This is called the **acceptance cone**, and its solid angle is $\Omega = \pi (\sin \theta_a)^2 = \pi (NA)^2$ steradians.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A step-index fiber has core index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding index $n_2 = 1.45$. Determine:
(a) the numerical aperture,
(b) the acceptance angle, and
(c) the critical angle.

**Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Aperture:**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}$$

$$\boxed{NA \approx 0.384}$$

**(b) Acceptance Angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_a \approx 22.6^\circ}$$

**(c) Critical Angle:**

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.45}{1.50}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9667)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.2^\circ}$$

**Sanity Check:** Since $\theta_c \approx 75.2^\circ$ is close to $90^\circ$, the light ray inside the core must graze the side wall at a very steep angle — consistent with rays traveling mostly parallel to the fiber axis. This is correct for low-NA fibers.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Linkage Between Concepts

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner often asks students to **prove** relationships or **derive** one formula from another. Memorize the logical chain below.

**Conceptual Chain:**

$$\text{Snell's Law} \;\longrightarrow\; \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right) \;\longrightarrow\; \theta = 90^\circ - \theta_1 \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Limiting Cases:**

- If $n_1 \to n_2$ (no index contrast): $NA \to 0$ → no light can be guided. Physical insight: a perfectly uniform glass rod cannot confine light.
- If $n_2 \to 0$ (cladding replaced by vacuum, hypothetical): $NA \to n_1$ → maximum light gathering; in practice unphysical because TIR fails when $n_2 < 0$.
- If $\Delta$ is small ($< 0.01$): $NA \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ — the "weakly guiding" approximation used in single-mode fiber analysis (V-number parameter).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Information Source] --> ENC[Encoder and E O Converter]
    ENC --> TX[Optical Transmitter LED or LASER]
    TX --> FO[Optical Fiber Channel Core Cladding Buffer]
    FO --> RP[Repeater or EDFA Amplifier Optional]
    RP --> FO2[Continued Optical Fiber]
    FO2 --> RX[Optical Receiver PIN or APD Photodetector]
    RX --> DEC[O E Converter and Decoder]
    DEC --> DST[Destination Sink]
```

**Functional Explanation:**

- **E/O Converter:** Electrical signal (voice/data) → modulates the light source.
- **LED vs LASER:** LED (incoherent, broad spectrum) for low-cost MMF links; LASER (coherent, narrow linewidth) for high-speed SMF links.
- **Receiver:** Photodetector converts optical power back into photocurrent.

### 4.2 Ray-Path Diagram — TIR Inside the Core

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ray enters from AIR n0 equals 1] --> B[Refracts at Core Entry Face angle theta1]
    B --> C[Strikes Core-Cladding Boundary angle theta]
    C --> D{Is theta greater than or equal to theta_c}
    D -- Yes --> E[Total Internal Reflection Ray Bounces]
    E --> F[Continues Zig-Zag Down the Core]
    F --> G[Reaches the Other End with Minimal Loss]
    D -- No --> E2[Ray Refracts Out into Cladding LOSS]
```

### 4.3 Fiber Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fiber["Optical Fiber Cross Section"]
        direction TB
        C[Core n1 High Refractive Index 8 to 62.5 micrometers]
        CL[Cladding n2 Lower Refractive Index 125 micrometers OD]
        BUF[Buffer Coating Polymer 250 micrometers OD]
        C --> CL
        CL --> BUF
    end
```

### 4.4 Acceptance Cone Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Fiber Axis] --> Y[Acceptance Cone Half Angle theta_a]
    Y --> Z[All rays inside cone: GUIDED by TIR]
    Y --> W[Rays outside cone: REFRACTED and LOST to cladding]
```

**Reading the Schematic:** The acceptance cone is centered on the fiber axis with half-angle $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$. Any external launch ray that lies within this cone will undergo TIR and reach the far end. Rays outside the cone escape into the cladding within a few centimeters — a phenomenon called **cladding modes**, which are typically stripped off in jacketed fibers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
>
> **Q1.** Define Numerical Aperture of an optical fiber. Mention its significance.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Numerical Aperture (NA) is defined as the sine of the acceptance angle $\theta_a$ of an optical fiber. Mathematically,
>
> $$NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$
>
> where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the core and cladding respectively. **Significance:** NA measures the *light-gathering ability* of the fiber — a higher NA means more optical power can be coupled into the fiber from the source. However, a higher NA also increases modal dispersion, limiting bandwidth. Hence, NA is a key design trade-off parameter. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for significance.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
>
> **Q2.** State and explain the conditions for Total Internal Reflection to occur in an optical fiber.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Total Internal Reflection (TIR) is the phenomenon in which a light ray traveling in a denser medium is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the interface with a rarer medium, with no loss of intensity.
>
> **Conditions for TIR:**
>
> 1. The light must travel from a medium of **higher refractive index** (denser, e.g., core with $n_1$) to a medium of **lower refractive index** (rarer, e.g., cladding with $n_2$). Thus $n_1 > n_2$. *(1 mark)*
> 2. The angle of incidence at the interface must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$. *(1 mark)*
>
> The critical angle is given by $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$. In an optical fiber, the core is designed with a slightly higher refractive index than the cladding, and rays are launched within the acceptance cone, so that TIR confines the light along the fiber length. *(1 mark for explanation + numerical example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optical fiber communication system. List **six** advantages of optical communication over conventional electrical communication. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive an expression for the Numerical Aperture of a step-index optical fiber in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. A fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Find the NA, the acceptance angle, and the critical angle. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):**

The block diagram of an OFC system is:

```
Info Source → Encoder → E/O Modulator → Optical Source (LED/LASER)
     → Fiber Channel → Repeater (optional) → Photodetector (PIN/APD)
     → O/E Converter → Decoder → Destination
```

**Working principle:**

1. The input information (analog voice/digital data) is encoded and converted into an electrical signal.
2. The **E/O converter** uses this signal to modulate the optical carrier (light from an LED or LASER diode). The modulation may be intensity, frequency, or phase-based.
3. The modulated light enters the optical fiber and propagates by successive **Total Internal Reflections** at the core–cladding boundary.
4. After traversing the fiber, the light falls on a **photodetector** (PIN or APD), which converts the optical power variations back into a corresponding electrical current/voltage.
5. The **O/E converter + decoder** reconstructs the original information for the destination.

**Six Advantages of Optical Communication (3 marks):**

1. **Enormous bandwidth** — supports Gbps to Tbps data rates.
2. **Low attenuation** — as low as 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm.
3. **Immunity to EMI** — dielectric medium, no pickup of electrical noise.
4. **Small size and light weight** — easy to install in ducts.
5. **High security** — difficult to tap without detection.
6. **No ground loops or spark hazards** — intrinsically safe in explosive environments.

> *(1 mark block diagram + 3 marks working explanation + 3 marks for any 6 advantages.)*

**(b) Derivation of NA + Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation** — already shown in Section 3.1. The student must reproduce the **5-step derivation** from Snell's law at the air-core interface and the TIR condition at the core-cladding interface, leading to:

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$.

**Step 1 — Numerical Aperture:**

$$NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588}$$

$$\boxed{NA \approx 0.2425}$$

**Step 2 — Acceptance Angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.03^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Critical Angle:**

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.46}{1.48}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.5^\circ$$

> *(4 marks derivation + 3 marks numerical: 1 mark NA, 1 mark acceptance angle, 1 mark critical angle.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** State and explain **Snell's Law of Refraction**. Define the critical angle and derive an expression for it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **features** of an optical communication system. Differentiate between **step-index** and **graded-index** fibers with diagrams. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Snell's Law and Critical Angle (7 marks):**

**Statement of Snell's Law (2 marks):**

When a ray of light travels from one medium to another, the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is a constant, equal to the ratio of the refractive indices of the two media. Mathematically:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

The angles are measured from the **normal** to the interface. The law follows from Fermat's principle of least time and is a direct consequence of the wave nature of light and the boundary conditions for electric/magnetic fields at the interface.

**Definition of Critical Angle (1 mark):**

The critical angle $\theta_c$ is the angle of incidence in the denser medium for which the angle of refraction in the rarer medium is exactly $90^\circ$ (the refracted ray grazes along the interface).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

At the critical angle, $\theta_1 = \theta_c$ and $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$.

Applying Snell's law:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ = n_2 (1) = n_2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}$$

For TIR, the angle of incidence must exceed $\theta_c$. The derivation implicitly assumes $n_1 > n_2$ (denser-to-rarer transition); if $n_1 < n_2$, the ratio exceeds 1 and no real critical angle exists.

**(b) Features and Fiber Comparison (7 marks):**

**Features of Optical Communication (3 marks):**
