1. **Huge bandwidth** — usable spectrum up to ~50 THz.
2. **Low loss** — modern fibers ~0.2 dB/km.
3. **EMI immune** — ideal in industrial/HV environments.
4. **Compact & lightweight** — saves duct space.
5. **Secure** — extremely difficult to tap.
6. **Long repeater spacing** — up to 100+ km with EDFA.

**Step-Index vs Graded-Index Fiber (4 marks):**

| Property | Step-Index Fiber | Graded-Index Fiber |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Refractive index profile** | Abrupt, uniform $n_1$ in core | Parabolic decrease from center to edge |
| **Ray trajectory** | Zig-zag, sharp reflections | Helical, smooth curves |
| **Modal dispersion** | High (max in SI-MMF) | Reduced by factor ~1000 |
| **Bandwidth-distance** | ~50 MHz·km | ~1 GHz·km |
| **Core diameter** | 50–62.5 µm | 50–62.5 µm |
| **Applications** | Short LAN, sensors | LAN, campus backbones |
| **NA profile** | Constant across core | Higher at center, lower at edges |

> *(Student should also draw a sketch of both fibers with refractive index profile vs radius.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\theta$ and $\theta_1$:** In the NA derivation, the angle at the side wall ($\theta$) and the refraction angle at the entry face ($\theta_1$) are **complementary** ($\theta + \theta_1 = 90^\circ$). Students often apply Snell's law using the wrong angle and get $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 + n_2^2}$ — a guaranteed zero for that sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to degrees:** Numerical answers for $\theta_a$ and $\theta_c$ must be in **degrees** (or clearly stated in radians). A bare $\sin^{-1}(0.384)$ without units loses a mark.
> 3. **Conflating "critical angle" and "acceptance angle":** They are entirely different — $\theta_c$ is inside the fiber (measured from normal to side wall), $\theta_a$ is outside (in air, measured from fiber axis). Writing $\theta_c = \theta_a$ is a critical error.
> 4. **Wrong direction of TIR:** TIR occurs when going from **denser to rarer**. In fibers, light bounces from core ($n_1$) into cladding ($n_2$). The mnemonic: *D-to-R = TIR*.
> 5. **Forgetting $n_0 = 1$ in air:** If the launching medium is **not** air (e.g., an index-matching gel with $n_0 \neq 1$), the full Snell's law form $n_0 \sin \theta_0 = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ must be used. Most textbook problems use air — but be alert.
> 6. **Modal dispersion vs chromatic dispersion:** KTU sometimes asks "what limits bandwidth in MMF?" — answer **modal dispersion** (different path lengths), not chromatic dispersion (different wavelengths).
> 7. **Sign convention in Snell's law:** Always measure from the **normal**, not the interface. Examiners will check for this explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] Optical communication uses **light** in the **near-IR** region (850, 1310, 1550 nm) as the carrier.
- [ ] Three main features to lead with: **huge bandwidth, low loss, EMI immunity**.
- [ ] OFC system blocks: **Source → Modulator → Fiber → Detector → Demodulator**.
- [ ] Fiber structure: **Core (n₁) → Cladding (n₂) → Buffer (jacket)**.
- [ ] TIR requires: **(i) denser to rarer, (ii) $\theta \geq \theta_c$**.
- [ ] Snell's Law: $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$.
- [ ] Critical angle: $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- [ ] Numerical Aperture: $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ — **dimensionless**.
- [ ] Acceptance angle: $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ — measured in air from the fiber axis.
- [ ] Higher NA ⇒ more light collected, but more modal dispersion.
- [ ] **Step-index** fiber: sharp index step, zig-zag rays, low bandwidth.
- [ ] **Graded-index** fiber: parabolic index, helical rays, high bandwidth.
- [ ] **Single-mode** fiber: 9 µm core, no modal dispersion, used for long haul.
- [ ] Acceptance cone half-angle = $\theta_a$; solid angle = $\pi (NA)^2$.
- [ ] Units reminder: angles in **degrees** unless specified, NA is dimensionless.
- [ ] Always state TIR conditions before invoking them in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Fiber Communication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Fiber Communication System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Optical Fiber Communication System (OFCS)** is a method of transmitting information from one place to another by sending pulses of infrared (or visible) light through an optically pure, hair-thin strand of glass or plastic fiber. The system relies on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** to guide the modulated light wave (carrier) along the fiber axis, providing immunity to electromagnetic interference, extremely high bandwidth, and low attenuation over long distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** OFCS is a communication link comprising an **optical transmitter**, a **fiber-optic channel** (the light-guiding medium), and an **optical receiver**, which together establish a high-capacity, secure, and noise-immune data transfer path. Light wavelengths used are typically in the **near-infrared region (850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm)**.

The formal transmission process is given by:
$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot e^{-\alpha L}
$$
where $P_{in}$ is the input optical power, $P_{out}$ is the output power, $\alpha$ is the **fiber attenuation coefficient** (in **dB/km**), and $L$ is the **fiber length** (in **km**).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose" of Light

Imagine shining a flashlight down a long, perfectly smooth, transparent glass tube. If the tube were perfectly straight, the light would simply exit the other side. But what if you could *bend* the tube? Normally, light would leak out at every bend. 

The optical fiber solves this engineering puzzle by wrapping the light-carrying **core** in another glass layer called the **cladding**, which has a *lower refractive index*. When light tries to escape, it hits the core-cladding boundary at such a steep angle that it is perfectly *reflected* back inside — just like a ball bouncing inside a curved hallway. This is **Total Internal Reflection**, and it is what allows light to zig-zag along a fiber that is bent, coiled, or wrapped around buildings without losing its energy.

**Key Engineering Insight:** The fiber is not a "wire for light" — it is a *waveguide* that traps light using refractive index differences, not electrical conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fiber optics matter in modern engineering:**
> - **Bandwidth:** Tens of **THz** versus MHz for copper cables.
> - **Loss:** As low as **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm.
> - **Security:** Almost impossible to tap without detection.
> - **EMI Immunity:** Insensitive to electrical noise, lightning, and RF interference.
> - **Weight & Size:** Dramatically lighter and thinner than copper.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed of light in vacuum ($c$) | $3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** | Fundamental constant |
| Refractive index of vacuum ($n_0$) | **1** (dimensionless) | Reference medium |
| Typical $n_{core}$ | **1.46 – 1.48** | Glass fiber (silica) |
| Typical $n_{clad}$ | **1.45 – 1.47** | Slightly less than core |
| Operating wavelengths | **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** | Near-infrared windows |
| Attenuation target | **< 0.5 dB/km** | Modern silica fiber |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of an OFCS

An OFCS is logically partitioned into three functional stages: the **Transmitter Section**, the **Fiber Channel**, and the **Receiver Section**.

**Stage 1 — Transmitter Section:**
- **Electrical Interface:** Accepts binary data (E.g., 1s and 0s from a computer/voice codec).
- **Driver / Modulator Circuit:** Converts electrical bits into current pulses.
- **Optical Source:** Either an **LED** (for short-haul, low-cost links) or a **LASER diode** (for long-haul, high-bit-rate links). The source emits infrared light whose *intensity* is modulated in step with the input data (OOK — On-Off Keying).
- **Coupling Lens:** Efficiently launches the emitted light into the fiber core.

**Stage 2 — Fiber Channel:**
- The **Optical Fiber** itself — a cylindrical dielectric waveguide.
- **Splicing / Connectors** to join fiber segments.
- **Optical Amplifiers** (e.g., **EDFA** — Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier) periodically boost the signal without converting to electricity.
- **Repeaters** (in older systems) perform O-E-O conversion to regenerate the signal.

**Stage 3 — Receiver Section:**
- **Photodetector:** Either a **PIN photodiode** or an **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** that converts incoming optical photons back into an electrical current.
- **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA / TIA):** Amplifies the weak photocurrent.
- **Signal Processing / Decision Circuit:** Recovers the original binary bitstream with minimal Bit Error Rate (BER).

---

### 2.2 Refractive Index & Snell's Law (The Optical Foundation)

The entire principle of fiber guiding rests on **Snell's Law** of refraction, which describes how a light ray bends when passing between media of different refractive indices.

Let $n_1$ be the refractive index of the core and $n_2$ be the refractive index of the cladding, where $n_1 > n_2$. A light ray traveling inside the core strikes the core-cladding interface at an angle $\phi$ (measured from the *normal* to the interface). The transmitted (refracted) ray follows:

$$
n_1 \sin(\phi_1) = n_2 \sin(\phi_2)
$$

If the angle of incidence $\phi_1$ is increased, the refracted angle $\phi_2$ reaches **90°**. Beyond this critical angle, no refraction occurs — the ray is *totally* reflected back into the core. This is the **Critical Angle $\phi_c$**:

$$
\sin(\phi_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition for Total Internal Reflection (TIR):** The angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary must be **strictly greater** than $\phi_c$, which requires the refractive index of the core to be **strictly greater** than that of the cladding.

---

### 2.3 Acceptance Angle & Numerical Aperture (NA)

Not every light ray entering the fiber will be guided. Only rays that strike the entry face at a sufficiently shallow angle (with respect to the fiber axis) will undergo TIR. This maximum entry angle is the **Acceptance Angle $\theta_a$**.

By applying Snell's law at the air-core boundary and using the critical angle condition at the core-cladding boundary, we arrive at the **Numerical Aperture (NA)**:

$$
\text{NA} = n_0 \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (typically air, so $n_0 = 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> The **NA** is a dimensionless figure-of-merit that quantifies the *light-gathering ability* of the fiber. A higher NA means a larger acceptance cone and easier coupling of light into the fiber.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following equations are guaranteed KTU question-stems. Memorize the variables and units — derivations of these are frequently asked.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | Speed of light inside a medium of index $n$ | m/s |
| 2 | $n_1 \sin(\phi_1) = n_2 \sin(\phi_2)$ | Snell's law of refraction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sin(\phi_c) = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Critical angle for TIR | rad / deg |
| 4 | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical Aperture of fiber | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ | Acceptance angle (in air) | rad / deg |
| 6 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Power after attenuation in fiber | W or dBm |
| 7 | $\alpha_{\text{dB}} = -\dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Attenuation coefficient | dB/km |
| 8 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Relative Refractive Index Difference | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot \text{NA}$ | Normalized Frequency (V-Number) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{\text{mod}} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | Number of guided modes (multimode) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{I_1 - I_0}{2\sqrt{2}\,\sigma}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate (digital optical link) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $NEP = \dfrac{h\nu}{\eta}$ | Noise Equivalent Power of detector | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ |
| 13 | $R = \dfrac{\eta q}{h\nu}$ | Responsivity of photodiode | A/W |

**Real-world Engineering Utility:** The **NA** formula determines the design of source-to-fiber couplers in **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home)** networks. The **V-number** dictates whether a fiber is *single-mode* or *multi-mode* — a fundamental design choice for telecom operators. The **attenuation equation** is used by network planners to compute *link budget* and *maximum repeater spacing* in trans-oceanic cables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Numerical Aperture (NA)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams. We derive NA from the two Snell's law applications at the two boundaries of the fiber.

**Step 1 — Setup the geometry.**

Consider a meridional ray entering the fiber from air ($n_0 = 1$) at an external angle $\theta_a$ to the fiber axis. At the air-core interface (point A), the angle of incidence is $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_a$. The ray refracts and travels inside the core at an angle $\theta_r$ to the fiber axis, where:

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_r = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90° - \theta_r
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's Law at the air-core interface (point A).**

The medium of incidence is air ($n_0 = 1$) and the medium of refraction is the core ($n_1$). Snell's law gives:

$$
n_0 \sin(\theta_1) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)
$$

Substituting $n_0 = 1$ and $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_r$:

$$
1 \cdot \sin(90° - \theta_r) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)
$$

Since $\sin(90° - x) = \cos(x)$:

$$
\cos(\theta_r) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Snell's Law at the core-cladding interface (point B).**

The refracted ray inside the core strikes the core-cladding boundary at an angle $\phi_1$ measured from the normal. From the geometry of the fiber, the angle $\phi_1$ and the refraction angle $\theta_r$ are complementary:

$$
\phi_1 = 90° - \theta_r
$$

For the ray to be guided by Total Internal Reflection, this angle must be **at least equal to** the critical angle $\phi_c$, where:

$$
\sin(\phi_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

Setting $\phi_1 = \phi_c$ (the limiting case):

$$
\sin(90° - \theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

Since $\sin(90° - x) = \cos(x)$:

$$
\cos(\theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $\cos(\theta_r)$.**

From Step 2, we have $\cos(\theta_r) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)$. From Step 3, we have $\cos(\theta_r) = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$. Equating:

$$
n_1 \sin(\theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

Solving for $\sin(\theta_r)$:

$$
\sin(\theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(x) + \cos^2(x) = 1$.**

We have $\cos(\theta_r) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)$, so:

$$
\sin^2(\theta_r) + \cos^2(\theta_r) = 1
$$

$$
\sin^2(\theta_r) + n_1^2 \sin^2(\theta_r) = 1
$$

$$
\sin^2(\theta_r) \left(1 + n_1^2\right) = 1
$$

This step uses an approximation commonly applied in fiber optics: since $\Delta$ is very small, $n_1 + n_2 \approx 2n_1$, leading to the simplified, exam-standard form. For the rigorous KTU-expected form, square the result of Step 2 directly:

$$
\cos^2(\theta_r) = 1 - \sin^2(\theta_r) = 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\cos(\theta_r) = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine with the air-core Snell's law.**

From Step 2: $\cos(\theta_r) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)$. But at the entry face, $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_r$, so $\sin(\theta_1) = \cos(\theta_r)$. Substituting:

$$
\sin(\theta_1) = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

Recognize that $\theta_1 = \theta_a$ (the **acceptance angle**) since the ray must strike the entry face within the acceptance cone. Also, since $n_0 = 1$:

$$
\text{NA} = n_0 \sin(\theta_a) = \sin(\theta_a) = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $n_1$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The final boxed result is the canonical KTU answer. Students often forget that the **denominator $n_1$ drops out** only when the fiber is *weakly guiding* ($\Delta \ll 1$). In a strict exam setting, writing $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ is universally accepted and expected.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Relationship Between $\Delta$ and NA

The **Relative Refractive Index Difference** $\Delta$ is defined as:

$$
\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}
$$

We now express NA in terms of $\Delta$:

**Step 1 — Square the NA expression.**

$$
\text{NA}^2 = n_1^2 - n_2^2 = (n_1 - n_2)(n_1 + n_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $n_1$.**

$$
\text{NA}^2 = n_1 \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}\right)(n_1 + n_2) = n_1 \cdot \Delta \cdot (n_1 + n_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the weakly guiding approximation** ($n_1 \approx n_2$):

$$
n_1 + n_2 \approx 2 n_1
$$

So:

$$
\text{NA}^2 \approx n_1 \cdot \Delta \cdot 2 n_1 = 2 n_1^2 \Delta
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NA} \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The approximation $n_1 + n_2 \approx 2n_1$ is valid **only** when $\Delta \ll 1$ (typically $\Delta < 0.01$). If a problem states the actual values of $n_1$ and $n_2$, compute NA directly as $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem Walkthrough — Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-pattern):** A silica optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.46$. Find:
1. The numerical aperture (NA).
2. The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ in air.
3. The critical angle $\phi_c$ at the core-cladding interface.
4. Whether the fiber is single-mode or multi-mode for a 1300 nm source, given core radius $a = 25 \,\mu\text{m}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute NA.**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2}
$$

Evaluating the squares:

$$
(1.48)^2 = 2.1904, \quad (1.46)^2 = 2.1316
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the acceptance angle.**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}) = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.04°
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the critical angle.**

$$
\sin(\phi_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.46}{1.48} = 0.9865
$$

$$
\phi_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.55°
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the V-number.**

$$
V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot \text{NA}
$$

Convert $a = 25 \,\mu\text{m} = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m and $\lambda = 1300 \text{ nm} = 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$
V = \frac{2 \pi (25 \times 10^{-6})}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 0.2425
$$

$$
V = (2\pi \cdot 19.23) \cdot 0.2425
$$

$$
V = 120.83 \cdot 0.2425 \approx 29.30
$$

Since $V \gg 2.405$ (the single-mode cutoff), the fiber is **multi-mode**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of modes** $N_{\text{mod}} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2} = \dfrac{(29.30)^2}{2} \approx 429$ modes. The fiber supports ~429 guided modes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Optical Fiber Communication System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow from electrical source to electrical destination, with all the functional subsystems a KTU examiner expects you to know.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elecSrc[A1 Electrical Source\nVoice or Data Bits] --> drv[A2 Driver Circuit\nand Coding]
    drv --> led[A3 Optical Source\nLED or LASER Diode]
    led --> coup[A4 Coupler\nLens or Butt Joint]
    coup --> fiber1[A5 Fiber Segment 1\nCore plus Cladding]
    fiber1 --> splice1[A6 Splicing Point\nFusion or Mechanical]
    splice1 --> fiber2[A7 Fiber Segment 2\nLong Haul Cable]
    fiber2 --> edfa[A8 Optical Amplifier\nEDFA Stage]
    edfa --> fiber3[A9 Fiber Segment 3\nFinal Drop]
    fiber3 --> conn[A10 Output Connector\nSC FC or LC Type]
    conn --> det[A11 Photodetector\nPIN or APD]
    det --> tia[A12 Transimpedance Amplifier\nTIA Stage]
    tia --> proc[A13 Signal Processor\nDecision and BER]
    proc --> dest[A14 Electrical Destination\nRecovered Bits]

    style elecSrc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style drv fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style led fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style coup fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style fiber1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style splice1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style fiber2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style edfa fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style fiber3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style conn fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style det fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style tia fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style proc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style dest fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Blue** blocks = **Transmitter Section**
- **Green** blocks = **Fiber Channel** (the light-guiding path)
- **Red** blocks = **Receiver Section**

---

### 4.2 Light Ray Geometry Inside the Step-Index Fiber

This Mermaid diagram represents the *optical geometry* of a meridional ray undergoing Total Internal Reflection inside a step-index fiber.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    air[A1 Air n0 equals 1] -->|incident ray| face1[A2 Entry Face]
    face1 -->|refracted ray| core[A3 Core with index n1]
    core -->|hits boundary at phi1| bdry1[A4 Boundary Point 1]
    bdry1 -->|reflected ray| core
    core -->|hits boundary at phi1| bdry2[A5 Boundary Point 2]
    bdry2 -->|reflected ray| core
    core -->|hits boundary at phi1| bdry3[A6 Boundary Point 3]
    bdry3 -->|reflected ray| core
    core -->|exits via refracted ray| face2[A7 Exit Face]
    face2 -->|output ray| air2[A8 Air n0 equals 1]

    style air fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style face1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style core fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style bdry1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style bdry2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style bdry3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style face2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style air2 fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The ray enters the fiber at the **entry face (A2)**, refracts into the core, and travels as a **zig-zag meridional ray** along the fiber axis.
- At every boundary point (A4, A5, A6), the ray is *totally internally reflected* because the incidence angle $\phi_1$ exceeds the critical angle $\phi_c$.
- At the **exit face (A7)**, the ray refracts back into air and emerges parallel to the original entry ray (by reversibility of light).

---

### 4.3 Classification Flow of Fiber Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[A1 Optical Fibers] --> mm[A2 Multimode Fibers]
    start --> sm[A3 Single Mode Fibers]

    mm --> si[A4 Step Index Multimode]
    mm --> gi[A5 Graded Index Multimode]

    si --> desc1[A6 Constant n1 across core\nSharp index change at boundary]
    gi --> desc2[A7 n1 varies parabolically\nMax at center zero at edge]

    sm --> smdesc[A8 Core diameter 8 to 10 um\nV less than 2.405\nLong haul telecom]

    style start fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style mm fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style sm fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style si fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style gi fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style desc1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b02a37
    style desc2 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b02a37
    style smdesc fill:#d2f4d4,stroke:#198754
```

**Engineering Trade-off Matrix:**

| Fiber Type | Core Diameter | Bandwidth | Typical Use | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Step-Index MM | 50–200 µm | Low (~20 MHz·km) | LANs, short links | Low |
| Graded-Index MM | 50–62.5 µm | High (~1 GHz·km) | LANs, campus backbones | Moderate |
| Single-Mode (SMF) | 8–10 µm | Very High (>100 GHz·km) | Long-haul, submarine | High |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term *Numerical Aperture* of an optical fiber. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber is defined as the sine of the maximum angle $\theta_a$ at which a light ray can enter the fiber and still be guided by total internal reflection. Mathematically:

$$
\text{NA} = n_0 \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the core and cladding respectively, and $n_0$ is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (usually air, $n_0 = 1$).

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies the **light-gathering ability** of the fiber — higher NA means more light can be coupled in.
2. It determines the **cone of acceptance** within which incident rays will be guided.
3. It influences the **number of modes** propagated in a multimode fiber (via the V-number).
4. It is a key design parameter for **source-to-fiber couplers** and connector alignment tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **(3 marks)** — Definition (1), Formula (1), Significance (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between *intrinsic* and *extrinsic* attenuation losses in optical fibers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Intrinsic Attenuation | Extrinsic Attenuation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Caused by the *inherent material properties* of the glass itself. | Caused by *external factors* introduced during manufacturing, installation, or use. |
| **Examples** | Rayleigh scattering (due to microscopic density fluctuations), infrared absorption (lattice vibrations of SiO₂). | Bending losses (microbends and macrobends), impurity absorption (OH⁻ ions, transition metals like Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺), connector/splice misalignment. |
| **Nature** | Fundamental, unavoidable, depends on wavelength. | Engineering, can be minimized by better manufacturing and handling. |
| **Dominant at** | Rayleigh scattering dominates at shorter wavelengths ($\propto 1/\lambda^4$); IR absorption dominates at long wavelengths (> 1600 nm). | Bending losses dominate when fibers are sharply curved below the critical bend radius. |

> [!NOTE]
> **(3 marks)** — Tabular comparison with 2–3 distinguishing points.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either Q(A) or Q(B) is given as choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 1

**Q(A). (a) Derive an expression for the Numerical Aperture (NA) of a step-index optical fiber in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. State the condition for total internal reflection. (7 marks)**

**(b) An optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Determine: (i) the numerical aperture, (ii) the acceptance angle in air, and (iii) the relative refractive index difference $\Delta$. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of NA:**

Consider a meridional ray entering the fiber from air ($n_0 = 1$) at angle $\theta_a$ to the fiber axis.

**Step 1:** Apply Snell's law at the **air-core boundary**:

$$
n_0 \sin(\theta_a) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)
$$

where $\theta_r$ is the refraction angle inside the core.

**Step 2:** The refracted ray inside the core strikes the core-cladding boundary. Let $\phi_1$ be the angle of incidence at this boundary. From the geometry:

$$
\phi_1 = 90° - \theta_r
$$

**Step 3:** For **Total Internal Reflection** to occur, $\phi_1$ must be **greater than or equal to** the critical angle $\phi_c$, where:

$$
\sin(\phi_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 4:** Setting $\phi_1 = \phi_c$ (the limiting case):

$$
\sin(90° - \theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

$$
\cos(\theta_r) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 5:** From Step 1: $\sin(\theta_a) = n_1 \sin(\theta_r)$. Using $\sin^2(\theta_r) + \cos^2(\theta_r) = 1$:

$$
\sin^2(\theta_r) = 1 - \cos^2(\theta_r) = 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}
$$

$$
\sin(\theta_r) = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back into Snell's law:

$$
\sin(\theta_a) = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

Since $n_0 = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}
$$

**Condition for TIR:** $n_1 > n_2$ and the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary must exceed $\phi_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

Given: $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$.

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}
$$

$$
(1.50)^2 = 2.2500, \quad (1.45)^2 = 2.1025
$$

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.3841
$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.3841) \approx 22.59°
$$

**(iii) Relative Refractive Index Difference:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.50 - 1.45}{1.50} = \frac{0.05}{1.50} \approx 0.0333 \;\;(\text{or } 3.33\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)** — Full derivation in (a); formula + substitution + numerical answer in (b).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 2

**Q(B). (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Optical Fiber Communication System. Identify the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the following losses in optical fibers with diagrams: (i) Rayleigh scattering loss, (ii) Bending loss. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working of OFCS:**

```
[Electrical Source] → [Driver / Coder] → [Optical Source (LED/LASER)] 
        → [Coupler] → [FIBER CHANNEL (Splices + Amplifiers + Connectors)] 
        → [Photodetector (PIN/APD)] → [Amplifier (TIA)] → [Signal Processor] 
        → [Electrical Destination]
```

**Working Principle (Block-by-Block):**

| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1. Electrical Source** | Generates the baseband signal (voice, video, data). |
| **2. Driver / Coder** | Converts bits into proper current pulses; performs line coding (NRZ, RZ). |
| **3. Optical Source** | An **LED** (incoherent, cheap, low power) or **Laser diode** (coherent, high power, high speed) that converts electrical pulses into modulated light. |
| **4. Coupler** | Focuses the emitted light into the tiny fiber core. |
| **5. Fiber Channel** | The optical fiber itself. Light propagates by **Total Internal Reflection**. May include splices, connectors, and **EDFAs** (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers) that boost the signal optically. |
| **6. Photodetector** | A **PIN diode** or **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** that absorbs photons and generates a small photocurrent. |
| **7. Amplifier (TIA)** | A **Transimpedance Amplifier** that converts the weak current into a usable voltage. |
| **8. Signal Processor** | Performs decision, clock recovery, decoding, and outputs the recovered bits to the destination. |

---

**(b) Fiber Losses:**

**(i) Rayleigh Scattering Loss:**

Rayleigh scattering is an **intrinsic loss** caused by microscopic density fluctuations and compositional variations frozen into the glass during the manufacturing process. These sub-micron irregularities act as scattering centers.

- The scattered light radiates in all directions; only the forward-scattered component continues to propagate.
- Loss is **wavelength-dependent**, scaling as $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^4}$ — meaning shorter wavelengths suffer more.
- It is the **dominant loss mechanism** in modern silica fibers in the 800–1500 nm range.
- Typical coefficient: $\sim 0.2$–$1$ dB/km depending on wavelength.

**(ii) Bending Loss:**

Bending loss occurs when the fiber is curved and can be of two types:

- **Macrobending Loss:** When the bend radius is comparable to the fiber diameter (e.g., sharp 90° bends). The light ray strikes the boundary at a sub-critical angle and *leaks* out into the cladding. Loss increases **exponentially** as bend radius decreases below a critical value (typically $\sim 1$–$2$ cm).
- **Microbending Loss:** Caused by tiny, random, microscopic distortions of the fiber axis due to external pressure, poor cabling, or temperature variations. These distortions couple power from guided modes to radiation modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)** — Block diagram (3) + Working explanation (4) for (a); Two loss mechanisms explained (3.5 each) for (b).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on OFCS questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{}$** in the NA formula — writing $\text{NA} = n_1^2 - n_2^2$ (wrong) instead of $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.
> 2. **Confusing angles** — mixing up $\theta_a$ (acceptance angle, measured from fiber axis) with $\phi_c$ (critical angle, measured from the normal). Always re-state *which angle* you are computing.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in attenuation** — $\alpha$ can be in **dB/km** (logarithmic) or **Np/km** (linear, nepers). Verify which unit the problem uses before plugging in.
> 4. **Mixing up $\Delta$ and $\text{NA}$** — the relative index difference $\Delta$ is dimensionless, not equal to NA.
> 5. **Skipping the "condition for TIR"** statement in derivations — examiners deduct up to 2 marks if $n_1 > n_2$ is not explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer of numerical problems — always write NA as "dimensionless" or $\theta_a$ as "degrees/radians."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **OFCS** = Optical Transmitter + Fiber Channel + Optical Receiver.
- [ ] **Light guidance** is based on **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core-cladding boundary.
- [ ] **TIR condition:** $n_1 > n_2$ and incidence angle $\phi_1 \ge \phi_c$ where $\sin(\phi_c) = n_2/n_1$.
- [ ] **NA = $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$** — figure-of-merit for light-gathering ability.
- [ ] **Acceptance angle** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ — half-angle of the entry cone.
- [ ] **Relative Index Difference** $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$, typically $\ll 1$.
- [ ] **V-number** $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot \text{NA}$ — single-mode cutoff is $V < 2.405$.
- [ ] **Number of modes** in multimode fiber $\approx V^2/2$.
- [ ] **Attenuation** units: **dB/km**; Loss = $\alpha L$.
- [ ] **Intrinsic losses:** Rayleigh scattering ($\propto 1/\lambda^4$), IR absorption.
- [ ] **Extrinsic losses:** Bending (macro & micro), impurity absorption, connector loss.
- [ ] **Optical sources:** LED (incoherent, low-cost), LASER (coherent, high-speed).
- [ ] **Optical detectors:** PIN (simple, low-noise), APD (high gain, internal multiplication).
- [ ] **EDFA** — Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier — in-line optical amplification at 1550 nm window.
- [ ] **Fiber types:** Step-Index MM, Graded-Index MM, Single-Mode.
- [ ] **Operating windows:** 850 nm (LAN), 1300 nm (zero-dispersion), 1550 nm (lowest loss).
- [ ] **Standard equation for power loss:** $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In Part B numerical problems, always show (1) the formula, (2) the substitution with units, and (3) the final boxed answer. Examiners award **method marks** even if the arithmetic slips — but only if your *logical steps* are visible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellular Communications: Principles of Cellular Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cellular Communications: Principles of Cellular Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cellular Network (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A **cellular network** is a radio network distributed over a geographic area called a *cell*, served by at least one fixed-location transceiver known as a *base station (BS)* or *cell site*. The cellular concept fundamentally relies on **frequency reuse**, where the same set of frequencies is reused in non-adjacent cells that are sufficiently separated spatially to limit co-channel interference.

The cellular architecture is the cornerstone of modern mobile communication systems (2G GSM, 3G UMTS, 4G LTE, 5G NR). Instead of using a single high-power transmitter to cover a wide area, the service area is subdivided into smaller geographic regions — *cells* — each with its own low-power transmitter.

### Key Terminology
| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cell** | A small geographic coverage region served by a base station. |
| **Base Station (BS)** | The fixed transceiver (tower + antenna system) that communicates with mobile stations inside its cell. |
| **Mobile Switching Center (MSC)** | The central coordinating exchange that connects calls between base stations and the public telephone network. |
| **Frequency Reuse** | The repeated use of the same frequency channel in geographically separated cells. |
| **Cluster (N)** | A group of N cells that collectively use all available channels exactly once. |
| **Co-channel Cell** | Cells in different clusters that use the same set of frequencies. |
| **Handoff / Handover** | The process of transferring an active call from one base station to another as the mobile crosses cell boundaries. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every concept from this module is built on the *cellular principle* — replace one high-power transmitter with many low-power transmitters, and reuse frequencies to multiply capacity N-fold.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a coffee shop that gets too crowded. Instead of building one giant coffee shop for the whole city, the owner builds many **small coffee shops on different streets**. Each small shop is a *cell*. The shop owner discovers that if two coffee shops sit right next to each other, customers hear each other's orders and get confused — this is **co-channel interference**. So the owner spaces them out: the next shop using the *same brand of coffee* must be at least three streets away. This separation distance is what we call the **frequency reuse distance**.

Now, what happens when a customer walks from one shop's area to another? The staff *pass the order* to the next shop seamlessly — this is **handover**. The whole system can now serve thousands of customers simultaneously, even though each small shop only has a few coffee types — because the same coffee types are used again and again in distant shops.

In cellular networks:
- **The city** = The service area (city, country, continent).
- **The small coffee shops** = Cells with their base stations.
- **The few coffee types in each shop** = The frequency channels.
- **The spacing rule** = The frequency reuse distance $D$.
- **The hand-off of the customer** = The handover procedure.
- **The order confusion** = Co-channel interference.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *Cells reuse, handsets move, handoffs happen, interference is the tax we pay for capacity.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Carrier frequencies:** 800 MHz (2G), 900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 2.1 GHz, 2.6 GHz, 3.5 GHz, 26/28/39 GHz (mmWave 5G).
- **Cell radius (typical):** Macro cell: **1 – 35 km**, Micro cell: **0.1 – 1 km**, Pico cell: **< 100 m**, Femto cell: **< 10 m**.
- **Hexagonal cell side (radius) $R$:** Used as the standard geometric model because hexagons tessellate perfectly and approximate circular coverage with minimal overlap.
- **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Standard reference:** The hexagonal cell layout (proposed by V. H. Mac Donald, 1979) is the **canonical engineering model** in all KTU 2024 cellular texts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cellular Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The cellular system maximizes capacity per unit bandwidth using five interlocking ideas. We examine each as a structured *why–how* pair.

### Step 1 — Subdivide the Service Area
- **Why:** A single high-power transmitter wastes spectrum because it serves all users over the entire region with one shared set of frequencies.
- **How:** Partition the region into hexagonal cells. Each cell uses a *subset* of the total channels assigned to it by a base station. The hexagonal shape tessellates without gaps, giving the most accurate approximation of a circular radiation footprint.

### Step 2 — Frequency Reuse (The Core Idea)
- **Why:** Total available bandwidth is finite and expensive.
- **How:** Divide the available frequency set into $K$ channel groups. Assign **one channel group per cell** within a cluster of $N$ cells. The same $K$ channel groups are then re-assigned in the next cluster, and so on. This multiplies system capacity by a factor of $N$.

### Step 3 — Cluster Size $N$
- **Why:** To keep co-channel interference tolerable.
- **How:** $N$ must follow the constraint of the hexagonal geometry:
$$
N = i^2 + ij + j^2
$$
where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers (not both zero). The **valid cluster sizes** are $N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, \dots$

### Step 4 — Co-channel Reuse Distance $D$
- **Why:** Reusing the same channel too close to its original cell causes destructive interference.
- **How:** $D$ is the distance between the centers of two co-channel cells. By hexagonal geometry:
$$
\frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$
where $R$ is the cell radius (hex side length).

### Step 5 — Co-channel Interference Ratio (CCIR or $C/I$)
- **Why:** It determines the actual quality of the reused channel.
- **How:** Using a propagation path-loss exponent of $\gamma$ (typically 4 in urban environments):
$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^{\gamma}}{i_0} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{\gamma}}{i_0}
$$
where $i_0$ is the **number of co-channel interferers in the first tier** (typically $i_0 = 6$ for omnidirectional hexagonal cells).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical formula table that the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to reproduce in a 14-mark derivation question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cluster size | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | dimensionless | $i,j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$, not both zero |
| 2 | Frequency reuse ratio (q) | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless | $D$ = co-channel distance, $R$ = cell radius |
| 3 | Co-channel interference ratio (omnidirectional) | $C/I = (q)^{\gamma} / 6 = (3N)^{\gamma/2} / 6$ | dimensionless | $\gamma = 4$ typical urban |
| 4 | Minimum C/I (for voice) | $C/I \ge 18 \text{ dB}$ (i.e. factor 63.1) | dB | Standard for analog FM |
| 5 | Capacity (channels/cell) | $C_{\text{cell}} = K / N$ | channels | $K$ = total channels allocated to system |
| 6 | Total system capacity | $C_{\text{sys}} = M \cdot K$ | channels | $M$ = total number of cells |
| 7 | Cell splitting new radius | $R_2 = R_1 / \sqrt{2}$ when traffic doubles | m | Used to scale down cell area |
| 8 | Sectoring capacity factor | $C/I$ improves by $10 \log_{10}(i_0)$ dB | dB | $i_0$ reduces from 6 to 2 (120° sector) or 1 (60° sector) |
| 9 | Handoff decision time | $\tau \le \Delta t_{\text{stay}}$ | s | Must be less than dwell time |
| 10 | Number of cells in service area | $M = \text{Area} / A_{\text{cell}} = (1.5 R^2 \sqrt{3})^{-1} \cdot \text{Area}$ | cells | Hex cell area = $1.5 R^2 \sqrt{3}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The unit **dB** is *not* a unit of physical quantity. Always write $C/I = 18$ dB but the linear form is $C/I = 63.1$. KTU examiners deduct marks if you mix dB and linear values without conversion.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The cellular principle is not just an academic idea — it powers the entire global mobile ecosystem:

- **Telecom Industry:** Operators (Jio, Airtel, Vi in India) deploy **hexagonal planning** for their GSM, LTE and 5G NR networks. Sectoring with 120° antennas is the universal macro-cell standard.
- **Public Safety:** Police, fire, and ambulance networks use **cluster size $N = 7$** to maximize capacity while keeping co-channel interference under 18 dB.
- **Satellite–Terrestrial Integration:** The same co-channel interference equations govern frequency coordination between terrestrial cellular and mobile satellite systems.
- **IoT & Smart Cities:** Massive **cell splitting** and **femto cell** deployment enable millions of low-power devices per square kilometre — the foundation of NB-IoT and 5G mMTC.

> [!TIP]
> **Why hexagonal cells?** Circles leave gaps; squares overlap or have non-uniform antenna patterns; **hexagons** model a circular footprint, tile perfectly, and give the cleanest closed-form geometry for $D$ and $N$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster Size $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$

**Goal:** Show that the number of cells per cluster in a hexagonal layout must satisfy this equation.

Consider a regular hexagonal lattice. Pick any cell. Move $i$ cells along one *principal axis* (chain of hexagons sharing an edge), then turn 60° counter-clockwise and move $j$ cells along that second chain. The end point of this *lattice vector* lies on a co-channel cell.

The center-to-center distance between these two co-channel cells is the **reuse distance $D$**. By the law of cosines on the hexagonal lattice (interior angle 60°):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= (iR_{\text{chain}})^2 + (jR_{\text{chain}})^2 - 2(iR_{\text{chain}})(jR_{\text{chain}})\cos(120^\circ) \\
    &= i^2 R^2 + j^2 R^2 + ij R^2 \\
    &= (i^2 + ij + j^2) R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R$ is the cell radius (hex side), and we used $\cos(120^\circ) = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ and the fact that the chain-to-chain distance equals $R$ in the lattice.

The total number of cells enclosed by the lattice vector equals $N$, so:
$$
N = \frac{D^2}{R^2} = i^2 + ij + j^2
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reuse Distance Ratio $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$

**Goal:** Express $D/R$ in the standard KTU form using the *shift parameters* $(i, j)$.

In the hexagonal lattice, the standard procedure is to rotate the lattice vector by 60° repeatedly. The **shortest** co-channel vector (and the one the KTU examiner expects) corresponds to choosing $i$ and $j$ such that the total displacement in the *x–y plane* gives the canonical expression.

The cell-center coordinates in a hexagonal grid are conveniently expressed as:

$$
x = R\left( i + \tfrac{j}{2} \right), \quad y = R \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, j
$$

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= x^2 + y^2 \\
    &= R^2 \left( i + \tfrac{j}{2} \right)^2 + R^2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{4} j^2 \\
    &= R^2 \left( i^2 + ij + \tfrac{j^2}{4} + \tfrac{3j^2}{4} \right) \\
    &= R^2 (i^2 + ij + j^2) = R^2 N
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the square root of both sides:
$$
D = R\sqrt{N}
$$

Wait — this gives $D = R\sqrt{N}$, not $D = R\sqrt{3N}$. The factor of $\sqrt{3}$ arises because the **distance between centers of two adjacent cells in the lattice** is not $R$ but $\sqrt{3}R$. Using the centre-to-centre distance of adjacent cells $R_{\text{adj}} = \sqrt{3}R$, the canonical form becomes:

$$
\frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad D = R\sqrt{3N}
$$

This is the form that appears in every KTU 2024 textbook and exam paper.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Co-channel Interference Ratio $C/I$

**Goal:** Compute the carrier-to-interference ratio for omnidirectional hexagonal cells in the worst case (first tier of co-channel cells).

Assume the mobile is at the cell boundary (worst-case path loss to its own base station) and that the first tier of co-channel cells lies at distance $D$ from the mobile (approximation valid at the boundary).

The received carrier power at distance $R$ from the desired BS:
$$
C = P_t \cdot R^{-\gamma}
$$

The received interference power from one co-channel BS at distance $D$:
$$
I_{\text{one}} = P_t \cdot D^{-\gamma}
$$

There are $i_0 = 6$ first-tier co-channel interferers (for the standard hexagonal layout). The total interference:
$$
I = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot D^{-\gamma}
$$

Hence:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{I} &= \frac{P_t R^{-\gamma}}{6 P_t D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{6} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^{\gamma} \\
           &= \frac{1}{6} (\sqrt{3N})^{\gamma} = \frac{(3N)^{\gamma/2}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

For urban path-loss exponent $\gamma = 4$:
$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3N)^2}{6} = \frac{9 N^2}{6} = 1.5 N^2
$$

In **decibels**:
$$
\left( \frac{C}{I} \right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 N^2)
$$

For $N = 7$: $C/I = 1.5 \times 49 = 73.5 \approx 18.66$ dB ✓ (just above the 18 dB threshold for analog FM).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Problem:** *"A cellular system uses a cluster size of 7, a cell radius of 4 km, and an urban path-loss exponent of 4. Compute the co-channel reuse ratio, the co-channel interference ratio, and the signal-to-interference ratio in dB."*

**Given:** $N = 7$, $R = 4$ km, $\gamma = 4$, $i_0 = 6$.

**Step 1 — Co-channel reuse ratio:**
$$
q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583
$$

**Step 2 — Co-channel distance:**
$$
D = R \sqrt{3N} = 4 \times \sqrt{21} = 4 \times 4.583 = 18.33 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Co-channel interference ratio (linear):**
$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3N)^{\gamma/2}}{6} = \frac{(3 \times 7)^2}{6} = \frac{21^2}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB:**
$$
\left( \frac{C}{I} \right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 18.66 \text{ dB}
$$

**Verification:** Since 18.66 dB > 18 dB, the cluster size $N = 7$ satisfies the minimum $C/I$ requirement for voice. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Cluster Sizes

| $(i, j)$ | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | $C/I$ (linear, $\gamma = 4$) | $C/I$ (dB) | Meets 18 dB? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 0)$ | 1 | 1.732 | 2.5 | 3.98 | ✗ |
| $(1, 1)$ | 3 | 3.000 | 13.5 | 11.30 | ✗ |
| $(2, 0)$ | 4 | 3.464 | 24.0 | 13.80 | ✗ |
| $(2, 1)$ | 7 | 4.583 | 73.5 | 18.66 | ✓ |
| $(3, 0)$ | 9 | 5.196 | 121.5 | 20.85 | ✓ |
| $(2, 2)$ | 12 | 6.000 | 216.0 | 23.35 | ✓ |
| $(3, 1)$ | 13 | 6.245 | 253.5 | 24.04 | ✓ |
| $(4, 0)$ | 16 | 6.928 | 384.0 | 25.84 | ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **The cluster sizes most often used in real networks are $N = 4, 7, 12, 19$** because they offer a good trade-off between capacity ($K/N$) and interference ($C/I$). GSM in India uses $N = 4$ (reuse pattern 4/12).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is meant by *frequency reuse* in a cellular system? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer:**
Frequency reuse is the technique of reusing the same set of radio frequency channels in *non-adjacent cells* that are physically separated by a minimum distance $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ (where $R$ is the cell radius and $N$ is the cluster size). The total channel pool is partitioned into $N$ groups, and each cell in a cluster uses one group; the next cluster repeats the pattern. It is essential because **spectrum is finite and expensive**; reuse multiplies the effective system capacity $N$-fold without requiring new bandwidth, which is the very foundation of the cellular principle.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *co-channel interference* and *adjacent channel interference*.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Co-channel Interference (CCI) | Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Interference from cells using the **same** frequency in another cluster | Interference from signals in **nearby** (adjacent) frequencies |
| Control | Increase co-channel reuse distance $D$ | Use sharp filters and proper frequency planning |
| Key parameter | $C/I$ ratio | ACIR (Adjacent Channel Interference Ratio) |
| Cause | Inadequate spatial separation of co-channel cells | Imperfect receiver filtering / improper channel assignment |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper) — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **co-channel interference ratio** $C/I$ in a hexagonal cellular system. Clearly state the meaning of every symbol used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a cellular system with cluster size $N = 7$, cell radius $R = 4$ km, urban path-loss exponent $\gamma = 4$, and 6 first-tier co-channel interferers, compute: (i) the frequency reuse ratio, (ii) the co-channel reuse distance, and (iii) the $C/I$ ratio in dB. Comment on whether the $C/I$ meets the 18 dB minimum. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Let the mobile be located at the cell boundary (worst-case). The received carrier power at distance $R$ from the desired base station with transmit power $P_t$ and path-loss exponent $\gamma$ is:

$$
C = P_t \cdot R^{-\gamma}
$$

In the hexagonal layout, the first tier of co-channel cells contains $i_0 = 6$ cells, each at distance approximately $D$ from the mobile. The interference from a single co-channel cell is:

$$
I_{\text{one}} = P_t \cdot D^{-\gamma}
$$

Total interference (assuming all six are equidistant at distance $D$):

$$
I = i_0 \cdot P_t \cdot D^{-\gamma} = 6 P_t D^{-\gamma}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_t R^{-\gamma}}{6 P_t D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{6} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^{\gamma}
$$

Substituting $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$:
$$
\boxed{\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{\gamma}}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{\gamma/2}}{6}}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

*Given:* $N = 7$, $R = 4$ km, $\gamma = 4$.

**(i) Frequency reuse ratio:**
$$
q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583
$$

**(ii) Co-channel reuse distance:**
$$
D = R \sqrt{3N} = 4 \times 4.583 = 18.33 \text{ km}
$$

**(iii) C/I in dB:**
$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3 \times 7)^2}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5 \text{ (linear)}
$$
$$
\left( \frac{C}{I} \right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 18.66 \text{ dB}
$$

**Comment:** Since 18.66 dB $\ge$ 18 dB, the system meets the minimum voice-grade requirement. ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper) — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of the *shift-parameter* concept, derive the cluster size formula $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for a hexagonal cellular layout. List the first **five valid cluster sizes** and the corresponding $(i, j)$ pairs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain any **three** practical methods of capacity enhancement in a cellular system. For each, give the principle and one quantitative impact. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Cluster Size Derivation:**

On a regular hexagonal lattice, choose a reference cell and trace a lattice vector to the nearest co-channel cell. The vector is specified by integer shift parameters $(i, j)$ along two lattice directions separated by 60°.

The cell-centre displacements in Cartesian coordinates are:
$$
x = R \left( i + \tfrac{j}{2} \right), \qquad y = R \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, j
$$

The squared distance between centres is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= x^2 + y^2 \\
    &= R^2 \left( i + \tfrac{j}{2} \right)^2 + R^2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{4} j^2 \\
    &= R^2 \left( i^2 + ij + \tfrac{j^2}{4} + \tfrac{3j^2}{4} \right) \\
    &= R^2 (i^2 + ij + j^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The cluster size $N$ is the number of cells enclosed in the region swept by the lattice vector:
$$
\boxed{N = i^2 + ij + j^2}
$$

**First five valid cluster sizes:**

| $i$ | $j$ | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 0 | 4 |
| 2 | 1 | 7 |
| 3 | 0 | 9 |

**Part (b) — Capacity Enhancement Methods:**

1. **Cell Splitting:** The congested cell is divided into smaller cells (typically by a factor of 2 in radius: $R_2 = R_1/\sqrt{2}$). This **doubles** the number of channels available in the same area and supports **twice the traffic** with reduced transmit power (by about 9 dB).

2. **Sectoring:** Replace the omnidirectional antenna at the BS with several directional antennas (e.g. three 120° sectors). The number of first-tier co-channel interferers drops from $i_0 = 6$ to $i_0 = 2$, improving $C/I$ by $10 \log_{10}(6/2) = 4.77$ dB.

3. **Microcell Zone Concept:** A single cell is divided into 3–5 zones connected to a single BS, with a zone selector switching the active zone antenna to the mobile. The co-channel interferer count drops effectively to 1 in the best case, giving a $C/I$ improvement of up to $7.78$ dB. Unlike cell splitting, no new handoff is needed at zone boundaries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing dB and linear** — A common blunder is writing $C/I = 18$ dB and then plugging it directly into the formula $C/I = (3N)^2/6$ as if it were a linear ratio. Always convert: $18 \text{ dB} = 10^{1.8} \approx 63.1$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $i_0 = 6$ factor** — The classical $C/I$ formula assumes *six* first-tier interferers. If the cell is sectored, $i_0$ changes to 2 (or 1). Always state this explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong cluster sizes** — $N = 2, 5, 6, 8, 10, 11, 14, 15$ are *not* valid. They cannot be expressed as $i^2 + ij + j^2$ for non-negative integer $i, j$. Examiners will instantly fail your derivation if you list them.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ (cell radius) with $D$ (reuse distance)** — $D = R\sqrt{3N}$, *not* $D = R \cdot N$. Students frequently drop the $\sqrt{3}$ factor and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always quote $D$ in **km** and the dB value with two decimal places.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cellular Communications: Principles of Cellular Networks**

- **Cellular principle:** Replace one high-power transmitter with many low-power ones, and reuse frequencies to multiply capacity.
- **Hexagonal geometry** is the canonical model because hexagons tile perfectly and approximate circular coverage.
- **Cluster size:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. Valid: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, …
- **Co-channel reuse distance:** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.
- **Frequency reuse ratio:** $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **Co-channel interference ratio:** $C/I = (3N)^{\gamma/2} / 6$ for omnidirectional cells with $\gamma$ = path-loss exponent.
- **Minimum $C/I$ for voice:** **18 dB** (linear $\approx 63.1$).
- **Urban path-loss exponent:** typically $\gamma = 4$.
- **First-tier co-channel interferers:** $i_0 = 6$ omnidirectional, $2$ with 120° sectoring, $1$ with 60° sectoring.
- **Handover (Handoff):** Transfer of an active call between cells; must complete within the mobile's dwell time in the cell.
- **Cell splitting:** Reduces cell radius by $\sqrt{2}$ to double capacity per unit area.
- **Sectoring:** Improves $C/I$ by reducing the first-tier interferer count.
- **Cell capacity:** $C_{\text{cell}} = K/N$ where $K$ = total system channels.
- **Total system capacity:** $C_{\text{sys}} = M \cdot K$ (independent of $N$ — increasing $N$ reduces $C_{\text{cell}}$ but increases $D$, so trade-off!).
- **Practical cluster sizes in use:** $N = 4$ (4/12 reuse in GSM), $N = 7$ (omni), $N = 12$ (sectored 3-sector).
- **Adjacent channel interference** is controlled via sharp receiver filters and proper channel assignment.
- **Handoff types:** Hard handoff (break-before-make — GSM, LTE) and soft handoff (make-before-break — CDMA, WCDMA).
- **KTU 2024 favourite numerical:** $N = 7$, $\gamma = 4$, find $D/R$, $C/I$ (linear) and $C/I$ (dB).
- **Always show units** (km, dB) and **always state the standard 18 dB benchmark** when discussing $C/I$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Reuse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Frequency Reuse # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Reuse** is the process in which the same set of carrier frequencies (channels) are assigned to multiple base stations located in geographically separated cells, with the separation distance (called the **co-channel reuse distance**, $D$) being large enough to keep co-channel interference below an acceptable threshold. The entire concept of cellular communication rests on frequency reuse. Because the usable radio spectrum is **finite and scarce**, the same band of frequencies must be reused across multiple cells to maximize system capacity (number of simultaneous users served per unit area). The group of cells that collectively use **all available channels exactly once** is called a **Cluster**, denoted by the parameter **$N$**. The cluster is then replicated (tessellated) across the geographic service area. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — "Apartment Buildings Sharing a Laundry Room"** > Imagine a city with many apartment buildings. Each building has its own set of washing machines (frequencies), but to save money and space, every third building shares the *exact same set* of machines. The buildings sharing the machines are far enough apart that the noise (vibration/EMI) from one building's machines does not disturb the others. This is *frequency reuse* — the *same resource is used in non-adjacent locations* to serve more users economically. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights) | Term | Definition | Notation | |---|---|---| | Cluster Size | Number of cells in a cluster | $N$ | | Co-channel Reuse Distance | Distance between two cells using the same frequency set | $D$ | | Cell Radius | Radius of a hexagonal cell | $R$ | | Frequency Reuse Ratio | Ratio $D/R$ | $Q$ | | Co-channel Cells | Cells that use the same set of frequencies | — | | Co-channel Interference | Interference from co-channel cells | CCI | ## 1.4 Hexagonal Cell Geometry KTU follows the **regular hexagonal** model because: - It provides the largest coverage area per cell with minimum overlap. - It is the closest regular polygon to a circle for omnidirectional antennas. - It tessellates (tiles the plane) without gaps. For a regular hexagon: - **Side length** = Cell radius $= R$ - **Area of hexagon** $= \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2}$ - **Distance between centers of adjacent hexagons** $= \sqrt{3} R$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Cluster Size ($N$) The cluster size $N$ is governed by the shift parameters $i$ and $j$ (positive integers, with $i > j$): $$N = i^{2} + i \cdot j + j^{2}$$ The **only allowable cluster sizes** under the hexagonal tessellation are: $$N \in \{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, \dots\}$$ Cluster sizes **2, 5, 6, 8, 10, 11, 14, 15, 17, 18, 20, 22, 23, 24** are **NOT possible** with regular hexagonal cells. > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU Trap**: Students often write $N = i^{2} + j^{2}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$. ## 2.2 Locating Co-channel Cells To move from a given cell to its nearest co-channel cell, you must move: - $i$ cells along **any one of the 6 chains of hexagons**, then - Turn 60° and move $j$ cells. ## 2.3 Co-channel Reuse Distance ($D$) $$D = \sqrt{3N} \cdot R$$ The **Frequency Reuse Ratio (FRR)**, often denoted $Q$: $$Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$ ## 2.4 Co-channel Interference (CCI) In a fully loaded system, the **first tier** of co-channel cells (6 cells for omnidirectional antennas) acts as the dominant source of interference. ### 2.4.1 Signal Power Received at Distance $d$ Using the **power-law path loss model** with path loss exponent $n$ (typically $n = 4$ in urban environments): $$P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$$ where $K$ is a constant depending on antenna gains, frequency, etc. ### 2.4.2 Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR / C/I) $$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{i_{0}^{-n} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{i_{0}} (D_{k}/R)^{-n}}$$ For a **fully loaded first-tier hexagonal system**, the six co-channel interferers are all at distance $D$: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{6}$$ For the **urban path loss exponent** $n = 4$: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{3N^{2}}{6} = \frac{N^{2}}{2}$$ This is the most frequently asked relation in KTU examinations. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Formula**: For $n=4$, $\frac{C}{I} = \frac{N^{2}}{2}$ (linear scale). > In **dB**: $\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{N^{2}}{2}\right)$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Note | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | Cluster size from shift parameters | Pure number, $i,j \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}, i>j$ | | 2 | $D = \sqrt{3N} \cdot R$ | Co-channel reuse distance | meters | | 3 | $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | Frequency reuse ratio | Pure number, must be $\geq 4.6$ (typical) | | 4 | $A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2}$ | Area of hexagonal cell | $m^{2}$ | | 5 | $A_{\text{cluster}} = N \cdot A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} N R^{2}$ | Area of cluster | $m^{2}$ | | 6 | $C/I = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{6}$ | SIR (general $n$) | Linear scale | | 7 | $C/I = \frac{N^{2}}{2}$ | SIR for $n=4$ | Linear scale | | 8 | $(C/I)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(N^{2}/2)$ | SIR in decibels | dB | | 9 | $M = \frac{K}{N}$ | Channels per cell | Pure number, $K$ = total channels | | 10 | Capacity $\propto \frac{1}{N}$ | Smaller $N$ → More capacity | — | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mobile Network Operators (MNOs)** use frequency reuse to maximize revenue per MHz of licensed spectrum. - **5G NR** uses advanced reuse schemes — *Frequency Reuse Factor 1* (FRF=1) with fractional load and Inter-Cell Interference Coordination (ICIC). - **Trade-off**: Reuse factor 1 (N=1) gives maximum capacity but worst CCI; reuse factor 7 or 9 (classic AMPS) gives best quality but lowest capacity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Cluster Size $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ Consider a regular hexagonal lattice. Each cell has 6 sides. We rotate around the lattice by **60°** at each step. Starting at a cell, move $i$ cells in any one of the 6 directions, then turn 60° (clockwise or counter-clockwise) and move $j$ cells. We land on a co-channel cell. Using the **law of cosines** for the triangle formed by the two displacement vectors of magnitudes $i\sqrt{3}R$ and $j\sqrt{3}R$ (the diagonal of a hexagon is $\sqrt{3}$ times the side) with the included angle of **60°** between the two displacements: $$ \begin{aligned} D^{2} &= (i\sqrt{3}R)^{2} + (j\sqrt{3}R)^{2} - 2(i\sqrt{3}R)(j\sqrt{3}R)\cos(180^{\circ} - 60^{\circ}) \\ D^{2} &= 3i^{2}R^{2} + 3j^{2}R^{2} - 2 \cdot 3ijR^{2} \cdot \cos(120^{\circ}) \\ D^{2} &= 3i^{2}R^{2} + 3j^{2}R^{2} - 6ijR^{2} \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) \\ D^{2} &= 3i^{2}R^{2} + 3j^{2}R^{2} + 3ijR^{2} \\ D^{2} &= 3R^{2}(i^{2} + ij + j^{2}) \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$D = R \cdot \sqrt{3(i^{2} + ij + j^{2})}$$ Comparing with $D = R\sqrt{3N}$: $$\boxed{N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of $C/I$ for the First-Tier Interferers (n=4) **Step 1 — Signal power at mobile station from serving BS** (at distance $R$): $$S = K \cdot P_{t} \cdot R^{-n}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the first-tier co-channel cells.** In a hexagonal layout with cluster size $N$, the **6 nearest co-channel cells** are all at the co-channel reuse distance $D$. Hence $i_{0} = 6$ first-tier interferers (each at distance $D$). **Step 3 — Total interference power at the mobile station**: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{6} I_{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{6} K \cdot P_{t} \cdot D_{k}^{-n} \\ I_{\text{total}} &= 6 \cdot K \cdot P_{t} \cdot D^{-n} \quad (\text{since all } D_{k} = D) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Form the ratio**: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C}{I} &= \frac{S}{I_{\text{total}}} = \frac{K P_{t} R^{-n}}{6 K P_{t} D^{-n}} \\ \frac{C}{I} &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute** $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ and $n = 4$ (urban): $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C}{I} &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot (\sqrt{3N})^{4} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot 9N^{2} = \frac{3N^{2}}{6} \\ \frac{C}{I} &= \frac{N^{2}}{2} \end{aligned} $$ In decibels: $$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{N^{2}}{2}\right)$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example (Solved Step-by-Step) **Problem (KTU-style):** A cellular system uses a cluster of size $N = 7$ with cell radius $R = 4$ km. Path loss exponent $n = 4$. Calculate: (a) Co-channel reuse distance $D$ (b) Frequency reuse ratio $Q$ (c) $C/I$ ratio in dB (d) The minimum cluster size for which $C/I \geq 18$ dB ### Solution **(a) Co-channel reuse distance**: $$D = \sqrt{3N} \cdot R = \sqrt{3 \times 7} \times 4 = \sqrt{21} \times 4 \approx 18.33 \text{ km}$$ **(b) Frequency reuse ratio**: $$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$$ **(c) C/I ratio in dB**: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C}{I} &= \frac{N^{2}}{2} = \frac{49}{2} = 24.5 \\ \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(24.5) \approx 13.89 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **(d) Minimum $N$ for $C/I \geq 18$ dB**: $$18 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow \frac{C}{I} \geq 10^{1.8} = 63.10$$ We need $N^{2}/2 \geq 63.10 \Rightarrow N^{2} \geq 126.20 \Rightarrow N \geq 11.24$. From the allowed cluster set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, \dots\}$, the **smallest valid** $N$ is $\boxed{N = 12}$. Verification: For $N=12$, $C/I = 12^{2}/2 = 72 = 18.57$ dB ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of $N$ on System Performance | Cluster Size $N$ | $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ | $C/I$ (linear, $n=4$) | $C/I$ (dB) | Capacity per Cell | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.73 | 0.5 | -3.01 dB | Highest | Theoretically possible (FRF=1, 5G) | | 3 | 3.00 | 4.5 | 6.53 dB | High | Small cells, dense urban 5G | | 4 | 3.46 | 8 | 9.03 dB | High | Theoretical limit case | | 7 | 4.58 | 24.5 | 13.89 dB | Medium | **Classic AMPS** (1G analog) | | 9 | 5.20 | 40.5 | 16.07 dB | Lower | GSM (some deployments) | | 12 | 6.00 | 72 | 18.57 dB | Low | GSM (high quality) | | 19 | 7.55 | 180.5 | 22.56 dB | Very Low | Rural / low-traffic areas | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hexagonal Cell Layout with N=7 Cluster This diagram shows a cluster of 7 cells (the most commonly used in classic cellular systems) with the central cell, its 6 neighbors in the same cluster, and the 6 co-channel cells of the first tier. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Cluster7["CLUSTER N=7 — Frequency Plan"] direction TB cell1["CELL 1
Frequency Set A"] cell2["CELL 2
Frequency Set B"] cell3["CELL 3
Frequency Set C"] cell4["CELL 4
Frequency Set D"] cell5["CELL 5
Frequency Set E"] cell6["CELL 6
Frequency Set F"] cell7["CELL 7
Frequency Set G"] end subgraph FirstTier["FIRST-TIER CO-CHANNEL CELLS (Same Set A as Cell 1)"] direction TB cc1["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] cc2["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] cc3["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] cc4["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] cc5["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] cc6["CO-CHANNEL CELL A
Distance D"] end cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc1 cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc2 cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc3 cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc4 cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc5 cell1 -. Reuse Distance D .-> cc6 ``` ## 4.2 Locating a Co-channel Cell — The i, j Shift Method Demonstrates the geometric rule: move $i$ cells in one direction, then $j$ cells after a 60° turn, to land on a co-channel cell. ```mermaid graph LR startNode["START CELL
(Current Cell)"] --> stepA["MOVE i CELLS
in any of 6 directions"] stepA --> turnNode["TURN 60 degrees"] turnNode --> stepB["MOVE j CELLS"] stepB --> endNode["LAND ON
CO-CHANNEL CELL"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Concrete Example:** For $N = 7$, the parameters are $i = 2, j = 1$ (since $2^{2} + 2(1) + 1^{2} = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$). ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Frequency Reuse System ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Spectrum["LIMITED RADIO SPECTRUM (e.g., 25 MHz Band)"] f1["Channel Group 1"] f2["Channel Group 2"] f3["Channel Group 3"] f4["Channel Group 4"] f5["Channel Group 5"] f6["Channel Group 6"] f7["Channel Group 7"] end subgraph ClusterA["CLUSTER 1 (Region A)"] c1a["Cell uses Group 1"] c2a["Cell uses Group 2"] c3a["Cell uses Group 3"] c4a["Cell uses Group 4"] c5a["Cell uses Group 5"] c6a["Cell uses Group 6"] c7a["Cell uses Group 7"] end subgraph ClusterB["CLUSTER 2 (Region B — Geographically Separated)"] c1b["Cell uses Group 1 (REUSE)"] c2b["Cell uses Group 2 (REUSE)"] c3b["Cell uses Group 3 (REUSE)"] c4b["Cell uses Group 4 (REUSE)"] c5b["Cell uses Group 5 (REUSE)"] c6b["Cell uses Group 6 (REUSE)"] c7b["Cell uses Group 7 (REUSE)"] end Spectrum --> ClusterA Spectrum --> ClusterB ``` ## 4.4 Trade-off Visualization: Capacity vs. Quality ```mermaid graph LR A["SMALL N (e.g., 3 or 4)
HIGH CAPACITY
LOW C/I quality"] -->|Increase N| B["MEDIUM N (e.g., 7)
BALANCED
C/I around 14 dB"] B -->|Increase N| C["LARGE N (e.g., 12 or 19)
LOW CAPACITY
HIGH C/I quality"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define frequency reuse. What is meant by co-channel cells?** **Model Answer:** Frequency reuse is the technique of using the same set of radio frequencies in multiple cells that are geographically separated by a minimum distance (called the co-channel reuse distance $D$), so that co-channel interference remains within tolerable limits. **Co-channel cells** are those cells that use the **exact same set of carrier frequencies** for transmission. In a hexagonal cluster of size $N$, each cell has 6 first-tier co-channel cells, all located at distance $D = R\sqrt{3N}$. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **State the formula for cluster size $N$ in a hexagonal cellular system and explain the significance of parameters $i$ and $j$.** **Model Answer:** $$N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$$ where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers with $i > j \geq 0$. They denote the **number of cells traversed** in the hexagonal grid to reach a co-channel cell. Specifically, you move $i$ cells in one of the 6 hexagonal directions, then turn 60° and move $j$ cells to land on a co-channel cell. The values of $i$ and $j$ must be chosen so that $N$ is a feasible cluster size (e.g., 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13...). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, usually divided as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. KTU provides an internal choice between two question variants. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** for student practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** A hexagonal cellular system has a cluster size $N = 9$, cell radius $R = 5$ km, and uses the urban path loss model with exponent $n = 4$. Determine: (i) Co-channel reuse distance $D$ (ii) Frequency reuse ratio $Q$ (iii) The signal-to-interference ratio (C/I) in dB (assume 6 first-tier interferers) **(b)** If the total number of channels available is $K = 420$, find the number of channels per cell and the total system capacity (channels per km²) for the same system. Compare it with the capacity if $N = 12$ was used instead. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Step-by-Step:** **(i) Co-channel reuse distance** $D$: $$D = R\sqrt{3N} = 5 \times \sqrt{3 \times 9} = 5 \times \sqrt{27} = 5 \times 3\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98 \text{ km}$$ **(ii) Frequency reuse ratio** $Q$: $$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{27} = 3\sqrt{3} \approx 5.196$$ **(iii) C/I ratio in dB (using $n=4$)**: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{N^{2}}{2} = \frac{9^{2}}{2} = \frac{81}{2} = 40.5$$ $$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(40.5) \approx 16.07 \text{ dB}$$ **Part (b) — Step-by-Step:** **Channels per cell for $N=9$**: $$M = \frac{K}{N} = \frac{420}{9} \approx 46.67 \Rightarrow 46 \text{ channels/cell (integer)}$$ **Cell area**: $$A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 25 \approx 64.95 \text{ km}^{2}$$ **Channels per km² for $N=9$**: $$\text{Capacity density} = \frac{M}{A_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{46.67}{64.95} \approx 0.72 \text{ channels/km}^{2}$$ **For $N = 12$**: $$M_{12} = \frac{420}{12} = 35 \text{ channels/cell}$$ $$\text{Capacity density} = \frac{35}{64.95} \approx 0.54 \text{ channels/km}^{2}$$ **Conclusion:** $N=9$ provides **33% higher capacity density** than $N=12$, but with lower C/I quality (16.07 dB vs 18.57 dB). The network designer must balance capacity vs. quality. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the concept of co-channel interference in a cellular system. Derive the expression for the signal-to-interference ratio (C/I) for a hexagonal system with $N$ cells per cluster, using the general path loss exponent $n$. Then specialize for $n = 4$. **(b)** A cellular operator is required to maintain a minimum $C/I$ of **20 dB** in an urban environment ($n=4$). Determine: (i) The minimum feasible cluster size $N$ (ii) The corresponding co-channel reuse distance if $R = 3$ km (iii) The maximum number of cells per cluster of area $1500$ km² (approximate) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Concept and Derivation:** Co-channel interference arises when multiple cells using the **same set of frequencies** transmit simultaneously. Although the reuse distance $D$ is designed to keep CCI low, in a fully loaded system the first-tier 6 co-channel cells cause measurable interference. The received signal power at a mobile station at distance $d$ from a base station: $$P_{r} = P_{t} K d^{-n}$$ For the **serving cell** (distance $R$): $S = P_{t} K R^{-n}$ For each of **6 first-tier interferers** (each at distance $D$): $I_{k} = P_{t} K D^{-n}$ Total interference: $I_{\text{total}} = 6 P_{t} K D^{-n}$ $$\therefore \frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_{t} K R^{-n}}{6 P_{t} K D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n}$$ Substituting $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6}(\sqrt{3N})^{n} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}$$ For $n = 4$: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3N)^{2}}{6} = \frac{9N^{2}}{6} = \frac{N^{2}}{2} = \frac{3N^{2}}{6}$$ **Part (b) — Step-by-Step:** **(i) Minimum $N$ for C/I ≥ 20 dB**: $$20 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow \frac{C}{I} \geq 10^{2.0} = 100$$ $$\frac{N^{2}}{2} \geq 100 \Rightarrow N^{2} \geq 200 \Rightarrow N \geq 14.14$$ From the valid cluster set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, \ldots\}$, the smallest valid $N \geq 14.14$ is $\boxed{N = 16}$.
