Verification: $C/I = 16^{2}/2 = 128 = 20.97$ dB ✓ (exceeds 20 dB) Note: $N = 13$ gives $C/I = 84.5 = 19.27$ dB (insufficient). **(ii) Co-channel reuse distance** for $N = 16, R = 3$ km: $$D = R\sqrt{3N} = 3 \times \sqrt{48} = 3 \times 4\sqrt{3} \approx 20.78 \text{ km}$$ **(iii) Maximum cells in 1500 km²**: Area of one cell: $A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 9 \approx 23.38$ km² Cells per cluster: 16 → Cluster area $= 16 \times 23.38 \approx 374.08$ km² Number of clusters in 1500 km²: $1500/374.08 \approx 4.01 \approx 4$ clusters Total cells: $4 \times 16 = 64$ cells. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Wrong cluster formula**: Writing $N = i^{2} + j^{2}$ instead of $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ → **Lose 2–3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to square in $C/I$ for $n=4$**: Writing $C/I = N/2$ instead of $N^{2}/2$ → **Lose 2 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting "6 first-tier interferers"**: Using a generic $i_{0}$ but not specifying 6 for the standard hexagonal case → **Lose 1 mark**. > 4. **Wrong unit conversion for dB**: Writing $20\log_{10}$ instead of $10\log_{10}$ (C/I is a power ratio, not a voltage ratio) → **Lose 1 mark**. > 5. **Using a forbidden $N$ value**: Picking $N = 5$ or $N = 6$ for examples — these cluster sizes **do not exist** for hexagonal cells. KTU examiners will deduct marks. > 6. **Confusing $D$ with $R$**: $D$ is reuse distance, $R$ is cell radius. $D > R$ always. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - Frequency Reuse uses **same frequencies in separated cells** to multiply capacity. - Cluster size formula: $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ (only specific values allowed: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21…). - Co-channel reuse distance: $D = R\sqrt{3N}$. - Frequency Reuse Ratio: $Q = \sqrt{3N}$, typically $Q \geq 4.6$. - For $n=4$ (urban): $C/I = N^{2}/2$ (linear) or $10\log_{10}(N^{2}/2)$ dB. - General: $C/I = (3N)^{n/2}/6$. - Channels per cell: $M = K/N$, where $K$ = total available channels. - **Smaller $N$ → Higher capacity but more interference**. - **Larger $N$ → Lower capacity but better quality**. - Cluster size of **7** is the most commonly cited classical example (AMPS 1G). - The **6 first-tier co-channel cells** dominate interference calculations. - Hexagonal cells tessellate perfectly; area = $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}R^{2}$. - Path loss exponent $n$: typically **3 to 5** for urban cellular environments; $n = 4$ is the standard KTU assumption. --- **End of Module 3 — Frequency Reuse Notes** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEEBT751 — Communication Techniques*

topics.title: Operation of Cellular System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Operation of Cellular System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cellular System Operation** is defined as the systematic procedure by which a limited radio spectrum is efficiently allocated, reused, and managed across a geographic service area to provide uninterrupted wireless communication to a large population of mobile users, while maintaining acceptable Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) and Quality of Service (QoS). The operation of a cellular system fundamentally rests on four operational pillars: 1. **Frequency Reuse** — the same set of carrier frequencies is reused in non-adjacent cells separated by a minimum reuse distance. 2. **Cell Splitting** — subdivision of congested large cells into smaller cells (microcells / picocells) to increase capacity. 3. **Sectoring** — directional antennas at the base station to reduce co-channel interference and improve capacity. 4. **Handoff (Handover)** — seamless transfer of an active call between cells (or sectors) as the mobile station moves across cell boundaries. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Postal Pin Code System** Imagine you are the Postmaster General of India. You have only **1,000 postmen** (your radio channels), but **10 million citizens** need daily mail delivery. The naive approach of giving every citizen a dedicated postman collapses. So you divide the country into **zones** (cells). Within a zone, you assign **10 postmen** to serve the local residents. Once you cross into the next zone, you **reuse the same 10 postmen** — because the first zone is far enough away that no confusion arises. This is **frequency reuse**. When a city zone gets overpopulated (congestion), you **split the zone into smaller localities** and allocate more postmen. This is **cell splitting**. To stop the postmen in adjacent localities from shouting into each other's territory, you ask them to **face only one direction (sectoring)** with directional horns. Finally, when a citizen **moves from one zone to another while on a phone call**, the local post office **transfers the call** to a new postman without dropping it. This is **handoff**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bold Highlights) The following standardized terms govern the operation of a cellular system: - **Cell Radius ($R$):** typically **1 km to 30 km** depending on terrain (urban microcells ≈ 0.5 km, rural macrocells up to **35 km**). - **Frequency Reuse Distance ($D$):** minimum physical distance between two cells using the **same frequency set**. - **Co-channel Reuse Ratio ($q$):** $q = D/R$, typically ranging between **3 and 5.5**. - **Cluster Size ($N$):** number of cells in a fundamental frequency reuse pattern; common values are **N = 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19**. - **Speed of Light ($c$):** **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** — fundamental for path loss and propagation delay calculations. ### 1.4 Block Picture of Cellular Operation ``` ┌──────────── MOBILE STATION (MS) ────────────┐ │ ◄───── Voice/Data signals via radio ─────► │ └──────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘ │ │ (Radio Link - Uplink / Downlink) ▼ ┌──────────── BASE STATION (BTS) ──────────────┐ │ Manages radio resources in its CELL │ └──────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘ │ │ (Wired Backhaul - T1/E1/Microwave) ▼ ┌──────── MOBILE SWITCHING CENTER (MSC) ───────┐ │ Coordinates handoff, authentication, │ │ billing, connection to PSTN / ISDN / DATA │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Pillars — Structured Logic Breakdown #### 2.1.1 Frequency Reuse - **Why:** Radio spectrum is finite and expensive. Without reuse, the system can only support as many users as there are channels — totally impractical. - **How:** The hexagonal service area is tiled into clusters. Each cluster uses every channel exactly **once**. Adjacent clusters reuse identical channel sets. - **Result:** System capacity scales linearly with the number of clusters deployed. The relationship between cluster size and frequency reuse distance is: $$ D = R \sqrt{3N} $$ where: - $D$ = **Frequency Reuse Distance (km)** - $R$ = **Cell Radius (km)** - $N$ = **Cluster Size (cells per cluster)** #### 2.1.2 Cluster Size ($N$) Cluster size must be of the form: $$ N = i^2 + ij + j^2 $$ where $i$ and $j$ are **non-negative integers** (commonly $i \geq j$). Allowed values: **N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, …** #### 2.1.3 Cell Splitting - **Why:** Traffic in a cell grows; existing channels become saturated. - **How:** A congested cell is divided into smaller cells (typically **half the radius**), each with its own base station and reduced transmit power. - **Trade-off:** Cell splitting increases capacity four-fold (area scales with $R^2$), but **increases handoff frequency**. #### 2.1.4 Sectoring - **Why:** Co-channel interference from neighboring cells limits capacity. - **How:** Omnidirectional antenna at the BS is replaced by **directional antennas** (typically **3 sectors of 120°** or **6 sectors of 60°**). - **Trade-off:** Sectoring reduces co-channel interference, hence **$N$ can be reduced** → more capacity. But trunking efficiency drops because channels are now split among sectors. #### 2.1.5 Handoff (Handover) Triggered when the received signal strength from the serving cell drops below a threshold OR when the signal from a neighboring cell becomes stronger. Types: - **Hard Handoff** — break-before-make (used in GSM, TDMA). - **Soft Handoff** — make-before-break (used in CDMA, WCDMA). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the ultimate exam-day cheat sheet for this topic.** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | Hexagonal Cell Area | $A_{cell} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2$ | Area of a regular hexagon of radius $R$ | $\text{km}^2$ | | 2 | Cluster Size | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Valid shift parameters $i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Reuse Distance | $D = R \sqrt{3N}$ | Center-to-center distance between co-channel cells | km | | 4 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | Must be $\geq 3$ for adequate SIR | dimensionless | | 5 | Downlink SIR | $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(D/R)^n}{i_0}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent, $i_0$ = number of co-channel interferers | dB | | 6 | Capacity per Cluster | $C_{cluster} = M \cdot k$ | $M$ = total channels, $k$ = channels per cell | Erlangs / channels | | 7 | Number of Clusters | $N_{clusters} = \dfrac{\text{Total Area}}{A_{cell} \cdot N}$ | How many cluster patterns tile the service area | dimensionless | | 8 | System Capacity (Cells) | $C_{sys} = M \cdot N_{clusters}$ | Total channels × number of clusters | channels | | 9 | Sectoring Capacity Gain | $\dfrac{C_{sectored}}{C_{omni}} = \dfrac{N_{omni}}{N_{sectored}}$ | Reducing $N$ via sectoring increases capacity | ratio | | 10 | Cell Splitting Capacity Gain | $\dfrac{C_{after}}{C_{before}} = \left(\dfrac{R_{before}}{R_{after}}\right)^2$ | Halving $R$ increases capacity 4× | ratio | | 11 | Handoff Dwell Time | $T_d \approx \dfrac{2R}{v}$ | $v$ = mobile velocity; for $R=1$ km, $v=60$ km/h $\Rightarrow T_d \approx 120$ s | seconds | | 12 | Cell Radius after Split | $R_{new} = \dfrac{R_{old}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | When transmitter power is halved | km | ### 2.3 Real-World Utility - **GSM (2G):** Uses **N = 4** or **N = 7** cluster patterns with frequency reuse and hard handoff. - **CDMA (3G / IS-95):** Uses **universal frequency reuse (N = 1)** but soft handoff and sectoring reduce interference. - **LTE / 5G NR:** Employs aggressive **cell splitting (small cells / femtocells)** and **sectoring with MIMO** for capacity. - **Engineering Application:** Telecom operators (Jio, Airtel, Vi) use these formulas to design cellular network layouts and minimize capital expenditure on base stations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Co-channel Reuse Ratio ($q = D/R$) > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **KTU favorite** and frequently appears as a 7-mark derivation problem. **Given:** Two adjacent hexagons in a cluster with centers separated by **distance $D$**. The cell radius is **$R$**. **Geometric Setup:** For a hexagonal cell, the distance from the center to a vertex is $R$, and the distance from the center to a midpoint of a side is $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} R$. The co-channel cells are offset by **$i$ cells along one axis and $j$ cells along a 60°-rotated axis**. **Step 1 — Express the displacement vector along the two axes.** Along the first axis, the displacement is $i$ cells, each of length $\sqrt{3} R$ (center-to-center distance across one hexagon side). $$ d_1 = i \cdot \sqrt{3} R $$ **Step 2 — Along the rotated axis.** $$ d_2 = j \cdot \sqrt{3} R $$ **Step 3 — Apply the law of cosines with the included angle of 60°.** $$ D^2 = d_1^2 + d_2^2 + 2 d_1 d_2 \cos(60^\circ) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\cos(60^\circ) = 1/2$.** $$ D^2 = 3R^2(i^2 + j^2 + ij) $$ **Step 5 — Recognize that $3(i^2 + j^2 + ij) = 3N$.** $$ D^2 = 3 N R^2 $$ **Step 6 — Take the square root to obtain the standard form.** $$ D = R \sqrt{3N} $$ **Step 7 — Define the co-channel reuse ratio $q$.** $$ q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Co-channel Interference in Omnidirectional Cells For an omnidirectional antenna at the BS, the **first tier of co-channel interferers** consists of **6 cells** ($i_0 = 6$). The signal-to-interference ratio at the mobile is: **Step 1 — Power received from the desired BS** (using path loss model $P_r \propto d^{-n}$). $$ P_s = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n} $$ **Step 2 — Power received from one of the 6 co-channel interferers**, each at distance $D$. $$ P_I^{(k)} = P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n} $$ **Step 3 — Total interference (worst case, summing all 6 interferers).** $$ I_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{6} P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n} = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n} $$ **Step 4 — Form the SIR ratio.** $$ \frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_t K R^{-n}}{6 P_t K D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^n $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$.** $$ \frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{6} (3N)^{n/2} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6} $$ **Step 6 — In decibels.** $$ \left( \frac{S}{I} \right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6} \right] $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cell Splitting — Capacity Quadrupling **Step 1 — Original cell radius $R$ supports a traffic $A_0$ Erlangs.** **Step 2 — After splitting, new radius $R' = R/2$.** **Step 3 — Number of new cells replacing one old cell.** $$ N_{new} = \frac{A_{old\,cell}}{A_{new\,cell}} = \left( \frac{R}{R'} \right)^2 = 4 $$ **Step 4 — Each new cell supports the same channel set but at lower power. Total capacity per area:** $$ C_{area}' = \frac{\text{channels per area after}}{\text{channels per area before}} = \frac{N_{cells}' \cdot \text{channels/cell}}{A_{cell}'} = 4 \cdot \frac{A_{cell}}{A_{cell}'} $$ **Step 5 — Since $A_{cell}' = A_{cell}/4$:** $$ C_{area}' = 4 \cdot 4 = 16 \quad \text{(NO — incorrect for capacity-increase interpretation)} $$ **Step 6 — Correct interpretation:** Halving $R$ creates **4× more cells per area**, each with the same channels → **4× capacity increase per area**. $$ \frac{C_{after}}{C_{before}} = \left( \frac{R_{before}}{R_{after}} \right)^2 = 4 $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!NOTE] > This comparison matrix is a **high-yield visual** that examiners love to set as Part A questions. | Parameter | Frequency Reuse | Cell Splitting | Sectoring | Handoff | |-----------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|---------| | **Primary Goal** | Spectrum efficiency | Capacity increase | Interference reduction | Call continuity | | **Effect on $N$** | Defines $N$ | No change in $N$ | Allows smaller $N$ | No effect on $N$ | | **Effect on SIR** | Reduces SIR (more interference) | No direct effect | Improves SIR (3–6 dB gain) | Indirect via channel assignment | | **Trunking Efficiency** | High | Same | Reduced (sectors divide channels) | No effect | | **Handoff Rate** | No change | Increases (smaller cells) | Increases (cell boundaries) | The phenomenon itself | | **Implementation Cost** | Low (planning only) | High (more BS) | Moderate (antennas) | Moderate (MSC software) | | **Used In** | All cellular systems | High-density urban | 2G/3G/4G/5G | All cellular systems | --- ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Style) > **Problem:** A cellular system uses a cluster size of **N = 7**. Cell radius is **R = 5 km**. A total of **420 channels** are allocated to the operator. Determine: (a) the frequency reuse distance, (b) the number of channels per cell, and (c) the co-channel reuse ratio. **Solution:** **(a) Frequency Reuse Distance:** $$ D = R \sqrt{3N} = 5 \sqrt{3 \times 7} = 5 \sqrt{21} $$ $$ D = 5 \times 4.583 = 22.91 \text{ km} $$ **(b) Channels per cell:** $$ k = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{420}{7} = 60 \text{ channels per cell} $$ **(c) Co-channel reuse ratio:** $$ q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583 $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hexagonal Cellular Layout with Frequency Reuse (N = 7) The following Mermaid block depicts the cluster structure for **N = 7** with $i = 2$, $j = 1$ (since $2^2 + 2\cdot 1 + 1^2 = 7$). Cells labeled with the same letter use identical channel sets. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph ClusterA["Cluster A - Cells 1 to 7"] C1["Cell A1
Channels 1-60"] C2["Cell A2
Channels 61-120"] C3["Cell A3
Channels 121-180"] C4["Cell A4
Channels 181-240"] C5["Cell A5
Channels 241-300"] C6["Cell A6
Channels 301-360"] C7["Cell A7
Channels 361-420"] end subgraph ClusterB["Cluster B - Same Frequencies Reused"] D1["Cell B1
Channels 1-60"] D2["Cell B2
Channels 61-120"] D3["Cell B3
Channels 121-180"] D4["Cell B4
Channels 181-240"] D5["Cell B5
Channels 241-300"] D6["Cell B6
Channels 301-360"] D7["Cell B7
Channels 361-420"] end C1 -.Co-channel Reuse Distance D.-> D1 C2 -.Reuse D.-> D2 C3 -.Reuse D.-> D3 C4 -.Reuse D.-> D4 C5 -.Reuse D.-> D5 C6 -.Reuse D.-> D6 C7 -.Reuse D.-> D7 style C1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Handoff Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR MS["Mobile Station
Moving Vehicle"] BS1["BS1 Serving Cell
Signal Strength Dropping"] BS2["BS2 Neighbour Cell
Signal Strengthening"] MSC["Mobile Switching Center
Handoff Decision Engine"] PSTN["PSTN / Core Network"] MS -->|Radio Link Active| BS1 MS -.->|Radio Link Candidate| BS2 BS1 -->|Reports RSSI / Pilot| MSC BS2 -->|Reports RSSI / Pilot| MSC MSC -->|Handover Command| BS1 MSC -->|Allocate Channel| BS2 BS1 -->|Release Old Channel| MSC BS2 -->|New Channel Active| MS MSC --> PSTN style MS fill:#FFA07A style BS1 fill:#87CEEB style BS2 fill:#98FB98 style MSC fill:#DDA0DD ``` ### 4.3 Cell Splitting and Sectoring Evolution Flow ```mermaid graph TD Start["Original Macrocell
R = 10 km, Omni Antenna, N=7"] --> Split["Cell Splitting Stage 1
R = 5 km, 4 sub-cells"] Split --> Sector["Sectoring Applied
3 sectors per BS, 120 deg"] Sector --> Split2["Cell Splitting Stage 2
R = 2.5 km, Picocells"] Split2 --> Final["High-Capacity Urban Layer
20x capacity gain over original"] Start -.->|"Capacity = 1x"| Final Split -.->|"Capacity = 4x"| Final Sector -.->|"Capacity = 12x"| Final Split2 -.->|"Capacity = 48x"| Final style Start fill:#FFB6C1 style Split fill:#FFD700 style Sector fill:#90EE90 style Split2 fill:#87CEEB style Final fill:#98FB98 ``` ### 4.4 Co-channel Interference — First-Tier Geometry ```mermaid graph TD Mobile["Mobile Station M
Located at Cell Center"] Desired["Desired BS
Distance R"] I1["Interferer 1
Distance D, 0 deg"] I2["Interferer 2
Distance D, 60 deg"] I3["Interferer 3
Distance D, 120 deg"] I4["Interferer 4
Distance D, 180 deg"] I5["Interferer 5
Distance D, 240 deg"] I6["Interferer 6
Distance D, 300 deg"] Desired -->|Strong Signal S| Mobile I1 -->|Weak Interference I1| Mobile I2 -->|Weak Interference I2| Mobile I3 -->|Weak Interference I3| Mobile I4 -->|Weak Interference I4| Mobile I5 -->|Weak Interference I5| Mobile I6 -->|Weak Interference I6| Mobile style Mobile fill:#FFD700 style Desired fill:#90EE90 style I1 fill:#FF6347 style I2 fill:#FF6347 style I3 fill:#FF6347 style I4 fill:#FF6347 style I5 fill:#FF6347 style I6 fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define frequency reuse. What is its significance in cellular systems? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 mark) **Definition:** Frequency reuse is the technique of using the same set of radio frequencies in non-adjacent cells separated by a minimum distance $D$, while ensuring that co-channel interference remains below an acceptable threshold. - (1 mark) **Mechanism:** The service area is divided into clusters of $N$ cells; frequencies are assigned uniquely within a cluster and reused in other clusters. - (1 mark) **Significance:** It enables **spectrum efficiency** — a limited number of channels can serve an unlimited number of users simply by tiling more clusters. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between hard handoff and soft handoff. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Hard Handoff | Soft Handoff | |---------|--------------|--------------| | (1 mark) Connection Type | **Break-before-make** | **Make-before-break** | | (1 mark) Used In | GSM, TDMA, LTE | CDMA, WCDMA (3G) | | (1 mark) Resource Use | One channel at a time | Multiple channels simultaneously (temporarily) | | Effect | Brief gap possible | Seamless, no gap | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]* **(a)** Derive the relationship between **frequency reuse distance $D$**, **cluster size $N$**, and **cell radius $R$** for a hexagonal cellular system. (7 Marks) **(b)** A cellular operator is allocated a total spectrum of **40 MHz** with a **channel bandwidth of 25 kHz** (full duplex). If the system uses a **cluster of size N = 7**, determine: - (i) Total number of duplex channels $M$ (1 mark) - (ii) Channels per cell $k$ (1 mark) - (iii) Reuse distance $D$ if cell radius $R = 4$ km (2 marks) - (iv) Co-channel reuse ratio $q$ (1 mark) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** - (2 marks) **Setup:** Hexagonal cell layout with center-to-vertex distance $R$. Two adjacent co-channel cells are separated by $i$ cells along one axis and $j$ cells along the 60°-rotated axis. - (2 marks) **Displacement vectors:** $$ d_1 = i \sqrt{3} R, \quad d_2 = j \sqrt{3} R $$ - (2 marks) **Apply cosine rule** with 60° included angle: $$ D^2 = 3R^2(i^2 + j^2 + ij) = 3R^2 N $$ - (1 mark) **Final result:** $$ \boxed{D = R \sqrt{3N}, \quad q = \sqrt{3N}} $$ **(b) Numerical (7 Marks):** **(i) Total duplex channels:** $$ M = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{25 \times 10^3} = 1600 \text{ channels} $$ **(ii) Channels per cell:** $$ k = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{1600}{7} = 228.57 \approx 228 \text{ channels per cell} $$ **(iii) Reuse distance:** $$ D = R \sqrt{3N} = 4 \sqrt{21} = 4 \times 4.583 = 18.33 \text{ km} $$ **(iv) Co-channel reuse ratio:** $$ q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583 $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]* **(a)** Explain the concept of **cell splitting** and **sectoring** as methods to increase cellular system capacity. State how each affects the co-channel interference and trunking efficiency. (7 Marks) **(b)** A hexagonal cellular system with **$N = 4$**, **$R = 2$ km**, and **path loss exponent $n = 4$** operates with **6 co-channel interferers** in the first tier. Calculate: - (i) Frequency reuse distance $D$ (1 mark) - (ii) Co-channel reuse ratio $q$ (1 mark) - (iii) Signal-to-Interference ratio $\dfrac{S}{I}$ in linear scale (2 marks) - (iv) $\dfrac{S}{I}$ in dB (1 mark) - (v) Comment on whether the SIR meets the typical voice quality threshold of 18 dB. (2 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Conceptual (7 Marks):** - (2 marks) **Cell Splitting:** Definition — division of congested cells into smaller cells of half the radius; each new cell requires its own BS; power is reduced; capacity increases by factor of 4 per unit area. - (2 marks) **Sectoring:** Definition — replacing omnidirectional antenna with directional antennas (3 × 120° or 6 × 60°); reduces first-tier interferers from 6 to 2 (for 3-sector) → SIR improves by ~6 dB; trunking efficiency decreases since channels are partitioned. - (1.5 marks) **Co-channel interference effect:** Splitting → no direct effect. Sectoring → reduces $i_0$ from 6 to 2 → lower interference. - (1.5 marks) **Trunking efficiency effect:** Splitting → unchanged per cell. Sectoring → channels are split among sectors → blocking probability rises for same offered load. **(b) Numerical (7 Marks):** **(i) Reuse distance:** $$ D = R \sqrt{3N} = 2 \sqrt{12} = 2 \times 3.464 = 6.928 \text{ km} $$ **(ii) Co-channel reuse ratio:** $$ q = \sqrt{12} = 3.464 $$ **(iii) SIR (linear):** $$ \frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{i_0} = \frac{(3.464)^4}{6} = \frac{143.79}{6} = 23.97 $$ **(iv) SIR (dB):** $$ \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(23.97) = 13.79 \text{ dB} $$ **(v) Comment:** The computed SIR is **13.79 dB**, which is **below the typical 18 dB voice quality threshold**. Therefore, **N = 4 is inadequate for voice-grade service** under the given path loss exponent $n = 4$. The operator should **increase $N$ to 7 or 9** or apply **sectoring** to raise SIR. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Operation of Cellular System" Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor:** Many students write $D = R\sqrt{N}$ instead of $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ — this is **incorrect** and loses 2–3 marks immediately. > 2. **Mixing up cluster formulas:** $N = i^2 + j^2$ (wrong) vs $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ (correct) — examiners check this stringently. > 3. **Ignoring the first-tier interferer count:** Using $i_0 = 1$ instead of $6$ (omni) or $2$ (3-sector) — SIR answer is completely wrong. > 4. **Confusing frequency reuse distance with co-channel cell separation:** $D$ is the center-to-center distance between cells using **same frequency**, not neighboring cells. > 5. **Not converting to dB when asked:** Always check whether the answer requires linear scale or dB. > 6. **Forgetting units:** km vs m, channels vs Erlangs — minor deduction but adds up. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operation of Cellular System** - **Cluster Size:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$; valid values are **3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19**. - **Reuse Distance:** $D = R \sqrt{3N}$ — memorize this formula verbatim. - **Co-channel Reuse Ratio:** $q = \sqrt{3N}$; must be **≥ 3** for adequate voice quality. - **SIR Formula (omnidirectional, 6 interferers):** $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}$; in dB use $10 \log_{10}$. - **SIR Formula (3-sector, 2 interferers):** $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(3N)^{n/2}}{2}$. - **Cell Splitting:** Halving $R$ → **4× capacity increase** per unit area. - **Sectoring:** 3 sectors (120°) reduces interferers from 6 to 2 → **~6 dB SIR improvement**; allows **smaller $N$**. - **Handoff Types:** Hard (GSM) vs Soft (CDMA); based on signal strength threshold or mobile location. - **Path Loss Exponent:** Typically **n = 3 to 4** in urban cellular environments. - **Hexagonal Cell Area:** $A = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2 \approx 2.598 R^2$ — useful for capacity-per-area calculations. - **Voice Quality Threshold:** **SIR ≥ 18 dB** for acceptable voice quality in narrowband FM. - **First-tier Co-channel Cell Count:** **6 cells** (omnidirectional), **2 cells** (3-sector), **1 cell** (6-sector). - **Trunking Efficiency Trade-off:** More cells/sectors → better interference control but worse trunking efficiency. - **Engineering Trade-off Principle:** Capacity ↑ = (smaller cells) OR (smaller $N$) OR (more sectors); all come at the cost of increased infrastructure or handoff complexity.

topics.title: Hand Off Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hand Off Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: HAND OFF STRATEGIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In cellular mobile communication systems, **Hand-off (Handover)** is defined as the mechanism that allows an ongoing call or data session to be transferred from one radio base station (cell) to another without interrupting the communication link, as the mobile station (MS) moves across cell boundaries or when radio conditions change.

Mathematically, the hand-off decision is triggered when the condition:

$$\text{RSS}_{\text{new}} > \text{RSS}_{\text{old}} + \text{Hysteresis Margin}$$

is satisfied for a sustained dwell time interval, ensuring that the transfer is not triggered by short-term signal fluctuations (fading).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Convention:** KTU examiners specifically use the term "Hand-off Strategies" (not handover). Students must use this exact wording in their answer scripts to score full marks on definition questions.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving on a highway speaking on a mobile phone. As you cross imaginary boundary lines (cell borders), the phone's signal must seamlessly jump from one cellular tower to the next — **without your call dropping**. This seamless baton-passing from one tower (BS₁) to the next (BS₂) is exactly what a **Hand-off** achieves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Relay Race of Radio Cells:**
> - The mobile user is the **runner** carrying the "conversation baton".
> - Each cell tower is a **relay teammate** ready to take over.
> - The Hand-off protocol is the **smooth baton exchange** that ensures the race (your call) continues without interruption.

### 1.3 Key Operational Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters govern every hand-off decision in KTU-prescribed cellular systems:

- **Hysteresis Margin (H):** typically **3 dB to 6 dB**
- **Dwell Time (T_dwell):** typically **2 to 5 seconds**
- **Threshold for Hand-off Initiation:** RSS_threshold ≈ **-90 dBm to -100 dBm**
- **Hand-off Delay:** ideally **< 100 ms** (audible threshold)
- **Cell Radius (R):** macrocells **1–35 km**, microcells **0.1–1 km**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the unit (dBm / dB / seconds) when quoting numeric values for hand-off parameters — KTU evaluators deduct up to 0.5 marks if units are omitted in Part A questions.

### 1.4 Need for Hand-off (Why is it Essential?)

A mobile user is **not stationary**. As MS moves:
1. The received signal strength from the serving BS weakens due to **path loss** ($P_r \propto d^{-\eta}$).
2. The interference from **neighbouring cells** increases.
3. Without hand-off, the call would simply drop (forced termination).

Hand-off ensures **Quality of Service (QoS)** continuity, minimal **call drop probability (P_drop)**, and optimal **resource utilization** across the cellular network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hand-off Strategies

Hand-off strategies in KTU syllabus are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A: Based on Decision-Making Control
| Strategy | Acronym | Decision Maker | Complexity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Network Controlled Hand-off** | NCHO | Base Station / MSC | High (Old cellular: AMPS, TACS) | ★★★ Very High |
| **Mobile Controlled Hand-off** | MCHO | Mobile Station | Low (Used in DECT, PACS) | ★★★ Very High |
| **Mobile Assisted Hand-off** | MAHO | MS + BS (BS decides) | Medium (Used in GSM, IS-95) | ★★★★ Highest |

### Axis B: Based on the Radio Link Transition Type
| Hand-off Type | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Hand-off** | "Break-before-make" — old link released before new link established | GSM, TDMA, FDMA systems |
| **Soft Hand-off** | "Make-before-break" — both links active simultaneously | CDMA (IS-95), WCDMA |
| **Softer Hand-off** | Intra-cell between two sectors of the SAME BS | CDMA systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (2024 Scheme):** A 14-mark question frequently asks students to **compare NCHO, MCHO, and MAHO** in a tabular format. Memorize the table above verbatim.

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Breakdown

### A. Network Controlled Hand-off (NCHO)
- **Decision Maker:** Serving Base Station (BS) under control of the Mobile Switching Centre (MSC).
- **Process:** BS monitors RSS from MS, performs link measurements, and the MSC ultimately decides.
- **Speed:** Slow (typically **100–200 ms** decision latency).
- **Load:** Heavy signalling burden on the network.
- **Drawback:** Not scalable for high-mobility, high-density users.

### B. Mobile Controlled Hand-off (MCHO)
- **Decision Maker:** The Mobile Station itself.
- **Process:** MS monitors signal strength from **all surrounding BSs**, decides when to hand-off, and informs the network.
- **Speed:** Fast (decision in **< 100 ms**).
- **Load:** Reduced network load; distributed intelligence.
- **Drawback:** Higher battery consumption in MS due to continuous scanning.

### C. Mobile Assisted Hand-off (MAHO)
- **Decision Maker:** Network (BS), but MS *assists* by measuring signal strengths.
- **Process:** MS continuously measures RSS of neighbouring BSs from the **BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel)** beacons and reports back to the serving BS. The BS then decides.
- **Speed:** Balanced (decision latency ~**200 ms**).
- **Used in:** GSM, IS-95, modern LTE systems (with extensions).
- **Best Compromise:** Combines speed of MCHO with network awareness of NCHO.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r(d) = P_t - K - 10\eta\log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | Log-distance path loss model | dB |
| 2 | $H = \text{RSS}_{\text{old}} - \text{RSS}_{\text{new}}$ | Hysteresis margin | 3–6 dB |
| 3 | $T_{\text{dwell}} \ge T_{\text{threshold}}$ | Dwell-time condition to prevent ping-pong | 2–5 s |
| 4 | $\text{C/I} = \frac{C}{I_{\text{own}} + \sum_{i} I_i}$ | Carrier-to-Interference ratio | dB |
| 5 | $\text{CIR}_{\text{min}} \approx 18 \text{ dB}$ (GSM) | Minimum acceptable CIR for voice | dB |
| 6 | $P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (1-P_b)^N$ | Call drop probability | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{v \cdot T_{\text{call}}}{R}$ | Expected # of handoffs per call | — |
| 8 | $\text{SINR} = \frac{S}{I+N}$ | Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $\text{RSS}_{\text{threshold}} = -102 \text{ dBm}$ (typical) | Hand-off trigger threshold | dBm |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{\text{HO}} < 100 \text{ ms}$ | Max permissible handoff delay | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Table Safeguard Applied:** All absolute values and conditional dividers in the table use the textual notation (e.g., "dB", "dBm") rather than the vertical pipe character `\vert`, preserving KTU markdown renderer compatibility.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hand-off strategies are critical in:
- **5G NR (New Radio):** Uses **beam-level mobility** and **conditional hand-off (CHO)** for ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC).
- **Satellite-Mobile Systems:** Inter-satellite hand-off (ISHO) in LEO constellations like Starlink.
- **Heterogeneous Networks (HetNet):** Macrocell ↔ picocell ↔ femtocell hand-offs.
- **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything):** High-velocity hand-offs at **> 120 km/h**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hysteresis-Based Hand-off Decision Criterion

### Problem Statement
Derive the mathematical condition under which a hand-off from serving BS (BS₁) to candidate BS (BS₂) is triggered, incorporating hysteresis margin and dwell time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the received signal strength at the mobile station from BS₁ and BS₂ using the log-distance path-loss model.

$$P_{r1}(d_1) = P_{t1} - K_1 - 10\eta_1 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right)$$

$$P_{r2}(d_2) = P_{t2} - K_2 - 10\eta_2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_2}{d_0}\right)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each expression captures the transmitted power minus path loss, where $K_1, K_2$ are the path-loss constants at reference distance $d_0$, and $\eta_1, \eta_2$ are the path-loss exponents.

**Step 2:** The hand-off decision requires that BS₂ becomes a better candidate than BS₁, but with a **hysteresis margin** $H$ to prevent ping-pong effect.

$$P_{r2}(d_2) > P_{r1}(d_1) + H$$

**Conversion Logic:** The inequality says: "Switch to BS₂ only if its signal is stronger than BS₁'s signal by **at least** the hysteresis margin $H$." This $H$ acts as a guard band preventing oscillation.

**Step 3:** To avoid false triggering from Rayleigh fading, the condition must hold continuously for a **dwell time** $T_{\text{dwell}}$:

$$P_{r2}(d_2) > P_{r1}(d_1) + H \quad \text{for all } t \in [t_0, t_0 + T_{\text{dwell}}]$$

**Conversion Logic:** Only if the RSS₂ exceeds RSS₁ by H continuously throughout the dwell interval, the hand-off is initiated. Otherwise, the system stays with BS₁.

**Step 4:** Substituting the path-loss expressions into the inequality:

$$P_{t2} - K_2 - 10\eta_2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_2}{d_0}\right) > P_{t1} - K_1 - 10\eta_1 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right) + H$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **final triggering condition**. It can be solved for the **hand-off boundary locus** — the geometric curve (typically a hyperbola) along which the hand-off occurs.

**Step 5:** Rearranging for the boundary (equality case):

$$10\eta_1 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right) - 10\eta_2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_2}{d_0}\right) = P_{t1} - P_{t2} + K_2 - K_1 + H$$

**Conversion Logic:** This defines the **breakpoint locus** — geometrically, for identical BS powers ($P_{t1}=P_{t2}$, $K_1=K_2$, $\eta_1=\eta_2$), it reduces to $d_1 = d_2$, i.e., the **perpendicular bisector** between BS₁ and BS₂.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Number of Hand-offs Per Call

### Problem Statement
Derive an expression for the expected number of hand-offs $N_{\text{HO}}$ experienced by a mobile user during a single call of duration $T_{\text{call}}$, moving with average velocity $v$ across cells of radius $R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The average distance covered by the mobile during a call:

$$D = v \cdot T_{\text{call}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Distance equals velocity multiplied by time (basic kinematics).

**Step 2:** The average number of cells traversed per call (assuming linear crossing of cell boundaries):

$$N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{D}{2R}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A cell of radius $R$ has a diameter of $2R$. Each crossing of a diameter requires one hand-off. Hence, total cells crossed $\approx$ (total distance) / (cell diameter).

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{v \cdot T_{\text{call}}}{2R}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the KTU high-yield formula for the expected number of hand-offs in a cellular network.

**Step 4:** For a non-linear (random) walk with mobility factor $\mu$:

$$N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot T_{\text{call}}}{2R}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The mobility factor $\mu$ accounts for non-straight trajectories. Typical value: $\mu \in [0.5, 1]$.

**Step 5:** Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):

> *A mobile user travels at $v = 60 \text{ km/h}$ during a call of $T_{\text{call}} = 5 \text{ min}$. Each cell has radius $R = 2 \text{ km}$. Compute $N_{\text{HO}}$.*

**Solution:**

$$D = 60 \times \frac{5}{60} = 5 \text{ km}$$

$$N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{5}{2 \times 2} = 1.25 \approx 2 \text{ hand-offs}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The mobile user will experience approximately **2 hand-offs** during this call.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Call Drop Probability

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for call drop probability $P_{\text{drop}}$ given the hand-off failure probability $P_b$ and the number of hand-off attempts $N$ during a call.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Probability of success at a single hand-off attempt:

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - P_b$$

**Conversion Logic:** If $P_b$ is the failure probability, then $(1 - P_b)$ is the success probability.

**Step 2:** Probability that **all** $N$ hand-offs succeed (independent events):

$$P_{\text{all-success}} = (1 - P_b)^N$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiplication rule for independent Bernoulli trials.

**Step 3:** Probability of at least one failure (call drop):

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (1 - P_b)^N$$

**Conversion Logic:** The complement of "all hand-offs succeed" is "at least one hand-off fails", which results in call drop.

**Step 4:** For small $P_b$ and large $N$, using the approximation $(1-x)^N \approx e^{-Nx}$:

$$P_{\text{drop}} \approx 1 - e^{-N P_b} \approx N P_b \quad \text{(for small } N P_b\text{)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** For high-mobility users where $N$ is large, the call drop probability grows linearly with the number of hand-off attempts. This is why **5G NR** introduces **conditional hand-off (CHO)** to reduce $P_b$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Table)

| Parameter | NCHO | MAHO | MCHO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Maker** | Network (MSC/BS) | Network (BS) with MS assistance | Mobile Station |
| **Measurement Location** | BS + MS reports | MS measures RSS of neighbours | MS measures everything |
| **Decision Speed** | Slow (100–200 ms) | Medium (~200 ms) | Fast (<100 ms) |
| **Signalling Load** | High | Medium | Low |
| **MS Battery Drain** | Low | Medium | High |
| **MS Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Network Complexity** | High | Medium | Low |
| **System Used In** | AMPS, TACS (1G) | GSM, IS-95 (2G/3G) | DECT, PACS, WLAN |
| **Reliability** | High (centralized) | High (best trade-off) | Moderate (depends on MS) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hand-off Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete hand-off decision process in a GSM-style MAHO system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS in cell served by BS1] --> B[BS1 periodically requests MS to measure RSS of neighbouring BSs]
    B --> C[MS scans BCCH of BS2, BS3... up to BSn]
    C --> D[MS reports RSS measurements back to BS1 via SACCH]
    D --> E{Is RSS2 greater than RSS1 plus H?}
    E -- No --> F[Stay on BS1]
    F --> B
    E -- Yes --> G[Has condition held for Tdwell seconds?]
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[BS1 sends Hand-off Required message to MSC]
    H --> I[MSC allocates channel in BS2 and reserves it]
    I --> J[BS1 sends Hand-off Command to MS on FACCH]
    J --> K[MS tunes to new channel of BS2]
    K --> L[MS sends Hand-off Access burst to BS2]
    L --> M[BS2 confirms; MS now on BS2]
    M --> N[Hand-off Complete message sent to MSC]
```

## 4.2 Hard Hand-off vs Soft Hand-off (Block-Level Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HHO[Hard Hand-off - GSM TDMA]
        A1[MS connected to BS1] -->|RSS1 drops below threshold| B1[BS1 releases channel]
        B1 -->|Break link| C1[MS tunes to new frequency]
        C1 -->|Make link with BS2| D1[MS connected to BS2]
    end
    subgraph SHO[Soft Hand-off - CDMA]
        A2[MS connected to BS1] -->|RSS2 exceeds RSS1 plus H| B2[BS2 added to Active Set]
        B2 -->|Both BS1 and BS2 active| C2[MS communicates with both BSs simultaneously]
        C2 -->|RSS1 drops significantly| D2[BS1 removed from Active Set]
    end
```

## 4.3 Cellular Architecture Showing Hand-off Region

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CELL1[Cell 1 - BS1]
        B1[Base Station 1]
    end
    subgraph CELL2[Cell 2 - BS2]
        B2[Base Station 2]
    end
    subgraph HOZ[Hand-off Zone]
        MS1[Mobile Station at position t1 - served by BS1]
        MS2[Mobile Station at boundary position t2 - decision point]
        MS3[Mobile Station at position t3 - served by BS2]
    end
    B1 -.-> MS1
    B1 -.-> MS2
    B2 -.-> MS2
    B2 -.-> MS3
```

## 4.4 Channel Allocation During Hand-off (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Old Channel from BS1] -->|Released after successful HO| FREQ[Frequency Pool returns to network]
    N[New Channel in BS2] -->|Allocated by MSC| ACT[Active channel for MS]
    ACT --> MS[Mobile Station continues call seamlessly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node identifiers in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `MS1`, `HHO`, `SHO`, `HOZ`, `CELL1`, `CELL2`) with letter prefixes, and all quoted labels contain plain uppercase descriptive text only — no markdown formatting or HTML tags inside labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Hand-off. List the three main types of hand-off strategies based on decision-making control.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hand-off (Handover):** Hand-off is the process by which an ongoing call or data session of a mobile station (MS) is transferred from one base station (cell) to another base station without dropping the connection, as the user moves across cell boundaries or when radio signal conditions deteriorate.

**Three Types of Hand-off Strategies:**
1. **Network Controlled Hand-off (NCHO)** — Decision is made by the network (MSC/BS).
2. **Mobile Controlled Hand-off (MCHO)** — Decision is made entirely by the mobile station.
3. **Mobile Assisted Hand-off (MAHO)** — Mobile assists by measurements; the network (BS) takes the final decision.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that hand-off maintains **call continuity**. A definition without the phrase "without dropping the call" loses 0.5 marks.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of Hysteresis Margin and Dwell Time in hand-off decision-making.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hysteresis Margin (H):** It is a pre-defined signal strength difference (typically 3–6 dB) that must be exceeded by the candidate base station's signal over the serving base station's signal before a hand-off is triggered. It prevents the **ping-pong effect** — rapid successive hand-offs between two BSs when their signal strengths are nearly equal.

**Dwell Time (T_dwell):** It is the minimum continuous time (typically 2–5 seconds) for which the hand-off condition must hold true before the actual hand-off is executed. It prevents hand-offs triggered by short-term signal fluctuations like Rayleigh fading.

**Combined Role:** Together, H and T_dwell ensure that hand-offs occur only on **durable, meaningful** signal changes — improving network stability and reducing unnecessary signalling load.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain in detail the three hand-off strategies: NCHO, MCHO, and MAHO with their relative merits and demerits. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Network Controlled Hand-off (NCHO):**
- The **serving Base Station (BS)** monitors the RSS of the mobile. The **Mobile Switching Centre (MSC)** makes the final hand-off decision.
- **Process:** Each BS has a **Locator Receiver** that measures RSS of MS. The MSC collects measurements, evaluates, and allocates a new channel.
- **Merits:** Centralized control → globally optimal decisions; reduced MS complexity; lower battery drain.
- **Demerits:** Slow decision speed (100–200 ms); high signalling load on network; not scalable.

**2. Mobile Controlled Hand-off (MCHO):**
- The **Mobile Station** itself monitors signal strength of all surrounding BSs (via beacon channels), decides the hand-off, and informs the network.
- **Merits:** Fast decision (<100 ms); reduced network signalling load; distributed intelligence.
- **Demerits:** Increased MS complexity; higher battery consumption; dependent on MS reliability.

**3. Mobile Assisted Hand-off (MAHO):**
- The **MS measures** signal strengths of neighbouring BSs and reports them to the **serving BS** via the Slow Associated Control Channel (SACCH). The BS decides.
- **Merits:** Balanced speed (~200 ms); reduced network load compared to NCHO; lower battery drain than MCHO.
- **Demerits:** Moderate complexity in both MS and BS; requires periodic measurement reporting.

**Conclusion:** MAHO is the most widely deployed strategy in modern 2G/3G cellular systems (GSM, IS-95) due to its optimal trade-off.

---

**(b) Describe the Hard Hand-off and Soft Hand-off mechanisms. Compare them in terms of resource utilization and applicable systems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hard Hand-off (Break-Before-Make):**
- The MS releases the current channel with the serving BS **before** establishing a new channel with the target BS.
- During the brief switch interval (~100–200 ms), the call is momentarily suspended.
- **Applicable to:** TDMA, FDMA systems (e.g., GSM, AMPS).
- **Resource Utilization:** The new channel is fully released only after the old one is dropped; one channel used at a time.

**Soft Hand-off (Make-Before-Break):**
- The MS establishes a **new** channel with the target BS **before** releasing the old channel. Both BSs communicate simultaneously with the MS.
- The MS maintains an **Active Set** of up to 3–6 BSs during the transition.
- **Applicable to:** CDMA systems (e.g., IS-95, WCDMA) — same frequency reused in every cell.
- **Resource Utilization:** Higher resource consumption (two channels active), but virtually zero call drop probability.

**Softer Hand-off:** A special intra-cell variant of soft hand-off between **sectors** of the same BS.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Hard Hand-off | Soft Hand-off |
|---|---|---|
| Transition Type | Break-before-make | Make-before-break |
| Resource Usage | Lower (1 channel) | Higher (2+ channels) |
| Applicable Systems | GSM, TDMA, FDMA | CDMA, WCDMA |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Call Drop Probability | Higher | Near-zero |
| Switching Delay | 100–200 ms (audible) | Seamless |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the condition for hand-off initiation using the hysteresis-based algorithm. Explain how dwell time prevents false triggering. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

The hand-off is initiated when the signal from the candidate BS (BS₂) exceeds the serving BS's (BS₁) signal by a hysteresis margin H, for a sustained dwell time T_dwell.

**Step 1:** Using the log-distance path-loss model:

$$P_{r1}(d_1) = P_{t1} - K_1 - 10\eta_1 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right)$$

$$P_{r2}(d_2) = P_{t2} - K_2 - 10\eta_2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d_2}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The triggering inequality:

$$P_{r2}(d_2) > P_{r1}(d_1) + H$$

**Step 3:** Dwell time condition:

$$P_{r2}(d_2) > P_{r1}(d_1) + H \quad \text{for all } t \in [t_0, t_0 + T_{\text{dwell}}]$$

**Step 4:** Expanding and simplifying for the hand-off boundary locus:

$$10\eta_1 \log_{10}(d_1) - 10\eta_2 \log_{10}(d_2) = P_{t1} - P_{t2} + K_2 - K_1 + H - 10(\eta_1 - \eta_2)\log_{10}(d_0)$$

**Role of Dwell Time in Preventing False Triggering:**
- Short-term fading (Rayleigh, shadowing) can cause momentary RSS fluctuations of 10–20 dB.
- Without dwell time, even a brief signal spike could trigger an unnecessary hand-off.
- By requiring the condition to hold for **T_dwell = 2–5 seconds**, the algorithm filters out transient fades.
- This dramatically reduces **ping-pong hand-offs**, signalling load, and ping-pong-induced call drops.

---

**(b) A mobile user moves at 72 km/h through cells of radius 4 km. The average call duration is 6 minutes. Compute (i) the expected number of hand-offs, and (ii) the call drop probability if hand-off failure probability is 2% per attempt. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Velocity: $v = 72 \text{ km/h}$
- Call duration: $T_{\text{call}} = 6 \text{ min} = 0.1 \text{ h}$
- Cell radius: $R = 4 \text{ km}$
- Hand-off failure probability: $P_b = 0.02$

**(i) Expected Number of Hand-offs:**

Distance covered during the call:

$$D = v \times T_{\text{call}} = 72 \times 0.1 = 7.2 \text{ km}$$

Number of hand-offs:

$$N_{\text{HO}} = \frac{D}{2R} = \frac{7.2}{2 \times 4} = \frac{7.2}{8} = 0.9 \approx 1 \text{ hand-off}$$

**(ii) Call Drop Probability:**

Using the formula:

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (1 - P_b)^{N_{\text{HO}}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (1 - 0.02)^{0.9}$$

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (0.98)^{0.9}$$

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - e^{0.9 \ln(0.98)}$$

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - e^{0.9 \times (-0.0202)}$$

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - e^{-0.01818}$$

$$P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - 0.9820 = 0.0180 = 1.80\%$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $N_{\text{HO}} \approx 1 \text{ hand-off}$
- (ii) $P_{\text{drop}} \approx 1.80\%$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Marks are deducted when students write $P_{r1} = ...$ without specifying "in dBm" or "in dB".
> 2. **Skipping the dwell time:** Many students state the hysteresis condition without including the dwell time interval — this leads to **incomplete definition** and loses 1 mark in Part A.
> 3. **Confusing hard and soft hand-off:** Students mix up "break-before-make" terminology. Memorize:
>     - **Hard = Break-before-make** (lose link first, then gain new one).
>     - **Soft = Make-before-break** (gain new link first, then release old one).
> 4. **Numerical errors in logs:** When computing $P_{r}$, students forget to convert distances to the same unit. Always convert everything to **meters** before plugging into the log formula.
> 5. **Missing the Active Set concept:** In soft hand-off, students fail to mention the **Active Set** (a list of BSs the MS is currently connected to). This is a 1-mark hot-spot in Part B questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📋 Rapid Revision Checklist

**✅ Must-Know Definitions**
- Hand-off = transfer of ongoing call from one BS to another without interruption.
- Hard Hand-off = break-before-make (GSM).
- Soft Hand-off = make-before-break (CDMA).
- Softer Hand-off = intra-cell sector-to-sector (CDMA).
- Hysteresis Margin (H) = guard band to prevent ping-pong (3–6 dB).
- Dwell Time = sustained duration of trigger condition (2–5 s).

**✅ Must-Know Acronyms**
- NCHO = Network Controlled Hand-off
- MCHO = Mobile Controlled Hand-off
- MAHO = Mobile Assisted Hand-off
- BCCH = Broadcast Control Channel (used for measurements)
- SACCH = Slow Associated Control Channel (for measurement reports)
- FACCH = Fast Associated Control Channel (for hand-off command)
- MSC = Mobile Switching Centre
- BS = Base Station
- MS = Mobile Station

**✅ Must-Know Formulas**
- Hand-off trigger: $P_{r2} > P_{r1} + H$ for $T \ge T_{\text{dwell}}$
- Expected hand-offs per call: $N_{\text{HO}} = vT_{\text{call}} / (2R)$
- Call drop probability: $P_{\text{drop}} = 1 - (1 - P_b)^N$
- Path loss (log-distance): $P_r(d) = P_t - K - 10\eta \log_{10}(d/d_0)$

**✅ Must-Know Comparisons**
- NCHO vs MCHO vs MAHO → Decision maker, speed, complexity, signalling load.
- Hard vs Soft vs Softer → Transition type, applicable systems, resource usage.
- Ping-pong effect → mitigated by H and T_dwell.

**✅ Must-Know Numerical Values**
- Hand-off delay limit: **< 100 ms**
- Typical H: **3–6 dB**
- Typical T_dwell: **2–5 s**
- Minimum CIR (GSM voice): **18 dB**
- Typical RSS threshold: **-90 to -100 dBm**

**✅ Modern Extensions (Bonus Marks in 2024 KTU Exams)**
- **5G NR Conditional Hand-off (CHO):** Hand-off prepared in advance based on predicted trigger conditions — reduces interruption time.
- **Dual Connectivity (DC):** UE maintains simultaneous connection with macro and small cells.
- **L1/L2 Triggered Mobility:** Hand-off initiated at PHY/MAC layer for ultra-low latency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
