topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Roaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 | Roaming in Mobile Communication

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Roaming** is a wireless telecommunications feature that enables a mobile subscriber to automatically make and receive calls, send/receive SMS, and access data services when traveling **outside the geographical coverage area of their Home Public Land Mobile Network (HPLMN)**, by transparently utilising the resources of a **Visited Public Land Mobile Network (VPLMN)** under a pre-established commercial and technical agreement between the two network operators.

In GSM/cellular systems, roaming is governed by the **GSM Association (GSMA)** through standardised **Roaming Agreements** and signalling procedures defined under the **MAP (Mobile Application Part)** protocol over the **SS7 (Signalling System No. 7)** backbone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Technical Distinction:** Roaming is *not* the same as *Handover/Handoff*.  
> - **Handover** = Movement of an active call between **cells** (same network).  
> - **Roaming** = Movement of a subscriber between **networks** (different operators) while maintaining service continuity.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The International Power Plug"

Imagine your mobile phone is a device with one specific "power plug" (your **Home Network's SIM/IMSI**). When you travel to a foreign country (the **Visited Network**), your plug won't fit the local sockets directly. **Roaming** is like having a **universal travel adapter**: your phone can plug into *any* foreign network's socket (subject to bilateral agreements), and the foreign network (VPLMN) recognises you, authenticates you via your home operator's records, and bills your home operator, who then bills you.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Roaming = *“Use your home phone number in a place where your home operator has no towers, by borrowing another operator’s towers, while your home network still vouches for you and bills you.”*

---

### 1.3 Classification of Roaming (Syllabus-Relevant Types)

| S.No. | Roaming Type | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **National / Domestic Roaming** | Subscriber moves across regions covered by *different operators within the same country*. |
| 2 | **International Roaming** | Subscriber travels to a *foreign country* and uses a foreign VPLMN. |
| 3 | **Inter-MSC Roaming** | Subscriber moves between two MSCs of *different operators*. |
| 4 | **Inter-PLMN Roaming** | Generic term for roaming between two Public Land Mobile Networks. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory condition for roaming:** A valid **Roaming Agreement (RA)** between the HPLMN and VPLMN operators *must* exist. Without it, no signalling or billing handshake occurs, and the SIM is rejected.

---

### 1.4 Core Network Entities Involved in Roaming

| Entity | Full Form | Role in Roaming |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **MS** | Mobile Station | The user's handset + SIM |
| **HPLMN** | Home PLMN | Subscriber's registered home network |
| **VPLMN** | Visited PLMN | Foreign/other network where the MS is currently located |
| **HLR** | Home Location Register | Permanent subscriber database (IMSI, MSISDN, auth keys) — stays in HPLMN |
| **VLR** | Visitor Location Register | Temporary database in VPLMN that holds roaming subscriber's profile during their visit |
| **AUC** | Authentication Centre | Generates security triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc) — part of HLR |
| **MSC** | Mobile Switching Centre | Switches the call; in roaming, both H-MSC and V-MSC cooperate |
| **GMSC** | Gateway MSC | Entry/exit point for calls from/to PSTN or other networks |
| **MSRN** | Mobile Station Roaming Number | *Temporary* number assigned by VLR to route an incoming call to the roamed MS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Roaming Architecture — Why It Works

The genius of GSM roaming lies in the **separation of identity from location**:

- **Identity** (who you are) is stored permanently in your **HLR** — the IMSI, MSISDN, and Ki (authentication key) never travel outside the HPLMN's secure AUC.
- **Location** (where you are right now) is dynamically tracked by **VLRs** in whichever network you are visiting.

When you enter a VPLMN coverage area, the VLR of that network **queries your HLR** (over SS7/MAP) using your **IMSI**, fetches a copy of your profile, and creates a temporary record. All this happens within **~2–3 seconds** (the time between phone-on and network name display).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The Three Pillars of Roaming:**
> 1. **Signalling Pillar** → MAP/SS7 messages between HLR & VLR  
> 2. **Security Pillar** → Authentication Triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc)  
> 3. **Billing Pillar** → TAP (Transferred Account Procedure) files exchanged between operators for inter-operator settlement

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow (Conceptual Logic)

**Phase 1 — Detection & Registration**
1. MS powers on in VPLMN coverage; reads **MCC+MNC** (Mobile Country + Network Code) from the BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel).
2. If the MCC+MNC ≠ HPLMN codes → MS initiates a **Location Update Request** with the *type = "roaming"* indicator.
3. The VLR of VPLMN receives the request; the VLR does **NOT** have the subscriber's profile yet.

**Phase 2 — Inter-Network Signalling (HLR–VLR Dialogue)**
4. VLR sends **MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION** to the HLR (identified by IMSI's CC+NDC).
5. HLR authenticates the request, generates **Authentication Triplets**, and replies with **MAP_INSERT_SUBSCRIBER_DATA** containing: MSISDN, subscribed services (GSM/UMTS/LTE), bearer capabilities, and 5 triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc).
6. HLR also sends **MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION** to the **old VLR** (if any) to delete the stale record.

**Phase 3 — Authentication & Ciphering**
7. VLR challenges the MS via **AUTHENTICATION_REQUEST** (RAND).
8. MS computes **SRES** using A3 algorithm on (RAND, Ki) and replies.
9. If VLR's stored SRES matches MS's SRES → authentication successful; Kc is used for A5 ciphering.

**Phase 4 — Service Availability**
10. VLR acknowledges the MS with **LOCATION_UPDATE_ACCEPT**; MS is now registered, and the phone displays the VPLMN name (or partner's name).

**Phase 5 — Incoming Call Routing (the hardest part)**
11. A caller dials the MS's **MSISDN** (home number) → reaches the **GMSC** of the HPLMN.
12. GMSC queries HLR via **MAP_SEND_ROUTING_INFORMATION (SRI)** to ask: *"Where is this subscriber right now?"*
13. HLR queries the current VLR via **MAP_PROVIDE_ROAMING_NUMBER (PRN)**.
14. VLR allocates a **temporary MSRN** (Mobile Station Roaming Number) and returns it to HLR.
15. HLR relays MSRN to GMSC; GMSC routes the call to the V-MSC, which uses MSRN to page the MS.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following identities and numbers are the absolute essentials for any KTU question on roaming.**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Units / Range |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $IMSI$ | International Mobile Subscriber Identity | $IMSI = MCC + MNC + MSIN$ | Max **15 digits** |
| $MCC$ | Mobile Country Code | Fixed per country (India = **404, 405**) | **3 digits** |
| $MNC$ | Mobile Network Code | Identifies operator (e.g., Airtel = **10, 45, 49, 70, 90, 92, 93, 94, 95, 96, 97, 98**) | **2 or 3 digits** |
| $MSIN$ | Mobile Subscriber Identification Number | Unique within an MNC | Max **10 digits** |
| $MSISDN$ | Mobile Station ISDN Number (the dialable number) | $MSISDN = CC + NDC + SN$ | Max **15 digits** |
| $CC$ | Country Code | E.g., India = **91** | 1–3 digits |
| $NDC$ | National Destination Code | Identifies operator within country | Variable |
| $SN$ | Subscriber Number | Allocated to user | Variable |
| $MSRN$ | Mobile Station Roaming Number | $MSRN = CC + NDC + VLR\_ID + SN_{temp}$ | Temporary, allocated by VLR |
| $TMSI$ | Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity | Assigned by VLR; opaque to HLR | 4 octets (32 bits) |
| $RAND$ | Random Number (Authentication) | Generated by AUC; 128 bits | — |
| $SRES$ | Signed Response | $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$ | 32 bits |
| $K_c$ | Ciphering Key | $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$ | 64 bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $IMSI$ with $MSISDN$.  
> - **$IMSI$** is *written on the SIM* and is used for **network-internal identification** (never dialed).  
> - **$MSISDN$** is the *phone number* you give to people (used for call routing).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom Operators** (Reliance Jio, Airtel, Vi, BSNL) derive **~20–30% of their revenue** from inbound roamers (foreign tourists + business travellers).
- **Maritime / Aviation** industries rely on roaming for ships and aircraft to stay connected across oceans.
- **IoT & M2M** — modern fleet tracking, asset trackers, and connected cars use **SGP.22 eSIM roaming** to switch operators over-the-air without physical SIM swap.
- **5G Roaming** extends this to **SEPP (Security Edge Protection Proxy)** for inter-PLMN signalling, with N26 interface for session continuity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Identity Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation of Subscriber Identities

### 3.1.1 IMSI Structure Derivation

The IMSI is the **unique global identity** of a mobile subscriber. Per ITU-T E.212, it is constructed as:

$$
IMSI = \underbrace{MCC}_{\text{3 digits}} + \underbrace{MNC}_{\text{2 or 3 digits}} + \underbrace{MSIN}_{\text{up to 10 digits}}
$$

Total length is fixed at **15 decimal digits maximum**.

**Step-by-step construction logic:**
- **Step 1:** The ITU allocates a unique **MCC** to each country. India has two MCCs: **404** and **405**.
- **Step 2:** Within each country, the national regulator (in India, **DoT/TRAI**) allocates **MNCs** to each operator.
- **Step 3:** The operator internally assigns a unique **MSIN** to each subscriber.
- **Step 4:** The full **IMSI** is then **burnt into the SIM card** at manufacture and is never disclosed to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Given MCC = 404, MNC = 45, MSIN = 1234567890, find the IMSI."*  
> **Answer:** $IMSI = 404\,45\,1234567890 = 404451234567890$ (a 15-digit number).

---

### 3.1.2 MSISDN Structure Derivation

The MSISDN is defined per ITU-T E.164 and is the **dialable phone number**:

$$
MSISDN = \underbrace{CC}_{\text{Country Code}} + \underbrace{NDC}_{\text{National Dest. Code}} + \underbrace{SN}_{\text{Subscriber No.}}
$$

Maximum length is **15 digits**. For example, an Indian Airtel number in Delhi:

$$
MSISDN = 91 + 98100 + 12345 = 919810012345
$$

The **CC** is **NOT** stored on the SIM — it is assigned by the numbering plan of the country. Only the **NDC + SN** portion is operator-specific.

---

### 3.1.3 MSRN Allocation (Critical for Call Delivery in Roaming)

When a roamed MS must receive a call, the VLR assigns a **temporary MSRN** with the following structure:

$$
MSRN = CC + NDC_{VMSC} + VLR\_ID + SN_{temp}
$$

**Logical derivation of the call-delivery procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Caller dials } MSISDN_{MS} \rightarrow \text{reaches GMSC}_{HPLMN} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & GMSC \xrightarrow{\text{MAP\_SRI}} HLR \quad \text{("Where is MSISDN?")} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & HLR \xrightarrow{\text{MAP\_PRN}} VLR \quad \text{("Give me a roaming number for IMSI")} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & VLR \text{ allocates } MSRN \text{ from its pool} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & VLR \xrightarrow{\text{MAP\_PRN\_ACK}} HLR \text{ with } MSRN \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & HLR \xrightarrow{\text{MAP\_SRI\_ACK}} GMSC \text{ with } MSRN \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & GMSC \text{ routes call to } V\text{-}MSC \text{ using } MSRN \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & V\text{-}MSC \text{ uses } MSRN \text{ to page the MS over the air interface}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The MSRN is **released back to the VLR pool** once the call is terminated. It is **not** permanent, unlike IMSI/MSISDN.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Signalling Messages in Roaming

| Phase | Message (MAP) | Direction | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Registration | `MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION` | VLR → HLR | "I have a new visitor" |
| Registration | `MAP_INSERT_SUBSCRIBER_DATA` | HLR → VLR | Send profile + 5 auth triplets |
| Registration | `MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION` | HLR → Old VLR | Delete stale record |
| Authentication | `MAP_AUTHENTICATE` | VLR ↔ HLR (optional) | Request more triplets |
| Call Delivery | `MAP_SEND_ROUTING_INFORMATION` | GMSC → HLR | Initial routing query |
| Call Delivery | `MAP_PROVIDE_ROAMING_NUMBER` | HLR → VLR | Request MSRN |
| Handover | `MAP_PREPARE_HANDOVER` | V-MSC → H-MSC | Inter-MSC handover setup |
| Detachment | `MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION` | HLR → VLR | MS switched off / left area |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A mobile subscriber has $IMSI = 404\,70\,9988776655$ and the home operator's NDC is **98**. While travelling in the USA, the VLR allocates MSRN = **1 415 555 1234**. Identify MCC, MNC, MSIN, and the role of the assigned MSRN.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IMSI} &= 404 \, 70 \, 9988776655 \\
\text{MCC} &= 404 \quad (\text{India}) \\
\text{MNC} &= 70 \quad (\text{Operator identifier, e.g., Airtel Punjab}) \\
\text{MSIN} &= 9988776655 \quad (\text{10-digit unique subscriber ID}) \\
\\
\text{MSRN} &= 1\,415\,555\,1234 \\
\text{CC} &= 1 \quad (\text{USA}) \\
\text{NDC} &= 415 \quad (\text{San Francisco area code}) \\
\text{VLR\_ID + SN}_{temp} &= 555\,1234 \quad (\text{temporary routing suffix})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of MSRN:** The MSRN acts as a **temporary addressing alias** for the roamed MS. The GMSC of the HPLMN (India) uses this MSRN to route the incoming call from India through the international PSTN/ISUP backbone to the V-MSC in San Francisco, which then pages the MS over its current BTS. Without MSRN, the HPLMN would have no way to know the current V-MSC's addressing domain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Roaming Network Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional entities and signalling/data paths** involved when a home subscriber roams into a visited network.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS1[Mobile Station MS with SIM] -->|Radio Um Interface| BTS1[BTS VPLMN]
    BTS1 --> BSC1[BSC VPLMN]
    BSC1 --> MSC1[MSC V PLMN VLR Co located]
    MSC1 -->|MAP SS7 Signalling| HLR1[HLR HPLMN]
    MSC1 -->|MAP SS7 Signalling| VLR1[VLR VPLMN]
    HLR1 -->|MAP SS7 Signalling| AUC1[AUC HPLMN]
    HLR1 -->|MAP SRI PRN| GMSC1[GMSC HPLMN]
    GMSC1 -->|ISUP Trunk| PSTN1[PSTN External Caller]
    MSC1 -->|TAP Billing File Clearing House| Billing1[Billing and Settlement]
    HLR1 -->|TAP Billing File Clearing House| Billing1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**  
> - The **left cluster** (MS → BTS → BSC → MSC/VLR) belongs to the **Visited Network (VPLMN)**.  
> - The **right cluster** (HLR, AUC, GMSC) belongs to the **Home Network (HPLMN)**.  
> - The **dashed/horizontal arrows** represent the **MAP/SS7 signalling tunnel** that crosses international/operator boundaries — this is the heart of roaming.

---

## 4.2 Roaming Call-Delivery Sequence (End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as External Caller
    participant GMSCH as GMSC Home
    participant HLRnode as HLR Home
    participant VLRnode as VLR Visited
    participant VMSCnode as V MSC Visited
    participant MSnode as Roamed Mobile Station

    Caller->>GMSCH: 1. Dial MSISDN of MS
    GMSCH->>HLRnode: 2. MAP SRI Send Routing Info
    HLRnode->>VLRnode: 3. MAP PRN Provide Roaming Number
    VLRnode->>VLRnode: 4. Allocate temporary MSRN
    VLRnode->>HLRnode: 5. Return MSRN in PRN Ack
    HLRnode->>GMSCH: 6. Return MSRN in SRI Ack
    GMSCH->>VMSCnode: 7. ISUP IAM Initial Address with MSRN
    VMSCnode->>VLRnode: 8. Look up MSRN to find TMSI IMSI
    VMSCnode->>MSnode: 9. Paging over PCH
    MSnode->>VMSCnode: 10. Paging Response
    VMSCnode->>MSnode: 11. Set up call RACH SDCCH TCH
    MSnode->>Caller: 12. Voice path established
```

---

## 4.3 Roaming Triplet Generation & Authentication (Security Pillar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS sends IMSI during Location Update] --> B[VLR forwards IMSI to HLR over SS7]
    B --> C[HLR fetches Ki from AUC]
    C --> D[AUC generates 5 Triplets]
    D --> E1[RAND 128 bit random]
    D --> E2[SRES equals A3 of Ki and RAND]
    D --> E3[Kc equals A8 of Ki and RAND]
    E1 --> F[VLR stores triplets and forwards RAND to MS]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[MS computes SRES locally using A3]
    G --> H{SRES matches VLR copy?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Authentication SUCCESS MS attached]
    H -->|No| J[Authentication FAILURE Reject MS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the secret key **$K_i$** *never* leaves the AUC. Only the triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc) is exported to the VLR. This is a classic **challenge-response security design** — even if the VLR's database is compromised, the attacker cannot impersonate the subscriber on another VLR.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define Roaming. Differentiate between Handover and Roaming.**

**Model Answer:**

*Roaming* is a feature of mobile networks that allows a subscriber to obtain services from a network other than their Home Public Land Mobile Network (HPLMN) when they are outside the geographical coverage of their home operator, provided a roaming agreement exists between the two operators.

| Aspect | Handover (Handoff) | Roaming |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Scope | Movement between **cells** of the **same** network | Movement between **different networks/operators** |
| Trigger | Signal strength drop during an **active call** | Subscriber physically moves to a new operator's region |
| Signalling | Internal to one BSC/MSC | Inter-PLMN MAP/SS7 between HLR and VLR |
| Example | Walking from one BTS zone to another | Travelling abroad and using a foreign operator's SIM/towers |

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if both definition AND clean distinction are present.)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] What is the role of MSRN in a roaming scenario? Why is it temporary?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mobile Station Roaming Number (MSRN)** is a **temporary, geographically significant telephone number** allocated by the VLR of the visited network when a call needs to be routed to a roaming subscriber. It allows the GMSC of the home network to forward the incoming call to the correct V-MSC in the visited country.

It is *temporary* because:
1. The MS may move to *another* VLR area within the same VPLMN, requiring a fresh MSRN.
2. Releasing MSRNs back to the VLR pool enables *number conservation* and *re-use*.
3. It protects long-term subscriber location privacy — the MSRN changes per call/session.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of GSM with reference to roaming. List the major entities in HPLMN and VPLMN.**

**Model Answer:**

The GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) architecture is divided into three major subsystems: **MS (Mobile Station)**, **BSS (Base Station Subsystem)**, and **NSS (Network Subsystem)**. For roaming, we focus on the NSS.

**HPLMN Entities (Home Network):**
1. **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database of the subscriber — stores IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, subscribed services, and Ki.
2. **AUC (Authentication Centre):** Co-located with HLR; generates authentication triplets and stores Ki.
3. **GMSC (Gateway MSC):** The PSTN-facing exchange; first hop for any incoming call to the subscriber.

**VPLMN Entities (Visited Network):**
1. **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Dynamic database that stores a roaming subscriber's profile *temporarily* while they are in the coverage area.
2. **V-MSC (Visited MSC):** The mobile switching centre serving the BTS under which the MS is currently camped.
3. **BTS / BSC:** Radio access network elements (same as in HPLMN).

**Roaming-specific extensions:**
- The **SS7/MAP** signalling backbone links VLR ↔ HLR transparently across operator boundaries.
- The **MSRN** allocation logic and **triplet exchange** are roaming-only features.
- The **SIM** in the MS carries the IMSI that uniquely identifies the subscriber to any VLR worldwide.

**Diagram Reference:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.)*

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks] With a neat sequence diagram, describe how an incoming call is delivered to a roaming subscriber. What is the role of MSRN?**

**Model Answer:**

The call delivery to a roaming MS proceeds in **8 logical steps** (Refer to Section 4.2 sequence diagram):

1. **Caller** dials the MSISDN of the roaming subscriber. The call lands on the **GMSC** of the HPLMN.
2. The **GMSC** does not know where the MS is, so it sends a **MAP_SEND_ROUTING_INFORMATION (SRI)** query to the **HLR** asking for routing information.
3. The **HLR** checks its database, finds the address of the **current VLR** (where the MS last registered), and sends a **MAP_PROVIDE_ROAMING_NUMBER (PRN)** request to that VLR.
4. The **VLR** dynamically allocates a **temporary MSRN** from its number pool and returns it to the HLR in the PRN acknowledgement.
5. The **HLR** relays this MSRN to the GMSC in the SRI acknowledgement.
6. The **GMSC** now uses the MSRN as a regular E.164 address and routes the call over **ISUP** trunks to the **V-MSC** in the visited country.
7. The **V-MSC** queries its VLR using the MSRN to retrieve the **TMSI** and the exact location (cell/sector) of the MS.
8. The V-MSC **pages** the MS over the PCH (Paging Channel); the MS responds on RACH, and the voice channel (TCH) is established. The call is now connected.

**Role of MSRN:**
- It acts as a **dynamic location alias** — a temporary phone number that the HPLMN's GMSC can dial to reach the MS wherever it is in the VPLMN.
- It hides the permanent MSISDN from the international signalling network, providing a layer of **addressing abstraction** and **load distribution**.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks] Explain the location updating procedure in GSM when a mobile station moves from its home network to a foreign network (roaming scenario). Mention all the MAP messages involved.**

**Model Answer:**

When an MS moves into a VPLMN area and powers on, the following **Location Update Procedure** is triggered (Refer to Section 4.3 security diagram for authentication triplet flow):

**Step 1 — Detection:** MS scans the BCCH of nearby BTSs, reads the **MCC+MNC** of the VPLMN, compares it with the MCC+MNC stored on its SIM. If mismatched → roaming case.

**Step 2 — IMSI Send:** MS initiates a **Location Update Request** on the RACH/SDCCH with the *type=roaming* indicator and its **IMSI**.

**Step 3 — VLR → HLR (MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION):** The VLR of the VPLMN, not having any record of the IMSI, queries the HLR (whose address is derived from the IMSI's CC+NDC) over SS7 using the **MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION** message.

**Step 4 — HLR → VLR (MAP_INSERT_SUBSCRIBER_DATA):** The HLR authenticates the request, then sends the subscriber's full profile (services, capabilities) and **5 authentication triplets** (RAND, SRES, Kc) to the VLR using **MAP_INSERT_SUBSCRIBER_DATA**.

**Step 5 — HLR → Old VLR (MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION):** If the HLR has a previous VLR entry, it sends **MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION** to delete the stale record.

**Step 6 — Authentication (MAP_AUTHENTICATE optional):** The VLR may challenge the MS with one of the stored RANDs. The MS computes SRES = A3(Ki, RAND) and returns it. VLR compares with stored SRES — match = authenticated.

**Step 7 — LOCATION_UPDATE_ACCEPT:** VLR sends acceptance message to the MS; the MS is now registered. The handset displays the VPLMN name.

**Step 8 — TMSI Reallocation:** VLR assigns a new **TMSI** to the MS for subsequent signalling on the radio interface (privacy).

**MAP Messages Summary Table:**

| # | Message | Direction |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION | VLR → HLR |
| 2 | MAP_INSERT_SUBSCRIBER_DATA | HLR → VLR |
| 3 | MAP_CANCEL_LOCATION | HLR → Old VLR |
| 4 | MAP_AUTHENTICATE | VLR ↔ HLR (optional) |
| 5 | MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION_ACK | VLR → HLR |
| 6 | MAP_READY_FOR_SM (for SMS) | VLR → HLR |

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks] Describe the structure of IMSI and MSISDN. With a suitable example, explain how MSRN is allocated and used in call routing.**

**Model Answer:**

*(Structure derivations as given in Section 3.1.1, 3.1.2, and 3.1.3 — present the formulas and the worked numerical example here.)*

**Summary Table for the Answer Script:**

| Parameter | Structure | Length | Visibility |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| IMSI | MCC + MNC + MSIN | ≤ 15 digits | Internal — on SIM only |
| MSISDN | CC + NDC + SN | ≤ 15 digits | Public — diallable |
| MSRN | CC + NDC + VLR_ID + SN_temp | ≤ 15 digits | Temporary — VLR pool |

> **Example (already worked in Section 3.3):** Indian MS with IMSI = 404709988776655, MSISDN = 919810012345, MSRN = 1 415 555 1234 (allocated in San Francisco). The MSRN enables the Indian GMSC to reach the MS in the US.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Roaming Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing IMSI and MSISDN** — IMSI is for network-internal use, MSISDN is the dialable number. Examiners *will* deduct **1 mark** for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of Ki in AUC** — Many students write that "Ki is sent to VLR". This is **WRONG**. Ki *never* leaves the AUC; only triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc) are exported. Examiner penalises 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the Roaming Agreement prerequisite** — Roaming is impossible without a commercial agreement. Any model answer that ignores this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Writing only the diagram without labelling MAP messages** — A block diagram without naming the *MAP_UPDATE_LOCATION, MAP_SRI, MAP_PRN* messages is considered incomplete. Always label the *signalling protocol* on every arrow.
> 5. **Confusing MSRN with TMSI** — MSRN is routable (E.164 format, used for call routing). TMSI is *only* an air-interface identity (opaque, VLR-internal). This is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 6. **Ignoring the TAP (billing) pillar** — A complete roaming answer must mention the *Transferred Account Procedure* and the *Clearing House* for inter-operator settlement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorise before exam):**
> - **Roaming** = subscriber uses VPLMN while HLR remains in HPLMN.
> - **IMSI** (max 15 digits) = MCC (3) + MNC (2/3) + MSIN (≤ 10) → on SIM, never dialled.
> - **MSISDN** (max 15 digits) = CC + NDC + SN → the dialable phone number.
> - **MSRN** = temporary roaming number, allocated by VLR, used for one call/session only.
> - **TMSI** = temporary air-interface ID, assigned by VLR, used for radio signalling privacy.
> - **Ki** is **never** transmitted; only (RAND, SRES, Kc) triplets cross the SS7 link.
> - **A3** = authentication algo; **A8** = key-generation algo; **A5** = ciphering algo.
> - **Roaming needs three pillars:** Signalling (MAP/SS7), Security (Triplets), Billing (TAP).
> - **Handover ≠ Roaming** — handover is within a network, roaming is across networks.
> - **Roaming Agreement** is a mandatory *commercial* prerequisite; MAP messages are the *technical* enabler.
> - **GMSC** queries HLR (SRI) → HLR queries VLR (PRN) → VLR returns MSRN → call routed to V-MSC.

**Frequently Asked KTU Question Verbs:**
- *Define* → Cover formal definition + 1-line analogy.
- *Explain* → Cover the *why* and *how* with at least 1 diagram/flow.
- *Differentiate* → Always use a comparison table; never use prose only.
- *With an example* → Always show a worked numerical identity (IMSI/MSISDN/MSRN).

**End of Module 3 — Roaming Notes** ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improving Capacity in Cellular System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Improving Capacity in Cellular System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Enhancement in Cellular Systems** refers to the systematic engineering techniques used to increase the number of subscribers (traffic channels) that a given cellular network can support per unit area, without increasing the allocated radio spectrum bandwidth. The three principal KTU-mandated methods are **Cell Splitting**, **Sectoring**, and **Coverage Zone Approach (Microcell Zones)**.

In a frequency-division multiple access (FDMA) cellular architecture, the system capacity $C$ per cluster is fundamentally bounded by:

$$C = \frac{W}{BN}$$

where $W$ is the total spectrum, $B$ is the channel bandwidth, and $N$ is the cluster size. To increase capacity, $N$ must be decreased — but only while preserving a tolerable Signal-to-Interference ratio $(S/I)$.

## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Highway Problem:** Imagine a 4-lane highway choked with traffic. You cannot widen the road (no extra spectrum). What can you do?
> 1. **Cell Splitting** = Split the highway into smaller parallel streets (more lanes per km²).
> 2. **Sectoring** = Replace the wide floodlight at intersections with three narrower spotlights (directional antennas reduce interference).
> 3. **Microcells** = Add small service roads feeding into the main highway (small low-power zones).

This is exactly what cellular engineers do to "widen the wireless highway" without acquiring new spectrum — the most expensive resource in telecommunications.

## 1.3 Why Capacity Improvement is Critical

- **Spectrum scarcity:** The usable RF spectrum below 3 GHz is fully allocated; regulators cannot give more.
- **Demand growth:** Mobile data traffic grows **exponentially** (~60% YoY in 5G era).
- **Economic pressure:** Operators must serve more subscribers per MHz to remain profitable.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Reducing cluster size $N$ from 7 to 4 increases capacity by a factor of $7/4 = 1.75$, but the co-channel reuse distance $D$ decreases — increasing interference. The art of cellular design is balancing **capacity** vs. **interference** vs. **coverage**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Cell Splitting

Cell splitting is the process of subdividing a congested cell into smaller cells (**microcells**), each with its own base station (BS), and a corresponding reduction in antenna height and transmitter power.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Identify congested cells** where Grade of Service (GoS) > 2% blocking probability.
2. **Define a radius reduction factor** $n$ such that the new cell radius $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}}/n$.
3. **Install new smaller cells** within the old cell footprint. Each old cell gives rise to $n^2$ new microcells.
4. **Reduce transmit power** so that the received power at the new (smaller) cell boundary equals the original power level at the old boundary.
5. **Re-tune handoff thresholds** and antenna downtilt to avoid excessive overlap.

### 2.1.2 Key Equations

Let the path loss obey $L \propto R^n$ where $n$ is the path-loss exponent (typically 3–4 in urban areas). To maintain equal signal strength at the new cell boundary:

$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} - 20 \log_{10}(n) \quad \text{dB}$$

Number of new channels per old cell footprint:
$$M_{\text{new}} = n^2 \cdot M_{\text{old}}$$

### 2.1.3 Engineering Utility
Cell splitting is the dominant capacity-expansion technique used by Indian telecom operators (Airtel, Jio, Vi) in dense urban zones like Mumbai, Delhi, and Kochi metros — typically reducing macrocell radius from 1 km → 250 m.

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Sectoring

Sectoring replaces the single omnidirectional antenna at the BS with $N$ directional antennas, each radiating into a sector of angle $\theta = 360°/N$. Typical configurations are:
- **120° sectoring** ($N = 3$) — most common
- **60° sectoring** ($N = 6$) — used for high-capacity hotspots

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. Each directional antenna illuminates only its sector.
2. The number of **first-tier co-channel interferers** is reduced from **6 (omnidirectional)** to **2 (120° sectoring)** or **1 (60° sectoring)**.
3. The $S/I$ ratio improves, allowing **smaller cluster sizes** $N$ → hence **higher capacity**.

### 2.2.2 Signal-to-Interference (S/I) Derivation

Co-channel reuse ratio: $q_0 = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$.

| Configuration | Interferers | $S/I$ Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Omnidirectional | 6 | $\dfrac{q_0^{n}}{6}$ |
| 120° sectoring | 2 | $\dfrac{q_0^{n}}{2}$ |
| 60° sectoring | 1 | $q_0^{n}$ |

where $n$ = path loss exponent (typically 4).

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Coverage Zone / Microcell Zones

Multiple **low-power RF zones** (typically 4–6 per cell) share a single BS via a **zone-diversity combiner**. Mobile unit is served by the **nearest zone site**, dramatically reducing co-channel interference.

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

1. Each cell is partitioned into $Z$ zones, each served by a zone-site amplifier.
2. A zone selector (at the BS) picks the zone giving strongest received signal.
3. Zone radius $R_z \approx R/3$ for typical deployments.

### 2.3.2 S/I Improvement

For $Z = 3$ zones with omnidirectional zone sites:
$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{q_0^{n}}{(1 + 1/(q_0^{n} - 0.44))^2} \quad \text{(approximate for } n = 4\text{)}$$

This achieves **performance comparable to 3-sector sectoring** without requiring directional antennas.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim — appears in **90% of KTU Part B problems** on Module 3.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Cluster size (Reuse pattern) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | cells |
| $Q$ | Co-channel reuse ratio | $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Co-channel reuse distance | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | meters |
| $C$ | Capacity per cluster | $C = W/(BN)$ | channels |
| $(S/I)$ | Omnidirectional SIR | $q_0^{n}/6$ | ratio or dB |
| $(S/I)_{120}$ | 120° sector SIR | $q_0^{n}/2$ | ratio or dB |
| $(S/I)_{60}$ | 60° sector SIR | $q_0^{n}$ | ratio or dB |
| $P_{t2}$ | New transmit power after split | $P_{t1}/n^{2}$ (linear) or $P_{t1} - 20\log_{10} n$ (dB) | W or dBW |
| $M_{\text{new}}$ | New channels per old cell | $n^2 \cdot M_{\text{old}}$ | channels |
| $R_z$ | Zone radius | $R/3$ (typical) | meters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Path loss exponent $n$** for KTU problems: $n = 2$ (free space), $n = 3$ (urban), $n = 4$ (heavy urban, lossy). Default to **$n = 4$** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **5G NR networks** use cell splitting extensively (small cells of 100 m radius).
- **LTE-Advanced** uses coordinated multipoint (CoMP), which is a sophisticated descendant of the coverage zone concept.
- **Indian Railway corridors** use sectoring along linear tracks to maximize frequency reuse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — S/I Ratio for 120° Sectoring

### Problem Setup

Consider a hexagonal cellular system with omni-BS replaced by three 120° directional antennas. Determine $S/I$ for a mobile unit at the cell boundary.

### Derivation

The received signal power from serving cell BS (distance $R$):
$$S = P_t \cdot k \cdot R^{-n}$$

The first-tier co-channel interferers are now only **two** instead of six (the other four are blocked by directional pattern). Their distances are approximately $D$ and $D$ (worst case):

$$I = P_t \cdot k \cdot D^{-n} + P_t \cdot k \cdot D^{-n} = 2 P_t k D^{-n}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_t k R^{-n}}{2 P_t k D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^n = \frac{q_0^{n}}{2}$$

Substituting $q_0 = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{\text{sector}} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{2}}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

For $N = 7$, $n = 4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{\text{omni}} &= \frac{(21)^{2}}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5 \\
\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{120°} &= \frac{(21)^{2}}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5
\end{aligned}$$

In dB: $73.5 \Rightarrow 18.66$ dB; $220.5 \Rightarrow 23.43$ dB. Improvement = **+4.77 dB**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Transmit Power After Cell Splitting

### Setup
Original cell radius $R_1$, new radius $R_2 = R_1/n$. Path loss exponent $n$. Maintain equal signal strength $P_r$ at boundary.

### Derivation

Original received power:
$$P_{r1} = P_{t1} - 10 n \log_{10}(R_1) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

New received power (must equal $P_{r1}$):
$$P_{r2} = P_{t2} - 10 n \log_{10}(R_2) = P_{t2} - 10 n \log_{10}(R_1/n)$$

Equating:
$$P_{t1} - 10 n \log_{10}(R_1) = P_{t2} - 10 n \log_{10}(R_1) + 10 n \log_{10}(n)$$

Solving:
$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} - 10 n \log_{10}(n)$$

For $n = 4$:
$$\boxed{P_{t2} = P_{t1} - 40 \log_{10}(n) \quad \text{dB}}$$

### Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Style)
A cell of radius $R = 4$ km is split into cells of radius $1$ km. $P_{t1} = 40$ W, $n = 4$.

Splitting factor: $n_{\text{split}} = 4/1 = 4$.

$$P_{t2} = 40 \cdot (4)^{-2} = 40/16 = 2.5 \text{ W}$$

Check: $10 \log_{10}(16) = 12.04$ dB. So $P_{t2} = 40 - 12.04 = 27.96$ dBW → $10^{2.796} \approx 625$ W? 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion pitfall:** $40$ W = $46.02$ dBW. So $P_{t2} = 46.02 - 12.04 = 33.98$ dBW $= 2500$ mW = 2.5 W. ✓ Always convert watts to dBW first.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Capacity Gain from Cell Splitting

### Setup

Original cell with $M$ channels per cell. After splitting with factor $n$ (radius reduced by $n$), each old cell is replaced by $n^2$ new microcells, each carrying $M$ channels.

### Derivation

Original total channels in region:
$$C_{\text{old}} = M \cdot N_{\text{old cells}}$$

New total channels:
$$C_{\text{new}} = M \cdot n^2 \cdot N_{\text{old cells}}$$

Capacity enhancement factor:
$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{C_{\text{new}}}{C_{\text{old}}} = n^2}$$

For $n = 2$: $\eta = 4$ (quadruple capacity). For $n = 4$: $\eta = 16$.

### Numerical Example
A 10-km² region has 4 macrocells of radius 1 km. Split each into microcells of radius 500 m.

- Old: 4 cells, 4M channels.
- New: $4 \times 4 = 16$ cells, 16M channels.
- Gain = **4×**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Cell Splitting | Sectoring | Microcell Zones |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware change | New BS needed | New antennas only | Zone amplifiers |
| Capital cost | **High** | Medium | Low |
| Capacity gain | $n^2$ | 3× (120°), 6× (60°) | ~3× |
| Handoff frequency | **Increases sharply** | Increases slightly | Minimal |
| Interference reduction | Indirect | **Direct** | Direct |
| Trunking efficiency | May decrease | Preserved | Preserved |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Which method increases capacity without increasing handoffs?"* → **Coverage zone (microcell zones)** is the answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cell Splitting — Before vs. After (Topological View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE SPLITTING (Radius R)"]
        BS1["BS-OLD\nR = 1 km\nP_t = 40 W"]

    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER SPLITTING (Radius R/n)"]
        BS2["BS-NEW-1\nR = 0.5 km\nP_t = 10 W"]
        BS3["BS-NEW-2\nR = 0.5 km\nP_t = 10 W"]
        BS4["BS-NEW-3\nR = 0.5 km\nP_t = 10 W"]
        BS5["BS-NEW-4\nR = 0.5 km\nP_t = 10 W"]
        BS2 --- MC1["Microcell-1\nChannel Group A"]
        BS3 --- MC2["Microcell-2\nChannel Group B"]
        BS4 --- MC3["Microcell-3\nChannel Group C"]
        BS5 --- MC4["Microcell-4\nChannel Group D"]
    end

    BEFORE -. SPLIT INTO .-> AFTER

    style BS1 fill:#ffcccc
    style BS2 fill:#ccffcc
    style BS3 fill:#ccffcc
    style BS4 fill:#ccffcc
    style BS5 fill:#ccffcc
```

---

## 4.2 Sectoring — Interference Reduction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OMNI["OMNI CONFIGURATION"]
        OBS["BS with\nOmni Antenna"]
        I1["Interferer-1"]
        I2["Interferer-2"]
        I3["Interferer-3"]
        I4["Interferer-4"]
        I5["Interferer-5"]
        I6["Interferer-6"]
        OBS ==> I1
        OBS ==> I2
        OBS ==> I3
        OBS ==> I4
        OBS ==> I5
        OBS ==> I6
    end

    subgraph SECTOR["120 DEG SECTORING"]
        SBS["BS with\n3 Directional\nAntennas"]
        IB1["Interferer-A\nBLOCKED"]
        IB2["Interferer-B\nACTIVE"]
        IB3["Interferer-C\nACTIVE"]
        IB4["Interferer-D\nBLOCKED"]
        IB5["Interferer-E\nBLOCKED"]
        IB6["Interferer-F\nBLOCKED"]
        SBS --> IB2
        SBS --> IB3
    end

    style OBS fill:#ffaaaa
    style SBS fill:#aaffaa
    style IB1 fill:#cccccc
    style IB4 fill:#cccccc
    style IB5 fill:#cccccc
    style IB6 fill:#cccccc

    style IB3 fill:#ffffaa
```

---

## 4.3 Coverage Zone Approach — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MOBILE["MOBILE UNIT"] -->|"RF Signal"| ZS1["Zone Site-1\n(Amplifier)"]
    MOBILE -->|"RF Signal"| ZS2["Zone Site-2\n(Amplifier)"]
    MOBILE -->|"RF Signal"| ZS3["Zone Site-3\n(Amplifier)"]

    ZS1 -->|"Coax/Fiber"| COMBINER["Zone Selector\n+ Diversity Combiner"]
    ZS2 -->|"Coax/Fiber"| COMBINER
    ZS3 -->|"Coax/Fiber"| COMBINER

    COMBINER -->|"Strongest Zone Selected"| BS["CENTRAL BS\n(Single BS)"]
    BS -->|"Backhaul"| MSC["Mobile Switching\nCenter"]

    style MOBILE fill:#cceeff
    style BS fill:#ffcc99
    style MSC fill:#ff99cc
    style COMBINER fill:#99ff99
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Only **one** zone site serves the mobile at any time. The selector picks the strongest received signal — this is what gives microcells their interference advantage.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Flow (When to Use What?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need More Capacity?"] --> Q1{"Congested\nregion size?"}
    Q1 -->|"Localized hotspot"| SPLIT["CELL SPLITTING\n(Deploy small cells)"]
    Q1 -->|"Whole sector busy"| Q2{"Budget\navailable?"}
    Q2 -->|"High"| SPLIT
    Q2 -->|"Medium"| SEC["SECTORING\n(120 or 60 deg)"]
    Q2 -->|"Low"| ZONE["COVERAGE ZONES\n(Microcell zones)"]

    SPLIT --> OUT1["Capacity ↑↑↑\nHandoffs ↑↑↑\nCost ↑↑↑"]
    SEC --> OUT2["Capacity ↑↑\nHandoffs ↑\nCost ↑"]
    ZONE --> OUT3["Capacity ↑↑\nHandoffs ≈ same\nCost ≈ same"]

    style START fill:#ffff99
    style SPLIT fill:#ffaaaa
    style SEC fill:#aaffaa
    style ZONE fill:#aaaaff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define cell splitting. State one advantage and one disadvantage.**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Cell splitting** is the process of subdividing a congested cell into smaller cells (microcells), each with its own base station, accompanied by reduction in antenna height and transmitter power.
> - **Advantage:** Capacity increases by factor $n^2$ (where $n$ is the radius reduction factor).
> - **Disadvantage:** Increased number of handoffs due to smaller cell coverage.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare omnidirectional and 120° sectoring in terms of first-tier interferers.**

> **Model Answer:**
> | Parameter | Omnidirectional | 120° Sectoring |
> |---|---|---|
> | First-tier interferers | 6 | 2 |
> | $S/I$ ratio (n=4) | $q_0^4/6$ | $q_0^4/2$ |
> | Capacity gain | 1× | ~3× |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **cell splitting** technique in cellular systems with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the new transmit power and the original transmit power when a cell of radius $R$ is split into $n$ smaller cells of radius $R/n$. Assume path loss exponent $\gamma = 4$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cellular system uses cluster size $N = 7$, cell radius $R = 5$ km, and path loss exponent $n = 4$. Calculate:
1. Co-channel reuse distance $D$.
2. $S/I$ ratio using omnidirectional antennas (in dB).
3. $S/I$ ratio using 120° sectoring (in dB).
4. Comment on the capacity improvement.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Cell Splitting Derivation

> **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
> Cell splitting involves subdividing a congested cell into smaller cells. Each smaller cell has its own BS with reduced antenna height and reduced transmit power. The number of channels per unit area increases proportionally to $n^2$ where $n$ is the radius splitting factor.

> **Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.

> **Derivation (2 marks):**
>
> Signal at old boundary: $P_{r1} = P_{t1} - 40 \log_{10}(R_1)$
> Signal at new boundary: $P_{r2} = P_{t2} - 40 \log_{10}(R_2)$
> Equating $P_{r1} = P_{r2}$:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> P_{t1} - 40 \log_{10}(R_1) &= P_{t2} - 40 \log_{10}(R_1/n) \\
> P_{t2} &= P_{t1} - 40 \log_{10}(n) \quad \text{(dB)}
> \end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations

**Step 1:** Co-channel reuse ratio:
$$q_0 = D/R = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583$$

**Step 2:** Reuse distance:
$$D = R \sqrt{3N} = 5 \times 4.583 = 22.91 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3:** Omnidirectional $S/I$:
$$\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{\text{omni}} = \frac{q_0^{4}}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^{4}}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$
$$\Rightarrow 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4:** 120° Sector $S/I$:
$$\frac{S}{I}\bigg|_{120°} = \frac{q_0^{4}}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5$$
$$\Rightarrow 10 \log_{10}(220.5) = 23.43 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5:** Improvement:
$$\Delta(S/I) = 23.43 - 18.66 = 4.77 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6:** Capacity Comment:
Since 120° sectoring yields higher $S/I$, the cluster size $N$ can be reduced (e.g., from 7 to 4), increasing capacity by $7/4 = 1.75$ times. Combined with sectoring gain of ~3×, the **net capacity improvement ≈ 5.25×**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **coverage zone (microcell zone) approach** for improving cellular capacity. Compare it with sectoring.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cell of radius 8 km is split into cells of radius 2 km. If original transmit power was 50 W, find:
1. The splitting factor $n$.
2. The new transmit power (in W and dBW).
3. The capacity improvement factor.
4. Justify the change in antenna height required.

Path loss exponent $n = 4$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Coverage Zone Approach (7 marks)

> **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram.
>
> **Explanation (4 marks):**
> 1. The coverage zone approach divides a cell into 3 (or more) **zones**, each served by a low-power zone site amplifier.
> 2. A **zone selector** at the central BS picks the zone with strongest received signal from the mobile.
> 3. The mobile is served by the **nearest zone site**, drastically reducing both transmit power and co-channel interference.
> 4. Handoffs are minimized because the **central BS remains the same**; only the active zone changes.
>
> **Comparison (1 mark):**
> | Aspect | Sectoring | Coverage Zones |
> |---|---|---|
> | Antenna type | Directional | Zone amplifiers + central BS |
> | Handoff increase | Yes | Negligible |
> | Trunking efficiency | Preserved | Preserved |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations

**Step 1:** Splitting factor:
$$n = R_{\text{old}}/R_{\text{new}} = 8/2 = 4$$

**Step 2:** New transmit power (linear):
$$P_{t2} = P_{t1}/n^{2} = 50/16 = 3.125 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to dBW:
$$P_{t1} = 10 \log_{10}(50) = 16.99 \text{ dBW}$$
$$P_{t2} = 16.99 - 40 \log_{10}(4) = 16.99 - 24.08 = -7.09 \text{ dBW}$$
Check: $10 \log_{10}(3.125) = 4.95$ dBW. 

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistake:** Students often forget that $-7.09$ dBW refers to a value less than 1 W. Many mark incorrectly. The correct chain: $50 \text{ W} \to 16.99$ dBW → subtract $12.04$ dB (for $n=4$ since $40 \log_{10}(4) = 24.08$ dB but here we use $40\log_{10}(4)/2 = 12.04$ dB for power reduction $1/n^2$) → $4.95$ dBW $= 3.125$ W. ✓

**Step 4:** Capacity improvement factor:
$$\eta = n^2 = 16$$

**Step 5:** Antenna height justification:
Original antenna height $h_1$ for radius $R_1 = 8$ km. New antenna height scales with cell radius:
$$h_2 = h_1 \times (R_2/R_1) = h_1/4$$
Reduced antenna height ensures **line-of-sight (LoS) coverage** matches the smaller cell boundary — avoiding overshoot into neighbouring cells.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing dBm, dBW, and linear watts in power calculations. Always convert to a consistent unit first.
> 2. **Path loss exponent misuse:** Using $n = 2$ (free space) when problem specifies urban ($n = 3$ or $4$).
> 3. **Cluster size formula error:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, not $N = 3K$ for arbitrary $K$.
> 4. **Interferer count:** Forgetting that 120° sectoring reduces interferers from 6 to **2**, not 3.
> 5. **Forgetting $n^2$ factor:** Capacity from splitting is $n^2$, not $2n$ or $n$ alone.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Exam:**

- ✅ Cell splitting increases capacity by **factor $n^2$** (where $n$ = radius reduction ratio).
- ✅ New transmit power: $P_{t2} = P_{t1}/n^{n_{\text{path}}}$ (linear) → reduce by $10 n_{\text{path}} \log_{10}(n)$ dB.
- ✅ Antenna height also reduces proportionally: $h_2 = h_1/n$.
- ✅ 120° sectoring: 6 → 2 interferers; $S/I$ improves by factor of **3** (linear) = **4.77 dB**.
- ✅ 60° sectoring: 6 → 1 interferer; $S/I$ improves by factor of **6** (linear) = **7.78 dB**.
- ✅ Coverage zones use a **single BS with multiple low-power zone amplifiers** — minimizes handoffs.
- ✅ Co-channel reuse ratio: $q_0 = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ — memorize this.
- ✅ $S/I$ for omnidirectional: $q_0^{n}/6$; for 120°: $q_0^{n}/2$; for 60°: $q_0^{n}$.
- ✅ Cell splitting = highest capacity gain, highest handoff rate, highest cost.
- ✅ Sectoring = moderate gain, no new BS required.
- ✅ Coverage zones = best trunking + lowest handoff penalty.
- ✅ Default path loss exponent in KTU problems = **$n = 4$** unless stated.
- ✅ Always convert W → dBW **before** subtracting dB values (chain rule).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"S-S-Z"** → **Splitting** (biggest gain), **Sectoring** (interference fix), **Zones** (handoff saver). Order them as: capacity-first → interference-second → handoff-third.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Modern Wireless Communication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Modern Wireless Communication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Wireless Communication System** is defined as a telecommunication network that uses electromagnetic waves (radio frequency, microwave, or millimeter-wave carriers) propagating through free space (the wireless channel) to transfer information between two or more points without the aid of physical wired conductors. The term *modern* specifically denotes systems designed post-1990s that employ **digital signal processing**, **packet-switched architectures**, and **adaptive radio resource management**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEBT751 – Communication Techniques), this topic serves as the capstone introduction to contemporary wireless systems that underpin **2G/3G/4G/5G cellular networks**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15)**, **satellite communications**, and the emerging **Internet of Things (IoT)** ecosystem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air-Mail" Postman

Imagine a postman who delivers letters in a busy town. Instead of walking door-to-door through fixed streets (wires), he stands at a central square and **shouts** the message. Different postmen use different **languages**, different **time slots**, or different **voice pitches** so their messages don't collide. The town's geometry, building density, and crowd noise determine how far his voice carries.

- **The postman** = transmitter (Base Station / Mobile Station)
- **The shouted voice** = electromagnetic wave traveling at the speed of light ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- **The listener** = receiver
- **The crowd noise** = thermal noise, interference, multipath fading
- **The town's geometry** = the wireless channel (path loss, shadowing)

## 1.3 Salient Features of Modern Systems

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Carrier Frequency Range | **700 MHz to 100 GHz** (including mmWave bands for 5G FR2) |
| Channel Bandwidth (B) | **200 kHz (GSM)** to **400 MHz (5G mmWave carrier aggregation)** |
| Propagation Speed | **Speed of Light** $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in free space |
| Typical Latency (5G URLLC) | **< 1 ms** |
| Peak Spectral Efficiency | **\approx 30 bps/Hz** (5G downlink, theoretical) |

## 1.4 Why "Modern"? — Evolution from Analog to Cognitive

The word *modern* implies three fundamental paradigm shifts:

1. **Digitization** — replacing analog FM/FDM modulation with **digital modulation** (BPSK, QPSK, QAM, OFDM).
2. **Intelligence at the Edge** — adaptive modulation, link adaptation, MIMO beamforming.
3. **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** — radio parameters controlled by firmware rather than fixed hardware, paving the way for **Cognitive Radio (CR)** and **network slicing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module emphasizes the **architectural transition from circuit-switched 1G/2G to packet-switched 4G/5G**, the role of **multiple access schemes** (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, OFDMA), and the **physical-layer challenges** unique to wireless channels (fading, interference, capacity limits).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generational Evolution of Wireless Systems

The journey from **1G to 5G** is not merely a speed upgrade; each generation introduced a fundamentally new air-interface technology.

| Generation | Era | Multiple Access | Modulation | Switching | Peak Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1G (AMPS, TACS)** | 1980s | FDMA | Analog FM | Circuit | 2.4 kbps |
| **2G (GSM)** | 1990s | TDMA/FDMA | GMSK | Circuit | 64 kbps |
| **2.5G (GPRS)** | 2000 | TDMA | GMSK/8-PSK | Packet | 171 kbps |
| **3G (UMTS, CDMA2000)** | 2001 | **CDMA** (WCDMA) | QPSK, 16-QAM | Packet + Circuit | 2 Mbps |
| **3.5G (HSPA+)** | 2008 | CDMA | 64-QAM | Packet | 42 Mbps |
| **4G (LTE / LTE-A)** | 2010 | **OFDMA / SC-FDMA** | Up to 256-QAM | All-IP Packet | 1 Gbps (theoretical) |
| **5G (NR)** | 2020 | **Flexible OFDMA** + **NOMA** (research) | Up to 256-QAM, LDPC/Polar | All-IP + Slicing | 20 Gbps (mmWave) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Leap:** Each generation introduced a *new multiple access scheme*. FDMA allocates frequency, TDMA allocates time, CDMA allocates unique codes, and OFDMA allocates both time-frequency **resource blocks**. Understanding this progression is critical for KTU 14-mark answers on "Compare FDMA/TDMA/CDMA/OFDMA."

## 2.2 The Wireless Channel — The Heart of the Problem

Unlike a wireline channel (deterministic and static), the wireless channel is **random, time-varying, and bandwidth-limited**. Three dominant impairments must be modeled:

### (a) Path Loss (Large-Scale Fading)
Signal power decays with distance. The **Friis Free-Space Equation** quantifies this:

$$
P_r(d) = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

where $P_t$ is transmit power, $G_t, G_r$ are antenna gains, $\lambda$ is wavelength, and $d$ is distance.

The **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** in dB is:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

(applicable when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**).

### (b) Shadowing (Large-Scale, Slow)
Obstacles (buildings, trees) cause **log-normal** attenuation around the mean path loss. Standard deviation typically $\sigma = 6$ to $10$ dB.

### (c) Multipath Fading (Small-Scale, Fast)
Multiple reflections arrive at the receiver with different phases, causing constructive/destructive interference. Modeled by **Rayleigh** (no line-of-sight) or **Rician** (with LOS) distributions.

## 2.3 The Shannon Capacity Theorem — The Fundamental Limit

The **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** defines the absolute theoretical maximum data rate over a band-limited, noisy channel:

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

where $B$ is bandwidth in Hz, $S$ is signal power, $N$ is noise power. The **Spectral Efficiency** is:

$$
\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second/Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Shannon's theorem sets the *ceiling*; real systems like 4G/5G use **adaptive modulation and coding** to push spectral efficiency close to $\log_2(1+S/N)$ by adjusting the modulation order (QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM → 256-QAM) based on instantaneous channel quality (CQI feedback).

## 2.4 Multiple Access — Sharing the Wireless Cake

| Technique | Resource Domain | Domain Allocated Per User | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency | Unique frequency band | 1G AMPS |
| **TDMA** | Time | Unique time slot within a frame | 2G GSM |
| **CDMA** | Code | Unique orthogonal/spreading code | 3G UMTS |
| **OFDMA** | Time + Frequency | Unique time-frequency **Resource Block (RB)** | 4G LTE, 5G NR |
| **SDMA / NOMA** | Space / Power | Unique spatial beam / power level | 5G Advanced, 6G research |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examiner's Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ | Friis received power | Watts |
| 2 | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss | dB (km, MHz) |
| 3 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bps |
| 4 | $\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Spectral efficiency | bps/Hz |
| 5 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | Watts |
| 6 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength–frequency relation | meters |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | dB |
| 8 | $\text{Delay Spread} \approx T_m$ | Determines coherence bandwidth $B_c \approx 1/T_m$ | seconds / Hz |
| 9 | $\text{Doppler Spread} = f_d = v \cos\theta / \lambda$ | Determines coherence time $T_c \approx 1/f_d$ | Hz / seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Cellular Capacity} = M \cdot N_{\text{ch}} \cdot (B_{\text{ch}} / \text{cluster size } K)$ | With $M$ cells and frequency reuse | Erlangs / bps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Note for Tables:** Throughout these notes, absolute values use the LaTeX `\vert` command (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of the literal pipe `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Transmission Equation (Free-Space Path Loss)

**Starting Premise:** A transmit antenna radiates isotropically. The radiated power density at distance $d$ is the total radiated power $P_t$ spread over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$.

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \left[\text{Watts/m}^2\right]
$$

**Step 1:** An isotropic radiator gives a gain $G_t = 1$. A practical transmit antenna with gain $G_t$ focuses this energy:

$$
S_{\text{eff}} = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2:** The effective aperture (collecting area) of a receive antenna is given by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 3:** The power collected by the receive antenna is the product of effective power density and effective aperture:

$$
P_r = S_{\text{eff}} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4 (Final Friis Equation):**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Converting to FSPL in dB:**

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = \frac{G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = c / f$ and using $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, after algebraic simplification with $d$ in **kilometers** and $f$ in **MHz**, the gain terms are normalized to $G_t = G_r = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Friis Equation

**Problem:** A base station transmits $P_t = 10$ W at $f = 900$ MHz with $G_t = 12$ dBi. The mobile receiver has $G_r = 0$ dBi. Find the received power at $d = 5$ km (assume free-space).

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6} = 0.333 \text{ m}
$$

Compute the free-space path loss:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(900) + 32.44
$$

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20(0.6990) + 20(2.9542) + 32.44 = 13.98 + 59.08 + 32.44 = 105.5 \text{ dB}
$$

Convert powers to dBm for clarity:

$$
P_t \text{ (dBm)} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(10^4) = 40 \text{ dBm}
$$

$$
P_r \text{ (dBm)} = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} = 40 + 12 + 0 - 105.5 = -53.5 \text{ dBm}
$$

Converting back to Watts:

$$
P_r = 10^{(-53.5/10)} \text{ mW} = 10^{-5.35} \text{ mW} \approx 4.47 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mW} \approx 4.47 \text{ nW}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Shannon Spectral Efficiency Limit

**Step 1:** A bandlimited AWGN channel has bandwidth $B$ Hz and additive Gaussian noise of power spectral density $N_0/2$ W/Hz. The total noise power is $N = N_0 B$.

**Step 2:** Shannon's 1948 paper proves that the maximum error-free data rate is:

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N_0 B}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Spectral efficiency, defined as $\eta = C / B$, gives:

$$
\eta = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Behavior:**

- For low SNR ($S/N \to 0$): $\eta \approx (S/N) / \ln 2 \to 0$ — capacity is power-limited.
- For high SNR ($S/N \to \infty$): $\eta \approx \log_2(S/N)$ — capacity is bandwidth-limited but grows logarithmically.

**Step 5 — Practical Gap:** A real 5G system at 64-QAM with a coding rate of 0.93 achieves $\eta \approx 4.5$ bps/Hz. Shannon's bound at SNR = 20 dB gives $\eta = 6.66$ bps/Hz, meaning the system operates at $\approx 67\%$ of the Shannon limit. This gap motivates research in **LDPC codes** and **polar codes**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Multiple Access Schemes

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | OFDMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domain | Frequency | Time | Code | Time-Frequency |
| Bandwidth Use | Narrow per user | Shared wideband | Spread spectrum | Subcarriers + slots |
| Synchronization | Required (carrier) | Required (slot) | Tight (chip-level) | Tight (sample-level) |
| Multipath Robustness | Poor | Poor | Excellent (RAKE) | Excellent (CP absorbs ISI) |
| Spectral Efficiency | Low | Medium | Medium | **Highest** |
| Peak System | 1G Analog | 2G GSM | 3G UMTS | 4G LTE / 5G NR |
| Hardware Cost | High filters | Medium | High (linear amplifiers) | Low (FFT-based) |

## 3.5 Link Budget Calculation Framework

A complete **link budget** evaluates whether a wireless link is feasible. The standard procedure is:

$$
P_r \text{ (dBm)} = P_t + G_t - L_t + G_r - L_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{shadow}} - L_{\text{fade margin}}
$$

where $L_t, L_r$ are feeder/cable losses and $L_{\text{shadow}}, L_{\text{fade margin}}$ are shadowing/fading margins (typically 8–15 dB each).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Modern Wireless Communication System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical **end-to-end architecture** of a digital wireless transceiver, mapping the signal flow from source bits through the wireless channel to the destination bits.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Source Bits] --> encA[Channel Encoder LDPC or Turbo]
    encA --> intlA[Interleaver]
    intlA --> modA[Digital Modulator QPSK to 256 QAM]
    modA --> ofdmA[OFDM Modulator IFFT and Cyclic Prefix]
    ofdmA --> dacA[DAC and RF Upconversion]
    dacA --> ampA[Power Amplifier]
    ampA --> antA[Transmit Antenna]
    antA -. Wireless Channel Multipath Noise Fading .-> antB[Receive Antenna]
    antB --> lnaB[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lnaB --> adcB[ADC and RF Downconversion]
    adcB --> ofdmB[OFDM Demodulator FFT and CP Removal]
    ofdmB --> chaB[Channel Estimation and Equalizer]
    chaB --> modB[Digital Demodulator]
    modB --> deintB[Deinterleaver]
    deintB --> decB[Channel Decoder LDPC Decoder]
    decB --> dst1[Destination Bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left-hand chain (Encoder → Interleaver → Modulator → OFDM → DAC → PA → Antenna) is the **transmitter**. The right-hand chain (Antenna → LNA → ADC → OFDM Demod → Equalizer → Demodulator → Deinterleaver → Decoder) is the **receiver**. The dotted line between the two antennas represents the **wireless channel** with multipath, noise, and fading impairments.

## 4.2 Generational Evolution Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    g1[1G AMPS - 1980s - Analog FM - FDMA] --> g2[2G GSM - 1990s - Digital TDMA - GMSK]
    g2 --> g25[2.5G GPRS EDGE]
    g25 --> g3[3G UMTS - 2001 - WCDMA - QPSK]
    g3 --> g35[3.5G HSPA+ - 64 QAM]
    g35 --> g4[4G LTE - 2010 - OFDMA SC FDMA - MIMO]
    g4 --> g5[5G NR - 2020 - mmWave Massive MIMO - Network Slicing]
    g5 --> g6[6G Research - AI Native THz ISAC]
```

## 4.3 Cellular Frequency Reuse Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cellA[Cluster Cell A Freq Set 1]
    cellB[Cluster Cell B Freq Set 2]
    cellC[Cluster Cell C Freq Set 3]
    cellD[Cluster Cell D Freq Set 4]
    cellE[Cluster Cell E Freq Set 5]
    cellF[Cluster Cell D Prime Same as D Reuse Pattern]
    cellA --- cellB
    cellB --- cellC
    cellC --- cellD
    cellD --- cellE
    cellE --- cellF
    cellF --- cellA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** A physical cellular layout would use hexagonal cells, but the Mermaid environment best renders this as a logical reuse cluster. The label `cellD` and `cellF` deliberately use distinct alphanumeric IDs per the safety protocol. The **frequency reuse factor** $K = N / M$ (where $N$ = total cells in cluster, $M$ = number of cells per cluster) governs co-channel interference and thus system capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL). Write its expression when the distance is in kilometers and frequency in MHz, assuming unity-gain antennas.**

**Model Answer:**
Free-space path loss is the attenuation experienced by a radio signal as it propagates through an unobstructed line-of-sight path in vacuum/air, due to the spherical spreading of electromagnetic waves. With unity-gain antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$), $d$ in km, and $f$ in MHz:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44
$$

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for formula)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA with respect to the resource domain shared and one disadvantage each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Scheme | Resource | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| FDMA | Frequency band | Guard bands reduce spectral efficiency |
| TDMA | Time slot | Requires tight synchronization |
| CDMA | Spreading code | Near-far problem; requires power control |

**(3 marks: 1 per scheme with disadvantage)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The choice is offered **within the same module** (here Module 4). Below are two completely independent alternative 14-mark questions.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Shannon-Hartley theorem. Discuss the implications on the design of modern wireless communication systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Theorem:** The Shannon-Hartley theorem gives the maximum achievable error-free data rate (channel capacity) over a band-limited channel corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise:

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Terms:**
- $C$ = channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless)

**Step 3 — Derivation of Spectral Efficiency:** Dividing both sides by $B$:

$$
\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second/Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Implications for System Design:**

1. **Power vs. Bandwidth Trade-off:** Doubling $B$ doubles $C$ linearly, whereas doubling $S/N$ increases $C$ only logarithmically. Therefore, **spectrum is the scarce resource** and modern systems maximize bandwidth efficiency.
2. **Adaptive Modulation:** Systems choose modulation order dynamically. At high SNR, 64-QAM or 256-QAM is used; at low SNR, QPSK or BPSK is selected to stay near Shannon's limit.
3. **Practical Capacity vs. Shannon Limit:** Real systems like LTE use LDPC/turbo codes to approach Shannon capacity within 1–2 dB SNR gap (the so-called "turbo cliff").
4. **MIMO Boost:** Using $M$ transmit and $N$ receive antennas, capacity scales approximately as:

$$
C_{\text{MIMO}} \approx \min(M, N) \cdot B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

   This is why 4G/5G deploy MIMO and **massive MIMO**.

**(7 marks: 2 theorem + 2 derivation + 3 design implications)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Friis transmission equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Power Density on a Sphere:** An isotropic radiator of power $P_t$ spreads energy uniformly over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$:

$$
S_{\text{iso}} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Directional Gain:** A real transmit antenna focuses energy, multiplying the density by $G_t$:

$$
S_{\text{eff}} = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective Aperture of Receiver:** The receive antenna's collecting area is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Received Power:**

$$
P_r = S_{\text{eff}} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

This is the Friis transmission equation.

**Step 5 — Term Meanings:**
- $P_t$: Transmitted power
- $P_r$: Received power
- $G_t$: Transmit antenna gain (linear)
- $G_r$: Receive antenna gain (linear)
- $\lambda$: Wavelength = $c/f$
- $d$: T-R separation distance
- $(4\pi)^2$: Geometric factor for isotropic antennas in free space

**Step 6 — Inverse Square Law:** Note $P_r \propto 1/d^2$, meaning every doubling of distance reduces received power by 6 dB (a "6-dB per octave" rule).

**(7 marks: 2 derivation + 2 term meaning + 1 inverse-square law + 2 practical context)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the cellular concept. How does frequency reuse improve system capacity? Derive the relationship between cluster size and co-channel interference.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Cellular Concept:** Cellular communication replaces a single high-power transmitter (covering a large area) with **many low-power transmitters (cells)**, each covering a small geographical area. The same set of frequencies can be **reused** in non-adjacent cells, multiplying system capacity without increasing bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Frequency Reuse:** A *cluster* of $N$ cells uses $N$ unique frequency groups. After $K$ cells, the pattern repeats. The **frequency reuse factor** is:

$$
\text{FRF} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

where $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for integers $i, j \geq 1$ (classic KTU problem).

**Step 3 — Common Cluster Sizes:**

| Shift parameters $(i, j)$ | Cluster size $N$ |
|---|---|
| $(1, 0)$ | 3 |
| $(1, 1)$ | 3 |
| $(2, 0)$ | 4 |
| $(2, 1)$ | 7 |
| $(3, 0)$ | 9 |

**Step 4 — Co-channel Reuse Distance:** The distance $D$ between the centers of two co-channel cells is:

$$
D = R \sqrt{3N}
$$

where $R$ is the cell radius.

**Step 5 — Co-channel Interference Ratio:** With six nearest co-channel interferers at distance $D$, the **C/I ratio** is:

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}
$$

For $n = 4$ (typical path-loss exponent in urban environments):

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3N)^2}{6} = \frac{9N^2}{6} = 1.5 N^2
$$

**Step 6 — Capacity:** Total system capacity scales as:

$$
C_{\text{system}} \propto \frac{M \cdot N_{\text{ch}}}{N}
$$

Lower $N$ → more reuse → higher capacity, but lower $C/I$ → more interference. This is the central **capacity-vs-quality trade-off** in cellular engineering.

**(7 marks: 2 cellular concept + 2 reuse ratio + 3 C/I derivation)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare OFDMA and CDMA. Why has OFDMA replaced CDMA in 4G and 5G systems?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Underlying Philosophy:**
- **CDMA (3G UMTS):** All users share the **entire bandwidth simultaneously**, separated by unique orthogonal/quasi-orthogonal spreading codes.
- **OFDMA (4G LTE, 5G NR):** The bandwidth is divided into many orthogonal **subcarriers**, and each user is assigned a *subset of subcarriers* in a given time slot, called a **Resource Block (RB)**.

**Step 2 — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | CDMA | OFDMA |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth Use | Wideband, shared | Subcarrier-based |
| Equalization | Complex (RAKE receiver) | Simple (one-tap per subcarrier) |
| Multipath Robustness | RAKE + multi-finger | Cyclic Prefix absorbs ISI |
| Spectral Efficiency | Low–Medium | High |
| Frequency-Selective Fading Handling | Difficult | Per-subcarrier adaptation |
| Cell-edge Performance | Poor (near-far) | Better (frequency reuse) |
| Peak System | UMTS, CDMA2000 | LTE, 5G NR |

**Step 3 — Reasons OFDMA Replaced CDMA:**

1. **Frequency-Selective Fading Mitigation:** OFDMA converts a frequency-selective channel into many flat-fading channels, making equalization trivial.
2. **Higher Spectral Efficiency:** With no spreading overhead, OFDMA can pack more users per Hz.
3. **Flexible Resource Allocation:** Subcarriers can be assigned to users *dynamically* based on instantaneous channel quality (channel-aware scheduling).
4. **MIMO Compatibility:** OFDMA pairs naturally with MIMO spatial multiplexing.
5. **Low PAPR for Uplink:** LTE uplink uses **SC-FDMA** (a single-carrier variant) to reduce peak-to-average power ratio, solving the high-PAPR issue of pure OFDMA.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** OFDMA's combination of *frequency diversity, scheduling flexibility, and MIMO compatibility* made it the de-facto choice for 4G and 5G, while CDMA has been phased out.

**(7 marks: 2 comparison philosophy + 2 table comparison + 3 reasons for OFDMA adoption)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Wireless Communication Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in FSPL:** Always specify whether $d$ is in km or m, and $f$ in MHz or Hz. The constant **32.44** is ONLY valid for km and MHz. Using meters and Hz gives a constant of **$-27.55$**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit ambiguity.
> 2. **Shannon vs. Nyquist Mix-up:** Nyquist's formula ($C = 2B\log_2 M$) is for **noiseless channels** with $M$-level signaling. Shannon's formula handles **noisy channels**. Do not interchange.
> 3. **Cluster Size Formula:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ is correct ONLY when $i, j$ are non-negative integers with $i \geq j$. Forgetting this constraint costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **OFDM "O" — Orthogonal:** Many students incorrectly state that OFDM uses "orthogonal codes". It uses **orthogonal subcarriers**, not codes.
> 5. **MIMO Capacity:** Forgetting that $C_{\text{MIMO}}$ scales with $\min(M, N)$, not $(M + N)$.
> 6. **Missing the Friis assumption:** Friis equation assumes **far-field, free-space, LOS** conditions. Mentioning this assumption explicitly gains 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

- [x] **Modern wireless systems** are *digital, packet-switched, adaptive, and software-defined*.
- [x] **Generations:** 1G (Analog FM, FDMA) → 2G (GSM, TDMA) → 3G (UMTS, CDMA) → 4G (LTE, OFDMA) → 5G (NR, mmWave, Massive MIMO).
- [x] **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi d)^2$; remember the inverse-square law and the constant **32.44** for km/MHz.
- [x] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — sets the theoretical ceiling; real systems approach it via adaptive modulation + LDPC coding.
- [x] **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = C/B$ in bps/Hz; 5G target is $\approx 30$ bps/Hz.
- [x] **Multiple access:** FDMA (frequency), TDMA (time), CDMA (code), OFDMA (time-frequency).
- [x] **Cellular cluster size:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ with $i \geq j \geq 0$. Smaller $N$ → higher capacity but more co-channel interference.
- [x] **Co-channel reuse distance:** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.
- [x] **C/I for $n = 4$:** $\dfrac{C}{I} = 1.5 N^2$ — minimum required is usually **18 dB** for voice quality.
- [x] **OFDM's key feature:** Orthogonal subcarriers with a Cyclic Prefix that absorbs multipath ISI, enabling simple one-tap equalization.
- [x] **5G key technologies:** mmWave (24–52 GHz), Massive MIMO (64–256 antennas), Network Slicing, URLLC (< 1 ms latency), eMBB (20 Gbps peak).
- [x] **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — used in $\lambda = c/f$ conversions.
- [x] **Modern systems use OFDMA, not CDMA,** because of superior frequency-selective fading handling, simpler equalization, MIMO compatibility, and dynamic scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
