topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Local Area Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a flexible data communication system that uses **radio frequency (RF)** or **infrared (IR)** waves as the physical transmission medium to provide network connectivity within a limited geographical area (typically up to **100 metres** for indoor deployments). WLANs are formally standardized under the **IEEE 802.11 family** of protocols, which define the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers of the OSI model, enabling devices known as *Stations (STA)* to communicate without physical cabling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The focus of Module 4 is the **IEEE 802.11 architecture**, the **CSMA/CA medium access protocol**, the **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** and **Point Coordination Function (PCF)**, the **WLAN frame structure**, and an introductory treatment of **WLAN security mechanisms (WEP, WPA, WPA2)**.

The formal architecture of an IEEE 802.11 network is built on the following fundamental building blocks:

- **Station (STA):** Any device equipped with a wireless network interface card (NIC), e.g., a laptop, smartphone, or IoT sensor.
- **Basic Service Set (BSS):** The fundamental building block, comprising a set of stations controlled by a single *coordination function*. The coverage area is called the *Basic Service Area (BSA)*, analogous to a "cell" in cellular telephony.
- **Access Point (AP):** A special STA that acts as a bridge between the wireless BSS and the underlying Distribution System (DS), usually an Ethernet LAN.
- **Distribution System (DS):** The logical backbone that interconnects multiple BSSs to form an Extended Service Set (ESS).
- **Extended Service Set (ESS):** A collection of one or more BSSs interconnected by a DS, appearing as a single logical network to higher layers.
- **Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS):** An *ad-hoc* network with no AP, where stations communicate peer-to-peer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core OSI Mapping:** IEEE 802.11 specifies only the **bottom two layers** — Layer 1 (Physical) and the lower half of Layer 2 (MAC). The upper MAC functions (LLC) are inherited from the **IEEE 802.2** standard, ensuring interoperability with other 802.x LANs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, open-plan office where people need to talk to one another, but the office has no telephone lines and no cubicles. To communicate, they must simply **speak out loud using a common shared "air space."**

- The "**air space**" is the **shared wireless medium** (the radio channel).
- The "**rule that only one person speaks at a time**" is the **MAC protocol (CSMA/CA)**.
- The "**moderator**" who grants speaking rights is the **Access Point** in infrastructure mode, or simply *good manners* (random backoff) in ad-hoc mode.
- The "**corridors connecting different office floors**" are the **Distribution System (DS)** that ties multiple "rooms" (BSSs) into one large building (ESS).
- The "**company directory**" mapping names to extensions is analogous to the **MAC address table** maintained at the AP.

If two people speak at the same instant, their words collide and become garbled — this is the **collision problem** in WLANs, which is fundamentally harder to detect than in wired Ethernet, motivating the *collision avoidance* (CA) strategy rather than *collision detection* (CD).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance & Standard Metrics**
> - **Operating Frequency Bands:** **2.4 GHz** (ISM band), **5 GHz** (UNII band), and **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E).
> - **Maximum Indoor Range:** ~**35 m** (802.11b) to ~**70 m** (802.11n/ac).
> - **Maximum Raw Data Rate:** **1 Mbps** (legacy) up to **9.6 Gbps** (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6).
> - **Speed of Light (RF propagation):** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.
> - **Wavelength of 2.4 GHz carrier:** $\lambda = c / f = \mathbf{12.5 \text{ cm}}$.
> - **Wavelength of 5 GHz carrier:** $\lambda = c / f = \mathbf{6 \text{ cm}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of IEEE 802.11 Architecture

The IEEE 802.11 standard is partitioned into layered functional blocks. Each block performs a discrete operation in transforming application-layer data into an over-the-air radio signal.

### A. Physical Layer (PHY) Variants

The 802.11 PHY has evolved through multiple generations. The four most exam-relevant variants for KTU 2024 are:

1. **802.11 (legacy, 1997)** — Infrared (IR) or **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** / **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** at **2.4 GHz**, raw rate **1 Mbps / 2 Mbps**.
2. **802.11b (1999)** — DSSS only at 2.4 GHz, rates up to **11 Mbps** (CCK modulation).
3. **802.11a (1999)** — **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** at **5 GHz**, rates up to **54 Mbps**.
4. **802.11g (2003)** — OFDM at 2.4 GHz, backward compatible with 802.11b, up to **54 Mbps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spread Spectrum?** Spread spectrum was originally a military technique. It *spreads* a narrowband signal over a wide frequency band using a pseudo-random code, making the signal resistant to jamming, interception, and multipath fading. Two main types: **FHSS** (hopping among many channels) and **DSSS** (multiplying data with a chipping code).

### B. MAC Layer Architecture

The 802.11 MAC is the most heavily tested component. It is divided into two coordination functions that control channel access:

#### B.1 Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) — Mandatory
- **CSMA/CA-based** contention mechanism.
- Uses **physical carrier sensing** (CCA — Clear Channel Assessment) and **virtual carrier sensing** (Network Allocation Vector, NAV).
- Employs **binary exponential backoff** for collision resolution.
- Uses **acknowledgment (ACK) frames** for reliability.
- Includes **RTS/CTS handshake** as an optional collision-avoidance enhancement.

#### B.2 Point Coordination Function (PCF) — Optional
- A **contention-free, polled access** mechanism.
- The **Point Coordinator (PC)** — usually located at the AP — sequentially polls stations in a round-robin manner.
- The beacon interval is divided into a **Contention Period (CP)** where DCF rules apply, and a **Contention-Free Period (CFP)** where only PCF polling occurs.

### C. The Hidden Node Problem & RTS/CTS

The **hidden node problem** is a critical KTU exam topic. It occurs when two stations (A and C) are within range of the AP (B) but out of radio range of each other. Carrier sensing at A and C fails, leading to a collision at B. The solution is the **RTS/CTS handshake**:

- **RTS (Request to Send):** A sends a small control frame to the AP announcing its intent to transmit.
- **CTS (Clear to Send):** The AP replies with a CTS that is heard by all stations in the BSA. Any station hearing the CTS silently defers (updates its NAV).
- This virtual carrier sensing effectively "reserves" the medium.

## 2.2 Why CSMA/CA and Not CSMA/CD?

In wired Ethernet (802.3), stations can detect collisions because the signal energy on a shared wire is the *sum* of all transmitting stations' energy (a collision produces an abnormally high voltage). This is **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)**.

In wireless networks, collision detection is **impractical** for three reasons:

1. **Implementing a collision detector in a radio is expensive** — radios are designed to receive very weak signals, not detect summed transmit energy.
2. **The near-far problem:** a strong nearby transmitter would mask a weak distant one.
3. **Station cannot transmit and listen simultaneously** on the same frequency with a single antenna (without complex echo-cancellation hardware).

Therefore, 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — a *proactive* strategy to *avoid* collisions rather than *detect* them after they occur.

## 2.3 CSMA/CA Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

1. A station with a new MAC frame to transmit senses the channel using **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)**.
2. If the channel is **idle for a DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** period, the station transmits immediately.
3. If the channel is **busy**, the station **defers**, waits for the channel to become idle, then waits an additional **DIFS**, and finally enters a **backoff** state.
4. The station selects a **random backoff counter** uniformly from $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, CW\}$, where $CW$ is the current contention window.
5. For every **slot time** ($\sigma$) the channel remains idle, the backoff counter is decremented. If the channel becomes busy, the counter is **frozen**.
6. When the counter reaches **zero**, the station transmits the frame.
7. The receiver, after a **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)**, transmits an **ACK** if the frame was received correctly.
8. If no ACK is received within a timeout, the sender **assumes a collision** and **doubles $CW$** up to $CW_{\max}$ (this is the *binary exponential backoff*).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Nearly Every KTU Numerical**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Data Rate} \; R &= \frac{N_{\text{bits per symbol}}}{T_{\text{symbol}}} = N_{\text{bits}} \cdot B_{\text{eff}} \\
\text{Slot Time } (\sigma) &=
\begin{cases}
20 \, \mu s & \text{for 802.11b (DSSS)} \\
9 \, \mu s  & \text{for 802.11a/g (OFDM)}
\end{cases} \\
\text{SIFS} &=
\begin{cases}
10 \, \mu s & \text{802.11b} \\
16 \, \mu s & \text{802.11a/g}
\end{cases} \\
\text{DIFS} &= \text{SIFS} + 2 \cdot \sigma \\
\text{EIFS} &= \text{SIFS} + T_{\text{ACK}} + \text{DIFS} \\
CW_{\text{new}} &= \min\left(2 \cdot (CW_{\text{old}} + 1) - 1, \; CW_{\max}\right) \\
CW_{\min} &= 31 \quad ; \quad CW_{\max} = 1023 \quad \text{(802.11 DSSS)} \\
\text{Throughput} \; S &= \frac{L}{T_{\text{frame}} + T_{\text{DIFS}} + T_{\text{backoff}} + T_{\text{SIFS}} + T_{\text{ACK}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | 802.11 (DSSS) | 802.11b | 802.11a/g (OFDM) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 5 GHz / 2.4 GHz | ISM/UNII allocation |
| Modulation | DBPSK / DQPSK | CCK | BPSK→64-QAM | Bits per subcarrier |
| Raw Bit Rate | 1 / 2 Mbps | 5.5 / 11 Mbps | 6 → 54 Mbps | PHY-layer speed |
| Slot Time $\sigma$ | 20 $\mu$s | 20 $\mu$s | 9 $\mu$s | MAC time quantum |
| SIFS | 10 $\mu$s | 10 $\mu$s | 16 $\mu$s | Highest-priority gap |
| DIFS | 50 $\mu$s | 50 $\mu$s | 34 $\mu$s | DCF data gap |
| $CW_{\min}$ | 31 | 31 | 15 | Initial backoff window |
| $CW_{\max}$ | 1023 | 1023 | 1023 | Saturation backoff window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** WLAN technology underpins all modern Wi-Fi deployments in homes, offices, hospitals (for wireless patient monitoring), industrial IoT (robotics coordination), and smart cities. The MAC throughput formula directly informs **AP capacity planning** — the number of clients an AP can support before QoS degrades.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DCF Backoff Process and Average Wait

The KTU board examiner often tests the **average backoff delay** and **saturation throughput** of 802.11 DCF. Below is an exhaustive derivation, suitable for the 14-mark part-(a) question.

### 3.1.1 Average Backoff Time per Transmission Attempt

The contention window $CW$ doubles (or rather, the upper bound is updated) upon each unsuccessful transmission attempt, following the rule:

$$
CW_i = \min\left(2^{i+4} - 1, \; CW_{\max}\right)
$$

where $i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ is the backoff stage and $m$ is the maximum retry limit.

The **average backoff counter** drawn uniformly from $\{0, 1, \ldots, CW_i - 1\}$ is:

$$
\overline{B_i} = \frac{CW_i - 1}{2}
$$

For the **first attempt** ($i = 0$), with $CW_0 = CW_{\min} = 31$:

$$
\overline{B_0} = \frac{31 - 1}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ slots}
$$

The **average time spent in backoff per attempt** is therefore:

$$
\overline{T_{\text{backoff}}} = \overline{B_i} \cdot \sigma
$$

For 802.11b ($\sigma = 20 \, \mu s$):

$$
\overline{T_{\text{backoff}}}^{(1)} = 15 \times 20 = 300 \, \mu s
$$

### 3.1.2 Total Average Delay for a Successful Frame

The total average delay $T_{\text{total}}$ for a successful frame transmission in DCF is the sum of:

1. **DIFS waiting time** — the station waits for DIFS if the channel was sensed busy.
2. **Average backoff** — $\overline{B_i} \cdot \sigma$ slots.
3. **Data frame transmission time** — $T_{\text{data}}$.
4. **SIFS waiting time** — for the ACK to follow.
5. **ACK transmission time** — $T_{\text{ACK}}$.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{DIFS}} + \overline{B_i} \cdot \sigma + T_{\text{data}} + T_{\text{SIFS}} + T_{\text{ACK}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Favourite Style)

> **Problem:** A wireless node operates under 802.11b DCF. The contention window minimum is 31, slot time is 20 $\mu$s, and DIFS = 50 $\mu$s. Compute the **average total delay** for a 1000-byte data frame at 11 Mbps, given SIFS = 10 $\mu$s and ACK = 14 bytes at 1 Mbps. Assume first transmission attempt.

**Step 1 — Compute frame transmission times.**

$$
T_{\text{data}} = \frac{L_{\text{data}}}{R} = \frac{1000 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{11 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = \frac{8000}{11 \times 10^6} = 727.27 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{ACK}} = \frac{14 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = 112 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average backoff.**

$$
\overline{B_0} = \frac{31 - 1}{2} = 15 \text{ slots}
$$

$$
T_{\text{backoff}} = 15 \times 20 \, \mu s = 300 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all components.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{DIFS}} + T_{\text{backoff}} + T_{\text{data}} + T_{\text{SIFS}} + T_{\text{ACK}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 50 + 300 + 727.27 + 10 + 112
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 1199.27 \, \mu s \approx 1.199 \, \text{ms}
$$

> **Step 4 — Throughput.**

$$
S = \frac{L_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{1199.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \approx 6.67 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Note the efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{R_{\text{raw}}} = \frac{6.67}{11} \approx 60.6\%
$$

This demonstrates a key KTU insight: **even under ideal conditions, DCF MAC overhead caps the effective throughput at roughly 50–60% of the raw PHY rate.** This is why the 54 Mbps raw rate of 802.11a/g yields only ~25 Mbps of TCP throughput in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Propagation Delay & Cell Coverage (Link Budget)

For a WLAN link, the **free-space path loss** is given by the **Friis transmission equation**:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2
$$

In dB form:

$$
P_r \, [\text{dBm}] = P_t \, [\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{c}{4 \pi}\right)
$$

Simplifying the constant $20 \log_{10}(c / 4\pi)$ at 2.4 GHz yields approximately **-40.2 dB**, so the 2.4 GHz path-loss equation becomes:

$$
\text{PL} \, [\text{dB}] = 40.2 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{GHz}})
$$

### 3.2.1 Numerical Example — Received Power

> **Problem:** A 2.4 GHz AP transmits with $P_t = 20$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi. Compute the received power at a distance of $d = 50$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute path loss.**

$$
\text{PL} = 40.2 + 20 \log_{10}(50) + 20 \log_{10}(2.4)
$$

$$
\text{PL} = 40.2 + 20 \times 1.699 + 20 \times 0.380
$$

$$
\text{PL} = 40.2 + 33.98 + 7.60 = 81.78 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute received power.**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL} = 20 + 2 + 2 - 81.78 = -57.78 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to milliwatts.**

$$
P_r = 10^{\frac{-57.78}{10}} = 10^{-5.778} = 1.66 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W} = 1.66 \, \mu\text{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 1.66 $\mu$W level is well above the typical 802.11b receiver sensitivity of **-82 dBm** at 11 Mbps, so the link is viable. As distance doubles, the path loss increases by **6 dB** (the "20 log" rule), halving the received power.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DCF vs PCF vs RTS/CTS

| Feature | Pure DCF (CSMA/CA) | PCF (Polling) | DCF + RTS/CTS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access Mode | Contention-based | Contention-free (polled) | Contention + reservation |
| Channel Sensing | Physical CCA + Virtual NAV | Centralized PC control | RTS/CTS exchange |
| Hidden Node Solution | None (vulnerable) | Yes (centralized) | Yes (virtual sensing) |
| Throughput Efficiency | Medium | Higher in heavy load | Better in heavy load |
| Latency Bound | Unbounded | Bounded (CFP max) | Bounded per station |
| Mandatory? | **Yes** | Optional | Optional |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | Medium | **Very High** |

## 3.4 Frame Structure Analysis (MAC Frame Format)

The 802.11 MAC frame has three main components. The full structure is:

$$
\text{Frame} = \underbrace{\text{MAC Header}}_{\text{30 bytes (min)}} + \underbrace{\text{Frame Body}}_{0-2312 \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{FCS}}_{\text{4 bytes CRC}}
$$

The MAC Header fields are:

- **Frame Control (2 bytes)** — contains protocol version, type (Management / Control / Data), subtype, To DS, From DS, More Fragments, Retry, Power Management, More Data, WEP, Order.
- **Duration / ID (2 bytes)** — used to update the **NAV** of other stations.
- **Address 1, 2, 3, 4 (6 bytes each)** — up to four MAC addresses for routing through the DS.
- **Sequence Control (2 bytes)** — fragment number and sequence number for de-duplication and reassembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Four Addresses?** Unlike Ethernet which has only source and destination, 802.11 frames may pass through an AP and a DS, requiring four addresses: **Source, Destination, Transmitter, Receiver** (e.g., the AP's BSSID and the original Ethernet MAC).

The three frame types and their sub-types are:

- **Management Frames (Type = 00):** Beacon, Probe Request/Response, Authentication, Association Request/Response, Disassociation, Deauthentication.
- **Control Frames (Type = 01):** RTS, CTS, ACK, CF-Poll, CF-End.
- **Data Frames (Type = 10):** Data, Null, Data + CF-Ack, Data + CF-Poll.
