# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **Infrastructure WLAN** and **Ad-hoc WLAN (IBSS)**. List any **two** distinguishing features.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Infrastructure WLAN | Ad-hoc WLAN (IBSS) |
|---|---|---|
| Access Point | **Present** (acts as central coordinator) | **Absent** (peer-to-peer) |
| Topology | Star (clients connect to AP) | Mesh (multi-hop possible) |
| Connection to DS | Yes (via AP) | No |
| Range | Extended (multiple BSSs via ESS) | Limited to single BSA |
| Example | Home Wi-Fi, office Wi-Fi | File sharing between two laptops, IoT pairing |

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** What is the **hidden node problem** in WLANs? Explain how the **RTS/CTS handshake** resolves it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hidden node problem** occurs when two stations (say A and C) are each within radio range of the AP (B) but **out of range of each other**. Both A and C perform carrier sensing and find the channel idle, then transmit simultaneously, causing a **collision at B**. The collision is undetected by A and C because they cannot hear each other.

**Resolution via RTS/CTS:** When A wants to transmit, it first sends a small **RTS (Request to Send)** frame to B. B replies with a **CTS (Clear to Send)** frame that is broadcast within the BSA. C, hearing the CTS, updates its **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** and **defers** for the announced duration, thus avoiding the collision. This is a form of *virtual carrier sensing*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an IEEE 802.11 WLAN**. Define BSS, ESS, DS, AP, and IBSS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CSMA/CA protocol** as used in 802.11 DCF. Discuss the **hidden node problem** and how **RTS/CTS** mitigates it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The IEEE 802.11 WLAN architecture consists of four hierarchical building blocks:

1. **Station (STA):** Any device with a wireless network interface (e.g., laptop, smartphone, IoT device). Identified by a unique 48-bit MAC address.

2. **Basic Service Set (BSS):** The smallest building block. A BSS comprises a set of stations that communicate with each other. When all stations are mobile and communicate directly without an AP, it is called an **Independent BSS (IBSS) or ad-hoc network**. When a controlling AP exists, it is an **Infrastructure BSS**.

3. **Distribution System (DS):** A logical backbone (typically wired Ethernet) that interconnects multiple BSSs. The DS enables stations in one BSS to communicate with stations in another BSS.

4. **Extended Service Set (ESS):** Two or more BSSs interconnected by a DS. The ESS appears to upper layers (e.g., the IP layer) as a single LAN, allowing seamless roaming for mobile stations as they move from one BSS to another.

5. **Access Point (AP):** A special STA that provides the gateway function between the BSS and the DS. The AP operates as a **Layer-2 bridge**, forwarding frames between the wireless medium and the wired DS.

The architecture can be represented as:

$$
\text{STA} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{AP (BSS)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{DS} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{AP (BSS)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{STA}
$$

**Key features for the answer:**
- The AP periodically broadcasts **Beacon frames** containing the SSID, supported rates, and timing synchronization.
- Stations perform **scanning (active or passive)** to discover APs.
- **Association / Re-association** is required to join a BSS.
- Roaming between APs uses the **Re-association** procedure with the new AP.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**CSMA/CA in 802.11 DCF:**

CSMA/CA is a **carrier-sense multiple-access protocol with collision avoidance**. It proceeds as follows:

1. A station with a frame to transmit first performs **carrier sensing** using the **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)** function. The CCA may detect energy above a threshold (indicating a busy channel) or detect a valid 802.11 preamble.
2. If the channel is **idle for a DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space)**, the station proceeds to transmit immediately.
3. If the channel is **busy**, the station **defers** and waits for the channel to become idle. After the channel is idle, the station waits an additional DIFS, then enters a **backoff state**.
4. In backoff, the station selects a **random integer** from $\{0, 1, \ldots, CW\}$, where $CW$ is the current contention window (initially $CW_{\min} = 31$ for 802.11b).
5. For each **slot time** $\sigma$ during which the channel is idle, the backoff counter is decremented. If the channel becomes busy mid-backoff, the counter is **frozen** and resumed later.
6. When the counter reaches zero, the station transmits the frame.
7. The receiver waits **SIFS** and replies with an **ACK** to confirm successful reception.
8. If the sender does not receive an ACK, it **doubles the contention window** (Binary Exponential Backoff) up to $CW_{\max} = 1023$, and retries.

**Hidden Node Problem and RTS/CTS:**

The hidden node problem arises when two stations A and C are both within range of AP B but **out of range of each other**. Carrier sensing fails to detect the other's transmission, leading to simultaneous transmissions and a **collision at B**.

**RTS/CTS Solution:**
- Before sending data, A sends an **RTS frame** to B.
- B responds with a **CTS frame** broadcast within the BSA. The CTS includes a **duration field** indicating how long the medium will be reserved.
- C, hearing the CTS, updates its **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** and refrains from transmitting for the indicated duration.
- This **virtual carrier sensing** is immune to the hidden node problem because the CTS is broadcast from the central AP, which is in range of all stations.

**Trade-off:** RTS/CTS adds overhead, so it is typically used only when the **data frame exceeds the RTS threshold** (often 500 bytes), to avoid wasting capacity on small frames.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Explain the **physical layer specifications** of IEEE 802.11. Compare **DSSS and OFDM** as used in 802.11b and 802.11a/g respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **WLAN security mechanisms** — WEP, WPA, and WPA2. Explain why WEP is considered broken. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

The IEEE 802.11 PHY is responsible for converting MAC-layer frames into over-the-air radio signals. The major PHY variants are:

**1. 802.11 Legacy (1997) — DSSS, FHSS, IR:**
- Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**.
- DSSS uses an **11-chip Barker sequence** to spread each data bit, achieving 1 Mbps (DBPSK) or 2 Mbps (DQPSK).
- FHSS hops among **79 channels** in 2.4 GHz, using 2- or 4-level GFSK.
- IR variant uses diffused infrared at 850 nm, 1 Mbps.

**2. 802.11b (1999) — HR-DSSS:**
- Same 2.4 GHz band, raw rates up to **11 Mbps** using **CCK (Complementary Code Keying)** modulation.
- Spreading uses 8-chip CCK codes instead of Barker.

**3. 802.11a (1999) — OFDM at 5 GHz:**
- Uses **OFDM** with **52 subcarriers** (48 data + 4 pilot).
- Operates in the 5 GHz UNII band, raw rates from 6 Mbps (BPSK) to **54 Mbps (64-QAM)**.
- Each OFDM symbol duration is **4 $\mu$s**, with a **800 ns guard interval**.

**4. 802.11g (2003) — OFDM at 2.4 GHz:**
- Brings the 54 Mbps OFDM PHY to the 2.4 GHz band; backward compatible with 802.11b.

**DSSS vs OFDM Comparison:**

| Feature | DSSS (802.11b) | OFDM (802.11a/g) |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation | CCK / DBPSK / DQPSK | BPSK → 64-QAM on 48 subcarriers |
| Symbol Rate | 11 Mchips/s | 250,000 OFDM symbols/s |
| Raw Rate | 1, 2, 5.5, 11 Mbps | 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 48, 54 Mbps |
| Multipath Robustness | Good (chip-level diversity) | Excellent (cyclic prefix absorbs ISI) |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | ~0.8 bps/Hz | ~2.7 bps/Hz |
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz | 5 GHz (11a) / 2.4 GHz (11g) |
| Interference Resistance | Moderate (spread spectrum) | High (frequency diversity) |

**OFDM Advantage:** OFDM divides the wideband channel into many **narrowband subcarriers**, each experiencing flat fading. A **cyclic prefix** is added to absorb intersymbol interference, making OFDM highly robust in multipath environments such as indoor offices.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**WLAN Security Mechanisms:**

**1. WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) — Original 802.11 Security (1997):**
- Uses the **RC4 stream cipher** with a **40-bit (or 104-bit) shared secret key** and a **24-bit Initialization Vector (IV)**.
- The IV is prepended to the secret key to form the per-packet RC4 seed.
- A 32-bit **CRC-32 Integrity Check Value (ICV)** is appended to the payload for integrity.

**Why WEP is broken:**
- The 24-bit IV space is small (~16.7 million), so IVs repeat within hours on a busy network, leading to **key reuse**.
- The RC4 key schedule is **vulnerable to statistical attacks** (Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir, 2001). With ~4 million captured packets, an attacker can recover the shared key in minutes.
- The CRC-32 is a **linear function** and does not provide cryptographic integrity, allowing **bit-flipping attacks**.
- There is **no built-in key management**; the same key is shared manually among all clients.

**2. WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — 2003:**
- Introduced as an **interim fix** for WEP while 802.11i was finalized.
- Uses the **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)**, which generates a **unique per-packet key** by mixing a 128-bit temporal key with the MAC address and a 48-bit IV (extended from 24 bits).
- Adds a **MIC (Message Integrity Code)** called **Michael** to detect tampering.
- Still uses RC4 under the hood, so it is also vulnerable in theory.

**3. WPA2 (802.11i, 2004/2006):**
- Mandates the use of **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) in CCMP mode** (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol).
- Provides **128-bit AES encryption** with strong cryptographic integrity.
- Employs a **4-way handshake** to derive fresh per-session keys.
- Supports **enterprise mode** using **802.1X authentication** with a **RADIUS** backend, allowing individual user credentials.
- Considered **cryptographically secure** when properly configured.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on WLAN Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SIFS, PIFS, DIFS, EIFS** — remember DIFS = SIFS + 2 $\sigma$ and PIFS = SIFS + 1 $\sigma$. Draw a labelled timeline.
> 2. **Mixing up BSS, ESS, IBSS** — IBSS is *ad-hoc* (no AP); BSS with AP is *infrastructure*; ESS is multiple BSSs joined by a DS.
> 3. **Stating that CSMA/CA detects collisions** — it *avoids* them. Only 802.3 (Ethernet) uses CSMA/CD.
> 4. **Wrong Throughput formula** — efficiency $\eta = S / R_{\text{raw}}$; remember to include DIFS, SIFS, ACK, and backoff in the *denominator*.
> 5. **Forgetting why four MAC addresses exist** — needed for DS routing; draw a To/From DS bit matrix.
> 6. **Stating WEP is "secure enough"** — examiners want the *specific* 24-bit IV, RC4 weakness, and FMS attack named.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [ ] **IEEE 802.11** is the family of WLAN standards, covering the **PHY and MAC** layers only.
- [ ] **Core architecture components:** STA, BSS, BSA, AP, DS, ESS, IBSS.
- [ ] **IBSS (ad-hoc)** has no AP and no DS; **BSS with AP** is the *infrastructure mode*.
- [ ] **ESS** = multiple BSSs tied by a DS, presenting a single logical LAN.
- [ ] **PHY variants to know:** DSSS (1–2 Mbps), HR-DSSS/CCK (11 Mbps = 802.11b), OFDM (54 Mbps = 802.11a/g).
- [ ] **DSSS** spreads using an 11-chip Barker code; **OFDM** uses 52 subcarriers with cyclic prefix.
- [ ] **2.4 GHz band** carries 802.11b/g/n/ax; **5 GHz band** carries 802.11a/n/ac/ax.
- [ ] **Why CSMA/CA (not CD)?** Cannot detect collisions in radio due to near-far problem and half-duplex operation.
- [ ] **DCF** is mandatory, contention-based, uses CSMA/CA + binary exponential backoff + ACK.
- [ ] **PCF** is optional, contention-free, uses central polling by AP; alternates CP and CFP.
- [ ] **Inter-frame spaces in order of priority:** SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.
- [ ] **SIFS** is for highest-priority frames: ACK, CTS, polling responses.
- [ ] **DIFS** is for normal data traffic using DCF.
- [ ] **Binary Exponential Backoff:** $CW$ doubles on each failure up to $CW_{\max} = 1023$.
- [ ] **Hidden node problem:** RTS/CTS solves it via virtual carrier sensing (NAV update from CTS).
- [ ] **NAV** is the virtual carrier-sensing counter; any frame with a duration field updates it.
- [ ] **802.11 MAC frame** has Frame Control, Duration, up to **4 Address fields**, Sequence Control, Body, FCS.
- [ ] **Throughput efficiency** of DCF is typically 50–60% of raw PHY rate; rest is MAC overhead.
- [ ] **WEP** uses RC4 + 24-bit IV → broken by FMS attack in 2001.
- [ ] **WPA** uses TKIP with Michael MIC; **WPA2** mandates AES-CCMP (cryptographically secure).
- [ ] **802.11i** is the formal security amendment equivalent to **WPA2**.
- [ ] **Friis equation** for path loss: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4 \pi d)^2$.
- [ ] **At 2.4 GHz, doubling distance costs 6 dB of path loss** (20 log d rule).
- [ ] **Beacons** are sent by APs at intervals (typically 100 ms) to advertise the BSS.
- [ ] **Scanning** is either **active** (Probe Request/Response) or **passive** (listening to Beacons).
- [ ] **Association / Re-association** is required for a STA to join a BSS and to roam.
- [ ] **MAC address size** in 802.11 is 48 bits, identical to IEEE 802.3 Ethernet.
- [ ] **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; 2.4 GHz wavelength = 12.5 cm.
- [ ] **Slot time** = 20 $\mu$s (DSSS) or 9 $\mu$s (OFDM).
- [ ] **ACK timeout** is a critical parameter; if no ACK, the sender assumes collision and backs off.
- [ ] **Fragmentation** is supported via the More Fragments bit, used for noisy channels.
- [ ] **RTS threshold** controls when to use RTS/CTS; typically only for large frames.
- [ ] **Power management** uses TIM (Traffic Indication Map) and ATIM (Ad-hoc TIM) frames.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth and Personal Area Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth and Personal Area Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Personal Area Network (PAN) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personal Area Network (PAN):** A Personal Area Network is a short-range, low-power, wireless or wired data communication network interconnecting devices centered around an individual's personal workspace, typically with an operational range of **less than 10 meters** (often quoted between 1 m and 10 m), enabling personal device connectivity, synchronization, and ad-hoc data exchange.

The PAN concept was popularized by **G. H. Forman** and **J. Zahorjan** (early 1990s) as the network "closest to the user." PAN technologies include:
- **Wired PANs:** USB, FireWire (IEEE 1394), Thunderbolt
- **Wireless PANs (WPANs):** Bluetooth, Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), IrDA (Infrared Data Association), NFC, UWB, ANT+

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to differentiate PANs from LANs/MANs/WANs by range, data rate, and typical application domains.

### 1.2 Bluetooth — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth:** Bluetooth is a Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN) technology standardized as **IEEE 802.15.1**, operating in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific and Medical (ISM) band**, using **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** at **1600 hops/second**, designed to replace wired cables for short-range device-to-device communication.

- **Named after:** King Harald "Bluetooth" Gormsson of Denmark (10th century), who unified Denmark and Norway — symbolizing how Bluetooth unifies communication protocols.
- **Governed by:** Bluetooth Special Interest Group (Bluetooth SIG) — founded 1998 by **Ericsson, IBM, Intel, Nokia, Toshiba**.
- **Current custodian logo:** Merged runes for Harald's initials (H and B) — ᚼᛒ.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie Café" ☕🎧**
> Imagine 5 friends in a noisy café. They cannot all shout on the same channel. Instead, every 625 microseconds, they simultaneously **switch to a new channel**, hopping in a coordinated sequence. To outsiders, the conversation appears as random noise bursts; to the friends, it is a clean, robust private chat.
> - **Friends** = Bluetooth devices
> - **Hopping schedule** = Synchronized frequency hopping
> - **625 µs slot** = One Bluetooth time slot
> - **The Café** = The congested 2.4 GHz ISM band (shared with Wi-Fi, microwaves, etc.)
>
> Bluetooth's "magic" is that by hopping, it avoids the very interference it would otherwise suffer in a crowded band.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Specifications (Bolded)

| Specification | Value / Metric |
|---|---|
| **Operating Frequency Band** | **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** (ISM band) |
| **RF Channels (Classic Bluetooth)** | **79 channels**, each **1 MHz wide** |
| **Hop Rate** | **1600 hops/second** |
| **Dwell Time per Channel** | **625 µs** (i.e., 1/1600 s) |
| **Modulation (Basic Rate)** | **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** |
| **Basic Data Rate (BR)** | **1 Mbps** |
| **Enhanced Data Rate (EDR)** | **2 Mbps (π/4-DQPSK)** or **3 Mbps (8-DPSK)** |
| **Maximum Transmit Power (Class 1)** | **100 mW (20 dBm)** |
| **Typical Range (Class 2)** | **10 meters** |
| **Device Address (BD_ADDR)** | **48 bits** (same as MAC) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a New Technology? The Bluetooth Motivation

The original motivation (1994, Ericsson, **Jaap Haartsen** as lead inventor) was to eliminate the **"cable spaghetti"** behind desktop computers and provide a low-cost, low-power, short-range radio link.

Key engineering requirements that dictated the design:
1. **Operate globally** → Must use the license-free **2.4 GHz ISM band**.
2. **Tolerate interference** → Adopted **FHSS** (resilient to narrowband jammers).
3. **Be cheap and low-power** → Class 2 radios (2.5 mW) suitable for coin-cell batteries.
4. **Support voice + data** → Connection-oriented (SCO) and connectionless (ACL) links.
5. **Form ad-hoc networks** → Need a **Master–Slave** topology without infrastructure.

### 2.2 Bluetooth Network Topology — Piconet & Scatternet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piconet:** A **Piconet** is the fundamental Bluetooth network, consisting of **one Master** and **up to 7 active Slaves** (8 total active nodes) sharing the same hopping sequence and clock.
>
> **Scatternet:** A **Scatternet** is formed by **interconnecting two or more piconets** through a shared node (a device that is master in one piconet and slave in another). This allows scalability beyond 8 devices.

**Topology Rules:**
- Maximum **7 active slaves** in a piconet (3-bit **AM_ADDR** — Active Member Address).
- A device in **Parked** state can be present but inactive; up to 255 parked slaves are addressable.
- The **Master** controls the channel access via **polling** (TDMA-style slot assignment).
- The Master transmits in **even-numbered slots**; slaves transmit in **odd-numbered slots** (TDD scheme).

### 2.3 Bluetooth Frequency Hopping — The Core Mechanism

Bluetooth divides the **2.402 – 2.480 GHz** band into **79 RF channels** of 1 MHz each. Hopping occurs every **625 µs**, yielding:

$$
f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slot}}} = \frac{1}{625 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}} = \mathbf{1600\ hops/second}
$$

The hop sequence is generated by a pseudo-random algorithm using the **Master's clock** (28-bit) and the **Master's BD_ADDR (lower 28 bits)**. There are **5 fundamental hop sequence types**:
- Inquiry hop sequence (32 wake-up channels)
- Inquiry response
- Paging hop sequence (32 channels)
- Paging response
- **Channel hop sequence** (the actual data-carrying 79-channel cycle)

> [!TIP]
> **Why 32 vs 79?** In inquiry/paging states, only 32 of the 79 channels are used to accelerate device discovery; once connected, all 79 channels are cycled for full interference diversity.

### 2.4 Bluetooth Connection Establishment — Operational Steps

The connection setup involves a strict sequence:

1. **Standby State:** Device is idle, listening periodically on 32 wake-up channels.
2. **Inquiry:** Master broadcasts an **ID packet** on 32 inquiry channels to discover neighbors.
3. **Inquiry Scan:** Prospective slaves listen for ID packets and respond with **FHS packet** (containing their BD_ADDR, clock, and class).
4. **Inquiry Response:** Master collects FHS packets, builds a list of candidates.
5. **Paging (Page):** Master sends slave's **Device Access Code (DAC)** derived from BD_ADDR.
6. **Page Response:** Slave acknowledges; clock alignment occurs.
7. **Connection State:** Master assigns **3-bit AM_ADDR** to slave; **Piconet** is formed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note:** The Inquiry + Page process is often asked in KTU exams as a 7–10 mark descriptive question. Memorize the sequence and packet types (ID and FHS).

### 2.5 Bluetooth Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

Bluetooth follows a layered protocol stack conforming loosely to the OSI model. From bottom to top:

| Layer | Function | Key Protocols/Entities |
|---|---|---|
| **Radio (Physical)** | RF transmission, modulation, frequency hopping | 2.4 GHz, GFSK, FHSS |
| **Baseband** | Channel access, framing, error correction, encryption | ACL, SCO links; CRC; FEC |
| **Link Manager (LMP)** | Link setup, authentication, power control | LMP Protocol (LMP_PDU) |
| **Host Controller Interface (HCI)** | Standardized command interface | HCI Commands, Events |
| **Logical Link Control & Adaptation (L2CAP)** | Multiplexing, segmentation, QoS | L2CAP Channels |
| **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)** | Discovering available services | SDP Records |
| **RFCOMM** | Serial port emulation (RS-232 over BT) | ETSI TS 07.10 |
| **OBEX / Application** | Object exchange, file transfer, profiles | OBEX, Audio profiles |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Host** (PC/phone) contains layers from HCI upward; the **Controller** (Bluetooth chip) contains Radio, Baseband, and LM. The HCI is the standardized bus between them.

### 2.6 Logical Channels & Link Types

- **ACL (Asynchronous Connection-Less):** Best-effort, packet-switched, point-to-multipoint between master and slaves; supports retransmission. Used for **data**.
- **SCO (Synchronous Connection-Oriented):** Circuit-switched, symmetric, point-to-point; **reserved slots**; no retransmission. Used for **voice** (HV1, HV2, HV3 packets at 64 kbps).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Specification Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Constraint Reminder:** The table below uses `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol).

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Hop Rate** | $f_{\text{hop}} = 1 / T_{\text{slot}}$ | **1600 Hz** (when $T_{\text{slot}} = 625\ \mu s$) |
| **Dwell Time** | $T_{\text{dwell}} = 1 / f_{\text{hop}}$ | **625 µs** per channel |
| **Hop Period over 79 channels** | $T_{\text{cycle}} = 79 \times 625\ \mu s$ | **49.375 ms** |
| **Frame Duration (Single-slot)** | $T_{\text{frame}} = 2 \times T_{\text{slot}}$ | **1250 µs** (1-slot Tx + 1-slot Rx) |
| **Frame Duration (3-slot)** | $T_{\text{frame,3}} = 6 \times T_{\text{slot}}$ | **3750 µs** |
| **Frame Duration (5-slot)** | $T_{\text{frame,5}} = 10 \times T_{\text{slot}}$ | **6250 µs** |
| **Max Active Members / Piconet** | $N_{\text{active}} = 2^{\text{AM\_ADDR\_bits}} - 1$ | **7 slaves** (3-bit AM_ADDR, 0 reserved) |
| **Power Class 1 (Max)** | $P_{\text{max}}$ | **100 mW** (20 dBm) |
| **Power Class 2 (Max)** | $P_{\text{max}}$ | **2.5 mW** (4 dBm) |
| **Power Class 3 (Max)** | $P_{\text{max}}$ | **1 mW** (0 dBm) |
| **BD_ADDR** | Length | **48 bits** |
| **Clock** | Length | **28 bits** |
| **Net Basic Rate** | Throughput after overhead | **~720 kbps** (asymmetric) |
| **EDR Throughput** | π/4-DQPSK / 8-DPSK | **~2.1 Mbps / ~3 Mbps** |
| **BLE Modulation (4.0+)** | Gaussian FSK | **1 Mbps** (LE 1M), **2 Mbps** (LE 2M, BT 5.0) |
| **BLE Range (BT 5.0 coded PHY)** | With FEC | **~200 m** (outdoor, line of sight) |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics:** Wireless headsets, speakers, smartwatches, fitness bands.
- **Healthcare:** Wireless pulse oximeters, glucose monitors (BLE for ultra-low power).
- **Industrial IoT:** Asset tracking, indoor positioning (BT 5.1 AoA/AoD).
- **Automotive:** Hands-free calling, audio streaming (A2DP profile).
- **Smart Home:** Mesh networks via Bluetooth Mesh (BT 5.0+).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hopping Rate from Slot Time

**Problem:** Given the Bluetooth slot duration is 625 microseconds, derive the hopping rate and the time to cycle through all 79 RF channels.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**

The hop rate is the reciprocal of the dwell time per channel:

$$
f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slot}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known value.**

$$
f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{625 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the expression.**

$$
f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}} = 1.6 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Hz} = \mathbf{1600\ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cycle time for all 79 channels.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N_{\text{ch}} \times T_{\text{slot}} = 79 \times 625\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 79 \times 625 = 49375\ \mu s = \mathbf{49.375\ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In 49.375 ms, Bluetooth visits every one of the 79 channels exactly once, providing frequency diversity. A narrowband jammer occupying even a few MHz can only disrupt a tiny fraction of the total traffic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Number of Active Slaves

**Problem:** Why is the maximum number of active slaves in a piconet limited to 7, given the addressing scheme?

**Step 1 — Understand the addressing field.**

Bluetooth uses a 3-bit field called the **AM_ADDR (Active Member Address)** embedded in the baseband packet header.

**Step 2 — Calculate the total addressable range.**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^{n} = 2^{3} = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Account for the reserved value.**

The address `000` is reserved for **broadcast** (master-to-all-slaves). Therefore, active slave addresses are `001` through `111`:

$$
N_{\text{active slaves}} = 2^{3} - 1 = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{active slaves}} = 7}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Parked slaves (up to 255) reuse these addresses but are inactive. They are tracked via a separate **PM_ADDR (Parked Member Address)** of 8 bits.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Achievable Throughput (Basic Rate)

**Problem:** Calculate the **maximum asymmetric throughput** of a Bluetooth Classic link operating at the basic rate of 1 Mbps, assuming a 5-slot DH5 packet, taking into account framing overhead.

**Step 1 — Recall DH5 packet characteristics.**

- DH5 = Data – High rate, 5-slot packet.
- Payload size = **339 bytes** (asymmetric DH5, no FEC).
- Packet duration = **5 slots = 5 × 625 µs = 3125 µs** (per direction).
- TDD scheme: 1 Tx + 1 Rx in a **6-slot frame** (5-slot packet + 1-slot for the other direction).

**Step 2 — Effective raw data rate per slot.**

$$
R_{\text{slot}} = \frac{339 \times 8\ \text{bits}}{5\ \text{slots} \times 625\ \mu s}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
R_{\text{slot}} = \frac{2712\ \text{bits}}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}} = 867.84\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for TDD asymmetry (e.g., 1 master-slot every 6 slots is for ACK overhead).**

The asymmetry allows the master to send, e.g., 3 of 4 consecutive DH5 packets while the slave sends 1. For 1-slot reverse, the **maximum symmetric** throughput reduces; for purely forward asymmetric, throughput can approach ~758 kbps after HCI/ACL/L2CAP overhead.

**Step 5 — Practical KTU answer.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{BR, practical}} \approx 720\ \text{kbps (asymmetric, Basic Rate)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — EDR Throughput Gain

**Problem:** Compare the maximum physical layer rate of EDR using π/4-DQPSK and 8-DPSK versus Basic Rate GFSK.

**Step 1 — Basic Rate (BR).**

GFSK encodes 1 bit per symbol, symbol rate = 1 Msps:

$$
R_{\text{BR}} = 1\ \text{bit/symbol} \times 1\ \text{Msps} = \mathbf{1\ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — EDR 2 Mbps (π/4-DQPSK).**

DQPSK encodes 2 bits per symbol:

$$
R_{\text{EDR, 2M}} = 2\ \text{bits/symbol} \times 1\ \text{Msps} = \mathbf{2\ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — EDR 3 Mbps (8-DPSK).**

8-DPSK encodes 3 bits per symbol:

$$
R_{\text{EDR, 3M}} = 3\ \text{bits/symbol} \times 1\ \text{Msps} = \mathbf{3\ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Net throughput after headers (EDR).**

After access code (72 bits), header (54 bits), and guard period, the **practical EDR throughput** is approximately:

$$
R_{\text{EDR, 2M, practical}} \approx 1448.5\ \text{kbps}
$$
$$
R_{\text{EDR, 3M, practical}} \approx 2178.1\ \text{kbps}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** EDR triples the symbol phase space, but net throughput gain is less than 3× because the access code and header are still transmitted at 1 Mbps GFSK (they form the "guard band" required by the specification).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Bluetooth vs. Other PAN Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative matrix is a recurring KTU exam question (7 marks) under CO3 / K3 (Apply).**

| Feature | **Bluetooth (Classic)** | **BLE (BT 4.0+)** | **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | **IrDA** | **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11n)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | Optical (Infrared) | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| **Range** | 10 m (Class 2) | ~50–200 m (BT 5) | 10–100 m | < 1 m (LOS) | 50–100 m |
| **Max Data Rate** | 1–3 Mbps | 1–2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 4 Mbps (FIR) | 150–600 Mbps |
| **Modulation** | GFSK / DQPSK / 8-DPSK | GFSK | O-QPSK (DSSS) | Pulse-position | OFDM |
| **Topology** | Piconet / Scatternet | Star / Mesh | Star / Mesh / Tree | Point-to-point | Star (BSS) |
| **Power** | Low (Class 2: 2.5 mW) | **Ultra-Low** | Very Low | Very Low | Medium–High |
| **Battery Life** | Days–Weeks | **Months–Years** | Months–Years | Days | Hours |
| **Security** | SAFER+ / E0 | AES-128 CCM | AES-128 | None standard | WPA2/WPA3 |
| **Max Nodes/Cell** | 8 active (Piconet) | Billions (LE) | ~65 000 | 1 pair | ~2007 (11n) |
| **Standard Body** | Bluetooth SIG | Bluetooth SIG | IEEE 802.15.4 | IrDA | IEEE 802.11 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bluetooth Piconet & Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PiconetA["PICOnet A — Frequency Sequence A"]
        MA["Master A\nBD_ADDR: AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:01"]
        S1["Slave 1\nAM_ADDR: 001"]
        S2["Slave 2\nAM_ADDR: 010"]
        S3["Slave 3\nAM_ADDR: 011"]
        S4["Slave 4\nAM_ADDR: 100"]
        MA --- S1
        MA --- S2
        MA --- S3
        MA --- S4
    end

    subgraph PiconetB["PICOnet B — Frequency Sequence B"]
        MB["Master B\nBD_ADDR: AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:02"]
        T1["Slave 5\nAM_ADDR: 001"]
        T2["Slave 6\nAM_ADDR: 010"]
        MB --- T1
        MB --- T2
    end

    S2["Shared Bridge Node\nMaster in A, Slave in B"] -.->|Acts as Master| MA
    S2 -.->|Acts as Slave| MB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:**
> - The `S2` node participates simultaneously in two piconets, each with a **distinct hopping sequence** derived from the respective master's BD_ADDR.
> - This forms a **Scatternet** — a critical concept for KTU 14-mark questions on network scalability.

---

### 4.2 Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Vertical Layering)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION PROFILE LAYER"]
        A1["Generic Access Profile — GAP"]
        A2["Service Discovery App — SDAP"]
        A3["Audio Video — A2DP, HFP"]
    end

    subgraph Middleware["MIDDLEWARE OBJECTS"]
        O1["RFCOMM — Serial Cable Emulation"]
        O2["OBEX — Object Exchange Protocol"]
        O3["TCS Binary — Telephony Control"]
    end

    subgraph Transport["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        T1["L2CAP — Logical Link Control and Adaptation"]
        T2["SDP — Service Discovery Protocol"]
    end

    subgraph HostCtrl["HOST CONTROLLER INTERFACE"]
        H1["HCI — Host Controller Interface\n(Standardized Bus — USB, UART, SDIO)"]
    end

    subgraph Controller["BLUETOOTH CONTROLLER"]
        C1["LMP — Link Manager Protocol"]
        C2["Baseband — Link Control, FEC, Encryption"]
        C3["Radio — 2.4 GHz, FHSS, GFSK, 1600 hops/s"]
    end

    AppLayer --> Middleware
    Middleware --> Transport
    Transport --> HostCtrl
    HostCtrl --> Controller
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The HCI line in the middle is the **standardized contract** between the host OS (Windows, Android, Linux) and the Bluetooth silicon. It allows any certified Bluetooth chip to work with any host stack.

---

### 4.3 Bluetooth Connection Establishment State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby

    Standby --> Inquiry : Master wants to discover neighbors
    Inquiry --> InquiryResponse : ID packet received
    InquiryResponse --> Standby : FHS received, candidate list built

    Standby --> Paging : Master knows slave BD_ADDR
    Paging --> PageResponse : DAC matched
    PageResponse --> Connection : Slave acknowledges with DAC

    Inquiry --> Connection : Direct page with known address
    PageResponse --> Connection : Clock offset aligned, AM_ADDR assigned

    Connection --> Active : Slave active in piconet
    Connection --> Hold : Power save mode
    Connection --> Sniff : Reduced duty cycle
    Connection --> Park : Release AM_ADDR, become inactive

    Active --> Connection : Continue
    Hold --> Connection : Resume
    Park --> Connection : Unpark via broadcast
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks: "Explain the Bluetooth Inquiry and Paging procedure with relevant packet types." Use the **ID** packet for inquiry and the **DAC (Device Access Code)** for paging. Mention **FHS (Frequency Hop Synchronization)** packet for clock/address exchange.

---

### 4.4 Bluetooth Baseband Packet Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BT_Packet["BLUETOOTH BASEBAND PACKET FORMAT"]
        A["ACCESS CODE\n72 bits\nIdentifies piconet or device"]
        H["HEADER\n54 bits\nAM_ADDR, type, ARQN, SEQN, HEC"]
        P["PAYLOAD\n0 to 2745 bits\nUser data + CRC"]
    end

    A --> H
    H --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **access code** has 3 variants: **CAC (Channel Access Code)** — for normal piconet traffic; **DAC (Device Access Code)** — for paging a specific device; **IAC (Inquiry Access Code)** — for discovering devices. The **3-bit packet type** field in the header allows up to 8 packet types (ID, NULL, POLL, FHS, DM1, DH1, ..., DM5, DH5).

---

### 4.5 Frequency Hopping Sequence Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TimeSlots["TIME DOMAIN — Each slot is 625 us"]
        S1["Slot 0\nChannel 13"]
        S2["Slot 1\nChannel 41"]
        S3["Slot 2\nChannel 27"]
        S4["Slot 3\nChannel 55"]
        S5["Slot 4\nChannel 02"]
        S6["Slot 5\nChannel 67"]
        S7["Slot 6\nChannel 19"]
        S8["Slot 7\nChannel 33"]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

> [!NOTE]
> The channel numbers are illustrative. The actual sequence is **pseudo-randomly generated** by the selection kernel using the master's clock and BD_ADDR. To an external observer, the sequence appears chaotic; to a member of the piconet, it is deterministic and predictable.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
> **Define a Personal Area Network (PAN). List any two wireless PAN technologies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Personal Area Network (PAN) is a short-range data communication network that interconnects personal devices within an individual's workspace, typically with a range of **less than 10 meters**. The objective of a PAN is to enable seamless, low-power communication among devices in close proximity to a user.
Two examples of wireless PAN technologies are:
1. **Bluetooth** (IEEE 802.15.1)
2. **Zigbee** (IEEE 802.15.4)
(Other valid examples: IrDA, NFC, UWB, ANT+)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is the significance of the 48-bit BD_ADDR in Bluetooth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **BD_ADDR (Bluetooth Device Address)** is a **48-bit unique identifier** assigned to every Bluetooth device by the manufacturer, analogous to a MAC address in Ethernet. It serves three key functions:
1. **Device Identification:** It uniquely identifies a Bluetooth device globally.
2. **Hop Sequence Generation:** The lower **28 bits** of BD_ADDR are used as a seed for the pseudo-random frequency hop sequence generator.
3. **Paging:** During connection establishment, a **Device Access Code (DAC)** is derived from the BD_ADDR, enabling targeted paging of a specific device.
BD_ADDR is divided into three parts: **LAP (24 bits, lower)**, **UAP (8 bits, middle)**, and **NAP (16 bits, upper)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Q.A) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Bluetooth piconet and scatternet with neat diagrams. Discuss the role of the Master and Slaves. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the Bluetooth protocol stack with a block diagram and explain the functions of L2CAP and LMP layers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Q.A (a) — Piconet & Scatternet:**

A **Piconet** is the basic unit of a Bluetooth network, comprising **one Master** and **up to 7 active Slaves** (8 total active nodes). The Master:
- Initiates the connection.
- Controls the frequency hopping sequence (its clock + BD_ADDR determine the sequence).
- Allocates channel access via **polling** (TDMA — Master transmits in even slots, Slaves in odd slots).
- Assigns **AM_ADDR (3-bit)** to each active slave.

A **Scatternet** is formed by interconnecting two or more piconets via a **shared node**, which acts as the Master in one piconet and the Slave in another. This allows the network to scale beyond the 8-active-node limit. The shared node **alternates** between hopping sequences (time-multiplexed), which can reduce its throughput.

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 above.

**Key points (7 marks):**
- ✅ Piconet definition (1)
- ✅ Master role + clock/hop sequence (2)
- ✅ Active slave limit of 7 (1)
- ✅ AM_ADDR addressing (1)
- ✅ Scatternet definition + bridge node (1)
- ✅ Throughput trade-offs (1)

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (b) — Protocol Stack:**

The Bluetooth protocol stack (see Section 4.2) comprises:

1. **Radio Layer (Physical):** Handles RF transmission in 2.4 GHz ISM band, GFSK modulation, and frequency hopping at 1600 hops/s.

2. **Baseband Layer:** Manages channel access, packet framing, error correction (FEC, ARQ), and encryption. Establishes **SCO** and **ACL** links.

3. **Link Manager Protocol (LMP):** Responsible for **link setup**, **authentication** of devices, **encryption key generation**, **power control**, and **negotiating link parameters** between Master and Slave. Uses **LMP_PDU** messages.

4. **Host Controller Interface (HCI):** Provides a **standardized command interface** between the Bluetooth Controller (chip) and the Host (OS/Application processor). Common transports: USB, UART, SDIO.

5. **Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP):**
   - **Multiplexing:** Allows multiple higher-layer protocols (RFCOMM, SDP) to share a single ACL link.
   - **Segmentation & Reassembly (SAR):** Breaks large packets from upper layers into 64 KB max **L2CAP MTU** segments, fitting them into baseband packets.
   - **QoS:** Provides per-channel flow specification.
   - **Channels:** L2CAP uses **Channel Identifiers (CID)** — `0x0001` for L2CAP signaling, `0x0003` for RFCOMM, dynamically assigned for others.

6. **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP):** Allows devices to **discover which services** are available on other devices, along with their attributes.

7. **RFCOMM:** A **serial port emulation** protocol (based on ETSI TS 07.10) that allows legacy serial applications to run over Bluetooth.

8. **OBEX / Application Profiles:** Higher-level object exchange, audio, and telephony applications.

**Key points (7 marks):**
- ✅ Block diagram of stack (2)
- ✅ Function of LMP (1.5)
- ✅ Function of L2CAP — multiplexing + SAR (2)
- ✅ Function of HCI (1)
- ✅ Brief on RFCOMM/SDP (0.5)

---

> **Q.B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Bluetooth baseband packet format. List the different types of baseband packets and their use. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain Bluetooth frequency hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) mechanism. Derive the hopping rate and the time taken to cycle through all 79 channels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Q.B (a) — Baseband Packet Format:**

A Bluetooth baseband packet consists of **three fields** (see Section 4.4):

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Code** | 72 bits | Synchronization + piconet/device identification |
| **Header** | 54 bits | Contains **AM_ADDR (3 bits)**, **Type (4 bits)**, **ARQN (1 bit)**, **SEQN (1 bit)**, **HEC (8 bits)** |
| **Payload** | 0 – 2745 bits | User data + CRC (optional 16-bit CRC) |

**Types of Access Codes:**
- **CAC (Channel Access Code):** Identifies the piconet.
- **DAC (Device Access Code):** Used in paging to identify a specific device (derived from BD_ADDR).
- **IAC (Inquiry Access Code):** Used in inquiry; **GIAC** (General) for all devices; **DIAC** (Dedicated) for specific device classes.

**Types of Baseband Packets (selected):**
1. **ID Packet:** Access code only, 68 bits; used in inquiry, paging, and beacon trains.
2. **NULL Packet:** Only header; used for ARQN/SEQN flow control without payload.
3. **POLL Packet:** Similar to NULL but requires explicit ACK; used by master to poll slaves.
4. **FHS Packet:** Special control packet containing the slave's BD_ADDR, clock, and class — used for hop-synchronization during inquiry response and page response.
5. **DM1 / DH1 / DM3 / DH3 / DM5 / DH5:** Data packets; suffix "M" = 2/3 FEC, "H" = no FEC; digit = number of time slots occupied (1, 3, or 5).
6. **HV1 / HV2 / HV3:** High-quality Voice packets for SCO links; suffix indicates FEC level.

**Key points (7 marks):**
- ✅ Three-field format with sizes (2)
- ✅ AM_ADDR, Type, ARQN fields explained (1.5)
- ✅ Access code variants — CAC/DAC/IAC (1.5)
- ✅ List and purpose of packet types (2)

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (b) — FHSS Mechanism:**

**Concept:**
Bluetooth operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** and employs **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** to combat interference and provide co-existence with other 2.4 GHz devices (Wi-Fi, microwaves, etc.). The band is divided into **79 RF channels** of 1 MHz each, spanning **2.402 GHz to 2.480 GHz**.

**Mechanism:**
- The Master device maintains a **28-bit free-running clock** and a unique **48-bit BD_ADDR**.
- A **selection kernel** generates a pseudo-random hop sequence using these values.
- The **piconet's hopping sequence** is determined by the **Master's clock and BD_ADDR**.
- All members of the piconet hop in **synchronization** to the same sequence; thus, any slave knows where the Master will be next.
- The hop rate is **1600 hops/second**, meaning the carrier frequency changes every **625 µs**.

**Derivation of Hopping Rate:**

Given slot duration $T_{\text{slot}} = 625\ \mu s = 625 \times 10^{-6}\ s$:

$$
f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slot}}} = \frac{1}{625 \times 10^{-6}} = 1600\ \text{Hops/second}
$$

**Time to Cycle All 79 Channels:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N_{\text{channels}} \times T_{\text{slot}} = 79 \times 625\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 49375\ \mu s = 49.375\ \text{ms}
$$

**Key Engineering Advantages:**
1. **Interference Rejection:** A narrowband jammer only corrupts 1 out of 79 channels momentarily.
2. **Co-existence with Wi-Fi:** Bluetooth avoids Wi-Fi's fixed channel by hopping across all 79.
3. **Security:** The pseudo-random sequence is hard to predict externally.

**Key points (7 marks):**
- ✅ FHSS concept + 79 channels + 1 MHz (1.5)
- ✅ Hop sequence generation (clock + BD_ADDR) (1.5)
- ✅ Derivation of hop rate (1600 Hz) (2)
- ✅ Derivation of cycle time (49.375 ms) (1.5)
- ✅ Two advantages (0.5)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Bluetooth/PAN Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong Max Slave Count:** Students often write "8 slaves" — the correct answer is **7 ACTIVE slaves** (1 master + 7 slaves = 8 total active nodes, 3-bit AM_ADDR with `000` reserved for broadcast).
> 2. **Confusing Hop Rate and Data Rate:** $f_{\text{hop}} = 1600$ Hz refers to **channel changes per second**, NOT data symbols/second. The basic data rate is **1 Mbps**, not 1600 bps!
> 3. **Mixing SCO and ACL:** **SCO** is for **synchronous voice** (no ARQ), **ACL** is for **asynchronous data** (uses ARQ). Confusing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Standard:** Bluetooth Classic is **IEEE 802.15.1**; BLE was folded into **802.15.1** then later maintained by Bluetooth SIG. Zigbee is **IEEE 802.15.4** — not Bluetooth. Wi-Fi is **IEEE 802.11** — not a PAN.
> 5. **Skipping the Inquiry-Page sequence:** Always mention both **Inquiry** (discover) and **Paging** (connect) steps with packet types (ID, FHS, DAC). Forgetting one costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always write **µs** (not "us"), **dBm** (not dB), **Mbps** (not MB/s).
> 7. **L2CAP MTU:** Students often omit that L2CAP **segments** large packets; the default MTU is 672 bytes (negotiable up to 64 KB).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Bluetooth and PAN**

- ⭐ **PAN Definition:** Short-range (<10 m) network centered on an individual. **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)**, Zigbee (802.15.4), IrDA, NFC, UWB are all PAN/WPAN technologies.

- ⭐ **Bluetooth History:** Named after King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark; **invented by Jaap Haartsen** (Ericsson, 1994); governed by **Bluetooth SIG** (Ericsson, IBM, Intel, Nokia, Toshiba).

- ⭐ **Physical Layer (Radio):** Operates in **2.4 GHz ISM band**; **79 channels × 1 MHz**; **1600 hops/second**; **GFSK modulation** for Basic Rate (1 Mbps); **π/4-DQPSK** for 2 Mbps EDR; **8-DPSK** for 3 Mbps EDR.

- ⭐ **Hop Rate Derivation:** $f_{\text{hop}} = 1 / T_{\text{slot}} = 1 / 625\ \mu s = 1600$ Hz. **Cycle time for 79 channels = 49.375 ms**.

- ⭐ **Piconet Topology:** 1 Master + **7 Active Slaves** (3-bit AM_ADDR, `000` = broadcast). Each slave has a unique AM_ADDR.

- ⭐ **Scatternet:** A device that is **Master in one piconet** and **Slave in another**, allowing multi-piconet interconnection.

- ⭐ **Piconet Operation:** Master transmits in **even-numbered slots**; slaves transmit in **odd-numbered slots** (TDD). Master controls channel access via **polling**.

- ⭐ **Connection States:** **Standby → Inquiry → Paging → Connection** → (Active / Hold / Sniff / Park).

- ⭐ **Key Packet Types:** **ID** (inquiry/paging), **FHS** (clock + address exchange), **NULL** (flow control), **POLL** (master poll), **DM/DH** (data), **HV** (voice).

- ⭐ **Baseband Packet Format:** Access Code (72 bits) + Header (54 bits) + Payload (0–2745 bits). Header contains AM_ADDR, Type, ARQN, SEQN, HEC.

- ⭐ **Protocol Stack (bottom-up):** Radio → Baseband → LMP → HCI → L2CAP → SDP → RFCOMM → Application Profiles.

- ⭐ **L2CAP Functions:** Multiplexing (multiple higher protocols on one ACL), Segmentation & Reassembly, QoS flow spec. Default MTU = 672 bytes (max 64 KB).

- ⭐ **LMP Functions:** Link setup, authentication, encryption (E0 stream cipher + SAFER+ for key generation), power control, role switch (master/slave swap).

- ⭐ **Link Types:** **ACL** = asynchronous, data, packet-switched, supports ARQ; **SCO** = synchronous, voice, reserved slots, no ARQ.

- ⭐ **Power Classes:** Class 1 = 100 mW (~100 m), Class 2 = 2.5 mW (~10 m), Class 3 = 1 mW (~1 m). **Class 2 is most common** in mobile devices.

- ⭐ **Device Address:** **48-bit BD_ADDR** divided into NAP (16) + UAP (8) + LAP (24). Used in paging and hop sequence.

- ⭐ **Bluetooth Versions:** 1.0 (1 Mbps BR) → 2.0+EDR (3 Mbps) → 3.0+HS (uses Wi-Fi for data) → **4.0 BLE** (low energy) → 5.0 (2 Mbps, 200 m range, Mesh) → 5.1 (AoA/AoD direction finding) → 5.2 (LE Audio, LC3 codec) → 5.3/5.4.

- ⭐ **BLE Key Specs:** 40 channels (3 advertising + 37 data) at 2 MHz spacing; **GFSK**; **AES-128 CCM** encryption; connection interval 7.5 ms – 4 s; **latency-critical IoT** use case.

- ⭐ **Inquiry vs. Paging:**
  - **Inquiry:** 32 wake-up channels; uses **IAC** and **FHS** packets; purpose = discovery.
  - **Paging:** 32 of 79 channels; uses **DAC** derived from BD_ADDR; purpose = connection.

- ⭐ **Comparison Hooks for Exam:**
  - Bluetooth vs. Wi-Fi: BT = short range + low power, Wi-Fi = long range + high data rate.
  - Bluetooth vs. Zigbee: BT = audio/voice, Zigbee = sensor mesh.
  - Bluetooth vs. IrDA: BT = RF omnidirectional, IrDA = optical line-of-sight.

- ⭐ **Key Performance Numbers to Memorize:**
  - Hop rate = **1600 Hz**
  - Dwell time = **625 µs**
  - Max active slaves = **7**
  - BD_ADDR = **48 bits**
  - Clock = **28 bits**
  - BR = 1 Mbps, EDR = 2/3 Mbps, BLE = 1/2 Mbps
  - Class 2 range = **10 m**

- ⭐ **Vocabulary Synonyms Often Asked:**
  - "Frequency Hopping Rate" = Hop Rate
  - "Active Member Address" = AM_ADDR
  - "Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol" = L2CAP
  - "Device Access Code" = DAC (used in paging)
  - "Frequency Hop Synchronization" = FHS packet

- ⭐ **Syllabus Outcome Mapping (CO3 / K3 — Apply):** Be prepared to **apply** the FHSS formula to compute cycle times for arbitrary channel counts, **analyze** piconet/scatternet capacity, and **compare** Bluetooth with at least 2 other PAN technologies in a tabular form.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Multiple Access: FDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Multiple Access: FDMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Multiple Access?

In any practical communication system, the fundamental resource that is shared among multiple users is the **transmission medium** (the radio channel, satellite transponder, optical fiber, or copper cable). Since deploying a dedicated physical link between every pair of users is economically and physically infeasible, engineers have developed a family of techniques known collectively as **Multiple Access (MA)** schemes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Multiple Access is a set of techniques that allow a large number of users to share a common communication channel (or a finite pool of resources such as bandwidth, time slots, or code sequences) in such a way that each user perceives the channel as if it were dedicated to their own transmission, while the system achieves high overall spectral and power efficiency.*

The three classical multiple access techniques are:

| Technique | Resource Shared | Domain of Separation |
|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency Bandwidth | Frequency |
| **TDMA** | Time Slots | Time |
| **CDMA** | Unique Code Sequences | Code |

### 1.2 Introduction to FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *FDMA is a multiple access technique in which the total available transmission bandwidth of the communication channel is subdivided into a number of non-overlapping frequency sub-bands (channels), and each sub-band is assigned exclusively to a different user for the entire duration of the communication. Each user transmits simultaneously, but on their own unique carrier frequency.*

In FDMA, the word **"Division"** refers to the partitioning of the frequency spectrum (the horizontal axis of the frequency-time plane). The word **"Multiple"** refers to the simultaneous coexistence of many independent users.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radio Highway" Analogy

Imagine a long, wide multi-lane highway connecting two cities:

- The **total width** of the highway is the **total system bandwidth** (e.g., **36 MHz** for a standard C-band satellite transponder).
- The highway is divided into many **parallel narrow lanes**. Each lane is a **frequency channel** assigned to one user.
- Cars in lane 1 never collide with cars in lane 2, because they are physically separated — just as signals in one FDMA frequency slot never collide with signals in another, because their frequency spectra do not overlap.
- Between any two lanes, there is a small empty buffer strip called a **"guard band"**, which prevents cars from drifting into adjacent lanes and causing accidents.

This is exactly how FDMA works. Every user is permanently assigned their own lane (frequency), and they may transmit continuously for the entire duration of their call without ever "waiting" for a time slot.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Minimum Voice Channel Bandwidth in FDMA:** **30 kHz** (standard in analog cellular AMPS systems).
- **Total Cellular Uplink Band (Reverse Channel):** **25 MHz** in AMPS.
- **C-band Satellite Transponder Bandwidth:** **36 MHz** or **54 MHz** typical.
- **Standard Guard Band in FDMA:** **10 kHz** (AMPS).
- **FDMA family classifications:** Single-channel-per-carrier (SCPC) and Multi-channel-per-carrier (MCPC).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> Students often confuse *multiplexing* with *multiple access*. **Multiplexing** is performed at a single node for many users under one administrative control (e.g., telephone exchange). **Multiple Access** is performed by many geographically distributed independent users contending for a shared medium. FDMA can be viewed as the FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) principle applied to a multi-user access scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of FDMA — Step-by-Step Logic

The implementation of FDMA in any system (satellite, radio, or cellular) follows a strict sequence:

1. **Spectrum Allocation:** The regulatory authority (e.g., ITU, FCC, or WPC in India) assigns a finite total bandwidth $B_{\text{total}}$ to the system operator.
2. **Channel Subdivision:** This $B_{\text{total}}$ is divided into $N$ equal, non-overlapping sub-bands, each of bandwidth $B_c$ (the channel bandwidth).
3. **Guard Band Insertion:** To prevent adjacent-channel interference (ACI) caused by imperfect analog filters, a small protection band $B_g$ is inserted between every pair of adjacent channels.
4. **Carrier Assignment:** Each user $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, N$) is assigned a unique carrier frequency $f_i$ located at the center of their sub-band.
5. **Simultaneous Transmission:** All $N$ users transmit **continuously and simultaneously**. Time is **not** divided.
6. **Filtering at Receiver:** The receiver of user $i$ employs a sharply-tuned bandpass filter (BPF) centered at $f_i$ to extract only their own signal and reject all others.
7. **Continuous-Time Operation:** Because the assignment is fixed for the duration of the call/session, FDMA is inherently a **continuous** (not burst) transmission scheme.

### 2.2 Why is the "Guard Band" Necessary?

Real-world bandpass filters cannot have an infinitely sharp roll-off (a "brick-wall" response is physically unrealizable per the Paley–Wiener criterion). When a user modulates their carrier, some energy inevitably "spills" outside the nominal $B_c$ into adjacent frequencies. If two users' spectra touched, this spill-over would cause **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)**. The guard band $B_g$ acts as a **buffer zone** to absorb this leakage.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Cost of Guard Bands:**
> Guard bands are pure waste from a spectral-efficiency standpoint — they carry no user data. This is one of FDMA's main disadvantages and is the **single most common board-exam pitfall**: students forget to subtract the guard bands when calculating the number of channels.

### 2.3 Intermodulation Distortion (IMD) — The Hidden Enemy

In a non-linear power amplifier (e.g., the Traveling Wave Tube — TWT — in a satellite transponder, or the High-Power Amplifier — HPA — at a base station), when multiple FDMA carriers pass through simultaneously, their non-linear interaction produces spurious output products at frequencies given by $m f_i \pm n f_j$ (for integers $m, n$), called **intermodulation products**. The third-order products ($2f_i \pm f_j$) are particularly troublesome because they often fall back **within** the FDMA band and cannot be filtered out. This forces the HPA to operate in **back-off** mode (typically 3 to 6 dB below saturation), wasting DC power.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_{\text{total}} = N \cdot B_c + (N+1) \cdot B_g$ | Total system bandwidth with guard bands | Hz |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_c + B_g}$ | Number of FDMA channels (exact, including 1 edge guard) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N \approx \dfrac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_c + B_g}$ | Approximation when $B_g \ll B_c$ (used in exams) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $B_{\text{useful}} = N \cdot B_c$ | Total useful bandwidth carrying data | Hz |
| 5 | $\eta_s = \dfrac{N \cdot B_c}{B_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{B_{\text{useful}}}{B_{\text{total}}}$ | Spectral Efficiency | unitless (0 to 1) |
| 6 | $\text{Capacity}_i = B_c \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}_i\right)$ | Shannon capacity of one FDMA user (bps) | bits/sec |
| 7 | $C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_c \log_2(1+\text{SNR}_i)$ | Sum capacity of the FDMA system | bits/sec |
| 8 | $\text{ACI} \propto \dfrac{1}{B_g}$ | Adjacent Channel Interference vs. guard band | qualitative |
| 9 | $\text{SINR}_i = \dfrac{P_i}{N_0 B_c + \sum_{j \ne i} P_j \cdot \alpha_{ij}}$ | Effective SINR for FDMA user (with imperfect filtering $\alpha_{ij}$) | linear scale |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **First-generation (1G) Analog Cellular:** The AMPS system used FDMA with 30 kHz voice channels across a 25 MHz band, yielding 416 channels per operator.
- **Satellite Communications (VSAT, INTELSAT):** A 36 MHz C-band transponder is divided into hundreds of SCPC (Single Channel Per Carrier) FDMA carriers for thin-route telephony.
- **FM Broadcast Radio (88–108 MHz):** Each station occupies a 200 kHz FDMA channel.
- **Cable TV (CATV):** Each TV channel is a 6 MHz (NTSC) or 8 MHz (PAL) FDMA sub-band on the coaxial cable.
- **Two-Way Land Mobile Radio (PMR, LMR):** Police, taxi, and military radios use narrowband FDMA.
- **Power Line Communication (PLC):** Some low-frequency OFDM-prime systems use FDMA-like allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key "Why" — Why did 2G GSM move AWAY from pure FDMA?**
> Because FDMA is **bandwidth-inefficient** (guard bands waste spectrum), **inflexible** (a silent user still occupies their channel), and **non-linear-amplifier-hostile** (intermodulation). GSM therefore adopted a hybrid **FDMA/TDMA** approach: first dividing the 25 MHz band into 124 FDMA carriers (200 kHz each), and then time-slicing each carrier among 8 users.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of FDMA Channels in a Given Bandwidth

**Problem Statement:** A communication system is allocated a total spectrum $B_{\text{total}}$. Each user requires a channel of bandwidth $B_c$. To prevent adjacent-channel interference, a guard band of width $B_g$ is reserved between every pair of adjacent channels (and one extra guard at each edge of the allocated band). Derive the maximum number of users $N$ that can be simultaneously supported.

**Step 1 — Visualize the spectrum allocation.**
The total spectrum is laid out as: Guard — Ch1 — Guard — Ch2 — Guard — Ch3 — … — Guard — Ch $N$ — Guard. There are $N$ channels and $(N+1)$ guard bands.

**Step 2 — Express the total bandwidth used.**
The total occupied bandwidth is the sum of the channel bandwidths plus the sum of the guard bands.

$$
B_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{N \cdot B_c}_{\text{all channels}} + \underbrace{(N+1) \cdot B_g}_{\text{all guards}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $N$.**
Expand and group terms containing $N$ on the right-hand side.

$$
B_{\text{total}} = N \cdot B_c + N \cdot B_g + B_g
$$

Factor out $N$ on the right.

$$
B_{\text{total}} = N \cdot (B_c + B_g) + B_g
$$

Subtract $B_g$ from both sides.

$$
B_{\text{total}} - B_g = N \cdot (B_c + B_g)
$$

Divide both sides by $(B_c + B_g)$.

$$
N = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_c + B_g}
$$

This is the **exact** expression. Boxed result:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{\text{exact}} = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_c + B_g}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximation for typical exam scenarios.**
If $B_g \ll B_c$ and $B_g \ll B_{\text{total}}$ (the usual case), we may approximate the edge guard as negligible, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{\text{approx}} \approx \dfrac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_c + B_g}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU board valuation style.**
Given: $B_{\text{total}} = 25$ MHz, $B_c = 30$ kHz, $B_g = 10$ kHz.

$$
N_{\text{approx}} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{3} + 10 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{6}}{40 \times 10^{3}} = 625 \text{ channels}
$$

Accounting for the edge guard, the textbook value is 416 (this is because the AMPS system uses 832 half-rate channels, divided between two operators, etc.). The simplified exam formula gives a quick first-order estimate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectral Efficiency of FDMA

**Step 1 — Define Spectral Efficiency.**
Spectral efficiency $\eta_s$ is the ratio of the bandwidth that carries **information** to the total bandwidth **allocated**.

$$
\eta_s = \dfrac{\text{Useful bandwidth}}{\text{Total bandwidth}} = \dfrac{N \cdot B_c}{B_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the exact expression for $N$.**

$$
\eta_s = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_c + B_g}\right) \cdot B_c}{B_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\eta_s = \dfrac{B_c \,(B_{\text{total}} - B_g)}{B_{\text{total}} \,(B_c + B_g)} = \dfrac{B_c}{B_c + B_g} \cdot \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $B_g \to 0$ (idealized FDMA).**

$$
\lim_{B_g \to 0} \eta_s = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \;(100\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Practical limit.**
With $B_g = 10$ kHz and $B_c = 30$ kHz, the inner factor is $\frac{30}{40} = 0.75$, and the outer factor $\frac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_{\text{total}}} \approx 1$. So $\eta_s \approx 75\%$, meaning **25% of the licensed spectrum is wasted as guard bands**. This is the single largest motivation for switching to TDMA/CDMA in 2G and beyond.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Capacity Allocation in FDMA

In an FDMA system, the total bandwidth $B_{\text{total}}$ is split into $N$ equal slices of width $B_c = B_{\text{total}} / N$. Each user $i$ transmits with power $P_i$ over an AWGN channel of noise PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Shannon's formula for one user.**

$$
C_i = B_c \, \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{P_i}{N_0 B_c}\right) \text{ bits/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total FDMA system capacity (assuming equal-power users $P_i = P_{\text{total}} / N$).**

$$
C_{\text{FDMA}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_c \, \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{total}} / N}{N_0 B_c}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Since all $N$ terms are identical:**

$$
C_{\text{FDMA}} = N \cdot B_c \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{total}} / N}{N_0 B_c}\right) = B_{\text{total}} \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{total}}}{N_0 \, B_{\text{total}}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Critical observation.**
The right-hand side is **independent of $N$**. This means: in a **single-cell, single-antenna, no-fading, no-ICI** AWGN channel, splitting bandwidth among many FDMA users gives the **same total Shannon capacity** as giving the entire bandwidth to one user. The "FDMA vs. single-user" comparison thus hinges entirely on **practical impairments**: guard bands, pilot overhead, amplifier back-off, and ICI.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages of FDMA

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplicity** | No synchronization in time domain; receivers use simple analog BPFs | — |
| **Continuous transmission** | Efficient for constant-bit-rate voice (no bursts needed) | — |
| **Latency** | Very low latency (no waiting for time slot) | — |
| **Spectral Efficiency** | — | Low: guard bands waste 10–30% of licensed spectrum |
| **Flexibility** | — | Inelastic: a silent user still occupies their band |
| **Power Amplifier** | — | Must operate in back-off (3–6 dB) to avoid IMD → reduced efficiency |
| **Capacity (cells)** | — | Susceptible to co-channel interference (CCI) in cellular reuse |
| **Hardware** | Simple per-user filters | Requires sharp, stable filters (drift = ACI) |
| **Fading robustness** | Per-channel equalization is simple | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
