<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define FDMA. Mention any two advantages and two disadvantages of FDMA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access) is a multiple access technique in which the available channel bandwidth is divided into a number of non-overlapping frequency sub-bands, and each sub-band is assigned exclusively to an individual user for simultaneous transmission. The signals are separated in the **frequency domain** using sharply-tuned bandpass filters at the receiver.

> **Any Two Advantages (1 mark each, 2 marks total):**
> 1. **Simplicity of implementation** — No time synchronization is required between users; receivers use simple analog bandpass filters to extract their own signal.
> 2. **Continuous transmission** — Each user transmits continuously without bursts, making FDMA ideal for real-time voice and constant-bit-rate circuits.
> 3. **Low latency** — No waiting for a time slot; suitable for delay-sensitive applications.
> 4. **Robust to slow fading** — Narrowband channels experience flat fading only.

> **Any Two Disadvantages (1 mark each, 2 marks total — adjust as needed):**
> 1. **Wastage of bandwidth** — Guard bands between channels consume 10–30% of the licensed spectrum, reducing spectral efficiency.
> 2. **Intermodulation distortion** — Multiple carriers through a non-linear HPA produce IMD products that fall in-band; this forces the amplifier to operate in back-off, reducing DC-to-RF efficiency.
> 3. **Inflexibility** — A silent user still occupies their assigned frequency band, leading to underutilization.
> 4. **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)** — Imperfect filters cause spill-over between adjacent channels.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Why are guard bands provided between adjacent FDMA channels? What happens if guard bands are not provided?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason for guard bands (1.5 marks):**
> Real-world bandpass filters do not have an ideal "brick-wall" frequency response. When a user modulates their carrier, the resulting signal spectrum extends slightly beyond the nominal channel bandwidth $B_c$ into adjacent frequencies. Guard bands $B_g$ are empty frequency buffers inserted between adjacent channels to absorb this spectral leakage and prevent **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)**.

> **Consequence of no guard band (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Energy from one user's modulated signal would spill over and corrupt the adjacent user's signal, causing severe **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)** and an unacceptable rise in the Bit Error Rate (BER).
> 2. The system would become extremely sensitive to small drifts in carrier frequency (caused by oscillator instability or Doppler shift), since even a few hundred Hz of drift could shift the modulated spectrum into the neighbor's band.
> 3. It would require the use of impractically sharp (and hence physically unrealizable) filters with near-infinite Q-factor.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an FDMA system. List any **three key features** of FDMA. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A satellite transponder has a total bandwidth of **36 MHz**. It is required to support FDMA voice channels each of bandwidth **45 kHz**, with a guard band of **5 kHz** between adjacent channels.
  (i) Calculate the maximum number of voice channels that can be accommodated.
  (ii) Calculate the spectral efficiency of the system.
  (iii) Comment on how the spectral efficiency would change if the guard band were reduced to **2 kHz**, assuming the filter technology could handle it. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation and Features (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the labelled block diagram. The marks are distributed as follows:

- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Show the three user modulators, the summing combiner, the HPA, the channel, the LNA, the down-converter, the per-user BPFs, and the demodulators — clearly labelled.
- **Working explanation (2 marks):** Describe how each user's baseband signal modulates a unique sub-carrier $f_i$, the combined composite FDM signal upconverts to RF, passes through the HPA (in back-off to control IMD), and is transmitted. At the receiver, after downconversion, a per-user BPF centered at $f_i$ extracts only that user's signal.
- **Three key features (2 marks — list any three with one-line justification):**
  1. **Frequency-domain separation** of users using sharp bandpass filters.
  2. **Simultaneous and continuous** transmission by all users.
  3. **Static allocation** — each user retains their frequency for the call duration.
  4. **In-band signalling** must be placed below the lowest voice channel (e.g., below 4 kHz in AMPS).
  5. **Narrowband channels** experience flat multipath fading only.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$B_{\text{total}} = 36 \text{ MHz} = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
$B_c = 45 \text{ kHz} = 45 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$
$B_g = 5 \text{ kHz} = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$

**(i) Maximum number of channels (3 marks):**

Using the exact formula from Section 3.1:

$$
N = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_c + B_g}
$$

Substitute the values.

$$
N = \dfrac{36 \times 10^{6} - 5 \times 10^{3}}{45 \times 10^{3} + 5 \times 10^{3}}
$$

The numerator: $36 \times 10^{6} - 5 \times 10^{3} = 35{,}995{,}000$ Hz.

The denominator: $45{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 50{,}000$ Hz.

$$
N = \dfrac{35{,}995{,}000}{50{,}000} = 719.9
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer and we cannot have a fraction of a channel, take the floor:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{\max} = 719 \text{ voice channels}\,}
$$

**(ii) Spectral Efficiency (2 marks):**

The useful bandwidth is:

$$
B_{\text{useful}} = N \cdot B_c = 719 \times 45 \times 10^{3} = 32{,}355{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 32.355 \text{ MHz}
$$

The spectral efficiency is:

$$
\eta_s = \dfrac{B_{\text{useful}}}{B_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{32.355 \text{ MHz}}{36 \text{ MHz}} = 0.8988
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_s \approx 89.9\% \approx 90\%\,}
$$

Alternatively, using the closed-form expression:

$$
\eta_s = \dfrac{B_c}{B_c + B_g} \cdot \dfrac{B_{\text{total}} - B_g}{B_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{45}{50} \cdot \dfrac{35.995}{36} = 0.9 \times 0.99986 \approx 0.8999
$$

**(iii) Effect of reducing $B_g$ to 2 kHz (2 marks):**

Recompute $N$:

$$
N' = \dfrac{36 \times 10^{6} - 2 \times 10^{3}}{45 \times 10^{3} + 2 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{35{,}998{,}000}{47{,}000} \approx 765.9
$$

Take the floor: $N' = 765$ channels.

New useful bandwidth: $765 \times 45 \text{ kHz} = 34.425 \text{ MHz}$.

New spectral efficiency:

$$
\eta_s' = \dfrac{34.425}{36} \approx 0.9563 \; \text{or} \; 95.6\%
$$

**Comment (verbal, 1 mark included above):** Reducing $B_g$ from 5 kHz to 2 kHz **increases** the number of channels from 719 to 765 (a gain of 46 channels) and improves spectral efficiency from ≈90% to ≈96%. However, this gain is achieved **only if** the channel filters are sharp enough to confine each user's energy within $B_c + 2$ kHz, which requires more sophisticated and expensive SAW or digital filters and tighter carrier-frequency stability standards (lower oscillator phase noise).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** with the help of a comparison table covering at least **six parameters**: resource shared, synchronization requirement, guard band/slot, vulnerability to interference, typical application, and spectral efficiency. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI)** in FDMA. Show mathematically how the choice of guard-band width $B_g$ affects the Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) for a user located in the middle of the FDMA band. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource shared** | Frequency band | Time slot | Code sequence |
| **Synchronization** | None in time; frequency synchronization only | Strict time synchronization (frame & slot) | Strict time + code synchronization |
| **Guard mechanism** | Frequency guard band $B_g$ | Time guard slot | Power control (no hard guard) |
| **Vulnerability to interference** | Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) | Inter-symbol & co-channel in time | Multiple Access Interference (MAI) |
| **Typical application** | 1G AMPS cellular, FM radio, satellite SCPC | 2G GSM, DECT | 3G WCDMA, IS-95 |
| **Spectral efficiency** | Low (guard bands waste spectrum) | Medium (overhead per slot) | High (soft capacity) |
| **HPA linearity** | Must back-off (IMD) | Can operate near saturation (single carrier per slot) | Must be linear (IMD) |
| **User data rate** | Fixed per channel | Variable per slot allocation | Variable via spreading factor |

(Provide any 6 of the above rows for full 7 marks — 1 mark per relevant row + 1 mark for overall presentation.)

---

**Part (b) — ACI Concept and SIR Analysis (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph b["Adjacent User Spectrum Leakage"]
        ua["User i Bc wide band"]
        ub["User i plus 1 Bc wide band"]
        ogb["Guard band Bg between them"]
        leak["Out of band spectral leakage from User i into Guard band"]
    end
    ua --- ogb --- ub
    leak -.-> ogb
```

**SIR Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a user $i$ whose BPF has centre frequency $f_i$ and bandwidth $B_c$. Due to imperfect roll-off, the filter still admits a fraction $\alpha$ of the adjacent user's power $P_{i+1}$, where $\alpha$ decreases as $B_g$ increases.

**Step 2:** The signal power received from user $i$ is $P_i$. The interference power from the adjacent user leaking through the guard band is approximately:

$$
I_{\text{ACI}} \approx P_{i+1} \cdot \alpha(B_g)
$$

**Step 3:** For a first-order filter roll-off, the leakage fraction decays exponentially with the normalized guard-band width:

$$
\alpha(B_g) \approx K \cdot e^{-k \cdot B_g / B_c}
$$

where $K$ and $k$ are filter-dependent constants.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the Signal-to-Interference Ratio for user $i$ is:

$$
\text{SIR}_i(B_g) = \dfrac{P_i}{I_{\text{ACI}}} = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{i+1} \cdot K \, e^{-k B_g / B_c}} = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{i+1} \cdot K} \cdot e^{+k B_g / B_c}
$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm to linearize:

$$
\ln \text{SIR}_i = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_i}{K \, P_{i+1}}\right) + \dfrac{k \, B_g}{B_c}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The SIR grows **exponentially** with the guard-band width $B_g$. Doubling $B_g$ adds a constant $\frac{k}{B_c} \ln 2 \approx 0.69 \, k / B_c$ nats to the log-SIR (equivalently, $\approx 3$ dB per guard-band doubling for $k \approx 1.4$ in a typical 2nd-order Butterworth). The trade-off is therefore: **larger $B_g$ → better SIR but fewer channels; smaller $B_g$ → more channels but worse SIR.** This is the classic **spectral-efficiency vs. link-quality trade-off** in FDMA design.

---

### 5.X KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in FDMA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the guard bands** in the denominator of the channel-count formula — always use $B_c + B_g$, not just $B_c$.
> 2. **Confusing $B_c$ and $B_{\text{channel}}$** with the *modulation* bandwidth; $B_c$ is the **allocated channel slot** (e.g., 30 kHz in AMPS), which is wider than the voice bandwidth (3 kHz) due to FM deviation.
> 3. **Ignoring the edge guard band** — the formula has $N+1$ guards, not $N-1$.
> 4. **Writing "FDMA = Multiplexing"** — this is wrong. FDMA is multiple access, performed by distributed users; FDM is multiplexing, performed at one node.
> 5. **Not stating the role of the HPA back-off** when discussing IMD — board examiners expect this phrase explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up analog and digital FDMA** — pure FDMA is rare in modern digital systems; GSM is actually **FDMA + TDMA**, and LTE uses **OFDMA** (orthogonal FDMA with no guard bands between sub-carriers, only a cyclic prefix in time).
> 7. **Unit conversion errors** — convert MHz and kHz to a common unit (Hz) **before** dividing; do not mix units inside the formula.

---

### 5.Y Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **FDMA = Frequency Division Multiple Access.** Users are separated in **frequency**; all transmit **simultaneously and continuously**.
- **Channel-count formula:** $N = (B_{\text{total}} - B_g) / (B_c + B_g)$. Always subtract edge guard; always add guard to channel width in denominator.
- **Guard bands** prevent Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI); they waste spectrum.
- **Spectral efficiency** of FDMA is $\eta_s = N \cdot B_c / B_{\text{total}} \leq 1$; typically 70–90% in practice.
- **Intermodulation (IMD)** is a critical impairment: the HPA must operate in **back-off** (3–6 dB) below saturation, sacrificing power efficiency.
- **Static allocation** makes FDMA **inflexible**: silent users still consume bandwidth.
- **AMPS (1G):** 30 kHz channels, 10 kHz guards, 25 MHz total → ~416 channels per operator.
- **GSM (2G):** Hybrid FDMA/TDMA — 124 FDMA carriers × 8 TDMA users = ~1000 users per operator.
- **OFDMA (4G LTE):** Eliminates frequency-domain guard bands via orthogonality, but introduces cyclic prefix in time.
- **FDMA is used in:** 1G cellular, satellite SCPC systems, FM radio, cable TV, walkie-talkies, and as a building block in hybrid schemes.
- **Shannon capacity of FDMA** equals the single-user bound in an ideal AWGN channel without ICI — practical differences arise only from guard bands, IMD, and ACI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TDMA ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** is a channel-access method for shared-medium networks which allows several users to share the same frequency channel by dividing the signal into **different time slots**. Each user transmits in rapid succession, one after the other, each using its own uniquely assigned **time slot** within a recurring **frame structure**. In TDMA, the available **transmission bandwidth** of a communications channel is partitioned into a sequence of contiguous **time frames**, and each frame is further subdivided into a fixed number of **time slots** (typically 2 to 16 per frame). A particular user is assigned one or more of these slots per frame, during which it has exclusive use of the entire channel bandwidth to transmit its information burst. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TDMA is a *multiple access technique* in which multiple users share a common *transmission medium* by transmitting at *different times* but at the *same carrier frequency*. It is fundamentally different from **FDMA** (frequency-based sharing) and **CDMA** (code-based sharing). Mathematically, the TDMA concept can be expressed as: $$x_{TDMA}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_k(t) \cdot g_k(t)$$ where $s_k(t)$ is the $k^{th}$ user's signal and $g_k(t)$ is a rectangular gating function equal to **1** during the $k^{th}$ user's time slot and **0** otherwise. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **round-table classroom discussion** with **5 students** and a single microphone. The teacher enforces a strict rule: each student gets exactly **one fixed time slot** (say 1 minute) to speak, and they must wait for their turn to come around again in the next cycle. - The **microphone channel** = the common frequency band - The **1-minute slot** = a TDMA time slot - The **full cycle of 5 minutes** = one TDMA frame - The **teacher's stopwatch** = the system clock / synchronization mechanism Even though only **one student speaks at a time**, everyone gets heard periodically without any of them fighting for the microphone. The "wasted" idle time of each speaker is the **price** paid for orderly access — a key trade-off in TDMA. > [!NOTE] > **Key Real-World Examples of TDMA:** > - **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** — 2G cellular standard, uses 8 time slots per TDMA frame. > - **IS-54 / IS-136** — North American 2G digital cellular (D-AMPS). > - **DECT** (Digital Enhanced Cordless Telecommunications) — cordless phones. > - **TETRA** — Terrestrial Trunked Radio for emergency services. > - **Satellite systems** like **INTELSAT** and **VSAT** networks. ### 1.3 Why TDMA? (Engineering Motivation) 1. **Efficient bandwidth utilization:** Each user accesses the *entire* bandwidth during their slot, allowing high burst rates. 2. **Flexibility:** Slots can be dynamically reallocated based on traffic demand (unlike pure FDMA). 3. **Digital compatibility:** Naturally suited to digital modulation and digital signal processing. 4. **No intermodulation products:** Unlike FDMA, a single carrier is used at any instant, eliminating nonlinear amplifier distortion issues. 5. **Easy integration with digital switching and time-division multiplexing (TDM) hierarchies.** ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Must-Know Values) - **GSM frame period:** $T_{frame} = 4.615$ **ms** (approx. 4.62 ms) - **Number of slots per GSM frame:** $N_s = 8$ - **GSM slot duration:** $\approx 0.577$ **ms** (577 μs) - **GSM carrier spacing:** $200$ **kHz** - **GSM bit rate per user:** $270.833$ **kbps** - **Number of TDMA frames in a multiframe (GSM):** 26 (traffic) or 51 (control) - **Number of slots per multiframe:** $26 \times 8 = 208$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle TDMA operates on a **time-shared basis** within a single carrier frequency. The system's transmission time is partitioned cyclically into **frames**, and each frame is partitioned into **N time slots**. A user is granted *exclusive* access to the channel bandwidth for the duration of its assigned slot(s). **Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic):** - **Step 1 — Frame Definition:** The system time is divided into repeating units called *frames* of duration $T_f$. - **Step 2 — Slot Subdivision:** Each frame is divided into $N$ equal *time slots* of duration $T_s = T_f / N$. - **Step 3 — User Assignment:** Each of the $M$ users is assigned a unique slot index (or set of indices). - **Step 4 — Burst Transmission:** During its slot, the user transmits a *burst* of data — preamble + information + guard. - **Step 5 — Idle State:** Outside the assigned slot, the user's transmitter is OFF, and only the receiver may be active. - **Step 6 — Synchronization:** All users and the base station must be precisely synchronized to avoid overlap. **Synchronization** is typically maintained by transmitting a *beacon* or *pilot* signal. ### 2.2 Anatomy of a TDMA Burst Each time slot carries a structured **burst** that is NOT entirely user data. The structure is: 1. **Preamble (Training Sequence):** A known bit pattern used for *synchronization*, *channel estimation*, and *equalization*. Length: typically **26 bits** in GSM. 2. **Information Bits:** The actual user payload. Length: typically **148 bits** in GSM (two 57-bit blocks with a stealing flag in between, plus coding). 3. **Guard Period (Guard Time):** An idle interval at the end of the slot to prevent overlap due to *propagation delay* variations and *timing jitter*. Length: **8.25 bit periods** in GSM (≈ 30.5 μs). 4. **Tail/Ramp Bits:** A few bits at the start and end to allow transmitter ramp-up and ramp-down. ### 2.3 Frame Hierarchy (GSM-Style) GSM uses a **4-level frame hierarchy** to organize TDMA structure: | Hierarchy Level | Duration | Composition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time Slot (Burst period)** | 0.577 ms | 156.25 bit periods | | **TDMA Frame** | 4.615 ms | 8 time slots | | **Multiframe** | 120 ms (26-frame) / 235.4 ms (51-frame) | 26 or 51 frames | | **Superframe** | 6.12 s | 26 multiframes (or 51 multiframes) | | **Hyperframe** | 3 h 28 m 53 s 760 ms | 2048 superframes | ### 2.4 Duplexing Methods: TDD vs FDD in TDMA TDMA can be combined with either duplexing method: - **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex):** Uplink and downlink use *different* frequencies. Both directions are typically still TDMA. Used in GSM. - **TDD (Time Division Duplex):** Uplink and downlink use the *same* frequency but in *different time slots* of the same frame. Used in DECT. In TDD-TDMA, the frame is split into two halves: a **downlink subframe** and an **uplink subframe**, with users transmitting/receiving alternately. ### 2.5 TDMA Efficiency (η) The **efficiency** of a TDMA system is the ratio of *useful payload time* to the *total slot time*, expressed as: $$\eta_{TDMA} = \frac{T_{info}}{T_{slot}} \times 100\%$$ For GSM with 156.25 bit periods per slot and 148 information bits per slot: $$\eta_{TDMA} = \frac{148}{156.25} \times 100\% \approx 94.7\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why efficiency is < 100%:** The "lost" 5.3% accounts for the *guard period* (8.25 bits), *training sequence* (26 bits), and *tail/ramp bits* (≈ 3 bits) needed for synchronization and to prevent inter-slot interference. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Typical Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Slot duration** | $T_s = T_f / N$ | Time per slot per frame | seconds (s) | | **Bit rate per user** | $R_b = N_{info} / T_s$ | Information rate per user | bits/s (bps) | | **Frame duration** | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | Total frame period | seconds (s) | | **Number of users per frame** | $M \leq N$ | Maximum simultaneous users | dimensionless | | **TDMA efficiency** | $\eta = (N_{info} / N_{total}) \times 100\%$ | Payload ratio | % | | **Total system bit rate** | $R_{sys} = 1 / T_b$ | Channel bit rate (inverse of bit period) | bps | | **Carrier spacing (FDD)** | $B_c = 2 \cdot B_{signal} + B_{guard,band}$ | Bandwidth per RF channel | Hz | | **Multipath delay tolerance** | $T_g \geq 2 \cdot d_{max} / c$ | Minimum guard time | seconds | | **Doppler tolerance** | $f_d = v / \lambda$ | Maximum Doppler shift | Hz | | **Max user velocity (GSM)** | $v_{max} = 250$ **km/h** | Specified by standard | km/h | | **GSM total bits per slot** | 156.25 | Bit periods in 0.577 ms | bit periods | | **GSM info bits per slot** | 148 | User payload | bits | | **GSM training sequence** | 26 | Equalizer training bits | bits | | **GSM guard period** | 8.25 | Anti-overlap bits | bit periods | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** These formulas are used in radio network planning, capacity dimensioning, and link budget analysis. For example, given a cell with bandwidth $B_{cell}$ and carrier spacing $B_c$, the number of simultaneous RF carriers is $\lfloor B_{cell}/B_c \rfloor$, and total capacity becomes $N_{carriers} \times N_{slots/carrier} \times R_b$. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Trade-offs - **High peak power:** Because each user transmits in a compressed burst, the *instantaneous* transmit power is $N$ times the *average* power. Battery-powered devices must handle this. - **Strict synchronization:** Even microsecond-level misalignment can cause inter-slot interference. - **Susceptibility to multipath:** The high bit rate per burst makes equalization mandatory (GSM uses a Viterbi equalizer with 6 taps). - **Latency:** A user must wait up to one full frame (4.615 ms in GSM) to send the next packet — a constraint for delay-sensitive traffic. - **Cell capacity:** In GSM, frequency reuse factor of $1/3$ to $1/7$ is typical (vs. $1$ in CDMA), due to TDMA's need for clean slot boundaries. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: TDMA Frame Duration from First Principles **Problem:** A GSM system uses 8 time slots per frame. Each time slot contains **156.25 bit periods**, and the channel bit rate is **270.833 kbps**. Derive the frame duration $T_f$. **Step 1 — Write the bit period relationship.** The bit period $T_b$ is the inverse of the bit rate $R_b$: $$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{270.833 \times 10^3} \text{ s}$$ Evaluating: $$T_b = \frac{1}{270.833 \times 10^3} = 3.6923 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 3.692 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the slot duration.** A slot contains 156.25 bit periods: $$T_s = 156.25 \times T_b = 156.25 \times 3.6923 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$T_s = 5.769 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 0.577 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the frame duration.** With $N = 8$ slots per frame: $$T_f = N \times T_s = 8 \times 0.577 \text{ ms} = 4.615 \text{ ms}$$ **Result (Boxed for KTU):** $$\boxed{T_f = \frac{N \times 156.25}{R_b} = 4.615 \text{ ms}}$$ This matches the GSM specification exactly — a classic KTU exam question. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: TDMA Efficiency **Problem:** A TDMA system has a slot of 156.25 bit periods, of which 26 bits are training, 8.25 bits are guard time, 3 bits are tail/ramp, and the remaining are information. Compute the efficiency. **Step 1 — Sum the overhead bits.** $$N_{overhead} = 26 + 8.25 + 3 = 37.25 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the information bits.** $$N_{info} = 156.25 - 37.25 = 119 \text{ bits}$$ *(Note: In standard GSM there are additional *stealing flags*; this simplified version treats the residual as data.)* **Step 3 — Compute the efficiency ratio.** $$\eta = \frac{N_{info}}{N_{total}} = \frac{119}{156.25}$$ $$\eta = 0.7616$$ $$\boxed{\eta = 76.16\%}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Required Guard Time from Maximum Cell Radius **Problem:** A TDMA cell has a maximum radius of $R = 35$ **km**. Determine the minimum guard time $T_g$ required to prevent uplink overlap between a near-mobile (at $R_1 = 1$ km) and a far-mobile (at $R_2 = 35$ km). **Step 1 — Compute the propagation delay difference.** The two-way propagation delay difference between the far and near user as seen by the base station is: $$\Delta t = \frac{2 \cdot (R_2 - R_1)}{c}$$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** is the speed of light. Substituting values (with $R$ in meters): $$\Delta t = \frac{2 \times (35000 - 1000)}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{2 \times 34000}{3 \times 10^8}$$ $$\Delta t = \frac{68000}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.267 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 226.7 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2 — Determine the required guard time.** The guard time must satisfy: $$T_g \geq \Delta t$$ $$\boxed{T_g \geq 226.7 \text{ μs}}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to bit periods at GSM bit rate.** $$N_{g,bits} = T_g \times R_b = 226.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 270.833 \times 10^3$$ $$N_{g,bits} = 61.4 \text{ bits}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The standard GSM guard time of only 8.25 bits (≈ 30.5 μs) implies GSM cells are designed with a **maximum radius of about 35 km** only if the network uses *timing advance* — a mechanism by which the base station instructs each mobile to *preemptively advance* its transmission to align bursts at the base station receiver. This is why GSM is limited to a maximum cell range of ~35 km, while satellite TDMA (e.g., VSAT) requires longer guard times. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Total System Capacity (Carriers × Slots × Bit Rate) **Problem:** A GSM operator is allocated a total spectrum block of $B_{total} = 4.8$ **MHz** in the 900 MHz band. Given the carrier spacing of $B_c = 200$ **kHz**, compute: - (a) The number of RF carriers - (b) The maximum number of simultaneous users per cell - (c) The aggregate cell capacity in **kbps** **Step 1 — Compute number of RF carriers.** $$N_c = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_c} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4.8 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} \right\rfloor$$ $$N_c = 24 \text{ carriers}$$ **Step 2 — Compute simultaneous users per cell.** Each RF carrier supports 8 TDMA slots. Assuming each user occupies 1 slot: $$M = N_c \times N_{slots} = 24 \times 8 = 192 \text{ users}$$ **Step 3 — Compute aggregate cell capacity.** Each user has a gross bit rate of 270.833 kbps (before channel coding). The aggregate gross capacity is: $$C_{agg} = M \times R_b = 192 \times 270.833 \text{ kbps}$$ $$C_{agg} = 52{,}000 \text{ kbps} = 52 \text{ Mbps}$$ > [!NOTE] > **After channel coding:** GSM uses a convolutional code of rate 1/2 (with puncturing to 2/3 in some channels), reducing the *net user data rate* to about **9.6 kbps** per voice user. So the *useful* aggregate capacity for voice is about $192 \times 9.6 = 1.84$ **Mbps**. **Final Capacity Table (Boxed):** $$\boxed{N_c = 24, \quad M = 192, \quad C_{agg} = 52 \text{ Mbps (gross)}, \quad C_{useful} \approx 1.84 \text{ Mbps}}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: TDMA vs FDMA vs CDMA | Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Domain of multiple access** | Frequency | Time | Code | | **Bandwidth efficiency** | Low | High | Very high | | **Synchronization requirement** | Low | High | Very high (chip-level) | | **Power control necessity** | Low | Moderate | Critical | | **Susceptibility to multipath** | Low | Moderate | High (use RAKE receiver) | | **Cell capacity** | Fixed | Flexible | Soft (interference-limited) | | **Frequency reuse** | Aggressive (1/7) | Moderate (1/3) | Universal (1/1) | | **Handoff complexity** | Low | Moderate | High | | **Privacy** | Low | Low | High (spread spectrum) | | **Example system** | Analog AMPS | GSM, IS-54 | IS-95, WCDMA | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA). List **three** advantages of TDMA over FDMA. **Model Answer:** TDMA is a multiple access technique in which several users share a common transmission medium by transmitting at *different time slots* but at the *same carrier frequency*. The available channel bandwidth is divided into a sequence of repeating **time frames**, with each frame further subdivided into $N$ time slots; each user is assigned one or more slots per frame for exclusive transmission. **Three advantages of TDMA over FDMA:** 1. **Higher bandwidth efficiency** — Each user accesses the *entire* bandwidth during its slot, allowing higher instantaneous bit rates. 2. **No intermodulation distortion** — A single carrier is active at any instant, eliminating nonlinear amplifier intermodulation products that plague FDMA. 3. **Flexible capacity allocation** — Slots can be dynamically reassigned based on traffic load (e.g., for handover or congestion relief), whereas FDMA channels are fixed-frequency and rigid. 4. *(Bonus)* — Easier integration with digital switching and TDM hierarchies; supports discontinuous transmission (DTX) for battery savings. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse "TDMA efficiency" with "frame efficiency." Be precise: the **TDMA efficiency** is the ratio of *information bits* to *total slot bits*, NOT the ratio of users to slots. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the role of the **guard period** in a TDMA burst. Why is it necessary? What is its typical length in GSM, and how does GSM compensate for the cell-radius limitation imposed by the guard period? **Model Answer:** The **guard period** (or *guard time*) is a small idle interval at the end of each TDMA time slot during which *no signal is transmitted*. It is **necessary** to prevent **inter-slot interference** caused by two physical effects: 1. **Propagation delay variation** — Mobiles at different distances from the base station experience different propagation delays. A far-mobile's burst may arrive *late* and overlap with the *next* slot's near-mobile burst. 2. **Timing jitter** — Imperfect synchronization between oscillators introduces small timing uncertainties. In **GSM**, the guard period is **8.25 bit periods** long, which at the GSM bit rate of 270.833 kbps corresponds to: $$T_g = \frac{8.25}{270.833 \times 10^3} \approx 30.5 \text{ μs}$$ This corresponds to a maximum *one-way* propagation difference of about **4.5 km** (i.e., a *one-way* range difference of 4.5 km, or a *cell radius* of up to ~35 km in GSM). **GSM's compensation mechanism — Timing Advance (TA):** The base station continuously measures the *uplink arrival time* of each mobile's burst and issues a **Timing Advance** command, instructing the mobile to *preemptively transmit earlier* by an amount equal to its observed round-trip delay. This shifts the bursts so they all align at the base station, effectively eliminating the propagation delay *at the receiver* and relaxing the guard time requirement. $$\boxed{T_g = 8.25 \text{ bits} = 30.5 \text{ μs}, \quad \text{Compensated by Timing Advance (TA)}}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **TDMA frame structure** of the GSM system. Define **frame, multiframe, superframe, and hyperframe**, and state their durations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A GSM system has the following parameters: - Number of slots per TDMA frame: $N = 8$ - Bits per slot: $156.25$ - Channel bit rate: $R_b = 270.833$ kbps - Information bits per slot: $148$ Compute: - (i) The frame duration $T_f$ - (ii) The slot duration $T_s$ - (iii) The TDMA efficiency $\eta$ - (iv) The user data rate per slot $R_u$ (assuming rate-1/2 convolutional coding) - (v) The total number of users supported by a cell with **6** RF carriers (assume 1 user per slot). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) GSM Frame Structure:** The GSM TDMA hierarchy has **four levels** of time organization, each building on the previous: 1. **Time Slot (Burst Period):** The smallest unit. Duration ≈ **0.577 ms** = 156.25 bit periods. Contains the burst (training + info + guard). 2. **TDMA Frame:** A group of **8 time slots**. Duration = **4.615 ms**. Each slot carries one user's burst. 3. **Multiframe:** Two types — - **Traffic Multiframe:** 26 frames, duration **120 ms** (used for traffic + SACCH). - **Control Multiframe:** 51 frames, duration **235.4 ms** (used for broadcast, paging, access grant). 4. **Superframe:** 26 × 51 = **1326 frames**, duration **6.12 s**. (Alternatively, 51 × 26 = 1326.) 5. **Hyperframe:** 2048 superframes, duration **3 h 28 m 53 s 760 ms**. Used for ciphering and frequency hopping sequences. **Block Diagram of Frame Hierarchy:** ``` ┌─────────────────────── Hyperframe (3h 28m 53s) ───────────────────────┐ │ ┌──── Superframe (6.12s) ────┐ ┌──── Superframe (6.12s) ────┐ ... │ │ │ ┌─ Multiframe (120ms) ─┐ │ │ ┌─ Multiframe (120ms) ─┐ │ │ │ │ │ Frame Frame ... Frame│ │ │ │ Frame Frame ... Frame│ │ │ │ │ │ (26 frames) │ │ │ │ (26 frames) │ │ │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ ... │ │ ... │ │ │ └───────────────────────────┘ └───────────────────────────┘ │ │ (51 multiframes per superframe = 1326 frames) │ │ │ │ (2048 superframes per hyperframe) │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **(b) Numerical Computations:** **(i) Frame duration:** $$T_f = N \times 156.25 \times T_b = \frac{8 \times 156.25}{270.833 \times 10^3}$$ $$T_f = \frac{1250}{270.833 \times 10^3} = 4.615 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{T_f = 4.615 \text{ ms}}$$ **(ii) Slot duration:** $$T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{4.615 \text{ ms}}{8}$$ $$\boxed{T_s = 0.577 \text{ ms}}$$ **(iii) TDMA efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{N_{info}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{148}{156.25} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta = 94.72\%}$$ **(iv) User data rate per slot (with rate-1/2 coding):** Gross bit rate per slot: $$R_{slot} = \frac{156.25 \text{ bits}}{T_s} = R_b = 270.833 \text{ kbps}$$ After rate-1/2 convolutional coding, the *information* bit rate becomes: $$R_u = \frac{1}{2} \times 270.833 \text{ kbps}$$ $$\boxed{R_u = 135.4 \text{ kbps (gross, before puncturing and source coding)}}$$ With **GSM's typical source coding + channel coding pipeline** (13 kbps voice codec → 260 bps error protection overhead → 1/2 rate convolutional code → 4 burst interleaving over 40 ms), the final *net* voice user data rate is: $$R_{net} \approx 9.6 \text{ kbps (standard GSM full-rate voice)}$$ **(v) Total users with 6 RF carriers:** $$M_{total} = 6 \text{ carriers} \times 8 \text{ slots/carrier} = 48 \text{ slots}$$ $$\boxed{M_{total} = 48 \text{ simultaneous users per cell}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake in TDMA Numericals:** > 1. **Mixing frame and multiframe:** $T_f = 4.615$ ms is the *frame*, not the *multiframe* (120 ms) or *superframe* (6.12 s). Read the question carefully. > 2. **Forgetting the channel coding rate:** If the question says "user data rate," you must apply the 1/2 code rate, not just report the raw bit rate. > 3. **Miscounting slot bits:** A GSM slot is **156.25** bit periods (NOT 156 or 157). The "0.25" comes from the fact that one slot ends mid-bit — a real engineering detail. > 4. **Confusing gross and net capacity:** 52 Mbps is *gross*; ~1.84 Mbps is *useful voice*. Many students lose marks by reporting the wrong one. --- **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 4 Question, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare **FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** in a detailed tabular format with at least **8** comparison parameters. Discuss the **synchronization requirements** in each, explaining why CDMA is the most demanding. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TDMA satellite system serves 500 users per transponder. Each user transmits a burst of 5 kbps during its assigned slot. The frame duration is 20 ms, and the guard time per slot is 0.05 ms. Compute: - (i) The total number of slots per frame - (ii) The slot duration available for data + overhead (excluding guard) - (iii) The total bits per slot (data + overhead) if the data rate per user is 5 kbps - (iv) The number of overhead bits per slot (excluding guard) - (v) The system efficiency (excluding guard) **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Detailed Comparison FDMA vs TDMA vs CDMA:** | Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Domain of separation** | Frequency | Time | Code (spreading sequence) | | **Bandwidth usage** | Narrow per user, simultaneous | Full bandwidth per user, sequential | Full bandwidth, simultaneous | | **Synchronization** | Frequency only (loose) | Time-slot aligned (tight) | Chip-level, code-phase, power (very tight) | | **Power control** | Not critical | Moderate | **Critically essential** (near-far problem) | | **Capacity** | Hard limit (channels) | Hard limit (slots) | **Soft limit** (interference-limited) | | **Multipath resistance** | Good | Moderate (needs equalizer) | Excellent (RAKE receiver exploits it) | | **Privacy / Security** | Poor (narrow receiver tunes in) | Poor | Excellent (pseudo-random code required) | | **Handoff complexity** | Low | Moderate | High (soft handoff possible) | | **Peak-to-average power** | 1 (continuous) | High (~N times) | Moderate | | **System example** | AMPS | GSM, IS-54 | IS-95, WCDMA, 3G | **Why CDMA needs the tightest synchronization:** CDMA assigns each user a unique **pseudo-random spreading code** (e.g., a Walsh-Hadamard code of length 64 to 256). For correct demodulation, the receiver must: - **Code-phase synchronize** to within **one chip period** $T_c$ (e.g., $T_c \approx 0.26$ μs in WCDMA → 3.84 Mcps). - **Maintain power control** within ±1 dB across all users, otherwise the *near-far problem* — a strong near user completely masks a weak far user — destroys capacity. - **Track Doppler** continuously for high-mobility users. In contrast, FDMA only needs to tune a narrowband filter to the assigned frequency (kilohertz accuracy is fine), and TDMA needs slot-level synchronization (microsecond accuracy in GSM). CDMA demands **nanosecond-level** accuracy, which is orders of magnitude tighter. **(b) Satellite TDMA Numerical:** **Given:** 500 users, 5 kbps per user, $T_f = 20$ ms, $T_g = 0.05$ ms. **(i) Number of slots per frame:** In a TDMA system, each user gets one slot per frame (assuming uniform allocation). Therefore: $$\boxed{N_{slots} = 500}$$ **(ii) Slot duration (excluding guard):** The frame is divided into 500 slots, each followed by a guard time. Total guard time per frame: $$T_{g,total} = 500 \times 0.05 \text{ ms} = 25 \text{ ms}$$ Total *useful* (slot) time per frame: $$T_{slot,total} = T_f - T_{g,total} = 20 - 25 = -5 \text{ ms}$$ **Negative result — parameter infeasibility!** This means the system as specified is **not physically realizable**. The total guard time exceeds the frame duration. To make it feasible, either: - Increase the frame duration: $T_f > 25$ ms (e.g., $T_f = 30$ ms gives 5 ms of usable slot time), OR - Reduce the number of users, OR - Reduce the guard time. **For a feasible design, take $T_f = 30$ ms** (so that $T_{slot,total} = 5$ ms). Per-slot useful duration: $$T_{slot} = \frac{T_{slot,total}}{500} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{500} = 0.01 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ μs}$$ $$\boxed{T_{slot,useful} = 10 \text{ μs}}$$ **(iii) Total bits per slot (data only):** $$N_{info} = R_b \times T_{slot,useful} = 5000 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.05 \text{ bits}$$ **This is a non-integer result, confirming parameter infeasibility.** A real satellite TDMA system would have slots on the order of **milliseconds** with **kilobits** of payload. The above figures are illustrative; the conceptual answer is that **each slot carries $R_b \times T_{slot}$ data bits plus overhead bits.** For a realistic system, suppose $T_{slot,useful} = 1$ ms. Then: $$N_{info} = 5000 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \text{ bits (data per slot)}$$ (For a more realistic example: $T_{slot,useful} = 1$ ms, $R_b = 64$ kbps PCM voice → 64 bits per slot, plus ~10 bits overhead = 74 bits total.) **(iv) Overhead bits per slot (excluding guard):** In a properly designed system, overhead includes: - Preamble for carrier/clock recovery: ~10–20 bits - Unique word for slot identification: ~8–16 bits - Signaling/control bits: ~5–10 bits **Total overhead ≈ 25–50 bits per slot.** (Specific values depend on the system's design document.) **(v) System efficiency (excluding guard):** $$\eta = \frac{N_{info}}{N_{info} + N_{overhead}} = \frac{64}{64 + 30} = 68.1\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 68\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall for Satellite TDMA Problems:** > 1. **Always check feasibility first!** When the sum of guard times exceeds the frame, the system cannot work. Examiners test this awareness. > 2. **For satellite TDMA, the guard time is LONG** (milliseconds, not microseconds), because the round-trip light time to a GEO satellite is ~240 ms. The guard time must accommodate the *differential* range among all earth stations in the beam (typically 0.05 to 0.5 ms). > 3. **Don't forget the unique word** (slot identifier) in the overhead count. It is NOT part of the guard time. > 4. **Reference burst:** Satellite TDMA systems also need a *reference burst* transmitted by one master station to provide timing for all others. This *occupies one of the slots*, reducing the number of traffic slots by 1. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — TDMA (Module 4)** **Core Concepts:** - [ ] TDMA = Time-based channel sharing on a *single* carrier frequency. - [ ] Frame = $N$ slots, slot duration $T_s = T_f / N$. - [ ] Each user occupies a *unique* slot per frame; transmits in *bursts*. **GSM-Specific Numbers (Memorize!):** - [ ] Frame duration: **4.615 ms** (8 slots) - [ ] Slot duration: **0.577 ms** (156.25 bit periods) - [ ] Carrier spacing: **200 kHz** - [ ] Channel bit rate: **270.833 kbps** - [ ] Net voice rate: **9.6 kbps** (after full-rate codec + 1/2 rate coding) - [ ] Training sequence: **26 bits** - [ ] Guard period: **8.25 bits** (≈ 30.5 μs) - [ ] Information bits: **148 bits** (two 57-bit blocks + 2 stealing flags) - [ ] Multiframe: 26 (traffic) / 51 (control) frames - [ ] Hyperframe: 2048 superframes = 3h 28m 53s 760ms **Frame Hierarchy (Bottom-Up):** - [ ] Burst → TDMA Frame (8 bursts) → Multiframe (26/51 frames) → Superframe (1326 frames) → Hyperframe (2048 superframes) **Key Mechanisms:** - [ ] **Guard period:** Prevents inter-slot overlap due to propagation delay. - [ ] **Timing Advance (TA):** GSM's mechanism to pre-compensate mobile transmission timing. - [ ] **Training sequence:** Enables channel equalization (Viterbi equalizer in GSM). - [ ] **Discontinuous Transmission (DTX):** Mobile transmitter OFF during silent periods — battery saving. - [ ] **Slow Frequency Hopping (SFH):** GSM hops 217 times/s for interference diversity. **Duplexing Options:** - [ ] **FDD + TDMA:** Uplink/downlink on *different* frequencies (GSM). - [ ] **TDD + TDMA:** Uplink/downlink in *different* slots of the *same* frequency (DECT). **Efficiency Formulas (Must Remember):** - [ ] Slot efficiency: $\eta = N_{info}/N_{total}$ → ~94.7% for GSM. - [ ] Frame efficiency: $\eta_f = (N_{info} \times N_{slots}) / (N_{total} \times N_{slots})$ — *same* as slot efficiency in single-slot-per-user case. - [ ] System efficiency: $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{slot} \times \eta_{coding}$ — accounts for channel coding overhead. **Comparison Shortcuts (for 14-mark answers):** - [ ] FDMA → Frequency, loose sync, fixed capacity. - [ ] TDMA → Time, tight sync, flexible capacity, high peak power. - [ ] CDMA → Code, very tight sync, soft capacity, near-far problem. **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** - [ ] Confusing *frame* (4.615 ms) with *multiframe* (120 ms). - [ ] Reporting gross bit rate (270.833 kbps) as the *user data rate* (9.6 kbps). - [ ] Ignoring the *timing advance* mechanism when asked about cell range. - [ ] Forgetting the *training sequence* in the overhead count. - [ ] Not checking feasibility in satellite TDMA numericals (guard time > frame time = infeasible). **Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Pattern):** - [ ] For 3-mark questions: Definition + 3 distinct points. Avoid repetition. - [ ] For 14-mark questions: Block diagram first, then derivation, then numerical, then comparison table. - [ ] Always box the final numerical answer in $\boxed{\quad}$. - [ ] Use proper SI units throughout. Marks are deducted for missing units.

topics.title: Spread Spectrum Multiple Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spread Spectrum Multiple Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Spread Spectrum — The Fundamental Idea

**Spread Spectrum (SS)** is a modulation and multiple-access technique in which the bandwidth of the transmitted signal is deliberately made **much wider** (typically 100 to 1000 times) than the bandwidth actually required to carry the information. This "spreading" of the signal spectrum is achieved using a *pseudo-random spreading code* that is independent of the original data, and a corresponding de-spreading process at the receiver recovers the original narrowband information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Spread Spectrum is a means of transmission in which the data sequence occupies a bandwidth in excess of the minimum bandwidth necessary to send it, by the use of a spreading code which is independent of the data sequence. The same code is used at the receiver to despread and recover the data.*

### The Three Pillars Justifying Spread Spectrum

For any system to be officially classified as *Spread Spectrum*, it must satisfy at least one of the following criteria:

1. **The transmitted signal occupies a bandwidth much greater than the bandwidth of the original information signal.**
2. **Spreading is accomplished by means of a spreading code (PN sequence) that is independent of the information-bearing signal.**
3. **At the receiver, de-spreading is performed by correlation of the received spread signal with a synchronized replica of the spreading code.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spread?** The total transmitted power remains the **same** as a narrowband transmission. By spreading this power over a wide band, the Power Spectral Density (PSD) becomes very low — often **below the thermal noise floor** — making the signal almost invisible to unintended listeners and highly resistant to jamming.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 The Pizza Shop Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine two friends **Anu** and **Ben** are whispering a secret inside a noisy crowded mall. To avoid being overheard:
> - **Without SS (Narrowband):** They speak continuously in a loud, clear voice. Anyone nearby hears everything. Interference from any single loud noise ruins the message.
> - **With SS (Spread Spectrum):** Instead of speaking continuously, they split the secret into tiny code-words (like "Apple", "Mango", "Cherry") and speak each code-word at *different times*, *different pitches*, and *different directions*, with each code-word lasting only microseconds. To an eavesdropper, it sounds like random noise. Only a person who **knows the exact code-book and the exact timing** (synchronized spreading code) can re-assemble the message.
>
> **Engineering Translation:** The original narrowband voice/data is multiplied by a high-speed **Pseudo-Noise (PN) sequence** (a binary code running at chip-rate $R_c$, much faster than bit-rate $R_b$). This *chops* the signal into tiny chips and scatters its energy across a huge bandwidth. A receiver without the matching PN code hears only noise.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Spread Spectrum? — Engineering Motivation

| Motivation | Real-World Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Anti-jamming (AJ)** | Military communication against intentional interference |
| **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** | Covert transmissions hidden below the noise floor |
| **Multiple Access** | CDMA cellular (IS-95, CDMA2000, WCDMA) |
| **Resistance to Multipath Fading** | RAKE receiver in 3G mobile systems |
| **Precise Ranging** | GPS uses DSSS for accurate time-of-arrival measurement |
| **EMI/EMC Reduction** | Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ISM band devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Shannon's Channel Capacity:**
> Shannon's theorem states that $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$. For a given $C$, if we **increase bandwidth $B$**, the required **$S/N$ (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) can be reduced**. Spread spectrum exploits this: by trading bandwidth for SNR, the system can operate reliably even when the signal is *weaker than the noise*. This is the deepest mathematical reason SS works.

---

## 1.4 Types of Spread Spectrum Systems

There are **three principal** SS techniques taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Type | Acronym | Spreading Method |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Sequence SS | **DSSS** | Information is multiplied by a high-rate PN code |
| Frequency Hopping SS | **FHSS** | Carrier frequency is hopped among many channels using a PN code |
| Time Hopping SS | **THSS** | Transmission occurs in bursts at pseudo-random time slots |

> This module focuses on **DSSS** and **FHSS**, since they form the basis of **CDMA**.

---

## 1.5 Multiple Access — Where Spread Spectrum Shines

**Multiple Access** allows many users to share a common communication channel simultaneously. Spread Spectrum enables a particularly elegant form called **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)**:

- Each user is assigned a **unique PN code** (orthogonal or quasi-orthogonal to others).
- All users transmit **simultaneously on the same frequency band**.
- Separation is achieved by code correlation at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tag — Multiple Access Comparison Snapshot:**
> - **FDMA:** Divide by *frequency*
> - **TDMA:** Divide by *time*
> - **CDMA:** Divide by *code*
> - **OFDMA:** Divide by *frequency-time blocks*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Pseudo-Noise (PN) Sequence — Heart of Spread Spectrum

A **Pseudo-Noise (PN) sequence** is a deterministic binary sequence that *appears random* but is generated by a known structure (a Linear Feedback Shift Register — LFSR). It has the statistical properties of true random noise but is *reproducible* at the receiver.

### 2.1.1 Essential Properties of a Good PN Sequence

| Property | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Balance Property** | In one period, the number of 1's differs from the number of 0's by **at most 1** | Ensures no DC bias |
| **Run-Length Property** | Half the runs are length 1, one-quarter length 2, one-eighth length 3, etc. | Mimics coin-flip statistics |
| **Autocorrelation** | Sharp peak at zero lag, near-zero elsewhere | Enables synchronization |
| **Cross-Correlation** | Low between two different PN sequences | Enables multiple access (CDMA) |
| **Long Period** | Period $N = 2^n - 1$ for an $n$-stage LFSR | Difficult to intercept / decode |

### 2.1.2 Maximal Length Sequence (m-sequence)

The **m-sequence** is the most widely used PN sequence, generated by an $n$-stage LFSR with proper feedback taps (primitive polynomials).

For an $n$-stage LFSR:

- **Period of the m-sequence:** $N = 2^n - 1$ chips
- **Chip duration:** $T_c$ (seconds per chip)
- **Sequence repeats every:** $T = N \cdot T_c$ seconds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example (KTU Frequent):** For $n = 5$ stages, period $N = 2^5 - 1 = 31$ chips. A common primitive polynomial is $x^5 + x^2 + 1$.

The **autocorrelation** of an m-sequence is a vital property:

$$
R_{PN}(\tau) = \begin{cases} 1, & \tau = 0 \pmod{N T_c} \\[4pt] -\dfrac{1}{N}, & \tau \neq 0 \pmod{N T_c} \end{cases}
$$

This means an m-sequence has a **huge peak at zero lag** and a small constant negative value elsewhere — perfect for synchronization.

---

## 2.2 Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)

### 2.2.1 The DSSS Transmitter

The information signal $b(t)$ (binary data at rate $R_b$) is multiplied by a PN sequence $c(t)$ (chips at rate $R_c$). Each data bit is divided into $L_c$ chips.

**Operation:**

$$
s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where:
- $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ is the data waveform
- $c(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ is the PN code waveform
- The product $b(t)\cdot c(t)$ is the **spread signal**

### 2.2.2 The DSSS Receiver (De-spreading)

The received signal is correlated (multiplied) with a **synchronized local replica** of the PN code, then integrated over one bit duration.

If the local code matches the transmitter code: $c(t)\cdot c(t) = 1$, so the original narrowband data re-emerges.

### 2.2.3 Processing Gain (Most Important KTU Formula)

**Processing Gain (PG)** quantifies the SS advantage. It is the ratio of spread bandwidth to information bandwidth:

$$
\text{PG} = \frac{B_{ss}}{B_{info}} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = L_c
$$

where $L_c$ is the number of chips per data bit. It is often expressed in dB:

$$
\boxed{\text{PG (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Meaning:** A PG of 30 dB means the SS signal is spread 1000× wider. A jammer would need 1000× more power to produce the same degradation at the receiver — this is the **anti-jamming margin**.

### 2.2.4 Jamming Margin

$$
\text{Jamming Margin } (M_j) = \text{PG (dB)} - \left[ \text{Required } \frac{S}{N} \text{ (dB)} \right]
$$

---

## 2.3 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

The carrier frequency $f_c$ is *rapidly switched* among a large set of frequencies, governed by a PN code. Each frequency is held for a short interval called the **dwell time** $T_d$.

### 2.3.2 Classification

| Type | Hopping Rate vs. Data Rate |
|---|---|
| **Slow Frequency Hopping (SFH)** | One or more data bits per hop (i.e., hop rate $<$ symbol rate) |
| **Fast Frequency Hopping (FFH)** | Multiple hops per data bit (i.e., hop rate $>$ symbol rate) |

### 2.3.3 Frequency Synthesizer Hopping Bandwidth

If the carrier hops over a total bandwidth $W_{ss}$ and there are $N_h$ distinct hop frequencies:

$$
N_h = \frac{W_{ss}}{\Delta f}
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the instantaneous channel bandwidth.

---

## 2.4 Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)

### 2.4.1 The CDMA Concept

In CDMA, each of the $K$ simultaneous users is assigned a unique PN code $c_k(t)$. All users transmit over the **same frequency band** at the **same time**, separated only by their codes.

Received composite signal:

$$
r(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} b_k(t)\, c_k(t)\, \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)
$$

The $k$-th user's receiver correlates $r(t)$ with $c_k(t)$. Because the cross-correlation between different codes is *low*, only the $k$-th user's signal is recovered.

### 2.4.2 Synchronous vs Asynchronous CDMA

| Type | When Used | Code Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous CDMA** | All users are time-aligned (e.g., IS-95 forward link from base station) | **Orthogonal** codes (e.g., Walsh-Hadamard) |
| **Asynchronous CDMA** | Users transmit independently (e.g., mobile uplink) | **Quasi-orthogonal** codes (e.g., Gold, Kasami, m-sequences) |

### 2.4.3 Multiple Access Capacity (KTU High-Yield)

For **asynchronous CDMA** with Processing Gain $L_c$, the number of users supported in a standard Gaussian approximation:

$$
K \approx \frac{L_c}{\frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \text{(acceptable BER factor)}}
$$

A commonly used bound:

$$
\boxed{K \le \frac{L_c}{3} \quad \text{(approximate, voice-grade BER } 10^{-3}\text{)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meaning of $L_c = R_c / R_b$:** Doubling the chip rate doubles the bandwidth but **doubles the number of users** the system can support.

---

## 2.5 Near-Far Problem (Critical CDMA Issue)

In a CDMA uplink, mobile stations are at *different distances* from the base station. If all transmit with equal power, the closest user's signal arrives **far stronger** than a far user's signal.

Because the cross-correlation between codes is *not exactly zero*, the strong signal acts as **interference** and can overwhelm the weaker user's signal — even with perfectly valid codes.

**Standard Solutions:**
1. **Power Control** — base station commands each mobile to adjust its transmitted power (used in IS-95, WCDMA). Power must be controlled to within $\pm 1$ dB.
2. **Multi-user Detection (MUD)** — sophisticated receiver algorithms that subtract interference.
3. **Near-far resistant codes** — codes with low cross-correlation (Gold, Kasami).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Processing Gain | $PG = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b} = \dfrac{B_{ss}}{B_{info}}$ | ratio / dB | Spreading factor |
| 2 | PG in dB | $PG_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(R_c/R_b)$ | dB | Anti-jamming measure |
| 3 | Jamming Margin | $M_j = PG_{dB} - (S/N)_{req,dB}$ | dB | How much jamming tolerated |
| 4 | m-sequence period | $N = 2^n - 1$ | chips | For $n$-stage LFSR |
| 5 | Number of chips per bit | $L_c = R_c / R_b$ | chips/bit | Spreading ratio |
| 6 | CDMA user capacity | $K \approx L_c / 3$ | users | Voice-grade BER |
| 7 | Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Trade BW for SNR |
| 8 | FHSS hop count | $N_h = W_{ss} / \Delta f$ | channels | Number of hop freqs |
| 9 | Energy per bit | $E_b = P \cdot T_b = P / R_b$ | Joules | Energy per info bit |
| 10 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b / B_{ss}$ | bps/Hz | Inversely scales with $L_c$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Always express PG in **dB** when solving KTU problems. Always mention units. Always relate $L_c$ to the bandwidth expansion factor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DSSS Output Bandwidth and Processing Gain

### Problem Statement
A DSSS system transmits data at $R_b = 9.6$ kbps using a PN code rate of $R_c = 1.2288$ Mcps. Calculate the processing gain, express it in dB, and find the spread signal bandwidth.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters**

$$
R_b = 9.6 \text{ kbps} = 9.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps}, \qquad R_c = 1.2288 \text{ Mcps} = 1.2288 \times 10^{6} \text{ chips/sec}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the number of chips per bit (spreading factor)**

$$
L_c = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^{6}}{9.6 \times 10^{3}} = 128 \text{ chips/bit}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Processing Gain (linear scale)**

$$
PG = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^{6}}{9.6 \times 10^{3}} = 128
$$

**Step 4: Convert Processing Gain to decibels**

$$
PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \times 2.1072 = 21.07 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the spread spectrum bandwidth**

For BPSK, the null-to-null bandwidth of the spread signal equals $2 R_c$ (since the main lobe width is $\pm R_c$ around the carrier):

$$
B_{ss} = 2 R_c = 2 \times 1.2288 \times 10^{6} = 2.4576 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many textbooks use the **first-null** bandwidth. In IS-95 CDMA, the standard channel spacing is exactly $1.2288$ MHz (i.e., $R_c$), so always check whether the question expects null-to-null or main-lobe bandwidth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jamming Margin Calculation

### Problem Statement
In a DSSS link, the processing gain is $PG_{dB} = 30$ dB. The required $\frac{E_b}{N_0}$ for the target BER is 10 dB. The receiver requires a $\frac{E_b}{N_{0,eff}}$ margin of 3 dB above the minimum. Find the jamming margin and the maximum tolerable $J/S$ ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the jamming margin definition**

$$
M_j = PG_{dB} - \left[\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req} + \text{margin}\right]_{dB}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$
M_j = 30 - (10 + 3) = 30 - 13 = 17 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Convert jamming margin to the jammer-to-signal ratio**

The jamming margin represents how much *more powerful* the jammer can be relative to the desired signal before the link fails:

$$
\left(\frac{J}{S}\right)_{max} = M_j = 17 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4: Express as a linear ratio**

$$
\frac{J}{S} = 10^{17/10} = 10^{1.7} \approx 50.12
$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation**

The system tolerates a jammer up to **50× stronger** than the desired signal. This is the **anti-jamming advantage** of spread spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 30 dB PG gives roughly 50× jamming tolerance — a direct trade-off for using 1000× more bandwidth than necessary.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CDMA Capacity from Processing Gain

### Problem Statement
A CDMA cellular system uses a chip rate of $R_c = 3.84$ Mcps (WCDMA) and a voice data rate of $R_b = 12.2$ kbps. The required $E_b/N_0$ for voice BER = $10^{-3}$ is approximately 7 dB. The system operates at a noise rise of 3 dB (i.e., the total interference is twice the thermal noise). Estimate the number of simultaneous users per cell.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the processing gain**

$$
L_c = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^{6}}{12.2 \times 10^{3}} = 314.75 \approx 315
$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(315) \approx 24.98 \text{ dB}$.

**Step 2: Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear scale**

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{7/10} = 5.012
$$

**Step 3: Apply the CDMA pole capacity formula**

For the standard noise-limited case (ignoring thermal noise), the **single-cell pole capacity** is:

$$
K_{max} = 1 + \frac{L_c}{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}
$$

where the "1" accounts for thermal noise. Substituting:

$$
K_{max} = 1 + \frac{314.75}{5.012} = 1 + 62.81 = 63.81
$$

**Step 4: Apply the noise-rise correction**

With interference-rise (loading) factor $\eta = 3$ dB (i.e., $10^{0.3} \approx 2$):

$$
K = \frac{K_{max}}{\eta} = \frac{63.81}{2} \approx 31.9 \approx 32 \text{ users}
$$

**Step 5: Sanity check with the simpler KTU approximation**

Using $K \approx L_c / 3 = 315 / 3 = 105$ users. The simpler formula is an *upper bound* for high PG with low $E_b/N_0$ — the more rigorous formula above gives a tighter, realistic estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Both forms appear in KTU questions. Always show the formula you are using and explain the assumptions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Autocorrelation of an m-sequence

### Goal
Show that the periodic autocorrelation of an m-sequence of period $N$ is:

$$
R_{PN}(\tau) = \begin{cases} N, & \tau = 0 \pmod{N T_c} \\ -1, & \tau \neq 0 \pmod{N T_c} \end{cases}
$$

and the *normalized* form is the one shown in Section 2.1.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the m-sequence**

Let $c(t)$ be a periodic bipolar PN waveform (taking values $\pm 1$) with chip period $T_c$ and period $N T_c$.

**Step 2: Write the autocorrelation integral**

$$
R_{PN}(\tau) = \frac{1}{N T_c} \int_0^{N T_c} c(t)\, c(t + \tau)\, dt
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate for $\tau = 0$**

Since $c(t) \cdot c(t) = c^2(t) = 1$ (because $c(t) = \pm 1$):

$$
R_{PN}(0) = \frac{1}{N T_c} \int_0^{N T_c} 1\, dt = \frac{N T_c}{N T_c} = 1 \quad \text{(normalized)}
$$

or $N$ if not normalized.

**Step 4: Evaluate for $\tau \neq 0$ (a multiple of $T_c$)**

By the **balance property** of m-sequences, in any full period there are $\dfrac{N+1}{2}$ *matches* (where aligned bits agree) and $\dfrac{N-1}{2}$ *mismatches* (where aligned bits disagree).

$$
R_{PN}(\tau \neq 0) = \frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{N+1}{2}(+1) + \frac{N-1}{2}(-1)\right]
$$

**Step 5: Simplify**

$$
R_{PN}(\tau \neq 0) = \frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{N+1 - N + 1}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{N}
$$

With a sign convention (mismatch counted negatively):

$$
R_{PN}(\tau \neq 0) = -\frac{1}{N} \quad \text{(normalized)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $N \to \infty$ (longer codes), the off-peak autocorrelation tends to **zero** — the PN signal becomes indistinguishable from true white noise. This is what gives spread spectrum its "noise-like" property.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Why DSSS Rejects Narrowband Jamming

### Setup
A DSSS signal $s(t) = b(t)\,c(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ plus a narrowband jammer $J(t) = J_0 \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ arrives at a receiver that correlates with $c(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

**Step 1: Signal at correlator output (after multiplication)**

$$
r(t)\cdot c(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = b(t)\,c^2(t)\,\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + J_0\,c(t)\,\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2: Use the identity $c^2(t) = 1$**

$$
= b(t)\,\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + J_0\,c(t)\,\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3: Use $\cos^2(x) = \frac{1}{2}(1 + \cos(2x))$**

$$
= \frac{b(t)}{2} + \frac{b(t)}{2}\cos(4\pi f_c t) + \frac{J_0\,c(t)}{2} + \frac{J_0\,c(t)}{2}\cos(4\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4: After low-pass filtering, recover baseband**

$$
y(t) = \frac{b(t)}{2} + \frac{J_0\,c(t)}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Compute powers**

- Signal power: $P_s = \frac{\overline{b^2(t)}}{4} = \frac{1}{4}$
- Jammer power (still spread by $c(t)$): $P_j = \frac{J_0^2}{4}$ (spread over $B_{ss}$, so PSD = $\dfrac{J_0^2}{4 B_{ss}}$)

**Step 6: Effective jamming suppression factor**

$$
\frac{(J/S)_{in}}{(J/S)_{out}} = \frac{B_{ss}}{B_{info}} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = PG
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The narrowband jammer is *spread* by the receiver's de-spreading operation, while the desired signal is *despread*.** This is the fundamental mechanism by which DSSS resists jamming.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 DSSS Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataA["Data b(t)\nRate Rb"] --> mult1["X\nMultiplier"]
    pnA["PN Code c(t)\nRate Rc"] --> mult1
    mult1 --> mod["Carrier\ncos(2 pi fc t)"]
    mod --> antennaA["Transmit\nAntenna"]
    antennaA -.->|"Channel + Jammer"| antennaB["Receive\nAntenna"]
    antennaB --> front["BPF + LNA\nFront End"]
    front --> mult2["X\nMultiplier 1"]
    pnB["Synchronized\nLocal PN c(t)"] --> mult2
    mult2 --> integrate["Integrate\nover Tb"]
    integrate --> decision["Threshold\nDetector"]
    decision --> output["Recovered\nData b(t)"]
```

## 4.2 FHSS Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataFH["Data b(t)"] --> modFH["FSK or BPSK\nModulator"]
    synth["Frequency\nSynthesizer"]
    pnFH["PN Code\nGenerator"] --> synth
    synth --> modFH
    modFH --> upconvert["Upconvert\nto fh"]
    upconvert --> txFH["Transmit\nAntenna"]
    txFH -.->|"Channel"| rxFH["Receive\nAntenna"]
    rxFH --> downconvert["Downconvert\nusing local synth"]
    pnFH2["Synchronized\nPN Code"] --> downconvert
    downconvert --> demodFH["Demodulator"]
    demodFH --> outFH["Recovered\nData"]
```

## 4.3 CDMA Multiple User Concept — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX["TRANSMITTERS"]
        u1["User 1\nb1 t"]
        u2["User 2\nb2 t"]
        uK["User K\nbK t"]
    end

    c1["Code c1 t"] --> u1
    c2["Code c2 t"] --> u2
    cK["Code cK t"] --> uK

    u1 --> sum["SUMMER\nCommon Channel\nSame Time\nSame Frequency"]
    u2 --> sum
    uK --> sum
    sum --> channel["AWGN Channel\nplus Jamming"]
    channel --> rx["RECEIVER BANK"]

    c1rx["c1 t"] --> rx
    c2rx["c2 t"] --> rx
    cKrx["cK t"] --> rx

    rx --> o1["b1 hat"]
    rx --> o2["b2 hat"]
    rx --> oK["bK hat"]
```

## 4.4 Spreading–Despreading Spectrum Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    narrow["Narrowband\nSignal\nBandwidth Binfo"] --> spreader["Spreader\nMultiply by PN"]
    spreader --> spread["Wideband Signal\nBandwidth Bss"]
    spread --> jammer["Channel with\nJammer and Noise"]
    jammer --> despreader["Despreader\nMultiply by PN"]
    despreader --> recovered["Narrowband Signal\nplus Spread Jammer"]
    recovered --> lpf["Low-Pass Filter\nBandwidth Binfo"]
    lpf --> data["Recovered Data\nplus Tiny Jammer"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When you see a CDMA diagram in the question paper, identify **(i)** the spreading operation, **(ii)** the code assignment per user, and **(iii)** the de-spreading correlator — these are the three grading checkpoints.

## 4.5 PN Sequence Generation Using LFSR (m-sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    d1["D1"] --> d2["D2"]
    d2 --> d3["D3"]
    d3 --> d4["D4"]
    d4 --> d5["D5"]
    d5 --> out["Output c t"]
    d1 -.->|"Feedback via XOR"| xor["XOR\ng5 plus g2"]
    xor --> d1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a 5-stage LFSR with primitive polynomial $g(x) = x^5 + x^2 + 1$, feedback is taken from stages 5 and 2 and XORed. This generates a maximum-length sequence with period $N = 31$.

## 4.6 RAKE Receiver Concept (Multipath Combiner)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ant["Antenna"] --> t1["Tap 1\nDelay 0"]
    ant --> t2["Tap 2\nDelay tau 1"]
    ant --> t3["Tap 3\nDelay tau 2"]
    t1 --> c1["Correlator\nwith c t"]
    t2 --> c2["Correlator\nwith c t"]
    t3 --> c3["Correlator\nwith c t"]
    c1 --> w1["Weight w1"]
    c2 --> w2["Weight w2"]
    c3 --> w3["Weight w3"]
    w1 --> sumR["Combiner\nMax Ratio"]
    w2 --> sumR
    w3 --> sumR
    sumR --> dec["Decision\nOutput"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tag — RAKE Receiver:** Spread spectrum's high chip rate $R_c$ allows *resolving* individual multipath components separated by as little as $T_c = 1/R_c$. The RAKE receiver correlates each multipath separately and combines them with weights $\propto$ SNR — a *diversity* gain of typically 3–6 dB.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define Spread Spectrum. Mention any two advantages of spread spectrum communication.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Spread Spectrum is a technique in which the bandwidth of the transmitted signal is intentionally made much larger (typically 100 to 1000 times) than the bandwidth required to send the information signal, by multiplying the data with a high-rate pseudo-random spreading code that is independent of the data.

> **Two advantages (1 mark):**
> 1. **Anti-jamming capability** — the spread signal is resistant to narrowband and partial-band jammers; the processing gain quantifies this advantage.
> 2. **Multiple access capability (CDMA)** — many users can share the same frequency band simultaneously by using unique PN codes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is writing *"spread spectrum means using more bandwidth."* This is a tautology and will lose 1 mark. You must specifically mention **spreading by an independent pseudo-random code**.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
What is meant by *Processing Gain*? For a DSSS system with chip rate 5 Mcps and data rate 25 kbps, calculate the processing gain in dB.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Processing Gain is the ratio of the spread bandwidth to the information bandwidth, equivalently the ratio of chip rate to data rate.

> **Calculation (2 marks):**
> $PG = R_c / R_b = 5 \times 10^6 / 25 \times 10^3 = 200$
> $PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(200) = 10 \times 2.301 = 23.01 \text{ dB}$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in every Part B question. Below are two fully solved independent options.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) with a neat block diagram of transmitter and receiver. Derive the expression for processing gain and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DSSS system uses a data rate of 19.2 kbps and achieves a processing gain of 511. The system must operate at a minimum $E_b/N_0$ of 12 dB. Find: (i) the chip rate, (ii) the spread bandwidth, (iii) the jamming margin if a 2 dB implementation margin is allowed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — DSSS Principle, Block Diagram, and Processing Gain

**Step 1 (2 marks): Principle.**
In DSSS, the binary information $b(t)$ at bit rate $R_b$ is multiplied by a high-speed PN code $c(t)$ at chip rate $R_c \gg R_b$. Each data bit is "chipped" into $L_c = R_c/R_b$ shorter pulses. The resulting wideband signal modulates a carrier and is transmitted. At the receiver, the same synchronized PN code is used to *despread* the signal — recovering the original narrowband data. A narrowband jammer, having no knowledge of the code, gets *spread further* by the receiver's correlator, and is suppressed by a low-pass filter.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Block diagram.**

```
Data b(t) --->[X]---+--->[X]---> Carrier cos(2 pi fc t) ---> TX Antenna
        ^           |
        |       PN code c(t)
        |
   (Receiver side)
   RX Antenna ---> BPF --->[X]---+---> Integrate over Tb ---> Threshold ---> b_hat(t)
                                    |
                              Synchronized c(t)
```

**Step 3 (2 marks): Processing gain derivation.**
The transmit bandwidth equals approximately $R_c$ (main lobe of BPSK). The information bandwidth equals $R_b$. Hence:

$$
PG = \frac{B_{ss}}{B_{info}} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = L_c
$$

In decibels: $PG_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(R_c/R_b)$.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Significance.**
The processing gain represents:
- **Anti-jamming margin** against narrowband and partial-band jammers.
- **Capacity of CDMA** — the number of orthogonal users.
- **LPI (Low Probability of Intercept)** — signal is hidden below the noise floor by spreading.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $R_b = 19.2$ kbps, $PG = 511$, $(E_b/N_0)_{min} = 12$ dB, implementation margin $= 2$ dB.

**Step 1: Compute chip rate (2 marks)**

$$
R_c = PG \times R_b = 511 \times 19.2 \times 10^{3} = 9.81 \times 10^{6} \text{ chips/s} = 9.81 \text{ Mcps}
$$

**Step 2: Compute spread bandwidth (2 marks)**

$$
B_{ss} \approx R_c = 9.81 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{(main-lobe)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute processing gain in dB (1 mark)**

$$
PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(511) = 10 \times 2.708 = 27.08 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute jamming margin (2 marks)**

$$
M_j = PG_{dB} - \left[\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req} + \text{impl. margin}\right]
$$

$$
M_j = 27.08 - (12 + 2) = 13.08 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The system can tolerate a jammer **20× stronger** than the desired signal (since $10^{13.08/10} \approx 20.3$). This is the practical benefit of $L_c = 511$ spreading.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear when plugging into the pole capacity formula. Always keep track of **dB vs linear** consistently. Also, do NOT confuse *information bandwidth* ($R_b$) with *chip bandwidth* ($R_c$) — examiners explicitly test this.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) system. Compare slow and fast frequency hopping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a CDMA system, the chip rate is 1.228 Mcps and the data rate per user is 9.6 kbps. There are 25 simultaneous users in a cell. Calculate the processing gain and verify whether the system is under-loaded, fully-loaded, or over-loaded. Assume a voice-grade $E_b/N_0$ requirement of 7 dB and a noise rise of 3 dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — FHSS Block Diagram and Comparison

**Step 1 (2 marks): FHSS Operation.**
In FHSS, the carrier frequency of the transmitted signal is rapidly switched (hopped) among $N_h$ discrete frequencies, governed by a PN code. Each frequency is held for a duration called the **dwell time** $T_d$. At the receiver, a *synchronized* local frequency synthesizer, controlled by an identical PN code, generates the same hopping pattern, and de-hopping restores the signal to a fixed intermediate frequency.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Block diagram.**

```
Data b(t) ---> [FSK/BFSK Mod] ---> [Mixer] ---> Antenna
                  ^                ^
                  |                |
       PN code c(t) ---> [Freq. Synthesizer] (TX)
       
       Antenna ---> [BPF] ---> [Mixer] ---> [FSK Demod] ---> b_hat(t)
                                  ^
                                  |
                PN code c(t) ---> [Freq. Synthesizer] (RX, synchronized)
```

**Step 3 (3 marks): Comparison Table.**

| Parameter | Slow FH (SFH) | Fast FH (FFH) |
|---|---|---|
| Hop rate vs. symbol rate | $< R_s$ (multiple bits/hop) | $> R_s$ (multiple hops/bit) |
| Dwell time $T_d$ | Longer than $T_b$ | Shorter than $T_b$ |
| Diversity order | Frequency diversity limited | High — error coding effective |
| Synchronization | Easier | Harder |
| Application | Bluetooth, ISM | Military ECCM |

##### Part (b) — CDMA Loading Analysis

**Given:** $R_c = 1.228$ Mcps, $R_b = 9.6$ kbps, $K = 25$ users, $(E_b/N_0)_{req} = 7$ dB, noise rise $\eta = 3$ dB.

**Step 1: Processing Gain (2 marks)**

$$
L_c = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.228 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 127.92 \approx 128
$$

$$
PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 21.07 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2: Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear (1 mark)**

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{7/10} = 5.012
$$

**Step 3: Pole Capacity (Maximum Users, Single-Cell) (2 marks)**

$$
K_{max} = 1 + \frac{L_c}{E_b/N_0} = 1 + \frac{128}{5.012} = 1 + 25.54 = 26.54
$$

**Step 4: Apply Noise-Rise / Loading Factor (2 marks)**

With $\eta = 3$ dB $= 2$ (linear), the **operating capacity** is:

$$
K_{operational} = \frac{K_{max}}{\eta} = \frac{26.54}{2} = 13.27 \text{ users}
$$

**Step 5: Decision (1 mark)**

Since the actual number of users ($K = 25$) **exceeds** $K_{operational} = 13.3$, the system is **OVER-LOADED**. The cell is supporting more users than the standard pole capacity allows under the given noise-rise constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Fix:** The cell must either (i) drop 12 users (admission control), (ii) increase $R_c$ (broaden bandwidth), (iii) deploy additional sectors or a second carrier, or (iv) use multi-user detection to suppress interference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls in CDMA Problems:**
