A Pizza, a Burger, or a Soft Drink. These are what customers actually *order* — Voice calls, SMS, MMS, Fax. The customer doesn't care how the kitchen cooked it; they want the final meal. > - **Supplementary Services** = The **extras on the side**. Extra cheese, a free drink refill, a waiter holding the door. They enhance the main dish but don't exist on their own. Call forwarding, call waiting, and caller ID are GSM's "side dishes." ### The Three Pillars of GSM Services | Pillar | Layer Level | Standard Reference | Function | |--------|-------------|-------------------|----------| | Bearer Services | Lower (Transport) | GSM 02.02 | Provides the raw data transmission pipeline | | Teleservices | Higher (Application) | GSM 02.03 | Provides end-user communication applications | | Supplementary Services | Enhancement | GSM 02.04 / 02.8x | Modifies or supplements a teleservice | ### Key Physical/Standard Metrics - **Operating Frequency Bands**: **900 MHz** (Primary GSM band), **1800 MHz** (DCS-1800), **1900 MHz** (PCS-1900 in North America). - **Channel Bandwidth**: **200 kHz** per RF carrier. - **Number of RF carriers in 900 MHz band**: **124** carriers. - **Modulation Technique**: **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) with a spectral efficiency of **1 bit/sec/Hz**. - **Standard Body**: **ETSI** (European Telecommunications Standards Institute). > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to "List and explain the three categories of GSM services." Memorize this classification precisely with the GSM 02.0x standard numbers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Bearer Services Bearer services are the **lower-layer telecommunication services** of GSM that provide the capability to transmit user data (information) between defined access points (i.e., the mobile station and the fixed network side, typically the **MSC** — Mobile Switching Center). They define the **"how" of transmission**. #### Characteristics of Bearer Services: - They operate at the **OSI Layers 1, 2, and 3**. - They are described by standardized attributes: - **Information Transfer Capability** (e.g., speech, digital, video). - **Information Transfer Mode** (Circuit-switched or Packet-switched). - **Information Transfer Rate** (e.g., 2.4 kbps, 4.8 kbps, 9.6 kbps). - **Signalling Access Protocol** (e.g., X.31, X.25). - They are essentially **"transparent"** — the network treats user data as a bitstream without interpreting its contents. #### Categories of Bearer Services: **A. Data CDA (Circuit-switched Data, Asynchronous)** - Asynchronous transmission between the mobile station and a data network. - Data rates: **300 bps to 9.6 kbps**. **B. Data CDS (Circuit-switched Data, Synchronous)** - Synchronous transmission with fixed clocking. - Data rates: **1.2 kbps, 2.4 kbps, 4.8 kbps, 9.6 kbps**. **C. Data PAD (Packet Assembly/Disassembly)** - Packet-switched access where a PAD facility in the GSM network connects the mobile user to a packet-switched public data network (PSPDN). - Uses the **X.25 protocol**. **D. Alternate Speech / Data** - Allows the user to alternate between speech and data during the same call (established via a single setup). **E. Speech followed by Data** - Speech is established first; after speech is finished, the call can be switched to data mode without releasing the connection. > [!NOTE] > **Why Bearer Services Matter:** > In the early 1990s, Bearer Services were groundbreaking because they allowed a mobile phone to act as a wireless modem for laptops — a precursor to modern mobile broadband. Today, they have evolved into **GPRS Bearer Services** and **UMTS Bearer Services** in 3G/4G systems. ### 2.2 Teleservices Teleservices are the **higher-layer telecommunication services** that the GSM network offers to its subscribers as complete applications, including terminal equipment functions. The network *interprets* and *processes* the user information, unlike bearer services. #### Key Teleservices in GSM: **1. Telephony (Emergency Calls included)** - The most fundamental GSM service — bidirectional voice communication. - Uses **digital speech encoding** at a net bit rate of **13 kbps** (gross **22.8 kbps** with channel coding), employing the **RPE-LTP** (Regular Pulse Excitation — Long-Term Prediction) codec. - Emergency calls (e.g., **112** in Europe, **911** in the US) are accepted *without a SIM card*. **2. Emergency Calls** - Special variant of Telephony that works even when no SIM is inserted. - Routed to the nearest emergency response center. **3. Short Message Service (SMS)** - Bidirectional transmission of **text messages up to 160 alphanumeric characters** (using 7-bit GSM alphabet). - Uses a **store-and-forward** mechanism via the **SMSC** (Short Message Service Center). - Operates over the **SDCCH** (Stand-alone Dedicated Control Channel) or **SACCH** signalling channels — does not occupy a traffic channel. **4. SMS Cell Broadcast** - One-way broadcasting of short messages to all subscribers in a specific geographical area (cell). - Used for weather alerts, traffic updates, commercial advertisements. **5. Unstructured Supplementary Service Data (USSD)** - Real-time, session-based messaging (e.g., balance enquiry: *123#). - Faster than SMS because it uses a real-time signalling channel. **6. Multimedia Message Service (MMS) — Introduced in Phase 2+** - Extension of SMS to support images, audio, video clips. - Requires GPRS or higher data rates. **7. Fax (Group 3)** - Facsimile transmission over GSM. - Modifies voice codec; uses the **T.30** fax protocol. **8. Internet Access** - Indirectly through bearer services + teleservice combination (e.g., CSD with PPP). ### 2.3 Supplementary Services Supplementary services **modify or supplement** an existing basic teleservice (typically telephony). They cannot exist standalone; they "ride on" a basic service. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference:** Defined under GSM 02.04, GSM 02.81, GSM 02.82, GSM 02.83, GSM 02.84, GSM 02.86, GSM 02.88. #### Phase 1 Supplementary Services: | Supplementary Service | Standard | Code / Description | |----------------------|----------|-------------------| | Calling Line Identification Presentation (CLIP) | GSM 02.81 | Displays caller's number on screen | | Calling Line Identification Restriction (CLIR) | GSM 02.81 | Hides caller's number | | Connected Line Identification Presentation (COLP) | GSM 02.81 | Displays the connected party's number | | Connected Line Identification Restriction (COLR) | GSM 02.81 | Restricts connected number display | | Call Forwarding Unconditional (CFU) | GSM 02.82 | Forwards *all* incoming calls | | Call Forwarding on Mobile Subscriber Busy (CFB) | GSM 02.82 | Forwarding when user is busy | | Call Forwarding on No Reply (CFNRy) | GSM 02.82 | Forwarding when user doesn't answer | | Call Forwarding on Mobile Subscriber Not Reachable (CFNRc) | GSM 02.82 | Forwarding when user is unreachable | | Call Waiting (CW) | GSM 02.83 | Alerts user of another incoming call | | Call Hold (CH) | GSM 02.83 | Allows user to put one call on hold | | Multi-Party Service (MPTY) | GSM 02.84 | Conference call (up to 5 subscribers + 1 initiator) | | Closed User Group (CUG) | GSM 02.85 | Restricts calling to a defined group | | Advice of Charge (AoC) | GSM 02.86 | Real-time billing information | | Barring of All Outgoing Calls (BAOC) | GSM 02.88 | Blocks all outgoing calls | | Barring of All Incoming Calls (BAIC) | GSM 02.88 | Blocks all incoming calls | #### Phase 2 Supplementary Services: - **Call Deflection (CD)** — forwards a call before answering to a third party. - **Explicit Call Transfer (ECT)** — transfers an active call to a third party. - **User-to-User Signalling (UUS)** — sends a small amount of data along with call setup. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of Supplementary Services:** > From a real-world engineering standpoint, supplementary services are the foundation of modern **Intelligent Network (IN)** features. They are implemented via the **CAMEL** (Customized Applications for Mobile network Enhanced Logic) subsystem in modern GSM/UMTS/LTE networks, enabling services like prepaid billing, roaming, and VPN. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Standard / Formula | Description | |---------|-------------------|-------------| | Number of RF carriers | **124** (900 MHz band) | Total available GSM channels | | Gross bit rate per timeslot | **270.833 kbps** | Burst data rate | | Speech codec rate | **13 kbps** (net) | RPE-LTP encoder output | | Total gross rate with coding | **22.8 kbps** | After convolutional coding | | SMS character limit | **160 characters** | 7-bit GSM alphabet | | Channel spacing | **200 kHz** | FDMA separation | | Time slots per frame | **8** | TDMA structure | | Frame duration | **4.615 ms** | 8 time slots | | Modulation | **GMSK** | BT product = 0.3 | | Hop rate | **217 hops/sec** | Slow frequency hopping | | Maximum access delay | $\vert T_{access} \vert = 235$ ms | Random access grant | | Maximum call setup time | $\vert T_{setup} \vert \approx 30$ sec | E2E call establishment | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Module Scope Note:** > GSM Services is primarily a *descriptive and architectural* topic under the KTU 2024 Communication Techniques syllabus. Mathematical derivations are limited to the supporting physical layer parameters (bit rate, frame timing, channel bandwidth). The following derivations establish the foundation that the GSM Services layer assumes. ### 3.1 Derivation of the Gross Bit Rate in a GSM Time Slot **Given:** - Channel bandwidth (BW) per carrier: $BW = 200 \text{ kHz}$ - Modulation: **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) with spectral efficiency of $\eta = 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$. **Step 1 — Compute the Bit Rate per Carrier** According to Nyquist's no-ISI rule applied to a bandwidth-limited, spectrally-efficient modulation like GMSK: $$ \begin{aligned} R_b &= \eta \times BW \\ R_b &= (1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}) \times (200 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}) \\ R_b &= 200 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ This is the bit rate per RF carrier (per 200 kHz channel). **Step 2 — Account for TDMA Time Slot Structure** GSM divides each 200 kHz RF carrier into **8 time slots** (TDMA). One time slot is shared among 8 users (each using it 1/8th of the time). Therefore, the gross bit rate available to a *single user* during their time slot is: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{user} &= \frac{R_b}{N_{slots}} \\ R_{user} &= \frac{200 \text{ kbps}}{8} \\ R_{user} &= 25 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ This is the *slot gross* rate. However, the actual transmitted data within a burst contains training sequences, guard periods, and stealing bits, so the *real* usable gross bit rate per burst is approximately: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{burst} &= \frac{148 \text{ payload bits}}{577 \text{ µs}} \\ R_{burst} &\approx 256.5 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ The KTU-accepted canonical figure is **270.833 kbps** (which is derived from 156.25 bits per quarter-symbol slot at 1.08 GMSK symbol rate). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** Examiners expect **270.833 kbps** as the canonical GSM gross bit rate per time slot. Memorize this value directly. ### 3.2 Derivation of Frame and Multiframe Duration **Step 1 — Frame Duration** Each TDMA frame has 8 time slots, and the burst length is $T_{burst} = 577$ µs. Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{frame} &= N_{slots} \times T_{burst} \\ T_{frame} &= 8 \times 577 \text{ µs} \\ T_{frame} &= 4616 \text{ µs} \\ T_{frame} &\approx 4.615 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Multiframe Duration (Traffic Channel — TCH)** A traffic multiframe contains **26 frames**. Duration: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{TCH\_multiframe} &= 26 \times T_{frame} \\ &= 26 \times 4.615 \text{ ms} \\ T_{TCH\_multiframe} &\approx 120 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Slow Frequency Hopping Rate** In GSM, frequency hopping occurs at the frame rate: $$ \begin{aligned} f_{hop} &= \frac{1}{T_{frame}} \\ &= \frac{1}{4.615 \times 10^{-3}} \\ f_{hop} &\approx 217 \text{ hops/sec} \end{aligned} $$ This is the canonical **217 hops/second**, which gives GSM its name "**Slow** Frequency Hopping" (vs. 1600 hops/sec in Bluetooth). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bearer vs. Teleservices vs. Supplementary | Parameter | Bearer Service | Teleservice | Supplementary Service | |-----------|----------------|-------------|----------------------| | OSI Layer | Layer 1, 2, 3 | Layer 4, 5, 6, 7 | Layer 5–7 (overlay) | | Standalone? | Yes (as pipeline) | Yes (as application) | No (depends on teleservice) | | User Terminal | TE (Terminal Equipment) | ME (Mobile Equipment) + SIM | ME + SIM | | Examples | CSD 9.6 kbps, X.25 PAD | Voice, SMS, MMS, Fax | CLIP, CFU, MPTY, AoC | | Network Interprets User Data? | **No** (transparent) | **Yes** (e.g., SMS store-forward) | Varies (e.g., AoC interprets CDR) | | Standard | GSM 02.02 | GSM 02.03 | GSM 02.04 + 02.8x | | Switching Type | Circuit or Packet | Circuit (mostly) | Modifies existing call | | QoS Profile | MT/MN/HT setting | Standardized per teleservice | Inherits from base service | ### 3.4 Mathematical Representation of GSM Service Architecture The GSM Service layer can be represented as a hierarchical mathematical function: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{GSM} &= S_{tel} \oplus S_{sup} \\ S_{tel} &= T_{voice} \cup T_{SMS} \cup T_{MMS} \cup T_{fax} \\ T_{voice} &= f_{RPE-LTP}(A/D(x_{audio})) \\ B_{Bearer} &= \{ B_{CDA}, B_{CDS}, B_{PAD}, B_{SD}, B_{ASD} \} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $S_{GSM}$ = Total GSM Service set. - $S_{tel}$ = Teleservice set. - $S_{sup}$ = Supplementary Service set. - $T_{voice}$ = Voice teleservice, produced by passing analog audio $x_{audio}$ through an RPE-LTP codec. - $B_{Bearer}$ = Bearer service subsets. - $\oplus$ = Service composition operator. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level GSM Service Architecture (Functional Block Flow) The following Mermaid diagram represents the functional architecture of GSM Services, mapping how Bearer, Teleservices, and Supplementary Services interact with the GSM network elements. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User Mobile Station ME plus SIM"]:::user --> B["GSM Services Layer"]:::svc B --> C["Teleservices Layer"]:::tel B --> D["Bearer Services Layer"]:::bear B --> E["Supplementary Services Layer"]:::sup C --> C1["Telephony Voice 13 kbps"] C --> C2["SMS 160 chars"] C --> C3["MMS Phase 2 plus"] C --> C4["Fax Group 3"] C --> C5["Cell Broadcast"] D --> D1["Circuit Data CSD 9.6 kbps"] D --> D2["Packet Data PAD X.25"] D --> D3["Alternate Speech Data"] D --> D4["Speech then Data"] E --> E1["CLIP and CLIR"] E --> E2["Call Forwarding CFU CFB CFNR"] E --> E3["Call Waiting and Hold"] E --> E4["Multi Party Conference MPTY"] E --> E5["Barring BAOC BAIC"] C1 --> F["MSC Mobile Switching Center"]:::core C2 --> F C3 --> F D1 --> G["IWF Interworking Function"]:::core D2 --> G F --> H["HLR and VLR and AUC"]:::db F --> I["PSTN and ISDN and PSPDN"]:::ext classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef svc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tel fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bear fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sup fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef core fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ext fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The user enters through the Mobile Station (ME + SIM). The "Services Layer" acts as a router — selecting the appropriate category. Teleservices flow directly to the **MSC** (for voice, SMS). Bearer Services flow through the **IWF (Interworking Function)** for data conversion (e.g., to PSTN or X.25 networks). Supplementary Services modify the call record stored in the **HLR/VLR** databases. ### 4.2 Call Forwarding Supplementary Service — Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as External Caller A participant MSC as MSC of User B participant HLR as HLR of User B participant VLR as VLR of User B participant UserB as User B Mobile participant UserC as Forwarded Number C Caller->>MSC: Initial Call Setup to B MSC->>HLR: Send RoutingInfo Request for B HLR-->>MSC: Activate CFU flag set TRUE MSC->>HLR: Get Forwarded Number HLR-->>MSC: ForwardedTo = C MSC->>UserC: Route call to C directly UserC-->>Caller: Call connected at C Note over UserB: B never rings
No charge to B typically ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Insight:** This sequence is a *favourite* in KTU Part B questions. Be ready to draw it when asked: *"Explain Call Forwarding Unconditional with a suitable diagram."* ### 4.3 GSM Service Delivery — Protocol Layer Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["Application Layer User Apps"]:::app --> L2["Teleservices GSM 02.03"]:::tel L2 --> L3["Supplementary Services GSM 02.04"]:::sup L3 --> L4["Bearer Services GSM 02.02"]:::bear L4 --> L5["Physical Layer TDMA plus FDMA"]:::phy L5 --> L6["Radio Interface Um Interface"]:::radio classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef tel fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef sup fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef bear fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef phy fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef radio fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *Define GSM Bearer Services and Teleservices. Give two examples of each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Bearer Services (1.5 Marks):** Bearer services are the lower-layer telecommunication services of GSM that provide the capability to transmit user information between defined access points (MS ↔ MSC). They operate at OSI layers 1, 2, and 3 and treat user data transparently. They are defined under **GSM 02.02**. Examples: 1. **Data CDS** — Synchronous circuit-switched data, up to 9.6 kbps. 2. **Data PAD** — Packet-switched data via PAD to PSPDN. **Teleservices (1.5 Marks):** Teleservices are higher-layer GSM services that provide complete end-user communication applications, including terminal functions. They are defined under **GSM 02.03**. Examples: 1. **Telephony** — Bidirectional voice using RPE-LTP codec at 13 kbps. 2. **Short Message Service (SMS)** — Text messages up to 160 characters via SMSC. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]** *What are supplementary services in GSM? List any four Phase 1 supplementary services with their standard codes.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Supplementary services are additional GSM services that **modify or supplement** a basic teleservice (typically telephony). They cannot stand alone and are defined under GSM 02.04 and the GSM 02.8x series. **Four Examples (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** | # | Service | Standard | |---|---------|----------| | 1 | Calling Line Identification Presentation (CLIP) | GSM 02.81 | | 2 | Call Forwarding Unconditional (CFU) | GSM 02.82 | | 3 | Call Waiting (CW) | GSM 02.83 | | 4 | Multi-Party Service (MPTY) | GSM 02.84 | --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in Communication Techniques carry **14 marks** and typically offer **internal choice** between two questions from the same module. Below are two fully-worked model answers. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]* **(a)** Explain in detail the three categories of GSM Services with suitable diagrams. (7 Marks) **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **Call Forwarding Unconditional (CFU)** supplementary service. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) The GSM Services are classified by ETSI into three major categories: **1. Bearer Services (2 Marks):** Bearer services provide the underlying **transport capability** for transmitting user data between access points (e.g., MS and MSC). They operate transparently on the user data. Bearer services are governed by **GSM 02.02**. They specify attributes like: - Information Transfer Capability (speech, digital, video) - Transfer Mode (Circuit/Packet) - Transfer Rate (e.g., **2.4, 4.8, 9.6 kbps**) - Signalling Access Protocol Examples: **Data CDA, Data CDS, Data PAD, Alternate Speech/Data.** **2. Teleservices (2 Marks):** Teleservices provide **complete end-user applications** including terminal functionality. The GSM network actively processes the user data. Governed by **GSM 02.03**. Examples: **Telephony, SMS, Fax, Cell Broadcast, Emergency Calls.** **3. Supplementary Services (2 Marks):** These services **modify or supplement** an existing basic teleservice. They cannot be used standalone. Governed by **GSM 02.04** + **GSM 02.8x** series. Examples: **CLIP, CLIR, CFU, CFB, MPTY, AoC, Call Barring.** **Diagram (1 Mark):** ```mermaid flowchart TD R["GSM Services"] --> A["Bearer Services GSM 02.02"] R --> B["Teleservices GSM 02.03"] R --> C["Supplementary Services GSM 02.04"] A --> A1["Transport Layer"] B --> B1["Application Layer"] C --> C1["Enhancement Layer"] classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef A1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 class R root ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Call Forwarding Unconditional (CFU)** — Standard **GSM 02.82**. **Concept (2 Marks):** CFU forwards **every incoming call** intended for a GSM subscriber to a designated forwarded-to number without ringing the original subscriber's phone. The forwarding happens regardless of the subscriber's state (busy, no-reply, unreachable, or idle). **Working Steps (3 Marks):** 1. User B (the subscriber) registers the forwarding destination (e.g., number C) via **USSD/MMI command `*21*#`**. This is stored in the **HLR** in B's subscriber profile. 2. When an external caller (A) dials B's number, the call enters B's serving **GMSC** (Gateway MSC). 3. The GMSC sends a **Send Routing Information (SRI)** request to B's **HLR**. 4. The HLR checks B's subscription — discovers CFU flag is **set TRUE** — and responds with the forwarded-to number (C) instead of B's current location. 5. The GMSC routes the call to C directly. 6. Subscriber B's phone does **not ring** — the user is unaware of the call attempt (unless Network-Indicator is enabled). **Diagram (2 Marks):** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Caller A participant GMSC as GMSC participant HLR as HLR of B participant B as Subscriber B participant C as Forwarded Number C A->>GMSC: Call to B GMSC->>HLR: SRI for B HLR-->>GMSC: CFU active, Forward to C GMSC->>C: Connect call to C Note over B: B does not ring ``` **Key Implementation Note:** The HLR stores the forwarding parameters. When B later cancels CFU with **#21#**, the HLR flag is reset, and calls reach B directly. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4]* (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a)** Discuss in detail the GSM Teleservices with reference to the underlying bearers and protocols. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the **Multi-Party Service (MPTY)** supplementary service. How does it differ from Call Hold and Call Waiting? (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Introduction (1 Mark):** GSM Teleservices, defined under **GSM 02.03**, are end-to-end applications provided to the user. Each teleservice uses one or more bearer services as the underlying transport mechanism. **List of Major Teleservices (3 Marks — 0.5 each):** | Teleservice | Bearer Used | Notes | |-------------|-------------|-------| | Telephony | Speech bearer (13 kbps RPE-LTP) | Bidirectional voice | | SMS | SDCCH/SACCH signalling | 160 chars, store-forward via SMSC | | Cell Broadcast | CBCH channel | One-way area messages | | Fax (Group 3) | Speech bearer with T.30 | Facsimile transmission | | USSD | FACCH signalling channel | Real-time queries | | MMS (Phase 2+) | GPRS/EDGE bearer | Multimedia messages | **Detailed Working — SMS (3 Marks):** 1. User A composes an SMS and presses "Send." 2. The MS transmits the SMS via the **SDCCH** (Stand-alone Dedicated Control Channel) during signalling to the **BTS → BSC → MSC**. 3. The MSC routes the SMS to the **SMSC** (Short Message Service Center) using the **MAP** protocol. 4. The SMSC stores the SMS and queries the **HLR** for the recipient's current location (MSC address). 5. The SMSC forwards the SMS to the recipient's current MSC. 6. The recipient's MS is paged; SMS is delivered over the SDCCH or SACCH. 7. A delivery report is sent back to the originating SMSC, which notifies User A. **Real-World Significance:** SMS remains one of the most revenue-generating services globally, despite the rise of IP-based messengers. It serves as a **fallback** during network outages and is the basis for OTP (One-Time Password) authentication systems. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Multi-Party Service (MPTY)** — Standard **GSM 02.84**. **Concept (2 Marks):** MPTY allows a GSM subscriber to establish a **conference call** between **3 to 6 participants** (1 initiator + up to 5 remote parties). The initiator can speak to all parties simultaneously and can selectively drop or add participants. **Working Steps (2 Marks):** 1. The conference initiator (B) must first establish a normal call with party A. 2. B places A on hold (using Call Hold) and makes a second call to party C. 3. After C answers, B issues the MPTY command (USSD `3` on most handsets). 4. The MSC uses a **bridge conference circuit** to merge the calls. All three parties can now speak/hear each other. 5. B can add party D, E, F using the same procedure (up to 5 remote parties). 6. B can **release a specific party** by selecting them in the menu; the remaining parties stay connected. **Comparison Table (3 Marks — 1 each):** | Parameter | Multi-Party (MPTY) | Call Hold (CH) | Call Waiting (CW) | |-----------|-------------------|----------------|-------------------| | Function | Merge multiple calls | Suspend one call, make another | Alert of new incoming call | | Max parties | 6 (1 + 5) | 2 | 2 | | Active call | All | One at a time | Original active + new alerted | | Charges | Often per-leg | Per active connection | Per active connection | | Standard | GSM 02.84 | GSM 02.83 | GSM 02.83 | **Key Difference:** - Call Waiting **notifies** but does **not merge** calls. - Call Hold **suspends** one call while making another (no merging). - Multi-Party **merges** multiple active calls into a single conference bridge. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Bearer with Teleservices:** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if a student gives "voice" as a Bearer Service example. Voice is a **Teleservice**. Bearer services are *data* transport capabilities. > 2. **Forgetting Standard References:** GSM 02.02 / 02.03 / 02.04 references are expected. Omitting them may cost **0.5–1 mark**. > 3. **Missing the "supplementary depends on teleservice" relationship:** CFU and CLIP cannot exist standalone. If a student implies they can, marks are deducted. > 4. **Confusing SMS character limit:** SMS limit is **160 characters** using the 7-bit GSM alphabet — not 140 (which is the Octet-level limit before segmentation). Mismatch costs marks. > 5. **Skipping the diagram:** Part B (7 marks) without a diagram loses **2 marks** at minimum, per KTU valuation norms. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 📘 **Three GSM Service Categories:** Bearer (GSM 02.02), Teleservices (GSM 02.03), Supplementary (GSM 02.04 + 02.8x). - 📘 **Bearer Services:** Transparent, transport-layer, no interpretation; circuit or packet; up to **9.6 kbps** in GSM Phase 1. - 📘 **Teleservices:** Application-layer; user-perceived services; include Voice, SMS, MMS, Fax. - 📘 **SMS Limit:** **160 characters** (7-bit GSM alphabet); uses **SDCCH/SACCH** — does not occupy a TCH. - 📘 **Supplementary Services:** Modify or supplement a teleservice; cannot exist standalone. - 📘 **Phase 1 Supplementary Services:** CLIP, CLIR, COLP, COLR, CFU, CFB, CFNRy, CFNRc, CW, CH, MPTY, CUG, AoC, BAOC, BAIC. - 📘 **Phase 2 Supplementary Services:** Call Deflection (CD), Explicit Call Transfer (ECT), User-to-User Signalling (UUS). - 📘 **MPTY:** Conference call — max **6 parties** (1 initiator + 5 remote). - 📘 **CFU:** Forwards ALL calls unconditionally — set via `*21*#`, cancel via `#21#`. - 📘 **Emergency Calls:** Work even **without a SIM card** (e.g., 112 in Europe, 911 in the US). - 📘 **Speech Codec:** RPE-LTP at **13 kbps** net; **22.8 kbps** gross with channel coding. - 📘 **Modulation:** **GMSK** with BT = 0.3. - 📘 **RF Carriers:** **124 carriers** in the 900 MHz band; 200 kHz spacing. - 📘 **Bit Rates to Memorize:** **270.833 kbps** (gross per slot), **4.615 ms** (frame duration), **217 hops/sec** (frequency hopping rate). - 📘 **Real-world application:** All modern Supplementary Services run on **CAMEL (Customized Applications for Mobile network Enhanced Logic)** — vital for prepaid billing and roaming.

topics.title: Architecture of GSM Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Architecture of GSM Network # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Global System for Mobile Communications (GSM)** is a second-generation (2G) digital cellular telecommunications standard defined by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)**. The **GSM Network Architecture** refers to the structured, modular arrangement of functional entities, interfaces, and signalling protocols that collectively enable seamless mobile voice, data, and supplementary service delivery across diverse geographical regions using **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** over Frequency Division Duplex (FDD) carriers. The architecture is formally partitioned into four principal subsystems: 1. **Mobile Station (MS)** — the user equipment domain 2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)** — the radio access network 3. **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)** — the core network 4. **Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS)** — the management plane > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The architecture is examined primarily from the viewpoint of (a) functional block identification, (b) signalling interface mapping (Um, Abis, A, B…G), and (c) the role of each database (HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR) in call routing, authentication, and mobility management. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — "The Post Office Network"** Imagine the GSM network as a **giant national postal system**: - The **Mobile Phone (MS)** is like a *letter sender/receiver* — it carries a unique ID (IMSI) and a SIM card (like an ID card). - The **BTS (Base Transceiver Station)** is the *local postbox* on a street corner — it just collects/drops letters wirelessly. - The **BSC (Base Station Controller)** is the *local post office* — manages many postboxes, handles radio resources. - The **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** is the *main sorting hub* of a city — routes calls between mobile users and to the landline PSTN. - The **HLR (Home Location Register)** is your *home address registry* in your home city — knows where you permanently belong. - The **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** is the *temporary visitor log* at whichever city you are currently visiting. - The **AUC (Authentication Center)** is the *security check* — verifies your ID is not forged. - The **EIR (Equipment Identity Register)** is the *blacklist of stolen phones*. This modular "postal hierarchy" ensures a call from Kerala can reach a roaming user in Delhi — automatically, securely, and seamlessly. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Uplink Band (MS → BTS) | **890 – 915 MHz** | | Downlink Band (BTS → MS) | **935 – 960 MHz** | | Bandwidth per Channel (RF) | **200 kHz** | | Duplex Spacing | **45 MHz** | | Number of RF Carriers | **124** | | TDMA Slots per Carrier | **8** | | Speech Codec | **RPE-LTP (13 kbps)** | | Modulation | **GMSK (BT = 0.3)** | | Access Scheme | **TDMA/FDMA (with FDD)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Subsystem Breakdown ### A. Mobile Station (MS) - **Components:** Mobile Equipment (ME) + Subscriber Identity Module (SIM). - **Identity stored:** IMEI (in ME), IMSI, Ki, TMSI (in SIM). - **Functions:** Radio transmission/reception, speech encoding/decoding, signalling, user authentication. - **Classes of MS:** Class 1 (vehicle-mounted, 20W), Class 2 (portable, 8W), Class 3 (handheld, 5W), Class 4 (handheld, 2W), Class 5 (handheld, 0.8W). ### B. Base Station Subsystem (BSS) The BSS bridges the MS and the NSS. It consists of two key elements: **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** - Houses the radio transceivers and antennas. - Performs channel coding, encryption, modulation/demodulation. - Handles the **air interface (Um)** directly with the MS. - One BTS can serve multiple cells (typically 1–3 sectors). **BSC (Base Station Controller):** - Manages multiple BTSs (typically several hundred). - Controls **radio channel allocation**, handover decisions, frequency hopping, and power control. - Acts as a concentrator for traffic to the MSC. ### C. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS) The "brain" of GSM. It performs call switching, subscriber management, and inter-system roaming. | NSS Element | Full Form | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | **MSC** | Mobile Switching Center | Call switching, routing, signalling coordination | | **HLR** | Home Location Register | Stores permanent subscriber data (IMSI, MSISDN, services) | | **VLR** | Visitor Location Register | Stores temporary data of visiting subscribers | | **AUC** | Authentication Center | Generates SRES, Kc, RAND triplets for security | | **EIR** | Equipment Identity Register | Whitelist/Blacklist of mobile equipment (IMEI-based) | | **GMSC** | Gateway MSC | Interface point between GSM network and external PSTN/ISDN | ### D. Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS) - **OMC (Operations and Maintenance Center)** — centralized network management. - Handles fault management, performance monitoring, configuration, and security. - Connected to all BSCs and MSCs via the **Q interface** (proprietary or TCP/IP-based). ## 2.2 Signalling Interfaces (KTU High-Yield) | Interface | Between | Protocol | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Um** | MS ↔ BTS | Radio (L1/L2/L3) | Air interface | | **Abis** | BTS ↔ BSC | LAPD-based | BTS control and traffic | | **A** | BSC ↔ MSC | BSSAP/SS7 | Call control, handover | | **B** | MSC ↔ VLR | MAP/SS7 | VLR updates | | **C** | MSC ↔ HLR | MAP/SS7 | Routing queries | | **D** | HLR ↔ VLR | MAP/SS7 | Subscriber data retrieval | | **E** | MSC ↔ MSC | MAP/SS7 | Inter-MSC handover | | **F** | EIR ↔ MSC | MAP/SS7 | IMEI validation | | **G** | VLR ↔ VLR | MAP/SS7 | Location update between VLRs | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic Tip for KTU Exams:** "**U** **A** are **B**ack **C**onnected to **D**ifferent **E**xternal **F**ixed **G**rids" → Um, Abis, A, B, C, D, E, F, G. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Relation | Notes | |---|---|---| | Total RF Carriers | $N_c = \dfrac{BW_{total}}{BW_{ch}} = \dfrac{25\,MHz}{200\,kHz} = 124$ | Uplink/Downlink each | | TDMA Frame Duration | $T_f = 4.615\,ms$ | 8 time slots | | Time Slot Duration | $T_s = \dfrac{4.615}{8} = 0.577\,ms$ | 156.25 bit periods | | Gross Bit Rate per Carrier | $R_b = \dfrac{156.25\,bits}{0.577\,ms} = 270.833\,kbps$ | Modulation = GMSK | | Speech + Coding Bit Rate | $R_{net} = 13\,kbps + \,coding$ | After RPE-LTP | | Total Channels per Cell | $C = N_c \times 8$ | 124 × 8 = 992 logical channels | | Duplex Spacing | $f_{down} - f_{up} = 45\,MHz$ | FDD separation | | Guard Band (Total) | $BW_{guard} = 25\,MHz - (124 \times 0.2\,MHz) = 0.2\,MHz$ | At edges of band | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables (e.g., $f_{down} \mid - \mid f_{up}$) when referring to absolute differences — never use the pipe symbol `|` to avoid table breakage. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telecom Operators (Airtel, Jio, Vi):** Use identical architectural blueprints to deploy nationwide 2G/2.5G/GPRS networks. - **IoT/M2M Communication:** GSM architecture (specifically GPRS subsystem) is still the backbone of low-power telemetry — smart meters, vehicle tracking. - **Roaming Services:** The HLR/VLR/AUC triad enables international roaming (e.g., a Keralite's phone working in Europe). - **Foundation for 3G/4G/5G:** The SGSN/GGSN of 2.5G evolved into the EPC (Evolved Packet Core) of 4G LTE. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Number of RF Carriers in the GSM Band We are given: - Total uplink bandwidth: **890 MHz to 915 MHz** → $BW_{up} = 25\,MHz$ - Channel bandwidth per carrier: **200 kHz** = $0.2\,MHz$ The number of RF carriers $N_c$ is calculated as the ratio of the total spectrum to the per-carrier bandwidth: $$ N_c = \dfrac{BW_{up}}{BW_{carrier}} = \dfrac{25\,MHz}{0.2\,MHz} $$ Performing the division explicitly: $$ N_c = \dfrac{25}{0.2} = \dfrac{25 \times 10}{2} = \dfrac{250}{2} = 125 $$ However, GSM **reserves one carrier (the topmost) as a guard band**, so the usable carriers are: $$ N_{c,usable} = 125 - 1 = 124 $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This 124-carrier count is **identical for both uplink and downlink** because GSM uses paired spectrum with a 45 MHz duplex spacing. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Logical Channels per Cell Each RF carrier in GSM is divided using **TDMA into 8 time slots per frame**. Thus, each carrier supports 8 logical channels. $$ C_{per\,carrier} = 8 \text{ time slots} $$ Total logical channels per cell (assuming all 124 carriers are reused or available in a sector): $$ C_{total} = N_{c,usable} \times C_{per\,carrier} = 124 \times 8 $$ Performing the multiplication: $$ C_{total} = 992 \text{ logical channels per cell} $$ > **Significance:** In a typical 3-sector cell, 992 channels are distributed such that ~330 are available per sector — sufficient for simultaneous voice calls + signalling overhead. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gross Bit Rate Calculation Given a TDMA frame duration of **$T_f = 4.615\,ms$** containing **8 time slots**, each time slot duration is: $$ T_s = \dfrac{T_f}{8} = \dfrac{4.615\,ms}{8} = 0.576875\,ms $$ Each time slot carries **156.25 bit periods**. The bit duration is: $$ T_b = \dfrac{T_s}{156.25} = \dfrac{576.875\,\mu s}{156.25} $$ Calculating: $$ T_b = 3.692\,\mu s $$ Thus, the gross bit rate per carrier is: $$ R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{1}{3.692 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 270.833\,kbps $$ This is the canonical GSM burst bit rate and is universally referenced as **270.833 kbps**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Speech Coding Budget The GSM speech encoder produces **260 bits every 20 ms** = **13 kbps** (RPE-LTP codec). The channel coding adds error protection bits: - Class Ia bits (most protected): 50 - Class Ib bits: 132 - Class II bits (unprotected): 78 Total per 20 ms frame: $$ \text{Coded bits} = 50 + 132 + 78 = 260 \text{ bits} $$ After convolutional coding (rate 1/2 applied to 50+132 bits = 182 bits → 364 bits) plus tail bits and stealing flags, the **full physical-layer frame carries 456 bits** per 20 ms. Total physical layer bit rate: $$ R_{physical} = \dfrac{456\,bits}{20\,ms} = 22.8\,kbps \text{ (per user)} $$ With 8 users per carrier, the carrier's required user-plane bit rate matches the **270.833 kbps** gross rate (overhead included). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: GSM Database Functions vs. Stored Data | Database | Stores | Updates On | Query Origin | |---|---|---|---| | **HLR** | IMSI, MSISDN, Ki, services, VLR address | Subscription change, location update | GMSC, VLR | | **VLR** | TMSI, MSRN, location area, IMSI (copied) | LA boundary crossing | MSC | | **AUC** | Ki, RAND, SRES, Kc triplets | Static (rarely) | HLR (internal) | | **EIR** | IMEI whitelist/grey/blacklist | Equipment status change | MSC at attach | ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Handover Decision Matrix | Handover Type | Trigger | Decision Authority | Interface Used | |---|---|---|---| | **Intra-cell (Intra-BTS)** | Time-slot reallocation | BSC | A (BSC ↔ MSC) | | **Inter-cell, Intra-BSC** | Better cell from same BSC | BSC | A | | **Inter-BSC, Intra-MSC** | New cell served by different BSC | MSC | E (MSC ↔ MSC) | | **Inter-MSC** | Subscriber crosses MSC boundary | Anchor MSC + Target MSC | E | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **A-bis interface** is **always between BTS and BSC only** — never with MSC. This is a frequent KTU MCQ trap. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level GSM Network Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB MS[Mobile Station
ME plus SIM Card] -->|Um Interface
Radio Air Link| BTS1[BTS Cell A1] MS -->|Um Interface
Radio Air Link| BTS2[BTS Cell A2] BTS1 -->|Abis Interface
LAPD| BSC1[BSC Site 1] BTS2 -->|Abis Interface
LAPD| BSC1 BTS3[BTS Cell B1] -->|Abis| BSC2[BSC Site 2] BTS4[BTS Cell B2] -->|Abis| BSC2 BSC1 -->|A Interface
BSSAP over SS7| MSC1[MSC Primary] BSC2 -->|A Interface
BSSAP over SS7| MSC1 MSC1 -->|B Interface
MAP| VLR1[VLR Region 1] MSC1 -->|C Interface
MAP| HLR[HLR Home Register] MSC1 -->|F Interface
MAP| EIR[EIR Equipment Register] MSC1 -->|D Interface
MAP| VLR1 HLR -->|Internal| AUC[AUC Auth Center] HLR -->|D Interface| VLR1 MSC1 -->|E Interface
MAP| MSC2[MSC Remote Region] MSC1 -->|Gateway Link| GMSC[Gateway MSC] GMSC -->|PSTN Trunk| PSTN[External PSTN Network] MSC1 -->|Q Interface| OMC[OMC Operation Center] BSC1 -->|Q Interface| OMC BSC2 -->|Q Interface| OMC ``` ## 4.2 Functional Plane Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PLANE1[TRANSMISSION PLANE] T1[Speech Path MS to BSC] T2[BSC to MSC Trunk] T3[MSC to PSTN Trunk] end subgraph PLANE2[SIGNALLING PLANE SS7] S1[DTAP Messages Um to A] S2[BSSMAP BSS to MSC] S3[MAP MSC to HLR VLR AUC EIR] end subgraph PLANE3[MANAGEMENT PLANE] M1[OMC Fault] M2[OMC Configuration] M3[OMC Performance] end MS[Mobile Station] --> T1 T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 MS -.-> S1 S1 -.-> S2 S2 -.-> S3 OMC[OMC] -.-> M1 OMC -.-> M2 OMC -.-> M3 ``` ## 4.3 Call Flow: Mobile-to-Mobile Call Setup (Originating Side) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MS as Mobile Station participant BTS as Base Transceiver Station participant BSC as Base Station Controller participant MSC as Mobile Switching Center participant VLR as Visitor Location Register participant HLR as Home Location Register participant GMSC as Gateway MSC MS->>BTS: Channel Request RACH BTS->>BSC: Forward Access on Abis BSC->>MSC: Assignment Required MSC->>VLR: Lookup TMSI and services VLR-->>MSC: Subscriber Valid MSC->>MS: Immediate Assignment SDCCH MS->>MSC: CM Service Request MSC->>VLR: Process Call MSC->>HLR: Send Routing Info MAP HLR->>VLR: Provide Roaming Number VLR-->>HLR: MSRN HLR-->>MSC: MSRN returned MSC->>GMSC: ISUP IAM with MSRN GMSC->>MSC: Route to called MSC MSC-->>MS: TCH Assigned Alerting ``` ## 4.4 Authentication Triplet Generation (AUC ↔ HLR ↔ MS) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AUC Database stores Ki per IMSI] --> B[Generate RAND random] B --> C[Compute SRES via A3 with Ki and RAND] B --> D[Compute Kc via A8 with Ki and RAND] C --> E[Triplet RAND SRES Kc] D --> E E --> F[HLR stores triplet] F --> G[VLR receives triplet] G --> H[MSC sends RAND to MS] H --> I[MS computes SRES locally using SIM Ki] I --> J{Compare SRES values} J -->|Match| K[AUTHENTICATION SUCCESS] J -->|Mismatch| L[AUTHENTICATION FAILURE] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List the four main subsystems of a GSM network. State the primary function of the Base Station Subsystem (BSS).** **Model Answer:** The four main subsystems of the GSM network are: 1. Mobile Station (MS) 2. Base Station Subsystem (BSS) 3. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS) 4. Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS) The **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)** is responsible for managing the **radio interface** with the mobile station. It comprises the **BTS (Base Transceiver Station)**, which handles the actual radio transmission and reception over the air interface (Um), and the **BSC (Base Station Controller)**, which controls multiple BTSs, performs **radio channel allocation, frequency hopping, handover management, and power control**. The BSS essentially bridges the MS with the MSC of the core network. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]** **Distinguish between HLR and VLR in a GSM network. Mention the interface used for communication between them.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | HLR (Home Location Register) | VLR (Visitor Location Register) | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Permanent home database per subscriber | Temporary database for visiting subscribers | | **Location** | Subscriber's home network | Current serving MSC's network | | **Data stored** | IMSI, MSISDN, Ki, subscribed services, current VLR address | TMSI, MSRN, current Location Area, IMSI (copy) | | **Update frequency** | On subscription change | On Location Area boundary crossing | | **Number per network** | One logical HLR per operator | Multiple VLRs (one per MSC region) | The interface used between HLR and VLR is the **D interface**, which uses the **MAP (Mobile Application Part) protocol** over the **SS7 signalling stack**. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 4]** **a) Describe the architecture of a GSM network with a neat block diagram. Explain the functions of MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, and EIR. (7 marks)** **b) Explain the various signalling interfaces in GSM. List the protocol stack layers used at the Um interface. (7 marks)** #### Part (a) — Model Solution: The GSM network architecture consists of four major subsystems, as illustrated below: **Block Diagram:** ``` +------------+ | OMC | (Operation & Maintenance) +-----+------+ | +---------+---------+ +------------+ | NSS | | BSS | | +-----+-----+ | | +-------+ | | | HLR | AUC | | <--> | | BSC | | | +-----+-----+ | | +--+-+-+ | | +-----+ | | | | | | | VLR | <------+ | +--+ |+-+ | | +-----+ | | |BTS|| | | +-----+ | | +---+ | | | EIR | | +-----------+ | +-----+ | | +-----+ | +-----------+ | | MSC | <------ | ---> | PSTN | | +-----+ | +-----------+ +-----------------+ ^ | (Um Interface) | [Mobile Station] ``` **Functions of NSS elements (7 marks detailed):** - **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** The central switching element. Routes incoming/outgoing calls, performs inter-MSC handover coordination, and acts as the interface to the PSTN. It also handles **ciphering** and initiates **authentication** requests. - **HLR:** The permanent database. Stores **IMSI, MSISDN, Ki, VLR address, and subscribed supplementary services**. It is queried via the **C interface** to determine the current location of a called mobile. - **VLR:** A temporary database attached to an MSC. Holds details (TMSI, MSRN, LA) for all subscribers currently inside its area. Avoids frequent HLR queries. - **AUC:** The security module. Stores the secret key **Ki** for each subscriber and generates **authentication triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc)** using algorithms **A3** and **A8**. The HLR fetches these triplets for authentication. - **EIR:** The equipment registry. Maintains three lists: **whitelist, greylist, and blacklist** of IMEI numbers. Stolen/blacklisted phones are denied service. #### Part (b) — Model Solution: **Signalling Interfaces (7 marks):** | Interface | Endpoints | Protocol | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Um | MS ↔ BTS | GSM L1/L2/L3 | Air interface | | Abis | BTS ↔ BSC | LAPD | BTS management & traffic | | A | BSC ↔ MSC | BSSAP (SS7) | Call control, handover signalling | | B | MSC ↔ VLR | MAP (SS7) | VLR location update | | C | MSC ↔ HLR | MAP (SS7) | Routing info retrieval | | D | HLR ↔ VLR | MAP (SS7) | Subscriber data exchange | | E | MSC ↔ MSC | MAP (SS7) | Inter-MSC handover | | F | MSC ↔ EIR | MAP (SS7) | IMEI validation | | G | VLR ↔ VLR | MAP (SS7) | Inter-VLR data transfer | **Um Interface Protocol Stack (3 layers):** - **Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** GMSK modulation, TDMA framing (8 slots/4.615 ms), 270.833 kbps. - **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer (LAPDm):** Modified LAPD for reliable radio link, ARQ, ciphering. - **Layer 3 — Network Layer:** Subdivided into **CM (Connection Management)**, **MM (Mobility Management)**, and **RR (Radio Resource Management)**. --- ### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2022, Module 4]** **a) Explain the functions of BTS and BSC. With a neat diagram, show how they connect to the MSC. (7 marks)** **b) Discuss the procedure for call setup from a mobile subscriber to a PSTN subscriber, including the role of GMSC. (7 marks)** #### Part (a) — Model Solution: **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** - Houses the **transmitter/receiver units** for one or more cells. - Performs **GMSK modulation/demodulation**, channel coding/decoding, equalization, and interleaving. - Implements the **Um interface** (physical + data link layer). - Carries out **measurement reporting** (signal strength, BER) for handover decisions. - Supports **frequency hopping** and **DTX (Discontinuous Transmission)**. **BSC (Base Station Controller):** - Controls and manages **multiple BTSs** (typically a few hundred). - Performs **radio channel allocation**, **paging**, and **access control**. - Manages **intra-BSC handovers** without MSC involvement. - Concentrates traffic to the MSC over the **A interface**. - Executes **power control** commands and processes measurement reports. **Connection Diagram:** ``` +-------+ Abis +-------+ | BTS | <---------> | | +-------+ | BSC | +-------+ Abis | | | BTS | <---------> +---+---+ +-------+ | +-------+ Abis | | BTS | <----- (omitted for brevity) +-------+ | | A Interface (BSSAP/SS7) v +-------+ | MSC | +-------+ ``` #### Part (b) — Model Solution: **Call Setup Procedure (Mobile → PSTN):** 1. **Channel Request (RACH):** MS sends a random access burst to BTS on the Random Access Channel. 2. **Immediate Assignment:** BSC allocates an **SDCCH (Standalone Dedicated Control Channel)** and instructs the MS via AGCH. 3. **CM Service Request:** MS sends a Connection Management Service Request containing the dialled PSTN number. 4. **Authentication & Ciphering:** MSC/VLR requests the **AUC triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc)** from HLR. MS computes SRES using SIM-based Ki; if matched, the connection is authenticated and ciphering with Kc is initiated. 5. **Subscriber Verification:** VLR confirms the subscriber is allowed to make outgoing calls. 6. **Routing via GMSC:** The MSC routes the call to the **Gateway MSC (GMSC)**. The GMSC acts as the **entry/exit point** between the GSM PLMN and the external PSTN. It performs **number analysis** and selects the appropriate outgoing trunk. 7. **TCH Assignment:** Once routing is complete, BSC allocates a **TCH (Traffic Channel)** and the MS moves from SDCCH to TCH. 8. **Alerting and Connect:** PSTN rings the called party; on answer, the call enters conversation state. **Role of GMSC:** It functions as the **switching gateway** between the GSM network and the fixed-line PSTN. For **incoming calls to mobile users**, the GMSC queries the HLR to obtain the **MSRN (Mobile Station Roaming Number)** and routes the call to the current VLR's MSC. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse BSS with BTS:** BSS = BTS + BSC. Students often list BSS and BTS as separate items in the four subsystems — this loses 1 mark. > 2. **Interface names are case-sensitive in KTU:** Write "Um", "Abis" (with capital B), "A-ter" (never "Ater" or "A-ter") — capitalization errors cost 0.5 mark per instance. > 3. **Always specify the protocol along with the interface:** A bare "Abis" earns partial credit; "Abis — LAPD protocol" earns full credit. > 4. **Never claim EIR stores IMSI** — it stores IMEI only. This is one of the most common 1-mark KTU MCQ traps. > 5. **In diagrams, ALWAYS show the interfaces as labelled arrows** between the blocks — examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if interfaces are not labelled. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GSM = 2G digital cellular standard** by ETSI, using **TDMA/FDD** with **GMSK** modulation. - **4 Subsystems:** MS, BSS (BTS + BSC), NSS (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR, GMSC), OSS (OMC). - **BTS = radio interface** (Um); **BSC = manages multiple BTSs** and is connected to BTS via **Abis** and to MSC via **A** interface. - **HLR = permanent home DB** (IMSI, Ki, services, VLR address); **VLR = temporary visitor DB** (TMSI, MSRN, LA). - **AUC** stores **Ki** and generates authentication triplets using **A3 (SRES)** and **A8 (Kc)** algorithms. - **EIR** stores **IMEI** lists (white/grey/black) — **not IMSI**. - **9 Standard Signalling Interfaces:** Um, Abis, A, B, C, D, E, F, G — all signalling beyond BSS uses **SS7/MAP** except Um (radio) and Abis (LAPD). - **Frequency bands:** Uplink 890–915 MHz, Downlink 935–960 MHz, **duplex spacing 45 MHz**, channel BW 200 kHz, **124 carriers** per direction, **8 TDMA slots/frame**, frame = 4.615 ms. - **Gross bit rate = 270.833 kbps** per carrier; speech codec = **RPE-LTP at 13 kbps**. - **GMSC = gateway to PSTN**; routes incoming calls by querying HLR for MSRN. - **Mnemonic for interfaces:** "**U** **A** **B**etter **C**onnectivity, **D**ifferent **E**xternal **F**ixes **G**racefully" → Um, Abis, A, B, C, D, E, F, G. - **Architecture evolved** into GPRS (2.5G with SGSN/GGSN) → UMTS (3G) → LTE EPC (4G) → 5GC (5G), but the **HLR/AUC evolved into HSS** (Home Subscriber Server) in modern networks.

topics.title: Overview of Mobile Communication Generations (1G to 4G)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Mobile Communication Generations (1G to 4G)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobile Communication Generations (1G to 4G)** refer to the successive evolutionary phases of wireless cellular telecommunications standards, each "generation" representing a paradigm shift in **multiple-access technique**, **modulation scheme**, **switching architecture**, and **data-handling capability**. The transitions are driven by the demand for higher spectral efficiency, greater user capacity, and richer service integration (voice → data → multimedia → IP-based broadband).

A generation (G) is fundamentally characterized by three intertwined metrics:
1. **Air-interface standard** (analog vs digital vs packet-switched IP).
2. **Peak user data rate** measured in **kbps**, **Mbps**, or **Gbps**.
3. **Service class** — from circuit-switched voice to converged IP multimedia.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Road-Width" Analogy**
> Imagine a single-lane village road. Only one bullock-cart can pass at a time, slowly — that is **1G (Analog, voice only)**.
> The road is now paved to a 2-lane highway; more cars can move, signals are clearer — that is **2G (Digital, voice + SMS)**.
> Add a third lane, put up traffic signals, and let trucks (data) use the road too — that is **2.5G / GPRS**.
> Widen it to a 4-lane expressway with digital toll booths and GPS — that is **3G (Mobile broadband, video calling)**.
> Finally, a multi-lane smart highway with no toll booths, all-electronic, IP-addressed vehicles — that is **4G (All-IP, LTE/WiMAX)**.

In short: **each generation is a "wider, smarter, faster" road for information**, and the shift is also a shift from **circuit-switched** to **packet-switched** infrastructure.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Markers (Bolded Metrics)

- **1G data rate:** ~ **2.4 kbps**
- **2G (GSM) data rate:** **9.6 kbps** to **64 kbps**
- **2.5G (GPRS) data rate:** **56–171 kbps**
- **3G (UMTS) data rate:** **144 kbps – 2 Mbps**
- **4G (LTE/LTE-A) data rate:** **100 Mbps (mobile) – 1 Gbps (fixed/pedestrian)**
- **Switching technology:** Circuit (1G, 2G) → Packet (3G, 4G)
- **Core metric for 4G compliance (ITU-R IMT-Advanced):** Peak spectral efficiency ≥ **15 bit/s/Hz** (DL) and **6.75 bit/s/Hz** (UL).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generation-Wise Operational Breakdown

**Generation 1G — Analog Cellular (≈ 1980s)**
- **Technology:** Analog Frequency Modulation (FM) with **FDMA** (Frequency Division Multiple Access).
- **Standards:** AMPS (USA), NMT (Nordic), TACS (UK), C-Netz (Germany).
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched, voice-only.
- **Limitations:** Poor voice quality, no security (no encryption), no roaming support, high battery drain.
- **Frequency band:** **800–900 MHz**.
- **Channel bandwidth:** **30 kHz** per user.

**Generation 2G — Digital Cellular (≈ 1990s)**
- **Technology:** Digital modulation — **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) in GSM, **QPSK** in IS-95.
- **Multiple Access:** **TDMA** (GSM) and **CDMA** (IS-95).
- **Standards:** GSM (Global System for Mobile), IS-95 (cdmaOne), D-AMPS.
- **Service upgrade:** Digital voice + **SMS** (Short Message Service) + low-rate data (CSD — 9.6 kbps).
- **Frequency bands:** **900 MHz / 1800 MHz** (GSM-900/DCS-1800), **1900 MHz** (PCS-1900).
- **Channel bandwidth:** **200 kHz** (GSM), **1.25 MHz** (IS-95).
- **Security:** Introduced **SIM card** and **A3/A8 ciphering algorithms**.

**Generation 2.5G / 2.75G — Stepping Stones to Data**
- **2.5G (GPRS — General Packet Radio Service):** Adds **packet switching over the GSM core**; theoretical max **171.2 kbps** (8 timeslots × 21.4 kbps).
- **2.75G (EDGE — Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution):** Uses **8-PSK** modulation (3 bits/symbol), boosting rates up to **384 kbps**.

**Generation 3G — Mobile Broadband (≈ 2000s)**
- **Technology:** Wideband Code Division Multiple Access (**WCDMA**) and **CDMA2000**.
- **Standards:** UMTS (3GPP), CDMA2000 (3GPP2), TD-SCDMA (China).
- **Multiple Access:** **CDMA** with 5 MHz carrier bandwidth.
- **Modulation:** **QPSK** (downlink) / **BPSK** (uplink); later **HSDPA → 16-QAM**, **HSUPA → QPSK/16-QAM**.
- **Data Rate:** **144 kbps (vehicular)**, **384 kbps (pedestrian)**, **2 Mbps (indoor)**.
- **Service:** Video calling, mobile TV, mobile internet, MMS, location-based services.
- **Key feature:** **Vodafone's "3G" data card**; first true mobile internet experience.

**Generation 3.5G / 3.75G — HSPA Family**
- **HSDPA (High-Speed Downlink Packet Access):** **3.6 Mbps → 14.4 Mbps** (with 16-QAM).
- **HSUPA (High-Speed Uplink Packet Access):** **5.76 Mbps** uplink.
- **HSPA+ (Evolved HSPA):** **42 Mbps** DL / **11.5 Mbps** UL using **64-QAM**, **MIMO 2×2**.

**Generation 4G — All-IP Mobile Broadband (≈ 2010s)**
- **Technology:** **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** + **MIMO** + **All-IP flat architecture (SAE/EPC)**.
- **Standards:** **LTE** (3GPP Release 8/9), **LTE-Advanced** (Release 10+), **WiMAX** (IEEE 802.16e/m).
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (downlink), **SC-FDMA** (uplink — to reduce PAPR).
- **Modulation:** **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** (up to 256-QAM in LTE-A Pro).
- **Data Rate:** **100 Mbps (high mobility) – 1 Gbps (low mobility)**.
- **Channel bandwidths:** **1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz** (scalable).
- **Latency:** **< 10 ms** (one-way, user-plane).
- **Core network:** Evolved Packet Core (EPC) — **fully packet-switched, no CS-domain**.

### 2.2 Comparative & Engineering Significance

Each generation reduced the **cost-per-bit** dramatically and increased **spectral efficiency** (bits/s/Hz). For 4G, the Shannon-inspired capacity in a given bandwidth is approached by advanced receiver processing (Turbo codes, then LDPC in 5G NR).

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Telecom operators** (e.g., Reliance Jio, Airtel in India) use this generational roadmap to plan **spectrum refarming** (releasing 3G spectrum to boost 4G LTE capacity).
- **Network planners** use 4G's OFDMA + MIMO to deliver **VoLTE** (Voice over LTE) — a fully packetized voice call.
- **Device chipset designers** (Qualcomm, MediaTek) design transceivers backward-compatible across 2G/3G/4G to ensure global roaming.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon's Channel Capacity (Why Each Generation's Data Rate Jumps)

The maximum achievable data rate (in **bit/s**) over an AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is given by the **Shannon-Hartley theorem**.

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\text{[bit/s]}$$

**Step-by-step analytical evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Recall that the noise power } N = N_0 \cdot B, \\
& \text{where } N_0 = k_B \cdot T \text{ is the noise spectral density (W/Hz).} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Substitute, we get } \frac{S}{N} = \frac{S}{N_0 B}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Therefore, the capacity equation becomes:} \\
& C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N_0 B}\right). \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{In cellular systems, the SNR is limited. For practical coding/modulation,} \\
& \text{achieved capacity } C_{\text{ach}} = \eta \cdot C_{\text{Shannon}}, \text{ where } \eta \in [0, 0.85] \text{ is implementation efficiency.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Engineering interpretation: as } B \text{ grows (e.g., 200 kHz} \to \text{20 MHz)}, \\
& \text{capacity grows almost linearly. This is why 4G uses wideband carriers.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check (4G, LTE cat-4):** $B = 20$ MHz, SNR $\approx 25$ dB $\Rightarrow S/N \approx 316$. 
> Shannon: $C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(317) \approx 164$ Mbps. Practical LTE achieves ~ **150 Mbps** (≈ 91% efficiency).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peak Data Rate of LTE (4G)

For a 4G LTE system with $N_{RB}$ Resource Blocks, $N_{SC}^{RB} = 12$ subcarriers per RB, and $N_{sym}$ OFDM symbols per subframe, the peak data rate depends on MIMO streams and modulation order $M$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Number of subcarriers } N_{SC} = 12 \times N_{RB}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Number of bits per symbol} = \log_2(M). \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{For 20 MHz, } N_{RB} = 100 \Rightarrow N_{SC} = 1200. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Symbol rate per subcarrier (CP-normal)} = 15{,}000 \text{ symbols/s for 15-kHz spacing} \\
& \text{per subframe of 1 ms containing 14 OFDM symbols.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Bits per subframe (1 ms)} = N_{SC} \times N_{sym} \times \log_2(M) \times N_{layers}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{For 64-QAM (6 bit/sym) and 2×2 MIMO, LTE cat-6 rate is:} \\
& R = 1200 \times 14 \times 6 \times 2 \;/\; 0.001 = 201.6 \text{ Mbps (≈ 200 Mbps).}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the peak rate quoted in LTE-Advanced with **2×2 MIMO and 64-QAM**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spectral Efficiency of Each Generation (in bit/s/Hz)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1G (AMPS):} \quad & \eta = \frac{2.4 \text{ kbps}}{30 \text{ kHz}} = 0.08 \text{ bit/s/Hz}. \\
\text{2G (GSM):} \quad & \eta = \frac{9.6 \text{ kbps}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = 0.048 \text{ bit/s/Hz}. \\
\text{2.5G (GPRS, 4 slots):} \quad & \eta = \frac{85.6 \text{ kbps}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = 0.428 \text{ bit/s/Hz}. \\
\text{3G (UMTS, 5 MHz):} \quad & \eta = \frac{2 \text{ Mbps}}{5 \text{ MHz}} = 0.4 \text{ bit/s/Hz} \text{ (peak sector, raw chip rate 3.84 Mcps)}. \\
\text{3.5G (HSPA+, 5 MHz):} \quad & \eta = \frac{42 \text{ Mbps}}{5 \text{ MHz}} = 8.4 \text{ bit/s/Hz}. \\
\text{4G (LTE-A, 20 MHz, 4×4 MIMO, 64-QAM):} \quad & \eta = \frac{600 \text{ Mbps}}{20 \text{ MHz}} = 30 \text{ bit/s/Hz}.
\end{aligned}
$$

This **monotonically increasing $\eta$** is the reason operators are migrating from 3G to 4G: **more bits per Hz = more revenue per MHz of spectrum**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why "4G" is a Pure IP Network

| **Layer** | **1G / 2G** | **3G** | **4G (LTE/EPC)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Circuit-Switched (CS) domain** | Yes (voice only) | Yes (voice + signaling) | **Removed (CSFB or VoLTE)** |
| **Packet-Switched (PS) domain** | No (or 2.5G) | Yes (limited) | **Yes (sole bearer)** |
| **Signaling Protocol** | SS7 | SS7 + Diameter | **Diameter + S1-AP (SCTP)** |
| **HLR / VLR** | Yes | Yes (HSS split) | **HSS only (flat)** |
| **Base-station interface** | Analog | ATM/AAL2 | **IP/SCTP (S1, X2)** |
| **Latency** | ~ 1 s (handoff) | 100 – 300 ms | **< 10 ms (one-way)** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evolution Flow Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["G1: 1980s AMPS NMT TACS Analog FM, FDMA, Voice Only, 2.4 kbps"] --> B["G2: 1991 GSM IS-95 Digital, TDMA and CDMA, SMS, 9.6 to 64 kbps"]
    B --> C["G2.5: 2000 GPRS Packet over GSM, 56 to 171 kbps"]
    C --> D["G2.75: 2003 EDGE 8-PSK, 384 kbps"]
    D --> E["G3: 2001 UMTS WCDMA 5 MHz, 144 kbps to 2 Mbps, Video call"]
    E --> F["G3.5: 2008 HSPA and HSPAplus, 16 and 64 QAM, 42 Mbps"]
    F --> G["G4: 2010 LTE OFDMA MIMO, 100 Mbps to 1 Gbps, All IP EPC"]
    G --> H["G5: 2020 NR Massive MIMO, mMTC, URLLC"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a generation's **functional capability** (technology + multiple access + peak rate). The arrows show the **technology inheritance path** — note that OFDMA in 4G does not exist in 3G; it is a clean architectural break introduced to defeat **ISI in wideband channels**.

### 4.2 Architecture Comparison — 3G vs 4G Core Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S3G["3G UTRAN plus CN Architecture"]
        N1["NodeB (Base Station)"] --> N2["RNC (Radio Network Controller)"]
        N2 --> N3["SGSN (Packet) and MSC (Circuit)"]
        N3 --> N4["HLR plus VLR and GGSN"]
    end
    subgraph S4G["4G E-UTRAN plus EPC Architecture Flat All IP"]
        E1["eNodeB (integrated BS plus controller)"] --> E2["MME plus S-GW plus P-GW EPC core"]
        E2 --> E3["HSS (single subscriber database)"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** In 3G, the **RNC** controlled multiple NodeBs; in 4G, the **eNodeB is the controller itself**. This is why 4G is called a "flat architecture" — fewer hops, lower latency, simpler mobility.

### 4.3 Multiple-Access Evolution Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1["FDMA used in 1G"] --> T2["TDMA used in 2G GSM"]
    T1 --> T3["CDMA used in 2G IS-95"]
    T2 --> T4["TDMA plus Packet used in GPRS and EDGE"]
    T3 --> T5["WCDMA used in 3G UMTS"]
    T5 --> T6["HSPA and HSPA+ same access, higher order modulation"]
    T6 --> T7["OFDMA used in 4G LTE"]
    T6 --> T8["SC-FDMA used in 4G LTE uplink"]
```
