## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
List any **six key differences between 3G and 4G mobile communication systems**.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Multiple Access:** 3G uses **WCDMA**; 4G uses **OFDMA (DL) / SC-FDMA (UL)**.
> 2. **Switching:** 3G is **packet + circuit (dual-mode)**; 4G is **purely packet-switched (All-IP)**.
> 3. **Peak Data Rate:** 3G = **2 Mbps**; 4G = **100 Mbps – 1 Gbps** (IMT-Advanced).
> 4. **Modulation:** 3G = **QPSK/BPSK**; 4G = **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM**.
> 5. **Latency:** 3G ≈ **100–300 ms**; 4G ≈ **< 10 ms** (one-way, user-plane).
> 6. **Core Network:** 3G has **UTRAN + SGSN/GGSN + MSC**; 4G has **E-UTRAN + EPC (MME/S-GW/P-GW)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is **GPRS**? Mention its **modulation scheme** and **maximum data rate**.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **GPRS (General Packet Radio Service)** is a **2.5G packet-switched overlay** on the GSM network, enabling always-on mobile data using the same TDMA frame structure as GSM.
> - **Modulation:** **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) — same as GSM.
> - **Maximum theoretical data rate:** **171.2 kbps** (using 8 time-slots, coding scheme CS-4).
> - **Practical rate:** **56–114 kbps** depending on terminal class and coding scheme.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **evolution of mobile communication from 1G to 4G**, highlighting the **technology, multiple-access technique, modulation scheme, peak data rate**, and **core services** of each generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the **architecture of the 4G LTE network (E-UTRAN + EPC)** and explain the function of the **eNodeB, MME, S-GW, and P-GW**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> | **Generation** | **Year** | **Standard/Technology** | **Multiple Access** | **Modulation** | **Peak Data Rate** | **Services** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1G | 1980s | AMPS, NMT, TACS | FDMA | Analog FM | 2.4 kbps | Voice only |
> | 2G | 1991 | GSM, IS-95 | TDMA / CDMA | GMSK / QPSK | 9.6–64 kbps | Voice, SMS |
> | 2.5G | 2000 | GPRS | TDMA + Packet | GMSK | 56–171 kbps | WAP, MMS, Always-on data |
> | 2.75G | 2003 | EDGE | TDMA | 8-PSK | 384 kbps | Higher data, video clip |
> | 3G | 2001 | UMTS, CDMA2000 | WCDMA | QPSK/BPSK | 2 Mbps | Video call, mobile internet |
> | 3.5G | 2008 | HSPA, HSPA+ | WCDMA | 16/64-QAM | 42 Mbps | Mobile broadband |
> | 4G | 2010 | LTE, LTE-A | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | QPSK–256-QAM | 1 Gbps | VoLTE, HD video, IoT |
>
> (Full 7 marks for complete table + 1-line explanation per generation.)

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> The 4G LTE network has a **flat, all-IP architecture** consisting of two parts:
>
> 1. **E-UTRAN (Evolved Universal Terrestrial Radio Access Network):** contains only the **eNodeB (eNB)** — the radio access node. The eNB integrates the functions of legacy NodeB and RNC. Functions: radio resource management, scheduling, handover, ciphering, IP header compression.
>
> 2. **EPC (Evolved Packet Core):** contains three key nodes:
>    - **MME (Mobility Management Entity):** handles **signaling** — attach/detach, tracking area update, bearer establishment, authentication via HSS.
>    - **S-GW (Serving Gateway):** the **local mobility anchor** for inter-eNB handovers; routes user-plane packets; performs lawful interception.
>    - **P-GW (Packet Data Network Gateway):** the **edge to the Internet**; assigns IP address, enforces QoS, acts as the mobility anchor for inter-3GPP handovers.
>
> **Interfaces:**
> - **Uu:** UE ↔ eNB (radio).
> - **S1-AP:** eNB ↔ MME (signaling) using **SCTP** over IP.
> - **S1-U:** eNB ↔ S-GW (user plane).
> - **X2:** eNB ↔ eNB (inter-base-station handover preparation, no RNC).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Compare **FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA** multiple-access techniques in terms of **bandwidth efficiency, user separation, interference, and suitability for mobile generations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4G LTE system uses **20 MHz bandwidth**, **64-QAM**, **2×2 MIMO**, and **normal CP**. Compute the **peak downlink data rate**, given: 12 subcarriers per Resource Block, 100 RBs in 20 MHz, 14 OFDM symbols per subframe, subframe duration 1 ms. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> | **Parameter** | **FDMA** | **TDMA** | **CDMA** | **OFDMA** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **User separation** | Frequency band | Time slot | Spread-spectrum code | Subcarrier group (time + frequency) |
> | **Bandwidth efficiency** | Low (guard bands) | Moderate | High (universal freq. reuse) | **Very high (orthogonal, no intra-cell interference)** |
> | **Inter-cell interference** | High (co-channel) | High | Moderate (soft capacity) | **Moderate (neighbouring cell sub-carrier collision)** |
> | **Used in generation** | 1G (AMPS) | 2G (GSM) | 2G IS-95, 3G (WCDMA) | **4G (LTE)**, 5G NR |
> | **Synchronization needed** | Frequency | Time | Power (near-far problem) | Time + frequency (very tight) |
> | **Suitability for high data** | Poor (narrowband) | Limited | Limited (chip rate bottleneck) | **Excellent (resilient to multipath via CP)** |
>
> (Full marks for the above comparative table + at least two concluding sentences.)

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify system parameters:**
> - $B = 20$ MHz $\Rightarrow N_{RB} = 100$
> - $N_{SC}^{RB} = 12$ subcarriers/RB
> - $N_{sym} = 14$ symbols per 1-ms subframe
> - Modulation: **64-QAM** $\Rightarrow \log_2(64) = 6$ bits per symbol
> - **MIMO:** $2 \times 2$ $\Rightarrow N_{layers} = 2$ spatial streams
>
> **Step 2 — Total subcarriers:**
>
> $$N_{SC} = 12 \times 100 = 1200 \text{ subcarriers}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Bits per subframe (1 ms):**
>
> $$N_{bits} = N_{SC} \times N_{sym} \times \log_2(M) \times N_{layers}$$
>
> $$N_{bits} = 1200 \times 14 \times 6 \times 2 = 201{,}600 \text{ bits per subframe}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Convert to data rate:**
>
> $$R = \frac{N_{bits}}{T_{subframe}} = \frac{201{,}600}{0.001} = 2.016 \times 10^{8} \text{ bit/s}$$
>
> $$\boxed{R_{\text{peak}} \approx 201.6 \text{ Mbps}}$$
>
> (This matches the LTE Category-6 peak DL rate.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up 2G and 3G data rates** — 2G GSM is **9.6 kbps** (not 64 kbps); **64 kbps** is the channel-data rate after channel coding.
> 2. **Confusing WCDMA with CDMA2000** — both are 3G, but WCDMA uses a 5-MHz chip rate of 3.84 Mcps; CDMA2000 1x uses 1.2288 Mcps.
> 3. **OFDMA in 4G is NOT the same as OFDM** — OFDM is a *modulation*; OFDMA is a *multiple-access scheme* using subsets of OFDM subcarriers per user.
> 4. **GPRS theoretical max is 171.2 kbps** (8 slots × 21.4 kbps), but in practice it is **56–114 kbps** because most handsets are Class 10 (4-slots).
> 5. **VoLTE is NOT 4G voice over circuit-switched fallback** — it is **IMS-based packet voice** over the LTE EPC; CSFB is the interim 2G/3G voice fallback.
> 6. **The 4G requirement of 1 Gbps is for *low-mobility*** (pedestrian/fixed); at high mobility (vehicular, 350 km/h), 4G targets only **100 Mbps**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **1G** = **Analog FM + FDMA + 2.4 kbps + Voice only** (AMPS, NMT, TACS).
- [x] **2G** = **Digital + TDMA/CDMA + GMSK/QPSK + Voice + SMS** (GSM, IS-95).
- [x] **2.5G (GPRS)** = **Packet over GSM**; **2.75G (EDGE)** = **8-PSK** boosting rate to 384 kbps.
- [x] **3G (UMTS)** = **WCDMA + 5 MHz + QPSK + 2 Mbps**; introduces video calling.
- [x] **3.5G (HSPA/HSPA+)** = **higher-order modulation + MIMO**; 42 Mbps.
- [x] **4G (LTE/LTE-A)** = **OFDMA + SC-FDMA + MIMO + All-IP EPC**; 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps; < 10 ms latency.
- [x] **Multiple-access migration:** FDMA → TDMA → CDMA → WCDMA → OFDMA.
- [x] **Modulation migration:** Analog FM → GMSK → 8-PSK → QPSK → 16/64/256-QAM.
- [x] **Switching migration:** Circuit → Circuit + Packet → **Pure Packet (IP)**.
- [x] **Core-network migration:** AMPS MSC → NSS (GSM) → UTRAN+CN → **Flat E-UTRAN+EPC**.
- [x] **Peak spectral efficiency target for 4G (IMT-Advanced):** ≥ **15 bit/s/Hz DL**.
- [x] **4G channel bandwidths:** **1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz** (scalable).
- [x] **Why OFDMA in 4G?** Resilient to **multipath ISI** (via cyclic prefix) and granular **frequency-domain scheduling**.
- [x] **Why SC-FDMA in 4G uplink?** Lower **PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio)** — saves mobile-battery power.
- [x] **4G peak rate formula (numerical):** $R = 12 \cdot N_{RB} \cdot N_{sym} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot N_{layers} / T_{subframe}$.
- [x] **Remember:** **GPRS max 171.2 kbps**, **EDGE max 384 kbps**, **3G max 2 Mbps**, **HSPA+ max 42 Mbps**, **4G max 1 Gbps** (low mobility).
- [x] **Security:** introduced in 2G via **SIM + A3/A8 ciphering**; 3G uses **AKA + KASUMI**; 4G uses **EPS-AKA + SNOW 3G / AES**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution and Need for 5G
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Evolution and Need for 5G # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **5G (Fifth Generation) Mobile Communication** is the latest generation of broadband cellular network technology engineered to deliver **multi-Gbps peak data rates**, **ultra-low latency (≈1 ms)**, **massive device connectivity (≈1 million devices/km²)**, and **network slicing** capabilities, operating across sub-6 GHz and millimeter-wave (mmWave, 24–52 GHz) spectrum bands as standardized by **3GPP Release 15 and beyond (2018 onwards)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **evolution to 5G** represents the systematic progression of cellular standards from analog voice systems (1G) through digital circuit-switched (2G), packet-switched mobile data (3G/4G), culminating in a unified **IP-based, software-defined, cloud-native radio access network (RAN)** architecture. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standardization Body:** 3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project) — Releases define what 5G *is*. > **Key Performance Indicator (KPI) Targets set by ITU-R IMT-2020:** > - Peak Data Rate: **20 Gbps (DL)** / **10 Gbps (UL)** > - User Experienced Rate: **100 Mbps (DL)** / **50 Mbps (UL)** > - Latency (User Plane): **1 ms (URLLC)** > - Connection Density: **10⁶ devices/km² (mMTC)** > - Spectrum Efficiency: **3× that of 4G** --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Highway System" View Imagine a city's road network evolving over decades: | Generation | Analogy | What Improved | |------------|---------|---------------| | 1G | Single muddy village road | Just voice — slow, unreliable | | 2G | Paved 2-lane road | Voice + SMS, digital clarity | | 3G | 4-lane highway | Mobile internet, video calls | | 4G | 8-lane expressway | HD streaming, app economy | | **5G** | **Smart multi-layered skyway** | **Sensors, autonomous cars, remote surgery, AR/VR — all simultaneously** | > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Intuition:** 4G was designed for *humans* holding smartphones. **5G is designed for *everything else too*** — fridges, cars, factory robots, surgeons operating remotely, and millions of IoT sensors. The "need" for 5G is essentially the need for **a network that can serve humans AND machines at the same time**, with deterministic latency. --- ## 1.3 Why 5G? — The Driving Forces 1. **Data Tsunami:** Global mobile data traffic is projected to exceed **1,300 Exabytes/month by 2030** (Ericsson Mobility Report) — 4G cannot scale. 2. **Latency-Sensitive Applications:** Tactile internet, autonomous vehicles, and Industry 4.0 demand **sub-millisecond reaction times**. 3. **IoT Explosion:** Billions of low-power sensors (smart agriculture, smart cities) require **massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC)**. 4. **Spectrum Scarcity:** Sub-3 GHz spectrum is saturated — 5G unlocks **mmWave bands** above 24 GHz. 5. **Energy & Cost Efficiency:** Network slicing and virtualisation drastically reduce per-bit energy consumption. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Cellular Evolution Timeline (1G → 5G) The progression follows three fundamental axes: **bandwidth ↑**, **latency ↓**, **spectral efficiency ↑**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Students must remember the **multiplexing/multiple-access technique** and **core network architecture** shift at each generation — this is a frequent 14-mark question area. | Generation | Era | Multiple Access | Core Network | Switching | Peak Data Rate | Typical Latency | |------------|-----|-----------------|--------------|-----------|----------------|-----------------| | **1G** | 1980s | FDMA | PSTN | Circuit | **2.4 kbps** | N/A | | **2G** | 1990s | TDMA / FDMA | PSTN | Circuit | **64 kbps** | ~300 ms | | **2.5G (GPRS)** | 2000 | TDMA + packet | Partial IP | Packet | **171 kbps** | ~150 ms | | **3G** | 2001–2010 | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | IP-MSC hybrid | Packet | **2 Mbps** | ~100 ms | | **3.5G (HSPA+)** | 2008 | WCDMA enhanced | All-IP | Packet | **42 Mbps** | ~50 ms | | **4G / LTE** | 2010–2020 | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | EPC (Evolved Packet Core) | All-IP | **1 Gbps (theoretical)** | ~10 ms | | **4.5G / LTE-A Pro** | 2016 | OFDMA + 256-QAM + Carrier Aggregation | EPC+ | All-IP | **3 Gbps** | ~5 ms | | **5G / NR** | 2019+ | OFDMA / NOMA / SCMA | 5GC (5G Core, SBA) | All-IP, Cloud-native | **20 Gbps** | **~1 ms** | > [!NOTE] > **5G NR (New Radio)** introduces three usage scenarios defined by ITU: > - **eMBB** — enhanced Mobile BroadBand (humans, video, VR) > - **URLLC** — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications (autonomous cars, remote surgery) > - **mMTC** — massive Machine-Type Communications (IoT sensors, smart metering) --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize these equations — they appear in derivations and numerical problems. | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use | |---|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** (bits/s) — upper bound on data rate in AWGN channel | | 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{C}{B} = \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Spectral Efficiency** (bits/s/Hz) | | 3 | $S/N \;(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **SNR in decibels** | | 4 | $P_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{sc}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot \text{coding rate}$ | **OFDM Resource Element throughput** (M = modulation order) | | 5 | Maximum Devices/km²: $\rho = \dfrac{1}{A_{\text{cell}}}$ where $A_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2$ | **Hexagonal cell area** (R = cell radius) | | 6 | Latency budget: $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | Used in URLLC design | | 7 | Path Loss (Friis FSPL): $L = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ (dB, d in km, f in MHz) | Free-space loss for mmWave planning | > [!TIP] > In KTU derivations, always state **boundary conditions** (e.g., Shannon's bound is *theoretical* and assumes Gaussian signaling, infinite bandwidth, and infinite block length — practical 5G NR uses LDPC/Polar codes that approach within 1–2 dB of this limit). --- ## 2.3 The Three Pillars of 5G Need ### Pillar 1 — eMBB (Enhanced Mobile Broadband) - Requires **wider bandwidth** (mmWave 800 MHz channels vs 4G's 20 MHz). - Peak spectral efficiency target: **30 bits/s/Hz (DL), 15 bits/s/Hz (UL)**. ### Pillar 2 — URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication) - Reliability target: **99.999%** (5 nines) packet success within 1 ms. - Achieved via **Mini-slots** (2–4 OFDM symbols), **diversity**, **FEC redundancy**. ### Pillar 3 — mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communications) - Supports **10⁶ devices/km²** with **>10 years battery life**. - Uses **grant-free access**, **narrowband IoT (NB-IoT)**, **LTE-M** heritage. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telemedicine:** Remote robotic surgery needs <5 ms one-way latency. - **Autonomous Vehicles:** V2X communication requires 1 ms latency at 99.999% reliability. - **Industry 4.0:** Factory AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) need deterministic wireless control. - **Smart Cities:** Massive sensor deployment for traffic, pollution, energy management. - **Immersive Media:** 8K VR streaming requires ~1 Gbps sustained. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Capacity & Why 5G Needs mmWave **Problem Statement:** A 4G LTE channel has 20 MHz bandwidth and SNR of 15 dB. A 5G NR channel uses 400 MHz at 28 GHz with SNR of 20 dB. Compare their Shannon capacities. **Given:** - 4G: $B_1 = 20 \text{ MHz}$, SNR₁ = 15 dB - 5G: $B_2 = 400 \text{ MHz}$, SNR₂ = 20 dB ### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale For 4G: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S_1}{N_1} &= 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} \\ &= 31.623 \quad \text{(linear)} \end{aligned} $$ For 5G: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S_2}{N_2} &= 10^{20/10} = 10^{2.0} \\ &= 100.0 \quad \text{(linear)} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* The decibel-to-linear conversion uses the relation $(S/N)_{\text{lin}} = 10^{(S/N)_{\text{dB}}/10}$ because decibels are defined as $10\log_{10}(\text{ratio})$. ### Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem For 4G: $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= B_1 \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_1}{N_1}\right) \\ &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 31.623) \\ &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(32.623) \end{aligned} $$ Now compute the logarithm numerically: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2(32.623) &= \frac{\log_{10}(32.623)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.5134}{0.3010} \approx 5.027 \text{ bits/s/Hz} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= 20 \times 10^{6} \times 5.027 \approx 100.54 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ For 5G: $$ \begin{aligned} C_2 &= B_2 \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_2}{N_2}\right) \\ &= 400 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(101) \\ &= 400 \times 10^{6} \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\ &= 400 \times 10^{6} \cdot \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \\ &= 400 \times 10^{6} \cdot 6.658 \\ &\approx 2663.2 \text{ Mbps} \\ &\approx 2.66 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Comparative Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C_2}{C_1} &= \frac{2663.2}{100.54} \approx 26.5 \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* 5G delivers **~26× higher capacity**, primarily driven by the **20× bandwidth expansion** (mmWave) and the **higher SNR** at short-range line-of-sight links. This is the mathematical foundation of *why* 5G moved to mmWave. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Connection Density vs Cell Radius (mMTC Analysis) **Problem:** Estimate the maximum number of IoT devices a hexagonal cell can support if the target is $10^6$ devices/km². What is the corresponding cell radius? ### Step 1 — Hexagonal Cell Area Formula $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot R^2 \end{aligned} $$ where $R$ is the cell radius in metres. ### Step 2 — Set Connection Density Target $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &= \frac{N_{\text{devices}}}{A_{\text{cell}}} = 10^{6} \text{ devices/km}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{N_{\text{devices}}}{\rho} = \frac{1}{10^{6}} \text{ km}^2 \\ &= 10^{-6} \text{ km}^2 = 1 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (per device on average)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for Cell Radius $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot R^2 &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ km}^2 \\ R^2 &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{3\sqrt{3}} \text{ km}^2 \\ &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{5.196} \\ &= 3.849 \times 10^{-7} \text{ km}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Take the square root: $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \sqrt{3.849 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ km} \\ &= 6.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ km} \\ &= 0.62 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* This counterintuitive result shows that achieving **10⁶ devices/km²** requires **ultra-dense small cells** (femtocells/picocells) of roughly **sub-metre radius**. This is why 5G must be deployed as **Ultra-Dense Networks (UDN)** with massive base-station densification — one of the core needs driving 5G architecture. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Generation Was "Needed" | Limitation in Previous Gen | Engineering Need | 5G Solution | |----------------------------|------------------|-------------| | Peak rate limited to ~1 Gbps | 4K/8K streaming, cloud gaming | mmWave + Massive MIMO → **20 Gbps** | | Latency ~10 ms (4G) | Tactile internet, V2X | Flexible numerology, Mini-slots → **1 ms** | | ~10⁴ devices/km² (4G) | IoT explosion | Grant-free access, NB-IoT → **10⁶ devices/km²** | | Monolithic EPC hardware | OpEx reduction | SBA (Service-Based Architecture) with NFV/SDN | | Static QoS | Vertical industries (healthcare, automotive) | **Network Slicing** (logical, isolated virtual networks) | > [!TIP] > KTU expects students to **link a 4G limitation → a 5G technical enabler → a use-case** in 14-mark answers. Memorize the above three rows at minimum. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Latency Budget for URLLC The **one-way latency** in a 5G radio interface is bounded by: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{URLLC}} = T_{\text{TTI}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{HARQ}} \end{aligned} $$ For a **mini-slot of 2 OFDM symbols** at 60 kHz subcarrier spacing (numerology $\mu = 2$): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{TTI}} &= 2 \times \frac{1}{\Delta f} = 2 \times \frac{1}{60{,}000} \\ &= 2 \times 16.67 \,\mu\text{s} = 33.33 \,\mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ Assuming: - $T_{\text{prop}} = 1\,\mu\text{s/km} \times 1\text{ km} = 1\,\mu\text{s}$ (small cell) - $T_{\text{proc}} \approx 50\,\mu\text{s}$ (encoder/decoder at gNB) - $T_{\text{HARQ}} \approx 100\,\mu\text{s}$ (no retransmission in URLLC) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{URLLC}} &= 33.33 + 1 + 50 + 100 = 184.33 \,\mu\text{s} \\ &\ll 1 \text{ ms (target)} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* This shows 5G can comfortably hit the **1 ms URLLC target** with **massive headroom** — a critical reason 4G cannot support industrial automation. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cellular Generation Evolution Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR A[1G 1980s
Analog
FDMA
Voice Only] --> B[2G 1990s
GSM TDMA
Voice + SMS
Digital] B --> C[3G 2001
WCDMA CDMA
Mobile Internet
Video Call] C --> D[4G 2010 LTE
OFDMA
All IP Core
Mobile Broadband] D --> E[5G 2020 NR
OFDMA + mmWave
Cloud Native SBA
eMBB URLLC mMTC] E --> F[Beyond 5G 6G Research
THz Communication
AI Native Network
Holographic Comms] A -.->|Limited capacity| A B -.->|Low data rate| B C -.->|High latency 100ms| C D -.->|Insufficient for IoT| D E -.->|Energy efficiency| E ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows indicate *temporal progression*; dashed self-loops indicate the *primary limitation* that triggered the move to the next generation. This is the exact "evolution" pattern KTU examiners test. --- ## 4.2 5G Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph US[USER SIDE] UE1[UE Smartphone
eMBB user] UE2[UE Autonomous Car
URLLC user] UE3[UE IoT Sensor
mMTC user] end subgraph RAN[5G NR RADIO ACCESS NETWORK] gNB1[gNB Macro Cell
Sub 6 GHz] gNB2[gNB Small Cell
mmWave 28 GHz] CU[Central Unit CU
High Layer Processing] DU[Distributed Unit DU
Real Time Processing] end subgraph CORE[5GC 5G CORE SERVICE BASED ARCHITECTURE] AMF[AMF
Access and Mobility Management] SMF[SMF
Session Management] UPF[UPF
User Plane Function
Data Forwarding] NRF[NRF
Network Repository Function] AUSF[AUSF
Authentication Server] UDM[UDM
Unified Data Management] end subgraph APP[APPLICATION LAYER] SLICE1[Network Slice A
eMBB for Streaming] SLICE2[Network Slice B
URLLC for V2X] SLICE3[Network Slice C
mMTC for Smart City] end UE1 --> gNB1 UE2 --> gNB2 UE3 --> gNB1 gNB1 --> CU gNB2 --> DU CU --> AMF DU --> AMF AMF --> SMF AMF --> AUSF AUSF --> UDM SMF --> UPF AMF --> NRF UPF --> SLICE1 UPF --> SLICE2 UPF --> SLICE3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Exam Note:** The diagram highlights the **CU/DU split** (Centralized vs Distributed unit) and the **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** of 5GC, both of which are *absent* in 4G EPC. Memorize the function acronyms: **AMF, SMF, UPF, NRF, AUSF, UDM**. --- ## 4.3 5G Spectrum Allocation Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW[LOW BAND BELOW 1 GHZ] LB[Coverage layer
Long range
Rural and indoor] end subgraph MID[MID BAND 1 TO 6 GHZ] MB[Capacity layer
Urban macro
Balanced coverage and capacity] end subgraph HIGH[HIGH BAND MM WAVE 24 TO 52 GHZ] HB[Hotspot layer
Short range dense urban
Ultra high throughput] end LOW --> MID --> HIGH ``` > [!TIP] > The **three-layer spectrum strategy** is a common 14-mark question. 4G primarily used the *Low Band*; 5G unlocks *Mid Band* (C-band 3.5 GHz) and *High Band* (mmWave). # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Don't confuse "5G NR" with "5G Core"** — NR is the radio interface, 5GC is the core network. Examiners deduct marks when these are conflated. > 2. **Never write "5G uses only mmWave"** — this is incorrect. 5G is a *multi-band* system (sub-1 GHz, sub-6 GHz, mmWave). Loss of up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark question for this error. > 3. **Do not say "4G has all-IP" without qualification** — 4G LTE *does* have an all-IP core (EPC), but its *radio interface* still uses circuit-switched signalling for voice (VoLTE). Be precise. > 4. **Always cite 3GPP Release numbers** (Rel-15 for first 5G NR spec, Rel-16 for URLLC enhancements, Rel-17 for mMTC enhancements). Examiners reward technical specificity. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List the three primary use-case categories defined by ITU-R for 5G (IMT-2020) and give one real-world example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three categories are: 1. **eMBB (enhanced Mobile Broadband):** Provides extremely high data rates for applications like **8K video streaming, VR/AR, and fixed wireless access**. Example: Live sports streaming in 4K on mobile devices. 2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications):** Delivers sub-millisecond latency with 99.999% reliability for **autonomous vehicles, remote robotic surgery, and industrial automation**. 3. **mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communications):** Supports massive density of low-power sensors (~10⁶/km²) for **smart agriculture, smart metering, and environmental monitoring**. *(1 mark each = 3 marks)* --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why does 5G use millimeter-wave (mmWave) frequencies in addition to sub-6 GHz bands?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 5G uses mmWave frequencies (24–52 GHz) because: 1. **Spectrum scarcity:** Sub-6 GHz spectrum is saturated and expensive. mmWave offers **vast untapped bandwidth** — channels up to 800 MHz wide (vs 20 MHz in 4G). *(1 mark)* 2. **Capacity demands:** Wide bandwidth translates to multi-Gbps data rates per Shannon's theorem $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$. *(1 mark)* 3. **Trade-off:** mmWave has limited range and poor wall penetration, so it is used as a **complementary hotspot layer** alongside sub-6 GHz for coverage. *(1 mark)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the evolution of cellular networks from 1G to 5G with a comparative table covering multiple-access technique, switching type, peak data rate, and typical latency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the key driving forces (technical and societal) that necessitated the development of 5G networks. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question A: #### Part (a) — Generation-by-Generation Evolution (7 marks) | Generation | Multiple Access | Switching | Peak Data Rate | Typical Latency | |------------|-----------------|-----------|----------------|-----------------| | 1G (1980s) | FDMA | Circuit | 2.4 kbps | N/A | | 2G (1990s) | TDMA / FDMA | Circuit | 64 kbps | ~300 ms | | 3G (2001) | WCDMA | Packet | 2 Mbps | ~100 ms | | 4G / LTE (2010) | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | All-IP | 1 Gbps | ~10 ms | | 5G / NR (2020) | OFDMA / NOMA | All-IP, Cloud | 20 Gbps | ~1 ms | **Narrative Explanation:** - **1G → 2G:** Transition from analog (FM modulation) to digital (GSM) with TDMA; introduced SMS. - **2G → 3G:** Shift to CDMA/WCDMA; enabled mobile internet (up to 2 Mbps); video calling emerged. - **3G → 4G:** Adoption of OFDMA and flat all-IP EPC architecture; HSPA+ was a bridge technology. - **4G → 5G:** Introduction of 5G NR with flexible numerology ($\mu = 0$ to $4$), massive MIMO, beamforming, and cloud-native SBA core. *(Award: 1 mark for table, 1 mark per generation transition = ~5 marks, 1 mark for 5G specifics, 1 mark for overall flow = 7 marks total)* #### Part (b) — Driving Forces for 5G (7 marks) 1. **Data Explosion:** Mobile data grew **~10× every 5 years**. 4G's 1 Gbps peak is insufficient for 4K/8K streaming and immersive VR/AR. 2. **Latency-Critical Applications:** Tactile internet, V2X, and Industry 4.0 require **<5 ms latency** — unachievable in 4G (~10 ms). 3. **IoT Proliferation:** Billions of sensors for smart cities, agriculture, and logistics require **10⁶ devices/km²** density. 4. **Spectrum Efficiency:** Sub-3 GHz spectrum is congested; 5G exploits **mmWave (24–52 GHz)** to gain wider channels. 5. **Vertical Industries:** Healthcare, automotive, manufacturing demand **customized, deterministic networks** delivered via network slicing. 6. **Energy & Cost:** 5G's virtualization (NFV/SDN) reduces per-bit energy consumption by ~90% vs 4G — a sustainability imperative. 7. **Digital Divide:** 5G FWA (Fixed Wireless Access) extends high-speed broadband to underserved rural areas. *(Award: 1 mark per point with adequate technical depth = 7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Describe the three ITU-defined 5G usage scenarios (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC) with their respective KPI targets and technical enablers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using Shannon's theorem, derive and compare the maximum achievable data rate of a 4G LTE channel (B = 20 MHz, SNR = 10 dB) versus a 5G NR mmWave channel (B = 800 MHz, SNR = 25 dB). Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B: #### Part (a) — 5G Usage Scenarios (7 marks) **1. eMBB — enhanced Mobile Broadband (3 marks)** - **KPI:** Peak rate 20 Gbps DL, user-experienced 100 Mbps DL. - **Enablers:** mmWave (24–52 GHz), Massive MIMO (64–256 antennas), Carrier Aggregation up to 16 carriers, 256-QAM modulation. **2. URLLC — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications (2 marks)** - **KPI:** 1 ms user-plane latency, 99.999% reliability. - **Enablers:** Flexible subcarrier spacing (up to 240 kHz), Mini-slots (2–4 OFDM symbols), diversity combining, packet duplication across carriers. **3. mMTC — massive Machine-Type Communications (2 marks)** - **KPI:** 10⁶ devices/km², >10 years battery life, low data rate. - **Enablers:** Grant-free random access, NB-IoT (180 kHz bandwidth), energy-efficient discontinuous reception (eDRX), power-saving modes. *(Total: 7 marks)* #### Part (b) — Shannon Capacity Comparison (7 marks) **Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{4G}} &= 10^{10/10} = 10 \\ \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{5G}} &= 10^{25/10} = 316.23 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute 4G capacity (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{4G}} &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10) \\ &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(11) \\ &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot 3.459 \\ &\approx 69.2 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute 5G capacity (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{5G}} &= 800 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 316.23) \\ &= 800 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(317.23) \\ &= 800 \times 10^{6} \cdot 8.31 \\ &\approx 6648 \text{ Mbps} \approx 6.65 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Ratio and comment (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C_{\text{5G}}}{C_{\text{4G}}} = \frac{6648}{69.2} \approx 96 \end{aligned} $$ > **Comment:** 5G achieves ~96× higher capacity primarily due to **40× bandwidth expansion** (mmWave) and **higher SNR** from short-range line-of-sight deployment. This validates IMT-2020's 20 Gbps peak target (using even wider mmWave channels and higher-order MIMO). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > Print this section and revise before the exam. - **5G = Fifth Generation** standardized by 3GPP Rel-15 (2018); targets set by ITU-R IMT-2020. - **Three use cases:** eMBB, URLLC, mMTC — memorize the acronym expansion and one example each. - **Peak rates:** 4G ≈ 1 Gbps; 5G ≈ 20 Gbps (DL) / 10 Gbps (UL). - **Latency:** 4G ≈ 10 ms; 5G URLLC ≈ 1 ms. - **Connection density:** 4G ≈ 10⁴/km²; 5G mMTC = 10⁶/km². - **Multiple-access shift:** TDMA → CDMA → OFDMA → OFDMA + NOMA/Grant-free. - **Core shift:** PSTN → EPC → 5GC (Service-Based Architecture with AMF, SMF, UPF, NRF, AUSF, UDM). - **Spectrum:** 5G uses three layers — Low (<1 GHz), Mid (1–6 GHz, e.g., 3.5 GHz C-band), High (24–52 GHz mmWave). - **Shannon's theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — fundamental capacity bound. - **Numerology:** 5G NR supports $\mu = 0,1,2,3,4$ giving subcarrier spacings of 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz. - **Network Slicing:** Allows multiple logical networks on shared physical infrastructure — one of the most exam-relevant 5G innovations. - **Massive MIMO:** 64–256 antenna arrays at the gNB enable beamforming and spatial multiplexing. - **Mini-slots:** 2–4 OFDM symbols — key to URLLC's low latency. - **Driving forces for 5G:** Data explosion, IoT, latency-sensitive apps, spectrum scarcity, vertical industries, sustainability. - **Architecture:** CU/DU split in RAN; SBA in core; NFV/SDN throughout. - **Beyond 5G → 6G:** Research focuses on THz, AI-native networks, holographic communication, sub-microsecond latency.

topics.title: 5G Network Architecture and Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT751 - COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** 5G Network Architecture and Components ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **5G Network Architecture** is the standardized, service-oriented framework defined by 3GPP (Release 15 and beyond) that separates the user plane (UP) and control plane (CP) functions, modularizes the core network into Network Functions (NFs), and introduces a cloud-native, software-driven **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)**. It integrates the **5G New Radio (NR)** access network with the **5G Core (5GC)** through well-defined reference points (N1–N50) to deliver enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication (URLLC), and massive Machine-Type Communication (mMTC). ### The Three Pillars of 5G (ITU IMT-2020 Vision) > [!NOTE] > - **eMBB** – Enhanced Mobile BroadBand: Peak data rates up to **20 Gbps** downlink and **10 Gbps** uplink. > - **URLLC** – Ultra-Reliable Low Latency: User-plane latency as low as **1 ms**, reliability of **99.999%**. > - **mMTC** – Massive Machine Type Communications: Connection density of **10⁶ devices/km²**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of 5G as a **modern, modular hotel chain** compared to the **monolithic, single-building hotel** that 4G was: - **Old 4G Hotel (EPC)**: One huge reception desk handling every guest query — check-in, room service, billing, complaints. Everything is bundled. Slow under load, hard to upgrade. - **New 5G Hotel (SBA)**: A **concierge app** where each service (check-in, billing, room service, security) is an independent microservice. They talk to each other through APIs. A new "spa booking" feature? Just plug in a new microservice — no need to rebuild the hotel. The **gNB** is the front-desk agent (the **Radio Access Network**), and the **5GC** is the concierge app running in the **cloud** — flexible, scalable, and easily updatable. ### Key Architectural Innovations | Innovation | Description | |---|---| | **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** | All 5G core NFs expose services via a common bus (Service-Based Interface, SBI) using HTTP/2 + JSON / REST or gRPC (from R17). | | **CUPS (Control/User Plane Separation)** | Control signaling is split from user data traffic, enabling independent scaling. | | **Network Slicing** | Multiple logical networks on shared physical infrastructure, each tuned to a use case. | | **Cloud-Native Design** | NFs run as Virtual Network Functions (VNFs) or Containerized Network Functions (CNFs) on commodity hardware. | | **Multi-Access Edge Computing (MEC)** | Computation pushed closer to the user, reducing latency for URLLC. | > [!NOTE] > **Bold Constants to Remember:** Peak rate **20 Gbps DL**, latency **1 ms**, device density **10⁶/km²**, mobility up to **500 km/h**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 5G Network — Two Major Domains The 5G system is officially called the **5G System (5GS)** and is logically divided into two cooperating domains: 1. **5G Access Network (5G-AN)** — comprises the **5G New Radio (NR)** base station, the **gNB** (next-generation Node B), and optionally an **ng-eNB** (next-generation eNB that connects LTE eNBs to the 5GC). 2. **5G Core Network (5GC)** — a fully cloud-native, service-based core that replaces the legacy 4G EPC (Evolved Packet Core). The **User Equipment (UE)** connects to the **gNB** over the **Uu (NR) interface**, and the gNB connects to the 5GC through the **NG interface** (split into **N2** for control and **N3** for user plane). ### 2.2 The 5G NR (New Radio) Radio Access Network - Operates in two frequency ranges: - **FR1 (Sub-6 GHz)**: 410 MHz – 7125 MHz. Used for coverage and mobility. - **FR2 (mmWave)**: 24250 MHz – 52600 MHz. Used for ultra-high throughput in dense hotspots. - The gNB may be **split functionally** into: - **CU (Centralized Unit)**: Handles higher-layer protocols (RRC, PDCP, SDAP). - **DU (Distributed Unit)**: Handles lower-layer protocols (RLC, MAC, PHY). - **RU (Radio Unit)**: Handles RF, antenna, and low-PHY. - Three CU-DU split options exist: **Option 1 (intra-PHY)**, **Option 2 (intra-MAC)**, **Option 3 (intra-RLC)**. ### 2.3 The 5G Core (5GC) — Network Functions The 5GC replaces the monolithic EPC nodes (MME, SGW, PGW) with modular **Network Functions (NFs)**, each performing a focused task. > [!IMPORTANT] > The following NFs are mandatory for a complete 5GC, per 3GPP TS 23.501: | Network Function (NF) | Legacy EPC Equivalent | Primary Role | |---|---|---| | **AMF** (Access and Mobility Management Function) | MME (partially) | Termination of NAS, registration, connection, mobility management, access authentication. | | **SMF** (Session Management Function) | MME + PGW-C (partially) | PDU session establishment, IP address allocation, UPF selection, charging rules. | | **UPF** (User Plane Function) | SGW-U + PGW-U | Packet routing/forwarding, QoS enforcement, anchor point for intra/inter-RAT mobility. | | **AUSF** (Authentication Server Function) | HSS (partially) | Authenticates the UE during registration. | | **UDM** (Unified Data Management) | HSS (partially) | Subscription data, credential generation, identifier management. | | **UDR** (Unified Data Repository) | HSS DB | Stores subscription data, policy data, exposure data. | | **PCF** (Policy Control Function) | PCRF | Policy framework, QoS enforcement rules, charging rules. | | **NRF** (NF Repository Function) | — (New) | Service discovery, NF profile registration, status notifications. | | **NSSF** (Network Slice Selection Function) | — (New) | Selects the AMF and determines the allowed **Network Slice(s)** for a UE. | | **NEF** (Network Exposure Function) | SCEF | Securely exposes 3GPP network capabilities to third parties/AFs. | | **AF** (Application Function) | — | Routes application traffic; may interact with PCF via N5. | | **NWDAF** (Network Data Analytics Function) | — (New, R15+) | Provides AI/ML-based analytics for network optimization. | ### 2.4 The Service-Based Architecture (SBA) and Reference Points In SBA, NFs communicate over a **Service-Based Interface (SBI)** using **HTTP/2 + JSON (RESTful APIs)**. The NRF acts as a "phone book" — a new NF registers itself, and other NFs query the NRF to discover its services. #### Two Types of Reference Points in 5GC > [!NOTE] > 1. **Service-Based Interfaces (SBIs)** — between NFs in the same domain (e.g., Namf, Nsmf, Nudm). > 2. **Point-to-Point Reference Points (N-Refs)** — for specific, often data-plane or inter-domain links (e.g., N1, N2, N3, N4, N5). | Reference Point | Connects | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **N1** | UE ↔ AMF | NAS signaling (non-3GPP access also). | | **N2** | (R)AN ↔ AMF | N2 signaling: NAS transport, NGAP. | | **N3** | (R)AN ↔ UPF | User-plane data transfer. | | **N4** | SMF ↔ UPF | PFCP (Packet Forwarding Control Protocol) — controls UPF behavior. | | **N5** | PCF ↔ AF | Application policy exchange. | | **N6** | UPF ↔ DN (Data Network) | Internet / external network. | | **N7** | SMF ↔ PCF | Policy & charging rules. | | **N8** | AMF ↔ UDM | Subscription data retrieval. | | **N9** | UPF ↔ UPF | Inter-UPF tunneling (e.g., for roaming or MEC). | | **N11** | AMF ↔ SMF | PDU session-related signaling. | | **N12** | AMF ↔ AUSF | Authentication. | | **N15** | PCF ↔ AMF (non-session policy) | UE access & mobility policy. | | **N22** | NSSF ↔ AMF | Slice selection. | ### 2.5 Protocol Stack — User Plane vs. Control Plane > [!IMPORTANT] > The protocol stack is the most exam-frequent diagram for KTU. Memorize it. **User Plane (UP) — UE → gNB → UPF → DN:** - **UE side**: App → IP → PDCP → RLC → MAC → PHY - **gNB (CU side)**: SDAP → PDCP (CU) | RLC/MAC/PHY (DU) - **UPF side**: GTP-U → UDP → IP → L2/L1 - **Tunneling** between gNB and UPF uses **GTP-U (GPRS Tunneling Protocol – User Plane)**. **Control Plane (CP) — UE → gNB → AMF:** - **UE side**: NAS (to AMF) over **N1**, RRC (to gNB) over SRB - **gNB**: RRC ↔ NGAP (N2) ↔ AMF - Between NFs in core: HTTP/2 + JSON / REST (SBI) ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Table | Parameter / Equation | Value or Description | Unit | |---|---|---| | Peak Downlink Data Rate | $R_{DL} = \frac{N_{RB} \cdot N_{SC}^{RB} \cdot N_{bits}^{sym} \cdot N_{layers} \cdot \nu_{LTE}}{T_{sym}}$ (scaled to 5G NR numerologies) | Gbps | | Max DL (5G NR theoretical) | **20 Gbps** | Gbps | | Max UL (5G NR theoretical) | **10 Gbps** | Gbps | | User-plane latency (URLLC) | **1 ms** one-way | ms | | Control-plane latency | **10–20 ms** (idle → connected) | ms | | Connection density | $10^{6}$ devices / km² | devices/km² | | Subcarrier Spacing (SCS) | $\Delta f = 2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \text{ kHz}$, $\mu \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ | kHz | | Max SCS ($\mu=4$, FR2) | $\Delta f = 240$ kHz | kHz | | OFDM Symbol Duration (CP included) | $T_{sym} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} + T_{CP}$ | seconds | | Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = 14 \cdot T_{sym} \cdot 2^{-\mu}$ | seconds | | Numerology ($\mu$) | 0: 15 kHz, 1: 30 kHz, 2: 60 kHz, 3: 120 kHz, 4: 240 kHz | — | | Max Bandwidth (FR1) | 100 MHz (per CC), up to 16 CCs in CA → **800 MHz** aggregated | MHz | | Max Bandwidth (FR2) | 400 MHz (per CC), up to 16 CCs → **6.4 GHz** aggregated | MHz | | Mobility Support | up to **500 km/h** | km/h | | Reliability (URLLC) | $1 - 10^{-5} = 99.999\%$ | — | > [!NOTE] > ⚠️ **Pipe Symbol Rule**: In the formulas above, the `|` symbol is **avoided**. For absolute value or norm, use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing inline: e.g., $\vert H(f) \vert$ or $\lVert x \rVert$. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart factories (Industry 4.0)**: URLLC with 1 ms latency to control robotic arms in real time. - **Autonomous vehicles**: V2X communication with mMTC for traffic infrastructure and URLLC for collision avoidance. - **Telemedicine**: Remote surgery using URLLC + MEC (sub-millisecond round-trip). - **Smart cities**: mMTC for millions of IoT sensors (lighting, parking, pollution). - **Immersive AR/VR**: eMBB + MEC for cloud-rendered graphics with low motion-to-photon latency. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: 5G NR Slot Duration for a Given Numerology The **numerology** $\mu$ in 5G NR defines the subcarrier spacing (SCS). Unlike LTE which uses a fixed 15 kHz SCS, 5G NR supports scalable numerologies to support diverse use cases. **Step 1 — Base Subcarrier Spacing (LTE baseline):** $$\Delta f_{LTE} = 15 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2 — Numerology Scaling Rule (per 3GPP TS 38.211):** $$\Delta f_{\mu} = 2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \text{ kHz}, \quad \mu = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$ This is the official 3GPP-defined scaling. Each increment of $\mu$ doubles the SCS. **Step 3 — Useful OFDM Symbol Duration (excluding CP):** $$T_{u} = \frac{1}{\Delta f_{\mu}}$$ **Step 4 — Including Cyclic Prefix (CP):** For normal CP, the CP duration depends on the symbol index within the slot (1st symbol has a slightly longer CP). For simplicity in exams: $$T_{sym} \approx \frac{1}{\Delta f_{\mu}} + T_{CP}$$ **Step 5 — Slot Duration (normal CP, 14 symbols per slot):** $$T_{slot} = 14 \cdot T_{sym}$$ **Step 6 — Substituting (for normal CP, CP overhead approximated):** $$T_{slot} = \frac{14 \cdot (1 + \alpha_{CP})}{\Delta f_{\mu}} = \frac{14 \cdot (1 + \alpha_{CP})}{2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \times 10^{3}} \text{ s}$$ where $\alpha_{CP}$ is the CP overhead factor (typically 0.07 for normal CP, 0.14 for extended CP). **Step 7 — Numerical Example (KSE): Compute $T_{slot}$ for $\mu = 2$ (60 kHz), normal CP:** $$\Delta f_2 = 2^{2} \cdot 15 = 60 \text{ kHz}$$ $$T_{u} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^{3}} = 16.67 \text{ \mu s}$$ $$T_{sym} \approx 16.67 + 1.17 = 17.84 \text{ \mu s (with CP)}$$ $$T_{slot} = 14 \cdot 17.84 = 249.76 \text{ \mu s} \approx 0.25 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 8 — Analytical Insight:** A subframe (1 ms) contains $2^{\mu}$ slots. For $\mu = 0$ (15 kHz, LTE-compatible), 1 ms = 1 subframe = 1 slot. For $\mu = 2$ (60 kHz), 1 subframe contains $2^2 = 4$ slots. This is why 5G NR can support **very low latency** at high frequencies — small slot duration means quicker transmission. ### 3.2 Derivation: Peak Data Rate Estimate for 5G NR The Shannon capacity is the upper bound. For 5G NR peak throughput, we use: $$R_{peak} = N_{streams} \cdot BW_{eff} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \cdot \eta$$ But the *practical* peak throughput (3GPP targets) is achieved under ideal conditions: **Step 1 — Maximum Aggregated Bandwidth (FR2, 16 CCs of 400 MHz each):** $$BW_{agg} = 16 \cdot 400 = 6400 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum Spectral Efficiency (DL, 64-QAM, 4×4 MIMO, FR2):** $$\eta_{max} \approx 30 \text{ bps/Hz (theoretical)}$$ **Step 3 — Peak DL Throughput:** $$R_{DL,peak} = 6400 \times 10^{6} \cdot 30 = 1.92 \times 10^{11} \text{ bps} \approx 19.2 \text{ Gbps} \approx 20 \text{ Gbps}$$ This matches the 3GPP-defined IMT-2020 peak data rate of **20 Gbps DL**. > [!NOTE] > The 3GPP-defined peak rate is computed using specific assumptions (e.g., 256-QAM, 8 layers, FR2, full 6.4 GHz BW). The above is an order-of-magnitude derivation suitable for KTU-style problems. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 4G EPC vs. 5GC | Feature | 4G EPC | 5GC | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Monolithic, point-to-point | Service-Based (SBA), modular | | Reference points | S1-MME, S1-U, S5, S6a, Gx, Rx | N1–N50 (N-Refs) + Namf, Nsmf, Nudm (SBIs) | | Signaling protocol | Diameter (mostly) | HTTP/2 + JSON / REST, gRPC | | Node model | Physical / virtual | Cloud-native, CNF-friendly | | CUPS | Optional (later releases) | Native, mandatory | | Network slicing | Not supported | End-to-end (RAN + Core + Transport) | | Latency target | ~50 ms | 1 ms (URLLC) | | Peak data rate | 1 Gbps | 20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL | | Authentication | 4G-AKA | 5G-AKA, EAP-AKA', EAP-TLS | | UE identifier | IMSI | SUPI (Subscription Permanent Identifier, encrypted → SUCI) | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners love the "Compare 4G EPC and 5GC" question. The 7-marker is often (a) Draw the architecture and (b) List 5 differences. Always include a labelled diagram and a comparison table. ### 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: UE Registration in 5G This is a high-frequency KTU topic. Let us walk through it rigorously. **Step 1 — UE → gNB: Random Access (RACH)** — UE sends a preamble on PRACH. **Step 2 — gNB → UE: Random Access Response (RAR)** — TA (timing advance), temp C-RNTI, UL grant. **Step 3 — UE → gNB: RRC Setup Request** — Initial NAS message (Registration Request) embedded in RRC. **Step 4 — gNB → AMF: Initial UE Message over N2** — NGAP message containing the NAS Registration Request. **Step 5 — AMF ↔ AUSF / UDM: Authentication & Security** — 5G-AKA or EAP-AKA'; mutual authentication. **Step 6 — AMF ↔ UDM: Subscription Retrieval** — Fetching subscriber profile, SMF selection data. **Step 7 — AMF ↔ NSSF: Slice Selection** — Determine allowed NSSAI (Network Slice Selection Assistance Information). **Step 8 — AMF ↔ PCF: Policy Retrieval** — UE access & mobility policy, UE slice policy. **Step 9 — AMF → UE: Security Mode Command / Complete** — NAS integrity & ciphering activated. **Step 10 — AMF → UE: Registration Accept** — Includes 5G-GUTI, allowed NSSAI, registration area. **Step 11 — UE → AMF: Registration Complete** — PDU session establishment may follow. Total control-plane latency: **~10–20 ms** (compared to ~100+ ms in 4G). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level 5G Network Architecture (Service-Based View) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph ACCESS["5G ACCESS NETWORK (5G AN)"] UE["UE
User Equipment"] GNB["gNB
Next Generation Node B
CU + DU + RU"] NGE["ng-eNB
Optional LTE anchor"] end subgraph CORESVC["5G CORE NETWORK (5GC) - SERVICE-BASED ARCHITECTURE"] direction TB BUS["SBI BUS
HTTP/2 + JSON / REST"] AMF["AMF
Access & Mobility"] SMF["SMF
Session Management"] UPF["UPF
User Plane Function"] AUSF["AUSF
Authentication Server"] UDM["UDM
Unified Data Mgmt"] UDR["UDR
Unified Data Repository"] PCF["PCF
Policy Control"] NRF["NRF
NF Repository"] NSSF["NSSF
Slice Selection"] NEF["NEF
Network Exposure"] NWDAF["NWDAF
Analytics Function"] end subgraph EDGE["EDGE / EXTERNAL"] MEC["MEC
Multi-Access Edge Cloud"] DN["DATA NETWORK
Internet / Cloud"] AF["AF
Application Function"] end UE -->|Uu NR| GNB GNB -->|N2 NGAP| AMF GNB -->|N3 GTP-U| UPF NGE -->|N2 NGAP| AMF AMF --- BUS SMF --- BUS AUSF --- BUS UDM --- BUS UDR --- BUS PCF --- BUS NRF --- BUS NSSF --- BUS NEF --- BUS NWDAF --- BUS UPF -->|N6| DN UPF -->|N9| MEC SMF -->|N4 PFCP| UPF AF -->|N5| PCF AF -.->|N33| NEF NSSF -->|N22| AMF AMF -->|N11| SMF AMF -->|N8| UDM AMF -->|N12| AUSF AMF -->|N15| PCF UDM -->|N36| UDR ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: UE PDU Session Establishment ```mermaid graph LR subgraph STEP1["STEP 1 - TRIGGER"] A1["UE initiates
PDU Session Request"] end subgraph STEP2["STEP 2 - AMF FORWARDING"] B1["AMF receives NAS
and forwards via N11"] B2["SMF checks subscription
via N7 PCF and N10 UDM"] end subgraph STEP3["STEP 3 - UPF CONTROL"] C1["SMF selects UPF
and sends N4 PFCP"] C2["UPF allocates IP
and sets up N3 tunnel"] end subgraph STEP4["STEP 4 - RAN TUNNEL"] D1["SMF signals AMF N2
AMF signals gNB N2"] D2["gNB establishes
DRB and GTP-U tunnel"] end subgraph STEP5["STEP 5 - ACCEPTANCE"] E1["UE receives PDU
Session Accept NAS"] E2["User data flows:
UE - gNB - UPF - DN"] end A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1 --> D2 --> E1 --> E2 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: CU-DU-RU Split in gNB (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CU["CENTRALIZED UNIT CU"] SDAP["SDAP
Service Data Adaptation"] PDCP["PDCP
Packet Data Convergence"] end subgraph DU["DISTRIBUTED UNIT DU"] RLC["RLC
Radio Link Control"] MAC["MAC
Medium Access Control"] HIGHPHY["High-PHY
FFT, Channel Coding"] end subgraph RU["RADIO UNIT RU"] LOWPHY["Low-PHY
Filter, DAC, ADC"] RF["RF & Antenna
Beamforming"] end SDAP --> PDCP --> RLC --> MAC --> HIGHPHY --> LOWPHY --> RF RF -->|OTA| UE["UE"] UE -->|OTA| RF ``` ### 4.4 Sequence Diagram: UE Registration Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as UE participant G as gNB participant A as AMF participant S as AUSF participant D as UDM participant N as NSSF participant P as PCF U->>G: RACH Preamble (PRACH) G-->>U: Random Access Response (RAR) U->>G: RRC Setup Request + NAS Registration Request G->>A: N2: Initial UE Message (NGAP) A->>A: Select AUSF & UDM via NRF discovery A->>S: N12: Authentication Request S->>D: N13: Fetch auth vector D-->>S: 5G-AKA vectors S-->>A: Authentication Response A-->>U: NAS Authentication Request U-->>A: NAS Authentication Response A->>N: N22: Slice Selection (Requested NSSAI) N-->>A: Allowed NSSAI + AMF slice config A->>P: N15: Policy retrieval P-->>A: Mobility & access policy A-->>G: N2: Security Mode Command G-->>U: RRC Security Mode Command U-->>G: RRC Security Mode Complete G->>A: N2: Security Mode Complete A-->>U: NAS Security Mode Command U-->>A: NAS Security Mode Complete A-->>U: NAS Registration Accept (5G-GUTI, Allowed NSSAI) U-->>A: NAS Registration Complete ``` > [!NOTE] > The sequence diagram is exam-friendly and earns full marks when drawn with all major messages labelled. Memorize the 8–10 main arrows. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]** *Define 5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA). How is it different from the 4G EPC point-to-point architecture?* **Model Answer:** 5G SBA is a modular architecture where each 5G core function (AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM, PCF, NRF, NSSF, etc.) exposes its services over a common **Service-Based Interface (SBI)** using **HTTP/2 + JSON (RESTful APIs)**. The **NRF** acts as a service registry. Unlike 4G EPC, which used rigid point-to-point reference points (S1-MME, S5, Gx) and Diameter protocol, 5GC uses dynamic service discovery, allowing NFs to scale independently and be deployed as cloud-native microservices. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]** *List any three Network Functions in the 5G Core and state their roles.* **Model Answer:** 1. **AMF (Access and Mobility Management Function)**: handles NAS signaling, registration, connection, and mobility. 2. **SMF (Session Management Function)**: manages PDU sessions, IP allocation, and UPF selection. 3. **UPF (User Plane Function)**: routes/forwards user data packets, enforces QoS, anchors mobility. (Also acceptable: AUSF for authentication, UDM for subscription data, PCF for policy, NSSF for slicing, NRF for service discovery.) --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — 5G Architecture & Components** > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **5G Service-Based Architecture**. Explain the role of **AMF, SMF, UPF, NRF, and NSSF**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **CU-DU-RU functional split** in the 5G NR gNB. List two advantages and two challenges of CU-DU split deployment. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) - Draw the 5G SBA diagram (use the Mermaid-rendered block diagram from Section 4.1 as reference; in the exam paper, draw a clean labelled block diagram). - Label the following clearly: - **UE → gNB (Uu interface, NR)** - **gNB → AMF (N2)** and **gNB → UPF (N3)** - **SBI Bus** connecting: AMF, SMF, AUSF, UDM, UDR, PCF, NRF, NSSF, NEF - **UPF → DN (N6)** - Briefly describe each NF's role (1-line each — 7 functions × ~1 line = enough for 7 marks when paired with diagram). **Exam-ready description of each NF:** | NF | One-line Role | |---|---| | AMF | Handles NAS, registration, connection, mobility. | | SMF | Manages PDU sessions, UPF selection, charging. | | UPF | Forwards user data, anchors mobility, enforces QoS. | | NRF | Service discovery, NF profile repository. | | NSSF | Selects the network slice and target AMF for a UE. | #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) **CU-DU-RU Functional Split:** - **CU (Centralized Unit)**: Hosts **SDAP** and **PDCP** layers. Non-real-time intensive. Can be deployed at a central cloud site. - **DU (Distributed Unit)**: Hosts **RLC, MAC, and high-PHY** (FFT, channel coding). Real-time constraints. Typically located at a regional aggregation site. - **RU (Radio Unit)**: Hosts **low-PHY** (filtering, DAC/ADC) and **RF/antenna** with beamforming. Located at the cell site (tower top or pole). **Two common split options (3GPP TR 38.801):** - **Option 2 (intra-MAC split)**: Most widely deployed. CU = PDCP/SDAP, DU = RLC/MAC/PHY. F1 interface between CU and DU. - **Option 7 (intra-PHY split)**: CU = upper PHY, DU = lower PHY + RF. Fronthaul demanding (e.g., eCPRI). **Advantages:** 1. **Centralization gains** — Cooperative Multi-Point (CoMP), better interference management. 2. **Cost-effective scaling** — Shared CU pool serves many DUs/RUs. Reduces CAPEX. 3. **Flexibility** — RAN functions can be hosted on COTS hardware in cloud. **Challenges:** 1. **Fronthaul bandwidth** — Option 7 requires ~10s of Gbps; needs fiber. 2. **Latency on fronthaul** — Strict timing requirements between CU-DU. 3. **Complexity in orchestration** — Multi-layer resource management is harder. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Architecture Diagram:** > 1. **Missing interface labels (N1, N2, N3, N4)**: A diagram without reference points loses 1–2 marks. Always label the interfaces. > 2. **Vague NF descriptions**: Don't just write "AMF handles mobility" — write "AMF terminates NAS signaling (N1), manages registration/connection/mobility, and selects SMF (N11)". > 3. **No SBI mention**: Failing to mention HTTP/2 + JSON or NRF means missing the core of SBA. > 4. **Confusing 5G-AN with 5GC**: Clearly demarcate the radio access network (gNB, ng-eNB) from the core network. > 5. **Skipping NSSAI / S-NSSAI in slicing questions**: Always mention the **NSSAI (Network Slice Selection Assistance Information)** and the role of **NSSF** — this is a 5G-exclusive feature. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — 5G Network Functions & Reference Points** > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the role of **AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM, PCF, and NRF** in the 5G Core. Show the **SBI-based interactions** between them. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **UE Registration Procedure** in 5G NR. List the key control-plane messages exchanged between the UE, gNB, AMF, AUSF, and UDM. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) Refer to the **NF Summary Table** in Section 2.3 above. Use the **SBA Diagram** from Section 4.1. Highlight that: - The **SBI bus** uses **HTTP/2 + JSON (RESTful APIs)**. - **NRF** is queried by AMF/SMF to discover AUSF, UDM, PCF endpoints — a process called **NF Service Discovery**. - The **Service Consumer** (e.g., AMF) calls a **Service Producer** (e.g., AUSF) via REST operations: `GET / POST / PUT / DELETE`. - Example: AMF calls `POST /nausf-auth/v1/ue-authentications` to authenticate a UE. **Interaction Summary:** | Source (Consumer) | Destination (Producer) | Operation | |---|---|---| | AMF | NRF | NFDiscover (service discovery) | | AMF | AUSF | Nausf_UEAuthentication | | AMF | UDM | Nudm_UECM_Registration | | AMF | PCF | Npcf_AMPolicyControl | | SMF | PCF | Npcf_SMPolicyControl | | SMF | UDM | Nudm_SM_Retrieval | | SMF | UPF | N4 (PFCP) — note: N4 is NOT SBI | #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) Follow the **UE Registration Sequence Diagram** from Section 4.4. List the key control-plane messages: | Step | Message | From → To | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | RACH Preamble | UE → gNB | Initial access. | | 2 | RAR (Random Access Response) | gNB → UE | Timing advance, UL grant. | | 3 | RRC Setup Request + NAS Registration Request | UE → gNB | Establishes SRB1, carries NAS. | | 4 | N2: Initial UE Message (NGAP) | gNB → AMF | Forwards NAS to core. | | 5 | N12: Nausf_UEAuthentication_Authenticate | AMF → AUSF | Start 5G-AKA. | | 6 | NAS Authentication Request / Response | AMF ↔ UE | Mutual authentication. | | 7 | N22: Nnssf_NSSelection_Get | AMF → NSSF | Slice selection. | | 8 | N8: Nudm_UECM_Registration / Nudm_SDM_Get | AMF → UDM | Subscription data fetch. | | 9 | N15: Npcf_AMPolicyControl_Get | AMF → PCF | Access & mobility policy. | | 10 | NAS Security Mode Command / Complete | AMF ↔ UE | Enable NAS security. | | 11 | NAS Registration Accept | AMF → UE | 5G-GUTI, Allowed NSSAI. | | 12 | NAS Registration Complete | UE → AMF | Confirmation. | **Control-plane latency target**: ~10–20 ms from idle to connected. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Registration Procedure:** > 1. **Confusing 4G (EPC) and 5G (5GC) messages**: 4G uses S1-AP; 5G uses **NGAP over N2**. > 2. **Skipping AUSF / UDM interaction**: Authentication is done by **AUSF**, not AMF. Subscription data is from **UDM**, not HSS (HSS is the 4G term). > 3. **Missing slice selection (NSSF)**: This is a 5G-only step. If asked about 5G and the answer doesn't mention NSSAI/NSSF, marks are deducted. > 4. **Writing HTTP/2 for N2 / N4**: **N2 uses NGAP/SCTP**, **N4 uses PFCP/UDP**. Only the SBI uses HTTP/2. > 5. **Not mentioning 5G-GUTI**: The Temporary Identifier in 5G is **5G-GUTI**, not GUTI (4G) or TMSI (2G/3G). --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > - **5G = 5GS = 5G-AN (gNB + ng-eNB) + 5GC (cloud-native SBA)**. > - **3 pillars**: eMBB (20 Gbps), URLLC (1 ms, 99.999%), mMTC (10⁶/km²). > - **Numerology $\mu$** in 5G NR: $\Delta f = 2^{\mu} \cdot 15$ kHz, with $\mu \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$. > - **Slot duration** at $\mu = 0$ is 1 ms (LTE-compatible); at $\mu = 2$ it is 0.25 ms. > - **SBA key idea**: NFs talk over **HTTP/2 + JSON** via a common bus; **NRF** is the service registry. > - **Mandatory 5GC NFs**: AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM, UDR, PCF, NRF, NSSF (3GPP TS 23.501). > - **Reference points** to memorize: N1 (UE-AMF, NAS), N2 (gNB-AMF, NGAP), N3 (gNB-UPF, GTP-U), N4 (SMF-UPF, PFCP), N6 (UPF-DN). > - **gNB split**: CU (SDAP + PDCP) ↔ DU (RLC + MAC + High-PHY) ↔ RU (Low-PHY + RF). > - **Network Slicing**: Selects S-NSSAI via **NSSF**; supports eMBB, URLLC, mMTC slices on shared infra. > - **CUPS**: Control Plane (AMF, SMF) is separated from User Plane (UPF) — native in 5G. > - **Identifiers**: SUPI (encrypted → SUCI), 5G-GUTI, PEI (Permanent Equipment Identifier). > - **Latency targets**: Control plane ~10–20 ms, User plane ~1 ms (URLLC). > - **Mobility**: Supported up to **500 km/h** (high-speed trains). > - **Peak rates**: 20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL (FR2, 16 CC aggregation, ideal conditions). > - **Authentication**: 5G-AKA, EAP-AKA', EAP-TLS — handled by **AUSF**. > - **Cloud-native**: NFs are VNFs or CNFs deployed on cloud infrastructure.
